 Book I Book I Book II Book II Book III Book III Book IV Book IV I I вҖңWell, Prince, so Genoa and Lucca are now just family estates of the Buonapartes. But I warn you, if you donвҖҷt tell me that this means war, if you still try to defend the infamies and horrors perpetrated by that AntichristвҒ вҖ”I really believe he is AntichristвҒ вҖ”I will have nothing more to do with you and you are no longer my friend, no longer my вҖҳfaithful slave,вҖҷ as you call yourself! But how do you do? I see I have frightened youвҒ вҖ”sit down and tell me all the news.вҖқ It was in July, 1805, and the speaker was the well-known Anna PГЎvlovna SchГ©rer, maid of honor and favorite of the Empress MГЎrya FГ«dorovna. With these words she greeted Prince VasГӯli KurГЎgin, a man of high rank and importance, who was the first to arrive at her reception. Anna PГЎvlovna had had a cough for some days. She was, as she said, suffering from la grippe; grippe being then a new word in St.В Petersburg, used only by the elite. All her invitations without exception, written in French, and delivered by a scarlet-liveried footman that morning, ran as follows: вҖңIf you have nothing better to do, Count (or Prince), and if the prospect of spending an evening with a poor invalid is not too terrible, I shall be very charmed to see you tonight between 7 and 10вҒ вҖ”Annette SchГ©rer.вҖқ вҖңHeavens! what a virulent attack!вҖқ replied the prince, not in the least disconcerted by this reception. He had just entered, wearing an embroidered court uniform, knee breeches, and shoes, and had stars on his breast and a serene expression on his flat face. He spoke in that refined French in which our grandfathers not only spoke but thought, and with the gentle, patronizing intonation natural to a man of importance who had grown old in society and at court. He went up to Anna PГЎvlovna, kissed her hand, presenting to her his bald, scented, and shining head, and complacently seated himself on the sofa. вҖңFirst of all, dear friend, tell me how you are. Set your friendвҖҷs mind at rest,вҖқ said he without altering his tone, beneath the politeness and affected sympathy of which indifference and even irony could be discerned. вҖңCan one be well while suffering morally? Can one be calm in times like these if one has any feeling?вҖқ said Anna PГЎvlovna. вҖңYou are staying the whole evening, I hope?вҖқ вҖңAnd the fГӘte at the English ambassadorвҖҷs? Today is Wednesday. I must put in an appearance there,вҖқ said the prince. вҖңMy daughter is coming for me to take me there.вҖқ вҖңI thought todayвҖҷs fГӘte had been canceled. I confess all these festivities and fireworks are becoming wearisome.вҖқ вҖңIf they had known that you wished it, the entertainment would have been put off,вҖқ said the prince, who, like a wound-up clock, by force of habit said things he did not even wish to be believed. вҖңDonвҖҷt tease! Well, and what has been decided about NovosГӯltsevвҖҷs dispatch? You know everything.вҖқ вҖңWhat can one say about it?вҖқ replied the prince in a cold, listless tone. вҖңWhat has been decided? They have decided that Buonaparte has burnt his boats, and I believe that we are ready to burn ours.вҖқ Prince VasГӯli always spoke languidly, like an actor repeating a stale part. Anna PГЎvlovna SchГ©rer on the contrary, despite her forty years, overflowed with animation and impulsiveness. To be an enthusiast had become her social vocation and, sometimes even when she did not feel like it, she became enthusiastic in order not to disappoint the expectations of those who knew her. The subdued smile which, though it did not suit her faded features, always played round her lips expressed, as in a spoiled child, a continual consciousness of her charming defect, which she neither wished, nor could, nor considered it necessary, to correct. In the midst of a conversation on political matters Anna PГЎvlovna burst out: вҖңOh, donвҖҷt speak to me of Austria. Perhaps I donвҖҷt understand things, but Austria never has wished, and does not wish, for war. She is betraying us! Russia alone must save Europe. Our gracious sovereign recognizes his high vocation and will be true to it. That is the one thing I have faith in! Our good and wonderful sovereign has to perform the noblest role on earth, and he is so virtuous and noble that God will not forsake him. He will fulfill his vocation and crush the hydra of revolution, which has become more terrible than ever in the person of this murderer and villain! We alone must avenge the blood of the just one.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Whom, I ask you, can we rely on?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ England with her commercial spirit will not and cannot understand the Emperor AlexanderвҖҷs loftiness of soul. She has refused to evacuate Malta. She wanted to find, and still seeks, some secret motive in our actions. What answer did NovosГӯltsev get? None. The English have not understood and cannot understand the self-abnegation of our Emperor who wants nothing for himself, but only desires the good of mankind. And what have they promised? Nothing! And what little they have promised they will not perform! Prussia has always declared that Bonaparte is invincible, and that all Europe is powerless before him.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And I donвҖҷt believe a word that Hardenburg says, or Haugwitz either. This famous Prussian neutrality is just a trap. I have faith only in God and the lofty destiny of our adored monarch. He will save Europe!вҖқ She suddenly paused, smiling at her own impetuosity. вҖңI think,вҖқ said the prince with a smile, вҖңthat if you had been sent instead of our dear Wintzingerode you would have captured the King of PrussiaвҖҷs consent by assault. You are so eloquent. Will you give me a cup of tea?вҖқ вҖңIn a moment. Apropos,вҖқ she added, becoming calm again, вҖңI am expecting two very interesting men tonight, le Vicomte de Mortemart, who is connected with the Montmorencys through the Rohans, one of the best French families. He is one of the genuine Г©migrГ©s, the good ones. And also the AbbГ© Morio. Do you know that profound thinker? He has been received by the Emperor. Had you heard?вҖқ вҖңI shall be delighted to meet them,вҖқ said the prince. вҖңBut tell me,вҖқ he added with studied carelessness as if it had only just occurred to him, though the question he was about to ask was the chief motive of his visit, вҖңis it true that the Dowager Empress wants Baron Funke to be appointed first secretary at Vienna? The baron by all accounts is a poor creature.вҖқ Prince VasГӯli wished to obtain this post for his son, but others were trying through the Dowager Empress MГЎrya FГ«dorovna to secure it for the baron. Anna PГЎvlovna almost closed her eyes to indicate that neither she nor anyone else had a right to criticize what the Empress desired or was pleased with. вҖңBaron Funke has been recommended to the Dowager Empress by her sister,вҖқ was all she said, in a dry and mournful tone. As she named the Empress, Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs face suddenly assumed an expression of profound and sincere devotion and respect mingled with sadness, and this occurred every time she mentioned her illustrious patroness. She added that Her Majesty had deigned to show Baron Funke beaucoup dвҖҷestime, and again her face clouded over with sadness. The prince was silent and looked indifferent. But, with the womanly and courtierlike quickness and tact habitual to her, Anna PГЎvlovna wished both to rebuke him (for daring to speak as he had done of a man recommended to the Empress) and at the same time to console him, so she said: вҖңNow about your family. Do you know that since your daughter came out everyone has been enraptured by her? They say she is amazingly beautiful.вҖқ The prince bowed to signify his respect and gratitude. вҖңI often think,вҖқ she continued after a short pause, drawing nearer to the prince and smiling amiably at him as if to show that political and social topics were ended and the time had come for intimate conversationвҒ вҖ”вҖңI often think how unfairly sometimes the joys of life are distributed. Why has fate given you two such splendid children? I donвҖҷt speak of Anatole, your youngest. I donвҖҷt like him,вҖқ she added in a tone admitting of no rejoinder and raising her eyebrows. вҖңTwo such charming children. And really you appreciate them less than anyone, and so you donвҖҷt deserve to have them.вҖқ And she smiled her ecstatic smile. вҖңI canвҖҷt help it,вҖқ said the prince. вҖңLavater would have said I lack the bump of paternity.вҖқ вҖңDonвҖҷt joke; I mean to have a serious talk with you. Do you know I am dissatisfied with your younger son? Between ourselvesвҖқ (and her face assumed its melancholy expression), вҖңhe was mentioned at Her MajestyвҖҷs and you were pitied.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The prince answered nothing, but she looked at him significantly, awaiting a reply. He frowned. вҖңWhat would you have me do?вҖқ he said at last. вҖңYou know I did all a father could for their education, and they have both turned out fools. Ippolit is at least a quiet fool, but Anatole is an active one. That is the only difference between them.вҖқ He said this smiling in a way more natural and animated than usual, so that the wrinkles round his mouth very clearly revealed something unexpectedly coarse and unpleasant. вҖңAnd why are children born to such men as you? If you were not a father there would be nothing I could reproach you with,вҖқ said Anna PГЎvlovna, looking up pensively. вҖңI am your faithful slave and to you alone I can confess that my children are the bane of my life. It is the cross I have to bear. That is how I explain it to myself. It canвҖҷt be helped!вҖқ He said no more, but expressed his resignation to cruel fate by a gesture. Anna PГЎvlovna meditated. вҖңHave you never thought of marrying your prodigal son Anatole?вҖқ she asked. вҖңThey say old maids have a mania for matchmaking, and though I donвҖҷt feel that weakness in myself as yet, I know a little person who is very unhappy with her father. She is a relation of yours, Princess MГЎrya BolkГіnskaya.вҖқ Prince VasГӯli did not reply, though, with the quickness of memory and perception befitting a man of the world, he indicated by a movement of the head that he was considering this information. вҖңDo you know,вҖқ he said at last, evidently unable to check the sad current of his thoughts, вҖңthat Anatole is costing me forty thousand rubles a year? And,вҖқ he went on after a pause, вҖңwhat will it be in five years, if he goes on like this?вҖқ Presently he added: вҖңThatвҖҷs what we fathers have to put up with.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Is this princess of yours rich?вҖқ вҖңHer father is very rich and stingy. He lives in the country. He is the well-known Prince BolkГіnski who had to retire from the army under the late Emperor, and was nicknamed вҖҳthe King of Prussia.вҖҷ He is very clever but eccentric, and a bore. The poor girl is very unhappy. She has a brother; I think you know him, he married Liza Meinen lately. He is an aide-de-camp of KutГәzovвҖҷs and will be here tonight.вҖқ вҖңListen, dear Annette,вҖқ said the prince, suddenly taking Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs hand and for some reason drawing it downwards. вҖңArrange that affair for me and I shall always be your most devoted slaveвҒ вҖ”slafe with an f, as a village elder of mine writes in his reports. She is rich and of good family and thatвҖҷs all I want.вҖқ And with the familiarity and easy grace peculiar to him, he raised the maid of honorвҖҷs hand to his lips, kissed it, and swung it to and fro as he lay back in his armchair, looking in another direction. вҖңAttendez,вҖқ said Anna PГЎvlovna, reflecting, вҖңIвҖҷll speak to Lise, young BolkГіnskiвҖҷs wife, this very evening, and perhaps the thing can be arranged. It shall be on your familyвҖҷs behalf that IвҖҷll start my apprenticeship as old maid.вҖқ X X Prince VasГӯli kept the promise he had given to Princess DrubetskГЎya who had spoken to him on behalf of her only son BorГӯs on the evening of Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs soiree. The matter was mentioned to the Emperor, an exception made, and BorГӯs transferred into the regiment of SemГ«nov Guards with the rank of cornet. He received, however, no appointment to KutГәzovвҖҷs staff despite all Anna MikhГЎylovnaвҖҷs endeavors and entreaties. Soon after Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs reception Anna MikhГЎylovna returned to Moscow and went straight to her rich relations, the RostГіvs, with whom she stayed when in the town and where her darling BorГ©nka, who had only just entered a regiment of the line and was being at once transferred to the Guards as a cornet, had been educated from childhood and lived for years at a time. The Guards had already left Petersburg on the tenth of August, and her son, who had remained in Moscow for his equipment, was to join them on the march to RadzivГӯlov. It was St.В NatГЎlyaвҖҷs day and the name day of two of the RostГіvsвҒ вҖ”the mother and the youngest daughterвҒ вҖ”both named NatГЎlya. Ever since the morning, carriages with six horses had been coming and going continually, bringing visitors to the Countess RostГіvaвҖҷs big house on the PovarskГЎya, so well known to all Moscow. The countess herself and her handsome eldest daughter were in the drawing room with the visitors who came to congratulate, and who constantly succeeded one another in relays. The countess was a woman of about forty-five, with a thin Oriental type of face, evidently worn out with childbearingвҒ вҖ”she had had twelve. A languor of motion and speech, resulting from weakness, gave her a distinguished air which inspired respect. Princess Anna MikhГЎylovna DrubetskГЎya, who as a member of the household was also seated in the drawing room, helped to receive and entertain the visitors. The young people were in one of the inner rooms, not considering it necessary to take part in receiving the visitors. The count met the guests and saw them off, inviting them all to dinner. вҖңI am very, very grateful to you, mon cher,вҖқ or вҖңma chГЁreвҖқвҒ вҖ”he called everyone without exception and without the slightest variation in his tone, вҖңmy dear,вҖқ whether they were above or below him in rankвҒ вҖ”вҖңI thank you for myself and for our two dear ones whose name day we are keeping. But mind you come to dinner or I shall be offended, ma chГЁre! On behalf of the whole family I beg you to come, mon cher!вҖқ These words he repeated to everyone without exception or variation, and with the same expression on his full, cheerful, clean-shaven face, the same firm pressure of the hand and the same quick, repeated bows. As soon as he had seen a visitor off he returned to one of those who were still in the drawing room, drew a chair toward him or her, and jauntily spreading out his legs and putting his hands on his knees with the air of a man who enjoys life and knows how to live, he swayed to and fro with dignity, offered surmises about the weather, or touched on questions of health, sometimes in Russian and sometimes in very bad but self-confident French; then again, like a man weary but unflinching in the fulfillment of duty, he rose to see some visitors off and, stroking his scanty gray hairs over his bald patch, also asked them to dinner. Sometimes on his way back from the anteroom he would pass through the conservatory and pantry into the large marble dining hall, where tables were being set out for eighty people; and looking at the footmen, who were bringing in silver and china, moving tables, and unfolding damask table linen, he would call DmГӯtri VasГӯlevich, a man of good family and the manager of all his affairs, and while looking with pleasure at the enormous table would say: вҖңWell, MГӯtenka, youвҖҷll see that things are all as they should be? ThatвҖҷs right! The great thing is the serving, thatвҖҷs it.вҖқ And with a complacent sigh he would return to the drawing room. вҖңMГЎrya LvГіvna KarГЎgina and her daughter!вҖқ announced the countessвҖҷ gigantic footman in his bass voice, entering the drawing room. The countess reflected a moment and took a pinch from a gold snuffbox with her husbandвҖҷs portrait on it. вҖңIвҖҷm quite worn out by these callers. However, IвҖҷll see her and no more. She is so affected. Ask her in,вҖқ she said to the footman in a sad voice, as if saying: вҖңVery well, finish me off.вҖқ A tall, stout, and proud-looking woman, with a round-faced smiling daughter, entered the drawing room, their dresses rustling. вҖңDear Countess, what an ageвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She has been laid up, poor childвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ at the RazoumowskyвҖҷs ballвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and Countess AprГЎksinaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I was so delightedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ came the sounds of animated feminine voices, interrupting one another and mingling with the rustling of dresses and the scraping of chairs. Then one of those conversations began which last out until, at the first pause, the guests rise with a rustle of dresses and say, вҖңI am so delightedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ MammaвҖҷs healthвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and Countess AprГЎksinaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and then, again rustling, pass into the anteroom, put on cloaks or mantles, and drive away. The conversation was on the chief topic of the day: the illness of the wealthy and celebrated beau of CatherineвҖҷs day, Count BezГәkhov, and about his illegitimate son Pierre, the one who had behaved so improperly at Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs reception. вҖңI am so sorry for the poor count,вҖқ said the visitor. вҖңHe is in such bad health, and now this vexation about his son is enough to kill him!вҖқ вҖңWhat is that?вҖқ asked the countess as if she did not know what the visitor alluded to, though she had already heard about the cause of Count BezГәkhovвҖҷs distress some fifteen times. вҖңThatвҖҷs what comes of a modern education,вҖқ exclaimed the visitor. вҖңIt seems that while he was abroad this young man was allowed to do as he liked, now in Petersburg I hear he has been doing such terrible things that he has been expelled by the police.вҖқ вҖңYou donвҖҷt say so!вҖқ replied the countess. вҖңHe chose his friends badly,вҖқ interposed Anna MikhГЎylovna. вҖңPrince VasГӯliвҖҷs son, he, and a certain DГіlokhov have, it is said, been up to heaven only knows what! And they have had to suffer for it. DГіlokhov has been degraded to the ranks and BezГәkhovвҖҷs son sent back to Moscow. Anatole KurГЎginвҖҷs father managed somehow to get his sonвҖҷs affair hushed up, but even he was ordered out of Petersburg.вҖқ вҖңBut what have they been up to?вҖқ asked the countess. вҖңThey are regular brigands, especially DГіlokhov,вҖқ replied the visitor. вҖңHe is a son of MГЎrya IvГЎnovna DГіlokhova, such a worthy woman, but there, just fancy! Those three got hold of a bear somewhere, put it in a carriage, and set off with it to visit some actresses! The police tried to interfere, and what did the young men do? They tied a policeman and the bear back to back and put the bear into the Moyka Canal. And there was the bear swimming about with the policeman on his back!вҖқ вҖңWhat a nice figure the policeman must have cut, my dear!вҖқ shouted the count, dying with laughter. вҖңOh, how dreadful! How can you laugh at it, Count?вҖқ Yet the ladies themselves could not help laughing. вҖңIt was all they could do to rescue the poor man,вҖқ continued the visitor. вҖңAnd to think it is KirГӯl VladГӯmirovich BezГәkhovвҖҷs son who amuses himself in this sensible manner! And he was said to be so well educated and clever. This is all that his foreign education has done for him! I hope that here in Moscow no one will receive him, in spite of his money. They wanted to introduce him to me, but I quite declined: I have my daughters to consider.вҖқ вҖңWhy do you say this young man is so rich?вҖқ asked the countess, turning away from the girls, who at once assumed an air of inattention. вҖңHis children are all illegitimate. I think Pierre also is illegitimate.вҖқ The visitor made a gesture with her hand. вҖңI should think he has a score of them.вҖқ Princess Anna MikhГЎylovna intervened in the conversation, evidently wishing to show her connections and knowledge of what went on in society. вҖңThe fact of the matter is,вҖқ said she significantly, and also in a half whisper, вҖңeveryone knows Count KirГӯl VladГӯmirovichвҖҷs reputation.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ He has lost count of his children, but this Pierre was his favorite.вҖқ вҖңHow handsome the old man still was only a year ago!вҖқ remarked the countess. вҖңI have never seen a handsomer man.вҖқ вҖңHe is very much altered now,вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna. вҖңWell, as I was saying, Prince VasГӯli is the next heir through his wife, but the count is very fond of Pierre, looked after his education, and wrote to the Emperor about him; so that in the case of his deathвҒ вҖ”and he is so ill that he may die at any moment, and Dr.В Lorrain has come from PetersburgвҒ вҖ”no one knows who will inherit his immense fortune, Pierre or Prince VasГӯli. Forty thousand serfs and millions of rubles! I know it all very well for Prince VasГӯli told me himself. Besides, KirГӯl VladГӯmirovich is my motherвҖҷs second cousin. HeвҖҷs also BorГӯsвҖҷs godfather,вҖқ she added, as if she attached no importance at all to the fact. вҖңPrince VasГӯli arrived in Moscow yesterday. I hear he has come on some inspection business,вҖқ remarked the visitor. вҖңYes, but between ourselves,вҖқ said the princess, вҖңthat is a pretext. The fact is he has come to see Count KirГӯl VladГӯmirovich, hearing how ill he is.вҖқ вҖңBut do you know, my dear, that was a capital joke,вҖқ said the count; and seeing that the elder visitor was not listening, he turned to the young ladies. вҖңI can just imagine what a funny figure that policeman cut!вҖқ And as he waved his arms to impersonate the policeman, his portly form again shook with a deep ringing laugh, the laugh of one who always eats well and, in particular, drinks well. вҖңSo do come and dine with us!вҖқ he said. XI XI Silence ensued. The countess looked at her callers, smiling affably, but not concealing the fact that she would not be distressed if they now rose and took their leave. The visitorвҖҷs daughter was already smoothing down her dress with an inquiring look at her mother, when suddenly from the next room were heard the footsteps of boys and girls running to the door and the noise of a chair falling over, and a girl of thirteen, hiding something in the folds of her short muslin frock, darted in and stopped short in the middle of the room. It was evident that she had not intended her flight to bring her so far. Behind her in the doorway appeared a student with a crimson coat collar, an officer of the Guards, a girl of fifteen, and a plump rosy-faced boy in a short jacket. The count jumped up and, swaying from side to side, spread his arms wide and threw them round the little girl who had run in. вҖңAh, here she is!вҖқ he exclaimed laughing. вҖңMy pet, whose name day it is. My dear pet!вҖқ вҖңMa chГЁre, there is a time for everything,вҖқ said the countess with feigned severity. вҖңYou spoil her, IlyГЎ,вҖқ she added, turning to her husband. вҖңHow do you do, my dear? I wish you many happy returns of your name day,вҖқ said the visitor. вҖңWhat a charming child,вҖқ she added, addressing the mother. This black-eyed, wide-mouthed girl, not pretty but full of lifeвҒ вҖ”with childish bare shoulders which after her run heaved and shook her bodice, with black curls tossed backward, thin bare arms, little legs in lace-frilled drawers, and feet in low slippersвҒ вҖ”was just at that charming age when a girl is no longer a child, though the child is not yet a young woman. Escaping from her father she ran to hide her flushed face in the lace of her motherвҖҷs mantillaвҒ вҖ”not paying the least attention to her severe remarkвҒ вҖ”and began to laugh. She laughed, and in fragmentary sentences tried to explain about a doll which she produced from the folds of her frock. вҖңDo you see?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ My dollвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ MimiвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You seeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ was all NatГЎsha managed to utter (to her everything seemed funny). She leaned against her mother and burst into such a loud, ringing fit of laughter that even the prim visitor could not help joining in. вҖңNow then, go away and take your monstrosity with you,вҖқ said the mother, pushing away her daughter with pretended sternness, and turning to the visitor she added: вҖңShe is my youngest girl.вҖқ NatГЎsha, raising her face for a moment from her motherвҖҷs mantilla, glanced up at her through tears of laughter, and again hid her face. The visitor, compelled to look on at this family scene, thought it necessary to take some part in it. вҖңTell me, my dear,вҖқ said she to NatГЎsha, вҖңis Mimi a relation of yours? A daughter, I suppose?вҖқ NatГЎsha did not like the visitorвҖҷs tone of condescension to childish things. She did not reply, but looked at her seriously. Meanwhile the younger generation: BorГӯs, the officer, Anna MikhГЎylovnaвҖҷs son; NikolГЎy, the undergraduate, the countвҖҷs eldest son; SГіnya, the countвҖҷs fifteen-year-old niece, and little PГ©trusha, his youngest boy, had all settled down in the drawing room and were obviously trying to restrain within the bounds of decorum the excitement and mirth that shone in all their faces. Evidently in the back rooms, from which they had dashed out so impetuously, the conversation had been more amusing than the drawing room talk of society scandals, the weather, and Countess AprГЎksina. Now and then they glanced at one another, hardly able to suppress their laughter. The two young men, the student and the officer, friends from childhood, were of the same age and both handsome fellows, though not alike. BorГӯs was tall and fair, and his calm and handsome face had regular, delicate features. NikolГЎy was short with curly hair and an open expression. Dark hairs were already showing on his upper lip, and his whole face expressed impetuosity and enthusiasm. NikolГЎy blushed when he entered the drawing room. He evidently tried to find something to say, but failed. BorГӯs on the contrary at once found his footing, and related quietly and humorously how he had known that doll Mimi when she was still quite a young lady, before her nose was broken; how she had aged during the five years he had known her, and how her head had cracked right across the skull. Having said this he glanced at NatГЎsha. She turned away from him and glanced at her younger brother, who was screwing up his eyes and shaking with suppressed laughter, and unable to control herself any longer, she jumped up and rushed from the room as fast as her nimble little feet would carry her. BorГӯs did not laugh. вҖңYou were meaning to go out, werenвҖҷt you, Mamma? Do you want the carriage?вҖқ he asked his mother with a smile. вҖңYes, yes, go and tell them to get it ready,вҖқ she answered, returning his smile. BorГӯs quietly left the room and went in search of NatГЎsha. The plump boy ran after them angrily, as if vexed that their program had been disturbed. XII XII The only young people remaining in the drawing room, not counting the young lady visitor and the countessвҖҷ eldest daughter (who was four years older than her sister and behaved already like a grown-up person), were NikolГЎy and SГіnya, the niece. SГіnya was a slender little brunette with a tender look in her eyes which were veiled by long lashes, thick black plaits coiling twice round her head, and a tawny tint in her complexion and especially in the color of her slender but graceful and muscular arms and neck. By the grace of her movements, by the softness and flexibility of her small limbs, and by a certain coyness and reserve of manner, she reminded one of a pretty, half-grown kitten which promises to become a beautiful little cat. She evidently considered it proper to show an interest in the general conversation by smiling, but in spite of herself her eyes under their thick long lashes watched her cousin who was going to join the army, with such passionate girlish adoration that her smile could not for a single instant impose upon anyone, and it was clear that the kitten had settled down only to spring up with more energy and again play with her cousin as soon as they too could, like NatГЎsha and BorГӯs, escape from the drawing room. вҖңAh yes, my dear,вҖқ said the count, addressing the visitor and pointing to NikolГЎy, вҖңhis friend BorГӯs has become an officer, and so for friendshipвҖҷs sake he is leaving the university and me, his old father, and entering the military service, my dear. And there was a place and everything waiting for him in the Archives Department! IsnвҖҷt that friendship?вҖқ remarked the count in an inquiring tone. вҖңBut they say that war has been declared,вҖқ replied the visitor. вҖңTheyвҖҷve been saying so a long while,вҖқ said the count, вҖңand theyвҖҷll say so again and again, and that will be the end of it. My dear, thereвҖҷs friendship for you,вҖқ he repeated. вҖңHeвҖҷs joining the hussars.вҖқ The visitor, not knowing what to say, shook her head. вҖңItвҖҷs not at all from friendship,вҖқ declared NikolГЎy, flaring up and turning away as if from a shameful aspersion. вҖңIt is not from friendship at all; I simply feel that the army is my vocation.вҖқ He glanced at his cousin and the young lady visitor; and they were both regarding him with a smile of approbation. вҖңSchubert, the colonel of the PГЎvlograd Hussars, is dining with us today. He has been here on leave and is taking NikolГЎy back with him. It canвҖҷt be helped!вҖқ said the count, shrugging his shoulders and speaking playfully of a matter that evidently distressed him. вҖңI have already told you, Papa,вҖқ said his son, вҖңthat if you donвҖҷt wish to let me go, IвҖҷll stay. But I know I am no use anywhere except in the army; I am not a diplomat or a government clerk.вҒ вҖ”I donвҖҷt know how to hide what I feel.вҖқ As he spoke he kept glancing with the flirtatiousness of a handsome youth at SГіnya and the young lady visitor. The little kitten, feasting her eyes on him, seemed ready at any moment to start her gambols again and display her kittenish nature. вҖңAll right, all right!вҖқ said the old count. вҖңHe always flares up! This Bonaparte has turned all their heads; they all think of how he rose from an ensign and became Emperor. Well, well, God grant it,вҖқ he added, not noticing his visitorвҖҷs sarcastic smile. The elders began talking about Bonaparte. Julie KarГЎgina turned to young RostГіv. вҖңWhat a pity you werenвҖҷt at the ArkhГЎrovsвҖҷ on Thursday. It was so dull without you,вҖқ said she, giving him a tender smile. The young man, flattered, sat down nearer to her with a coquettish smile, and engaged the smiling Julie in a confidential conversation without at all noticing that his involuntary smile had stabbed the heart of SГіnya, who blushed and smiled unnaturally. In the midst of his talk he glanced round at her. She gave him a passionately angry glance, and hardly able to restrain her tears and maintain the artificial smile on her lips, she got up and left the room. All NikolГЎyвҖҷs animation vanished. He waited for the first pause in the conversation, and then with a distressed face left the room to find SГіnya. вҖңHow plainly all these young people wear their hearts on their sleeves!вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna, pointing to NikolГЎy as he went out. вҖңCousinageвҒ вҖ”dangereux voisinage,вҖқ3 she added. вҖңYes,вҖқ said the countess when the brightness these young people had brought into the room had vanished; and as if answering a question no one had put but which was always in her mind, вҖңand how much suffering, how much anxiety one has had to go through that we might rejoice in them now! And yet really the anxiety is greater now than the joy. One is always, always anxious! Especially just at this age, so dangerous both for girls and boys.вҖқ вҖңIt all depends on the bringing up,вҖқ remarked the visitor. вҖңYes, youвҖҷre quite right,вҖқ continued the countess. вҖңTill now I have always, thank God, been my childrenвҖҷs friend and had their full confidence,вҖқ said she, repeating the mistake of so many parents who imagine that their children have no secrets from them. вҖңI know I shall always be my daughtersвҖҷ first confidante, and that if NikГіlenka, with his impulsive nature, does get into mischief (a boy canвҖҷt help it), he will all the same never be like those Petersburg young men.вҖқ вҖңYes, they are splendid, splendid youngsters,вҖқ chimed in the count, who always solved questions that seemed to him perplexing by deciding that everything was splendid. вҖңJust fancy: wants to be an hussar. WhatвҖҷs one to do, my dear?вҖқ вҖңWhat a charming creature your younger girl is,вҖқ said the visitor; вҖңa little volcano!вҖқ вҖңYes, a regular volcano,вҖқ said the count. вҖңTakes after me! And what a voice she has; though sheвҖҷs my daughter, I tell the truth when I say sheвҖҷll be a singer, a second Salomoni! We have engaged an Italian to give her lessons.вҖқ вҖңIsnвҖҷt she too young? I have heard that it harms the voice to train it at that age.вҖқ вҖңOh no, not at all too young!вҖқ replied the count. вҖңWhy, our mothers used to be married at twelve or thirteen.вҖқ вҖңAnd sheвҖҷs in love with BorГӯs already. Just fancy!вҖқ said the countess with a gentle smile, looking at BorГӯs and went on, evidently concerned with a thought that always occupied her: вҖңNow you see if I were to be severe with her and to forbid itвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ goodness knows what they might be up to on the slyвҖқ (she meant that they would be kissing), вҖңbut as it is, I know every word she utters. She will come running to me of her own accord in the evening and tell me everything. Perhaps I spoil her, but really that seems the best plan. With her elder sister I was stricter.вҖқ вҖңYes, I was brought up quite differently,вҖқ remarked the handsome elder daughter, Countess VГ©ra, with a smile. But the smile did not enhance VГ©raвҖҷs beauty as smiles generally do; on the contrary it gave her an unnatural, and therefore unpleasant, expression. VГ©ra was good-looking, not at all stupid, quick at learning, was well brought up, and had a pleasant voice; what she said was true and appropriate, yet, strange to say, everyoneвҒ вҖ”the visitors and countess alikeвҒ вҖ”turned to look at her as if wondering why she had said it, and they all felt awkward. вҖңPeople are always too clever with their eldest children and try to make something exceptional of them,вҖқ said the visitor. вҖңWhatвҖҷs the good of denying it, my dear? Our dear countess was too clever with VГ©ra,вҖқ said the count. вҖңWell, what of that? SheвҖҷs turned out splendidly all the same,вҖқ he added, winking at VГ©ra. The guests got up and took their leave, promising to return to dinner. вҖңWhat manners! I thought they would never go,вҖқ said the countess, when she had seen her guests out. XIII XIII When NatГЎsha ran out of the drawing room she only went as far as the conservatory. There she paused and stood listening to the conversation in the drawing room, waiting for BorГӯs to come out. She was already growing impatient, and stamped her foot, ready to cry at his not coming at once, when she heard the young manвҖҷs discreet steps approaching neither quickly nor slowly. At this NatГЎsha dashed swiftly among the flower tubs and hid there. BorГӯs paused in the middle of the room, looked round, brushed a little dust from the sleeve of his uniform, and going up to a mirror examined his handsome face. NatГЎsha, very still, peered out from her ambush, waiting to see what he would do. He stood a little while before the glass, smiled, and walked toward the other door. NatГЎsha was about to call him but changed her mind. вҖңLet him look for me,вҖқ thought she. Hardly had BorГӯs gone than SГіnya, flushed, in tears, and muttering angrily, came in at the other door. NatГЎsha checked her first impulse to run out to her, and remained in her hiding place, watchingвҒ вҖ”as under an invisible capвҒ вҖ”to see what went on in the world. She was experiencing a new and peculiar pleasure. SГіnya, muttering to herself, kept looking round toward the drawing room door. It opened and NikolГЎy came in. вҖңSГіnya, what is the matter with you? How can you?вҖқ said he, running up to her. вҖңItвҖҷs nothing, nothing; leave me alone!вҖқ sobbed SГіnya. вҖңAh, I know what it is.вҖқ вҖңWell, if you do, so much the better, and you can go back to her!вҖқ вҖңSГі-o-onya! Look here! How can you torture me and yourself like that, for a mere fancy?вҖқ said NikolГЎy taking her hand. SГіnya did not pull it away, and left off crying. NatГЎsha, not stirring and scarcely breathing, watched from her ambush with sparkling eyes. вҖңWhat will happen now?вҖқ thought she. вҖңSГіnya! What is anyone in the world to me? You alone are everything!вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңAnd I will prove it to you.вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt like you to talk like that.вҖқ вҖңWell, then, I wonвҖҷt; only forgive me, SГіnya!вҖқ He drew her to him and kissed her. вҖңOh, how nice,вҖқ thought NatГЎsha; and when SГіnya and NikolГЎy had gone out of the conservatory she followed and called BorГӯs to her. вҖңBorГӯs, come here,вҖқ said she with a sly and significant look. вҖңI have something to tell you. Here, here!вҖқ and she led him into the conservatory to the place among the tubs where she had been hiding. BorГӯs followed her, smiling. вҖңWhat is the something?вҖқ asked he. She grew confused, glanced round, and, seeing the doll she had thrown down on one of the tubs, picked it up. вҖңKiss the doll,вҖқ said she. BorГӯs looked attentively and kindly at her eager face, but did not reply. вҖңDonвҖҷt you want to? Well, then, come here,вҖқ said she, and went further in among the plants and threw down the doll. вҖңCloser, closer!вҖқ she whispered. She caught the young officer by his cuffs, and a look of solemnity and fear appeared on her flushed face. вҖңAnd me? Would you like to kiss me?вҖқ she whispered almost inaudibly, glancing up at him from under her brows, smiling, and almost crying from excitement. BorГӯs blushed. вҖңHow funny you are!вҖқ he said, bending down to her and blushing still more, but he waited and did nothing. Suddenly she jumped up onto a tub to be higher than he, embraced him so that both her slender bare arms clasped him above his neck, and, tossing back her hair, kissed him full on the lips. Then she slipped down among the flowerpots on the other side of the tubs and stood, hanging her head. вҖңNatГЎsha,вҖқ he said, вҖңyou know that I love you, butвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYou are in love with me?вҖқ NatГЎsha broke in. вҖңYes, I am, but please donвҖҷt let us do like that.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ In another four yearsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ then I will ask for your hand.вҖқ NatГЎsha considered. вҖңThirteen, fourteen, fifteen, sixteen,вҖқ she counted on her slender little fingers. вҖңAll right! Then itвҖҷs settled?вҖқ A smile of joy and satisfaction lit up her eager face. вҖңSettled!вҖқ replied BorГӯs. вҖңForever?вҖқ said the little girl. вҖңTill death itself?вҖқ She took his arm and with a happy face went with him into the adjoining sitting room. XIV XIV After receiving her visitors, the countess was so tired that she gave orders to admit no more, but the porter was told to be sure to invite to dinner all who came вҖңto congratulate.вҖқ The countess wished to have a tГӘte-Г -tГӘte talk with the friend of her childhood, Princess Anna MikhГЎylovna, whom she had not seen properly since she returned from Petersburg. Anna MikhГЎylovna, with her tear-worn but pleasant face, drew her chair nearer to that of the countess. вҖңWith you I will be quite frank,вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna. вҖңThere are not many left of us old friends! ThatвҖҷs why I so value your friendship.вҖқ Anna MikhГЎylovna looked at VГ©ra and paused. The countess pressed her friendвҖҷs hand. вҖңVГ©ra,вҖқ she said to her eldest daughter who was evidently not a favorite, вҖңhow is it you have so little tact? DonвҖҷt you see you are not wanted here? Go to the other girls, orвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The handsome VГ©ra smiled contemptuously but did not seem at all hurt. вҖңIf you had told me sooner, Mamma, I would have gone,вҖқ she replied as she rose to go to her own room. But as she passed the sitting room she noticed two couples sitting, one pair at each window. She stopped and smiled scornfully. SГіnya was sitting close to NikolГЎy who was copying out some verses for her, the first he had ever written. BorГӯs and NatГЎsha were at the other window and ceased talking when VГ©ra entered. SГіnya and NatГЎsha looked at VГ©ra with guilty, happy faces. It was pleasant and touching to see these little girls in love; but apparently the sight of them roused no pleasant feeling in VГ©ra. вҖңHow often have I asked you not to take my things?вҖқ she said. вҖңYou have a room of your own,вҖқ and she took the inkstand from NikolГЎy. вҖңIn a minute, in a minute,вҖқ he said, dipping his pen. вҖңYou always manage to do things at the wrong time,вҖқ continued VГ©ra. вҖңYou came rushing into the drawing room so that everyone felt ashamed of you.вҖқ Though what she said was quite just, perhaps for that very reason no one replied, and the four simply looked at one another. She lingered in the room with the inkstand in her hand. вҖңAnd at your age what secrets can there be between NatГЎsha and BorГӯs, or between you two? ItвҖҷs all nonsense!вҖқ вҖңNow, VГ©ra, what does it matter to you?вҖқ said NatГЎsha in defense, speaking very gently. She seemed that day to be more than ever kind and affectionate to everyone. вҖңVery silly,вҖқ said VГ©ra. вҖңI am ashamed of you. Secrets indeed!вҖқ вҖңAll have secrets of their own,вҖқ answered NatГЎsha, getting warmer. вҖңWe donвҖҷt interfere with you and Berg.вҖқ вҖңI should think not,вҖқ said VГ©ra, вҖңbecause there can never be anything wrong in my behavior. But IвҖҷll just tell Mamma how you are behaving with BorГӯs.вҖқ вҖңNatГЎlya IlynГӯchna behaves very well to me,вҖқ remarked BorГӯs. вҖңI have nothing to complain of.вҖқ вҖңDonвҖҷt, BorГӯs! You are such a diplomat that it is really tiresome,вҖқ said NatГЎsha in a mortified voice that trembled slightly. (She used the word вҖңdiplomat,вҖқ which was just then much in vogue among the children, in the special sense they attached to it.) вҖңWhy does she bother me?вҖқ And she added, turning to VГ©ra, вҖңYouвҖҷll never understand it, because youвҖҷve never loved anyone. You have no heart! You are a Madame de Genlis and nothing moreвҖқ (this nickname, bestowed on VГ©ra by NikolГЎy, was considered very stinging), вҖңand your greatest pleasure is to be unpleasant to people! Go and flirt with Berg as much as you please,вҖқ she finished quickly. вҖңI shall at any rate not run after a young man before visitorsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWell, now youвҖҷve done what you wanted,вҖқ put in NikolГЎyвҒ вҖ”вҖңsaid unpleasant things to everyone and upset them. LetвҖҷs go to the nursery.вҖқ All four, like a flock of scared birds, got up and left the room. вҖңThe unpleasant things were said to me,вҖқ remarked VГ©ra, вҖңI said none to anyone.вҖқ вҖңMadame de Genlis! Madame de Genlis!вҖқ shouted laughing voices through the door. The handsome VГ©ra, who produced such an irritating and unpleasant effect on everyone, smiled and, evidently unmoved by what had been said to her, went to the looking glass and arranged her hair and scarf. Looking at her own handsome face she seemed to become still colder and calmer. In the drawing room the conversation was still going on. вҖңAh, my dear,вҖқ said the countess, вҖңmy life is not all roses either. DonвҖҷt I know that at the rate we are living our means wonвҖҷt last long? ItвҖҷs all the Club and his easygoing nature. Even in the country do we get any rest? Theatricals, hunting, and heaven knows what besides! But donвҖҷt letвҖҷs talk about me; tell me how you managed everything. I often wonder at you, AnnetteвҒ вҖ”how at your age you can rush off alone in a carriage to Moscow, to Petersburg, to those ministers and great people, and know how to deal with them all! ItвҖҷs quite astonishing. How did you get things settled? I couldnвҖҷt possibly do it.вҖқ вҖңAh, my love,вҖқ answered Anna MikhГЎylovna, вҖңGod grant you never know what it is to be left a widow without means and with a son you love to distraction! One learns many things then,вҖқ she added with a certain pride. вҖңThat lawsuit taught me much. When I want to see one of those big people I write a note: вҖҳPrincess So-and-So desires an interview with So-and-So,вҖҷ and then I take a cab and go myself two, three, or four timesвҒ вҖ”till I get what I want. I donвҖҷt mind what they think of me.вҖқ вҖңWell, and to whom did you apply about BorГ©nka?вҖқ asked the countess. вҖңYou see yours is already an officer in the Guards, while my NikolГәshka is going as a cadet. ThereвҖҷs no one to interest himself for him. To whom did you apply?вҖқ вҖңTo Prince VasГӯli. He was so kind. He at once agreed to everything, and put the matter before the Emperor,вҖқ said Princess Anna MikhГЎylovna enthusiastically, quite forgetting all the humiliation she had endured to gain her end. вҖңHas Prince VasГӯli aged much?вҖқ asked the countess. вҖңI have not seen him since we acted together at the RumyГЎntsovsвҖҷ theatricals. I expect he has forgotten me. He paid me attentions in those days,вҖқ said the countess, with a smile. вҖңHe is just the same as ever,вҖқ replied Anna MikhГЎylovna, вҖңoverflowing with amiability. His position has not turned his head at all. He said to me, вҖҳI am sorry I can do so little for you, dear Princess. I am at your command.вҖҷ Yes, he is a fine fellow and a very kind relation. But, Nathalie, you know my love for my son: I would do anything for his happiness! And my affairs are in such a bad way that my position is now a terrible one,вҖқ continued Anna MikhГЎylovna, sadly, dropping her voice. вҖңMy wretched lawsuit takes all I have and makes no progress. Would you believe it, I have literally not a penny and donвҖҷt know how to equip BorГӯs.вҖқ She took out her handkerchief and began to cry. вҖңI need five hundred rubles, and have only one twenty-five-ruble note. I am in such a state.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ My only hope now is in Count KirГӯl VladГӯmirovich BezГәkhov. If he will not assist his godsonвҒ вҖ”you know he is BГіrisвҖҷs godfatherвҒ вҖ”and allow him something for his maintenance, all my trouble will have been thrown away.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I shall not be able to equip him.вҖқ The countessвҖҷ eyes filled with tears and she pondered in silence. вҖңI often think, though, perhaps itвҖҷs a sin,вҖқ said the princess, вҖңthat here lives Count KirГӯl VladГӯmirovich BezГәkhov so rich, all aloneвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ that tremendous fortuneвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and what is his life worth? ItвҖҷs a burden to him, and BorГӯsвҖҷs life is only just beginning.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңSurely he will leave something to BorГӯs,вҖқ said the countess. вҖңHeaven only knows, my dear! These rich grandees are so selfish. Still, I will take BorГӯs and go to see him at once, and I shall speak to him straight out. Let people think what they will of me, itвҖҷs really all the same to me when my sonвҖҷs fate is at stake.вҖқ The princess rose. вҖңItвҖҷs now two oвҖҷclock and you dine at four. There will just be time.вҖқ And like a practical Petersburg lady who knows how to make the most of time, Anna MikhГЎylovna sent someone to call her son, and went into the anteroom with him. вҖңGoodbye, my dear,вҖқ said she to the countess who saw her to the door, and added in a whisper so that her son should not hear, вҖңWish me good luck.вҖқ вҖңAre you going to Count KirГӯl VladГӯmirovich, my dear?вҖқ said the count coming out from the dining hall into the anteroom, and he added: вҖңIf he is better, ask Pierre to dine with us. He has been to the house, you know, and danced with the children. Be sure to invite him, my dear. We will see how TarГЎs distinguishes himself today. He says Count OrlГіv never gave such a dinner as ours will be!вҖқ XV XV вҖңMy dear Boris,вҖқ said Princess Anna MikhГЎylovna to her son as Countess RostГіvaвҖҷs carriage in which they were seated drove over the straw covered street and turned into the wide courtyard of Count KirГӯl VladГӯmirovich BezГәkhovвҖҷs house. вҖңMy dear BorГӯs,вҖқ said the mother, drawing her hand from beneath her old mantle and laying it timidly and tenderly on her sonвҖҷs arm, вҖңbe affectionate and attentive to him. Count KirГӯl VladГӯmirovich is your godfather after all, and your future depends on him. Remember that, my dear, and be nice to him, as you so well know how to be.вҖқ вҖңIf only I knew that anything besides humiliation would come of itвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ answered her son coldly. вҖңBut I have promised and will do it for your sake.вҖқ Although the hall porter saw someoneвҖҷs carriage standing at the entrance, after scrutinizing the mother and son (who without asking to be announced had passed straight through the glass porch between the rows of statues in niches) and looking significantly at the ladyвҖҷs old cloak, he asked whether they wanted the count or the princesses, and, hearing that they wished to see the count, said his excellency was worse today, and that his excellency was not receiving anyone. вҖңWe may as well go back,вҖқ said the son in French. вҖңMy dear!вҖқ exclaimed his mother imploringly, again laying her hand on his arm as if that touch might soothe or rouse him. BorГӯs said no more, but looked inquiringly at his mother without taking off his cloak. вҖңMy friend,вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna in gentle tones, addressing the hall porter, вҖңI know Count KirГӯl VladГӯmirovich is very illвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ thatвҖҷs why I have comeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I am a relation. I shall not disturb him, my friendвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I only need see Prince VasГӯli SergГ©evich: he is staying here, is he not? Please announce me.вҖқ The hall porter sullenly pulled a bell that rang upstairs, and turned away. вҖңPrincess DrubetskГЎya to see Prince VasГӯli SergГ©evich,вҖқ he called to a footman dressed in knee breeches, shoes, and a swallowtail coat, who ran downstairs and looked over from the halfway landing. The mother smoothed the folds of her dyed silk dress before a large Venetian mirror in the wall, and in her trodden-down shoes briskly ascended the carpeted stairs. вҖңMy dear,вҖқ she said to her son, once more stimulating him by a touch, вҖңyou promised me!вҖқ The son, lowering his eyes, followed her quietly. They entered the large hall, from which one of the doors led to the apartments assigned to Prince VasГӯli. Just as the mother and son, having reached the middle of the hall, were about to ask their way of an elderly footman who had sprung up as they entered, the bronze handle of one of the doors turned and Prince VasГӯli came outвҒ вҖ”wearing a velvet coat with a single star on his breast, as was his custom when at homeвҒ вҖ”taking leave of a good-looking, dark-haired man. This was the celebrated Petersburg doctor, Lorrain. вҖңThen it is certain?вҖқ said the prince. вҖңPrince, humanum est errare,4 butвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ replied the doctor, swallowing his rвҖҷs, and pronouncing the Latin words with a French accent. вҖңVery well, very wellвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Seeing Anna MikhГЎylovna and her son, Prince VasГӯli dismissed the doctor with a bow and approached them silently and with a look of inquiry. The son noticed that an expression of profound sorrow suddenly clouded his motherвҖҷs face, and he smiled slightly. вҖңAh, Prince! In what sad circumstances we meet again! And how is our dear invalid?вҖқ said she, as though unaware of the cold offensive look fixed on her. Prince VasГӯli stared at her and at BorГӯs questioningly and perplexed. BorГӯs bowed politely. Prince VasГӯli without acknowledging the bow turned to Anna MikhГЎylovna, answering her query by a movement of the head and lips indicating very little hope for the patient. вҖңIs it possible?вҖқ exclaimed Anna MikhГЎylovna. вҖңOh, how awful! It is terrible to think.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ This is my son,вҖқ she added, indicating BorГӯs. вҖңHe wanted to thank you himself.вҖқ BorГӯs bowed again politely. вҖңBelieve me, Prince, a motherвҖҷs heart will never forget what you have done for us.вҖқ вҖңI am glad I was able to do you a service, my dear Anna MikhГЎylovna,вҖқ said Prince VasГӯli, arranging his lace frill, and in tone and manner, here in Moscow to Anna MikhГЎylovna whom he had placed under an obligation, assuming an air of much greater importance than he had done in Petersburg at Anna SchГ©rerвҖҷs reception. вҖңTry to serve well and show yourself worthy,вҖқ added he, addressing BorГӯs with severity. вҖңI am glad.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Are you here on leave?вҖқ he went on in his usual tone of indifference. вҖңI am awaiting orders to join my new regiment, your excellency,вҖқ replied BorГӯs, betraying neither annoyance at the princeвҖҷs brusque manner nor a desire to enter into conversation, but speaking so quietly and respectfully that the prince gave him a searching glance. вҖңAre you living with your mother?вҖқ вҖңI am living at Countess RostГіvaвҖҷs,вҖқ replied BorГӯs, again adding, вҖңyour excellency.вҖқ вҖңThat is, with IlyГЎ RostГіv who married Nathalie ShinshinГЎ,вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna. вҖңI know, I know,вҖқ answered Prince VasГӯli in his monotonous voice. вҖңI never could understand how Nathalie made up her mind to marry that unlicked bear! A perfectly absurd and stupid fellow, and a gambler too, I am told.вҖқ вҖңBut a very kind man, Prince,вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna with a pathetic smile, as though she too knew that Count RostГіv deserved this censure, but asked him not to be too hard on the poor old man. вҖңWhat do the doctors say?вҖқ asked the princess after a pause, her worn face again expressing deep sorrow. вҖңThey give little hope,вҖқ replied the prince. вҖңAnd I should so like to thank Uncle once for all his kindness to me and BorГӯs. He is his godson,вҖқ she added, her tone suggesting that this fact ought to give Prince VasГӯli much satisfaction. Prince VasГӯli became thoughtful and frowned. Anna MikhГЎylovna saw that he was afraid of finding in her a rival for Count BezГәkhovвҖҷs fortune, and hastened to reassure him. вҖңIf it were not for my sincere affection and devotion to Uncle,вҖқ said she, uttering the word with peculiar assurance and unconcern, вҖңI know his character: noble, uprightвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but you see he has no one with him except the young princesses.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ They are still young.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ She bent her head and continued in a whisper: вҖңHas he performed his final duty, Prince? How priceless are those last moments! It can make things no worse, and it is absolutely necessary to prepare him if he is so ill. We women, Prince,вҖқ and she smiled tenderly, вҖңalways know how to say these things. I absolutely must see him, however painful it may be for me. I am used to suffering.вҖқ Evidently the prince understood her, and also understood, as he had done at Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs, that it would be difficult to get rid of Anna MikhГЎylovna. вҖңWould not such a meeting be too trying for him, dear Anna MikhГЎylovna?вҖқ said he. вҖңLet us wait until evening. The doctors are expecting a crisis.вҖқ вҖңBut one cannot delay, Prince, at such a moment! Consider that the welfare of his soul is at stake. Ah, it is awful: the duties of a ChristianвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ A door of one of the inner rooms opened and one of the princesses, the countвҖҷs niece, entered with a cold, stern face. The length of her body was strikingly out of proportion to her short legs. Prince VasГӯli turned to her. вҖңWell, how is he?вҖқ вҖңStill the same; but what can you expect, this noiseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said the princess, looking at Anna MikhГЎylovna as at a stranger. вҖңAh, my dear, I hardly knew you,вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna with a happy smile, ambling lightly up to the countвҖҷs niece. вҖңI have come, and am at your service to help you nurse my uncle. I imagine what you have gone through,вҖқ and she sympathetically turned up her eyes. The princess gave no reply and did not even smile, but left the room as Anna MikhГЎylovna took off her gloves and, occupying the position she had conquered, settled down in an armchair, inviting Prince VasГӯli to take a seat beside her. вҖңBorГӯs,вҖқ she said to her son with a smile, вҖңI shall go in to see the count, my uncle; but you, my dear, had better go to Pierre meanwhile and donвҖҷt forget to give him the RostГіvsвҖҷ invitation. They ask him to dinner. I suppose he wonвҖҷt go?вҖқ she continued, turning to the prince. вҖңOn the contrary,вҖқ replied the prince, who had plainly become depressed, вҖңI shall be only too glad if you relieve me of that young man.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Here he is, and the count has not once asked for him.вҖқ He shrugged his shoulders. A footman conducted BorГӯs down one flight of stairs and up another, to Pyotr KirГӯlovichвҖҷs rooms. XVI XVI Pierre, after all, had not managed to choose a career for himself in Petersburg, and had been expelled from there for riotous conduct and sent to Moscow. The story told about him at Count RostГіvвҖҷs was true. Pierre had taken part in tying a policeman to a bear. He had now been for some days in Moscow and was staying as usual at his fatherвҖҷs house. Though he expected that the story of his escapade would be already known in Moscow and that the ladies about his fatherвҒ вҖ”who were never favorably disposed toward himвҒ вҖ”would have used it to turn the count against him, he nevertheless on the day of his arrival went to his fatherвҖҷs part of the house. Entering the drawing room, where the princesses spent most of their time, he greeted the ladies, two of whom were sitting at embroidery frames while a third read aloud. It was the eldest who was readingвҒ вҖ”the one who had met Anna MikhГЎylovna. The two younger ones were embroidering: both were rosy and pretty and they differed only in that one had a little mole on her lip which made her much prettier. Pierre was received as if he were a corpse or a leper. The eldest princess paused in her reading and silently stared at him with frightened eyes; the second assumed precisely the same expression; while the youngest, the one with the mole, who was of a cheerful and lively disposition, bent over her frame to hide a smile probably evoked by the amusing scene she foresaw. She drew her wool down through the canvas and, scarcely able to refrain from laughing, stooped as if trying to make out the pattern. вҖңHow do you do, cousin?вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңYou donвҖҷt recognize me?вҖқ вҖңI recognize you only too well, too well.вҖқ вҖңHow is the count? Can I see him?вҖқ asked Pierre, awkwardly as usual, but unabashed. вҖңThe count is suffering physically and mentally, and apparently you have done your best to increase his mental sufferings.вҖқ вҖңCan I see the count?вҖқ Pierre again asked. вҖңHm.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ If you wish to kill him, to kill him outright, you can see himвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Olga, go and see whether UncleвҖҷs beef tea is readyвҒ вҖ”it is almost time,вҖқ she added, giving Pierre to understand that they were busy, and busy making his father comfortable, while evidently he, Pierre, was only busy causing him annoyance. Olga went out. Pierre stood looking at the sisters; then he bowed and said: вҖңThen I will go to my rooms. You will let me know when I can see him.вҖқ And he left the room, followed by the low but ringing laughter of the sister with the mole. Next day Prince VasГӯli had arrived and settled in the countвҖҷs house. He sent for Pierre and said to him: вҖңMy dear fellow, if you are going to behave here as you did in Petersburg, you will end very badly; that is all I have to say to you. The count is very, very ill, and you must not see him at all.вҖқ Since then Pierre had not been disturbed and had spent the whole time in his rooms upstairs. When BorГӯs appeared at his door Pierre was pacing up and down his room, stopping occasionally at a corner to make menacing gestures at the wall, as if running a sword through an invisible foe, and glaring savagely over his spectacles, and then again resuming his walk, muttering indistinct words, shrugging his shoulders and gesticulating. вҖңEngland is done for,вҖқ said he, scowling and pointing his finger at someone unseen. вҖңMr.В Pitt, as a traitor to the nation and to the rights of man, is sentenced toвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ But before PierreвҒ вҖ”who at that moment imagined himself to be Napoleon in person and to have just effected the dangerous crossing of the Straits of Dover and captured LondonвҒ вҖ”could pronounce PittвҖҷs sentence, he saw a well-built and handsome young officer entering his room. Pierre paused. He had left Moscow when BorГӯs was a boy of fourteen, and had quite forgotten him, but in his usual impulsive and hearty way he took BorГӯs by the hand with a friendly smile. вҖңDo you remember me?вҖқ asked BorГӯs quietly with a pleasant smile. вҖңI have come with my mother to see the count, but it seems he is not well.вҖқ вҖңYes, it seems he is ill. People are always disturbing him,вҖқ answered Pierre, trying to remember who this young man was. BorГӯs felt that Pierre did not recognize him but did not consider it necessary to introduce himself, and without experiencing the least embarrassment looked Pierre straight in the face. вҖңCount RostГіv asks you to come to dinner today,вҖқ said he, after a considerable pause which made Pierre feel uncomfortable. вҖңAh, Count RostГіv!вҖқ exclaimed Pierre joyfully. вҖңThen you are his son, IlyГЎ? Only fancy, I didnвҖҷt know you at first. Do you remember how we went to the Sparrow Hills with Madame Jacquot?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ItвҖҷs such an ageвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYou are mistaken,вҖқ said BorГӯs deliberately, with a bold and slightly sarcastic smile. вҖңI am BorГӯs, son of Princess Anna MikhГЎylovna DrubetskГЎya. RostГіv, the father, is IlyГЎ, and his son is NikolГЎy. I never knew any Madame Jacquot.вҖқ Pierre shook his head and arms as if attacked by mosquitoes or bees. вҖңOh dear, what am I thinking about? IвҖҷve mixed everything up. One has so many relatives in Moscow! So you are BorГӯs? Of course. Well, now we know where we are. And what do you think of the Boulogne expedition? The English will come off badly, you know, if Napoleon gets across the Channel. I think the expedition is quite feasible. If only Villeneuve doesnвҖҷt make a mess of things!вҖқ BorГӯs knew nothing about the Boulogne expedition; he did not read the papers and it was the first time he had heard VilleneuveвҖҷs name. вҖңWe here in Moscow are more occupied with dinner parties and scandal than with politics,вҖқ said he in his quiet ironical tone. вҖңI know nothing about it and have not thought about it. Moscow is chiefly busy with gossip,вҖқ he continued. вҖңJust now they are talking about you and your father.вҖқ Pierre smiled in his good-natured way as if afraid for his companionвҖҷs sake that the latter might say something he would afterwards regret. But BorГӯs spoke distinctly, clearly, and dryly, looking straight into PierreвҖҷs eyes. вҖңMoscow has nothing else to do but gossip,вҖқ BorГӯs went on. вҖңEverybody is wondering to whom the count will leave his fortune, though he may perhaps outlive us all, as I sincerely hope he willвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYes, it is all very horrid,вҖқ interrupted Pierre, вҖңvery horrid.вҖқ Pierre was still afraid that this officer might inadvertently say something disconcerting to himself. вҖңAnd it must seem to you,вҖқ said BorГӯs flushing slightly, but not changing his tone or attitude, вҖңit must seem to you that everyone is trying to get something out of the rich man?вҖқ вҖңSo it does,вҖқ thought Pierre. вҖңBut I just wish to say, to avoid misunderstandings, that you are quite mistaken if you reckon me or my mother among such people. We are very poor, but for my own part at any rate, for the very reason that your father is rich, I donвҖҷt regard myself as a relation of his, and neither I nor my mother would ever ask or take anything from him.вҖқ For a long time Pierre could not understand, but when he did, he jumped up from the sofa, seized BorГӯs under the elbow in his quick, clumsy way, and, blushing far more than BorГӯs, began to speak with a feeling of mingled shame and vexation. вҖңWell, this is strange! Do you suppose IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ who could think?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I know very wellвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ But BorГӯs again interrupted him. вҖңI am glad I have spoken out fully. Perhaps you did not like it? You must excuse me,вҖқ said he, putting Pierre at ease instead of being put at ease by him, вҖңbut I hope I have not offended you. I always make it a rule to speak outвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, what answer am I to take? Will you come to dinner at the RostГіvsвҖҷ?вҖқ And BorГӯs, having apparently relieved himself of an onerous duty and extricated himself from an awkward situation and placed another in it, became quite pleasant again. вҖңNo, but I say,вҖқ said Pierre, calming down, вҖңyou are a wonderful fellow! What you have just said is good, very good. Of course you donвҖҷt know me. We have not met for such a long timeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ not since we were children. You might think that IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I understand, quite understand. I could not have done it myself, I should not have had the courage, but itвҖҷs splendid. I am very glad to have made your acquaintance. ItвҖҷs queer,вҖқ he added after a pause, вҖңthat you should have suspected me!вҖқ He began to laugh. вҖңWell, what of it! I hope weвҖҷll get better acquainted,вҖқ and he pressed BorГӯsвҖҷ hand. вҖңDo you know, I have not once been in to see the count. He has not sent for me.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I am sorry for him as a man, but what can one do?вҖқ вҖңAnd so you think Napoleon will manage to get an army across?вҖқ asked BorГӯs with a smile. Pierre saw that BorГӯs wished to change the subject, and being of the same mind he began explaining the advantages and disadvantages of the Boulogne expedition. A footman came in to summon BorГӯsвҒ вҖ”the princess was going. Pierre, in order to make BorГӯsвҖҷ better acquaintance, promised to come to dinner, and warmly pressing his hand looked affectionately over his spectacles into BorГӯsвҖҷ eyes. After he had gone Pierre continued pacing up and down the room for a long time, no longer piercing an imaginary foe with his imaginary sword, but smiling at the remembrance of that pleasant, intelligent, and resolute young man. As often happens in early youth, especially to one who leads a lonely life, he felt an unaccountable tenderness for this young man and made up his mind that they would be friends. Prince VasГӯli saw the princess off. She held a handkerchief to her eyes and her face was tearful. вҖңIt is dreadful, dreadful!вҖқ she was saying, вҖңbut cost me what it may I shall do my duty. I will come and spend the night. He must not be left like this. Every moment is precious. I canвҖҷt think why his nieces put it off. Perhaps God will help me to find a way to prepare him!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Adieu, Prince! May God support youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAdieu, ma bonne,вҖқ answered Prince VasГӯli turning away from her. вҖңOh, he is in a dreadful state,вҖқ said the mother to her son when they were in the carriage. вҖңHe hardly recognizes anybody.вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt understand, MammaвҒ вҖ”what is his attitude to Pierre?вҖқ asked the son. вҖңThe will will show that, my dear; our fate also depends on it.вҖқ вҖңBut why do you expect that he will leave us anything?вҖқ вҖңAh, my dear! He is so rich, and we are so poor!вҖқ вҖңWell, that is hardly a sufficient reason, MammaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңOh, Heaven! How ill he is!вҖқ exclaimed the mother. XVII XVII After Anna MikhГЎylovna had driven off with her son to visit Count KirГӯl VladГӯmirovich BezГәkhov, Countess RostГіva sat for a long time all alone applying her handkerchief to her eyes. At last she rang. вҖңWhat is the matter with you, my dear?вҖқ she said crossly to the maid who kept her waiting some minutes. вҖңDonвҖҷt you wish to serve me? Then IвҖҷll find you another place.вҖқ The countess was upset by her friendвҖҷs sorrow and humiliating poverty, and was therefore out of sorts, a state of mind which with her always found expression in calling her maid вҖңmy dearвҖқ and speaking to her with exaggerated politeness. вҖңI am very sorry, maвҖҷam,вҖқ answered the maid. вҖңAsk the count to come to me.вҖқ The count came waddling in to see his wife with a rather guilty look as usual. вҖңWell, little countess? What a sautГ© of game au madГЁre we are to have, my dear! I tasted it. The thousand rubles I paid for TarГЎs were not ill-spent. He is worth it!вҖқ He sat down by his wife, his elbows on his knees and his hands ruffling his gray hair. вҖңWhat are your commands, little countess?вҖқ вҖңYou see, my dearвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ WhatвҖҷs that mess?вҖқ she said, pointing to his waistcoat. вҖңItвҖҷs the sautГ©, most likely,вҖқ she added with a smile. вҖңWell, you see, Count, I want some money.вҖқ Her face became sad. вҖңOh, little countess!вҖқвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and the count began bustling to get out his pocketbook. вҖңI want a great deal, Count! I want five hundred rubles,вҖқ and taking out her cambric handkerchief she began wiping her husbandвҖҷs waistcoat. вҖңYes, immediately, immediately! Hey, whoвҖҷs there?вҖқ he called out in a tone only used by persons who are certain that those they call will rush to obey the summons. вҖңSend MГӯtenka to me!вҖқ MГӯtenka, a man of good family who had been brought up in the countвҖҷs house and now managed all his affairs, stepped softly into the room. вҖңThis is what I want, my dear fellow,вҖқ said the count to the deferential young man who had entered. вҖңBring meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he reflected a moment, вҖңyes, bring me seven hundred rubles, yes! But mind, donвҖҷt bring me such tattered and dirty notes as last time, but nice clean ones for the countess.вҖқ вҖңYes, MГӯtenka, clean ones, please,вҖқ said the countess, sighing deeply. вҖңWhen would you like them, your excellency?вҖқ asked MГӯtenka. вҖңAllow me to inform youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But, donвҖҷt be uneasy,вҖқ he added, noticing that the count was beginning to breathe heavily and quickly which was always a sign of approaching anger. вҖңI was forgettingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Do you wish it brought at once?вҖқ вҖңYes, yes; just so! Bring it. Give it to the countess.вҖқ вҖңWhat a treasure that MГӯtenka is,вҖқ added the count with a smile when the young man had departed. вҖңThere is never any вҖҳimpossibleвҖҷ with him. ThatвҖҷs a thing I hate! Everything is possible.вҖқ вҖңAh, money, Count, money! How much sorrow it causes in the world,вҖқ said the countess. вҖңBut I am in great need of this sum.вҖқ вҖңYou, my little countess, are a notorious spendthrift,вҖқ said the count, and having kissed his wifeвҖҷs hand he went back to his study. When Anna MikhГЎylovna returned from Count BezГәkhovвҖҷs the money, all in clean notes, was lying ready under a handkerchief on the countessвҖҷ little table, and Anna MikhГЎylovna noticed that something was agitating her. вҖңWell, my dear?вҖқ asked the countess. вҖңOh, what a terrible state he is in! One would not know him, he is so ill! I was only there a few moments and hardly said a wordвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAnnette, for heavenвҖҷs sake donвҖҷt refuse me,вҖқ the countess began, with a blush that looked very strange on her thin, dignified, elderly face, and she took the money from under the handkerchief. Anna MikhГЎylovna instantly guessed her intention and stooped to be ready to embrace the countess at the appropriate moment. вҖңThis is for BorГӯs from me, for his outfit.вҖқ Anna MikhГЎylovna was already embracing her and weeping. The countess wept too. They wept because they were friends, and because they were kindhearted, and because theyвҒ вҖ”friends from childhoodвҒ вҖ”had to think about such a base thing as money, and because their youth was over.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But those tears were pleasant to them both. XVIII XVIII Countess RostГіva, with her daughters and a large number of guests, was already seated in the drawing room. The count took the gentlemen into his study and showed them his choice collection of Turkish pipes. From time to time he went out to ask: вҖңHasnвҖҷt she come yet?вҖқ They were expecting MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna AkhrosГӯmova, known in society as le terrible dragon, a lady distinguished not for wealth or rank, but for common sense and frank plainness of speech. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna was known to the Imperial family as well as to all Moscow and Petersburg, and both cities wondered at her, laughed privately at her rudenesses, and told good stories about her, while none the less all without exception respected and feared her. In the countвҖҷs room, which was full of tobacco smoke, they talked of the war that had been announced in a manifesto, and about the recruiting. None of them had yet seen the manifesto, but they all knew it had appeared. The count sat on the sofa between two guests who were smoking and talking. He neither smoked nor talked, but bending his head first to one side and then to the other watched the smokers with evident pleasure and listened to the conversation of his two neighbors, whom he egged on against each other. One of them was a sallow, clean-shaven civilian with a thin and wrinkled face, already growing old, though he was dressed like a most fashionable young man. He sat with his legs up on the sofa as if quite at home and, having stuck an amber mouthpiece far into his mouth, was inhaling the smoke spasmodically and screwing up his eyes. This was an old bachelor, ShinshГӯn, a cousin of the countessвҖҷ, a man with вҖңa sharp tongueвҖқ as they said in Moscow society. He seemed to be condescending to his companion. The latter, a fresh, rosy officer of the Guards, irreproachably washed, brushed, and buttoned, held his pipe in the middle of his mouth and with red lips gently inhaled the smoke, letting it escape from his handsome mouth in rings. This was Lieutenant Berg, an officer in the SemГ«nov regiment with whom BorГӯs was to travel to join the army, and about whom NatГЎsha had teased her elder sister VГ©ra, speaking of Berg as her вҖңintended.вҖқ The count sat between them and listened attentively. His favorite occupation when not playing boston, a card game he was very fond of, was that of listener, especially when he succeeded in setting two loquacious talkers at one another. вҖңWell, then, old chap, mon trГЁs honorable Alphonse KГЎrlovich,вҖқ said ShinshГӯn, laughing ironically and mixing the most ordinary Russian expressions with the choicest French phrasesвҒ вҖ”which was a peculiarity of his speech. вҖңVous comptez vous faire des rentes sur lвҖҷГ©tat;5 you want to make something out of your company?вҖқ вҖңNo, Pyotr NikolГЎevich; I only want to show that in the cavalry the advantages are far less than in the infantry. Just consider my own position now, Pyotr NikolГЎevichвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Berg always spoke quietly, politely, and with great precision. His conversation always related entirely to himself; he would remain calm and silent when the talk related to any topic that had no direct bearing on himself. He could remain silent for hours without being at all put out of countenance himself or making others uncomfortable, but as soon as the conversation concerned himself he would begin to talk circumstantially and with evident satisfaction. вҖңConsider my position, Pyotr NikolГЎevich. Were I in the cavalry I should get not more than two hundred rubles every four months, even with the rank of lieutenant; but as it is I receive two hundred and thirty,вҖқ said he, looking at ShinshГӯn and the count with a joyful, pleasant smile, as if it were obvious to him that his success must always be the chief desire of everyone else. вҖңBesides that, Pyotr NikolГЎevich, by exchanging into the Guards I shall be in a more prominent position,вҖқ continued Berg, вҖңand vacancies occur much more frequently in the Foot Guards. Then just think what can be done with two hundred and thirty rubles! I even manage to put a little aside and to send something to my father,вҖқ he went on, emitting a smoke ring. вҖңLa balance y estвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ6 A German knows how to skin a flint, as the proverb says,вҖқ remarked ShinshГӯn, moving his pipe to the other side of his mouth and winking at the count. The count burst out laughing. The other guests seeing that ShinshГӯn was talking came up to listen. Berg, oblivious of irony or indifference, continued to explain how by exchanging into the Guards he had already gained a step on his old comrades of the Cadet Corps; how in wartime the company commander might get killed and he, as senior in the company, might easily succeed to the post; how popular he was with everyone in the regiment, and how satisfied his father was with him. Berg evidently enjoyed narrating all this, and did not seem to suspect that others, too, might have their own interests. But all he said was so prettily sedate, and the naivete of his youthful egotism was so obvious, that he disarmed his hearers. вҖңWell, my boy, youвҖҷll get along wherever you goвҒ вҖ”foot or horseвҒ вҖ”that IвҖҷll warrant,вҖқ said ShinshГӯn, patting him on the shoulder and taking his feet off the sofa. Berg smiled joyously. The count, followed by his guests, went into the drawing room. It was just the moment before a big dinner when the assembled guests, expecting the summons to zakГәska,7 avoid engaging in any long conversation but think it necessary to move about and talk, in order to show that they are not at all impatient for their food. The host and hostess look toward the door, and now and then glance at one another, and the visitors try to guess from these glances who, or what, they are waiting forвҒ вҖ”some important relation who has not yet arrived, or a dish that is not yet ready. Pierre had come just at dinnertime and was sitting awkwardly in the middle of the drawing room on the first chair he had come across, blocking the way for everyone. The countess tried to make him talk, but he went on naively looking around through his spectacles as if in search of somebody and answered all her questions in monosyllables. He was in the way and was the only one who did not notice the fact. Most of the guests, knowing of the affair with the bear, looked with curiosity at this big, stout, quiet man, wondering how such a clumsy, modest fellow could have played such a prank on a policeman. вҖңYou have only lately arrived?вҖқ the countess asked him. вҖңOui, madame,вҖқ replied he, looking around him. вҖңYou have not yet seen my husband?вҖқ вҖңNon, madame.вҖқ He smiled quite inappropriately. вҖңYou have been in Paris recently, I believe? I suppose itвҖҷs very interesting.вҖқ вҖңVery interesting.вҖқ The countess exchanged glances with Anna MikhГЎylovna. The latter understood that she was being asked to entertain this young man, and sitting down beside him she began to speak about his father; but he answered her, as he had the countess, only in monosyllables. The other guests were all conversing with one another. вҖңThe RazoumovskysвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ It was charmingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You are very kindвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Countess AprГЎksinaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ was heard on all sides. The countess rose and went into the ballroom. вҖңMГЎrya DmГӯtrievna?вҖқ came her voice from there. вҖңHerself,вҖқ came the answer in a rough voice, and MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna entered the room. All the unmarried ladies and even the married ones except the very oldest rose. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna paused at the door. Tall and stout, holding high her fifty-year-old head with its gray curls, she stood surveying the guests, and leisurely arranged her wide sleeves as if rolling them up. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna always spoke in Russian. вҖңHealth and happiness to her whose name day we are keeping and to her children,вҖқ she said, in her loud, full-toned voice which drowned all others. вҖңWell, you old sinner,вҖқ she went on, turning to the count who was kissing her hand, вҖңyouвҖҷre feeling dull in Moscow, I daresay? Nowhere to hunt with your dogs? But what is to be done, old man?
