Just see how these nestlings are growing up,вҖқ and she pointed to the girls. вҖңYou must look for husbands for them whether you like it or not.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWell,вҖқ said she, вҖңhowвҖҷs my Cossack?вҖқ (MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna always called NatГЎsha a Cossack) and she stroked the childвҖҷs arm as she came up fearless and gay to kiss her hand. вҖңI know sheвҖҷs a scamp of a girl, but I like her.вҖқ She took a pair of pear-shaped ruby earrings from her huge reticule and, having given them to the rosy NatГЎsha, who beamed with the pleasure of her saintвҖҷs-day fГӘte, turned away at once and addressed herself to Pierre. вҖңEh, eh, friend! Come here a bit,вҖқ said she, assuming a soft high tone of voice. вҖңCome here, my friendвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and she ominously tucked up her sleeves still higher. Pierre approached, looking at her in a childlike way through his spectacles. вҖңCome nearer, come nearer, friend! I used to be the only one to tell your father the truth when he was in favor, and in your case itвҖҷs my evident duty.вҖқ She paused. All were silent, expectant of what was to follow, for this was clearly only a prelude. вҖңA fine lad! My word! A fine lad!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ His father lies on his deathbed and he amuses himself setting a policeman astride a bear! For shame, sir, for shame! It would be better if you went to the war.вҖқ She turned away and gave her hand to the count, who could hardly keep from laughing. вҖңWell, I suppose it is time we were at table?вҖқ said MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. The count went in first with MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, the countess followed on the arm of a colonel of hussars, a man of importance to them because NikolГЎy was to go with him to the regiment; then came Anna MikhГЎylovna with ShinshГӯn. Berg gave his arm to VГ©ra. The smiling Julie KarГЎgina went in with NikolГЎy. After them other couples followed, filling the whole dining hall, and last of all the children, tutors, and governesses followed singly. The footmen began moving about, chairs scraped, the band struck up in the gallery, and the guests settled down in their places. Then the strains of the countвҖҷs household band were replaced by the clatter of knives and forks, the voices of visitors, and the soft steps of the footmen. At one end of the table sat the countess with MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna on her right and Anna MikhГЎylovna on her left, the other lady visitors were farther down. At the other end sat the count, with the hussar colonel on his left and ShinshГӯn and the other male visitors on his right. Midway down the long table on one side sat the grown-up young people: VГ©ra beside Berg, and Pierre beside BorГӯs; and on the other side, the children, tutors, and governesses. From behind the crystal decanters and fruit vases, the count kept glancing at his wife and her tall cap with its light-blue ribbons, and busily filled his neighborsвҖҷ glasses, not neglecting his own. The countess in turn, without omitting her duties as hostess, threw significant glances from behind the pineapples at her husband whose face and bald head seemed by their redness to contrast more than usual with his gray hair. At the ladiesвҖҷ end an even chatter of voices was heard all the time, at the menвҖҷs end the voices sounded louder and louder, especially that of the colonel of hussars who, growing more and more flushed, ate and drank so much that the count held him up as a pattern to the other guests. Berg with tender smiles was saying to VГ©ra that love is not an earthly but a heavenly feeling. BorГӯs was telling his new friend Pierre who the guests were and exchanging glances with NatГЎsha, who was sitting opposite. Pierre spoke little but examined the new faces, and ate a great deal. Of the two soups he chose turtle with savory patties and went on to the game without omitting a single dish or one of the wines. These latter the butler thrust mysteriously forward, wrapped in a napkin, from behind the next manвҖҷs shoulders and whispered: вҖңDry MadeiraвҖқвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңHungarianвҖқвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ or вҖңRhine wineвҖқ as the case might be. Of the four crystal glasses engraved with the countвҖҷs monogram that stood before his plate, Pierre held out one at random and drank with enjoyment, gazing with ever-increasing amiability at the other guests. NatГЎsha, who sat opposite, was looking at BorГӯs as girls of thirteen look at the boy they are in love with and have just kissed for the first time. Sometimes that same look fell on Pierre, and that funny lively little girlвҖҷs look made him inclined to laugh without knowing why. NikolГЎy sat at some distance from SГіnya, beside Julie KarГЎgina, to whom he was again talking with the same involuntary smile. SГіnya wore a company smile but was evidently tormented by jealousy; now she turned pale, now blushed and strained every nerve to overhear what NikolГЎy and Julie were saying to one another. The governess kept looking round uneasily as if preparing to resent any slight that might be put upon the children. The German tutor was trying to remember all the dishes, wines, and kinds of dessert, in order to send a full description of the dinner to his people in Germany; and he felt greatly offended when the butler with a bottle wrapped in a napkin passed him by. He frowned, trying to appear as if he did not want any of that wine, but was mortified because no one would understand that it was not to quench his thirst or from greediness that he wanted it, but simply from a conscientious desire for knowledge. XIX XIX At the menвҖҷs end of the table the talk grew more and more animated. The colonel told them that the declaration of war had already appeared in Petersburg and that a copy, which he had himself seen, had that day been forwarded by courier to the commander in chief. вҖңAnd why the deuce are we going to fight Bonaparte?вҖқ remarked ShinshГӯn. вҖңHe has stopped AustriaвҖҷs cackle and I fear it will be our turn next.вҖқ The colonel was a stout, tall, plethoric German, evidently devoted to the service and patriotically Russian. He resented ShinshГӯnвҖҷs remark. вҖңIt is for the reasson, my goot sir,вҖқ said he, speaking with a German accent, вҖңfor the reasson zat ze Emperor knows zat. He declares in ze manifessto zat he cannot fiew wiz indifference ze danger vreatening Russia and zat ze safety and dignity of ze Empire as vell as ze sanctity of its alliancesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he spoke this last word with particular emphasis as if in it lay the gist of the matter. Then with the unerring official memory that characterized him he repeated from the opening words of the manifesto: вҖҰ and the wish, which constitutes the EmperorвҖҷs sole and absolute aimвҒ вҖ”to establish peace in Europe on firm foundationsвҒ вҖ”has now decided him to despatch part of the army abroad and to create a new condition for the attainment of that purpose. вҖңZat, my dear sir, is vyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he concluded, drinking a tumbler of wine with dignity and looking to the count for approval. вҖңConnaissez-vous le Proverbe:8 вҖҳJerome, Jerome, do not roam, but turn spindles at home!вҖҷ?вҖқ said ShinshГӯn, puckering his brows and smiling. вҖңCela nous convient Г  merveille.9 SuvГіrov nowвҒ вҖ”he knew what he was about; yet they beat him Г  plate couture,10 and where are we to find SuvГіrovs now? Je vous demande un peu,вҖқ11 said he, continually changing from French to Russian. вҖңVe must vight to the last tr-r-op of our plood!вҖқ said the colonel, thumping the table; вҖңand ve must tie for our Emperor, and zen all vill pe vell. And ve must discuss it as little as po-o-ossibleвҖқвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ he dwelt particularly on the word possibleвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңas po-o-ossible,вҖқ he ended, again turning to the count. вҖңZat is how ve old hussars look at it, and zereвҖҷs an end of it! And how do you, a young man and a young hussar, how do you judge of it?вҖқ he added, addressing NikolГЎy, who when he heard that the war was being discussed had turned from his partner with eyes and ears intent on the colonel. вҖңI am quite of your opinion,вҖқ replied NikolГЎy, flaming up, turning his plate round and moving his wineglasses about with as much decision and desperation as though he were at that moment facing some great danger. вҖңI am convinced that we Russians must die or conquer,вҖқ he concluded, consciousвҒ вҖ”as were othersвҒ вҖ”after the words were uttered that his remarks were too enthusiastic and emphatic for the occasion and were therefore awkward. вҖңWhat you said just now was splendid!вҖқ said his partner Julie. SГіnya trembled all over and blushed to her ears and behind them and down to her neck and shoulders while NikolГЎy was speaking. Pierre listened to the colonelвҖҷs speech and nodded approvingly. вҖңThatвҖҷs fine,вҖқ said he. вҖңThe young manвҖҷs a real hussar!вҖқ shouted the colonel, again thumping the table. вҖңWhat are you making such a noise about over there?вҖқ MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs deep voice suddenly inquired from the other end of the table. вҖңWhat are you thumping the table for?вҖқ she demanded of the hussar, вҖңand why are you exciting yourself? Do you think the French are here?вҖқ вҖңI am speaking ze truce,вҖқ replied the hussar with a smile. вҖңItвҖҷs all about the war,вҖқ the count shouted down the table. вҖңYou know my sonвҖҷs going, MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna? My son is going.вҖқ вҖңI have four sons in the army but still I donвҖҷt fret. It is all in GodвҖҷs hands. You may die in your bed or God may spare you in a battle,вҖқ replied MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs deep voice, which easily carried the whole length of the table. вҖңThatвҖҷs true!вҖқ Once more the conversations concentrated, the ladiesвҖҷ at the one end and the menвҖҷs at the other. вҖңYou wonвҖҷt ask,вҖқ NatГЎshaвҖҷs little brother was saying; вҖңI know you wonвҖҷt ask!вҖқ вҖңI will,вҖқ replied NatГЎsha. Her face suddenly flushed with reckless and joyous resolution. She half rose, by a glance inviting Pierre, who sat opposite, to listen to what was coming, and turning to her mother: вҖңMamma!вҖқ rang out the clear contralto notes of her childish voice, audible the whole length of the table. вҖңWhat is it?вҖқ asked the countess, startled; but seeing by her daughterвҖҷs face that it was only mischief, she shook a finger at her sternly with a threatening and forbidding movement of her head. The conversation was hushed. вҖңMamma! What sweets are we going to have?вҖқ and NatГЎshaвҖҷs voice sounded still more firm and resolute. The countess tried to frown, but could not. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna shook her fat finger. вҖңCossack!вҖқ she said threateningly. Most of the guests, uncertain how to regard this sally, looked at the elders. вҖңYou had better take care!вҖқ said the countess. вҖңMamma! What sweets are we going to have?вҖқ NatГЎsha again cried boldly, with saucy gaiety, confident that her prank would be taken in good part. SГіnya and fat little PГ©tya doubled up with laughter. вҖңYou see! I have asked,вҖқ whispered NatГЎsha to her little brother and to Pierre, glancing at him again. вҖңIce pudding, but you wonвҖҷt get any,вҖқ said MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. NatГЎsha saw there was nothing to be afraid of and so she braved even MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. вҖңMГЎrya DmГӯtrievna! What kind of ice pudding? I donвҖҷt like ice cream.вҖқ вҖңCarrot ices.вҖқ вҖңNo! What kind, MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna? What kind?вҖқ she almost screamed; вҖңI want to know!вҖқ MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna and the countess burst out laughing, and all the guests joined in. Everyone laughed, not at MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs answer but at the incredible boldness and smartness of this little girl who had dared to treat MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna in this fashion. NatГЎsha only desisted when she had been told that there would be pineapple ice. Before the ices, champagne was served round. The band again struck up, the count and countess kissed, and the guests, leaving their seats, went up to вҖңcongratulateвҖқ the countess, and reached across the table to clink glasses with the count, with the children, and with one another. Again the footmen rushed about, chairs scraped, and in the same order in which they had entered but with redder faces, the guests returned to the drawing room and to the countвҖҷs study. II II Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs drawing room was gradually filling. The highest Petersburg society was assembled there: people differing widely in age and character but alike in the social circle to which they belonged. Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs daughter, the beautiful ElГЁn, came to take her father to the ambassadorвҖҷs entertainment; she wore a ball dress and her badge as maid of honor. The youthful little Princess BolkГіnskaya, known as la femme la plus sГ©duisante de PГ©tersbourg,1 was also there. She had been married during the previous winter, and being pregnant did not go to any large gatherings, but only to small receptions. Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs son, Ippolit, had come with Mortemart, whom he introduced. The AbbГ© Morio and many others had also come. To each new arrival Anna PГЎvlovna said, вҖңYou have not yet seen my aunt,вҖқ or вҖңYou do not know my aunt?вҖқ and very gravely conducted him or her to a little old lady, wearing large bows of ribbon in her cap, who had come sailing in from another room as soon as the guests began to arrive; and slowly turning her eyes from the visitor to her aunt, Anna PГЎvlovna mentioned each oneвҖҷs name and then left them. Each visitor performed the ceremony of greeting this old aunt whom not one of them knew, not one of them wanted to know, and not one of them cared about; Anna PГЎvlovna observed these greetings with mournful and solemn interest and silent approval. The aunt spoke to each of them in the same words, about their health and her own, and the health of Her Majesty, вҖңwho, thank God, was better today.вҖқ And each visitor, though politeness prevented his showing impatience, left the old woman with a sense of relief at having performed a vexatious duty and did not return to her the whole evening. The young Princess BolkГіnskaya had brought some work in a gold-embroidered velvet bag. Her pretty little upper lip, on which a delicate dark down was just perceptible, was too short for her teeth, but it lifted all the more sweetly, and was especially charming when she occasionally drew it down to meet the lower lip. As is always the case with a thoroughly attractive woman, her defectвҒ вҖ”the shortness of her upper lip and her half-open mouthвҒ вҖ”seemed to be her own special and peculiar form of beauty. Everyone brightened at the sight of this pretty young woman, so soon to become a mother, so full of life and health, and carrying her burden so lightly. Old men and dull dispirited young ones who looked at her, after being in her company and talking to her a little while, felt as if they too were becoming, like her, full of life and health. All who talked to her, and at each word saw her bright smile and the constant gleam of her white teeth, thought that they were in a specially amiable mood that day. The little princess went round the table with quick, short, swaying steps, her workbag on her arm, and gaily spreading out her dress sat down on a sofa near the silver samovar, as if all she was doing was a pleasure to herself and to all around her. вҖңI have brought my work,вҖқ said she in French, displaying her bag and addressing all present. вҖңMind, Annette, I hope you have not played a wicked trick on me,вҖқ she added, turning to her hostess. вҖңYou wrote that it was to be quite a small reception, and just see how badly I am dressed.вҖқ And she spread out her arms to show her short-waisted, lace-trimmed, dainty gray dress, girdled with a broad ribbon just below the breast. вҖңSoyez tranquille, Lise, you will always be prettier than anyone else,вҖқ replied Anna PГЎvlovna. вҖңYou know,вҖқ said the princess in the same tone of voice and still in French, turning to a general, вҖңmy husband is deserting me? He is going to get himself killed. Tell me what this wretched war is for?вҖқ she added, addressing Prince VasГӯli, and without waiting for an answer she turned to speak to his daughter, the beautiful ElГЁn. вҖңWhat a delightful woman this little princess is!вҖқ said Prince VasГӯli to Anna PГЎvlovna. One of the next arrivals was a stout, heavily built young man with close-cropped hair, spectacles, the light-colored breeches fashionable at that time, a very high ruffle, and a brown dress coat. This stout young man was an illegitimate son of Count BezГәkhov, a well-known grandee of CatherineвҖҷs time who now lay dying in Moscow. The young man had not yet entered either the military or civil service, as he had only just returned from abroad where he had been educated, and this was his first appearance in society. Anna PГЎvlovna greeted him with the nod she accorded to the lowest hierarchy in her drawing room. But in spite of this lowest-grade greeting, a look of anxiety and fear, as at the sight of something too large and unsuited to the place, came over her face when she saw Pierre enter. Though he was certainly rather bigger than the other men in the room, her anxiety could only have reference to the clever though shy, but observant and natural, expression which distinguished him from everyone else in that drawing room. вҖңIt is very good of you, Monsieur Pierre, to come and visit a poor invalid,вҖқ said Anna PГЎvlovna, exchanging an alarmed glance with her aunt as she conducted him to her. Pierre murmured something unintelligible, and continued to look round as if in search of something. On his way to the aunt he bowed to the little princess with a pleased smile, as to an intimate acquaintance. Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs alarm was justified, for Pierre turned away from the aunt without waiting to hear her speech about Her MajestyвҖҷs health. Anna PГЎvlovna in dismay detained him with the words: вҖңDo you know the AbbГ© Morio? He is a most interesting man.вҖқ вҖңYes, I have heard of his scheme for perpetual peace, and it is very interesting but hardly feasible.вҖқ вҖңYou think so?вҖқ rejoined Anna PГЎvlovna in order to say something and get away to attend to her duties as hostess. But Pierre now committed a reverse act of impoliteness. First he had left a lady before she had finished speaking to him, and now he continued to speak to another who wished to get away. With his head bent, and his big feet spread apart, he began explaining his reasons for thinking the abbГ©вҖҷs plan chimerical. вҖңWe will talk of it later,вҖқ said Anna PГЎvlovna with a smile. And having got rid of this young man who did not know how to behave, she resumed her duties as hostess and continued to listen and watch, ready to help at any point where the conversation might happen to flag. As the foreman of a spinning mill, when he has set the hands to work, goes round and notices here a spindle that has stopped or there one that creaks or makes more noise than it should, and hastens to check the machine or set it in proper motion, so Anna PГЎvlovna moved about her drawing room, approaching now a silent, now a too-noisy group, and by a word or slight rearrangement kept the conversational machine in steady, proper, and regular motion. But amid these cares her anxiety about Pierre was evident. She kept an anxious watch on him when he approached the group round Mortemart to listen to what was being said there, and again when he passed to another group whose center was the abbГ©. Pierre had been educated abroad, and this reception at Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs was the first he had attended in Russia. He knew that all the intellectual lights of Petersburg were gathered there and, like a child in a toyshop, did not know which way to look, afraid of missing any clever conversation that was to be heard. Seeing the self-confident and refined expression on the faces of those present he was always expecting to hear something very profound. At last he came up to Morio. Here the conversation seemed interesting and he stood waiting for an opportunity to express his own views, as young people are fond of doing. XX XX The card tables were drawn out, sets made up for boston, and the countвҖҷs visitors settled themselves, some in the two drawing rooms, some in the sitting room, some in the library. The count, holding his cards fanwise, kept himself with difficulty from dropping into his usual after-dinner nap, and laughed at everything. The young people, at the countessвҖҷ instigation, gathered round the clavichord and harp. Julie by general request played first. After she had played a little air with variations on the harp, she joined the other young ladies in begging NatГЎsha and NikolГЎy, who were noted for their musical talent, to sing something. NatГЎsha, who was treated as though she were grown up, was evidently very proud of this but at the same time felt shy. вҖңWhat shall we sing?вҖқ she said. вҖңвҖҠвҖҳThe Brook,вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ suggested NikolГЎy. вҖңWell, then, letвҖҷs be quick. BorГӯs, come here,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңBut where is SГіnya?вҖқ She looked round and seeing that her friend was not in the room ran to look for her. Running into SГіnyaвҖҷs room and not finding her there, NatГЎsha ran to the nursery, but SГіnya was not there either. NatГЎsha concluded that she must be on the chest in the passage. The chest in the passage was the place of mourning for the younger female generation in the RostГіv household. And there in fact was SГіnya lying face downward on NurseвҖҷs dirty feather bed on the top of the chest, crumpling her gauzy pink dress under her, hiding her face with her slender fingers, and sobbing so convulsively that her bare little shoulders shook. NatГЎshaвҖҷs face, which had been so radiantly happy all that saintвҖҷs day, suddenly changed: her eyes became fixed, and then a shiver passed down her broad neck and the corners of her mouth drooped. вҖңSГіnya! What is it? What is the matter?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ OoвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ OoвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ OoвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ!вҖқ And NatГЎshaвҖҷs large mouth widened, making her look quite ugly, and she began to wail like a baby without knowing why, except that SГіnya was crying. SГіnya tried to lift her head to answer but could not, and hid her face still deeper in the bed. NatГЎsha wept, sitting on the blue-striped feather bed and hugging her friend. With an effort SГіnya sat up and began wiping her eyes and explaining. вҖңNikГіlenka is going away in a weekвҖҷs time, hisвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ papersвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ have comeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ he told me himselfвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but still I should not cry,вҖқ and she showed a paper she held in her handвҒ вҖ”with the verses NikolГЎy had written, вҖңstill, I should not cry, but you canвҖҷtвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ no one can understandвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ what a soul he has!вҖқ And she began to cry again because he had such a noble soul. вҖңItвҖҷs all very well for youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I am not enviousвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I love you and BorГӯs also,вҖқ she went on, gaining a little strength; вҖңhe is niceвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ there are no difficulties in your way.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But NikolГЎy is my cousinвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ one would have toвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the Metropolitan himselfвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and even then it canвҖҷt be done. And besides, if she tells MammaвҖқ (SГіnya looked upon the countess as her mother and called her so) вҖңthat I am spoiling NikolГЎyвҖҷs career and am heartless and ungrateful, while trulyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ God is my witness,вҖқ and she made the sign of the cross, вҖңI love her so much, and all of you, only VГ©raвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And what for? What have I done to her? I am so grateful to you that I would willingly sacrifice everything, only I have nothing.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ SГіnya could not continue, and again hid her face in her hands and in the feather bed. NatГЎsha began consoling her, but her face showed that she understood all the gravity of her friendвҖҷs trouble. вҖңSГіnya,вҖқ she suddenly exclaimed, as if she had guessed the true reason of her friendвҖҷs sorrow, вҖңIвҖҷm sure VГ©ra has said something to you since dinner? HasnвҖҷt she?вҖқ вҖңYes, these verses NikolГЎy wrote himself and I copied some others, and she found them on my table and said sheвҖҷd show them to Mamma, and that I was ungrateful, and that Mamma would never allow him to marry me, but that heвҖҷll marry Julie. You see how heвҖҷs been with her all dayвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ NatГЎsha, what have I done to deserve it?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ And again she began to sob, more bitterly than before. NatГЎsha lifted her up, hugged her, and, smiling through her tears, began comforting her. вҖңSГіnya, donвҖҷt believe her, darling! DonвҖҷt believe her! Do you remember how we and NikГіlenka, all three of us, talked in the sitting room after supper? Why, we settled how everything was to be. I donвҖҷt quite remember how, but donвҖҷt you remember that it could all be arranged and how nice it all was? ThereвҖҷs Uncle ShinshГӯnвҖҷs brother has married his first cousin. And we are only second cousins, you know. And BorГӯs says it is quite possible. You know I have told him all about it. And he is so clever and so good!вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңDonвҖҷt you cry, SГіnya, dear love, darling SГіnya!вҖқ and she kissed her and laughed. вҖңVГ©raвҖҷs spiteful; never mind her! And all will come right and she wonвҖҷt say anything to Mamma. NikГіlenka will tell her himself, and he doesnвҖҷt care at all for Julie.вҖқ NatГЎsha kissed her on the hair. SГіnya sat up. The little kitten brightened, its eyes shone, and it seemed ready to lift its tail, jump down on its soft paws, and begin playing with the ball of worsted as a kitten should. вҖңDo you think so?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Really? Truly?вҖқ she said, quickly smoothing her frock and hair. вҖңReally, truly!вҖқ answered NatГЎsha, pushing in a crisp lock that had strayed from under her friendвҖҷs plaits. Both laughed. вҖңWell, letвҖҷs go and sing вҖҳThe Brook.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ вҖңCome along!вҖқ вҖңDo you know, that fat Pierre who sat opposite me is so funny!вҖқ said NatГЎsha, stopping suddenly. вҖңI feel so happy!вҖқ And she set off at a run along the passage. SГіnya, shaking off some down which clung to her and tucking away the verses in the bosom of her dress close to her bony little chest, ran after NatГЎsha down the passage into the sitting room with flushed face and light, joyous steps. At the visitorsвҖҷ request the young people sang the quartette, вҖңThe Brook,вҖқ with which everyone was delighted. Then NikolГЎy sang a song he had just learned: At nighttime in the moonвҖҷs fair glow How sweet, as fancies wander free, To feel that in this world thereвҖҷs one Who still is thinking but of thee! That while her fingers touch the harp Wafting sweet music oвҖҷer the lea, It is for thee thus swells her heart, Sighing its message out to thee.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ A day or two, then bliss unspoilt, But oh! till then I cannot live!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ He had not finished the last verse before the young people began to get ready to dance in the large hall, and the sound of the feet and the coughing of the musicians were heard from the gallery. Pierre was sitting in the drawing room where ShinshГӯn had engaged him, as a man recently returned from abroad, in a political conversation in which several others joined but which bored Pierre. When the music began NatГЎsha came in and walking straight up to Pierre said, laughing and blushing: вҖңMamma told me to ask you to join the dancers.вҖқ вҖңI am afraid of mixing the figures,вҖқ Pierre replied; вҖңbut if you will be my teacherвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ And lowering his big arm he offered it to the slender little girl. While the couples were arranging themselves and the musicians tuning up, Pierre sat down with his little partner. NatГЎsha was perfectly happy; she was dancing with a grown-up man, who had been abroad. She was sitting in a conspicuous place and talking to him like a grown-up lady. She had a fan in her hand that one of the ladies had given her to hold. Assuming quite the pose of a society woman (heaven knows when and where she had learned it) she talked with her partner, fanning herself and smiling over the fan. вҖңDear, dear! Just look at her!вҖқ exclaimed the countess as she crossed the ballroom, pointing to NatГЎsha. NatГЎsha blushed and laughed. вҖңWell, really, Mamma! Why should you? What is there to be surprised at?вҖқ In the midst of the third Г©cossaise there was a clatter of chairs being pushed back in the sitting room where the count and MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna had been playing cards with the majority of the more distinguished and older visitors. They now, stretching themselves after sitting so long, and replacing their purses and pocketbooks, entered the ballroom. First came MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna and the count, both with merry countenances. The count, with playful ceremony somewhat in ballet style, offered his bent arm to MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. He drew himself up, a smile of debonair gallantry lit up his face and as soon as the last figure of the Г©cossaise was ended, he clapped his hands to the musicians and shouted up to their gallery, addressing the first violin: вҖңSemГ«n! Do you know the Daniel Cooper?вҖқ This was the countвҖҷs favorite dance, which he had danced in his youth. (Strictly speaking, Daniel Cooper was one figure of the anglaise.) вҖңLook at Papa!вҖқ shouted NatГЎsha to the whole company, and quite forgetting that she was dancing with a grown-up partner she bent her curly head to her knees and made the whole room ring with her laughter. And indeed everybody in the room looked with a smile of pleasure at the jovial old gentleman, who standing beside his tall and stout partner, MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, curved his arms, beat time, straightened his shoulders, turned out his toes, tapped gently with his foot, and, by a smile that broadened his round face more and more, prepared the onlookers for what was to follow. As soon as the provocatively gay strains of Daniel Cooper (somewhat resembling those of a merry peasant dance) began to sound, all the doorways of the ballroom were suddenly filled by the domestic serfsвҒ вҖ”the men on one side and the women on the otherвҒ вҖ”who with beaming faces had come to see their master making merry. вҖңJust look at the master! A regular eagle he is!вҖқ loudly remarked the nurse, as she stood in one of the doorways. The count danced well and knew it. But his partner could not and did not want to dance well. Her enormous figure stood erect, her powerful arms hanging down (she had handed her reticule to the countess), and only her stern but handsome face really joined in the dance. What was expressed by the whole of the countвҖҷs plump figure, in MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna found expression only in her more and more beaming face and quivering nose. But if the count, getting more and more into the swing of it, charmed the spectators by the unexpectedness of his adroit maneuvers and the agility with which he capered about on his light feet, MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna produced no less impression by slight exertionsвҒ вҖ”the least effort to move her shoulders or bend her arms when turning, or stamp her footвҒ вҖ”which everyone appreciated in view of her size and habitual severity. The dance grew livelier and livelier. The other couples could not attract a momentвҖҷs attention to their own evolutions and did not even try to do so. All were watching the count and MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. NatГЎsha kept pulling everyone by sleeve or dress, urging them to вҖңlook at Papa!вҖқ though as it was they never took their eyes off the couple. In the intervals of the dance the count, breathing deeply, waved and shouted to the musicians to play faster. Faster, faster, and faster; lightly, more lightly, and yet more lightly whirled the count, flying round MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, now on his toes, now on his heels; until, turning his partner round to her seat, he executed the final pas, raising his soft foot backwards, bowing his perspiring head, smiling and making a wide sweep with his arm, amid a thunder of applause and laughter led by NatГЎsha. Both partners stood still, breathing heavily and wiping their faces with their cambric handkerchiefs. вҖңThatвҖҷs how we used to dance in our time, ma chГЁre,вҖқ said the count. вҖңThat was a Daniel Cooper!вҖқ exclaimed MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, tucking up her sleeves and puffing heavily. XXI XXI While in the RostГіvsвҖҷ ballroom the sixth anglaise was being danced, to a tune in which the weary musicians blundered, and while tired footmen and cooks were getting the supper, Count BezГәkhov had a sixth stroke. The doctors pronounced recovery impossible. After a mute confession, communion was administered to the dying man, preparations made for the sacrament of unction, and in his house there was the bustle and thrill of suspense usual at such moments. Outside the house, beyond the gates, a group of undertakers, who hid whenever a carriage drove up, waited in expectation of an important order for an expensive funeral. The Military Governor of Moscow, who had been assiduous in sending aides-de-camp to inquire after the countвҖҷs health, came himself that evening to bid a last farewell to the celebrated grandee of CatherineвҖҷs court, Count BezГәkhov. The magnificent reception room was crowded. Everyone stood up respectfully when the Military Governor, having stayed about half an hour alone with the dying man, passed out, slightly acknowledging their bows and trying to escape as quickly as possible from the glances fixed on him by the doctors, clergy, and relatives of the family. Prince VasГӯli, who had grown thinner and paler during the last few days, escorted him to the door, repeating something to him several times in low tones. When the Military Governor had gone, Prince VasГӯli sat down all alone on a chair in the ballroom, crossing one leg high over the other, leaning his elbow on his knee and covering his face with his hand. After sitting so for a while he rose, and, looking about him with frightened eyes, went with unusually hurried steps down the long corridor leading to the back of the house, to the room of the eldest princess. Those who were in the dimly lit reception room spoke in nervous whispers, and, whenever anyone went into or came from the dying manвҖҷs room, grew silent and gazed with eyes full of curiosity or expectancy at his door, which creaked slightly when opened. вҖңThe limits of human lifeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ are fixed and may not be oвҖҷerpassed,вҖқ said an old priest to a lady who had taken a seat beside him and was listening naively to his words. вҖңI wonder, is it not too late to administer unction?вҖқ asked the lady, adding the priestвҖҷs clerical title, as if she had no opinion of her own on the subject. вҖңAh, madam, it is a great sacrament,вҖқ replied the priest, passing his hand over the thin grizzled strands of hair combed back across his bald head. вҖңWho was that? The Military Governor himself?вҖқ was being asked at the other side of the room. вҖңHow young-looking he is!вҖқ вҖңYes, and he is over sixty. I hear the count no longer recognizes anyone. They wished to administer the sacrament of unction.вҖқ вҖңI knew someone who received that sacrament seven times.вҖқ The second princess had just come from the sickroom with her eyes red from weeping and sat down beside Dr.В Lorrain, who was sitting in a graceful pose under a portrait of Catherine, leaning his elbow on a table. вҖңBeautiful,вҖқ said the doctor in answer to a remark about the weather. вҖңThe weather is beautiful, Princess; and besides, in Moscow one feels as if one were in the country.вҖқ вҖңYes, indeed,вҖқ replied the princess with a sigh. вҖңSo he may have something to drink?вҖқ Lorrain considered. вҖңHas he taken his medicine?вҖқ вҖңYes.вҖқ The doctor glanced at his watch. вҖңTake a glass of boiled water and put a pinch of cream of tartar,вҖқ and he indicated with his delicate fingers what he meant by a pinch. вҖңDere has neffer been a gase,вҖқ a German doctor was saying to an aide-de-camp, вҖңdat one liffs after de sird stroke.вҖқ вҖңAnd what a well-preserved man he was!вҖқ remarked the aide-de-camp. вҖңAnd who will inherit his wealth?вҖқ he added in a whisper. вҖңIt vonвҖҷt go begging,вҖқ replied the German with a smile. Everyone again looked toward the door, which creaked as the second princess went in with the drink she had prepared according to LorrainвҖҷs instructions. The German doctor went up to Lorrain. вҖңDo you think he can last till morning?вҖқ asked the German, addressing Lorrain in French which he pronounced badly. Lorrain, pursing up his lips, waved a severely negative finger before his nose. вҖңTonight, not later,вҖқ said he in a low voice, and he moved away with a decorous smile of self-satisfaction at being able clearly to understand and state the patientвҖҷs condition. Meanwhile Prince VasГӯli had opened the door into the princessвҖҷ room. In this room it was almost dark; only two tiny lamps were burning before the icons and there was a pleasant scent of flowers and burnt pastilles. The room was crowded with small pieces of furniture, whatnots, cupboards, and little tables. The quilt of a high, white feather bed was just visible behind a screen. A small dog began to bark. вҖңAh, is it you, cousin?вҖқ She rose and smoothed her hair, which was as usual so extremely smooth that it seemed to be made of one piece with her head and covered with varnish. вҖңHas anything happened?вҖқ she asked. вҖңI am so terrified.вҖқ вҖңNo, there is no change. I only came to have a talk about business, KГЎtish,вҖқ12 muttered the prince, seating himself wearily on the chair she had just vacated. вҖңYou have made the place warm, I must say,вҖқ he remarked. вҖңWell, sit down: letвҖҷs have a talk.вҖқ вҖңI thought perhaps something had happened,вҖқ she said with her unchanging stonily severe expression; and, sitting down opposite the prince, she prepared to listen. вҖңI wished to get a nap, mon cousin, but I canвҖҷt.вҖқ вҖңWell, my dear?вҖқ said Prince VasГӯli, taking her hand and bending it downwards as was his habit. It was plain that this вҖңwell?вҖқ referred to much that they both understood without naming. The princess, who had a straight, rigid body, abnormally long for her legs, looked directly at Prince VasГӯli with no sign of emotion in her prominent gray eyes. Then she shook her head and glanced up at the icons with a sigh. This might have been taken as an expression of sorrow and devotion, or of weariness and hope of resting before long. Prince VasГӯli understood it as an expression of weariness. вҖңAnd I?вҖқ he said; вҖңdo you think it is easier for me? I am as worn out as a post horse, but still I must have a talk with you, KГЎtish, a very serious talk.вҖқ Prince VasГӯli said no more and his cheeks began to twitch nervously, now on one side, now on the other, giving his face an unpleasant expression which was never to be seen on it in a drawing room. His eyes too seemed strange; at one moment they looked impudently sly and at the next glanced round in alarm. The princess, holding her little dog on her lap with her thin bony hands, looked attentively into Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs eyes evidently resolved not to be the first to break silence, if she had to wait till morning. вҖңWell, you see, my dear princess and cousin, KaterГӯna SemГ«novna,вҖқ continued Prince VasГӯli, returning to his theme, apparently not without an inner struggle; вҖңat such a moment as this one must think of everything. One must think of the future, of all of youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I love you all, like children of my own, as you know.вҖқ The princess continued to look at him without moving, and with the same dull expression. вҖңAnd then of course my family has also to be considered,вҖқ Prince VasГӯli went on, testily pushing away a little table without looking at her. вҖңYou know, KГЎtish, that weвҒ вҖ”you three sisters MГЎmontov, and my wifeвҒ вҖ”are the countвҖҷs only direct heirs. I know, I know how hard it is for you to talk or think of such matters. It is no easier for me; but, my dear, I am getting on for sixty and must be prepared for anything. Do you know I have sent for Pierre? The count,вҖқ pointing to his portrait, вҖңdefinitely demanded that he should be called.вҖқ Prince VasГӯli looked questioningly at the princess, but could not make out whether she was considering what he had just said or whether she was simply looking at him. вҖңThere is one thing I constantly pray God to grant, mon cousin,вҖқ she replied, вҖңand it is that He would be merciful to him and would allow his noble soul peacefully to leave thisвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYes, yes, of course,вҖқ interrupted Prince VasГӯli impatiently, rubbing his bald head and angrily pulling back toward him the little table that he had pushed away. вҖңButвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ in short, the fact isвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ you know yourself that last winter the count made a will by which he left all his property, not to us his direct heirs, but to Pierre.вҖқ вҖңHe has made wills enough!вҖқ quietly remarked the princess. вҖңBut he cannot leave the estate to Pierre. Pierre is illegitimate.вҖқ вҖңBut, my dear,вҖқ said Prince VasГӯli suddenly, clutching the little table and becoming more animated and talking more rapidly: вҖңwhat if a letter has been written to the Emperor in which the count asks for PierreвҖҷs legitimation? Do you understand that in consideration of the countвҖҷs services, his request would be granted?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The princess smiled as people do who think they know more about the subject under discussion than those they are talking with. вҖңI can tell you more,вҖқ continued Prince VasГӯli, seizing her hand, вҖңthat letter was written, though it was not sent, and the Emperor knew of it. The only question is, has it been destroyed or not? If not, then as soon as all is over,вҖқ and Prince VasГӯli sighed to intimate what he meant by the words all is over, вҖңand the countвҖҷs papers are opened, the will and letter will be delivered to the Emperor, and the petition will certainly be granted. Pierre will get everything as the legitimate son.вҖқ вҖңAnd our share?вҖқ asked the princess smiling ironically, as if anything might happen, only not that. вҖңBut, my poor Catiche, it is as clear as daylight! He will then be the legal heir to everything and you wonвҖҷt get anything. You must know, my dear, whether the will and letter were written, and whether they have been destroyed or not. And if they have somehow been overlooked, you ought to know where they are, and must find them, becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhat next?вҖқ the princess interrupted, smiling sardonically and not changing the expression of her eyes. вҖңI am a woman, and you think we are all stupid; but I know this: an illegitimate son cannot inheritвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ un bГўtard!вҖқ13 she added, as if supposing that this translation of the word would effectively prove to Prince VasГӯli the invalidity of his contention. вҖңWell, really, KГЎtish! CanвҖҷt you understand! You are so intelligent, how is it you donвҖҷt see that if the count has written a letter to the Emperor begging him to recognize Pierre as legitimate, it follows that Pierre will not be Pierre but will become Count BezГәkhov, and will then inherit everything under the will? And if the will and letter are not destroyed, then you will have nothing but the consolation of having been dutiful et tout ce qui sвҖҷensuit!14 ThatвҖҷs certain.вҖқ вҖңI know the will was made, but I also know that it is invalid; and you, mon cousin, seem to consider me a perfect fool,вҖқ said the princess with the expression women assume when they suppose they are saying something witty and stinging. вҖңMy dear Princess KaterГӯna SemГ«novna,вҖқ began Prince VasГӯli impatiently, вҖңI came here not to wrangle with you, but to talk about your interests as with a kinswoman, a good, kind, true relation. And I tell you for the tenth time that if the letter to the Emperor and the will in PierreвҖҷs favor are among the countвҖҷs papers, then, my dear girl, you and your sisters are not heiresses! If you donвҖҷt believe me, then believe an expert. I have just been talking to DmГӯtri OnГәfrichвҖқ (the family solicitor) вҖңand he says the same.вҖқ At this a sudden change evidently took place in the princessвҖҷ ideas; her thin lips grew white, though her eyes did not change, and her voice when she began to speak passed through such transitions as she herself evidently did not expect. вҖңThat would be a fine thing!вҖқ said she. вҖңI never wanted anything and I donвҖҷt now.вҖқ She pushed the little dog off her lap and smoothed her dress. вҖңAnd this is gratitudeвҒ вҖ”this is recognition for those who have sacrificed everything for his sake!вҖқ she cried. вҖңItвҖҷs splendid! Fine! I donвҖҷt want anything, Prince.вҖқ вҖңYes, but you are not the only one. There are your sistersвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ replied Prince VasГӯli. But the princess did not listen to him. вҖңYes, I knew it long ago but had forgotten. I knew that I could expect nothing but meanness, deceit, envy, intrigue, and ingratitudeвҒ вҖ”the blackest ingratitudeвҒ вҖ”in this houseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңDo you or do you not know where that will is?вҖқ insisted Prince VasГӯli, his cheeks twitching more than ever. вҖңYes, I was a fool! I still believed in people, loved them, and sacrificed myself. But only the base, the vile succeed! I know who has been intriguing!вҖқ The princess wished to rise, but the prince held her by the hand. She had the air of one who has suddenly lost faith in the whole human race. She gave her companion an angry glance. вҖңThere is still time, my dear. You must remember, KГЎtish, that it was all done casually in a moment of anger, of illness, and was afterwards forgotten. Our duty, my dear, is to rectify his mistake, to ease his last moments by not letting him commit this injustice, and not to let him die feeling that he is rendering unhappy those whoвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWho sacrificed everything for him,вҖқ chimed in the princess, who would again have risen had not the prince still held her fast, вҖңthough he never could appreciate it. No, mon cousin,вҖқ she added with a sigh, вҖңI shall always remember that in this world one must expect no reward, that in this world there is neither honor nor justice. In this world one has to be cunning and cruel.вҖқ вҖңNow come, come! Be reasonable. I know your excellent heart.вҖқ вҖңNo, I have a wicked heart.вҖқ вҖңI know your heart,вҖқ repeated the prince. вҖңI value your friendship and wish you to have as good an opinion of me. DonвҖҷt upset yourself, and let us talk sensibly while there is still time, be it a day or be it but an hour.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Tell me all you know about the will, and above all where it is. You must know. We will take it at once and show it to the count. He has, no doubt, forgotten it and will wish to destroy it. You understand that my sole desire is conscientiously to carry out his wishes; that is my only reason for being here. I came simply to help him and you.вҖқ вҖңNow I see it all! I know who has been intriguingвҒ вҖ”I know!вҖқ cried the princess. вҖңThatвҖҷs not the point, my dear.вҖқ вҖңItвҖҷs that protГ©gГ© of yours, that sweet Princess DrubetskГЎya, that Anna MikhГЎylovna whom I would not take for a housemaidвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the infamous, vile woman!вҖқ вҖңDo not let us lose any timeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAh, donвҖҷt talk to me! Last winter she wheedled herself in here and told the count such vile, disgraceful things about us, especially about SophieвҒ вҖ”I canвҖҷt repeat themвҒ вҖ”that it made the count quite ill and he would not see us for a whole fortnight. I know it was then he wrote this vile, infamous paper, but I thought the thing was invalid.вҖқ вҖңWeвҖҷve got to it at lastвҒ вҖ”why did you not tell me about it sooner?вҖқ вҖңItвҖҷs in the inlaid portfolio that he keeps under his pillow,вҖқ said the princess, ignoring his question. вҖңNow I know! Yes; if I have a sin, a great sin, it is hatred of that vile woman!вҖқ almost shrieked the princess, now quite changed. вҖңAnd what does she come worming herself in here for? But I will give her a piece of my mind. The time will come!вҖқ XXII XXII While these conversations were going on in the reception room and the princessвҖҷ room, a carriage containing Pierre (who had been sent for) and Anna MikhГЎylovna (who found it necessary to accompany him) was driving into the court of Count BezГәkhovвҖҷs house. As the wheels rolled softly over the straw beneath the windows, Anna MikhГЎylovna, having turned with words of comfort to her companion, realized that he was asleep in his corner and woke him up. Rousing himself, Pierre followed Anna MikhГЎylovna out of the carriage, and only then began to think of the interview with his dying father which awaited him. He noticed that they had not come to the front entrance but to the back door. While he was getting down from the carriage steps two men, who looked like tradespeople, ran hurriedly from the entrance and hid in the shadow of the wall. Pausing for a moment, Pierre noticed several other men of the same kind hiding in the shadow of the house on both sides. But neither Anna MikhГЎylovna nor the footman nor the coachman, who could not help seeing these people, took any notice of them. вҖңIt seems to be all right,вҖқ Pierre concluded, and followed Anna MikhГЎylovna. She hurriedly ascended the narrow dimly lit stone staircase, calling to Pierre, who was lagging behind, to follow. Though he did not see why it was necessary for him to go to the count at all, still less why he had to go by the back stairs, yet judging by Anna MikhГЎylovnaвҖҷs air of assurance and haste, Pierre concluded that it was all absolutely necessary. Halfway up the stairs they were almost knocked over by some men who, carrying pails, came running downstairs, their boots clattering. These men pressed close to the wall to let Pierre and Anna MikhГЎylovna pass and did not evince the least surprise at seeing them there. вҖңIs this the way to the princessesвҖҷ apartments?вҖқ asked Anna MikhГЎylovna of one of them. вҖңYes,вҖқ replied a footman in a bold loud voice, as if anything were now permissible; вҖңthe door to the left, maвҖҷam.вҖқ вҖңPerhaps the count did not ask for me,вҖқ said Pierre when he reached the landing. вҖңIвҖҷd better go to my own room.вҖқ Anna MikhГЎylovna paused and waited for him to come up. вҖңAh, my friend!вҖқ she said, touching his arm as she had done her sonвҖҷs when speaking to him that afternoon, вҖңbelieve me I suffer no less than you do, but be a man!вҖқ вҖңBut really, hadnвҖҷt I better go away?вҖқ he asked, looking kindly at her over his spectacles. вҖңAh, my dear friend! Forget the wrongs that may have been done you. Think that he is your fatherвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ perhaps in the agony of death.вҖқ She sighed. вҖңI have loved you like a son from the first. Trust yourself to me, Pierre. I shall not forget your interests.вҖқ Pierre did not understand a word, but the conviction that all this had to be grew stronger, and he meekly followed Anna MikhГЎylovna who was already opening a door. This door led into a back anteroom. An old man, a servant of the princesses, sat in a corner knitting a stocking. Pierre had never been in this part of the house and did not even know of the existence of these rooms. Anna MikhГЎylovna, addressing a maid who was hurrying past with a decanter on a tray as вҖңmy dearвҖқ and вҖңmy sweet,вҖқ asked about the princessвҖҷ health and then led Pierre along a stone passage. The first door on the left led into the princessesвҖҷ apartments. The maid with the decanter in her haste had not closed the door (everything in the house was done in haste at that time), and Pierre and Anna MikhГЎylovna in passing instinctively glanced into the room, where Prince VasГӯli and the eldest princess were sitting close together talking. Seeing them pass, Prince VasГӯli drew back with obvious impatience, while the princess jumped up and with a gesture of desperation slammed the door with all her might. This action was so unlike her usual composure and the fear depicted on Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs face so out of keeping with his dignity that Pierre stopped and glanced inquiringly over his spectacles at his guide. Anna MikhГЎylovna evinced no surprise, she only smiled faintly and sighed, as if to say that this was no more than she had expected. вҖңBe a man, my friend. I will look after your interests,вҖқ said she in reply to his look, and went still faster along the passage. Pierre could not make out what it was all about, and still less what вҖңwatching over his interestsвҖқ meant, but he decided that all these things had to be. From the passage they went into a large, dimly lit room adjoining the countвҖҷs reception room. It was one of those sumptuous but cold apartments known to Pierre only from the front approach, but even in this room there now stood an empty bath, and water had been spilled on the carpet. They were met by a deacon with a censer and by a servant who passed out on tiptoe without heeding them. They went into the reception room familiar to Pierre, with two Italian windows opening into the conservatory, with its large bust and full length portrait of Catherine the Great. The same people were still sitting here in almost the same positions as before, whispering to one another. All became silent and turned to look at the pale tear-worn Anna MikhГЎylovna as she entered, and at the big stout figure of Pierre who, hanging his head, meekly followed her. Anna MikhГЎylovnaвҖҷs face expressed a consciousness that the decisive moment had arrived. With the air of a practical Petersburg lady she now, keeping Pierre close beside her, entered the room even more boldly than that afternoon. She felt that as she brought with her the person the dying man wished to see, her own admission was assured. Casting a rapid glance at all those in the room and noticing the countвҖҷs confessor there, she glided up to him with a sort of amble, not exactly bowing yet seeming to grow suddenly smaller, and respectfully received the blessing first of one and then of another priest. вҖңGod be thanked that you are in time,вҖқ said she to one of the priests; вҖңall we relatives have been in such anxiety. This young man is the countвҖҷs son,вҖқ she added more softly. вҖңWhat a terrible moment!вҖқ Having said this she went up to the doctor. вҖңDear doctor,вҖқ said she, вҖңthis young man is the countвҖҷs son. Is there any hope?вҖқ The doctor cast a rapid glance upwards and silently shrugged his shoulders. Anna MikhГЎylovna with just the same movement raised her shoulders and eyes, almost closing the latter, sighed, and moved away from the doctor to Pierre. To him, in a particularly respectful and tenderly sad voice, she said: вҖңTrust in His mercy!вҖқ and pointing out a small sofa for him to sit and wait for her, she went silently toward the door that everyone was watching and it creaked very slightly as she disappeared behind it. Pierre, having made up his mind to obey his monitress implicitly, moved toward the sofa she had indicated. As soon as Anna MikhГЎylovna had disappeared he noticed that the eyes of all in the room turned to him with something more than curiosity and sympathy. He noticed that they whispered to one another, casting significant looks at him with a kind of awe and even servility. A deference such as he had never before received was shown him. A strange lady, the one who had been talking to the priests, rose and offered him her seat; an aide-de-camp picked up and returned a glove Pierre had dropped; the doctors became respectfully silent as he passed by, and moved to make way for him. At first Pierre wished to take another seat so as not to trouble the lady, and also to pick up the glove himself and to pass round the doctors who were not even in his way; but all at once he felt that this would not do, and that tonight he was a person obliged to perform some sort of awful rite which everyone expected of him, and that he was therefore bound to accept their services. He took the glove in silence from the aide-de-camp, and sat down in the ladyвҖҷs chair, placing his huge hands symmetrically on his knees in the naive attitude of an Egyptian statue, and decided in his own mind that all was as it should be, and that in order not to lose his head and do foolish things he must not act on his own ideas tonight, but must yield himself up entirely to the will of those who were guiding him. Not two minutes had passed before Prince VasГӯli with head erect majestically entered the room. He was wearing his long coat with three stars on his breast. He seemed to have grown thinner since the morning; his eyes seemed larger than usual when he glanced round and noticed Pierre. He went up to him, took his hand (a thing he never used to do), and drew it downwards as if wishing to ascertain whether it was firmly fixed on. вҖңCourage, courage, my friend! He has asked to see you. That is well!вҖқ and he turned to go. But Pierre thought it necessary to ask: вҖңHow isвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and hesitated, not knowing whether it would be proper to call the dying man вҖңthe count,вҖқ yet ashamed to call him вҖңfather.вҖқ вҖңHe had another stroke about half an hour ago. Courage, my friendвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ PierreвҖҷs mind was in such a confused state that the word вҖңstrokeвҖқ suggested to him a blow from something. He looked at Prince VasГӯli in perplexity, and only later grasped that a stroke was an attack of illness. Prince VasГӯli said something to Lorrain in passing and went through the door on tiptoe. He could not walk well on tiptoe and his whole body jerked at each step. The eldest princess followed him, and the priests and deacons and some servants also went in at the door. Through that door was heard a noise of things being moved about, and at last Anna MikhГЎylovna, still with the same expression, pale but resolute in the discharge of duty, ran out and touching Pierre lightly on the arm said: вҖңThe divine mercy is inexhaustible! Unction is about to be administered. Come.вҖқ Pierre went in at the door, stepping on the soft carpet, and noticed that the strange lady, the aide-de-camp, and some of the servants, all followed him in, as if there were now no further need for permission to enter that room. XXIII XXIII Pierre well knew this large room divided by columns and an arch, its walls hung round with Persian carpets. The part of the room behind the columns, with a high silk-curtained mahogany bedstead on one side and on the other an immense case containing icons, was brightly illuminated with red light like a Russian church during evening service. Under the gleaming icons stood a long invalid chair, and in that chair on snowy-white smooth pillows, evidently freshly changed, Pierre sawвҒ вҖ”covered to the waist by a bright green quiltвҒ вҖ”the familiar, majestic figure of his father, Count BezГәkhov, with that gray mane of hair above his broad forehead which reminded one of a lion, and the deep characteristically noble wrinkles of his handsome, ruddy face. He lay just under the icons; his large thick hands outside the quilt. Into the right hand, which was lying palm downwards, a wax taper had been thrust between forefinger and thumb, and an old servant, bending over from behind the chair, held it in position. By the chair stood the priests, their long hair falling over their magnificent glittering vestments, with lighted tapers in their hands, slowly and solemnly conducting the service. A little behind them stood the two younger princesses holding handkerchiefs to their eyes, and just in front of them their eldest sister, Catiche, with a vicious and determined look steadily fixed on the icons, as though declaring to all that she could not answer for herself should she glance round. Anna MikhГЎylovna, with a meek, sorrowful, and all-forgiving expression on her face, stood by the door near the strange lady. Prince VasГӯli in front of the door, near the invalid chair, a wax taper in his left hand, was leaning his left arm on the carved back of a velvet chair he had turned round for the purpose, and was crossing himself with his right hand, turning his eyes upward each time he touched his forehead. His face wore a calm look of piety and resignation to the will of God. вҖңIf you do not understand these sentiments,вҖқ he seemed to be saying, вҖңso much the worse for you!вҖқ Behind him stood the aide-de-camp, the doctors, and the menservants; the men and women had separated as in church. All were silently crossing themselves, and the reading of the church service, the subdued chanting of deep bass voices, and in the intervals sighs and the shuffling of feet were the only sounds that could be heard. Anna MikhГЎylovna, with an air of importance that showed that she felt she quite knew what she was about, went across the room to where Pierre was standing and gave him a taper. He lit it and, distracted by observing those around him, began crossing himself with the hand that held the taper. Sophie, the rosy, laughter-loving, youngest princess with the mole, watched him. She smiled, hid her face in her handkerchief, and remained with it hidden for awhile; then looking up and seeing Pierre she again began to laugh. She evidently felt unable to look at him without laughing, but could not resist looking at him: so to be out of temptation she slipped quietly behind one of the columns. In the midst of the service the voices of the priests suddenly ceased, they whispered to one another, and the old servant who was holding the countвҖҷs hand got up and said something to the ladies. Anna MikhГЎylovna stepped forward and, stooping over the dying man, beckoned to Lorrain from behind her back. The French doctor held no taper; he was leaning against one of the columns in a respectful attitude implying that he, a foreigner, in spite of all differences of faith, understood the full importance of the rite now being performed and even approved of it. He now approached the sick man with the noiseless step of one in full vigor of life, with his delicate white fingers raised from the green quilt the hand that was free, and turning sideways felt the pulse and reflected a moment. The sick man was given something to drink, there was a stir around him, then the people resumed their places and the service continued. During this interval Pierre noticed that Prince VasГӯli left the chair on which he had been leaning, andвҒ вҖ”with an air which intimated that he knew what he was about and if others did not understand him it was so much the worse for themвҒ вҖ”did not go up to the dying man, but passed by him, joined the eldest princess, and moved with her to the side of the room where stood the high bedstead with its silken hangings. On leaving the bed both Prince VasГӯli and the princess passed out by a back door, but returned to their places one after the other before the service was concluded. Pierre paid no more attention to this occurrence than to the rest of what went on, having made up his mind once for all that what he saw happening around him that evening was in some way essential. The chanting of the service ceased, and the voice of the priest was heard respectfully congratulating the dying man on having received the sacrament. The dying man lay as lifeless and immovable as before. Around him everyone began to stir: steps were audible and whispers, among which Anna MikhГЎylovnaвҖҷs was the most distinct. Pierre heard her say: вҖңCertainly he must be moved onto the bed; here it will be impossibleвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The sick man was so surrounded by doctors, princesses, and servants that Pierre could no longer see the reddish-yellow face with its gray maneвҒ вҖ”which, though he saw other faces as well, he had not lost sight of for a single moment during the whole service. He judged by the cautious movements of those who crowded round the invalid chair that they had lifted the dying man and were moving him. вҖңCatch hold of my arm or youвҖҷll drop him!вҖқ he heard one of the servants say in a frightened whisper. вҖңCatch hold from underneath. Here!вҖқ exclaimed different voices; and the heavy breathing of the bearers and the shuffling of their feet grew more hurried, as if the weight they were carrying were too much for them. As the bearers, among whom was Anna MikhГЎylovna, passed the young man he caught a momentary glimpse between their heads and backs of the dying manвҖҷs high, stout, uncovered chest and powerful shoulders, raised by those who were holding him under the armpits, and of his gray, curly, leonine head. This head, with its remarkably broad brow and cheekbones, its handsome, sensual mouth, and its cold, majestic expression, was not disfigured by the approach of death. It was the same as Pierre remembered it three months before, when the count had sent him to Petersburg. But now this head was swaying helplessly with the uneven movements of the bearers, and the cold listless gaze fixed itself upon nothing. After a few minutesвҖҷ bustle beside the high bedstead, those who had carried the sick man dispersed. Anna MikhГЎylovna touched PierreвҖҷs hand and said, вҖңCome.вҖқ Pierre went with her to the bed on which the sick man had been laid in a stately pose in keeping with the ceremony just completed. He lay with his head propped high on the pillows. His hands were symmetrically placed on the green silk quilt, the palms downward. When Pierre came up the count was gazing straight at him, but with a look the significance of which could not be understood by mortal man. Either this look meant nothing but that as long as one has eyes they must look somewhere, or it meant too much. Pierre hesitated, not knowing what to do, and glanced inquiringly at his guide. Anna MikhГЎylovna made a hurried sign with her eyes, glancing at the sick manвҖҷs hand and moving her lips as if to send it a kiss. Pierre, carefully stretching his neck so as not to touch the quilt, followed her suggestion and pressed his lips to the large boned, fleshy hand. Neither the hand nor a single muscle of the countвҖҷs face stirred. Once more Pierre looked questioningly at Anna MikhГЎylovna to see what he was to do next. Anna MikhГЎylovna with her eyes indicated a chair that stood beside the bed. Pierre obediently sat down, his eyes asking if he were doing right. Anna MikhГЎylovna nodded approvingly. Again Pierre fell into the naively symmetrical pose of an Egyptian statue, evidently distressed that his stout and clumsy body took up so much room and doing his utmost to look as small as possible. He looked at the count, who still gazed at the spot where PierreвҖҷs face had been before he sat down. Anna MikhГЎylovna indicated by her attitude her consciousness of the pathetic importance of these last moments of meeting between the father and son. This lasted about two minutes, which to Pierre seemed an hour. Suddenly the broad muscles and lines of the countвҖҷs face began to twitch. The twitching increased, the handsome mouth was drawn to one side (only now did Pierre realize how near death his father was), and from that distorted mouth issued an indistinct, hoarse sound. Anna MikhГЎylovna looked attentively at the sick manвҖҷs eyes, trying to guess what he wanted; she pointed first to Pierre, then to some drink, then named Prince VasГӯli in an inquiring whisper, then pointed to the quilt. The eyes and face of the sick man showed impatience. He made an effort to look at the servant who stood constantly at the head of the bed. вҖңWants to turn on the other side,вҖқ whispered the servant, and got up to turn the countвҖҷs heavy body toward the wall. Pierre rose to help him. While the count was being turned over, one of his arms fell back helplessly and he made a fruitless effort to pull it forward. Whether he noticed the look of terror with which Pierre regarded that lifeless arm, or whether some other thought flitted across his dying brain, at any rate he glanced at the refractory arm, at PierreвҖҷs terror-stricken face, and again at the arm, and on his face a feeble, piteous smile appeared, quite out of keeping with his features, that seemed to deride his own helplessness. At sight of this smile Pierre felt an unexpected quivering in his breast and a tickling in his nose, and tears dimmed his eyes. The sick man was turned on to his side with his face to the wall. He sighed. вҖңHe is dozing,вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna, observing that one of the princesses was coming to take her turn at watching. вҖңLet us go.вҖқ Pierre went out. XXIV XXIV There was now no one in the reception room except Prince VasГӯli and the eldest princess, who were sitting under the portrait of Catherine the Great and talking eagerly. As soon as they saw Pierre and his companion they became silent, and Pierre thought he saw the princess hide something as she whispered: вҖңI canвҖҷt bear the sight of that woman.вҖқ вҖңCatiche has had tea served in the small drawing room,вҖқ said Prince VasГӯli to Anna MikhГЎylovna. вҖңGo and take something, my poor Anna MikhГЎylovna, or you will not hold out.вҖқ To Pierre he said nothing, merely giving his arm a sympathetic squeeze below the shoulder. Pierre went with Anna MikhГЎylovna into the small drawing room. вҖңThere is nothing so refreshing after a sleepless night as a cup of this delicious Russian tea,вҖқ Lorrain was saying with an air of restrained animation as he stood sipping tea from a delicate Chinese handleless cup before a table on which tea and a cold supper were laid in the small circular room. Around the table all who were at Count BezГәkhovвҖҷs house that night had gathered to fortify themselves. Pierre well remembered this small circular drawing room with its mirrors and little tables. During balls given at the house Pierre, who did not know how to dance, had liked sitting in this room to watch the ladies who, as they passed through in their ball dresses with diamonds and pearls on their bare shoulders, looked at themselves in the brilliantly lighted mirrors which repeated their reflections several times. Now this same room was dimly lighted by two candles. On one small table tea things and supper dishes stood in disorder, and in the middle of the night a motley throng of people sat there, not merrymaking, but somberly whispering, and betraying by every word and movement that they none of them forgot what was happening and what was about to happen in the bedroom. Pierre did not eat anything though he would very much have liked to. He looked inquiringly at his monitress and saw that she was again going on tiptoe to the reception room where they had left Prince VasГӯli and the eldest princess. Pierre concluded that this also was essential, and after a short interval followed her. Anna MikhГЎylovna was standing beside the princess, and they were both speaking in excited whispers. вҖңPermit me, Princess, to know what is necessary and what is not necessary,вҖқ said the younger of the two speakers, evidently in the same state of excitement as when she had slammed the door of her room. вҖңBut, my dear princess,вҖқ answered Anna MikhГЎylovna blandly but impressively, blocking the way to the bedroom and preventing the other from passing, вҖңwonвҖҷt this be too much for poor Uncle at a moment when he needs repose? Worldly conversation at a moment when his soul is already preparedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Prince VasГӯli was seated in an easy chair in his familiar attitude, with one leg crossed high above the other. His cheeks, which were so flabby that they looked heavier below, were twitching violently; but he wore the air of a man little concerned in what the two ladies were saying. вҖңCome, my dear Anna MikhГЎylovna, let Catiche do as she pleases. You know how fond the count is of her.вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt even know what is in this paper,вҖқ said the younger of the two ladies, addressing Prince VasГӯli and pointing to an inlaid portfolio she held in her hand. вҖңAll I know is that his real will is in his writing table, and this is a paper he has forgotten.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ She tried to pass Anna MikhГЎylovna, but the latter sprang so as to bar her path. вҖңI know, my dear, kind princess,вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna, seizing the portfolio so firmly that it was plain she would not let go easily. вҖңDear princess, I beg and implore you, have some pity on him! Je vous en conjureвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The princess did not reply. Their efforts in the struggle for the portfolio were the only sounds audible, but it was evident that if the princess did speak, her words would not be flattering to Anna MikhГЎylovna. Though the latter held on tenaciously, her voice lost none of its honeyed firmness and softness. вҖңPierre, my dear, come here. I think he will not be out of place in a family consultation; is it not so, Prince?вҖқ вҖңWhy donвҖҷt you speak, cousin?вҖқ suddenly shrieked the princess so loud that those in the drawing room heard her and were startled. вҖңWhy do you remain silent when heaven knows who permits herself to interfere, making a scene on the very threshold of a dying manвҖҷs room? Intriguer!вҖқ she hissed viciously, and tugged with all her might at the portfolio. But Anna MikhГЎylovna went forward a step or two to keep her hold on the portfolio, and changed her grip. Prince VasГӯli rose. вҖңOh!вҖқ said he with reproach and surprise, вҖңthis is absurd! Come, let go I tell you.вҖқ The princess let go. вҖңAnd you too!вҖқ But Anna MikhГЎylovna did not obey him. вҖңLet go, I tell you! I will take the responsibility. I myself will go and ask him, I!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ does that satisfy you?вҖқ вҖңBut, Prince,вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna, вҖңafter such a solemn sacrament, allow him a momentвҖҷs peace! Here, Pierre, tell them your opinion,вҖқ said she, turning to the young man who, having come quite close, was gazing with astonishment at the angry face of the princess which had lost all dignity, and at the twitching cheeks of Prince VasГӯli. вҖңRemember that you will answer for the consequences,вҖқ said Prince VasГӯli severely. вҖңYou donвҖҷt know what you are doing.вҖқ вҖңVile woman!вҖқ shouted the princess, darting unexpectedly at Anna MikhГЎylovna and snatching the portfolio from her. Prince VasГӯli bent his head and spread out his hands. At this moment that terrible door, which Pierre had watched so long and which had always opened so quietly, burst noisily open and banged against the wall, and the second of the three sisters rushed out wringing her hands. вҖңWhat are you doing!вҖқ she cried vehemently. вҖңHe is dying and you leave me alone with him!вҖқ Her sister dropped the portfolio. Anna MikhГЎylovna, stooping, quickly caught up the object of contention and ran into the bedroom. The eldest princess and Prince VasГӯli, recovering themselves, followed her. A few minutes later the eldest sister came out with a pale hard face, again biting her underlip. At sight of Pierre her expression showed an irrepressible hatred. вҖңYes, now you may be glad!вҖқ said she; вҖңthis is what you have been waiting for.вҖқ And bursting into tears she hid her face in her handkerchief and rushed from the room. Prince VasГӯli came next. He staggered to the sofa on which Pierre was sitting and dropped onto it, covering his face with his hand. Pierre noticed that he was pale and that his jaw quivered and shook as if in an ague. вҖңAh, my friend!вҖқ said he, taking Pierre by the elbow; and there was in his voice a sincerity and weakness Pierre had never observed in it before. вҖңHow often we sin, how much we deceive, and all for what? I am near sixty, dear friendвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I tooвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ All will end in death, all! Death is awfulвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and he burst into tears. Anna MikhГЎylovna came out last. She approached Pierre with slow, quiet steps. вҖңPierre!вҖқ she said. Pierre gave her an inquiring look. She kissed the young man on his forehead, wetting him with her tears. Then after a pause she said: вҖңHe is no more.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre looked at her over his spectacles. вҖңCome, I will go with you. Try to weep, nothing gives such relief as tears.вҖқ She led him into the dark drawing room and Pierre was glad no one could see his face. Anna MikhГЎylovna left him, and when she returned he was fast asleep with his head on his arm. In the morning Anna MikhГЎylovna said to Pierre: вҖңYes, my dear, this is a great loss for us all, not to speak of you. But God will support you: you are young, and are now, I hope, in command of an immense fortune. The will has not yet been opened. I know you well enough to be sure that this will not turn your head, but it imposes duties on you, and you must be a man.вҖқ Pierre was silent. вҖңPerhaps later on I may tell you, my dear boy, that if I had not been there, God only knows what would have happened! You know, Uncle promised me only the day before yesterday not to forget Boris. But he had no time. I hope, my dear friend, you will carry out your fatherвҖҷs wish?вҖқ Pierre understood nothing of all this and coloring shyly looked in silence at Princess Anna MikhГЎylovna. After her talk with Pierre, Anna MikhГЎylovna returned to the RostГіvsвҖҷ and went to bed. On waking in the morning she told the RostГіvs and all her acquaintances the details of Count BezГәkhovвҖҷs death. She said the count had died as she would herself wish to die, that his end was not only touching but edifying. As to the last meeting between father and son, it was so touching that she could not think of it without tears, and did not know which had behaved better during those awful momentsвҒ вҖ”the father who so remembered everything and everybody at last and had spoken such pathetic words to the son, or Pierre, whom it had been pitiful to see, so stricken was he with grief, though he tried hard to hide it in order not to sadden his dying father. вҖңIt is painful, but it does one good. It uplifts the soul to see such men as the old count and his worthy son,вҖқ said she. Of the behavior of the eldest princess and Prince VasГӯli she spoke disapprovingly, but in whispers and as a great secret. XXV XXV At Bald Hills, Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich BolkГіnskiвҖҷs estate, the arrival of young Prince AndrГ©y and his wife was daily expected, but this expectation did not upset the regular routine of life in the old princeвҖҷs household. General in Chief Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich (nicknamed in society, вҖңthe King of PrussiaвҖқ) ever since the Emperor Paul had exiled him to his country estate had lived there continuously with his daughter, Princess MГЎrya, and her companion, Mademoiselle Bourienne. Though in the new reign he was free to return to the capitals, he still continued to live in the country, remarking that anyone who wanted to see him could come the hundred miles from Moscow to Bald Hills, while he himself needed no one and nothing. He used to say that there are only two sources of human viceвҒ вҖ”idleness and superstition, and only two virtuesвҒ вҖ”activity and intelligence. He himself undertook his daughterвҖҷs education, and to develop these two cardinal virtues in her gave her lessons in algebra and geometry till she was twenty, and arranged her life so that her whole time was occupied. He was himself always occupied: writing his memoirs, solving problems in higher mathematics, turning snuffboxes on a lathe, working in the garden, or superintending the building that was always going on at his estate. As regularity is a prime condition facilitating activity, regularity in his household was carried to the highest point of exactitude. He always came to table under precisely the same conditions, and not only at the same hour but at the same minute. With those about him, from his daughter to his serfs, the prince was sharp and invariably exacting, so that without being a hard-hearted man he inspired such fear and respect as few hard-hearted men would have aroused.
