Although he was in retirement and had now no influence in political affairs, every high official appointed to the province in which the princeвҖҷs estate lay considered it his duty to visit him and waited in the lofty antechamber just as the architect, gardener, or Princess MГЎrya did, till the prince appeared punctually to the appointed hour. Everyone sitting in this antechamber experienced the same feeling of respect and even fear when the enormously high study door opened and showed the figure of a rather small old man, with powdered wig, small withered hands, and bushy gray eyebrows which, when he frowned, sometimes hid the gleam of his shrewd, youthfully glittering eyes. On the morning of the day that the young couple were to arrive, Princess MГЎrya entered the antechamber as usual at the time appointed for the morning greeting, crossing herself with trepidation and repeating a silent prayer. Every morning she came in like that, and every morning prayed that the daily interview might pass off well. An old powdered manservant who was sitting in the antechamber rose quietly and said in a whisper: вҖңPlease walk in.вҖқ Through the door came the regular hum of a lathe. The princess timidly opened the door which moved noiselessly and easily. She paused at the entrance. The prince was working at the lathe and after glancing round continued his work. The enormous study was full of things evidently in constant use. The large table covered with books and plans, the tall glass-fronted bookcases with keys in the locks, the high desk for writing while standing up, on which lay an open exercise book, and the lathe with tools laid ready to hand and shavings scattered aroundвҒ вҖ”all indicated continuous, varied, and orderly activity. The motion of the small foot shod in a Tartar boot embroidered with silver, and the firm pressure of the lean sinewy hand, showed that the prince still possessed the tenacious endurance and vigor of hardy old age. After a few more turns of the lathe he removed his foot from the pedal, wiped his chisel, dropped it into a leather pouch attached to the lathe, and, approaching the table, summoned his daughter. He never gave his children a blessing, so he simply held out his bristly cheek (as yet unshaven) and, regarding her tenderly and attentively, said severely: вҖңQuite well? All right then, sit down.вҖқ He took the exercise book containing lessons in geometry written by himself and drew up a chair with his foot. вҖңFor tomorrow!вҖқ said he, quickly finding the page and making a scratch from one paragraph to another with his hard nail. The princess bent over the exercise book on the table. вҖңWait a bit, hereвҖҷs a letter for you,вҖқ said the old man suddenly, taking a letter addressed in a womanвҖҷs hand from a bag hanging above the table, onto which he threw it. At the sight of the letter red patches showed themselves on the princessвҖҷ face. She took it quickly and bent her head over it. вҖңFrom HГ©loГҜse?вҖқ asked the prince with a cold smile that showed his still sound, yellowish teeth. вҖңYes, itвҖҷs from Julie,вҖқ replied the princess with a timid glance and a timid smile. вҖңIвҖҷll let two more letters pass, but the third IвҖҷll read,вҖқ said the prince sternly; вҖңIвҖҷm afraid you write much nonsense. IвҖҷll read the third!вҖқ вҖңRead this if you like, Father,вҖқ said the princess, blushing still more and holding out the letter. вҖңThe third, I said the third!вҖқ cried the prince abruptly, pushing the letter away, and leaning his elbows on the table he drew toward him the exercise book containing geometrical figures. вҖңWell, madam,вҖқ he began, stooping over the book close to his daughter and placing an arm on the back of the chair on which she sat, so that she felt herself surrounded on all sides by the acrid scent of old age and tobacco, which she had known so long. вҖңNow, madam, these triangles are equal; please note that the angle ABCвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The princess looked in a scared way at her fatherвҖҷs eyes glittering close to her; the red patches on her face came and went, and it was plain that she understood nothing and was so frightened that her fear would prevent her understanding any of her fatherвҖҷs further explanations, however clear they might be. Whether it was the teacherвҖҷs fault or the pupilвҖҷs, this same thing happened every day: the princessвҖҷ eyes grew dim, she could not see and could not hear anything, but was only conscious of her stern fatherвҖҷs withered face close to her, of his breath and the smell of him, and could think only of how to get away quickly to her own room to make out the problem in peace. The old man was beside himself: moved the chair on which he was sitting noisily backward and forward, made efforts to control himself and not become vehement, but almost always did become vehement, scolded, and sometimes flung the exercise book away. The princess gave a wrong answer. вҖңWell now, isnвҖҷt she a fool!вҖқ shouted the prince, pushing the book aside and turning sharply away; but rising immediately, he paced up and down, lightly touched his daughterвҖҷs hair and sat down again. He drew up his chair, and continued to explain. вҖңThis wonвҖҷt do, Princess; it wonвҖҷt do,вҖқ said he, when Princess MГЎrya, having taken and closed the exercise book with the next dayвҖҷs lesson, was about to leave: вҖңMathematics are most important, madam! I donвҖҷt want to have you like our silly ladies. Get used to it and youвҖҷll like it,вҖқ and he patted her cheek. вҖңIt will drive all the nonsense out of your head.вҖқ She turned to go, but he stopped her with a gesture and took an uncut book from the high desk. вҖңHere is some sort of Key to the Mysteries that your HГ©loГҜse has sent you. Religious! I donвҖҷt interfere with anyoneвҖҷs beliefвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I have looked at it. Take it. Well, now go. Go.вҖқ He patted her on the shoulder and himself closed the door after her. Princess MГЎrya went back to her room with the sad, scared expression that rarely left her and which made her plain, sickly face yet plainer. She sat down at her writing table, on which stood miniature portraits and which was littered with books and papers. The princess was as untidy as her father was tidy. She put down the geometry book and eagerly broke the seal of her letter. It was from her most intimate friend from childhood; that same Julie KarГЎgina who had been at the RostГіvsвҖҷ name-day party. Julie wrote in French: Dear and precious Friend, How terrible and frightful a thing is separation! Though I tell myself that half my life and half my happiness are wrapped up in you, and that in spite of the distance separating us our hearts are united by indissoluble bonds, my heart rebels against fate and in spite of the pleasures and distractions around me I cannot overcome a certain secret sorrow that has been in my heart ever since we parted. Why are we not together as we were last summer, in your big study, on the blue sofa, the confidential sofa? Why cannot I now, as three months ago, draw fresh moral strength from your look, so gentle, calm, and penetrating, a look I loved so well and seem to see before me as I write? Having read thus far, Princess MГЎrya sighed and glanced into the mirror which stood on her right. It reflected a weak, ungraceful figure and thin face. Her eyes, always sad, now looked with particular hopelessness at her reflection in the glass. вҖңShe flatters me,вҖқ thought the princess, turning away and continuing to read. But Julie did not flatter her friend, the princessвҖҷ eyesвҒ вҖ”large, deep and luminous (it seemed as if at times there radiated from them shafts of warm light)вҒ вҖ”were so beautiful that very often in spite of the plainness of her face they gave her an attraction more powerful than that of beauty. But the princess never saw the beautiful expression of her own eyesвҒ вҖ”the look they had when she was not thinking of herself. As with everyone, her face assumed a forced unnatural expression as soon as she looked in a glass. She went on reading: All Moscow talks of nothing but war. One of my two brothers is already abroad, the other is with the Guards, who are starting on their march to the frontier. Our dear Emperor has left Petersburg and it is thought intends to expose his precious person to the chances of war. God grant that the Corsican monster who is destroying the peace of Europe may be overthrown by the angel whom it has pleased the Almighty, in His goodness, to give us as sovereign! To say nothing of my brothers, this war has deprived me of one of the associations nearest my heart. I mean young Nicolas Rostoff, who with his enthusiasm could not bear to remain inactive and has left the university to join the army. I will confess to you, dear Marie, that in spite of his extreme youth his departure for the army was a great grief to me. This young man, of whom I spoke to you last summer, is so noble-minded and full of that real youthfulness which one seldom finds nowadays among our old men of twenty and, particularly, he is so frank and has so much heart. He is so pure and poetic that my relations with him, transient as they were, have been one of the sweetest comforts to my poor heart, which has already suffered so much. Someday I will tell you about our parting and all that was said then. That is still too fresh. Ah, dear friend, you are happy not to know these poignant joys and sorrows. You are fortunate, for the latter are generally the stronger! I know very well that Count Nicolas is too young ever to be more to me than a friend, but this sweet friendship, this poetic and pure intimacy, were what my heart needed. But enough of this! The chief news, about which all Moscow gossips, is the death of old Count BezГәkhov, and his inheritance. Fancy! The three princesses have received very little, Prince Basile nothing, and it is Monsieur Pierre who has inherited all the property and has besides been recognized as legitimate; so that he is now Count BezГәkhov and possessor of the finest fortune in Russia. It is rumored that Prince Basile played a very despicable part in this affair and that he returned to Petersburg quite crestfallen. I confess I understand very little about all these matters of wills and inheritance; but I do know that since this young man, whom we all used to know as plain Monsieur Pierre, has become Count BezГәkhov and the owner of one of the largest fortunes in Russia, I am much amused to watch the change in the tone and manners of the mammas burdened by marriageable daughters, and of the young ladies themselves, toward him, though, between you and me, he always seemed to me a poor sort of fellow. As for the past two years people have amused themselves by finding husbands for me (most of whom I donвҖҷt even know), the matchmaking chronicles of Moscow now speak of me as the future Countess BezГәkhova. But you will understand that I have no desire for the post. Apropos of marriages: do you know that a while ago that universal auntie Anna MikhГЎylovna told me, under the seal of strict secrecy, of a plan of marriage for you. It is neither more nor less than with Prince BasileвҖҷs son Anatole, whom they wish to reform by marrying him to someone rich and distinguГ©e, and it is on you that his relationsвҖҷ choice has fallen. I donвҖҷt know what you will think of it, but I consider it my duty to let you know of it. He is said to be very handsome and a terrible scapegrace. That is all I have been able to find out about him. But enough of gossip. I am at the end of my second sheet of paper, and Mamma has sent for me to go and dine at the AprГЎksinsвҖҷ. Read the mystical book I am sending you; it has an enormous success here. Though there are things in it difficult for the feeble human mind to grasp, it is an admirable book which calms and elevates the soul. Adieu! Give my respects to monsieur your father and my compliments to Mademoiselle Bourienne. I embrace you as I love you. Julie P.S. Let me have news of your brother and his charming little wife. The princess pondered awhile with a thoughtful smile and her luminous eyes lit up so that her face was entirely transformed. Then she suddenly rose and with her heavy tread went up to the table. She took a sheet of paper and her hand moved rapidly over it. This is the reply she wrote, also in French: Dear and precious Friend, Your letter of the 13th has given me great delight. So you still love me, my romantic Julie? Separation, of which you say so much that is bad, does not seem to have had its usual effect on you. You complain of our separation. What then should I say, if I dared complain, I who am deprived of all who are dear to me? Ah, if we had not religion to console us life would be very sad. Why do you suppose that I should look severely on your affection for that young man? On such matters I am only severe with myself. I understand such feelings in others, and if never having felt them I cannot approve of them, neither do I condemn them. Only it seems to me that Christian love, love of oneвҖҷs neighbor, love of oneвҖҷs enemy, is worthier, sweeter, and better than the feelings which the beautiful eyes of a young man can inspire in a romantic and loving young girl like yourself. The news of Count BezГәkhovвҖҷs death reached us before your letter and my father was much affected by it. He says the count was the last representative but one of the great century, and that it is his own turn now, but that he will do all he can to let his turn come as late as possible. God preserve us from that terrible misfortune! I cannot agree with you about Pierre, whom I knew as a child. He always seemed to me to have an excellent heart, and that is the quality I value most in people. As to his inheritance and the part played by Prince Basile, it is very sad for both. Ah, my dear friend, our divine SaviourвҖҷs words, that it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter the Kingdom of God, are terribly true. I pity Prince Basile but am still more sorry for Pierre. So young, and burdened with such richesвҒ вҖ”to what temptations he will be exposed! If I were asked what I desire most on earth, it would be to be poorer than the poorest beggar. A thousand thanks, dear friend, for the volume you have sent me and which has such success in Moscow. Yet since you tell me that among some good things it contains others which our weak human understanding cannot grasp, it seems to me rather useless to spend time in reading what is unintelligible and can therefore bear no fruit. I never could understand the fondness some people have for confusing their minds by dwelling on mystical books that merely awaken their doubts and excite their imagination, giving them a bent for exaggeration quite contrary to Christian simplicity. Let us rather read the Epistles and Gospels. Let us not seek to penetrate what mysteries they contain; for how can we, miserable sinners that we are, know the terrible and holy secrets of Providence while we remain in this flesh which forms an impenetrable veil between us and the Eternal? Let us rather confine ourselves to studying those sublime rules which our divine Saviour has left for our guidance here below. Let us try to conform to them and follow them, and let us be persuaded that the less we let our feeble human minds roam, the better we shall please God, who rejects all knowledge that does not come from Him; and the less we seek to fathom what He has been pleased to conceal from us, the sooner will He vouchsafe its revelation to us through His divine Spirit. My father has not spoken to me of a suitor, but has only told me that he has received a letter and is expecting a visit from Prince Basile. In regard to this project of marriage for me, I will tell you, dear sweet friend, that I look on marriage as a divine institution to which we must conform. However painful it may be to me, should the Almighty lay the duties of wife and mother upon me I shall try to perform them as faithfully as I can, without disquieting myself by examining my feelings toward him whom He may give me for husband. I have had a letter from my brother, who announces his speedy arrival at Bald Hills with his wife. This pleasure will be but a brief one, however, for he will leave us again to take part in this unhappy war into which we have been drawn, God knows how or why. Not only where you areвҒ вҖ”at the heart of affairs and of the worldвҒ вҖ”is the talk all of war, even here amid fieldwork and the calm of natureвҒ вҖ”which townsfolk consider characteristic of the countryвҒ вҖ”rumors of war are heard and painfully felt. My father talks of nothing but marches and countermarches, things of which I understand nothing; and the day before yesterday during my daily walk through the village I witnessed a heartrending scene.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ It was a convoy of conscripts enrolled from our people and starting to join the army. You should have seen the state of the mothers, wives, and children of the men who were going and should have heard the sobs. It seems as though mankind has forgotten the laws of its divine Saviour, Who preached love and forgiveness of injuriesвҒ вҖ”and that men attribute the greatest merit to skill in killing one another. Adieu, dear and kind friend; may our divine Saviour and His most Holy Mother keep you in their holy and all-powerful care! Marie вҖңAh, you are sending off a letter, Princess? I have already dispatched mine. I have written to my poor mother,вҖқ said the smiling Mademoiselle Bourienne rapidly, in her pleasant mellow tones and with guttural rвҖҷs. She brought into Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs strenuous, mournful, and gloomy world a quite different atmosphere, careless, lighthearted, and self-satisfied. вҖңPrincess, I must warn you,вҖқ she added, lowering her voice and evidently listening to herself with pleasure, and speaking with exaggerated grasseyement, вҖңthe prince has been scolding Michel Ivanoff. He is in a very bad humor, very morose. Be prepared.вҖқ вҖңAh, dear friend,вҖқ replied Princess MГЎrya, вҖңI have asked you never to warn me of the humor my father is in. I do not allow myself to judge him and would not have others do so.вҖқ The princess glanced at her watch and, seeing that she was five minutes late in starting her practice on the clavichord, went into the sitting room with a look of alarm. Between twelve and two oвҖҷclock, as the day was mapped out, the prince rested and the princess played the clavichord. XXVI XXVI The gray-haired valet was sitting drowsily listening to the snoring of the prince, who was in his large study. From the far side of the house through the closed doors came the sound of difficult passagesвҒ вҖ”twenty times repeatedвҒ вҖ”of a sonata by Dussek. Just then a closed carriage and another with a hood drove up to the porch. Prince AndrГ©y got out of the carriage, helped his little wife to alight, and let her pass into the house before him. Old TГӯkhon, wearing a wig, put his head out of the door of the antechamber, reported in a whisper that the prince was sleeping, and hastily closed the door. TГӯkhon knew that neither the sonвҖҷs arrival nor any other unusual event must be allowed to disturb the appointed order of the day. Prince AndrГ©y apparently knew this as well as TГӯkhon; he looked at his watch as if to ascertain whether his fatherвҖҷs habits had changed since he was at home last, and, having assured himself that they had not, he turned to his wife. вҖңHe will get up in twenty minutes. Let us go across to MГЎryaвҖҷs room,вҖқ he said. The little princess had grown stouter during this time, but her eyes and her short, downy, smiling lip lifted when she began to speak just as merrily and prettily as ever. вҖңWhy, this is a palace!вҖқ she said to her husband, looking around with the expression with which people compliment their host at a ball. вҖңLetвҖҷs come, quick, quick!вҖқ And with a glance round, she smiled at TГӯkhon, at her husband, and at the footman who accompanied them. вҖңIs that Marie practicing? LetвҖҷs go quietly and take her by surprise.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y followed her with a courteous but sad expression. вҖңYouвҖҷve grown older, TГӯkhon,вҖқ he said in passing to the old man, who kissed his hand. Before they reached the room from which the sounds of the clavichord came, the pretty, fair-haired Frenchwoman, Mademoiselle Bourienne, rushed out apparently beside herself with delight. вҖңAh! what joy for the princess!вҖқ exclaimed she: вҖңAt last! I must let her know.вҖқ вҖңNo, no, please notвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You are Mademoiselle Bourienne,вҖқ said the little princess, kissing her. вҖңI know you already through my sister-in-lawвҖҷs friendship for you. She was not expecting us?вҖқ They went up to the door of the sitting room from which came the sound of the oft-repeated passage of the sonata. Prince AndrГ©y stopped and made a grimace, as if expecting something unpleasant. The little princess entered the room. The passage broke off in the middle, a cry was heard, then Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs heavy tread and the sound of kissing. When Prince AndrГ©y went in the two princesses, who had only met once before for a short time at his wedding, were in each otherвҖҷs arms warmly pressing their lips to whatever place they happened to touch. Mademoiselle Bourienne stood near them pressing her hand to her heart, with a beatific smile and obviously equally ready to cry or to laugh. Prince AndrГ©y shrugged his shoulders and frowned, as lovers of music do when they hear a false note. The two women let go of one another, and then, as if afraid of being too late, seized each otherвҖҷs hands, kissing them and pulling them away, and again began kissing each other on the face, and then to Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs surprise both began to cry and kissed again. Mademoiselle Bourienne also began to cry. Prince AndrГ©y evidently felt ill at ease, but to the two women it seemed quite natural that they should cry, and apparently it never entered their heads that it could have been otherwise at this meeting. вҖңAh! my dear!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Ah! Marie!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ they suddenly exclaimed, and then laughed. вҖңI dreamed last nightвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқвҒ вҖ”вҖңYou were not expecting us?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAh! Marie, you have got thinner?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAnd you have grown stouter!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI knew the princess at once,вҖқ put in Mademoiselle Bourienne. вҖңAnd I had no idea!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ exclaimed Princess MГЎrya. вҖңAh, AndrГ©, I did not see you.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y and his sister, hand in hand, kissed one another, and he told her she was still the same crybaby as ever. Princess MГЎrya had turned toward her brother, and through her tears the loving, warm, gentle look of her large luminous eyes, very beautiful at that moment, rested on Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs face. The little princess talked incessantly, her short, downy upper lip continually and rapidly touching her rosy nether lip when necessary and drawing up again next moment when her face broke into a smile of glittering teeth and sparkling eyes. She told of an accident they had had on the SpГЎsski Hill which might have been serious for her in her condition, and immediately after that informed them that she had left all her clothes in Petersburg and that heaven knew what she would have to dress in here; and that AndrГ©y had quite changed, and that Kitty OdГҪntsova had married an old man, and that there was a suitor for MГЎrya, a real one, but that they would talk of that later. Princess MГЎrya was still looking silently at her brother and her beautiful eyes were full of love and sadness. It was plain that she was following a train of thought independent of her sister-in-lawвҖҷs words. In the midst of a description of the last Petersburg fГӘte she addressed her brother: вҖңSo you are really going to the war, AndrГ©?вҖқ she said sighing. Liza sighed too. вҖңYes, and even tomorrow,вҖқ replied her brother. вҖңHe is leaving me here, God knows why, when he might have had promotionвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Princess MГЎrya did not listen to the end, but continuing her train of thought turned to her sister-in-law with a tender glance at her figure. вҖңIs it certain?вҖқ she said. The face of the little princess changed. She sighed and said: вҖңYes, quite certain. Ah! it is very dreadfulвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Her lip descended. She brought her face close to her sister-in-lawвҖҷs and unexpectedly again began to cry. вҖңShe needs rest,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y with a frown. вҖңDonвҖҷt you, Liza? Take her to your room and IвҖҷll go to Father. How is he? Just the same?вҖқ вҖңYes, just the same. Though I donвҖҷt know what your opinion will be,вҖқ answered the princess joyfully. вҖңAnd are the hours the same? And the walks in the avenues? And the lathe?вҖқ asked Prince AndrГ©y with a scarcely perceptible smile which showed that, in spite of all his love and respect for his father, he was aware of his weaknesses. вҖңThe hours are the same, and the lathe, and also the mathematics and my geometry lessons,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya gleefully, as if her lessons in geometry were among the greatest delights of her life. When the twenty minutes had elapsed and the time had come for the old prince to get up, TГӯkhon came to call the young prince to his father. The old man made a departure from his usual routine in honor of his sonвҖҷs arrival: he gave orders to admit him to his apartments while he dressed for dinner. The old prince always dressed in old-fashioned style, wearing an antique coat and powdered hair; and when Prince AndrГ©y entered his fatherвҖҷs dressing room (not with the contemptuous look and manner he wore in drawing rooms, but with the animated face with which he talked to Pierre), the old man was sitting on a large leather-covered chair, wrapped in a powdering mantle, entrusting his head to TГӯkhon. вҖңAh! hereвҖҷs the warrior! Wants to vanquish Bonaparte?вҖқ said the old man, shaking his powdered head as much as the tail, which TГӯkhon was holding fast to plait, would allow. вҖңYou at least must tackle him properly, or else if he goes on like this heвҖҷll soon have us, too, for his subjects! How are you?вҖқ And he held out his cheek. The old man was in a good temper after his nap before dinner. (He used to say that a nap вҖңafter dinner was silverвҒ вҖ”before dinner, golden.вҖқ) He cast happy, sidelong glances at his son from under his thick, bushy eyebrows. Prince AndrГ©y went up and kissed his father on the spot indicated to him. He made no reply on his fatherвҖҷs favorite topicвҒ вҖ”making fun of the military men of the day, and more particularly of Bonaparte. вҖңYes, Father, I have come to you and brought my wife who is pregnant,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, following every movement of his fatherвҖҷs face with an eager and respectful look. вҖңHow is your health?вҖқ вҖңOnly fools and rakes fall ill, my boy. You know me: I am busy from morning till night and abstemious, so of course I am well.вҖқ вҖңThank God,вҖқ said his son smiling. вҖңGod has nothing to do with it! Well, go on,вҖқ he continued, returning to his hobby; вҖңtell me how the Germans have taught you to fight Bonaparte by this new science you call вҖҳstrategy.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ Prince AndrГ©y smiled. вҖңGive me time to collect my wits, Father,вҖқ said he, with a smile that showed that his fatherвҖҷs foibles did not prevent his son from loving and honoring him. вҖңWhy, I have not yet had time to settle down!вҖқ вҖңNonsense, nonsense!вҖқ cried the old man, shaking his pigtail to see whether it was firmly plaited, and grasping his by the hand. вҖңThe house for your wife is ready. Princess MГЎrya will take her there and show her over, and theyвҖҷll talk nineteen to the dozen. ThatвҖҷs their womanвҖҷs way! I am glad to have her. Sit down and talk. About MikhelsonвҖҷs army I understandвҒ вҖ”TolstГіyвҖҷs tooвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ a simultaneous expedition.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But whatвҖҷs the southern army to do? Prussia is neutralвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I know that. What about Austria?вҖқ said he, rising from his chair and pacing up and down the room followed by TГӯkhon, who ran after him, handing him different articles of clothing. вҖңWhat of Sweden? How will they cross Pomerania?вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y, seeing that his father insisted, beganвҒ вҖ”at first reluctantly, but gradually with more and more animation, and from habit changing unconsciously from Russian to French as he went onвҒ вҖ”to explain the plan of operation for the coming campaign. He explained how an army, ninety thousand strong, was to threaten Prussia so as to bring her out of her neutrality and draw her into the war; how part of that army was to join some Swedish forces at Stralsund; how two hundred and twenty thousand Austrians, with a hundred thousand Russians, were to operate in Italy and on the Rhine; how fifty thousand Russians and as many English were to land at Naples, and how a total force of five hundred thousand men was to attack the French from different sides. The old prince did not evince the least interest during this explanation, but as if he were not listening to it continued to dress while walking about, and three times unexpectedly interrupted. Once he stopped it by shouting: вҖңThe white one, the white one!вҖқ This meant that TГӯkhon was not handing him the waistcoat he wanted. Another time he interrupted, saying: вҖңAnd will she soon be confined?вҖқ and shaking his head reproachfully said: вҖңThatвҖҷs bad! Go on, go on.вҖқ The third interruption came when Prince AndrГ©y was finishing his description. The old man began to sing, in the cracked voice of old age: вҖңMalbrook sвҖҷen va-t-en guerre. Dieu sait quand reviendra.вҖқ15 His son only smiled. вҖңI donвҖҷt say itвҖҷs a plan I approve of,вҖқ said the son; вҖңI am only telling you what it is. Napoleon has also formed his plan by now, not worse than this one.вҖқ вҖңWell, youвҖҷve told me nothing new,вҖқ and the old man repeated, meditatively and rapidly: вҖңDieu sait quand reviendra. Go to the dining room.вҖқ XXVII XXVII At the appointed hour the prince, powdered and shaven, entered the dining room where his daughter-in-law, Princess MГЎrya, and Mademoiselle Bourienne were already awaiting him together with his architect, who by a strange caprice of his employerвҖҷs was admitted to table though the position of that insignificant individual was such as could certainly not have caused him to expect that honor. The prince, who generally kept very strictly to social distinctions and rarely admitted even important government officials to his table, had unexpectedly selected MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich (who always went into a corner to blow his nose on his checked handkerchief) to illustrate the theory that all men are equals, and had more than once impressed on his daughter that MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich was вҖңnot a whit worse than you or I.вҖқ At dinner the prince usually spoke to the taciturn MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich more often than to anyone else. In the dining room, which like all the rooms in the house was exceedingly lofty, the members of the household and the footmenвҒ вҖ”one behind each chairвҒ вҖ”stood waiting for the prince to enter. The head butler, napkin on arm, was scanning the setting of the table, making signs to the footmen, and anxiously glancing from the clock to the door by which the prince was to enter. Prince AndrГ©y was looking at a large gilt frame, new to him, containing the genealogical tree of the Princes BolkГіnski, opposite which hung another such frame with a badly painted portrait (evidently by the hand of the artist belonging to the estate) of a ruling prince, in a crownвҒ вҖ”an alleged descendant of RГәrik and ancestor of the BolkГіnskis. Prince AndrГ©y, looking again at that genealogical tree, shook his head, laughing as a man laughs who looks at a portrait so characteristic of the original as to be amusing. вҖңHow thoroughly like him that is!вҖқ he said to Princess MГЎrya, who had come up to him. Princess MГЎrya looked at her brother in surprise. She did not understand what he was laughing at. Everything her father did inspired her with reverence and was beyond question. вҖңEveryone has his AchillesвҖҷ heel,вҖқ continued Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңFancy, with his powerful mind, indulging in such nonsense!вҖқ Princess MГЎrya could not understand the boldness of her brotherвҖҷs criticism and was about to reply, when the expected footsteps were heard coming from the study. The prince walked in quickly and jauntily as was his wont, as if intentionally contrasting the briskness of his manners with the strict formality of his house. At that moment the great clock struck two and another with a shrill tone joined in from the drawing room. The prince stood still; his lively glittering eyes from under their thick, bushy eyebrows sternly scanned all present and rested on the little princess. She felt, as courtiers do when the Tsar enters, the sensation of fear and respect which the old man inspired in all around him. He stroked her hair and then patted her awkwardly on the back of her neck. вҖңIвҖҷm glad, glad, to see you,вҖқ he said, looking attentively into her eyes, and then quickly went to his place and sat down. вҖңSit down, sit down! Sit down, MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich!вҖқ He indicated a place beside him to his daughter-in-law. A footman moved the chair for her. вҖңHo, ho!вҖқ said the old man, casting his eyes on her rounded figure. вҖңYouвҖҷve been in a hurry. ThatвҖҷs bad!вҖқ He laughed in his usual dry, cold, unpleasant way, with his lips only and not with his eyes. вҖңYou must walk, walk as much as possible, as much as possible,вҖқ he said. The little princess did not, or did not wish to, hear his words. She was silent and seemed confused. The prince asked her about her father, and she began to smile and talk. He asked about mutual acquaintances, and she became still more animated and chattered away giving him greetings from various people and retelling the town gossip. вҖңCountess AprГЎksina, poor thing, has lost her husband and she has cried her eyes out,вҖқ she said, growing more and more lively. As she became animated the prince looked at her more and more sternly, and suddenly, as if he had studied her sufficiently and had formed a definite idea of her, he turned away and addressed MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich. вҖңWell, MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich, our Buonaparte will be having a bad time of it. Prince AndrГ©yвҖқ (he always spoke thus of his son) вҖңhas been telling me what forces are being collected against him! While you and I never thought much of him.вҖқ MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich did not at all know when вҖңyou and IвҖқ had said such things about Bonaparte, but understanding that he was wanted as a peg on which to hang the princeвҖҷs favorite topic, he looked inquiringly at the young prince, wondering what would follow. вҖңHe is a great tactician!вҖқ said the prince to his son, pointing to the architect. And the conversation again turned on the war, on Bonaparte, and the generals and statesmen of the day. The old prince seemed convinced not only that all the men of the day were mere babies who did not know the A.B.C. of war or of politics, and that Bonaparte was an insignificant little Frenchy, successful only because there were no longer any PotГ«mkins or SuvГіrovs left to oppose him; but he was also convinced that there were no political difficulties in Europe and no real war, but only a sort of puppet show at which the men of the day were playing, pretending to do something real. Prince AndrГ©y gaily bore with his fatherвҖҷs ridicule of the new men, and drew him on and listened to him with evident pleasure. вҖңThe past always seems good,вҖқ said he, вҖңbut did not SuvГіrov himself fall into a trap Moreau set him, and from which he did not know how to escape?вҖқ вҖңWho told you that? Who?вҖқ cried the prince. вҖңSuvГіrov!вҖқ And he jerked away his plate, which TГӯkhon briskly caught. вҖңSuvГіrov!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Consider, Prince AndrГ©y. TwoвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Frederick and SuvГіrov; Moreau!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Moreau would have been a prisoner if SuvГіrov had had a free hand; but he had the Hofs-kriegs-wurst-schnapps-Rath on his hands. It would have puzzled the devil himself! When you get there youвҖҷll find out what those Hofs-kriegs-wurst-Raths are! SuvГіrov couldnвҖҷt manage them so what chance has MikhГЎil KutГәzov? No, my dear boy,вҖқ he continued, вҖңyou and your generals wonвҖҷt get on against Bonaparte; youвҖҷll have to call in the French, so that birds of a feather may fight together. The German, Pahlen, has been sent to New York in America, to fetch the Frenchman, Moreau,вҖқ he said, alluding to the invitation made that year to Moreau to enter the Russian service.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңWonderful!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Were the PotГ«mkins, SuvГіrovs, and OrlГіvs Germans? No, lad, either you fellows have all lost your wits, or I have outlived mine. May God help you, but weвҖҷll see what will happen. Bonaparte has become a great commander among them! Hm!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt at all say that all the plans are good,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңI am only surprised at your opinion of Bonaparte. You may laugh as much as you like, but all the same Bonaparte is a great general!вҖқ вҖңMikhГЎil IvГЎnovich!вҖқ cried the old prince to the architect who, busy with his roast meat, hoped he had been forgotten: вҖңDidnвҖҷt I tell you Bonaparte was a great tactician? Here, he says the same thing.вҖқ вҖңTo be sure, your excellency,вҖқ replied the architect. The prince again laughed his frigid laugh. вҖңBonaparte was born with a silver spoon in his mouth. He has got splendid soldiers. Besides he began by attacking Germans. And only idlers have failed to beat the Germans. Since the world began everybody has beaten the Germans. They beat no oneвҒ вҖ”except one another. He made his reputation fighting them.вҖқ And the prince began explaining all the blunders which, according to him, Bonaparte had made in his campaigns and even in politics. His son made no rejoinder, but it was evident that whatever arguments were presented he was as little able as his father to change his opinion. He listened, refraining from a reply, and involuntarily wondered how this old man, living alone in the country for so many years, could know and discuss so minutely and acutely all the recent European military and political events. вҖңYou think IвҖҷm an old man and donвҖҷt understand the present state of affairs?вҖқ concluded his father. вҖңBut it troubles me. I donвҖҷt sleep at night. Come now, where has this great commander of yours shown his skill?вҖқ he concluded. вҖңThat would take too long to tell,вҖқ answered the son. вҖңWell, then go off to your Buonaparte! Mademoiselle Bourienne, hereвҖҷs another admirer of that powder-monkey emperor of yours,вҖқ he exclaimed in excellent French. вҖңYou know, Prince, I am not a Bonapartist!вҖқ вҖңDieu sait quand reviendra,вҖқ hummed the prince out of tune and, with a laugh still more so, he quitted the table. The little princess during the whole discussion and the rest of the dinner sat silent, glancing with a frightened look now at her father-in-law and now at Princess MГЎrya. When they left the table she took her sister-in-lawвҖҷs arm and drew her into another room. вҖңWhat a clever man your father is,вҖқ said she; вҖңperhaps that is why I am afraid of him.вҖқ вҖңOh, he is so kind!вҖқ answered Princess MГЎrya. XXVIII XXVIII Prince AndrГ©y was to leave next evening. The old prince, not altering his routine, retired as usual after dinner. The little princess was in her sister-in-lawвҖҷs room. Prince AndrГ©y in a traveling coat without epaulettes had been packing with his valet in the rooms assigned to him. After inspecting the carriage himself and seeing the trunks put in, he ordered the horses to be harnessed. Only those things he always kept with him remained in his room; a small box, a large canteen fitted with silver plate, two Turkish pistols and a saberвҒ вҖ”a present from his father who had brought it from the siege of OchГЎkov. All these traveling effects of Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs were in very good order: new, clean, and in cloth covers carefully tied with tapes. When starting on a journey or changing their mode of life, men capable of reflection are generally in a serious frame of mind. At such moments one reviews the past and plans for the future. Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs face looked very thoughtful and tender. With his hands behind him he paced briskly from corner to corner of the room, looking straight before him and thoughtfully shaking his head. Did he fear going to the war, or was he sad at leaving his wife?вҒ вҖ”perhaps both, but evidently he did not wish to be seen in that mood, for hearing footsteps in the passage he hurriedly unclasped his hands, stopped at a table as if tying the cover of the small box, and assumed his usual tranquil and impenetrable expression. It was the heavy tread of Princess MГЎrya that he heard. вҖңI hear you have given orders to harness,вҖқ she cried, panting (she had apparently been running), вҖңand I did so wish to have another talk with you alone! God knows how long we may again be parted. You are not angry with me for coming? You have changed so, AndrГәsha,вҖқ she added, as if to explain such a question. She smiled as she uttered his pet name, вҖңAndrГәsha.вҖқ It was obviously strange to her to think that this stern handsome man should be AndrГәshaвҒ вҖ”the slender mischievous boy who had been her playfellow in childhood. вҖңAnd where is Liza?вҖқ he asked, answering her question only by a smile. вҖңShe was so tired that she has fallen asleep on the sofa in my room. Oh, AndrГ©! What a treasure of a wife you have,вҖқ said she, sitting down on the sofa, facing her brother. вҖңShe is quite a child: such a dear, merry child. I have grown so fond of her.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y was silent, but the princess noticed the ironical and contemptuous look that showed itself on his face. вҖңOne must be indulgent to little weaknesses; who is free from them, AndrГ©? DonвҖҷt forget that she has grown up and been educated in society, and so her position now is not a rosy one. We should enter into everyoneвҖҷs situation. Tout comprendre, cвҖҷest tout pardonner.16 Think what it must be for her, poor thing, after what she has been used to, to be parted from her husband and be left alone in the country, in her condition! ItвҖҷs very hard.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y smiled as he looked at his sister, as we smile at those we think we thoroughly understand. вҖңYou live in the country and donвҖҷt think the life terrible,вҖқ he replied. вҖңIвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ thatвҖҷs different. Why speak of me? I donвҖҷt want any other life, and canвҖҷt, for I know no other. But think, AndrГ©: for a young society woman to be buried in the country during the best years of her life, all aloneвҒ вҖ”for Papa is always busy, and IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ well, you know what poor resources I have for entertaining a woman used to the best society. There is only Mademoiselle Bourienne.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt like your Mademoiselle Bourienne at all,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңNo? She is very nice and kind and, above all, sheвҖҷs much to be pitied. She has no one, no one. To tell the truth, I donвҖҷt need her, and sheвҖҷs even in my way. You know I always was a savage, and now am even more so. I like being alone.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Father likes her very much. She and MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich are the two people to whom he is always gentle and kind, because he has been a benefactor to them both. As Sterne says: вҖҳWe donвҖҷt love people so much for the good they have done us, as for the good we have done them.вҖҷ Father took her when she was homeless after losing her own father. She is very good-natured, and my father likes her way of reading. She reads to him in the evenings and reads splendidly.вҖқ вҖңTo be quite frank, Marie, I expect FatherвҖҷs character sometimes makes things trying for you, doesnвҖҷt it?вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y asked suddenly. Princess MГЎrya was first surprised and then aghast at this question. вҖңFor me? For me?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Trying for me!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said she. вҖңHe always was rather harsh; and now I should think heвҖҷs getting very trying,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, apparently speaking lightly of their father in order to puzzle or test his sister. вҖңYou are good in every way, AndrГ©, but you have a kind of intellectual pride,вҖқ said the princess, following the train of her own thoughts rather than the trend of the conversationвҒ вҖ”вҖңand thatвҖҷs a great sin. How can one judge Father? But even if one might, what feeling except veneration could such a man as my father evoke? And I am so contented and happy with him. I only wish you were all as happy as I am.вҖқ Her brother shook his head incredulously. вҖңThe only thing that is hard for meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I will tell you the truth, AndrГ©вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ is FatherвҖҷs way of treating religious subjects. I donвҖҷt understand how a man of his immense intellect can fail to see what is as clear as day, and can go so far astray. That is the only thing that makes me unhappy. But even in this I can see lately a shade of improvement. His satire has been less bitter of late, and there was a monk he received and had a long talk with.вҖқ вҖңAh! my dear, I am afraid you and your monk are wasting your powder,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y banteringly yet tenderly. вҖңAh! mon ami, I only pray, and hope that God will hear me. AndrГ©вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she said timidly after a momentвҖҷs silence, вҖңI have a great favor to ask of you.вҖқ вҖңWhat is it, dear?вҖқ вҖңNoвҒ вҖ”promise that you will not refuse! It will give you no trouble and is nothing unworthy of you, but it will comfort me. Promise, AndrГәsha!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said she, putting her hand in her reticule but not yet taking out what she was holding inside it, as if what she held were the subject of her request and must not be shown before the request was granted. She looked timidly at her brother. вҖңEven if it were a great deal of troubleвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ answered Prince AndrГ©y, as if guessing what it was about. вҖңThink what you please! I know you are just like Father. Think as you please, but do this for my sake! Please do! FatherвҖҷs father, our grandfather, wore it in all his wars.вҖқ (She still did not take out what she was holding in her reticule.) вҖңSo you promise?вҖқ вҖңOf course. What is it?вҖқ вҖңAndrГ©, I bless you with this icon and you must promise me you will never take it off. Do you promise?вҖқ вҖңIf it does not weigh a hundredweight and wonвҖҷt break my neckвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ To please youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. But immediately, noticing the pained expression his joke had brought to his sisterвҖҷs face, he repented and added: вҖңI am glad; really, dear, I am very glad.вҖқ вҖңAgainst your will He will save and have mercy on you and bring you to Himself, for in Him alone is truth and peace,вҖқ said she in a voice trembling with emotion, solemnly holding up in both hands before her brother a small, oval, antique, dark-faced icon of the Saviour in a gold setting, on a finely wrought silver chain. She crossed herself, kissed the icon, and handed it to AndrГ©y. вҖңPlease, AndrГ©, for my sake!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Rays of gentle light shone from her large, timid eyes. Those eyes lit up the whole of her thin, sickly face and made it beautiful. Her brother would have taken the icon, but she stopped him. AndrГ©y understood, crossed himself and kissed the icon. There was a look of tenderness, for he was touched, but also a gleam of irony on his face. вҖңThank you, my dear.вҖқ She kissed him on the forehead and sat down again on the sofa. They were silent for a while. вҖңAs I was saying to you, AndrГ©, be kind and generous as you always used to be. DonвҖҷt judge Liza harshly,вҖқ she began. вҖңShe is so sweet, so good-natured, and her position now is a very hard one.вҖқ вҖңI do not think I have complained of my wife to you, MГЎsha, or blamed her. Why do you say all this to me?вҖқ Red patches appeared on Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs face and she was silent as if she felt guilty. вҖңI have said nothing to you, but you have already been talked to. And I am sorry for that,вҖқ he went on. The patches grew deeper on her forehead, neck, and cheeks. She tried to say something but could not. Her brother had guessed right: the little princess had been crying after dinner and had spoken of her forebodings about her confinement, and how she dreaded it, and had complained of her fate, her father-in-law, and her husband. After crying she had fallen asleep. Prince AndrГ©y felt sorry for his sister. вҖңKnow this, MГЎsha: I canвҖҷt reproach, have not reproached, and never shall reproach my wife with anything, and I cannot reproach myself with anything in regard to her; and that always will be so in whatever circumstances I may be placed. But if you want to know the truthвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ if you want to know whether I am happy? No! Is she happy? No! But why this is so I donвҖҷt knowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ As he said this he rose, went to his sister, and, stooping, kissed her forehead. His fine eyes lit up with a thoughtful, kindly, and unaccustomed brightness, but he was looking not at his sister but over her head toward the darkness of the open doorway. вҖңLet us go to her, I must say goodbye. OrвҒ вҖ”go and wake and IвҖҷll come in a moment. PetrГәshka!вҖқ he called to his valet: вҖңCome here, take these away. Put this on the seat and this to the right.вҖқ Princess MГЎrya rose and moved to the door, then stopped and said: вҖңAndrГ©, if you had faith you would have turned to God and asked Him to give you the love you do not feel, and your prayer would have been answered.вҖқ вҖңWell, maybe!вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңGo, MГЎsha; IвҖҷll come immediately.вҖқ On the way to his sisterвҖҷs room, in the passage which connected one wing with the other, Prince AndrГ©y met Mademoiselle Bourienne smiling sweetly. It was the third time that day that, with an ecstatic and artless smile, she had met him in secluded passages. вҖңOh! I thought you were in your room,вҖқ she said, for some reason blushing and dropping her eyes. Prince AndrГ©y looked sternly at her and an expression of anger suddenly came over his face. He said nothing to her but looked at her forehead and hair, without looking at her eyes, with such contempt that the Frenchwoman blushed and went away without a word. When he reached his sisterвҖҷs room his wife was already awake and her merry voice, hurrying one word after another, came through the open door. She was speaking as usual in French, and as if after long self-restraint she wished to make up for lost time. вҖңNo, but imagine the old Countess Zouboff, with false curls and her mouth full of false teeth, as if she were trying to cheat old age.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Ha, ha, ha! Marie!вҖқ This very sentence about Countess ZГәbova and this same laugh Prince AndrГ©y had already heard from his wife in the presence of others some five times. He entered the room softly. The little princess, plump and rosy, was sitting in an easy chair with her work in her hands, talking incessantly, repeating Petersburg reminiscences and even phrases. Prince AndrГ©y came up, stroked her hair, and asked if she felt rested after their journey. She answered him and continued her chatter. The coach with six horses was waiting at the porch. It was an autumn night, so dark that the coachman could not see the carriage pole. Servants with lanterns were bustling about in the porch. The immense house was brilliant with lights shining through its lofty windows. The domestic serfs were crowding in the hall, waiting to bid goodbye to the young prince. The members of the household were all gathered in the reception hall: MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich, Mademoiselle Bourienne, Princess MГЎrya, and the little princess. Prince AndrГ©y had been called to his fatherвҖҷs study as the latter wished to say goodbye to him alone. All were waiting for them to come out. When Prince AndrГ©y entered the study the old man in his old-age spectacles and white dressing gown, in which he received no one but his son, sat at the table writing. He glanced round. вҖңGoing?вҖқ And he went on writing. вҖңIвҖҷve come to say goodbye.вҖқ вҖңKiss me here,вҖқ and he touched his cheek: вҖңThanks, thanks!вҖқ вҖңWhat do you thank me for?вҖқ вҖңFor not dillydallying and not hanging to a womanвҖҷs apron strings. The Service before everything. Thanks, thanks!вҖқ And he went on writing, so that his quill spluttered and squeaked. вҖңIf you have anything to say, say it. These two things can be done together,вҖқ he added. вҖңAbout my wifeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I am ashamed as it is to leave her on your hands.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhy talk nonsense? Say what you want.вҖқ вҖңWhen her confinement is due, send to Moscow for an accoucheur.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Let him be here.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The old prince stopped writing and, as if not understanding, fixed his stern eyes on his son. вҖңI know that no one can help if nature does not do her work,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, evidently confused. вҖңI know that out of a million cases only one goes wrong, but it is her fancy and mine. They have been telling her things. She has had a dream and is frightened.вҖқ вҖңHmвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ HmвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ muttered the old prince to himself, finishing what he was writing. вҖңIвҖҷll do it.вҖқ He signed with a flourish and suddenly turning to his son began to laugh. вҖңItвҖҷs a bad business, eh?вҖқ вҖңWhat is bad, Father?вҖқ вҖңThe wife!вҖқ said the old prince, briefly and significantly. вҖңI donвҖҷt understand!вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңNo, it canвҖҷt be helped, lad,вҖқ said the prince. вҖңTheyвҖҷre all like that; one canвҖҷt unmarry. DonвҖҷt be afraid; I wonвҖҷt tell anyone, but you know it yourself.вҖқ He seized his son by the hand with small bony fingers, shook it, looked straight into his sonвҖҷs face with keen eyes which seemed to see through him, and again laughed his frigid laugh. The son sighed, thus admitting that his father had understood him. The old man continued to fold and seal his letter, snatching up and throwing down the wax, the seal, and the paper, with his accustomed rapidity. вҖңWhatвҖҷs to be done? SheвҖҷs pretty! I will do everything. Make your mind easy,вҖқ said he in abrupt sentences while sealing his letter. AndrГ©y did not speak; he was both pleased and displeased that his father understood him. The old man got up and gave the letter to his son. вҖңListen!вҖқ said he; вҖңdonвҖҷt worry about your wife: what can be done shall be. Now listen! Give this letter to MikhГЎil IlariГіnovich.17 I have written that he should make use of you in proper places and not keep you long as an adjutant: a bad position! Tell him I remember and like him. Write and tell me how he receives you. If he is all rightвҒ вҖ”serve him. NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich BolkГіnskiвҖҷs son need not serve under anyone if he is in disfavor. Now come here.вҖқ He spoke so rapidly that he did not finish half his words, but his son was accustomed to understand him. He led him to the desk, raised the lid, drew out a drawer, and took out an exercise book filled with his bold, tall, close handwriting. вҖңI shall probably die before you. So remember, these are my memoirs; hand them to the Emperor after my death. Now here is a Lombard bond and a letter; it is a premium for the man who writes a history of SuvГіrovвҖҷs wars. Send it to the Academy. Here are some jottings for you to read when I am gone. You will find them useful.вҖқ AndrГ©y did not tell his father that he would no doubt live a long time yet. He felt that he must not say it. вҖңI will do it all, Father,вҖқ he said. вҖңWell, now, goodbye!вҖқ He gave his son his hand to kiss, and embraced him. вҖңRemember this, Prince AndrГ©y, if they kill you it will hurt me, your old fatherвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he paused unexpectedly, and then in a querulous voice suddenly shrieked: вҖңbut if I hear that you have not behaved like a son of NikolГЎy BolkГіnski, I shall be ashamed!вҖқ вҖңYou need not have said that to me, Father,вҖқ said the son with a smile. The old man was silent. вҖңI also wanted to ask you,вҖқ continued Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңif IвҖҷm killed and if I have a son, do not let him be taken away from youвҒ вҖ”as I said yesterdayвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ let him grow up with you.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Please.вҖқ вҖңNot let the wife have him?вҖқ said the old man, and laughed. They stood silent, facing one another. The old manвҖҷs sharp eyes were fixed straight on his sonвҖҷs. Something twitched in the lower part of the old princeвҖҷs face. вҖңWeвҖҷve said goodbye. Go!вҖқ he suddenly shouted in a loud, angry voice, opening his door. вҖңWhat is it? What?вҖқ asked both princesses when they saw for a moment at the door Prince AndrГ©y and the figure of the old man in a white dressing gown, spectacled and wigless, shouting in an angry voice. Prince AndrГ©y sighed and made no reply. вҖңWell!вҖқ he said, turning to his wife. And this вҖңWell!вҖқ sounded coldly ironic, as if he were saying: вҖңNow go through your performance.вҖқ вҖңAndrГ©, already!вҖқ said the little princess, turning pale and looking with dismay at her husband. He embraced her. She screamed and fell unconscious on his shoulder. He cautiously released the shoulder she leaned on, looked into her face, and carefully placed her in an easy chair. вҖңAdieu, Marie,вҖқ said he gently to his sister, taking her by the hand and kissing her, and then he left the room with rapid steps. The little princess lay in the armchair, Mademoiselle Bourienne chafing her temples. Princess MГЎrya, supporting her sister-in-law, still looked with her beautiful eyes full of tears at the door through which Prince AndrГ©y had gone and made the sign of the cross in his direction. From the study, like pistol shots, came the frequent sound of the old man angrily blowing his nose. Hardly had Prince AndrГ©y gone when the study door opened quickly and the stern figure of the old man in the white dressing gown looked out. вҖңGone? ThatвҖҷs all right!вҖқ said he; and looking angrily at the unconscious little princess, he shook his head reprovingly and slammed the door. III III Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs reception was in full swing. The spindles hummed steadily and ceaselessly on all sides. With the exception of the aunt, beside whom sat only one elderly lady, who with her thin careworn face was rather out of place in this brilliant society, the whole company had settled into three groups. One, chiefly masculine, had formed round the abbГ©. Another, of young people, was grouped round the beautiful Princess ElГЁn, Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs daughter, and the little Princess BolkГіnskaya, very pretty and rosy, though rather too plump for her age. The third group was gathered round Mortemart and Anna PГЎvlovna. The vicomte was a nice-looking young man with soft features and polished manners, who evidently considered himself a celebrity but out of politeness modestly placed himself at the disposal of the circle in which he found himself. Anna PГЎvlovna was obviously serving him up as a treat to her guests. As a clever maГ®tre dвҖҷhГҙtel serves up as a specially choice delicacy a piece of meat that no one who had seen it in the kitchen would have cared to eat, so Anna PГЎvlovna served up to her guests, first the vicomte and then the abbГ©, as peculiarly choice morsels. The group about Mortemart immediately began discussing the murder of the Duc dвҖҷEnghien. The vicomte said that the Duc dвҖҷEnghien had perished by his own magnanimity, and that there were particular reasons for BonaparteвҖҷs hatred of him. вҖңAh, yes! Do tell us all about it, Vicomte,вҖқ said Anna PГЎvlovna, with a pleasant feeling that there was something Г  la Louis XV in the sound of that sentence: вҖңContez nous Г§ela, Vicomte.вҖқ The vicomte bowed and smiled courteously in token of his willingness to comply. Anna PГЎvlovna arranged a group round him, inviting everyone to listen to his tale. вҖңThe vicomte knew the duc personally,вҖқ whispered Anna PГЎvlovna to one of the guests. вҖңThe vicomte is a wonderful raconteur,вҖқ said she to another. вҖңHow evidently he belongs to the best society,вҖқ said she to a third; and the vicomte was served up to the company in the choicest and most advantageous style, like a well-garnished joint of roast beef on a hot dish. The vicomte wished to begin his story and gave a subtle smile. вҖңCome over here, HГ©lГЁne, dear,вҖқ said Anna PГЎvlovna to the beautiful young princess who was sitting some way off, the center of another group. The princess smiled. She rose with the same unchanging smile with which she had first entered the roomвҒ вҖ”the smile of a perfectly beautiful woman. With a slight rustle of her white dress trimmed with moss and ivy, with a gleam of white shoulders, glossy hair, and sparkling diamonds, she passed between the men who made way for her, not looking at any of them but smiling on all, as if graciously allowing each the privilege of admiring her beautiful figure and shapely shoulders, back, and bosomвҒ вҖ”which in the fashion of those days were very much exposedвҒ вҖ”and she seemed to bring the glamour of a ballroom with her as she moved toward Anna PГЎvlovna. ElГЁn was so lovely that not only did she not show any trace of coquetry, but on the contrary she even appeared shy of her unquestionable and all too victorious beauty. She seemed to wish, but to be unable, to diminish its effect. вҖңHow lovely!вҖқ said everyone who saw her; and the vicomte lifted his shoulders and dropped his eyes as if startled by something extraordinary when she took her seat opposite and beamed upon him also with her unchanging smile. вҖңMadame, I doubt my ability before such an audience,вҖқ said he, smilingly inclining his head. The princess rested her bare round arm on a little table and considered a reply unnecessary. She smilingly waited. All the time the story was being told she sat upright, glancing now at her beautiful round arm, altered in shape by its pressure on the table, now at her still more beautiful bosom, on which she readjusted a diamond necklace. From time to time she smoothed the folds of her dress, and whenever the story produced an effect she glanced at Anna PГЎvlovna, at once adopted just the expression she saw on the maid of honorвҖҷs face, and again relapsed into her radiant smile. The little princess had also left the tea table and followed ElГЁn. вҖңWait a moment, IвҖҷll get my work.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Now then, what are you thinking of?вҖқ she went on, turning to Prince Ippolit. вҖңFetch me my workbag.вҖқ There was a general movement as the princess, smiling and talking merrily to everyone at once, sat down and gaily arranged herself in her seat. вҖңNow I am all right,вҖқ she said, and asking the vicomte to begin, she took up her work. Prince Ippolit, having brought the workbag, joined the circle and moving a chair close to hers seated himself beside her. Le charmant Hippolyte was surprising by his extraordinary resemblance to his beautiful sister, but yet more by the fact that in spite of this resemblance he was exceedingly ugly. His features were like his sisterвҖҷs, but while in her case everything was lit up by a joyous, self-satisfied, youthful, and constant smile of animation, and by the wonderful classic beauty of her figure, his face on the contrary was dulled by imbecility and a constant expression of sullen self-confidence, while his body was thin and weak. His eyes, nose, and mouth all seemed puckered into a vacant, wearied grimace, and his arms and legs always fell into unnatural positions. вҖңItвҖҷs not going to be a ghost story?вҖқ said he, sitting down beside the princess and hastily adjusting his lorgnette, as if without this instrument he could not begin to speak. вҖңWhy no, my dear fellow,вҖқ said the astonished narrator, shrugging his shoulders. вҖңBecause I hate ghost stories,вҖқ said Prince Ippolit in a tone which showed that he only understood the meaning of his words after he had uttered them. He spoke with such self-confidence that his hearers could not be sure whether what he said was very witty or very stupid. He was dressed in a dark-green dress coat, knee breeches of the color of cuisse de nymphe effrayГ©e, as he called it, shoes, and silk stockings. The vicomte told his tale very neatly. It was an anecdote, then current, to the effect that the Duc dвҖҷEnghien had gone secretly to Paris to visit Mademoiselle George; that at her house he came upon Bonaparte, who also enjoyed the famous actressвҖҷ favors, and that in his presence Napoleon happened to fall into one of the fainting fits to which he was subject, and was thus at the ducвҖҷs mercy. The latter spared him, and this magnanimity Bonaparte subsequently repaid by death. The story was very pretty and interesting, especially at the point where the rivals suddenly recognized one another; and the ladies looked agitated. вҖңCharming!вҖқ said Anna PГЎvlovna with an inquiring glance at the little princess. вҖңCharming!вҖқ whispered the little princess, sticking the needle into her work as if to testify that the interest and fascination of the story prevented her from going on with it. The vicomte appreciated this silent praise and smiling gratefully prepared to continue, but just then Anna PГЎvlovna, who had kept a watchful eye on the young man who so alarmed her, noticed that he was talking too loudly and vehemently with the abbГ©, so she hurried to the rescue. Pierre had managed to start a conversation with the abbГ© about the balance of power, and the latter, evidently interested by the young manвҖҷs simple-minded eagerness, was explaining his pet theory. Both were talking and listening too eagerly and too naturally, which was why Anna PГЎvlovna disapproved. вҖңThe means areвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the balance of power in Europe and the rights of the people,вҖқ the abbГ© was saying. вҖңIt is only necessary for one powerful nation like RussiaвҒ вҖ”barbaric as she is said to beвҒ вҖ”to place herself disinterestedly at the head of an alliance having for its object the maintenance of the balance of power of Europe, and it would save the world!вҖқ вҖңBut how are you to get that balance?вҖқ Pierre was beginning. At that moment Anna PГЎvlovna came up and, looking severely at Pierre, asked the Italian how he stood Russian climate. The ItalianвҖҷs face instantly changed and assumed an offensively affected, sugary expression, evidently habitual to him when conversing with women. вҖңI am so enchanted by the brilliancy of the wit and culture of the society, more especially of the feminine society, in which I have had the honor of being received, that I have not yet had time to think of the climate,вҖқ said he. Not letting the abbГ© and Pierre escape, Anna PГЎvlovna, the more conveniently to keep them under observation, brought them into the larger circle. IV IV Just then another visitor entered the drawing room: Prince AndrГ©y BolkГіnski, the little princessвҖҷ husband. He was a very handsome young man, of medium height, with firm, clearcut features. Everything about him, from his weary, bored expression to his quiet, measured step, offered a most striking contrast to his quiet, little wife. It was evident that he not only knew everyone in the drawing room, but had found them to be so tiresome that it wearied him to look at or listen to them. And among all these faces that he found so tedious, none seemed to bore him so much as that of his pretty wife. He turned away from her with a grimace that distorted his handsome face, kissed Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs hand, and screwing up his eyes scanned the whole company. вҖңYou are off to the war, Prince?вҖқ said Anna PГЎvlovna. вҖңGeneral Koutouzoff,вҖқ said BolkГіnski, speaking French and stressing the last syllable of the generalвҖҷs name like a Frenchman, вҖңhas been pleased to take me as an aide-de-camp.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAnd Lise, your wife?вҖқ вҖңShe will go to the country.вҖқ вҖңAre you not ashamed to deprive us of your charming wife?вҖқ вҖңAndrГ©,вҖқ said his wife, addressing her husband in the same coquettish manner in which she spoke to other men, вҖңthe vicomte has been telling us such a tale about Mademoiselle George and Bonaparte!вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y screwed up his eyes and turned away. Pierre, who from the moment Prince AndrГ©y entered the room had watched him with glad, affectionate eyes, now came up and took his arm. Before he looked round Prince AndrГ©y frowned again, expressing his annoyance with whoever was touching his arm, but when he saw PierreвҖҷs beaming face he gave him an unexpectedly kind and pleasant smile. вҖңThere now!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ So you, too, are in the great world?вҖқ said he to Pierre. вҖңI knew you would be here,вҖқ replied Pierre. вҖңI will come to supper with you. May I?вҖқ he added in a low voice so as not to disturb the vicomte who was continuing his story. вҖңNo, impossible!вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, laughing and pressing PierreвҖҷs hand to show that there was no need to ask the question. He wished to say something more, but at that moment Prince VasГӯli and his daughter got up to go and the two young men rose to let them pass. вҖңYou must excuse me, dear Vicomte,вҖқ said Prince VasГӯli to the Frenchman, holding him down by the sleeve in a friendly way to prevent his rising. вҖңThis unfortunate fГӘte at the ambassadorвҖҷs deprives me of a pleasure, and obliges me to interrupt you. I am very sorry to leave your enchanting party,вҖқ said he, turning to Anna PГЎvlovna. His daughter, Princess ElГЁn, passed between the chairs, lightly holding up the folds of her dress, and the smile shone still more radiantly on her beautiful face. Pierre gazed at her with rapturous, almost frightened, eyes as she passed him. вҖңVery lovely,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңVery,вҖқ said Pierre. In passing Prince VasГӯli seized PierreвҖҷs hand and said to Anna PГЎvlovna: вҖңEducate this bear for me! He has been staying with me a whole month and this is the first time I have seen him in society. Nothing is so necessary for a young man as the society of clever women.вҖқ Anna PГЎvlovna smiled and promised to take Pierre in hand. She knew his father to be a connection of Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs. The elderly lady who had been sitting with the old aunt rose hurriedly and overtook Prince VasГӯli in the anteroom. All the affectation of interest she had assumed had left her kindly and tear-worn face and it now expressed only anxiety and fear. вҖңHow about my son BorГӯs, Prince?вҖқ said she, hurrying after him into the anteroom. вҖңI canвҖҷt remain any longer in Petersburg. Tell me what news I may take back to my poor boy.вҖқ Although Prince VasГӯli listened reluctantly and not very politely to the elderly lady, even betraying some impatience, she gave him an ingratiating and appealing smile, and took his hand that he might not go away. вҖңWhat would it cost you to say a word to the Emperor, and then he would be transferred to the Guards at once?вҖқ said she. вҖңBelieve me, Princess, I am ready to do all I can,вҖқ answered Prince VasГӯli, вҖңbut it is difficult for me to ask the Emperor. I should advise you to appeal to RumyГЎntsev through Prince GolГӯtsyn. That would be the best way.вҖқ The elderly lady was a Princess DrubetskГЎya, belonging to one of the best families in Russia, but she was poor, and having long been out of society had lost her former influential connections. She had now come to Petersburg to procure an appointment in the Guards for her only son. It was, in fact, solely to meet Prince VasГӯli that she had obtained an invitation to Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs reception and had sat listening to the vicomteвҖҷs story. Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs words frightened her, an embittered look clouded her once handsome face, but only for a moment; then she smiled again and clutched Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs arm more tightly. вҖңListen to me, Prince,вҖқ said she. вҖңI have never yet asked you for anything and I never will again, nor have I ever reminded you of my fatherвҖҷs friendship for you; but now I entreat you for GodвҖҷs sake to do this for my sonвҒ вҖ”and I shall always regard you as a benefactor,вҖқ she added hurriedly. вҖңNo, donвҖҷt be angry, but promise! I have asked GolГӯtsyn and he has refused. Be the kindhearted man you always were,вҖқ she said, trying to smile though tears were in her eyes. вҖңPapa, we shall be late,вҖқ said Princess ElГЁn, turning her beautiful head and looking over her classically molded shoulder as she stood waiting by the door. Influence in society, however, is a capital which has to be economized if it is to last. Prince VasГӯli knew this, and having once realized that if he asked on behalf of all who begged of him, he would soon be unable to ask for himself, he became chary of using his influence. But in Princess DrubetskГЎyaвҖҷs case he felt, after her second appeal, something like qualms of conscience. She had reminded him of what was quite true; he had been indebted to her father for the first steps in his career. Moreover, he could see by her manners that she was one of those womenвҒ вҖ”mostly mothersвҒ вҖ”who, having once made up their minds, will not rest until they have gained their end, and are prepared if necessary to go on insisting day after day and hour after hour, and even to make scenes. This last consideration moved him. вҖңMy dear Anna MikhГЎylovna,вҖқ said he with his usual familiarity and weariness of tone, вҖңit is almost impossible for me to do what you ask; but to prove my devotion to you and how I respect your fatherвҖҷs memory, I will do the impossibleвҒ вҖ”your son shall be transferred to the Guards. Here is my hand on it. Are you satisfied?вҖқ вҖңMy dear benefactor! This is what I expected from youвҒ вҖ”I knew your kindness!вҖқ He turned to go. вҖңWaitвҒ вҖ”just a word! When he has been transferred to the GuardsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she faltered. вҖңYou are on good terms with MikhГЎil IlariГіnovich KutГәzovвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ recommend BorГӯs to him as adjutant! Then I shall be at rest, and thenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Prince VasГӯli smiled. вҖңNo, I wonвҖҷt promise that. You donвҖҷt know how KutГәzov is pestered since his appointment as Commander in Chief. He told me himself that all the Moscow ladies have conspired to give him all their sons as adjutants.вҖқ вҖңNo, but do promise! I wonвҖҷt let you go! My dear benefactorвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңPapa,вҖқ said his beautiful daughter in the same tone as before, вҖңwe shall be late.вҖқ вҖңWell, au revoir! Goodbye! You hear her?вҖқ вҖңThen tomorrow you will speak to the Emperor?вҖқ вҖңCertainly; but about KutГәzov, I donвҖҷt promise.вҖқ вҖңDo promise, do promise, Basile!вҖқ cried Anna MikhГЎylovna as he went, with the smile of a coquettish girl, which at one time probably came naturally to her, but was now very ill-suited to her careworn face. Apparently she had forgotten her age and by force of habit employed all the old feminine arts. But as soon as the prince had gone her face resumed its former cold, artificial expression. She returned to the group where the vicomte was still talking, and again pretended to listen, while waiting till it would be time to leave. Her task was accomplished. V V вҖңAnd what do you think of this latest comedy, the coronation at Milan?вҖқ asked Anna PГЎvlovna, вҖңand of the comedy of the people of Genoa and Lucca laying their petitions before Monsieur Buonaparte, and Monsieur Buonaparte sitting on a throne and granting the petitions of the nations? Adorable! It is enough to make oneвҖҷs head whirl! It is as if the whole world had gone crazy.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y looked Anna PГЎvlovna straight in the face with a sarcastic smile. вҖңвҖҠвҖҳDieu me la donne, gare Г  qui la touche!вҖҷ2 They say he was very fine when he said that,вҖқ he remarked, repeating the words in Italian: вҖңвҖҠвҖҳDio mi lвҖҷha dato. Guai a chi la tocchi!вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ вҖңI hope this will prove the last drop that will make the glass run over,вҖқ Anna PГЎvlovna continued. вҖңThe sovereigns will not be able to endure this man who is a menace to everything.вҖқ вҖңThe sovereigns? I do not speak of Russia,вҖқ said the vicomte, polite but hopeless: вҖңThe sovereigns, madameвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What have they done for Louis XVII, for the Queen, or for Madame Elizabeth? Nothing!вҖқ and he became more animated. вҖңAnd believe me, they are reaping the reward of their betrayal of the Bourbon cause. The sovereigns! Why, they are sending ambassadors to compliment the usurper.вҖқ And sighing disdainfully, he again changed his position. Prince Ippolit, who had been gazing at the vicomte for some time through his lorgnette, suddenly turned completely round toward the little princess, and having asked for a needle began tracing the CondГ© coat of arms on the table. He explained this to her with as much gravity as if she had asked him to do it. вҖңBГўton de gueules, engrГӘlГ© de gueules dвҖҷazurвҒ вҖ”maison CondГ©,вҖқ said he. The princess listened, smiling. вҖңIf Bonaparte remains on the throne of France a year longer,вҖқ the vicomte continued, with the air of a man who, in a matter with which he is better acquainted than anyone else, does not listen to others but follows the current of his own thoughts, вҖңthings will have gone too far. By intrigues, violence, exile, and executions, French societyвҒ вҖ”I mean good French societyвҒ вҖ”will have been forever destroyed, and thenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He shrugged his shoulders and spread out his hands. Pierre wished to make a remark, for the conversation interested him, but Anna PГЎvlovna, who had him under observation, interrupted: вҖңThe Emperor Alexander,вҖқ said she, with the melancholy which always accompanied any reference of hers to the Imperial family, вҖңhas declared that he will leave it to the French people themselves to choose their own form of government; and I believe that once free from the usurper, the whole nation will certainly throw itself into the arms of its rightful king,вҖқ she concluded, trying to be amiable to the royalist emigrant. вҖңThat is doubtful,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңMonsieur le Vicomte quite rightly supposes that matters have already gone too far. I think it will be difficult to return to the old regime.вҖқ вҖңFrom what I have heard,вҖқ said Pierre, blushing and breaking into the conversation, вҖңalmost all the aristocracy has already gone over to BonaparteвҖҷs side.вҖқ вҖңIt is the Bonapartists who say that,вҖқ replied the vicomte without looking at Pierre. вҖңAt the present time it is difficult to know the real state of French public opinion.вҖқ вҖңBonaparte has said so,вҖқ remarked Prince AndrГ©y with a sarcastic smile. It was evident that he did not like the vicomte and was aiming his remarks at him, though without looking at him. вҖңвҖҠвҖҳI showed them the path to glory, but they did not follow it,вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ Prince AndrГ©y continued after a short silence, again quoting NapoleonвҖҷs words. вҖңвҖҠвҖҳI opened my antechambers and they crowded in.вҖҷ I do not know how far he was justified in saying so.вҖқ вҖңNot in the least,вҖқ replied the vicomte. вҖңAfter the murder of the duc even the most partial ceased to regard him as a hero. If to some people,вҖқ he went on, turning to Anna PГЎvlovna, вҖңhe ever was a hero, after the murder of the duc there was one martyr more in heaven and one hero less on earth.вҖқ Before Anna PГЎvlovna and the others had time to smile their appreciation of the vicomteвҖҷs epigram, Pierre again broke into the conversation, and though Anna PГЎvlovna felt sure he would say something inappropriate, she was unable to stop him. вҖңThe execution of the Duc dвҖҷEnghien,вҖқ declared Monsieur Pierre, вҖңwas a political necessity, and it seems to me that Napoleon showed greatness of soul by not fearing to take on himself the whole responsibility of that deed.вҖқ вҖңDieu! Mon Dieu!вҖқ muttered Anna PГЎvlovna in a terrified whisper. вҖңWhat, Monsieur PierreвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Do you consider that assassination shows greatness of soul?вҖқ said the little princess, smiling and drawing her work nearer to her. вҖңOh!
