Oh!вҖқ exclaimed several voices. вҖңCapital!вҖқ said Prince Ippolit in English, and began slapping his knee with the palm of his hand. The vicomte merely shrugged his shoulders. Pierre looked solemnly at his audience over his spectacles and continued. вҖңI say so,вҖқ he continued desperately, вҖңbecause the Bourbons fled from the Revolution leaving the people to anarchy, and Napoleon alone understood the Revolution and quelled it, and so for the general good, he could not stop short for the sake of one manвҖҷs life.вҖқ вҖңWonвҖҷt you come over to the other table?вҖқ suggested Anna PГЎvlovna. But Pierre continued his speech without heeding her. вҖңNo,вҖқ cried he, becoming more and more eager, вҖңNapoleon is great because he rose superior to the Revolution, suppressed its abuses, preserved all that was good in itвҒ вҖ”equality of citizenship and freedom of speech and of the pressвҒ вҖ”and only for that reason did he obtain power.вҖқ вҖңYes, if having obtained power, without availing himself of it to commit murder he had restored it to the rightful king, I should have called him a great man,вҖқ remarked the vicomte. вҖңHe could not do that. The people only gave him power that he might rid them of the Bourbons and because they saw that he was a great man. The Revolution was a grand thing!вҖқ continued Monsieur Pierre, betraying by this desperate and provocative proposition his extreme youth and his wish to express all that was in his mind. вҖңWhat? Revolution and regicide a grand thing?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, after thatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But wonвҖҷt you come to this other table?вҖқ repeated Anna PГЎvlovna. вҖңRousseauвҖҷs Contrat Social,вҖқ said the vicomte with a tolerant smile. вҖңI am not speaking of regicide, I am speaking about ideas.вҖқ вҖңYes: ideas of robbery, murder, and regicide,вҖқ again interjected an ironical voice. вҖңThose were extremes, no doubt, but they are not what is most important. What is important are the rights of man, emancipation from prejudices, and equality of citizenship, and all these ideas Napoleon has retained in full force.вҖқ вҖңLiberty and equality,вҖқ said the vicomte contemptuously, as if at last deciding seriously to prove to this youth how foolish his words were, вҖңhigh-sounding words which have long been discredited. Who does not love liberty and equality? Even our Saviour preached liberty and equality. Have people since the Revolution become happier? On the contrary. We wanted liberty, but Bonaparte has destroyed it.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y kept looking with an amused smile from Pierre to the vicomte and from the vicomte to their hostess. In the first moment of PierreвҖҷs outburst Anna PГЎvlovna, despite her social experience, was horror-struck. But when she saw that PierreвҖҷs sacrilegious words had not exasperated the vicomte, and had convinced herself that it was impossible to stop him, she rallied her forces and joined the vicomte in a vigorous attack on the orator. вҖңBut, my dear Monsieur Pierre,вҖқ said she, вҖңhow do you explain the fact of a great man executing a ducвҒ вҖ”or even an ordinary man whoвҒ вҖ”is innocent and untried?вҖқ вҖңI should like,вҖқ said the vicomte, вҖңto ask how monsieur explains the 18th Brumaire; was not that an imposture? It was a swindle, and not at all like the conduct of a great man!вҖқ вҖңAnd the prisoners he killed in Africa? That was horrible!вҖқ said the little princess, shrugging her shoulders. вҖңHeвҖҷs a low fellow, say what you will,вҖқ remarked Prince Ippolit. Pierre, not knowing whom to answer, looked at them all and smiled. His smile was unlike the half-smile of other people. When he smiled, his grave, even rather gloomy, look was instantaneously replaced by anotherвҒ вҖ”a childlike, kindly, even rather silly look, which seemed to ask forgiveness. The vicomte who was meeting him for the first time saw clearly that this young Jacobin was not so terrible as his words suggested. All were silent. вҖңHow do you expect him to answer you all at once?вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңBesides, in the actions of a statesman one has to distinguish between his acts as a private person, as a general, and as an emperor. So it seems to me.вҖқ вҖңYes, yes, of course!вҖқ Pierre chimed in, pleased at the arrival of this reinforcement. вҖңOne must admit,вҖқ continued Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңthat Napoleon as a man was great on the bridge of Arcola, and in the hospital at Jaffa where he gave his hand to the plague-stricken; butвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but there are other acts which it is difficult to justify.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y, who had evidently wished to tone down the awkwardness of PierreвҖҷs remarks, rose and made a sign to his wife that it was time to go. Suddenly Prince Ippolit started up making signs to everyone to attend, and asking them all to be seated began: вҖңI was told a charming Moscow story today and must treat you to it. Excuse me, VicomteвҒ вҖ”I must tell it in Russian or the point will be lost.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ And Prince Ippolit began to tell his story in such Russian as a Frenchman would speak after spending about a year in Russia. Everyone waited, so emphatically and eagerly did he demand their attention to his story. вҖңThere is in Moscow a lady, une dame, and she is very stingy. She must have two footmen behind her carriage, and very big ones. That was her taste. And she had a ladyвҖҷs maid, also big. She saidвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Here Prince Ippolit paused, evidently collecting his ideas with difficulty. вҖңShe saidвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Oh yes! She said, вҖҳGirl,вҖҷ to the maid, вҖҳput on a livery, get up behind the carriage, and come with me while I make some calls.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ Here Prince Ippolit spluttered and burst out laughing long before his audience, which produced an effect unfavorable to the narrator. Several persons, among them the elderly lady and Anna PГЎvlovna, did however smile. вҖңShe went. Suddenly there was a great wind. The girl lost her hat and her long hair came down.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Here he could contain himself no longer and went on, between gasps of laughter: вҖңAnd the whole world knew.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ And so the anecdote ended. Though it was unintelligible why he had told it, or why it had to be told in Russian, still Anna PГЎvlovna and the others appreciated Prince IppolitвҖҷs social tact in so agreeably ending PierreвҖҷs unpleasant and unamiable outburst. After the anecdote the conversation broke up into insignificant small talk about the last and next balls, about theatricals, and who would meet whom, and when and where. VI VI Having thanked Anna PГЎvlovna for her charming soiree, the guests began to take their leave. Pierre was ungainly. Stout, about the average height, broad, with huge red hands; he did not know, as the saying is, how to enter a drawing room and still less how to leave one; that is, how to say something particularly agreeable before going away. Besides this he was absentminded. When he rose to go, he took up instead of his own, the generalвҖҷs three-cornered hat, and held it, pulling at the plume, till the general asked him to restore it. All his absentmindedness and inability to enter a room and converse in it was, however, redeemed by his kindly, simple, and modest expression. Anna PГЎvlovna turned toward him and, with a Christian mildness that expressed forgiveness of his indiscretion, nodded and said: вҖңI hope to see you again, but I also hope you will change your opinions, my dear Monsieur Pierre.вҖқ When she said this, he did not reply and only bowed, but again everybody saw his smile, which said nothing, unless perhaps, вҖңOpinions are opinions, but you see what a capital, good-natured fellow I am.вҖқ And everyone, including Anna PГЎvlovna, felt this. Prince AndrГ©y had gone out into the hall, and, turning his shoulders to the footman who was helping him on with his cloak, listened indifferently to his wifeвҖҷs chatter with Prince Ippolit who had also come into the hall. Prince Ippolit stood close to the pretty, pregnant princess, and stared fixedly at her through his eyeglass. вҖңGo in, Annette, or you will catch cold,вҖқ said the little princess, taking leave of Anna PГЎvlovna. вҖңIt is settled,вҖқ she added in a low voice. Anna PГЎvlovna had already managed to speak to Liza about the match she contemplated between Anatole and the little princessвҖҷ sister-in-law. вҖңI rely on you, my dear,вҖқ said Anna PГЎvlovna, also in a low tone. вҖңWrite to her and let me know how her father looks at the matter. Au revoir!вҖқвҒ вҖ”and she left the hall. Prince Ippolit approached the little princess and, bending his face close to her, began to whisper something. Two footmen, the princessвҖҷ and his own, stood holding a shawl and a cloak, waiting for the conversation to finish. They listened to the French sentences which to them were meaningless, with an air of understanding but not wishing to appear to do so. The princess as usual spoke smilingly and listened with a laugh. вҖңI am very glad I did not go to the ambassadorвҖҷs,вҖқ said Prince Ippolit вҖңвҒ вҖ”so dullвҒ вҖ”. It has been a delightful evening, has it not? Delightful!вҖқ вҖңThey say the ball will be very good,вҖқ replied the princess, drawing up her downy little lip. вҖңAll the pretty women in society will be there.вҖқ вҖңNot all, for you will not be there; not all,вҖқ said Prince Ippolit smiling joyfully; and snatching the shawl from the footman, whom he even pushed aside, he began wrapping it round the princess. Either from awkwardness or intentionally (no one could have said which) after the shawl had been adjusted he kept his arm around her for a long time, as though embracing her. Still smiling, she gracefully moved away, turning and glancing at her husband. Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs eyes were closed, so weary and sleepy did he seem. вҖңAre you ready?вҖқ he asked his wife, looking past her. Prince Ippolit hurriedly put on his cloak, which in the latest fashion reached to his very heels, and, stumbling in it, ran out into the porch following the princess, whom a footman was helping into the carriage. вҖңPrincesse, au revoir,вҖқ cried he, stumbling with his tongue as well as with his feet. The princess, picking up her dress, was taking her seat in the dark carriage, her husband was adjusting his saber; Prince Ippolit, under pretense of helping, was in everyoneвҖҷs way. вҖңAllow me, sir,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y in Russian in a cold, disagreeable tone to Prince Ippolit who was blocking his path. вҖңI am expecting you, Pierre,вҖқ said the same voice, but gently and affectionately. The postilion started, the carriage wheels rattled. Prince Ippolit laughed spasmodically as he stood in the porch waiting for the vicomte whom he had promised to take home. вҖңWell, mon cher,вҖқ said the vicomte, having seated himself beside Ippolit in the carriage, вҖңyour little princess is very nice, very nice indeed, quite French,вҖқ and he kissed the tips of his fingers. Ippolit burst out laughing. вҖңDo you know, you are a terrible chap for all your innocent airs,вҖқ continued the vicomte. вҖңI pity the poor husband, that little officer who gives himself the airs of a monarch.вҖқ Ippolit spluttered again, and amid his laughter said, вҖңAnd you were saying that the Russian ladies are not equal to the French? One has to know how to deal with them.вҖқ Pierre reaching the house first went into Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs study like one quite at home, and from habit immediately lay down on the sofa, took from the shelf the first book that came to his hand (it was CaesarвҖҷs Commentaries), and resting on his elbow, began reading it in the middle. вҖңWhat have you done to Mlle.В SchГ©rer? She will be quite ill now,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, as he entered the study, rubbing his small white hands. Pierre turned his whole body, making the sofa creak. He lifted his eager face to Prince AndrГ©y, smiled, and waved his hand. вҖңThat abbГ© is very interesting but he does not see the thing in the right light.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ In my opinion perpetual peace is possible butвҒ вҖ”I do not know how to express itвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ not by a balance of political power.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ It was evident that Prince AndrГ©y was not interested in such abstract conversation. вҖңOne canвҖҷt everywhere say all one thinks, mon cher. Well, have you at last decided on anything? Are you going to be a guardsman or a diplomatist?вҖқ asked Prince AndrГ©y after a momentary silence. Pierre sat up on the sofa, with his legs tucked under him. вҖңReally, I donвҖҷt yet know. I donвҖҷt like either the one or the other.вҖқ вҖңBut you must decide on something! Your father expects it.вҖқ Pierre at the age of ten had been sent abroad with an abbГ© as tutor, and had remained away till he was twenty. When he returned to Moscow his father dismissed the abbГ© and said to the young man, вҖңNow go to Petersburg, look round, and choose your profession. I will agree to anything. Here is a letter to Prince VasГӯli, and here is money. Write to me all about it, and I will help you in everything.вҖқ Pierre had already been choosing a career for three months, and had not decided on anything. It was about this choice that Prince AndrГ©y was speaking. Pierre rubbed his forehead. вҖңBut he must be a Freemason,вҖқ said he, referring to the abbГ© whom he had met that evening. вҖңThat is all nonsense.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y again interrupted him, вҖңlet us talk business. Have you been to the Horse Guards?вҖқ вҖңNo, I have not; but this is what I have been thinking and wanted to tell you. There is a war now against Napoleon. If it were a war for freedom I could understand it and should be the first to enter the army; but to help England and Austria against the greatest man in the world is not right.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y only shrugged his shoulders at PierreвҖҷs childish words. He put on the air of one who finds it impossible to reply to such nonsense, but it would in fact have been difficult to give any other answer than the one Prince AndrГ©y gave to this naive question. вҖңIf no one fought except on his own conviction, there would be no wars,вҖқ he said. вҖңAnd that would be splendid,вҖқ said Pierre. Prince AndrГ©y smiled ironically. вҖңVery likely it would be splendid, but it will never come about.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWell, why are you going to the war?вҖқ asked Pierre. вҖңWhat for? I donвҖҷt know. I must. Besides that I am goingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He paused. вҖңI am going because the life I am leading here does not suit me!вҖқ VII VII The rustle of a womanвҖҷs dress was heard in the next room. Prince AndrГ©y shook himself as if waking up, and his face assumed the look it had had in Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs drawing room. Pierre removed his feet from the sofa. The princess came in. She had changed her gown for a house dress as fresh and elegant as the other. Prince AndrГ©y rose and politely placed a chair for her. вҖңHow is it,вҖқ she began, as usual in French, settling down briskly and fussily in the easy chair, вҖңhow is it Annette never got married? How stupid you men all are not to have married her! Excuse me for saying so, but you have no sense about women. What an argumentative fellow you are, Monsieur Pierre!вҖқ вҖңAnd I am still arguing with your husband. I canвҖҷt understand why he wants to go to the war,вҖқ replied Pierre, addressing the princess with none of the embarrassment so commonly shown by young men in their intercourse with young women. The princess started. Evidently PierreвҖҷs words touched her to the quick. вҖңAh, that is just what I tell him!вҖқ said she. вҖңI donвҖҷt understand it; I donвҖҷt in the least understand why men canвҖҷt live without wars. How is it that we women donвҖҷt want anything of the kind, donвҖҷt need it? Now you shall judge between us. I always tell him: Here he is UncleвҖҷs aide-de-camp, a most brilliant position. He is so well known, so much appreciated by everyone. The other day at the AprГЎksinsвҖҷ I heard a lady asking, вҖҳIs that the famous Prince AndrГ©?вҖҷ I did indeed.вҖқ She laughed. вҖңHe is so well received everywhere. He might easily become aide-de-camp to the Emperor. You know the Emperor spoke to him most graciously. Annette and I were speaking of how to arrange it. What do you think?вҖқ Pierre looked at his friend and, noticing that he did not like the conversation, gave no reply. вҖңWhen are you starting?вҖқ he asked. вҖңOh, donвҖҷt speak of his going, donвҖҷt! I wonвҖҷt hear it spoken of,вҖқ said the princess in the same petulantly playful tone in which she had spoken to Ippolit in the drawing room and which was so plainly ill-suited to the family circle of which Pierre was almost a member. вҖңToday when I remembered that all these delightful associations must be broken offвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and then you know, AndrГ©вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ (she looked significantly at her husband) вҖңIвҖҷm afraid, IвҖҷm afraid!вҖқ she whispered, and a shudder ran down her back. Her husband looked at her as if surprised to notice that someone besides Pierre and himself was in the room, and addressed her in a tone of frigid politeness. вҖңWhat is it you are afraid of, Liza? I donвҖҷt understand,вҖқ said he. вҖңThere, what egotists men all are: all, all egotists! Just for a whim of his own, goodness only knows why, he leaves me and locks me up alone in the country.вҖқ вҖңWith my father and sister, remember,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y gently. вҖңAlone all the same, without my friends.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And he expects me not to be afraid.вҖқ Her tone was now querulous and her lip drawn up, giving her not a joyful, but an animal, squirrel-like expression. She paused as if she felt it indecorous to speak of her pregnancy before Pierre, though the gist of the matter lay in that. вҖңI still canвҖҷt understand what you are afraid of,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y slowly, not taking his eyes off his wife. The princess blushed, and raised her arms with a gesture of despair. вҖңNo, AndrГ©y, I must say you have changed. Oh, how you haveвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYour doctor tells you to go to bed earlier,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңYou had better go.вҖқ The princess said nothing, but suddenly her short downy lip quivered. Prince AndrГ©y rose, shrugged his shoulders, and walked about the room. Pierre looked over his spectacles with naive surprise, now at him and now at her, moved as if about to rise too, but changed his mind. вҖңWhy should I mind Monsieur Pierre being here?вҖқ exclaimed the little princess suddenly, her pretty face all at once distorted by a tearful grimace. вҖңI have long wanted to ask you, AndrГ©y, why you have changed so to me? What have I done to you? You are going to the war and have no pity for me. Why is it?вҖқ вҖңLiza!вҖқ was all Prince AndrГ©y said. But that one word expressed an entreaty, a threat, and above all conviction that she would herself regret her words. But she went on hurriedly: вҖңYou treat me like an invalid or a child. I see it all! Did you behave like that six months ago?вҖқ вҖңLiza, I beg you to desist,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y still more emphatically. Pierre, who had been growing more and more agitated as he listened to all this, rose and approached the princess. He seemed unable to bear the sight of tears and was ready to cry himself. вҖңCalm yourself, Princess! It seems so to you becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I assure you I myself have experiencedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and soвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No, excuse me! An outsider is out of place hereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No, donвҖҷt distress yourselfвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Goodbye!вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y caught him by the hand. вҖңNo, wait, Pierre! The princess is too kind to wish to deprive me of the pleasure of spending the evening with you.вҖқ вҖңNo, he thinks only of himself,вҖқ muttered the princess without restraining her angry tears. вҖңLiza!вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y dryly, raising his voice to the pitch which indicates that patience is exhausted. Suddenly the angry, squirrel-like expression of the princessвҖҷ pretty face changed into a winning and piteous look of fear. Her beautiful eyes glanced askance at her husbandвҖҷs face, and her own assumed the timid, deprecating expression of a dog when it rapidly but feebly wags its drooping tail. вҖңMon Dieu, mon Dieu!вҖқ she muttered, and lifting her dress with one hand she went up to her husband and kissed him on the forehead. вҖңGood night, Liza,вҖқ said he, rising and courteously kissing her hand as he would have done to a stranger. VIII VIII The friends were silent. Neither cared to begin talking. Pierre continually glanced at Prince AndrГ©y; Prince AndrГ©y rubbed his forehead with his small hand. вҖңLet us go and have supper,вҖқ he said with a sigh, going to the door. They entered the elegant, newly decorated, and luxurious dining room. Everything from the table napkins to the silver, china, and glass bore that imprint of newness found in the households of the newly married. Halfway through supper Prince AndrГ©y leaned his elbows on the table and, with a look of nervous agitation such as Pierre had never before seen on his face, began to talkвҒ вҖ”as one who has long had something on his mind and suddenly determines to speak out. вҖңNever, never marry, my dear fellow! ThatвҖҷs my advice: never marry till you can say to yourself that you have done all you are capable of, and until you have ceased to love the woman of your choice and have seen her plainly as she is, or else you will make a cruel and irrevocable mistake. Marry when you are old and good for nothingвҒ вҖ”or all that is good and noble in you will be lost. It will all be wasted on trifles. Yes! Yes! Yes! DonвҖҷt look at me with such surprise. If you marry expecting anything from yourself in the future, you will feel at every step that for you all is ended, all is closed except the drawing room, where you will be ranged side by side with a court lackey and an idiot!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But whatвҖҷs the good?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and he waved his arm. Pierre took off his spectacles, which made his face seem different and the good-natured expression still more apparent, and gazed at his friend in amazement. вҖңMy wife,вҖқ continued Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңis an excellent woman, one of those rare women with whom a manвҖҷs honor is safe; but, O God, what would I not give now to be unmarried! You are the first and only one to whom I mention this, because I like you.вҖқ As he said this Prince AndrГ©y was less than ever like that BolkГіnski who had lolled in Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs easy chairs and with half-closed eyes had uttered French phrases between his teeth. Every muscle of his thin face was now quivering with nervous excitement; his eyes, in which the fire of life had seemed extinguished, now flashed with brilliant light. It was evident that the more lifeless he seemed at ordinary times, the more impassioned he became in these moments of almost morbid irritation. вҖңYou donвҖҷt understand why I say this,вҖқ he continued, вҖңbut it is the whole story of life. You talk of Bonaparte and his career,вҖқ said he (though Pierre had not mentioned Bonaparte), вҖңbut Bonaparte when he worked went step by step toward his goal. He was free, he had nothing but his aim to consider, and he reached it. But tie yourself up with a woman and, like a chained convict, you lose all freedom! And all you have of hope and strength merely weighs you down and torments you with regret. Drawing rooms, gossip, balls, vanity, and trivialityвҒ вҖ”these are the enchanted circle I cannot escape from. I am now going to the war, the greatest war there ever was, and I know nothing and am fit for nothing. I am very amiable and have a caustic wit,вҖқ continued Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңand at Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs they listen to me. And that stupid set without whom my wife cannot exist, and those womenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ If you only knew what those society women are, and women in general! My father is right. Selfish, vain, stupid, trivial in everythingвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs what women are when you see them in their true colors! When you meet them in society it seems as if there were something in them, but thereвҖҷs nothing, nothing, nothing! No, donвҖҷt marry, my dear fellow; donвҖҷt marry!вҖқ concluded Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңIt seems funny to me,вҖқ said Pierre, вҖңthat you, you should consider yourself incapable and your life a spoiled life. You have everything before you, everything. And youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He did not finish his sentence, but his tone showed how highly he thought of his friend and how much he expected of him in the future. вҖңHow can he talk like that?вҖқ thought Pierre. He considered his friend a model of perfection because Prince AndrГ©y possessed in the highest degree just the very qualities Pierre lacked, and which might be best described as strength of will. Pierre was always astonished at Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs calm manner of treating everybody, his extraordinary memory, his extensive reading (he had read everything, knew everything, and had an opinion about everything), but above all at his capacity for work and study. And if Pierre was often struck by AndrГ©yвҖҷs lack of capacity for philosophical meditation (to which he himself was particularly addicted), he regarded even this not as a defect but as a sign of strength. Even in the best, most friendly and simplest relations of life, praise and commendation are essential, just as grease is necessary to wheels that they may run smoothly. вҖңMy part is played out,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңWhatвҖҷs the use of talking about me? Let us talk about you,вҖқ he added after a silence, smiling at his reassuring thoughts. That smile was immediately reflected on PierreвҖҷs face. вҖңBut what is there to say about me?вҖқ said Pierre, his face relaxing into a careless, merry smile. вҖңWhat am I? An illegitimate son!вҖқ He suddenly blushed crimson, and it was plain that he had made a great effort to say this. вҖңWithout a name and without meansвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And it reallyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ But he did not say what вҖңit reallyвҖқ was. вҖңFor the present I am free and am all right. Only I havenвҖҷt the least idea what I am to do; I wanted to consult you seriously.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y looked kindly at him, yet his glanceвҒ вҖ”friendly and affectionate as it wasвҒ вҖ”expressed a sense of his own superiority. вҖңI am fond of you, especially as you are the one live man among our whole set. Yes, youвҖҷre all right! Choose what you will; itвҖҷs all the same. YouвҖҷll be all right anywhere. But look here: give up visiting those KurГЎgins and leading that sort of life. It suits you so badlyвҒ вҖ”all this debauchery, dissipation, and the rest of it!вҖқ вҖңWhat would you have, my dear fellow?вҖқ answered Pierre, shrugging his shoulders. вҖңWomen, my dear fellow; women!вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt understand it,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңWomen who are comme il faut, thatвҖҷs a different matter; but the KurГЎginsвҖҷ set of women, вҖҳwomen and wine,вҖҷ I donвҖҷt understand!вҖқ Pierre was staying at Prince VasГӯli KurГЎginвҖҷs and sharing the dissipated life of his son Anatole, the son whom they were planning to reform by marrying him to Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs sister. вҖңDo you know?вҖқ said Pierre, as if suddenly struck by a happy thought, вҖңseriously, I have long been thinking of it.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Leading such a life I canвҖҷt decide or think properly about anything. OneвҖҷs head aches, and one spends all oneвҖҷs money. He asked me for tonight, but I wonвҖҷt go.вҖқ вҖңYou give me your word of honor not to go?вҖқ вҖңOn my honor!вҖқ IX IX It was past one oвҖҷclock when Pierre left his friend. It was a cloudless, northern, summer night. Pierre took an open cab intending to drive straight home. But the nearer he drew to the house the more he felt the impossibility of going to sleep on such a night. It was light enough to see a long way in the deserted street and it seemed more like morning or evening than night. On the way Pierre remembered that Anatole KurГЎgin was expecting the usual set for cards that evening, after which there was generally a drinking bout, finishing with visits of a kind Pierre was very fond of. вҖңI should like to go to KurГЎginвҖҷs,вҖқ thought he. But he immediately recalled his promise to Prince AndrГ©y not to go there. Then, as happens to people of weak character, he desired so passionately once more to enjoy that dissipation he was so accustomed to that he decided to go. The thought immediately occurred to him that his promise to Prince AndrГ©y was of no account, because before he gave it he had already promised Prince Anatole to come to his gathering; вҖңbesides,вҖқ thought he, вҖңall such вҖҳwords of honorвҖҷ are conventional things with no definite meaning, especially if one considers that by tomorrow one may be dead, or something so extraordinary may happen to one that honor and dishonor will be all the same!вҖқ Pierre often indulged in reflections of this sort, nullifying all his decisions and intentions. He went to KurГЎginвҖҷs. Reaching the large house near the Horse GuardsвҖҷ barracks, in which Anatole lived, Pierre entered the lighted porch, ascended the stairs, and went in at the open door. There was no one in the anteroom; empty bottles, cloaks, and overshoes were lying about; there was a smell of alcohol, and sounds of voices and shouting in the distance. Cards and supper were over, but the visitors had not yet dispersed. Pierre threw off his cloak and entered the first room, in which were the remains of supper. A footman, thinking no one saw him, was drinking on the sly what was left in the glasses. From the third room came sounds of laughter, the shouting of familiar voices, the growling of a bear, and general commotion. Some eight or nine young men were crowding anxiously round an open window. Three others were romping with a young bear, one pulling him by the chain and trying to set him at the others. вҖңI bet a hundred on Stevens!вҖқ shouted one. вҖңMind, no holding on!вҖқ cried another. вҖңI bet on DГіlokhov!вҖқ cried a third. вҖңKurГЎgin, you part our hands.вҖқ вҖңThere, leave MГӯshka alone; hereвҖҷs a bet on.вҖқ вҖңAt one draught, or he loses!вҖқ shouted a fourth. вҖңYГЎkov, bring a bottle!вҖқ shouted the host, a tall, handsome fellow who stood in the midst of the group, without a coat, and with his fine linen shirt unfastened in front. вҖңWait a bit, you fellows.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Here is PГ©trusha! Good man!вҖқ cried he, addressing Pierre. Another voice, from a man of medium height with clear blue eyes, particularly striking among all these drunken voices by its sober ring, cried from the window: вҖңCome here; part the bets!вҖқ This was DГіlokhov, an officer of the SemГ«nov regiment, a notorious gambler and duelist, who was living with Anatole. Pierre smiled, looking about him merrily. вҖңI donвҖҷt understand. WhatвҖҷs it all about?вҖқ вҖңWait a bit, he is not drunk yet! A bottle here,вҖқ said Anatole, and taking a glass from the table he went up to Pierre. вҖңFirst of all you must drink!вҖқ Pierre drank one glass after another, looking from under his brows at the tipsy guests who were again crowding round the window, and listening to their chatter. Anatole kept on refilling PierreвҖҷs glass while explaining that DГіlokhov was betting with Stevens, an English naval officer, that he would drink a bottle of rum sitting on the outer ledge of the third floor window with his legs hanging out. вҖңGo on, you must drink it all,вҖқ said Anatole, giving Pierre the last glass, вҖңor I wonвҖҷt let you go!вҖқ вҖңNo, I wonвҖҷt,вҖқ said Pierre, pushing Anatole aside, and he went up to the window. DГіlokhov was holding the EnglishmanвҖҷs hand and clearly and distinctly repeating the terms of the bet, addressing himself particularly to Anatole and Pierre. DГіlokhov was of medium height, with curly hair and light-blue eyes. He was about twenty-five. Like all infantry officers he wore no mustache, so that his mouth, the most striking feature of his face, was clearly seen. The lines of that mouth were remarkably finely curved. The middle of the upper lip formed a sharp wedge and closed firmly on the firm lower one, and something like two distinct smiles played continually round the two corners of the mouth; this, together with the resolute, insolent intelligence of his eyes, produced an effect which made it impossible not to notice his face. DГіlokhov was a man of small means and no connections. Yet, though Anatole spent tens of thousands of rubles, DГіlokhov lived with him and had placed himself on such a footing that all who knew them, including Anatole himself, respected him more than they did Anatole. DГіlokhov could play all games and nearly always won. However much he drank, he never lost his clearheadedness. Both KurГЎgin and DГіlokhov were at that time notorious among the rakes and scapegraces of Petersburg. The bottle of rum was brought. The window frame which prevented anyone from sitting on the outer sill was being forced out by two footmen, who were evidently flurried and intimidated by the directions and shouts of the gentlemen around. Anatole with his swaggering air strode up to the window. He wanted to smash something. Pushing away the footmen he tugged at the frame, but could not move it. He smashed a pane. вҖңYou have a try, Hercules,вҖқ said he, turning to Pierre. Pierre seized the crossbeam, tugged, and wrenched the oak frame out with a crash. вҖңTake it right out, or theyвҖҷll think IвҖҷm holding on,вҖқ said DГіlokhov. вҖңIs the Englishman bragging?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Eh? Is it all right?вҖқ said Anatole. вҖңFirst-rate,вҖқ said Pierre, looking at DГіlokhov, who with a bottle of rum in his hand was approaching the window, from which the light of the sky, the dawn merging with the afterglow of sunset, was visible. DГіlokhov, the bottle of rum still in his hand, jumped onto the window sill. вҖңListen!вҖқ cried he, standing there and addressing those in the room. All were silent. вҖңI bet fifty imperialsвҖқвҒ вҖ”he spoke French that the Englishman might understand him, but he did not speak it very wellвҒ вҖ”вҖңI bet fifty imperialsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ or do you wish to make it a hundred?вҖқ added he, addressing the Englishman. вҖңNo, fifty,вҖқ replied the latter. вҖңAll right. Fifty imperialsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ that I will drink a whole bottle of rum without taking it from my mouth, sitting outside the window on this spotвҖқ (he stooped and pointed to the sloping ledge outside the window) вҖңand without holding on to anything. Is that right?вҖқ вҖңQuite right,вҖқ said the Englishman. Anatole turned to the Englishman and taking him by one of the buttons of his coat and looking down at himвҒ вҖ”the Englishman was shortвҒ вҖ”began repeating the terms of the wager to him in English. вҖңWait!вҖқ cried DГіlokhov, hammering with the bottle on the window sill to attract attention. вҖңWait a bit, KurГЎgin. Listen! If anyone else does the same, I will pay him a hundred imperials. Do you understand?вҖқ The Englishman nodded, but gave no indication whether he intended to accept this challenge or not. Anatole did not release him, and though he kept nodding to show that he understood, Anatole went on translating DГіlokhovвҖҷs words into English. A thin young lad, an hussar of the Life Guards, who had been losing that evening, climbed on the window sill, leaned over, and looked down. вҖңOh! Oh! Oh!вҖқ he muttered, looking down from the window at the stones of the pavement. вҖңShut up!вҖқ cried DГіlokhov, pushing him away from the window. The lad jumped awkwardly back into the room, tripping over his spurs. Placing the bottle on the window sill where he could reach it easily, DГіlokhov climbed carefully and slowly through the window and lowered his legs. Pressing against both sides of the window, he adjusted himself on his seat, lowered his hands, moved a little to the right and then to the left, and took up the bottle. Anatole brought two candles and placed them on the window sill, though it was already quite light. DГіlokhovвҖҷs back in his white shirt, and his curly head, were lit up from both sides. Everyone crowded to the window, the Englishman in front. Pierre stood smiling but silent. One man, older than the others present, suddenly pushed forward with a scared and angry look and wanted to seize hold of DГіlokhovвҖҷs shirt. вҖңI say, this is folly! HeвҖҷll be killed,вҖқ said this more sensible man. Anatole stopped him. вҖңDonвҖҷt touch him! YouвҖҷll startle him and then heвҖҷll be killed. Eh?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What then?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Eh?вҖқ DГіlokhov turned round and, again holding on with both hands, arranged himself on his seat. вҖңIf anyone comes meddling again,вҖқ said he, emitting the words separately through his thin compressed lips, вҖңI will throw him down there. Now then!вҖқ Saying this he again turned round, dropped his hands, took the bottle and lifted it to his lips, threw back his head, and raised his free hand to balance himself. One of the footmen who had stooped to pick up some broken glass remained in that position without taking his eyes from the window and from DГіlokhovвҖҷs back. Anatole stood erect with staring eyes. The Englishman looked on sideways, pursing up his lips. The man who had wished to stop the affair ran to a corner of the room and threw himself on a sofa with his face to the wall. Pierre hid his face, from which a faint smile forgot to fade though his features now expressed horror and fear. All were still. Pierre took his hands from his eyes. DГіlokhov still sat in the same position, only his head was thrown further back till his curly hair touched his shirt collar, and the hand holding the bottle was lifted higher and higher and trembled with the effort. The bottle was emptying perceptibly and rising still higher and his head tilting yet further back. вҖңWhy is it so long?вҖқ thought Pierre. It seemed to him that more than half an hour had elapsed. Suddenly DГіlokhov made a backward movement with his spine, and his arm trembled nervously; this was sufficient to cause his whole body to slip as he sat on the sloping ledge. As he began slipping down, his head and arm wavered still more with the strain. One hand moved as if to clutch the window sill, but refrained from touching it. Pierre again covered his eyes and thought he would never open them again. Suddenly he was aware of a stir all around. He looked up: DГіlokhov was standing on the window sill, with a pale but radiant face. вҖңItвҖҷs empty.вҖқ He threw the bottle to the Englishman, who caught it neatly. DГіlokhov jumped down. He smelt strongly of rum. вҖңWell done!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Fine fellow!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ThereвҖҷs a bet for you!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Devil take you!вҖқ came from different sides. The Englishman took out his purse and began counting out the money. DГіlokhov stood frowning and did not speak. Pierre jumped upon the window sill. вҖңGentlemen, who wishes to bet with me? IвҖҷll do the same thing!вҖқ he suddenly cried. вҖңEven without a bet, there! Tell them to bring me a bottle. IвҖҷll do it.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Bring a bottle!вҖқ вҖңLet him do it, let him do it,вҖқ said DГіlokhov, smiling. вҖңWhat next? Have you gone mad?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No one would let you!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Why, you go giddy even on a staircase,вҖқ exclaimed several voices. вҖңIвҖҷll drink it! LetвҖҷs have a bottle of rum!вҖқ shouted Pierre, banging the table with a determined and drunken gesture and preparing to climb out of the window. They seized him by his arms; but he was so strong that everyone who touched him was sent flying. вҖңNo, youвҖҷll never manage him that way,вҖқ said Anatole. вҖңWait a bit and IвҖҷll get round him.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Listen! IвҖҷll take your bet tomorrow, but now we are all going to вё»вҖҷs.вҖқ вҖңCome on then,вҖқ cried Pierre. вҖңCome on!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And weвҖҷll take MГӯshka with us.вҖқ And he caught the bear, took it in his arms, lifted it from the ground, and began dancing round the room with it. I I In October, 1805, a Russian army was occupying the villages and towns of the Archduchy of Austria, and yet other regiments freshly arriving from Russia were settling near the fortress of Braunau and burdening the inhabitants on whom they were quartered. Braunau was the headquarters of the commander in chief, KutГәzov. On October 11, 1805, one of the infantry regiments that had just reached Braunau had halted half a mile from the town, waiting to be inspected by the commander in chief. Despite the un-Russian appearance of the locality and surroundingsвҒ вҖ”fruit gardens, stone fences, tiled roofs, and hills in the distanceвҒ вҖ”and despite the fact that the inhabitants (who gazed with curiosity at the soldiers) were not Russians, the regiment had just the appearance of any Russian regiment preparing for an inspection anywhere in the heart of Russia. On the evening of the last dayвҖҷs march an order had been received that the commander in chief would inspect the regiment on the march. Though the words of the order were not clear to the regimental commander, and the question arose whether the troops were to be in marching order or not, it was decided at a consultation between the battalion commanders to present the regiment in parade order, on the principle that it is always better to вҖңbow too low than not bow low enough.вҖқ So the soldiers, after a twenty-mile march, were kept mending and cleaning all night long without closing their eyes, while the adjutants and company commanders calculated and reckoned, and by morning the regimentвҒ вҖ”instead of the straggling, disorderly crowd it had been on its last march the day beforeвҒ вҖ”presented a well-ordered array of two thousand men each of whom knew his place and his duty, had every button and every strap in place, and shone with cleanliness. And not only externally was all in order, but had it pleased the commander in chief to look under the uniforms he would have found on every man a clean shirt, and in every knapsack the appointed number of articles, вҖңawl, soap, and all,вҖқ as the soldiers say. There was only one circumstance concerning which no one could be at ease. It was the state of the soldiersвҖҷ boots. More than half the menвҖҷs boots were in holes. But this defect was not due to any fault of the regimental commander, for in spite of repeated demands boots had not been issued by the Austrian commissariat, and the regiment had marched some seven hundred miles. The commander of the regiment was an elderly, choleric, stout, and thickset general with grizzled eyebrows and whiskers, and wider from chest to back than across the shoulders. He had on a brand-new uniform showing the creases where it had been folded and thick gold epaulettes which seemed to stand rather than lie down on his massive shoulders. He had the air of a man happily performing one of the most solemn duties of his life. He walked about in front of the line and at every step pulled himself up, slightly arching his back. It was plain that the commander admired his regiment, rejoiced in it, and that his whole mind was engrossed by it, yet his strut seemed to indicate that, besides military matters, social interests and the fair sex occupied no small part of his thoughts. вҖңWell, MikhГЎil MГӯtrich, sir?вҖқ he said, addressing one of the battalion commanders who smilingly pressed forward (it was plain that they both felt happy). вҖңWe had our hands full last night. However, I think the regiment is not a bad one, eh?вҖқ The battalion commander perceived the jovial irony and laughed. вҖңIt would not be turned off the field even on the TsarГӯtsin Meadow.вҖқ вҖңWhat?вҖқ asked the commander. At that moment, on the road from the town on which signalers had been posted, two men appeared on horse back. They were an aide-de-camp followed by a Cossack. The aide-de-camp was sent to confirm the order which had not been clearly worded the day before, namely, that the commander in chief wished to see the regiment just in the state in which it had been on the march: in their greatcoats, and packs, and without any preparation whatever. A member of the Hofkriegsrath from Vienna had come to KutГәzov the day before with proposals and demands for him to join up with the army of the Archduke Ferdinand and Mack, and KutГәzov, not considering this junction advisable, meant, among other arguments in support of his view, to show the Austrian general the wretched state in which the troops arrived from Russia. With this object he intended to meet the regiment; so the worse the condition it was in, the better pleased the commander in chief would be. Though the aide-de-camp did not know these circumstances, he nevertheless delivered the definite order that the men should be in their greatcoats and in marching order, and that the commander in chief would otherwise be dissatisfied. On hearing this the regimental commander hung his head, silently shrugged his shoulders, and spread out his arms with a choleric gesture. вҖңA fine mess weвҖҷve made of it!вҖқ he remarked. вҖңThere now! DidnвҖҷt I tell you, MikhГЎil MГӯtrich, that if it was said вҖҳon the marchвҖҷ it meant in greatcoats?вҖқ said he reproachfully to the battalion commander. вҖңOh, my God!вҖқ he added, stepping resolutely forward. вҖңCompany commanders!вҖқ he shouted in a voice accustomed to command. вҖңSergeants major!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ How soon will he be here?вҖқ he asked the aide-de-camp with a respectful politeness evidently relating to the personage he was referring to. вҖңIn an hourвҖҷs time, I should say.вҖқ вҖңShall we have time to change clothes?вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt know, General.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The regimental commander, going up to the line himself, ordered the soldiers to change into their greatcoats. The company commanders ran off to their companies, the sergeants major began bustling (the greatcoats were not in very good condition), and instantly the squares that had up to then been in regular order and silent began to sway and stretch and hum with voices. On all sides soldiers were running to and fro, throwing up their knapsacks with a jerk of their shoulders and pulling the straps over their heads, unstrapping their overcoats and drawing the sleeves on with upraised arms. In half an hour all was again in order, only the squares had become gray instead of black. The regimental commander walked with his jerky steps to the front of the regiment and examined it from a distance. вҖңWhatever is this? This!вҖқ he shouted and stood still. вҖңCommander of the third company!вҖқ вҖңCommander of the third company wanted by the general!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ commander to the generalвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ third company to the commander.вҖқ The words passed along the lines and an adjutant ran to look for the missing officer. When the eager but misrepeated words had reached their destination in a cry of: вҖңThe general to the third company,вҖқ the missing officer appeared from behind his company and, though he was a middle-aged man and not in the habit of running, trotted awkwardly stumbling on his toes toward the general. The captainвҖҷs face showed the uneasiness of a schoolboy who is told to repeat a lesson he has not learned. Spots appeared on his nose, the redness of which was evidently due to intemperance, and his mouth twitched nervously. The general looked the captain up and down as he came up panting, slackening his pace as he approached. вҖңYou will soon be dressing your men in petticoats! What is this?вҖқ shouted the regimental commander, thrusting forward his jaw and pointing at a soldier in the ranks of the third company in a greatcoat of bluish cloth, which contrasted with the others. вҖңWhat have you been after? The commander in chief is expected and you leave your place? Eh? IвҖҷll teach you to dress the men in fancy coats for a parade.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ EhвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ?вҖқ The commander of the company, with his eyes fixed on his superior, pressed two fingers more and more rigidly to his cap, as if in this pressure lay his only hope of salvation. вҖңWell, why donвҖҷt you speak? Whom have you got there dressed up as a Hungarian?вҖқ said the commander with an austere gibe. вҖңYour excellencyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWell, your excellency, what? Your excellency! But what about your excellency?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ nobody knows.вҖқ вҖңYour excellency, itвҖҷs the officer DГіlokhov, who has been reduced to the ranks,вҖқ said the captain softly. вҖңWell? Has he been degraded into a field marshal, or into a soldier? If a soldier, he should be dressed in regulation uniform like the others.вҖқ вҖңYour excellency, you gave him leave yourself, on the march.вҖқ вҖңGave him leave? Leave? ThatвҖҷs just like you young men,вҖқ said the regimental commander cooling down a little. вҖңLeave indeed.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ One says a word to you and youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What?вҖқ he added with renewed irritation, вҖңI beg you to dress your men decently.вҖқ And the commander, turning to look at the adjutant, directed his jerky steps down the line. He was evidently pleased at his own display of anger and walking up to the regiment wished to find a further excuse for wrath. Having snapped at an officer for an unpolished badge, at another because his line was not straight, he reached the third company. вҖңH-o-o-w are you standing? WhereвҖҷs your leg? Your leg?вҖқ shouted the commander with a tone of suffering in his voice, while there were still five men between him and DГіlokhov with his bluish-gray uniform. DГіlokhov slowly straightened his bent knee, looking straight with his clear, insolent eyes in the generalвҖҷs face. вҖңWhy a blue coat? Off with itвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Sergeant major! Change his coatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the rasвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he did not finish. вҖңGeneral, I must obey orders, but I am not bound to endureвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ DГіlokhov hurriedly interrupted. вҖңNo talking in the ranks!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No talking, no talking!вҖқ вҖңNot bound to endure insults,вҖқ DГіlokhov concluded in loud, ringing tones. The eyes of the general and the soldier met. The general became silent, angrily pulling down his tight scarf. вҖңI request you to have the goodness to change your coat,вҖқ he said as he turned away. X X Prince AndrГ©y stayed at BrГјnn with BilГӯbin, a Russian acquaintance of his in the diplomatic service. вҖңAh, my dear prince! I could not have a more welcome visitor,вҖқ said BilГӯbin as he came out to meet Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңFranz, put the princeвҖҷs things in my bedroom,вҖқ said he to the servant who was ushering BolkГіnski in. вҖңSo youвҖҷre a messenger of victory, eh? Splendid! And I am sitting here ill, as you see.вҖқ After washing and dressing, Prince AndrГ©y came into the diplomatвҖҷs luxurious study and sat down to the dinner prepared for him. BilГӯbin settled down comfortably beside the fire. After his journey and the campaign during which he had been deprived of all the comforts of cleanliness and all the refinements of life, Prince AndrГ©y felt a pleasant sense of repose among luxurious surroundings such as he had been accustomed to from childhood. Besides it was pleasant, after his reception by the Austrians, to speak if not in Russian (for they were speaking French) at least with a Russian who would, he supposed, share the general Russian antipathy to the Austrians which was then particularly strong. BilГӯbin was a man of thirty-five, a bachelor, and of the same circle as Prince AndrГ©y. They had known each other previously in Petersburg, but had become more intimate when Prince AndrГ©y was in Vienna with KutГәzov. Just as Prince AndrГ©y was a young man who gave promise of rising high in the military profession, so to an even greater extent BilГӯbin gave promise of rising in his diplomatic career. He was still a young man but no longer a young diplomat, as he had entered the service at the age of sixteen, had been in Paris and Copenhagen, and now held a rather important post in Vienna. Both the foreign minister and our ambassador in Vienna knew him and valued him. He was not one of those many diplomats who are esteemed because they have certain negative qualities, avoid doing certain things, and speak French. He was one of those, who, liking work, knew how to do it, and despite his indolence would sometimes spend a whole night at his writing table. He worked well whatever the import of his work. It was not the question вҖңWhat for?вҖқ but the question вҖңHow?вҖқ that interested him. What the diplomatic matter might be he did not care, but it gave him great pleasure to prepare a circular, memorandum, or report, skillfully, pointedly, and elegantly. BilГӯbinвҖҷs services were valued not only for what he wrote, but also for his skill in dealing and conversing with those in the highest spheres. BilГӯbin liked conversation as he liked work, only when it could be made elegantly witty. In society he always awaited an opportunity to say something striking and took part in a conversation only when that was possible. His conversation was always sprinkled with wittily original, finished phrases of general interest. These sayings were prepared in the inner laboratory of his mind in a portable form as if intentionally, so that insignificant society people might carry them from drawing room to drawing room. And, in fact, BilibineвҖҷs witticisms were hawked about in the Viennese drawing rooms and often had an influence on matters considered important. His thin, worn, sallow face was covered with deep wrinkles, which always looked as clean and well washed as the tips of oneвҖҷs fingers after a Russian bath. The movement of these wrinkles formed the principal play of expression on his face. Now his forehead would pucker into deep folds and his eyebrows were lifted, then his eyebrows would descend and deep wrinkles would crease his cheeks. His small, deep-set eyes always twinkled and looked out straight. вҖңWell, now tell me about your exploits,вҖқ said he. BolkГіnski, very modestly without once mentioning himself, described the engagement and his reception by the Minister of War. вҖңThey received me and my news as one receives a dog in a game of skittles,вҖқ said he in conclusion. BilГӯbin smiled and the wrinkles on his face disappeared. вҖңCependant, mon cher,вҖқ he remarked, examining his nails from a distance and puckering the skin above his left eye, вҖңmalgrГ© la haute estime que je professe pour the Orthodox Russian army, jвҖҷavoue que votre victoire nвҖҷest pas des plus victorieuses.вҖқ25 He went on talking in this way in French, uttering only those words in Russian on which he wished to put a contemptuous emphasis. вҖңCome now! You with all your forces fall on the unfortunate Mortier and his one division, and even then Mortier slips through your fingers! WhereвҖҷs the victory?вҖқ вҖңBut seriously,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңwe can at any rate say without boasting that it was a little better than at UlmвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhy didnвҖҷt you capture one, just one, marshal for us?вҖқ вҖңBecause not everything happens as one expects or with the smoothness of a parade. We had expected, as I told you, to get at their rear by seven in the morning but had not reached it by five in the afternoon.вҖқ вҖңAnd why didnвҖҷt you do it at seven in the morning? You ought to have been there at seven in the morning,вҖқ returned BilГӯbin with a smile. вҖңYou ought to have been there at seven in the morning.вҖқ вҖңWhy did you not succeed in impressing on Bonaparte by diplomatic methods that he had better leave Genoa alone?вҖқ retorted Prince AndrГ©y in the same tone. вҖңI know,вҖқ interrupted BilГӯbin, вҖңyouвҖҷre thinking itвҖҷs very easy to take marshals, sitting on a sofa by the fire! That is true, but still why didnвҖҷt you capture him? So donвҖҷt be surprised if not only the Minister of War but also his Most August Majesty the Emperor and King Francis is not much delighted by your victory. Even I, a poor secretary of the Russian Embassy, do not feel any need in token of my joy to give my Franz a thaler, or let him go with his Liebchen to the PraterвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ True, we have no Prater hereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He looked straight at Prince AndrГ©y and suddenly unwrinkled his forehead. вҖңIt is now my turn to ask you вҖҳwhy?вҖҷ mon cher,вҖқ said BolkГіnski. вҖңI confess I do not understand: perhaps there are diplomatic subtleties here beyond my feeble intelligence, but I canвҖҷt make it out. Mack loses a whole army, the Archduke Ferdinand and the Archduke Karl give no signs of life and make blunder after blunder. KutГәzov alone at last gains a real victory, destroying the spell of the invincibility of the French, and the Minister of War does not even care to hear the details.вҖқ вҖңThatвҖҷs just it, my dear fellow. You see itвҖҷs hurrah for the Tsar, for Russia, for the Orthodox Greek faith! All that is beautiful, but what do we, I mean the Austrian court, care for your victories? Bring us nice news of a victory by the Archduke Karl or Ferdinand (one archdukeвҖҷs as good as another, as you know) and even if it is only over a fire brigade of BonaparteвҖҷs, that will be another story and weвҖҷll fire off some cannon! But this sort of thing seems done on purpose to vex us. The Archduke Karl does nothing, the Archduke Ferdinand disgraces himself. You abandon Vienna, give up its defenseвҒ вҖ”as much as to say: вҖҳHeaven is with us, but heaven help you and your capital!вҖҷ The one general whom we all loved, Schmidt, you expose to a bullet, and then you congratulate us on the victory! Admit that more irritating news than yours could not have been conceived. ItвҖҷs as if it had been done on purpose, on purpose. Besides, suppose you did gain a brilliant victory, if even the Archduke Karl gained a victory, what effect would that have on the general course of events? ItвҖҷs too late now when Vienna is occupied by the French army!вҖқ вҖңWhat? Occupied? Vienna occupied?вҖқ вҖңNot only occupied, but Bonaparte is at SchГ¶nbrunn, and the count, our dear Count Vrbna, goes to him for orders.вҖқ After the fatigues and impressions of the journey, his reception, and especially after having dined, BolkГіnski felt that he could not take in the full significance of the words he heard. вҖңCount Lichtenfels was here this morning,вҖқ BilГӯbin continued, вҖңand showed me a letter in which the parade of the French in Vienna was fully described: Prince Murat et tout le tremblementвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You see that your victory is not a matter for great rejoicing and that you canвҖҷt be received as a savior.вҖқ вҖңReally I donвҖҷt care about that, I donвҖҷt care at all,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, beginning to understand that his news of the battle before Krems was really of small importance in view of such events as the fall of AustriaвҖҷs capital. вҖңHow is it Vienna was taken? What of the bridge and its celebrated bridgehead and Prince Auersperg? We heard reports that Prince Auersperg was defending Vienna?вҖқ he said. вҖңPrince Auersperg is on this, on our side of the river, and is defending usвҒ вҖ”doing it very badly, I think, but still he is defending us. But Vienna is on the other side. No, the bridge has not yet been taken and I hope it will not be, for it is mined and orders have been given to blow it up. Otherwise we should long ago have been in the mountains of Bohemia, and you and your army would have spent a bad quarter of an hour between two fires.вҖқ вҖңBut still this does not mean that the campaign is over,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңWell, I think it is. The bigwigs here think so too, but they darenвҖҷt say so. It will be as I said at the beginning of the campaign, it wonвҖҷt be your skirmishing at DГјrrenstein, or gunpowder at all, that will decide the matter, but those who devised it,вҖқ said BilГӯbin quoting one of his own mots, releasing the wrinkles on his forehead, and pausing. вҖңThe only question is what will come of the meeting between the Emperor Alexander and the King of Prussia in Berlin? If Prussia joins the Allies, AustriaвҖҷs hand will be forced and there will be war. If not it is merely a question of settling where the preliminaries of the new Campo Formio are to be drawn up.вҖқ вҖңWhat an extraordinary genius!вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y suddenly exclaimed, clenching his small hand and striking the table with it, вҖңand what luck the man has!вҖқ вҖңBuonaparte?вҖқ said BilГӯbin inquiringly, puckering up his forehead to indicate that he was about to say something witty. вҖңBuonaparte?вҖқ he repeated, accentuating the u: вҖңI think, however, now that he lays down laws for Austria at SchГ¶nbrunn, il faut lui faire grГўce de lвҖҷu!26 I shall certainly adopt an innovation and call him simply Bonaparte!вҖқ вҖңBut joking apart,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңdo you really think the campaign is over?вҖқ вҖңThis is what I think. Austria has been made a fool of, and she is not used to it. She will retaliate. And she has been fooled in the first place because her provinces have been pillagedвҒ вҖ”they say the Holy Russian army loots terriblyвҒ вҖ”her army is destroyed, her capital taken, and all this for the beaux yeux27 of His Sardinian Majesty. And thereforeвҒ вҖ”this is between ourselvesвҒ вҖ”I instinctively feel that we are being deceived, my instinct tells me of negotiations with France and projects for peace, a secret peace concluded separately.вҖқ вҖңImpossible!вҖқ cried Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңThat would be too base.вҖқ вҖңIf we live we shall see,вҖқ replied BilГӯbin, his face again becoming smooth as a sign that the conversation was at an end. When Prince AndrГ©y reached the room prepared for him and lay down in a clean shirt on the feather bed with its warmed and fragrant pillows, he felt that the battle of which he had brought tidings was far, far away from him. The alliance with Prussia, AustriaвҖҷs treachery, BonaparteвҖҷs new triumph, tomorrowвҖҷs levee and parade, and the audience with the Emperor Francis occupied his thoughts. He closed his eyes, and immediately a sound of cannonading, of musketry and the rattling of carriage wheels seemed to fill his ears, and now again drawn out in a thin line the musketeers were descending the hill, the French were firing, and he felt his heart palpitating as he rode forward beside Schmidt with the bullets merrily whistling all around, and he experienced tenfold the joy of living, as he had not done since childhood. He woke upвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңYes, that all happened!вҖқ he said, and, smiling happily to himself like a child, he fell into a deep, youthful slumber. XI XI Next day he woke late. Recalling his recent impressions, the first thought that came into his mind was that today he had to be presented to the Emperor Francis; he remembered the Minister of War, the polite Austrian adjutant, BilГӯbin, and last nightвҖҷs conversation. Having dressed for his attendance at court in full parade uniform, which he had not worn for a long time, he went into BilГӯbinвҖҷs study fresh, animated, and handsome, with his hand bandaged. In the study were four gentlemen of the diplomatic corps. With Prince Ippolit KurГЎgin, who was a secretary to the embassy, BolkГіnski was already acquainted. BilГӯbin introduced him to the others. The gentlemen assembled at BilГӯbinвҖҷs were young, wealthy, gay society men, who here, as in Vienna, formed a special set which BilГӯbin, their leader, called les nГҙtres.28 This set, consisting almost exclusively of diplomats, evidently had its own interests which had nothing to do with war or politics but related to high society, to certain women, and to the official side of the service. These gentlemen received Prince AndrГ©y as one of themselves, an honor they did not extend to many. From politeness and to start conversation, they asked him a few questions about the army and the battle, and then the talk went off into merry jests and gossip. вҖңBut the best of it was,вҖқ said one, telling of the misfortune of a fellow diplomat, вҖңthat the Chancellor told him flatly that his appointment to London was a promotion and that he was so to regard it. Can you fancy the figure he cut?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңBut the worst of it, gentlemenвҒ вҖ”I am giving KurГЎgin away to youвҒ вҖ”is that that man suffers, and this Don Juan, wicked fellow, is taking advantage of it!вҖқ Prince Ippolit was lolling in a lounge chair with his legs over its arm. He began to laugh. вҖңTell me about that!вҖқ he said. вҖңOh, you Don Juan! You serpent!вҖқ cried several voices. вҖңYou, BolkГіnski, donвҖҷt know,вҖқ said BilГӯbin turning to Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңthat all the atrocities of the French army (I nearly said of the Russian army) are nothing compared to what this man has been doing among the women!вҖқ вҖңLa femme est la compagne de lвҖҷhomme,вҖқ29 announced Prince Ippolit, and began looking through a lorgnette at his elevated legs. BilГӯbin and the rest of вҖңoursвҖқ burst out laughing in IppolitвҖҷs face, and Prince AndrГ©y saw that Ippolit, of whomвҒ вҖ”he had to admitвҒ вҖ”he had almost been jealous on his wifeвҖҷs account, was the butt of this set. вҖңOh, I must give you a treat,вҖқ BilГӯbin whispered to BolkГіnski. вҖңKurГЎgin is exquisite when he discusses politicsвҒ вҖ”you should see his gravity!вҖқ He sat down beside Ippolit and wrinkling his forehead began talking to him about politics. Prince AndrГ©y and the others gathered round these two. вҖңThe Berlin cabinet cannot express a feeling of alliance,вҖқ began Ippolit gazing round with importance at the others, вҖңwithout expressingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ as in its last noteвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ you understandвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Besides, unless His Majesty the Emperor derogates from the principle of our allianceвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңWait, I have not finishedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he said to Prince AndrГ©y, seizing him by the arm, вҖңI believe that intervention will be stronger than nonintervention. AndвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he paused. вҖңFinally one cannot impute the nonreceipt of our dispatch of November 18. That is how it will end.вҖқ And he released BolkГіnskiвҖҷs arm to indicate that he had now quite finished. вҖңDemosthenes, I know thee by the pebble thou secretest in thy golden mouth!вҖқ said BilГӯbin, and the mop of hair on his head moved with satisfaction. Everybody laughed, and Ippolit louder than anyone. He was evidently distressed, and breathed painfully, but could not restrain the wild laughter that convulsed his usually impassive features. вҖңWell now, gentlemen,вҖқ said BilГӯbin, вҖңBolkГіnski is my guest in this house and in BrГјnn itself. I want to entertain him as far as I can, with all the pleasures of life here. If we were in Vienna it would be easy, but here, in this wretched Moravian hole, it is more difficult, and I beg you all to help me. BrГјnnвҖҷs attractions must be shown him. You can undertake the theater, I society, and you, Ippolit, of course the women.вҖқ вҖңWe must let him see AmГ©lie, sheвҖҷs exquisite!вҖқ said one of вҖңours,вҖқ kissing his finger tips. вҖңIn general we must turn this bloodthirsty soldier to more humane interests,вҖқ said BilГӯbin. вҖңI shall scarcely be able to avail myself of your hospitality, gentlemen, it is already time for me to go,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y looking at his watch. вҖңWhere to?вҖқ вҖңTo the Emperor.вҖқ вҖңOh! Oh! Oh!вҖқ вҖңWell, au revoir, BolkГіnski! Au revoir, Prince! Come back early to dinner,вҖқ cried several voices. вҖңWeвҖҷll take you in hand.вҖқ вҖңWhen speaking to the Emperor, try as far as you can to praise the way that provisions are supplied and the routes indicated,вҖқ said BilГӯbin, accompanying him to the hall. вҖңI should like to speak well of them, but as far as I know the facts, I canвҖҷt,вҖқ replied BolkГіnski, smiling. вҖңWell, talk as much as you can, anyway. He has a passion for giving audiences, but he does not like talking himself and canвҖҷt do it, as you will see.вҖқ XII XII At the levee Prince AndrГ©y stood among the Austrian officers as he had been told to, and the Emperor Francis merely looked fixedly into his face and just nodded to him with his long head. But after it was over, the adjutant he had seen the previous day ceremoniously informed BolkГіnski that the Emperor desired to give him an audience. The Emperor Francis received him standing in the middle of the room. Before the conversation began Prince AndrГ©y was struck by the fact that the Emperor seemed confused and blushed as if not knowing what to say. вҖңTell me, when did the battle begin?вҖқ he asked hurriedly. Prince AndrГ©y replied. Then followed other questions just as simple: вҖңWas KutГәzov well? When had he left Krems?вҖқ and so on. The Emperor spoke as if his sole aim were to put a given number of questionsвҒ вҖ”the answers to these questions, as was only too evident, did not interest him. вҖңAt what oвҖҷclock did the battle begin?вҖқ asked the Emperor. вҖңI cannot inform Your Majesty at what oвҖҷclock the battle began at the front, but at DГјrrenstein, where I was, our attack began after five in the afternoon,вҖқ replied BolkГіnski growing more animated and expecting that he would have a chance to give a reliable account, which he had ready in his mind, of all he knew and had seen. But the Emperor smiled and interrupted him. вҖңHow many miles?вҖқ вҖңFrom where to where, Your Majesty?вҖқ вҖңFrom DГјrrenstein to Krems.вҖқ вҖңThree and a half miles, Your Majesty.вҖқ вҖңThe French have abandoned the left bank?вҖқ вҖңAccording to the scouts the last of them crossed on rafts during the night.вҖқ вҖңIs there sufficient forage in Krems?вҖқ вҖңForage has not been supplied to the extentвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The Emperor interrupted him. вҖңAt what oвҖҷclock was General Schmidt killed?вҖқ вҖңAt seven oвҖҷclock, I believe.вҖқ вҖңAt seven oвҖҷclock? ItвҖҷs very sad, very sad!вҖқ The Emperor thanked Prince AndrГ©y and bowed. Prince AndrГ©y withdrew and was immediately surrounded by courtiers on all sides. Everywhere he saw friendly looks and heard friendly words. YesterdayвҖҷs adjutant reproached him for not having stayed at the palace, and offered him his own house. The Minister of War came up and congratulated him on the Maria Theresa Order of the third grade, which the Emperor was conferring on him. The EmpressвҖҷ chamberlain invited him to see Her Majesty. The archduchess also wished to see him. He did not know whom to answer, and for a few seconds collected his thoughts. Then the Russian ambassador took him by the shoulder, led him to the window, and began to talk to him. Contrary to BilГӯbinвҖҷs forecast the news he had brought was joyfully received. A thanksgiving service was arranged, KutГәzov was awarded the Grand Cross of Maria Theresa, and the whole army received rewards. BolkГіnski was invited everywhere, and had to spend the whole morning calling on the principal Austrian dignitaries. Between four and five in the afternoon, having made all his calls, he was returning to BilГӯbinвҖҷs house thinking out a letter to his father about the battle and his visit to BrГјnn. At the door he found a vehicle half full of luggage. Franz, BilГӯbinвҖҷs man, was dragging a portmanteau with some difficulty out of the front door. Before returning to BilГӯbinвҖҷs Prince AndrГ©y had gone to a bookshop to provide himself with some books for the campaign, and had spent some time in the shop. вҖңWhat is it?вҖқ he asked. вҖңOh, your excellency!вҖқ said Franz, with difficulty rolling the portmanteau into the vehicle, вҖңwe are to move on still farther. The scoundrel is again at our heels!вҖқ вҖңEh? What?вҖқ asked Prince AndrГ©y. BilГӯbin came out to meet him. His usually calm face showed excitement. вҖңThere now! Confess that this is delightful,вҖқ said he. вҖңThis affair of the Thabor Bridge, at Vienna.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ They have crossed without striking a blow!вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y could not understand. вҖңBut where do you come from not to know what every coachman in the town knows?вҖқ вҖңI come from the archduchessвҖҷ. I heard nothing there.вҖқ вҖңAnd you didnвҖҷt see that everybody is packing up?вҖқ вҖңI did notвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What is it all about?вҖқ inquired Prince AndrГ©y impatiently. вҖңWhatвҖҷs it all about? Why, the French have crossed the bridge that Auersperg was defending, and the bridge was not blown up: so Murat is now rushing along the road to BrГјnn and will be here in a day or two.вҖқ вҖңWhat? Here? But why did they not blow up the bridge, if it was mined?вҖқ вҖңThat is what I ask you. No one, not even Bonaparte, knows why.вҖқ BolkГіnski shrugged his shoulders. вҖңBut if the bridge is crossed it means that the army too is lost? It will be cut off,вҖқ said he. вҖңThatвҖҷs just it,вҖқ answered BilГӯbin. вҖңListen! The French entered Vienna as I told you. Very well. Next day, which was yesterday, those gentlemen, messieurs les marГ©chaux,30 Murat, Lannes, and Belliard, mount and ride to the bridge. (Observe that all three are Gascons.) вҖҳGentlemen,вҖҷ says one of them, вҖҳyou know the Thabor Bridge is mined and doubly mined and that there are menacing fortifications at its head and an army of fifteen thousand men has been ordered to blow up the bridge and not let us cross? But it will please our sovereign the Emperor Napoleon if we take this bridge, so let us three go and take it!вҖҷ вҖҳYes, letвҖҷs!вҖҷ say the others. And off they go and take the bridge, cross it, and now with their whole army are on this side of the Danube, marching on us, you, and your lines of communication.вҖқ вҖңStop jesting,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y sadly and seriously. This news grieved him and yet he was pleased. As soon as he learned that the Russian army was in such a hopeless situation it occurred to him that it was he who was destined to lead it out of this position; that here was the Toulon that would lift him from the ranks of obscure officers and offer him the first step to fame! Listening to BilГӯbin he was already imagining how on reaching the army he would give an opinion at the war council which would be the only one that could save the army, and how he alone would be entrusted with the executing of the plan. вҖңStop this jesting,вҖқ he said. вҖңI am not jesting,вҖқ BilГӯbin went on. вҖңNothing is truer or sadder. These gentlemen ride onto the bridge alone and wave white handkerchiefs; they assure the officer on duty that they, the marshals, are on their way to negotiate with Prince Auersperg. He lets them enter the tГӘte-de-pont.31 They spin him a thousand gasconades, saying that the war is over, that the Emperor Francis is arranging a meeting with Bonaparte, that they desire to see Prince Auersperg, and so on. The officer sends for Auersperg; these gentlemen embrace the officers, crack jokes, sit on the cannon, and meanwhile a French battalion gets to the bridge unobserved, flings the bags of incendiary material into the water, and approaches the tГӘte-de-pont. At length appears the lieutenant general, our dear Prince Auersperg von Mautern himself. вҖҳDearest foe! Flower of the Austrian army, hero of the Turkish wars! Hostilities are ended, we can shake one anotherвҖҷs hand.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The Emperor Napoleon burns with impatience to make Prince AuerspergвҖҷs acquaintance.вҖҷ In a word, those gentlemen, Gascons indeed, so bewildered him with fine words, and he is so flattered by his rapidly established intimacy with the French marshals, and so dazzled by the sight of MuratвҖҷs mantle and ostrich plumes, quвҖҷil nвҖҷy voit que du feu, et oublie celui quвҖҷil devait faire faire sur lвҖҷennemi!вҖқ32 In spite of the animation of his speech, BilГӯbin did not forget to pause after this mot to give time for its due appreciation. вҖңThe French battalion rushes to the bridgehead, spikes the guns, and the bridge is taken! But what is best of all,вҖқ he went on, his excitement subsiding under the delightful interest of his own story, вҖңis that the sergeant in charge of the cannon which was to give the signal to fire the mines and blow up the bridge, this sergeant, seeing that the French troops were running onto the bridge, was about to fire, but Lannes stayed his hand. The sergeant, who was evidently wiser than his general, goes up to Auersperg and says: вҖҳPrince, you are being deceived, here are the French!вҖҷ Murat, seeing that all is lost if the sergeant is allowed to speak, turns to Auersperg with feigned astonishment (he is a true Gascon) and says: вҖҳI donвҖҷt recognize the world-famous Austrian discipline, if you allow a subordinate to address you like that!вҖҷ It was a stroke of genius. Prince Auersperg feels his dignity at stake and orders the sergeant to be arrested. Come, you must own that this affair of the Thabor Bridge is delightful! It is not exactly stupidity, nor rascality.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңIt may be treachery,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, vividly imagining the gray overcoats, wounds, the smoke of gunpowder, the sounds of firing, and the glory that awaited him. вҖңNot that either. That puts the court in too bad a light,вҖқ replied BilГӯbin. вҖңItвҖҷs not treachery nor rascality nor stupidity: it is just as at UlmвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ it isвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқвҒ вҖ”he seemed to be trying to find the right expression. вҖңCвҖҷestвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ cвҖҷest du Mack. Nous sommes mackГ©s (It isвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ it is a bit of Mack. We are Macked),вҖқ he concluded, feeling that he had produced a good epigram, a fresh one that would be repeated. His hitherto puckered brow became smooth as a sign of pleasure, and with a slight smile he began to examine his nails. вҖңWhere are you off to?вҖқ he said suddenly to Prince AndrГ©y who had risen and was going toward his room. вҖңI am going away.вҖқ вҖңWhere to?вҖқ вҖңTo the army.вҖқ вҖңBut you meant to stay another two days?вҖқ вҖңBut now I am off at once.вҖқ And Prince AndrГ©y after giving directions about his departure went to his room. вҖңDo you know, mon cher,вҖқ said BilГӯbin following him, вҖңI have been thinking about you. Why are you going?вҖқ And in proof of the conclusiveness of his opinion all the wrinkles vanished from his face. Prince AndrГ©y looked inquiringly at him and gave no reply. вҖңWhy are you going? I know you think it your duty to gallop back to the army now that it is in danger. I understand that. Mon cher, it is heroism!вҖқ вҖңNot at all,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңBut as you are a philosopher, be a consistent one, look at the other side of the question and you will see that your duty, on the contrary, is to take care of yourself. Leave it to those who are no longer fit for anything else.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You have not been ordered to return and have not been dismissed from here; therefore, you can stay and go with us wherever our ill luck takes us. They say we are going to OlmГјtz, and OlmГјtz is a very decent town. You and I will travel comfortably in my calГЁche.вҖқ вҖңDo stop joking, BilГӯbin,вҖқ cried BolkГіnski. вҖңI am speaking sincerely as a friend! Consider! Where and why are you going, when you might remain here? You are faced by one of two things,вҖқ and the skin over his left temple puckered, вҖңeither you will not reach your regiment before peace is concluded, or you will share defeat and disgrace with KutГәzovвҖҷs whole army.вҖқ And BilГӯbin unwrinkled his temple, feeling that the dilemma was insoluble. вҖңI cannot argue about it,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y coldly, but he thought: вҖңI am going to save the army.вҖқ вҖңMy dear fellow, you are a hero!вҖқ said BilГӯbin. XIII XIII That same night, having taken leave of the Minister of War, BolkГіnski set off to rejoin the army, not knowing where he would find it and fearing to be captured by the French on the way to Krems. In BrГјnn everybody attached to the court was packing up, and the heavy baggage was already being dispatched to OlmГјtz. Near Hetzelsdorf Prince AndrГ©y struck the high road along which the Russian army was moving with great haste and in the greatest disorder. The road was so obstructed with carts that it was impossible to get by in a carriage. Prince AndrГ©y took a horse and a Cossack from a Cossack commander, and hungry and weary, making his way past the baggage wagons, rode in search of the commander in chief and of his own luggage.
