Very sinister reports of the position of the army reached him as he went along, and the appearance of the troops in their disorderly flight confirmed these rumors. вҖңCette armГ©e russe que lвҖҷor de lвҖҷAngleterre a transportГ©e des extrГ©mitГ©s de lвҖҷunivers, nous allons lui faire Г©prouver le mГӘme sortвҒ вҖ”(le sort de lвҖҷarmГ©e dвҖҷUlm).вҖқ33 He remembered these words in BonaparteвҖҷs address to his army at the beginning of the campaign, and they awoke in him astonishment at the genius of his hero, a feeling of wounded pride, and a hope of glory. вҖңAnd should there be nothing left but to die?вҖқ he thought. вҖңWell, if need be, I shall do it no worse than others.вҖқ He looked with disdain at the endless confused mass of detachments, carts, guns, artillery, and again baggage wagons and vehicles of all kinds overtaking one another and blocking the muddy road, three and sometimes four abreast. From all sides, behind and before, as far as ear could reach, there were the rattle of wheels, the creaking of carts and gun carriages, the tramp of horses, the crack of whips, shouts, the urging of horses, and the swearing of soldiers, orderlies, and officers. All along the sides of the road fallen horses were to be seen, some flayed, some not, and broken-down carts beside which solitary soldiers sat waiting for something, and again soldiers straggling from their companies, crowds of whom set off to the neighboring villages, or returned from them dragging sheep, fowls, hay, and bulging sacks. At each ascent or descent of the road the crowds were yet denser and the din of shouting more incessant. Soldiers floundering knee-deep in mud pushed the guns and wagons themselves. Whips cracked, hoofs slipped, traces broke, and lungs were strained with shouting. The officers directing the march rode backward and forward between the carts. Their voices were but feebly heard amid the uproar and one saw by their faces that they despaired of the possibility of checking this disorder. вҖңHere is our dear Orthodox Russian army,вҖқ thought BolkГіnski, recalling BilГӯbinвҖҷs words. Wishing to find out where the commander in chief was, he rode up to a convoy. Directly opposite to him came a strange one-horse vehicle, evidently rigged up by soldiers out of any available materials and looking like something between a cart, a cabriolet, and a calГЁche. A soldier was driving, and a woman enveloped in shawls sat behind the apron under the leather hood of the vehicle. Prince AndrГ©y rode up and was just putting his question to a soldier when his attention was diverted by the desperate shrieks of the woman in the vehicle. An officer in charge of transport was beating the soldier who was driving the womanвҖҷs vehicle for trying to get ahead of others, and the strokes of his whip fell on the apron of the equipage. The woman screamed piercingly. Seeing Prince AndrГ©y she leaned out from behind the apron and, waving her thin arms from under the woolen shawl, cried: вҖңMr.В Aide-de-camp! Mr.В Aide-de-camp!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ For heavenвҖҷs sakeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Protect me! What will become of us? I am the wife of the doctor of the Seventh Chasseurs.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ They wonвҖҷt let us pass, we are left behind and have lost our peopleвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңIвҖҷll flatten you into a pancake!вҖқ shouted the angry officer to the soldier. вҖңTurn back with your slut!вҖқ вҖңMr.В Aide-de-camp! Help me!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What does it all mean?вҖқ screamed the doctorвҖҷs wife. вҖңKindly let this cart pass. DonвҖҷt you see itвҖҷs a woman?вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y riding up to the officer. The officer glanced at him, and without replying turned again to the soldier. вҖңIвҖҷll teach you to push on!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Back!вҖқ вҖңLet them pass, I tell you!вҖқ repeated Prince AndrГ©y, compressing his lips. вҖңAnd who are you?вҖқ cried the officer, turning on him with tipsy rage, вҖңwho are you? Are you in command here? Eh? I am commander here, not you! Go back or IвҖҷll flatten you into a pancake,вҖқ repeated he. This expression evidently pleased him. вҖңThat was a nice snub for the little aide-de-camp,вҖқ came a voice from behind. Prince AndrГ©y saw that the officer was in that state of senseless, tipsy rage when a man does not know what he is saying. He saw that his championship of the doctorвҖҷs wife in her queer trap might expose him to what he dreaded more than anything in the worldвҒ вҖ”to ridicule; but his instinct urged him on. Before the officer finished his sentence Prince AndrГ©y, his face distorted with fury, rode up to him and raised his riding whip. вҖңKindвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ly letвҒ вҖ”themвҒ вҖ”pass!вҖқ The officer flourished his arm and hastily rode away. вҖңItвҖҷs all the fault of these fellows on the staff that thereвҖҷs this disorder,вҖқ he muttered. вҖңDo as you like.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y without lifting his eyes rode hastily away from the doctorвҖҷs wife, who was calling him her deliverer, and recalling with a sense of disgust the minutest details of this humiliating scene he galloped on to the village where he was told who the commander in chief was. On reaching the village he dismounted and went to the nearest house, intending to rest if but for a moment, eat something, and try to sort out the stinging and tormenting thoughts that confused his mind. вҖңThis is a mob of scoundrels and not an army,вҖқ he was thinking as he went up to the window of the first house, when a familiar voice called him by name. He turned round. NesvГӯtskiвҖҷs handsome face looked out of the little window. NesvГӯtski, moving his moist lips as he chewed something, and flourishing his arm, called him to enter. вҖңBolkГіnski! BolkГіnski!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ DonвҖҷt you hear? Eh? Come quickвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he shouted. Entering the house, Prince AndrГ©y saw NesvГӯtski and another adjutant having something to eat. They hastily turned round to him asking if he had any news. On their familiar faces he read agitation and alarm. This was particularly noticeable on NesvГӯtskiвҖҷs usually laughing countenance. вҖңWhere is the commander in chief?вҖқ asked BolkГіnski. вҖңHere, in that house,вҖқ answered the adjutant. вҖңWell, is it true that itвҖҷs peace and capitulation?вҖқ asked NesvГӯtski. вҖңI was going to ask you. I know nothing except that it was all I could do to get here.вҖқ вҖңAnd we, my dear boy! ItвҖҷs terrible! I was wrong to laugh at Mack, weвҖҷre getting it still worse,вҖқ said NesvГӯtski. вҖңBut sit down and have something to eat.вҖқ вҖңYou wonвҖҷt be able to find either your baggage or anything else now, Prince. And God only knows where your man Pyotr is,вҖқ said the other adjutant. вҖңWhere are headquarters?вҖқ вҖңWe are to spend the night in Znaim.вҖқ вҖңWell, I have got all I need into packs for two horses,вҖқ said NesvГӯtski. вҖңTheyвҖҷve made up splendid packs for meвҒ вҖ”fit to cross the Bohemian mountains with. ItвҖҷs a bad lookout, old fellow! But whatвҖҷs the matter with you? You must be ill to shiver like that,вҖқ he added, noticing that Prince AndrГ©y winced as at an electric shock. вҖңItвҖҷs nothing,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y. He had just remembered his recent encounter with the doctorвҖҷs wife and the convoy officer. вҖңWhat is the commander in chief doing here?вҖқ he asked. вҖңI canвҖҷt make out at all,вҖқ said NesvГӯtski. вҖңWell, all I can make out is that everything is abominable, abominable, quite abominable!вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, and he went off to the house where the commander in chief was. Passing by KutГәzovвҖҷs carriage and the exhausted saddle horses of his suite, with their Cossacks who were talking loudly together, Prince AndrГ©y entered the passage. KutГәzov himself, he was told, was in the house with Prince BagratiГіn and Weyrother. Weyrother was the Austrian general who had succeeded Schmidt. In the passage little KozlГіvski was squatting on his heels in front of a clerk. The clerk, with cuffs turned up, was hastily writing at a tub turned bottom upwards. KozlГіvskiвҖҷs face looked wornвҒ вҖ”he too had evidently not slept all night. He glanced at Prince AndrГ©y and did not even nod to him. вҖңSecond lineвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ have you written it?вҖқ he continued dictating to the clerk. вҖңThe Kiev Grenadiers, PodolianвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңOne canвҖҷt write so fast, your honor,вҖқ said the clerk, glancing angrily and disrespectfully at KozlГіvski. Through the door came the sounds of KutГәzovвҖҷs voice, excited and dissatisfied, interrupted by another, an unfamiliar voice. From the sound of these voices, the inattentive way KozlГіvski looked at him, the disrespectful manner of the exhausted clerk, the fact that the clerk and KozlГіvski were squatting on the floor by a tub so near to the commander in chief, and from the noisy laughter of the Cossacks holding the horses near the window, Prince AndrГ©y felt that something important and disastrous was about to happen. He turned to KozlГіvski with urgent questions. вҖңImmediately, Prince,вҖқ said KozlГіvski. вҖңDispositions for BagratiГіn.вҖқ вҖңWhat about capitulation?вҖқ вҖңNothing of the sort. Orders are issued for a battle.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y moved toward the door from whence voices were heard. Just as he was going to open it the sounds ceased, the door opened, and KutГәzov with his eagle nose and puffy face appeared in the doorway. Prince AndrГ©y stood right in front of KutГәzov but the expression of the commander in chiefвҖҷs one sound eye showed him to be so preoccupied with thoughts and anxieties as to be oblivious of his presence. He looked straight at his adjutantвҖҷs face without recognizing him. вҖңWell, have you finished?вҖқ said he to KozlГіvski. вҖңOne moment, your excellency.вҖқ BagratiГіn, a gaunt middle-aged man of medium height with a firm, impassive face of Oriental type, came out after the commander in chief. вҖңI have the honor to present myself,вҖқ repeated Prince AndrГ©y rather loudly, handing KutГәzov an envelope. вҖңAh, from Vienna? Very good. Later, later!вҖқ KutГәzov went out into the porch with BagratiГіn. вҖңWell, goodbye, Prince,вҖқ said he to BagratiГіn. вҖңMy blessing, and may Christ be with you in your great endeavor!вҖқ His face suddenly softened and tears came into his eyes. With his left hand he drew BagratiГіn toward him, and with his right, on which he wore a ring, he made the sign of the cross over him with a gesture evidently habitual, offering his puffy cheek, but BagratiГіn kissed him on the neck instead. вҖңChrist be with you!вҖқ KutГәzov repeated and went toward his carriage. вҖңGet in with me,вҖқ said he to BolkГіnski. вҖңYour excellency, I should like to be of use here. Allow me to remain with Prince BagratiГіnвҖҷs detachment.вҖқ вҖңGet in,вҖқ said KutГәzov, and noticing that BolkГіnski still delayed, he added: вҖңI need good officers myself, need them myself!вҖқ They got into the carriage and drove for a few minutes in silence. вҖңThere is still much, much before us,вҖқ he said, as if with an old manвҖҷs penetration he understood all that was passing in BolkГіnskiвҖҷs mind. вҖңIf a tenth part of his detachment returns I shall thank God,вҖқ he added as if speaking to himself. Prince AndrГ©y glanced at KutГәzovвҖҷs face only a foot distant from him and involuntarily noticed the carefully washed seams of the scar near his temple, where an Ismail bullet had pierced his skull, and the empty eye socket. вҖңYes, he has a right to speak so calmly of those menвҖҷs death,вҖқ thought BolkГіnski. вҖңThat is why I beg to be sent to that detachment,вҖқ he said. KutГәzov did not reply. He seemed to have forgotten what he had been saying, and sat plunged in thought. Five minutes later, gently swaying on the soft springs of the carriage, he turned to Prince AndrГ©y. There was not a trace of agitation on his face. With delicate irony he questioned Prince AndrГ©y about the details of his interview with the Emperor, about the remarks he had heard at court concerning the Krems affair, and about some ladies they both knew. XIV XIV On November 1 KutГәzov had received, through a spy, news that the army he commanded was in an almost hopeless position. The spy reported that the French, after crossing the bridge at Vienna, were advancing in immense force upon KutГәzovвҖҷs line of communication with the troops that were arriving from Russia. If KutГәzov decided to remain at Krems, NapoleonвҖҷs army of one hundred and fifty thousand men would cut him off completely and surround his exhausted army of forty thousand, and he would find himself in the position of Mack at Ulm. If KutГәzov decided to abandon the road connecting him with the troops arriving from Russia, he would have to march with no road into unknown parts of the Bohemian mountains, defending himself against superior forces of the enemy and abandoning all hope of a junction with BuxhГ¶wden. If KutГәzov decided to retreat along the road from Krems to OlmГјtz, to unite with the troops arriving from Russia, he risked being forestalled on that road by the French who had crossed the Vienna bridge, and encumbered by his baggage and transport, having to accept battle on the march against an enemy three times as strong, who would hem him in from two sides. KutГәzov chose this latter course. The French, the spy reported, having crossed the Vienna bridge, were advancing by forced marches toward Znaim, which lay sixty-six miles off on the line of KutГәzovвҖҷs retreat. If he reached Znaim before the French, there would be great hope of saving the army; to let the French forestall him at Znaim meant the exposure of his whole army to a disgrace such as that of Ulm, or to utter destruction. But to forestall the French with his whole army was impossible. The road for the French from Vienna to Znaim was shorter and better than the road for the Russians from Krems to Znaim. The night he received the news, KutГәzov sent BagratiГіnвҖҷs vanguard, four thousand strong, to the right across the hills from the Krems-Znaim to the Vienna-Znaim road. BagratiГіn was to make this march without resting, and to halt facing Vienna with Znaim to his rear, and if he succeeded in forestalling the French he was to delay them as long as possible. KutГәzov himself with all his transport took the road to Znaim. Marching thirty miles that stormy night across roadless hills, with his hungry, ill-shod soldiers, and losing a third of his men as stragglers by the way, BagratiГіn came out on the Vienna-Znaim road at HollabrГјnn a few hours ahead of the French who were approaching HollabrГјnn from Vienna. KutГәzov with his transport had still to march for some days before he could reach Znaim. Hence BagratiГіn with his four thousand hungry, exhausted men would have to detain for days the whole enemy army that came upon him at HollabrГјnn, which was clearly impossible. But a freak of fate made the impossible possible. The success of the trick that had placed the Vienna bridge in the hands of the French without a fight led Murat to try to deceive KutГәzov in a similar way. Meeting BagratiГіnвҖҷs weak detachment on the Znaim road he supposed it to be KutГәzovвҖҷs whole army. To be able to crush it absolutely he awaited the arrival of the rest of the troops who were on their way from Vienna, and with this object offered a three daysвҖҷ truce on condition that both armies should remain in position without moving. Murat declared that negotiations for peace were already proceeding, and that he therefore offered this truce to avoid unnecessary bloodshed. Count Nostitz, the Austrian general occupying the advanced posts, believed MuratвҖҷs emissary and retired, leaving BagratiГіnвҖҷs division exposed. Another emissary rode to the Russian line to announce the peace negotiations and to offer the Russian army the three daysвҖҷ truce. BagratiГіn replied that he was not authorized either to accept or refuse a truce and sent his adjutant to KutГәzov to report the offer he had received. A truce was KutГәzovвҖҷs sole chance of gaining time, giving BagratiГіnвҖҷs exhausted troops some rest, and letting the transport and heavy convoys (whose movements were concealed from the French) advance if but one stage nearer Znaim. The offer of a truce gave the only, and a quite unexpected, chance of saving the army. On receiving the news he immediately dispatched Adjutant General Wintzingerode, who was in attendance on him, to the enemy camp. Wintzingerode was not merely to agree to the truce but also to offer terms of capitulation, and meanwhile KutГәzov sent his adjutants back to hasten to the utmost the movements of the baggage trains of the entire army along the Krems-Znaim road. BagratiГіnвҖҷs exhausted and hungry detachment, which alone covered this movement of the transport and of the whole army, had to remain stationary in face of an enemy eight times as strong as itself. KutГәzovвҖҷs expectations that the proposals of capitulation (which were in no way binding) might give time for part of the transport to pass, and also that MuratвҖҷs mistake would very soon be discovered, proved correct. As soon as Bonaparte (who was at SchГ¶nbrunn, sixteen miles from HollabrГјnn) received MuratвҖҷs dispatch with the proposal of a truce and a capitulation, he detected a ruse and wrote the following letter to Murat: SchГ¶nbrunn, 25th Brumaire, 1805, at eight oвҖҷclock in the morning To Prince Murat, I cannot find words to express to you my displeasure. You command only my advance guard, and have no right to arrange an armistice without my order. You are causing me to lose the fruits of a campaign. Break the armistice immediately and march on the enemy. Inform him that the general who signed that capitulation had no right to do so, and that no one but the Emperor of Russia has that right. If, however, the Emperor of Russia ratifies that convention, I will ratify it; but it is only a trick. March on, destroy the Russian army.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You are in a position to seize its baggage and artillery. The Russian EmperorвҖҷs aide-de-camp is an impostor. Officers are nothing when they have no powers; this one had none.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The Austrians let themselves be tricked at the crossing of the Vienna bridge, you are letting yourself be tricked by an aide-de-camp of the Emperor. Napoleon BonaparteвҖҷs adjutant rode full gallop with this menacing letter to Murat. Bonaparte himself, not trusting to his generals, moved with all the Guards to the field of battle, afraid of letting a ready victim escape, and BagratiГіnвҖҷs four thousand men merrily lighted campfires, dried and warmed themselves, cooked their porridge for the first time for three days, and not one of them knew or imagined what was in store for him. XV XV Between three and four oвҖҷclock in the afternoon Prince AndrГ©y, who had persisted in his request to KutГәzov, arrived at Grunth and reported himself to BagratiГіn. BonaparteвҖҷs adjutant had not yet reached MuratвҖҷs detachment and the battle had not yet begun. In BagratiГіnвҖҷs detachment no one knew anything of the general position of affairs. They talked of peace but did not believe in its possibility; others talked of a battle but also disbelieved in the nearness of an engagement. BagratiГіn, knowing BolkГіnski to be a favorite and trusted adjutant, received him with distinction and special marks of favor, explaining to him that there would probably be an engagement that day or the next, and giving him full liberty to remain with him during the battle or to join the rearguard and have an eye on the order of retreat, вҖңwhich is also very important.вҖқ вҖңHowever, there will hardly be an engagement today,вҖқ said BagratiГіn as if to reassure Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңIf he is one of the ordinary little staff dandies sent to earn a medal he can get his reward just as well in the rearguard, but if he wishes to stay with me, let himвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ heвҖҷll be of use here if heвҖҷs a brave officer,вҖқ thought BagratiГіn. Prince AndrГ©y, without replying, asked the princeвҖҷs permission to ride round the position to see the disposition of the forces, so as to know his bearings should he be sent to execute an order. The officer on duty, a handsome, elegantly dressed man with a diamond ring on his forefinger, who was fond of speaking French though he spoke it badly, offered to conduct Prince AndrГ©y. On all sides they saw rain-soaked officers with dejected faces who seemed to be seeking something, and soldiers dragging doors, benches, and fencing from the village. вҖңThere now, Prince! We canвҖҷt stop those fellows,вҖқ said the staff officer pointing to the soldiers. вҖңThe officers donвҖҷt keep them in hand. And there,вҖқ he pointed to a sutlerвҖҷs tent, вҖңthey crowd in and sit. This morning I turned them all out and now look, itвҖҷs full again. I must go there, Prince, and scare them a bit. It wonвҖҷt take a moment.вҖқ вҖңYes, letвҖҷs go in and I will get myself a roll and some cheese,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y who had not yet had time to eat anything. вҖңWhy didnвҖҷt you mention it, Prince? I would have offered you something.вҖқ They dismounted and entered the tent. Several officers, with flushed and weary faces, were sitting at the table eating and drinking. вҖңNow what does this mean, gentlemen?вҖқ said the staff officer, in the reproachful tone of a man who has repeated the same thing more than once. вҖңYou know it wonвҖҷt do to leave your posts like this. The prince gave orders that no one should leave his post. Now you, Captain,вҖқ and he turned to a thin, dirty little artillery officer who without his boots (he had given them to the canteen keeper to dry), in only his stockings, rose when they entered, smiling not altogether comfortably. вҖңWell, arenвҖҷt you ashamed of yourself, Captain TГәshin?вҖқ he continued. вҖңOne would think that as an artillery officer you would set a good example, yet here you are without your boots! The alarm will be sounded and youвҖҷll be in a pretty position without your boots!вҖқ (The staff officer smiled.) вҖңKindly return to your posts, gentlemen, all of you, all!вҖқ he added in a tone of command. Prince AndrГ©y smiled involuntarily as he looked at the artillery officer TГәshin, who silent and smiling, shifting from one stockinged foot to the other, glanced inquiringly with his large, intelligent, kindly eyes from Prince AndrГ©y to the staff officer. вҖңThe soldiers say it feels easier without boots,вҖқ said Captain TГәshin smiling shyly in his uncomfortable position, evidently wishing to adopt a jocular tone. But before he had finished he felt that his jest was unacceptable and had not come off. He grew confused. вҖңKindly return to your posts,вҖқ said the staff officer trying to preserve his gravity. Prince AndrГ©y glanced again at the artillery officerвҖҷs small figure. There was something peculiar about it, quite unsoldierly, rather comic, but extremely attractive. The staff officer and Prince AndrГ©y mounted their horses and rode on. Having ridden beyond the village, continually meeting and overtaking soldiers and officers of various regiments, they saw on their left some entrenchments being thrown up, the freshly dug clay of which showed up red. Several battalions of soldiers, in their shirt sleeves despite the cold wind, swarmed in these earthworks like a host of white ants; spadefuls of red clay were continually being thrown up from behind the bank by unseen hands. Prince AndrГ©y and the officer rode up, looked at the entrenchment, and went on again. Just behind it they came upon some dozens of soldiers, continually replaced by others, who ran from the entrenchment. They had to hold their noses and put their horses to a trot to escape from the poisoned atmosphere of these latrines. вҖңVoilГ  lвҖҷagrГ©ment des camps, monsieur le prince,вҖқ34 said the staff officer. They rode up the opposite hill. From there the French could already be seen. Prince AndrГ©y stopped and began examining the position. вҖңThatвҖҷs our battery,вҖқ said the staff officer indicating the highest point. вҖңItвҖҷs in charge of the queer fellow we saw without his boots. You can see everything from there; letвҖҷs go there, Prince.вҖқ вҖңThank you very much, I will go on alone,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, wishing to rid himself of this staff officerвҖҷs company, вҖңplease donвҖҷt trouble yourself further.вҖқ The staff officer remained behind and Prince AndrГ©y rode on alone. The farther forward and nearer the enemy he went, the more orderly and cheerful were the troops. The greatest disorder and depression had been in the baggage train he had passed that morning on the Znaim road seven miles away from the French. At Grunth also some apprehension and alarm could be felt, but the nearer Prince AndrГ©y came to the French lines the more confident was the appearance of our troops. The soldiers in their greatcoats were ranged in lines, the sergeants major and company officers were counting the men, poking the last man in each section in the ribs and telling him to hold his hand up. Soldiers scattered over the whole place were dragging logs and brushwood and were building shelters with merry chatter and laughter; around the fires sat others, dressed and undressed, drying their shirts and leg bands or mending boots or overcoats and crowding round the boilers and porridge cookers. In one company dinner was ready, and the soldiers were gazing eagerly at the steaming boiler, waiting till the sample, which a quartermaster sergeant was carrying in a wooden bowl to an officer who sat on a log before his shelter, had been tasted. Another company, a lucky one for not all the companies had vodka, crowded round a pockmarked, broad-shouldered sergeant major who, tilting a keg, filled one after another the canteen lids held out to him. The soldiers lifted the canteen lids to their lips with reverential faces, emptied them, rolling the vodka in their mouths, and walked away from the sergeant major with brightened expressions, licking their lips and wiping them on the sleeves of their greatcoats. All their faces were as serene as if all this were happening at home awaiting peaceful encampment, and not within sight of the enemy before an action in which at least half of them would be left on the field. After passing a chasseur regiment and in the lines of the Kiev grenadiersвҒ вҖ”fine fellows busy with similar peaceful affairsвҒ вҖ”near the shelter of the regimental commander, higher than and different from the others, Prince AndrГ©y came out in front of a platoon of grenadiers before whom lay a naked man. Two soldiers held him while two others were flourishing their switches and striking him regularly on his bare back. The man shrieked unnaturally. A stout major was pacing up and down the line, and regardless of the screams kept repeating: вҖңItвҖҷs a shame for a soldier to steal; a soldier must be honest, honorable, and brave, but if he robs his fellows there is no honor in him, heвҖҷs a scoundrel. Go on! Go on!вҖқ So the swishing sound of the strokes, and the desperate but unnatural screams, continued. вҖңGo on, go on!вҖқ said the major. A young officer with a bewildered and pained expression on his face stepped away from the man and looked round inquiringly at the adjutant as he rode by. Prince AndrГ©y, having reached the front line, rode along it. Our front line and that of the enemy were far apart on the right and left flanks, but in the center where the men with a flag of truce had passed that morning, the lines were so near together that the men could see one anotherвҖҷs faces and speak to one another. Besides the soldiers who formed the picket line on either side, there were many curious onlookers who, jesting and laughing, stared at their strange foreign enemies. Since early morningвҒ вҖ”despite an injunction not to approach the picket lineвҒ вҖ”the officers had been unable to keep sightseers away. The soldiers forming the picket line, like showmen exhibiting a curiosity, no longer looked at the French but paid attention to the sightseers and grew weary waiting to be relieved. Prince AndrГ©y halted to have a look at the French. вҖңLook! Look there!вҖқ one soldier was saying to another, pointing to a Russian musketeer who had gone up to the picket line with an officer and was rapidly and excitedly talking to a French grenadier. вҖңHark to him jabbering! Fine, isnвҖҷt it? ItвҖҷs all the Frenchy can do to keep up with him. There now, SГӯdorov!вҖқ вҖңWait a bit and listen. ItвҖҷs fine!вҖқ answered SГӯdorov, who was considered an adept at French. The soldier to whom the laughers referred was DГіlokhov. Prince AndrГ©y recognized him and stopped to listen to what he was saying. DГіlokhov had come from the left flank where their regiment was stationed, with his captain. вҖңNow then, go on, go on!вҖқ incited the officer, bending forward and trying not to lose a word of the speech which was incomprehensible to him. вҖңMore, please: more! WhatвҖҷs he saying?вҖқ DГіlokhov did not answer the captain; he had been drawn into a hot dispute with the French grenadier. They were naturally talking about the campaign. The Frenchman, confusing the Austrians with the Russians, was trying to prove that the Russians had surrendered and had fled all the way from Ulm, while DГіlokhov maintained that the Russians had not surrendered but had beaten the French. вҖңWe have orders to drive you off here, and we shall drive you off,вҖқ said DГіlokhov. вҖңOnly take care you and your Cossacks are not all captured!вҖқ said the French grenadier. The French onlookers and listeners laughed. вҖңWeвҖҷll make you dance as we did under SuvГіrovвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ,вҖқ35 said DГіlokhov. вҖңQuвҖҷ est-ce quвҖҷil chante?вҖқ36 asked a Frenchman. вҖңItвҖҷs ancient history,вҖқ said another, guessing that it referred to a former war. вҖңThe Emperor will teach your Suvara as he has taught the othersвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңBonaparteвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ began DГіlokhov, but the Frenchman interrupted him. вҖңNot Bonaparte. He is the Emperor! SacrГ© nomвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ!вҖқ cried he angrily. вҖңThe devil skin your Emperor.вҖқ And DГіlokhov swore at him in coarse soldierвҖҷs Russian and shouldering his musket walked away. вҖңLet us go, IvГЎn LukГӯch,вҖқ he said to the captain. вҖңAh, thatвҖҷs the way to talk French,вҖқ said the picket soldiers. вҖңNow, SГӯdorov, you have a try!вҖқ SГӯdorov, turning to the French, winked, and began to jabber meaningless sounds very fast: вҖңKari, mala, tafa, safi, muter, KaskГЎ,вҖқ he said, trying to give an expressive intonation to his voice. вҖңHo! ho! ho! Ha! ha! ha! ha! Ouh! ouh!вҖқ came peals of such healthy and good-humored laughter from the soldiers that it infected the French involuntarily, so much so that the only thing left to do seemed to be to unload the muskets, explode the ammunition, and all return home as quickly as possible. But the guns remained loaded, the loopholes in blockhouses and entrenchments looked out just as menacingly, and the unlimbered cannon confronted one another as before. XVI XVI Having ridden round the whole line from right flank to left, Prince AndrГ©y made his way up to the battery from which the staff officer had told him the whole field could be seen. Here he dismounted, and stopped beside the farthest of the four unlimbered cannon. Before the guns an artillery sentry was pacing up and down; he stood at attention when the officer arrived, but at a sign resumed his measured, monotonous pacing. Behind the guns were their limbers and still farther back picket ropes and artillerymenвҖҷs bonfires. To the left, not far from the farthest cannon, was a small, newly constructed wattle shed from which came the sound of officersвҖҷ voices in eager conversation. It was true that a view over nearly the whole Russian position and the greater part of the enemyвҖҷs opened out from this battery. Just facing it, on the crest of the opposite hill, the village of SchГ¶n Grabern could be seen, and in three places to left and right the French troops amid the smoke of their campfires, the greater part of whom were evidently in the village itself and behind the hill. To the left from that village, amid the smoke, was something resembling a battery, but it was impossible to see it clearly with the naked eye. Our right flank was posted on a rather steep incline which dominated the French position. Our infantry were stationed there, and at the farthest point the dragoons. In the center, where TГәshinвҖҷs battery stood and from which Prince AndrГ©y was surveying the position, was the easiest and most direct descent and ascent to the brook separating us from SchГ¶n Grabern. On the left our troops were close to a copse, in which smoked the bonfires of our infantry who were felling wood. The French line was wider than ours, and it was plain that they could easily outflank us on both sides. Behind our position was a steep and deep dip, making it difficult for artillery and cavalry to retire. Prince AndrГ©y took out his notebook and, leaning on the cannon, sketched a plan of the position. He made some notes on two points, intending to mention them to BagratiГіn. His idea was, first, to concentrate all the artillery in the center, and secondly, to withdraw the cavalry to the other side of the dip. Prince AndrГ©y, being always near the commander in chief, closely following the mass movements and general orders, and constantly studying historical accounts of battles, involuntarily pictured to himself the course of events in the forthcoming action in broad outline. He imagined only important possibilities: вҖңIf the enemy attacks the right flank,вҖқ he said to himself, вҖңthe Kiev grenadiers and the PodГіlsk chasseurs must hold their position till reserves from the center come up. In that case the dragoons could successfully make a flank counterattack. If they attack our center we, having the center battery on this high ground, shall withdraw the left flank under its cover, and retreat to the dip by echelons.вҖқ So he reasoned.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ All the time he had been beside the gun, he had heard the voices of the officers distinctly, but as often happens had not understood a word of what they were saying. Suddenly, however, he was struck by a voice coming from the shed, and its tone was so sincere that he could not but listen. вҖңNo, friend,вҖқ said a pleasant and, as it seemed to Prince AndrГ©y, a familiar voice, вҖңwhat I say is that if it were possible to know what is beyond death, none of us would be afraid of it. ThatвҖҷs so, friend.вҖқ Another, a younger voice, interrupted him: вҖңAfraid or not, you canвҖҷt escape it anyhow.вҖқ вҖңAll the same, one is afraid! Oh, you clever people,вҖқ said a third manly voice interrupting them both. вҖңOf course you artillery men are very wise, because you can take everything along with youвҒ вҖ”vodka and snacks.вҖқ And the owner of the manly voice, evidently an infantry officer, laughed. вҖңYes, one is afraid,вҖқ continued the first speaker, he of the familiar voice. вҖңOne is afraid of the unknown, thatвҖҷs what it is. Whatever we may say about the soul going to the skyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ we know there is no sky but only an atmosphere.вҖқ The manly voice again interrupted the artillery officer. вҖңWell, stand us some of your herb vodka, TГәshin,вҖқ it said. вҖңWhy,вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңthatвҖҷs the captain who stood up in the sutlerвҖҷs hut without his boots.вҖқ He recognized the agreeable, philosophizing voice with pleasure. вҖңSome herb vodka? Certainly!вҖқ said TГәshin. вҖңBut still, to conceive a future lifeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He did not finish. Just then there was a whistle in the air; nearer and nearer, faster and louder, louder and faster, a cannon ball, as if it had not finished saying what was necessary, thudded into the ground near the shed with super human force, throwing up a mass of earth. The ground seemed to groan at the terrible impact. And immediately TГәshin, with a short pipe in the corner of his mouth and his kind, intelligent face rather pale, rushed out of the shed followed by the owner of the manly voice, a dashing infantry officer who hurried off to his company, buttoning up his coat as he ran. XVII XVII Mounting his horse again Prince AndrГ©y lingered with the battery, looking at the puff from the gun that had sent the ball. His eyes ran rapidly over the wide space, but he only saw that the hitherto motionless masses of the French now swayed and that there really was a battery to their left. The smoke above it had not yet dispersed. Two mounted Frenchmen, probably adjutants, were galloping up the hill. A small but distinctly visible enemy column was moving down the hill, probably to strengthen the front line. The smoke of the first shot had not yet dispersed before another puff appeared, followed by a report. The battle had begun! Prince AndrГ©y turned his horse and galloped back to Grunth to find Prince BagratiГіn. He heard the cannonade behind him growing louder and more frequent. Evidently our guns had begun to reply. From the bottom of the slope, where the parleys had taken place, came the report of musketry. Lemarrois had just arrived at a gallop with BonaparteвҖҷs stern letter, and Murat, humiliated and anxious to expiate his fault, had at once moved his forces to attack the center and outflank both the Russian wings, hoping before evening and before the arrival of the Emperor to crush the contemptible detachment that stood before him. вҖңIt has begun. Here it is!вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y, feeling the blood rush to his heart. вҖңBut where and how will my Toulon present itself?вҖқ Passing between the companies that had been eating porridge and drinking vodka a quarter of an hour before, he saw everywhere the same rapid movement of soldiers forming ranks and getting their muskets ready, and on all their faces he recognized the same eagerness that filled his heart. вҖңIt has begun! Here it is, dreadful but enjoyable!вҖқ was what the face of each soldier and each officer seemed to say. Before he had reached the embankments that were being thrown up, he saw, in the light of the dull autumn evening, mounted men coming toward him. The foremost, wearing a Cossack cloak and lambskin cap and riding a white horse, was Prince BagratiГіn. Prince AndrГ©y stopped, waiting for him to come up; Prince BagratiГіn reined in his horse and recognizing Prince AndrГ©y nodded to him. He still looked ahead while Prince AndrГ©y told him what he had seen. The feeling, вҖңIt has begun! Here it is!вҖқ was seen even on Prince BagratiГіnвҖҷs hard brown face with its half-closed, dull, sleepy eyes. Prince AndrГ©y gazed with anxious curiosity at that impassive face and wished he could tell what, if anything, this man was thinking and feeling at that moment. вҖңIs there anything at all behind that impassive face?вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y asked himself as he looked. Prince BagratiГіn bent his head in sign of agreement with what Prince AndrГ©y told him, and said, вҖңVery good!вҖқ in a tone that seemed to imply that everything that took place and was reported to him was exactly what he had foreseen. Prince AndrГ©y, out of breath with his rapid ride, spoke quickly. Prince BagratiГіn, uttering his words with an Oriental accent, spoke particularly slowly, as if to impress the fact that there was no need to hurry. However, he put his horse to a trot in the direction of TГәshinвҖҷs battery. Prince AndrГ©y followed with the suite. Behind Prince BagratiГіn rode an officer of the suite, the princeвҖҷs personal adjutant, ZherkГіv, an orderly officer, the staff officer on duty, riding a fine bobtailed horse, and a civilianвҒ вҖ”an accountant who had asked permission to be present at the battle out of curiosity. The accountant, a stout, full-faced man, looked around him with a naive smile of satisfaction and presented a strange appearance among the hussars, Cossacks, and adjutants, in his camlet coat, as he jolted on his horse with a convoy officerвҖҷs saddle. вҖңHe wants to see a battle,вҖқ said ZherkГіv to BolkГіnski, pointing to the accountant, вҖңbut he feels a pain in the pit of his stomach already.вҖқ вҖңOh, leave off!вҖқ said the accountant with a beaming but rather cunning smile, as if flattered at being made the subject of ZherkГіvвҖҷs joke, and purposely trying to appear stupider than he really was. вҖңIt is very strange, mon Monsieur Prince,вҖқ said the staff officer. (He remembered that in French there is some peculiar way of addressing a prince, but could not get it quite right.) By this time they were all approaching TГәshinвҖҷs battery, and a ball struck the ground in front of them. вҖңWhatвҖҷs that that has fallen?вҖқ asked the accountant with a naive smile. вҖңA French pancake,вҖқ answered ZherkГіv. вҖңSo thatвҖҷs what they hit with?вҖқ asked the accountant. вҖңHow awful!вҖқ He seemed to swell with satisfaction. He had hardly finished speaking when they again heard an unexpectedly violent whistling which suddenly ended with a thud into something softвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ fвҖ‘fвҖ‘flop! and a Cossack, riding a little to their right and behind the accountant, crashed to earth with his horse. ZherkГіv and the staff officer bent over their saddles and turned their horses away. The accountant stopped, facing the Cossack, and examined him with attentive curiosity. The Cossack was dead, but the horse still struggled. Prince BagratiГіn screwed up his eyes, looked round, and, seeing the cause of the confusion, turned away with indifference, as if to say, вҖңIs it worth while noticing trifles?вҖқ He reined in his horse with the care of a skillful rider and, slightly bending over, disengaged his saber which had caught in his cloak. It was an old-fashioned saber of a kind no longer in general use. Prince AndrГ©y remembered the story of SuvГіrov giving his saber to BagratiГіn in Italy, and the recollection was particularly pleasant at that moment. They had reached the battery at which Prince AndrГ©y had been when he examined the battlefield. вҖңWhose company?вҖқ asked Prince BagratiГіn of an artilleryman standing by the ammunition wagon. He asked, вҖңWhose company?вҖқ but he really meant, вҖңAre you frightened here?вҖқ and the artilleryman understood him. вҖңCaptain TГәshinвҖҷs, your excellency!вҖқ shouted the red-haired, freckled gunner in a merry voice, standing to attention. вҖңYes, yes,вҖқ muttered BagratiГіn as if considering something, and he rode past the limbers to the farthest cannon. As he approached, a ringing shot issued from it deafening him and his suite, and in the smoke that suddenly surrounded the gun they could see the gunners who had seized it straining to roll it quickly back to its former position. A huge, broad-shouldered gunner, Number One, holding a mop, his legs far apart, sprang to the wheel; while Number Two with a trembling hand placed a charge in the cannonвҖҷs mouth. The short, round-shouldered Captain TГәshin, stumbling over the tail of the gun carriage, moved forward and, not noticing the general, looked out shading his eyes with his small hand. вҖңLift it two lines more and it will be just right,вҖқ cried he in a feeble voice to which he tried to impart a dashing note, ill-suited to his weak figure. вҖңNumber Two!вҖқ he squeaked. вҖңFire, MedvГ©dev!вҖқ BagratiГіn called to him, and TГәshin, raising three fingers to his cap with a bashful and awkward gesture not at all like a military salute but like a priestвҖҷs benediction, approached the general. Though TГәshinвҖҷs guns had been intended to cannonade the valley, he was firing incendiary balls at the village of SchГ¶n Grabern visible just opposite, in front of which large masses of French were advancing. No one had given TГәshin orders where and at what to fire, but after consulting his sergeant major, ZakharchГ©nko, for whom he had great respect, he had decided that it would be a good thing to set fire to the village. вҖңVery good!вҖқ said BagratiГіn in reply to the officerвҖҷs report, and began deliberately to examine the whole battlefield extended before him. The French had advanced nearest on our right. Below the height on which the Kiev regiment was stationed, in the hollow where the rivulet flowed, the soul-stirring rolling and crackling of musketry was heard, and much farther to the right beyond the dragoons, the officer of the suite pointed out to BagratiГіn a French column that was outflanking us. To the left the horizon bounded by the adjacent wood. Prince BagratiГіn ordered two battalions from the center to be sent to reinforce the right flank. The officer of the suite ventured to remark to the prince that if these battalions went away, the guns would remain without support. Prince BagratiГіn turned to the officer and with his dull eyes looked at him in silence. It seemed to Prince AndrГ©y that the officerвҖҷs remark was just and that really no answer could be made to it. But at that moment an adjutant galloped up with a message from the commander of the regiment in the hollow and news that immense masses of the French were coming down upon them and that his regiment was in disorder and was retreating upon the Kiev grenadiers. Prince BagratiГіn bowed his head in sign of assent and approval. He rode off at a walk to the right and sent an adjutant to the dragoons with orders to attack the French. But this adjutant returned half an hour later with the news that the commander of the dragoons had already retreated beyond the dip in the ground, as a heavy fire had been opened on him and he was losing men uselessly, and so had hastened to throw some sharpshooters into the wood. вҖңVery good!вҖқ said BagratiГіn. As he was leaving the battery, firing was heard on the left also, and as it was too far to the left flank for him to have time to go there himself, Prince BagratiГіn sent ZherkГіv to tell the general in command (the one who had paraded his regiment before KutГәzov at Braunau) that he must retreat as quickly as possible behind the hollow in the rear, as the right flank would probably not be able to withstand the enemyвҖҷs attack very long. About TГәshin and the battalion that had been in support of his battery all was forgotten. Prince AndrГ©y listened attentively to BagratiГіnвҖҷs colloquies with the commanding officers and the orders he gave them and, to his surprise, found that no orders were really given, but that Prince BagratiГіn tried to make it appear that everything done by necessity, by accident, or by the will of subordinate commanders was done, if not by his direct command, at least in accord with his intentions. Prince AndrГ©y noticed, however, that though what happened was due to chance and was independent of the commanderвҖҷs will, owing to the tact BagratiГіn showed, his presence was very valuable. Officers who approached him with disturbed countenances became calm; soldiers and officers greeted him gaily, grew more cheerful in his presence, and were evidently anxious to display their courage before him. XVIII XVIII Prince BagratiГіn, having reached the highest point of our right flank, began riding downhill to where the roll of musketry was heard but where on account of the smoke nothing could be seen. The nearer they got to the hollow the less they could see but the more they felt the nearness of the actual battlefield. They began to meet wounded men. One with a bleeding head and no cap was being dragged along by two soldiers who supported him under the arms. There was a gurgle in his throat and he was spitting blood. A bullet had evidently hit him in the throat or mouth. Another was walking sturdily by himself but without his musket, groaning aloud and swinging his arm which had just been hurt, while blood from it was streaming over his greatcoat as from a bottle. He had that moment been wounded and his face showed fear rather than suffering. Crossing a road they descended a steep incline and saw several men lying on the ground; they also met a crowd of soldiers some of whom were unwounded. The soldiers were ascending the hill breathing heavily, and despite the generalвҖҷs presence were talking loudly and gesticulating. In front of them rows of gray cloaks were already visible through the smoke, and an officer catching sight of BagratiГіn rushed shouting after the crowd of retreating soldiers, ordering them back. BagratiГіn rode up to the ranks along which shots crackled now here and now there, drowning the sound of voices and the shouts of command. The whole air reeked with smoke. The excited faces of the soldiers were blackened with it. Some were using their ramrods, others putting powder on the touchpans or taking charges from their pouches, while others were firing, though who they were firing at could not be seen for the smoke which there was no wind to carry away. A pleasant humming and whistling of bullets were often heard. вҖңWhat is this?вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y approaching the crowd of soldiers. вҖңIt canвҖҷt be an attack, for they are not moving; it canвҖҷt be a squareвҒ вҖ”for they are not drawn up for that.вҖқ The commander of the regiment, a thin, feeble-looking old man with a pleasant smileвҒ вҖ”his eyelids drooping more than half over his old eyes, giving him a mild expression, rode up to BagratiГіn and welcomed him as a host welcomes an honored guest. He reported that his regiment had been attacked by French cavalry and that, though the attack had been repulsed, he had lost more than half his men. He said the attack had been repulsed, employing this military term to describe what had occurred to his regiment, but in reality he did not himself know what had happened during that half-hour to the troops entrusted to him, and could not say with certainty whether the attack had been repulsed or his regiment had been broken up. All he knew was that at the commencement of the action balls and shells began flying all over his regiment and hitting men and that afterwards someone had shouted вҖңCavalry!вҖқ and our men had begun firing. They were still firing, not at the cavalry which had disappeared, but at French infantry who had come into the hollow and were firing at our men. Prince BagratiГіn bowed his head as a sign that this was exactly what he had desired and expected. Turning to his adjutant he ordered him to bring down the two battalions of the Sixth Chasseurs whom they had just passed. Prince AndrГ©y was struck by the changed expression on Prince BagratiГіnвҖҷs face at this moment. It expressed the concentrated and happy resolution you see on the face of a man who on a hot day takes a final run before plunging into the water. The dull, sleepy expression was no longer there, nor the affectation of profound thought. The round, steady, hawkвҖҷs eyes looked before him eagerly and rather disdainfully, not resting on anything although his movements were still slow and measured. The commander of the regiment turned to Prince BagratiГіn, entreating him to go back as it was too dangerous to remain where they were. вҖңPlease, your excellency, for GodвҖҷs sake!вҖқ he kept saying, glancing for support at an officer of the suite who turned away from him. вҖңThere, you see!вҖқ and he drew attention to the bullets whistling, singing, and hissing continually around them. He spoke in the tone of entreaty and reproach that a carpenter uses to a gentleman who has picked up an ax: вҖңWe are used to it, but you, sir, will blister your hands.вҖқ He spoke as if those bullets could not kill him, and his half-closed eyes gave still more persuasiveness to his words. The staff officer joined in the colonelвҖҷs appeals, but BagratiГіn did not reply; he only gave an order to cease firing and reform, so as to give room for the two approaching battalions. While he was speaking, the curtain of smoke that had concealed the hollow, driven by a rising wind, began to move from right to left as if drawn by an invisible hand, and the hill opposite, with the French moving about on it, opened out before them. All eyes fastened involuntarily on this French column advancing against them and winding down over the uneven ground. One could already see the soldiersвҖҷ shaggy caps, distinguish the officers from the men, and see the standard flapping against its staff. вҖңThey march splendidly,вҖқ remarked someone in BagratiГіnвҖҷs suite. The head of the column had already descended into the hollow. The clash would take place on this side of itвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The remains of our regiment which had been in action rapidly formed up and moved to the right; from behind it, dispersing the laggards, came two battalions of the Sixth Chasseurs in fine order. Before they had reached BagratiГіn, the weighty tread of the mass of men marching in step could be heard. On their left flank, nearest to BagratiГіn, marched a company commander, a fine round-faced man, with a stupid and happy expressionвҒ вҖ”the same man who had rushed out of the wattle shed. At that moment he was clearly thinking of nothing but how dashing a fellow he would appear as he passed the commander. With the self-satisfaction of a man on parade, he stepped lightly with his muscular legs as if sailing along, stretching himself to his full height without the smallest effort, his ease contrasting with the heavy tread of the soldiers who were keeping step with him. He carried close to his leg a narrow unsheathed sword (small, curved, and not like a real weapon) and looked now at the superior officers and now back at the men without losing step, his whole powerful body turning flexibly. It was as if all the powers of his soul were concentrated on passing the commander in the best possible manner, and feeling that he was doing it well he was happy. вҖңLeftвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ leftвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ leftвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he seemed to repeat to himself at each alternate step; and in time to this, with stern but varied faces, the wall of soldiers burdened with knapsacks and muskets marched in step, and each one of these hundreds of soldiers seemed to be repeating to himself at each alternate step, вҖңLeftвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ leftвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ leftвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ A fat major skirted a bush, puffing and falling out of step; a soldier who had fallen behind, his face showing alarm at his defection, ran at a trot, panting to catch up with his company. A cannon ball, cleaving the air, flew over the heads of BagratiГіn and his suite, and fell into the column to the measure of вҖңLeftвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ left!вҖқ вҖңClose up!вҖқ came the company commanderвҖҷs voice in jaunty tones. The soldiers passed in a semicircle round something where the ball had fallen, and an old trooper on the flank, a noncommissioned officer who had stopped beside the dead men, ran to catch up his line and, falling into step with a hop, looked back angrily, and through the ominous silence and the regular tramp of feet beating the ground in unison, one seemed to hear leftвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ leftвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ left. вҖңWell done, lads!вҖқ said Prince BagratiГіn. вҖңGlad to do our best, your exвҖҷlen-lency!вҖқ came a confused shout from the ranks. A morose soldier marching on the left turned his eyes on BagratiГіn as he shouted, with an expression that seemed to say: вҖңWe know that ourselves!вҖқ Another, without looking round, as though fearing to relax, shouted with his mouth wide open and passed on. The order was given to halt and down knapsacks. BagratiГіn rode round the ranks that had marched past him and dismounted. He gave the reins to a Cossack, took off and handed over his felt coat, stretched his legs, and set his cap straight. The head of the French column, with its officers leading, appeared from below the hill. вҖңForward, with God!вҖқ said BagratiГіn, in a resolute, sonorous voice, turning for a moment to the front line, and slightly swinging his arms, he went forward uneasily over the rough field with the awkward gait of a cavalryman. Prince AndrГ©y felt that an invisible power was leading him forward, and experienced great happiness. The French were already near. Prince AndrГ©y, walking beside BagratiГіn, could clearly distinguish their bandoliers, red epaulets, and even their faces. (He distinctly saw an old French officer who, with gaitered legs and turned-out toes, climbed the hill with difficulty.) Prince BagratiГіn gave no further orders and silently continued to walk on in front of the ranks. Suddenly one shot after another rang out from the French, smoke appeared all along their uneven ranks, and musket shots sounded. Several of our men fell, among them the round-faced officer who had marched so gaily and complacently. But at the moment the first report was heard, BagratiГіn looked round and shouted, вҖңHurrah!вҖқ вҖңHurrahвҒ вҖ”ah!вҒ вҖ”ah!вҖқ rang a long-drawn shout from our ranks, and passing BagratiГіn and racing one another they rushed in an irregular but joyous and eager crowd down the hill at their disordered foe. XIX XIX The attack of the Sixth Chasseurs secured the retreat of our right flank. In the center TГәshinвҖҷs forgotten battery, which had managed to set fire to the SchГ¶n Grabern village, delayed the French advance. The French were putting out the fire which the wind was spreading, and thus gave us time to retreat. The retirement of the center to the other side of the dip in the ground at the rear was hurried and noisy, but the different companies did not get mixed. But our leftвҒ вҖ”which consisted of the AzГіv and PodГіlsk infantry and the PГЎvlograd hussarsвҒ вҖ”was simultaneously attacked and outflanked by superior French forces under Lannes and was thrown into confusion. BagratiГіn had sent ZherkГіv to the general commanding that left flank with orders to retreat immediately. ZherkГіv, not removing his hand from his cap, turned his horse about and galloped off. But no sooner had he left BagratiГіn than his courage failed him. He was seized by panic and could not go where it was dangerous. Having reached the left flank, instead of going to the front where the firing was, he began to look for the general and his staff where they could not possibly be, and so did not deliver the order. The command of the left flank belonged by seniority to the commander of the regiment KutГәzov had reviewed at Braunau and in which DГіlokhov was serving as a private. But the command of the extreme left flank had been assigned to the commander of the PГЎvlograd regiment in which RostГіv was serving, and a misunderstanding arose. The two commanders were much exasperated with one another and, long after the action had begun on the right flank and the French were already advancing, were engaged in discussion with the sole object of offending one another. But the regiments, both cavalry and infantry, were by no means ready for the impending action. From privates to general they were not expecting a battle and were engaged in peaceful occupations, the cavalry feeding the horses and the infantry collecting wood. вҖңHe higher iss dan I in rank,вҖқ said the German colonel of the hussars, flushing and addressing an adjutant who had ridden up, вҖңso let him do what he vill, but I cannot sacrifice my hussarsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Bugler, sount ze retreat!вҖқ But haste was becoming imperative. Cannon and musketry, mingling together, thundered on the right and in the center, while the capotes of LannesвҖҷ sharpshooters were already seen crossing the milldam and forming up within twice the range of a musket shot. The general in command of the infantry went toward his horse with jerky steps, and having mounted drew himself up very straight and tall and rode to the PГЎvlograd commander. The commanders met with polite bows but with secret malevolence in their hearts. вҖңOnce again, Colonel,вҖқ said the general, вҖңI canвҖҷt leave half my men in the wood. I beg of you, I beg of you,вҖқ he repeated, вҖңto occupy the position and prepare for an attack.вҖқ вҖңI peg of you yourself not to mix in vot is not your business!вҖқ suddenly replied the irate colonel. вҖңIf you vere in the cavalryвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI am not in the cavalry, Colonel, but I am a Russian general and if you are not aware of the factвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңQuite avare, your excellency,вҖқ suddenly shouted the colonel, touching his horse and turning purple in the face. вҖңVill you be so goot to come to ze front and see dat zis position iss no goot? I donвҖҷt vish to destroy my men for your pleasure!вҖқ вҖңYou forget yourself, Colonel. I am not considering my own pleasure and I wonвҖҷt allow it to be said!вҖқ Taking the colonelвҖҷs outburst as a challenge to his courage, the general expanded his chest and rode, frowning, beside him to the front line, as if their differences would be settled there amongst the bullets. They reached the front, several bullets sped over them, and they halted in silence. There was nothing fresh to be seen from the line, for from where they had been before it had been evident that it was impossible for cavalry to act among the bushes and broken ground, as well as that the French were outflanking our left. The general and colonel looked sternly and significantly at one another like two fighting cocks preparing for battle, each vainly trying to detect signs of cowardice in the other. Both passed the examination successfully. As there was nothing to be said, and neither wished to give occasion for it to be alleged that he had been the first to leave the range of fire, they would have remained there for a long time testing each otherвҖҷs courage had it not been that just then they heard the rattle of musketry and a muffled shout almost behind them in the wood. The French had attacked the men collecting wood in the copse. It was no longer possible for the hussars to retreat with the infantry. They were cut off from the line of retreat on the left by the French. However inconvenient the position, it was now necessary to attack in order to cut a way through for themselves. The squadron in which RostГіv was serving had scarcely time to mount before it was halted facing the enemy. Again, as at the Enns bridge, there was nothing between the squadron and the enemy, and again that terrible dividing line of uncertainty and fearвҒ вҖ”resembling the line separating the living from the deadвҒ вҖ”lay between them. All were conscious of this unseen line, and the question whether they would cross it or not, and how they would cross it, agitated them all. The colonel rode to the front, angrily gave some reply to questions put to him by the officers, and, like a man desperately insisting on having his own way, gave an order. No one said anything definite, but the rumor of an attack spread through the squadron. The command to form up rang out and the sabers whizzed as they were drawn from their scabbards. Still no one moved. The troops of the left flank, infantry and hussars alike, felt that the commander did not himself know what to do, and this irresolution communicated itself to the men. вҖңIf only they would be quick!вҖқ thought RostГіv, feeling that at last the time had come to experience the joy of an attack of which he had so often heard from his fellow hussars. вҖңFoвҖҷward, with God, lads!вҖқ rang out DenГӯsovвҖҷs voice. вҖңAt a twot foвҖҷward!вҖқ The horsesвҖҷ croups began to sway in the front line. Rook pulled at the reins and started of his own accord. Before him, on the right, RostГіv saw the front lines of his hussars and still farther ahead a dark line which he could not see distinctly but took to be the enemy. Shots could be heard, but some way off. вҖңFaster!вҖқ came the word of command, and RostГіv felt RookвҖҷs flanks drooping as he broke into a gallop. RostГіv anticipated his horseвҖҷs movements and became more and more elated. He had noticed a solitary tree ahead of him. This tree had been in the middle of the line that had seemed so terribleвҒ вҖ”and now he had crossed that line and not only was there nothing terrible, but everything was becoming more and more happy and animated. вҖңOh, how I will slash at him!вҖқ thought RostГіv, gripping the hilt of his saber. вҖңHur-a-a-a-ah!вҖқ came a roar of voices. вҖңLet anyone come my way now,вҖқ thought RostГіv driving his spurs into Rook and letting him go at a full gallop so that he outstripped the others. Ahead, the enemy was already visible. Suddenly something like a birch broom seemed to sweep over the squadron. RostГіv raised his saber, ready to strike, but at that instant the trooper NikГӯtenko, who was galloping ahead, shot away from him, and RostГіv felt as in a dream that he continued to be carried forward with unnatural speed but yet stayed on the same spot. From behind him BondarchГәk, an hussar he knew, jolted against him and looked angrily at him. BondarchГәkвҖҷs horse swerved and galloped past. вҖңHow is it I am not moving? I have fallen, I am killed!вҖқ RostГіv asked and answered at the same instant. He was alone in the middle of a field. Instead of the moving horses and hussarsвҖҷ backs, he saw nothing before him but the motionless earth and the stubble around him. There was warm blood under his arm. вҖңNo, I am wounded and the horse is killed.вҖқ Rook tried to rise on his forelegs but fell back, pinning his riderвҖҷs leg. Blood was flowing from his head; he struggled but could not rise. RostГіv also tried to rise but fell back, his sabretache having become entangled in the saddle. Where our men were, and where the French, he did not know. There was no one near. Having disentangled his leg, he rose. вҖңWhere, on which side, was now the line that had so sharply divided the two armies?вҖқ he asked himself and could not answer. вҖңCan something bad have happened to me?вҖқ he wondered as he got up: and at that moment he felt that something superfluous was hanging on his benumbed left arm. The wrist felt as if it were not his. He examined his hand carefully, vainly trying to find blood on it. вҖңAh, here are people coming,вҖқ he thought joyfully, seeing some men running toward him. вҖңThey will help me!вҖқ In front came a man wearing a strange shako and a blue cloak, swarthy, sunburned, and with a hooked nose. Then came two more, and many more running behind. One of them said something strange, not in Russian. In among the hindmost of these men wearing similar shakos was a Russian hussar. He was being held by the arms and his horse was being led behind him. вҖңIt must be one of ours, a prisoner. Yes. Can it be that they will take me too? Who are these men?вҖқ thought RostГіv, scarcely believing his eyes. вҖңCan they be French?вҖқ He looked at the approaching Frenchmen, and though but a moment before he had been galloping to get at them and hack them to pieces, their proximity now seemed so awful that he could not believe his eyes. вҖңWho are they? Why are they running? Can they be coming at me? And why? To kill me? Me whom everyone is so fond of?вҖқ He remembered his motherвҖҷs love for him, and his familyвҖҷs, and his friendsвҖҷ, and the enemyвҖҷs intention to kill him seemed impossible. вҖңBut perhaps they may do it!вҖқ For more than ten seconds he stood not moving from the spot or realizing the situation. The foremost Frenchman, the one with the hooked nose, was already so close that the expression of his face could be seen. And the excited, alien face of that man, his bayonet hanging down, holding his breath, and running so lightly, frightened RostГіv. He seized his pistol and, instead of firing it, flung it at the Frenchman and ran with all his might toward the bushes. He did not now run with the feeling of doubt and conflict with which he had trodden the Enns bridge, but with the feeling of a hare fleeing from the hounds. One single sentiment, that of fear for his young and happy life, possessed his whole being. Rapidly leaping the furrows, he fled across the field with the impetuosity he used to show at catchplay, now and then turning his good-natured, pale, young face to look back. A shudder of terror went through him: вҖңNo, better not look,вҖқ he thought, but having reached the bushes he glanced round once more. The French had fallen behind, and just as he looked round the first man changed his run to a walk and, turning, shouted something loudly to a comrade farther back. RostГіv paused. вҖңNo, thereвҖҷs some mistake,вҖқ thought he. вҖңThey canвҖҷt have wanted to kill me.вҖқ But at the same time, his left arm felt as heavy as if a seventy-pound weight were tied to it. He could run no more. The Frenchman also stopped and took aim. RostГіv closed his eyes and stooped down. One bullet and then another whistled past him. He mustered his last remaining strength, took hold of his left hand with his right, and reached the bushes. Behind these were some Russian sharpshooters. II II вҖңHeвҖҷs coming!вҖқ shouted the signaler at that moment. The regimental commander, flushing, ran to his horse, seized the stirrup with trembling hands, threw his body across the saddle, righted himself, drew his saber, and with a happy and resolute countenance, opening his mouth awry, prepared to shout. The regiment fluttered like a bird preening its plumage and became motionless. вҖңAttention!вҖқ shouted the regimental commander in a soul-shaking voice which expressed joy for himself, severity for the regiment, and welcome for the approaching chief. Along the broad country road, edged on both sides by trees, came a high, light blue Viennese calГЁche, slightly creaking on its springs and drawn by six horses at a smart trot. Behind the calГЁche galloped the suite and a convoy of Croats. Beside KutГәzov sat an Austrian general, in a white uniform that looked strange among the Russian black ones. The calГЁche stopped in front of the regiment. KutГәzov and the Austrian general were talking in low voices and KutГәzov smiled slightly as treading heavily he stepped down from the carriage just as if those two thousand men breathlessly gazing at him and the regimental commander did not exist. The word of command rang out, and again the regiment quivered, as with a jingling sound it presented arms. Then amidst a dead silence the feeble voice of the commander in chief was heard. The regiment roared, вҖңHealth to your exвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ lenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ lenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ lency!вҖқ and again all became silent. At first KutГәzov stood still while the regiment moved; then he and the general in white, accompanied by the suite, walked between the ranks. From the way the regimental commander saluted the commander in chief and devoured him with his eyes, drawing himself up obsequiously, and from the way he walked through the ranks behind the generals, bending forward and hardly able to restrain his jerky movements, and from the way he darted forward at every word or gesture of the commander in chief, it was evident that he performed his duty as a subordinate with even greater zeal than his duty as a commander. Thanks to the strictness and assiduity of its commander the regiment, in comparison with others that had reached Braunau at the same time, was in splendid condition. There were only 217 sick and stragglers. Everything was in good order except the boots. KutГәzov walked through the ranks, sometimes stopping to say a few friendly words to officers he had known in the Turkish war, sometimes also to the soldiers. Looking at their boots he several times shook his head sadly, pointing them out to the Austrian general with an expression which seemed to say that he was not blaming anyone, but could not help noticing what a bad state of things it was. The regimental commander ran forward on each such occasion, fearing to miss a single word of the commander in chiefвҖҷs regarding the regiment. Behind KutГәzov, at a distance that allowed every softly spoken word to be heard, followed some twenty men of his suite. These gentlemen talked among themselves and sometimes laughed. Nearest of all to the commander in chief walked a handsome adjutant. This was Prince BolkГіnski. Beside him was his comrade NesvГӯtski, a tall staff officer, extremely stout, with a kindly, smiling, handsome face and moist eyes. NesvГӯtski could hardly keep from laughter provoked by a swarthy hussar officer who walked beside him. This hussar, with a grave face and without a smile or a change in the expression of his fixed eyes, watched the regimental commanderвҖҷs back and mimicked his every movement. Each time the commander started and bent forward, the hussar started and bent forward in exactly the same manner. NesvГӯtski laughed and nudged the others to make them look at the wag. KutГәzov walked slowly and languidly past thousands of eyes which were starting from their sockets to watch their chief. On reaching the third company he suddenly stopped. His suite, not having expected this, involuntarily came closer to him. вҖңAh, TimГіkhin!вҖқ said he, recognizing the red-nosed captain who had been reprimanded on account of the blue greatcoat. One would have thought it impossible for a man to stretch himself more than TimГіkhin had done when he was reprimanded by the regimental commander, but now that the commander in chief addressed him he drew himself up to such an extent that it seemed he could not have sustained it had the commander in chief continued to look at him, and so KutГәzov, who evidently understood his case and wished him nothing but good, quickly turned away, a scarcely perceptible smile flitting over his scarred and puffy face. вҖңAnother Ismail comrade,вҖқ said he. вҖңA brave officer! Are you satisfied with him?вҖқ he asked the regimental commander. And the latterвҒ вҖ”unconscious that he was being reflected in the hussar officer as in a looking glassвҒ вҖ”started, moved forward, and answered: вҖңHighly satisfied, your excellency!вҖқ вҖңWe all have our weaknesses,вҖқ said KutГәzov smiling and walking away from him. вҖңHe used to have a predilection for Bacchus.вҖқ The regimental commander was afraid he might be blamed for this and did not answer. The hussar at that moment noticed the face of the red-nosed captain and his drawn-in stomach, and mimicked his expression and pose with such exactitude that NesvГӯtski could not help laughing. KutГәzov turned round. The officer evidently had complete control of his face, and while KutГәzov was turning managed to make a grimace and then assume a most serious, deferential, and innocent expression. The third company was the last, and KutГәzov pondered, apparently trying to recollect something. Prince AndrГ©y stepped forward from among the suite and said in French: вҖңYou told me to remind you of the officer DГіlokhov, reduced to the ranks in this regiment.вҖқ вҖңWhere is DГіlokhov?вҖқ asked KutГәzov. DГіlokhov, who had already changed into a soldierвҖҷs gray greatcoat, did not wait to be called. The shapely figure of the fair-haired soldier, with his clear blue eyes, stepped forward from the ranks, went up to the commander in chief, and presented arms. вҖңHave you a complaint to make?вҖқ KutГәzov asked with a slight frown. вҖңThis is DГіlokhov,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңAh!вҖқ said KutГәzov. вҖңI hope this will be a lesson to you. Do your duty. The Emperor is gracious, and I shanвҖҷt forget you if you deserve well.вҖқ The clear blue eyes looked at the commander in chief just as boldly as they had looked at the regimental commander, seeming by their expression to tear open the veil of convention that separates a commander in chief so widely from a private. вҖңOne thing I ask of your excellency,вҖқ DГіlokhov said in his firm, ringing, deliberate voice. вҖңI ask an opportunity to atone for my fault and prove my devotion to His Majesty the Emperor and to Russia!вҖқ KutГәzov turned away. The same smile of the eyes with which he had turned from Captain TimГіkhin again flitted over his face. He turned away with a grimace as if to say that everything DГіlokhov had said to him and everything he could say had long been known to him, that he was weary of it and it was not at all what he wanted. He turned away and went to the carriage. The regiment broke up into companies, which went to their appointed quarters near Braunau, where they hoped to receive boots and clothes and to rest after their hard marches. вҖңYou wonвҖҷt bear me a grudge, ProkhГіr IgnГЎtych?вҖқ said the regimental commander, overtaking the third company on its way to its quarters and riding up to Captain TimГіkhin who was walking in front. (The regimental commanderвҖҷs face now that the inspection was happily over beamed with irrepressible delight.) вҖңItвҖҷs in the EmperorвҖҷs serviceвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ it canвҖҷt be helpedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ one is sometimes a bit hasty on paradeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I am the first to apologize, you know me!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ He was very pleased!вҖқ And he held out his hand to the captain. вҖңDonвҖҷt mention it, General, as if IвҖҷd be so bold!вҖқ replied the captain, his nose growing redder as he gave a smile which showed where two front teeth were missing that had been knocked out by the butt end of a gun at Ismail. вҖңAnd tell Mr.В DГіlokhov that I wonвҖҷt forget himвҒ вҖ”he may be quite easy. And tell me, pleaseвҒ вҖ”IвҖҷve been meaning to askвҒ вҖ”how is he behaving himself, and in generalвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAs far as the service goes he is quite punctilious, your excellency; but his characterвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said TimГіkhin. вҖңAnd what about his character?вҖқ asked the regimental commander. вҖңItвҖҷs different on different days,вҖқ answered the captain. вҖңOne day he is sensible, well educated, and good-natured, and the next heвҖҷs a wild beast.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ In Poland, if you please, he nearly killed a Jew.вҖқ вҖңOh, well, well!вҖқ remarked the regimental commander. вҖңStill, one must have pity on a young man in misfortune. You know he has important connectionsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, then, you justвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI will, your excellency,вҖқ said TimГіkhin, showing by his smile that he understood his commanderвҖҷs wish. вҖңWell, of course, of course!вҖқ The regimental commander sought out DГіlokhov in the ranks and, reining in his horse, said to him: вҖңAfter the next affairвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ epaulettes.вҖқ DГіlokhov looked round but did not say anything, nor did the mocking smile on his lips change. вҖңWell, thatвҖҷs all right,вҖқ continued the regimental commander. вҖңA cup of vodka for the men from me,вҖқ he added so that the soldiers could hear. вҖңI thank you all! God be praised!вҖқ and he rode past that company and overtook the next one. вҖңWell, heвҖҷs really a good fellow, one can serve under him,вҖқ said TimГіkhin to the subaltern beside him. вҖңIn a word, a hearty oneвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said the subaltern, laughing (the regimental commander was nicknamed King of Hearts). The cheerful mood of their officers after the inspection infected the soldiers. The company marched on gaily. The soldiersвҖҷ voices could be heard on every side. вҖңAnd they said KutГәzov was blind of one eye?вҖқ вҖңAnd so he is! Quite blind!вҖқ вҖңNo, friend, he is sharper-eyed than you are. Boots and leg bandsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ he noticed everythingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhen he looked at my feet, friendвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ well, thinks IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAnd that other one with him, the Austrian, looked as if he were smeared with chalkвҒ вҖ”as white as flour! I suppose they polish him up as they do the guns.вҖқ вҖңI say, FГ©deshon!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Did he say when the battles are to begin? You were near him. Everybody said that Bonaparte himself was at Braunau.вҖқ вҖңBonaparte himself!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Just listen to the fool, what he doesnвҖҷt know! The Prussians are up in arms now. The Austrians, you see, are putting them down. When theyвҖҷve been put down, the war with Bonaparte will begin. And he says Bonaparte is in Braunau! Shows youвҖҷre a fool. YouвҖҷd better listen more carefully!вҖқ вҖңWhat devils these quartermasters are! See, the fifth company is turning into the village alreadyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ they will have their buckwheat cooked before we reach our quarters.вҖқ вҖңGive me a biscuit, you devil!вҖқ вҖңAnd did you give me tobacco yesterday? ThatвҖҷs just it, friend! Ah, well, never mind, here you are.вҖқ вҖңThey might call a halt here or weвҖҷll have to do another four miles without eating.вҖқ вҖңWasnвҖҷt it fine when those Germans gave us lifts!
