You just sit still and are drawn along.вҖқ вҖңAnd here, friend, the people are quite beggarly. There they all seemed to be PolesвҒ вҖ”all under the Russian crownвҒ вҖ”but here theyвҖҷre all regular Germans.вҖқ вҖңSingers to the frontвҖқ came the captainвҖҷs order. And from the different ranks some twenty men ran to the front. A drummer, their leader, turned round facing the singers, and flourishing his arm, began a long-drawn-out soldiersвҖҷ song, commencing with the words: вҖңMorning dawned, the sun was rising,вҖқ and concluding: вҖңOn then, brothers, on to glory, led by Father KГЎmenski.вҖқ This song had been composed in the Turkish campaign and now being sung in Austria, the only change being that the words вҖңFather KГЎmenskiвҖқ were replaced by вҖңFather KutГәzov.вҖқ Having jerked out these last words as soldiers do and waved his arms as if flinging something to the ground, the drummerвҒ вҖ”a lean, handsome soldier of fortyвҒ вҖ”looked sternly at the singers and screwed up his eyes. Then having satisfied himself that all eyes were fixed on him, he raised both arms as if carefully lifting some invisible but precious object above his head and, holding it there for some seconds, suddenly flung it down and began: вҖңOh, my bower, oh, my bowerвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ!вҖқ вҖңOh, my bower newвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ!вҖқ chimed in twenty voices, and the castanet player, in spite of the burden of his equipment, rushed out to the front and, walking backwards before the company, jerked his shoulders and flourished his castanets as if threatening someone. The soldiers, swinging their arms and keeping time spontaneously, marched with long steps. Behind the company the sound of wheels, the creaking of springs, and the tramp of horsesвҖҷ hoofs were heard. KutГәzov and his suite were returning to the town. The commander in chief made a sign that the men should continue to march at ease, and he and all his suite showed pleasure at the sound of the singing and the sight of the dancing soldier and the gay and smartly marching men. In the second file from the right flank, beside which the carriage passed the company, a blue-eyed soldier involuntarily attracted notice. It was DГіlokhov marching with particular grace and boldness in time to the song and looking at those driving past as if he pitied all who were not at that moment marching with the company. The hussar cornet of KutГәzovвҖҷs suite who had mimicked the regimental commander, fell back from the carriage and rode up to DГіlokhov. Hussar cornet ZherkГіv had at one time, in Petersburg, belonged to the wild set led by DГіlokhov. ZherkГіv had met DГіlokhov abroad as a private and had not seen fit to recognize him. But now that KutГәzov had spoken to the gentleman ranker, he addressed him with the cordiality of an old friend. вҖңMy dear fellow, how are you?вҖқ said he through the singing, making his horse keep pace with the company. вҖңHow am I?вҖқ DГіlokhov answered coldly. вҖңI am as you see.вҖқ The lively song gave a special flavor to the tone of free and easy gaiety with which ZherkГіv spoke, and to the intentional coldness of DГіlokhovвҖҷs reply. вҖңAnd how do you get on with the officers?вҖқ inquired ZherkГіv. вҖңAll right. They are good fellows. And how have you wriggled onto the staff?вҖқ вҖңI was attached; IвҖҷm on duty.вҖқ Both were silent. вҖңShe let the hawk fly upward from her wide right sleeve,вҖқ went the song, arousing an involuntary sensation of courage and cheerfulness. Their conversation would probably have been different but for the effect of that song. вҖңIs it true that Austrians have been beaten?вҖқ asked DГіlokhov. вҖңThe devil only knows! They say so.вҖқ вҖңIвҖҷm glad,вҖқ answered DГіlokhov briefly and clearly, as the song demanded. вҖңI say, come round some evening and weвҖҷll have a game of faro!вҖқ said ZherkГіv. вҖңWhy, have you too much money?вҖқ вҖңDo come.вҖқ вҖңI canвҖҷt. IвҖҷve sworn not to. I wonвҖҷt drink and wonвҖҷt play till I get reinstated.вҖқ вҖңWell, thatвҖҷs only till the first engagement.вҖқ вҖңWe shall see.вҖқ They were again silent. вҖңCome if you need anything. One can at least be of use on the staffвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ DГіlokhov smiled. вҖңDonвҖҷt trouble. If I want anything, I wonвҖҷt begвҒ вҖ”IвҖҷll take it!вҖқ вҖңWell, never mind; I onlyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAnd I onlyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңGoodbye.вҖқ вҖңGood healthвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңItвҖҷs a long, long way. To my native landвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ ZherkГіv touched his horse with the spurs; it pranced excitedly from foot to foot uncertain with which to start, then settled down, galloped past the company, and overtook the carriage, still keeping time to the song. XX XX The infantry regiments that had been caught unawares in the outskirts of the wood ran out of it, the different companies getting mixed, and retreated as a disorderly crowd. One soldier, in his fear, uttered the senseless cry, вҖңCut off!вҖқ that is so terrible in battle, and that word infected the whole crowd with a feeling of panic. вҖңSurrounded! Cut off? WeвҖҷre lost!вҖқ shouted the fugitives. The moment he heard the firing and the cry from behind, the general realized that something dreadful had happened to his regiment, and the thought that he, an exemplary officer of many yearsвҖҷ service who had never been to blame, might be held responsible at headquarters for negligence or inefficiency so staggered him that, forgetting the recalcitrant cavalry colonel, his own dignity as a general, and above all quite forgetting the danger and all regard for self-preservation, he clutched the crupper of his saddle and, spurring his horse, galloped to the regiment under a hail of bullets which fell around, but fortunately missed him. His one desire was to know what was happening and at any cost correct, or remedy, the mistake if he had made one, so that he, an exemplary officer of twenty-two yearsвҖҷ service, who had never been censured, should not be held to blame. Having galloped safely through the French, he reached a field behind the copse across which our men, regardless of orders, were running and descending the valley. That moment of moral hesitation which decides the fate of battles had arrived. Would this disorderly crowd of soldiers attend to the voice of their commander, or would they, disregarding him, continue their flight? Despite his desperate shouts that used to seem so terrible to the soldiers, despite his furious purple countenance distorted out of all likeness to his former self, and the flourishing of his saber, the soldiers all continued to run, talking, firing into the air, and disobeying orders. The moral hesitation which decided the fate of battles was evidently culminating in a panic. The general had a fit of coughing as a result of shouting and of the powder smoke and stopped in despair. Everything seemed lost. But at that moment the French who were attacking, suddenly and without any apparent reason, ran back and disappeared from the outskirts, and Russian sharpshooters showed themselves in the copse. It was TimГіkhinвҖҷs company, which alone had maintained its order in the wood and, having lain in ambush in a ditch, now attacked the French unexpectedly. TimГіkhin, armed only with a sword, had rushed at the enemy with such a desperate cry and such mad, drunken determination that, taken by surprise, the French had thrown down their muskets and run. DГіlokhov, running beside TimГіkhin, killed a Frenchman at close quarters and was the first to seize the surrendering French officer by his collar. Our fugitives returned, the battalions reformed, and the French who had nearly cut our left flank in half were for the moment repulsed. Our reserve units were able to join up, and the fight was at an end. The regimental commander and Major EkonГіmov had stopped beside a bridge, letting the retreating companies pass by them, when a soldier came up and took hold of the commanderвҖҷs stirrup, almost leaning against him. The man was wearing a bluish coat of broadcloth, he had no knapsack or cap, his head was bandaged, and over his shoulder a French munition pouch was slung. He had an officerвҖҷs sword in his hand. The soldier was pale, his blue eyes looked impudently into the commanderвҖҷs face, and his lips were smiling. Though the commander was occupied in giving instructions to Major EkonГіmov, he could not help taking notice of the soldier. вҖңYour excellency, here are two trophies,вҖқ said DГіlokhov, pointing to the French sword and pouch. вҖңI have taken an officer prisoner. I stopped the company.вҖқ DГіlokhov breathed heavily from weariness and spoke in abrupt sentences. вҖңThe whole company can bear witness. I beg you will remember this, your excellency!вҖқ вҖңAll right, all right,вҖқ replied the commander, and turned to Major EkonГіmov. But DГіlokhov did not go away; he untied the handkerchief around his head, pulled it off, and showed the blood congealed on his hair. вҖңA bayonet wound. I remained at the front. Remember, your excellency!вҖқ TГәshinвҖҷs battery had been forgotten and only at the very end of the action did Prince BagratiГіn, still hearing the cannonade in the center, send his orderly staff officer, and later Prince AndrГ©y also, to order the battery to retire as quickly as possible. When the supports attached to TГәshinвҖҷs battery had been moved away in the middle of the action by someoneвҖҷs order, the battery had continued firing and was only not captured by the French because the enemy could not surmise that anyone could have the effrontery to continue firing from four quite undefended guns. On the contrary, the energetic action of that battery led the French to suppose that hereвҒ вҖ”in the centerвҒ вҖ”the main Russian forces were concentrated. Twice they had attempted to attack this point, but on each occasion had been driven back by grapeshot from the four isolated guns on the hillock. Soon after Prince BagratiГіn had left him, TГәshin had succeeded in setting fire to SchГ¶n Grabern. вҖңLook at them scurrying! ItвҖҷs burning! Just see the smoke! Fine! Grand! Look at the smoke, the smoke!вҖқ exclaimed the artillerymen, brightening up. All the guns, without waiting for orders, were being fired in the direction of the conflagration. As if urging each other on, the soldiers cried at each shot: вҖңFine! ThatвҖҷs good! Look at itвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Grand!вҖқ The fire, fanned by the breeze, was rapidly spreading. The French columns that had advanced beyond the village went back; but as though in revenge for this failure, the enemy placed ten guns to the right of the village and began firing them at TГәshinвҖҷs battery. In their childlike glee, aroused by the fire and their luck in successfully cannonading the French, our artillerymen only noticed this battery when two balls, and then four more, fell among our guns, one knocking over two horses and another tearing off a munition-wagon driverвҖҷs leg. Their spirits once roused were, however, not diminished, but only changed character. The horses were replaced by others from a reserve gun carriage, the wounded were carried away, and the four guns were turned against the ten-gun battery. TГәshinвҖҷs companion officer had been killed at the beginning of the engagement and within an hour seventeen of the forty men of the gunsвҖҷ crews had been disabled, but the artillerymen were still as merry and lively as ever. Twice they noticed the French appearing below them, and then they fired grapeshot at them. Little TГәshin, moving feebly and awkwardly, kept telling his orderly to вҖңrefill my pipe for that one!вҖқ and then, scattering sparks from it, ran forward shading his eyes with his small hand to look at the French. вҖңSmack at вҖҷem, lads!вҖқ he kept saying, seizing the guns by the wheels and working the screws himself. Amid the smoke, deafened by the incessant reports which always made him jump, TГәshin not taking his pipe from his mouth ran from gun to gun, now aiming, now counting the charges, now giving orders about replacing dead or wounded horses and harnessing fresh ones, and shouting in his feeble voice, so high pitched and irresolute. His face grew more and more animated. Only when a man was killed or wounded did he frown and turn away from the sight, shouting angrily at the men who, as is always the case, hesitated about lifting the injured or dead. The soldiers, for the most part handsome fellows and, as is always the case in an artillery company, a head and shoulders taller and twice as broad as their officerвҒ вҖ”all looked at their commander like children in an embarrassing situation, and the expression on his face was invariably reflected on theirs. Owing to the terrible uproar and the necessity for concentration and activity, TГәshin did not experience the slightest unpleasant sense of fear, and the thought that he might be killed or badly wounded never occurred to him. On the contrary, he became more and more elated. It seemed to him that it was a very long time ago, almost a day, since he had first seen the enemy and fired the first shot, and that the corner of the field he stood on was well-known and familiar ground. Though he thought of everything, considered everything, and did everything the best of officers could do in his position, he was in a state akin to feverish delirium or drunkenness. From the deafening sounds of his own guns around him, the whistle and thud of the enemyвҖҷs cannon balls, from the flushed and perspiring faces of the crew bustling round the guns, from the sight of the blood of men and horses, from the little puffs of smoke on the enemyвҖҷs side (always followed by a ball flying past and striking the earth, a man, a gun, a horse), from the sight of all these things a fantastic world of his own had taken possession of his brain and at that moment afforded him pleasure. The enemyвҖҷs guns were in his fancy not guns but pipes from which occasional puffs were blown by an invisible smoker. вҖңThereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ heвҖҷs puffing again,вҖқ muttered TГәshin to himself, as a small cloud rose from the hill and was borne in a streak to the left by the wind. вҖңNow look out for the ballвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ weвҖҷll throw it back.вҖқ вҖңWhat do you want, your honor?вҖқ asked an artilleryman, standing close by, who heard him muttering. вҖңNothingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ only a shellвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he answered. вҖңCome along, our MatvГ©vna!вҖқ he said to himself. вҖңMatvГ©vnaвҖқ37 was the name his fancy gave to the farthest gun of the battery, which was large and of an old pattern. The French swarming round their guns seemed to him like ants. In that world, the handsome drunkard Number One of the second gunвҖҷs crew was вҖңuncleвҖқ; TГәshin looked at him more often than at anyone else and took delight in his every movement. The sound of musketry at the foot of the hill, now diminishing, now increasing, seemed like someoneвҖҷs breathing. He listened intently to the ebb and flow of these sounds. вҖңAh! Breathing again, breathing!вҖқ he muttered to himself. He imagined himself as an enormously tall, powerful man who was throwing cannon balls at the French with both hands. вҖңNow then, MatvГ©vna, dear old lady, donвҖҷt let me down!вҖқ he was saying as he moved from the gun, when a strange, unfamiliar voice called above his head: вҖңCaptain TГәshin! Captain!вҖқ TГәshin turned round in dismay. It was the staff officer who had turned him out of the booth at Grunth. He was shouting in a gasping voice: вҖңAre you mad? You have twice been ordered to retreat, and youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhy are they down on me?вҖқ thought TГәshin, looking in alarm at his superior. вҖңIвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ donвҖҷtвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he muttered, holding up two fingers to his cap. вҖңIвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ But the staff officer did not finish what he wanted to say. A cannon ball, flying close to him, caused him to duck and bend over his horse. He paused, and just as he was about to say something more, another ball stopped him. He turned his horse and galloped off. вҖңRetire! All to retire!вҖқ he shouted from a distance. The soldiers laughed. A moment later, an adjutant arrived with the same order. It was Prince AndrГ©y. The first thing he saw on riding up to the space where TГәshinвҖҷs guns were stationed was an unharnessed horse with a broken leg, that lay screaming piteously beside the harnessed horses. Blood was gushing from its leg as from a spring. Among the limbers lay several dead men. One ball after another passed over as he approached and he felt a nervous shudder run down his spine. But the mere thought of being afraid roused him again. вҖңI cannot be afraid,вҖқ thought he, and dismounted slowly among the guns. He delivered the order and did not leave the battery. He decided to have the guns removed from their positions and withdrawn in his presence. Together with TГәshin, stepping across the bodies and under a terrible fire from the French, he attended to the removal of the guns. вҖңA staff officer was here a minute ago, but skipped off,вҖқ said an artilleryman to Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңNot like your honor!вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y said nothing to TГәshin. They were both so busy as to seem not to notice one another. When having limbered up the only two cannon that remained uninjured out of the four, they began moving down the hill (one shattered gun and one unicorn were left behind), Prince AndrГ©y rode up to TГәshin. вҖңWell, till we meet againвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he said, holding out his hand to TГәshin. вҖңGoodbye, my dear fellow,вҖқ said TГәshin. вҖңDear soul! Goodbye, my dear fellow!вҖқ and for some unknown reason tears suddenly filled his eyes. XXI XXI The wind had fallen and black clouds, merging with the powder smoke, hung low over the field of battle on the horizon. It was growing dark and the glow of two conflagrations was the more conspicuous. The cannonade was dying down, but the rattle of musketry behind and on the right sounded oftener and nearer. As soon as TГәshin with his guns, continually driving round or coming upon wounded men, was out of range of fire and had descended into the dip, he was met by some of the staff, among them the staff officer and ZherkГіv, who had been twice sent to TГәshinвҖҷs battery but had never reached it. Interrupting one another, they all gave, and transmitted, orders as to how to proceed, reprimanding and reproaching him. TГәshin gave no orders, and, silentlyвҒ вҖ”fearing to speak because at every word he felt ready to weep without knowing whyвҒ вҖ”rode behind on his artillery nag. Though the orders were to abandon the wounded, many of them dragged themselves after troops and begged for seats on the gun carriages. The jaunty infantry officer who just before the battle had rushed out of TГәshinвҖҷs wattle shed was laid, with a bullet in his stomach, on вҖңMatvГ©vnaвҖҷsвҖқ carriage. At the foot of the hill, a pale hussar cadet, supporting one hand with the other, came up to TГәshin and asked for a seat. вҖңCaptain, for GodвҖҷs sake! IвҖҷve hurt my arm,вҖқ he said timidly. вҖңFor GodвҖҷs sakeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I canвҖҷt walk. For GodвҖҷs sake!вҖқ It was plain that this cadet had already repeatedly asked for a lift and been refused. He asked in a hesitating, piteous voice. вҖңTell them to give me a seat, for GodвҖҷs sake!вҖқ вҖңGive him a seat,вҖқ said TГәshin. вҖңLay a cloak for him to sit on, lad,вҖқ he said, addressing his favorite soldier. вҖңAnd where is the wounded officer?вҖқ вҖңHe has been set down. He died,вҖқ replied someone. вҖңHelp him up. Sit down, dear fellow, sit down! Spread out the cloak, AntГіnov.вҖқ The cadet was RostГіv. With one hand he supported the other; he was pale and his jaw trembled, shivering feverishly. He was placed on вҖңMatvГ©vna,вҖқ the gun from which they had removed the dead officer. The cloak they spread under him was wet with blood which stained his breeches and arm. вҖңWhat, are you wounded, my lad?вҖқ said TГәshin, approaching the gun on which RostГіv sat. вҖңNo, itвҖҷs a sprain.вҖқ вҖңThen what is this blood on the gun carriage?вҖқ inquired TГәshin. вҖңIt was the officer, your honor, stained it,вҖқ answered the artilleryman, wiping away the blood with his coat sleeve, as if apologizing for the state of his gun. It was all that they could do to get the guns up the rise aided by the infantry, and having reached the village of Gruntersdorf they halted. It had grown so dark that one could not distinguish the uniforms ten paces off, and the firing had begun to subside. Suddenly, nearby on the right, shouting and firing were again heard. Flashes of shot gleamed in the darkness. This was the last French attack and was met by soldiers who had sheltered in the village houses. They all rushed out of the village again, but TГәshinвҖҷs guns could not move, and the artillerymen, TГәshin, and the cadet exchanged silent glances as they awaited their fate. The firing died down and soldiers, talking eagerly, streamed out of a side street. вҖңNot hurt, PetrГіv?вҖқ asked one. вҖңWeвҖҷve given it вҖҷem hot, mate! They wonвҖҷt make another push now,вҖқ said another. вҖңYou couldnвҖҷt see a thing. How they shot at their own fellows! Nothing could be seen. Pitch-dark, brother! IsnвҖҷt there something to drink?вҖқ The French had been repulsed for the last time. And again and again in the complete darkness TГәshinвҖҷs guns moved forward, surrounded by the humming infantry as by a frame. In the darkness, it seemed as though a gloomy unseen river was flowing always in one direction, humming with whispers and talk and the sound of hoofs and wheels. Amid the general rumble, the groans and voices of the wounded were more distinctly heard than any other sound in the darkness of the night. The gloom that enveloped the army was filled with their groans, which seemed to melt into one with the darkness of the night. After a while the moving mass became agitated, someone rode past on a white horse followed by his suite, and said something in passing: вҖңWhat did he say? Where to, now? Halt, is it? Did he thank us?вҖқ came eager questions from all sides. The whole moving mass began pressing closer together and a report spread that they were ordered to halt: evidently those in front had halted. All remained where they were in the middle of the muddy road. Fires were lighted and the talk became more audible. Captain TГәshin, having given orders to his company, sent a soldier to find a dressing station or a doctor for the cadet, and sat down by a bonfire the soldiers had kindled on the road. RostГіv, too, dragged himself to the fire. From pain, cold, and damp, a feverish shivering shook his whole body. Drowsiness was irresistibly mastering him, but he kept awake by an excruciating pain in his arm, for which he could find no satisfactory position. He kept closing his eyes and then again looking at the fire, which seemed to him dazzlingly red, and at the feeble, round-shouldered figure of TГәshin who was sitting cross-legged like a Turk beside him. TГәshinвҖҷs large, kind, intelligent eyes were fixed with sympathy and commiseration on RostГіv, who saw that TГәshin with his whole heart wished to help him but could not. From all sides were heard the footsteps and talk of the infantry, who were walking, driving past, and settling down all around. The sound of voices, the tramping feet, the horsesвҖҷ hoofs moving in mud, the crackling of wood fires near and afar, merged into one tremulous rumble. It was no longer, as before, a dark, unseen river flowing through the gloom, but a dark sea swelling and gradually subsiding after a storm. RostГіv looked at and listened listlessly to what passed before and around him. An infantryman came to the fire, squatted on his heels, held his hands to the blaze, and turned away his face. вҖңYou donвҖҷt mind your honor?вҖқ he asked TГәshin. вҖңIвҖҷve lost my company, your honor. I donвҖҷt know whereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ such bad luck!вҖқ With the soldier, an infantry officer with a bandaged cheek came up to the bonfire, and addressing TГәshin asked him to have the guns moved a trifle to let a wagon go past. After he had gone, two soldiers rushed to the campfire. They were quarreling and fighting desperately, each trying to snatch from the other a boot they were both holding on to. вҖңYou picked it up?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I dare say! YouвҖҷre very smart!вҖқ one of them shouted hoarsely. Then a thin, pale soldier, his neck bandaged with a bloodstained leg band, came up and in angry tones asked the artillerymen for water. вҖңMust one die like a dog?вҖқ said he. TГәshin told them to give the man some water. Then a cheerful soldier ran up, begging a little fire for the infantry. вҖңA nice little hot torch for the infantry! Good luck to you, fellow countrymen. Thanks for the fireвҒ вҖ”weвҖҷll return it with interest,вҖқ said he, carrying away into the darkness a glowing stick. Next came four soldiers, carrying something heavy on a cloak, and passed by the fire. One of them stumbled. вҖңWho the devil has put the logs on the road?вҖқ snarled he. вҖңHeвҖҷs deadвҒ вҖ”why carry him?вҖқ said another. вҖңShut up!вҖқ And they disappeared into the darkness with their load. вҖңStill aching?вҖқ TГәshin asked RostГіv in a whisper. вҖңYes.вҖқ вҖңYour honor, youвҖҷre wanted by the general. He is in the hut here,вҖқ said a gunner, coming up to TГәshin. вҖңComing, friend.вҖқ TГәshin rose and, buttoning his greatcoat and pulling it straight, walked away from the fire. Not far from the artillery campfire, in a hut that had been prepared for him, Prince BagratiГіn sat at dinner, talking with some commanding officers who had gathered at his quarters. The little old man with the half-closed eyes was there greedily gnawing a mutton bone, and the general who had served blamelessly for twenty-two years, flushed by a glass of vodka and the dinner; and the staff officer with the signet ring, and ZherkГіv, uneasily glancing at them all, and Prince AndrГ©y, pale, with compressed lips and feverishly glittering eyes. In a corner of the hut stood a standard captured from the French, and the accountant with the naive face was feeling its texture, shaking his head in perplexityвҒ вҖ”perhaps because the banner really interested him, perhaps because it was hard for him, hungry as he was, to look on at a dinner where there was no place for him. In the next hut there was a French colonel who had been taken prisoner by our dragoons. Our officers were flocking in to look at him. Prince BagratiГіn was thanking the individual commanders and inquiring into details of the action and our losses. The general whose regiment had been inspected at Braunau was informing the prince that as soon as the action began he had withdrawn from the wood, mustered the men who were woodcutting, and, allowing the French to pass him, had made a bayonet charge with two battalions and had broken up the French troops. вҖңWhen I saw, your excellency, that their first battalion was disorganized, I stopped in the road and thought: вҖҳIвҖҷll let them come on and will meet them with the fire of the whole battalionвҖҷвҒ вҖ”and thatвҖҷs what I did.вҖқ The general had so wished to do this and was so sorry he had not managed to do it that it seemed to him as if it had really happened. Perhaps it might really have been so? Could one possibly make out amid all that confusion what did or did not happen? вҖңBy the way, your excellency, I should inform you,вҖқ he continuedвҒ вҖ”remembering DГіlokhovвҖҷs conversation with KutГәzov and his last interview with the gentleman-rankerвҒ вҖ”вҖңthat Private DГіlokhov, who was reduced to the ranks, took a French officer prisoner in my presence and particularly distinguished himself.вҖқ вҖңI saw the PГЎvlograd hussars attack there, your excellency,вҖқ chimed in ZherkГіv, looking uneasily around. He had not seen the hussars all that day, but had heard about them from an infantry officer. вҖңThey broke up two squares, your excellency.вҖқ Several of those present smiled at ZherkГіvвҖҷs words, expecting one of his usual jokes, but noticing that what he was saying redounded to the glory of our arms and of the dayвҖҷs work, they assumed a serious expression, though many of them knew that what he was saying was a lie devoid of any foundation. Prince BagratiГіn turned to the old colonel: вҖңGentlemen, I thank you all; all arms have behaved heroically: infantry, cavalry, and artillery. How was it that two guns were abandoned in the center?вҖқ he inquired, searching with his eyes for someone. (Prince BagratiГіn did not ask about the guns on the left flank; he knew that all the guns there had been abandoned at the very beginning of the action.) вҖңI think I sent you?вҖқ he added, turning to the staff officer on duty. вҖңOne was damaged,вҖқ answered the staff officer, вҖңand the other I canвҖҷt understand. I was there all the time giving orders and had only just left.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ It is true that it was hot there,вҖқ he added, modestly. Someone mentioned that Captain TГәshin was bivouacking close to the village and had already been sent for. вҖңOh, but you were there?вҖқ said Prince BagratiГіn, addressing Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңOf course, we only just missed one another,вҖқ said the staff officer, with a smile to BolkГіnski. вҖңI had not the pleasure of seeing you,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, coldly and abruptly. All were silent. TГәshin appeared at the threshold and made his way timidly from behind the backs of the generals. As he stepped past the generals in the crowded hut, feeling embarrassed as he always was by the sight of his superiors, he did not notice the staff of the banner and stumbled over it. Several of those present laughed. вҖңHow was it a gun was abandoned?вҖқ asked BagratiГіn, frowning, not so much at the captain as at those who were laughing, among whom ZherkГіv laughed loudest. Only now, when he was confronted by the stern authorities, did his guilt and the disgrace of having lost two guns and yet remaining alive present themselves to TГәshin in all their horror. He had been so excited that he had not thought about it until that moment. The officersвҖҷ laughter confused him still more. He stood before BagratiГіn with his lower jaw trembling and was hardly able to mutter: вҖңI donвҖҷt knowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ your excellencyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I had no menвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ your excellency.вҖқ вҖңYou might have taken some from the covering troops.вҖқ TГәshin did not say that there were no covering troops, though that was perfectly true. He was afraid of getting some other officer into trouble, and silently fixed his eyes on BagratiГіn as a schoolboy who has blundered looks at an examiner. The silence lasted some time. Prince BagratiГіn, apparently not wishing to be severe, found nothing to say; the others did not venture to intervene. Prince AndrГ©y looked at TГәshin from under his brows and his fingers twitched nervously. вҖңYour excellency!вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y broke the silence with his abrupt voice, вҖңyou were pleased to send me to Captain TГәshinвҖҷs battery. I went there and found two thirds of the men and horses knocked out, two guns smashed, and no supports at all.вҖқ Prince BagratiГіn and TГәshin looked with equal intentness at BolkГіnski, who spoke with suppressed agitation. вҖңAnd, if your excellency will allow me to express my opinion,вҖқ he continued, вҖңwe owe todayвҖҷs success chiefly to the action of that battery and the heroic endurance of Captain TГәshin and his company,вҖқ and without awaiting a reply, Prince AndrГ©y rose and left the table. Prince BagratiГіn looked at TГәshin, evidently reluctant to show distrust in BolkГіnskiвҖҷs emphatic opinion yet not feeling able fully to credit it, bent his head, and told TГәshin that he could go. Prince AndrГ©y went out with him. вҖңThank you; you saved me, my dear fellow!вҖқ said TГәshin. Prince AndrГ©y gave him a look, but said nothing and went away. He felt sad and depressed. It was all so strange, so unlike what he had hoped. вҖңWho are they? Why are they here? What do they want? And when will all this end?вҖқ thought RostГіv, looking at the changing shadows before him. The pain in his arm became more and more intense. Irresistible drowsiness overpowered him, red rings danced before his eyes, and the impression of those voices and faces and a sense of loneliness merged with the physical pain. It was they, these soldiersвҒ вҖ”wounded and unwoundedвҒ вҖ”it was they who were crushing, weighing down, and twisting the sinews and scorching the flesh of his sprained arm and shoulder. To rid himself of them he closed his eyes. For a moment he dozed, but in that short interval innumerable things appeared to him in a dream: his mother and her large white hand, SГіnyaвҖҷs thin little shoulders, NatГЎshaвҖҷs eyes and laughter, DenГӯsov with his voice and mustache, and TelyГЎnin and all that affair with TelyГЎnin and BogdГЎnich. That affair was the same thing as this soldier with the harsh voice, and it was that affair and this soldier that were so agonizingly, incessantly pulling and pressing his arm and always dragging it in one direction. He tried to get away from them, but they would not for an instant let his shoulder move a hairвҖҷs breadth. It would not acheвҒ вҖ”it would be wellвҒ вҖ”if only they did not pull it, but it was impossible to get rid of them. He opened his eyes and looked up. The black canopy of night hung less than a yard above the glow of the charcoal. Flakes of falling snow were fluttering in that light. TГәshin had not returned, the doctor had not come. He was alone now, except for a soldier who was sitting naked at the other side of the fire, warming his thin yellow body. вҖңNobody wants me!вҖқ thought RostГіv. вҖңThere is no one to help me or pity me. Yet I was once at home, strong, happy, and loved.вҖқ He sighed and, doing so, groaned involuntarily. вҖңEh, is anything hurting you?вҖқ asked the soldier, shaking his shirt out over the fire, and not waiting for an answer he gave a grunt and added: вҖңWhat a lot of men have been crippled todayвҒ вҖ”frightful!вҖқ RostГіv did not listen to the soldier. He looked at the snowflakes fluttering above the fire and remembered a Russian winter at his warm, bright home, his fluffy fur coat, his quickly gliding sleigh, his healthy body, and all the affection and care of his family. вҖңAnd why did I come here?вҖқ he wondered. Next day the French army did not renew their attack, and the remnant of BagratiГіnвҖҷs detachment was reunited to KutГәzovвҖҷs army. III III On returning from the review, KutГәzov took the Austrian general into his private room and, calling his adjutant, asked for some papers relating to the condition of the troops on their arrival, and the letters that had come from the Archduke Ferdinand, who was in command of the advanced army. Prince AndrГ©y BolkГіnski came into the room with the required papers. KutГәzov and the Austrian member of the Hofkriegsrath were sitting at the table on which a plan was spread out. вҖңAh!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said KutГәzov glancing at BolkГіnski as if by this exclamation he was asking the adjutant to wait, and he went on with the conversation in French. вҖңAll I can say, General,вҖқ said he with a pleasant elegance of expression and intonation that obliged one to listen to each deliberately spoken word. It was evident that KutГәzov himself listened with pleasure to his own voice. вҖңAll I can say, General, is that if the matter depended on my personal wishes, the will of His Majesty the Emperor Francis would have been fulfilled long ago. I should long ago have joined the archduke. And believe me on my honour that to me personally it would be a pleasure to hand over the supreme command of the army into the hands of a better informed and more skillful generalвҒ вҖ”of whom Austria has so manyвҒ вҖ”and to lay down all this heavy responsibility. But circumstances are sometimes too strong for us, General.вҖқ And KutГәzov smiled in a way that seemed to say, вҖңYou are quite at liberty not to believe me and I donвҖҷt even care whether you do or not, but you have no grounds for telling me so. And that is the whole point.вҖқ The Austrian general looked dissatisfied, but had no option but to reply in the same tone. вҖңOn the contrary,вҖқ he said, in a querulous and angry tone that contrasted with his flattering words, вҖңon the contrary, your excellencyвҖҷs participation in the common action is highly valued by His Majesty; but we think the present delay is depriving the splendid Russian troops and their commander of the laurels they have been accustomed to win in their battles,вҖқ he concluded his evidently prearranged sentence. KutГәzov bowed with the same smile. вҖңBut that is my conviction, and judging by the last letter with which His Highness the Archduke Ferdinand has honored me, I imagine that the Austrian troops, under the direction of so skillful a leader as General Mack, have by now already gained a decisive victory and no longer need our aid,вҖқ said KutГәzov. The general frowned. Though there was no definite news of an Austrian defeat, there were many circumstances confirming the unfavorable rumors that were afloat, and so KutГәzovвҖҷs suggestion of an Austrian victory sounded much like irony. But KutГәzov went on blandly smiling with the same expression, which seemed to say that he had a right to suppose so. And, in fact, the last letter he had received from MackвҖҷs army informed him of a victory and stated strategically the position of the army was very favorable. вҖңGive me that letter,вҖқ said KutГәzov turning to Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңPlease have a look at itвҖқвҒ вҖ”and KutГәzov with an ironical smile about the corners of his mouth read to the Austrian general the following passage, in German, from the Archduke FerdinandвҖҷs letter: We have fully concentrated forces of nearly seventy thousand men with which to attack and defeat the enemy should he cross the Lech. Also, as we are masters of Ulm, we cannot be deprived of the advantage of commanding both sides of the Danube, so that should the enemy not cross the Lech, we can cross the Danube, throw ourselves on his line of communications, recross the river lower down, and frustrate his intention should he try to direct his whole force against our faithful ally. We shall therefore confidently await the moment when the Imperial Russian army will be fully equipped, and shall then, in conjunction with it, easily find a way to prepare for the enemy the fate he deserves. KutГәzov sighed deeply on finishing this paragraph and looked at the member of the Hofkriegsrath mildly and attentively. вҖңBut you know the wise maxim your excellency, advising one to expect the worst,вҖқ said the Austrian general, evidently wishing to have done with jests and to come to business. He involuntarily looked round at the aide-de-camp. вҖңExcuse me, General,вҖқ interrupted KutГәzov, also turning to Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңLook here, my dear fellow, get from KozlГіvski all the reports from our scouts. Here are two letters from Count Nostitz and here is one from His Highness the Archduke Ferdinand and here are these,вҖқ he said, handing him several papers, вҖңmake a neat memorandum in French out of all this, showing all the news we have had of the movements of the Austrian army, and then give it to his excellency.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y bowed his head in token of having understood from the first not only what had been said but also what KutГәzov would have liked to tell him. He gathered up the papers and with a bow to both, stepped softly over the carpet and went out into the waiting room. Though not much time had passed since Prince AndrГ©y had left Russia, he had changed greatly during that period. In the expression of his face, in his movements, in his walk, scarcely a trace was left of his former affected languor and indolence. He now looked like a man who has time to think of the impression he makes on others, but is occupied with agreeable and interesting work. His face expressed more satisfaction with himself and those around him, his smile and glance were brighter and more attractive. KutГәzov, whom he had overtaken in Poland, had received him very kindly, promised not to forget him, distinguished him above the other adjutants, and had taken him to Vienna and given him the more serious commissions. From Vienna KutГәzov wrote to his old comrade, Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs father. Your son bids fair to become an officer distinguished by his industry, firmness, and expedition. I consider myself fortunate to have such a subordinate by me. On KutГәzovвҖҷs staff, among his fellow officers and in the army generally, Prince AndrГ©y had, as he had had in Petersburg society, two quite opposite reputations. Some, a minority, acknowledged him to be different from themselves and from everyone else, expected great things of him, listened to him, admired, and imitated him, and with them Prince AndrГ©y was natural and pleasant. Others, the majority, disliked him and considered him conceited, cold, and disagreeable. But among these people Prince AndrГ©y knew how to take his stand so that they respected and even feared him. Coming out of KutГәzovвҖҷs room into the waiting room with the papers in his hand Prince AndrГ©y came up to his comrade, the aide-de-camp on duty, KozlГіvski, who was sitting at the window with a book. вҖңWell, Prince?вҖқ asked KozlГіvski. вҖңI am ordered to write a memorandum explaining why we are not advancing.вҖқ вҖңAnd why is it?вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y shrugged his shoulders. вҖңAny news from Mack?вҖқ вҖңNo.вҖқ вҖңIf it were true that he has been beaten, news would have come.вҖқ вҖңProbably,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y moving toward the outer door. But at that instant a tall Austrian general in a greatcoat, with the order of Maria Theresa on his neck and a black bandage round his head, who had evidently just arrived, entered quickly, slamming the door. Prince AndrГ©y stopped short. вҖңCommander in Chief KutГәzov?вҖқ said the newly arrived general speaking quickly with a harsh German accent, looking to both sides and advancing straight toward the inner door. вҖңThe commander in chief is engaged,вҖқ said KozlГіvski, going hurriedly up to the unknown general and blocking his way to the door. вҖңWhom shall I announce?вҖқ The unknown general looked disdainfully down at KozlГіvski, who was rather short, as if surprised that anyone should not know him. вҖңThe commander in chief is engaged,вҖқ repeated KozlГіvski calmly. The generalвҖҷs face clouded, his lips quivered and trembled. He took out a notebook, hurriedly scribbled something in pencil, tore out the leaf, gave it to KozlГіvski, stepped quickly to the window, and threw himself into a chair, gazing at those in the room as if asking, вҖңWhy do they look at me?вҖқ Then he lifted his head, stretched his neck as if he intended to say something, but immediately, with affected indifference, began to hum to himself, producing a queer sound which immediately broke off. The door of the private room opened and KutГәzov appeared in the doorway. The general with the bandaged head bent forward as though running away from some danger, and, making long, quick strides with his thin legs, went up to KutГәzov. вҖңVous voyez le malheureux Mack,вҖқ he uttered in a broken voice. KutГәzovвҖҷs face as he stood in the open doorway remained perfectly immobile for a few moments. Then wrinkles ran over his face like a wave and his forehead became smooth again, he bowed his head respectfully, closed his eyes, silently let Mack enter his room before him, and closed the door himself behind him. The report which had been circulated that the Austrians had been beaten and that the whole army had surrendered at Ulm proved to be correct. Within half an hour adjutants had been sent in various directions with orders which showed that the Russian troops, who had hitherto been inactive, would also soon have to meet the enemy. Prince AndrГ©y was one of those rare staff officers whose chief interest lay in the general progress of the war. When he saw Mack and heard the details of his disaster he understood that half the campaign was lost, understood all the difficulties of the Russian armyвҖҷs position, and vividly imagined what awaited it and the part he would have to play. Involuntarily he felt a joyful agitation at the thought of the humiliation of arrogant Austria and that in a weekвҖҷs time he might, perhaps, see and take part in the first Russian encounter with the French since SuvГіrov met them. He feared that BonaparteвҖҷs genius might outweigh all the courage of the Russian troops, and at the same time could not admit the idea of his hero being disgraced. Excited and irritated by these thoughts Prince AndrГ©y went toward his room to write to his father, to whom he wrote every day. In the corridor he met NesvГӯtski, with whom he shared a room, and the wag ZherkГіv; they were as usual laughing. вҖңWhy are you so glum?вҖқ asked NesvГӯtski noticing Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs pale face and glittering eyes. вҖңThereвҖҷs nothing to be gay about,вҖқ answered BolkГіnski. Just as Prince AndrГ©y met NesvГӯtski and ZherkГіv, there came toward them from the other end of the corridor, Strauch, an Austrian general who was on KutГәzovвҖҷs staff in charge of the provisioning of the Russian army, and the member of the Hofkriegsrath who had arrived the previous evening. There was room enough in the wide corridor for the generals to pass the three officers quite easily, but ZherkГіv, pushing NesvГӯtski aside with his arm, said in a breathless voice, вҖңTheyвҖҷre coming!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ theyвҖҷre coming!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Stand aside, make way, please make way!вҖқ The generals were passing by, looking as if they wished to avoid embarrassing attentions. On the face of the wag ZherkГіv there suddenly appeared a stupid smile of glee which he seemed unable to suppress. вҖңYour excellency,вҖқ said he in German, stepping forward and addressing the Austrian general, вҖңI have the honor to congratulate you.вҖқ He bowed his head and scraped first with one foot and then with the other, awkwardly, like a child at a dancing lesson. The member of the Hofkriegsrath looked at him severely but, seeing the seriousness of his stupid smile, could not but give him a momentвҖҷs attention. He screwed up his eyes showing that he was listening. вҖңI have the honor to congratulate you. General Mack has arrived, quite well, only a little bruised just here,вҖқ he added, pointing with a beaming smile to his head. The general frowned, turned away, and went on. вҖңGott, wie naiv!вҖқ18 said he angrily, after he had gone a few steps. NesvГӯtski with a laugh threw his arms round Prince AndrГ©y, but BolkГіnski, turning still paler, pushed him away with an angry look and turned to ZherkГіv. The nervous irritation aroused by the appearance of Mack, the news of his defeat, and the thought of what lay before the Russian army found vent in anger at ZherkГіvвҖҷs untimely jest. вҖңIf you, sir, choose to make a buffoon of yourself,вҖқ he said sharply, with a slight trembling of the lower jaw, вҖңI canвҖҷt prevent your doing so; but I warn you that if you dare to play the fool in my presence, I will teach you to behave yourself.вҖқ NesvГӯtski and ZherkГіv were so surprised by this outburst that they gazed at BolkГіnski silently with wide-open eyes. вҖңWhatвҖҷs the matter? I only congratulated them,вҖқ said ZherkГіv. вҖңI am not jesting with you; please be silent!вҖқ cried BolkГіnski, and taking NesvГӯtskiвҖҷs arm he left ZherkГіv, who did not know what to say. вҖңCome, whatвҖҷs the matter, old fellow?вҖқ said NesvГӯtski trying to soothe him. вҖңWhatвҖҷs the matter?вҖқ exclaimed Prince AndrГ©y standing still in his excitement. вҖңDonвҖҷt you understand that either we are officers serving our Tsar and our country, rejoicing in the successes and grieving at the misfortunes of our common cause, or we are merely lackeys who care nothing for their masterвҖҷs business. Quarante mille hommes massacrГ©s et lвҖҷarmГ©e de nos alliГ©s dГ©truite, et vous trouvez lГ  le mot pour rire,вҖқ19 he said, as if strengthening his views by this French sentence. вҖңCвҖҷest bien pour un garГ§on de rien comme cet individu dont vous avez fait un ami, mais pas pour vous, pas pour vous.20 Only a hobbledehoy could amuse himself in this way,вҖқ he added in RussianвҒ вҖ”but pronouncing the word with a French accentвҒ вҖ”having noticed that ZherkГіv could still hear him. He waited a moment to see whether the cornet would answer, but he turned and went out of the corridor. IV IV The PГЎvlograd Hussars were stationed two miles from Braunau. The squadron in which NikolГЎy RostГіv served as a cadet was quartered in the German village of Salzeneck. The best quarters in the village were assigned to cavalry-captain DenГӯsov, the squadron commander, known throughout the whole cavalry division as VГЎska DenГӯsov. Cadet RostГіv, ever since he had overtaken the regiment in Poland, had lived with the squadron commander. On October 11, the day when all was astir at headquarters over the news of MackвҖҷs defeat, the camp life of the officers of this squadron was proceeding as usual. DenГӯsov, who had been losing at cards all night, had not yet come home when RostГіv rode back early in the morning from a foraging expedition. RostГіv in his cadet uniform, with a jerk to his horse, rode up to the porch, swung his leg over the saddle with a supple youthful movement, stood for a moment in the stirrup as if loathe to part from his horse, and at last sprang down and called to his orderly. вҖңAh, BondarГ©nko, dear friend!вҖқ said he to the hussar who rushed up headlong to the horse. вҖңWalk him up and down, my dear fellow,вҖқ he continued, with that gay brotherly cordiality which goodhearted young people show to everyone when they are happy. вҖңYes, your excellency,вҖқ answered the Ukrainian gaily, tossing his head. вҖңMind, walk him up and down well!вҖқ Another hussar also rushed toward the horse, but BondarГ©nko had already thrown the reins of the snaffle bridle over the horseвҖҷs head. It was evident that the cadet was liberal with his tips and that it paid to serve him. RostГіv patted the horseвҖҷs neck and then his flank, and lingered for a moment. вҖңSplendid! What a horse he will be!вҖқ he thought with a smile, and holding up his saber, his spurs jingling, he ran up the steps of the porch. His landlord, who in a waistcoat and a pointed cap, pitchfork in hand, was clearing manure from the cowhouse, looked out, and his face immediately brightened on seeing RostГіv. вҖңSchГ¶n gut Morgen! SchГ¶n gut Morgen!вҖқ21 he said winking with a merry smile, evidently pleased to greet the young man. вҖңSchon fleissig?вҖқ22 said RostГіv with the same gay brotherly smile which did not leave his eager face. вҖңHoch Oestreicher! Hoch Russen! Kaiser Alexander hoch!вҖқ23 said he, quoting words often repeated by the German landlord. The German laughed, came out of the cowshed, pulled off his cap, and waving it above his head cried: вҖңUnd die ganze Welt hoch!вҖқ24 RostГіv waved his cap above his head like the German and cried laughing, вҖңUnd vivat die ganze Welt!вҖқ Though neither the German cleaning his cowshed nor RostГіv back with his platoon from foraging for hay had any reason for rejoicing, they looked at each other with joyful delight and brotherly love, wagged their heads in token of their mutual affection, and parted smiling, the German returning to his cowshed and RostГіv going to the cottage he occupied with DenГӯsov. вҖңWhat about your master?вҖқ he asked LavrГәshka, DenГӯsovвҖҷs orderly, whom all the regiment knew for a rogue. вҖңHasnвҖҷt been in since the evening. Must have been losing,вҖқ answered LavrГәshka. вҖңI know by now, if he wins he comes back early to brag about it, but if he stays out till morning it means heвҖҷs lost and will come back in a rage. Will you have coffee?вҖқ вҖңYes, bring some.вҖқ Ten minutes later LavrГәshka brought the coffee. вҖңHeвҖҷs coming!вҖқ said he. вҖңNow for trouble!вҖқ RostГіv looked out of the window and saw DenГӯsov coming home. DenГӯsov was a small man with a red face, sparkling black eyes, and black tousled mustache and hair. He wore an unfastened cloak, wide breeches hanging down in creases, and a crumpled shako on the back of his head. He came up to the porch gloomily, hanging his head. вҖңLavwГәska!вҖқ he shouted loudly and angrily, вҖңtake it off, blockhead!вҖқ вҖңWell, I am taking it off,вҖқ replied LavrГәshkaвҖҷs voice. вҖңAh, youвҖҷre up already,вҖқ said DenГӯsov, entering the room. вҖңLong ago,вҖқ answered RostГіv, вҖңI have already been for the hay, and have seen FrГӨulein Mathilde.вҖқ вҖңWeally! And IвҖҷve been losing, bwother. I lost yesterday like a damned fool!вҖқ cried DenГӯsov, not pronouncing his rвҖҷs. вҖңSuch ill luck! Such ill luck. As soon as you left, it began and went on. Hullo there! Tea!вҖқ Puckering up his face though smiling, and showing his short strong teeth, he began with stubby fingers of both hands to ruffle up his thick tangled black hair. вҖңAnd what devil made me go to that wat?вҖқ (an officer nicknamed вҖңthe ratвҖқ) he said, rubbing his forehead and whole face with both hands. вҖңJust fancy, he didnвҖҷt let me win a single cahd, not one cahd.вҖқ He took the lighted pipe that was offered to him, gripped it in his fist, and tapped it on the floor, making the sparks fly, while he continued to shout. вҖңHe lets one win the singles and collahs it as soon as one doubles it; gives the singles and snatches the doubles!вҖқ He scattered the burning tobacco, smashed the pipe, and threw it away. Then he remained silent for a while, and all at once looked cheerfully with his glittering, black eyes at RostГіv. вҖңIf at least we had some women here; but thereвҖҷs nothing foh one to do but dwink. If we could only get to fighting soon. Hullo, whoвҖҷs there?вҖқ he said, turning to the door as he heard a tread of heavy boots and the clinking of spurs that came to a stop, and a respectful cough. вҖңThe squadron quartermaster!вҖқ said LavrГәshka. DenГӯsovвҖҷs face puckered still more. вҖңWetched!вҖқ he muttered, throwing down a purse with some gold in it. вҖңWostГіv, deah fellow, just see how much there is left and shove the purse undah the pillow,вҖқ he said, and went out to the quartermaster. RostГіv took the money and, mechanically arranging the old and new coins in separate piles, began counting them. вҖңAh! TelyГЎnin! How dвҖҷye do? They plucked me last night,вҖқ came DenГӯsovвҖҷs voice from the next room. вҖңWhere? At BykovвҖҷs, at the ratвҖҷsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I knew it,вҖқ replied a piping voice, and Lieutenant TelyГЎnin, a small officer of the same squadron, entered the room. RostГіv thrust the purse under the pillow and shook the damp little hand which was offered him. TelyГЎnin for some reason had been transferred from the Guards just before this campaign. He behaved very well in the regiment but was not liked; RostГіv especially detested him and was unable to overcome or conceal his groundless antipathy to the man. вҖңWell, young cavalryman, how is my Rook behaving?вҖқ he asked. (Rook was a young horse TelyГЎnin had sold to RostГіv.) The lieutenant never looked the man he was speaking to straight in the face; his eyes continually wandered from one object to another. вҖңI saw you riding this morningвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he added. вҖңOh, heвҖҷs all right, a good horse,вҖқ answered RostГіv, though the horse for which he had paid seven hundred rubbles was not worth half that sum. вҖңHeвҖҷs begun to go a little lame on the left foreleg,вҖқ he added. вҖңThe hoofвҖҷs cracked! ThatвҖҷs nothing. IвҖҷll teach you what to do and show you what kind of rivet to use.вҖқ вҖңYes, please do,вҖқ said RostГіv. вҖңIвҖҷll show you, IвҖҷll show you! ItвҖҷs not a secret. And itвҖҷs a horse youвҖҷll thank me for.вҖқ вҖңThen IвҖҷll have it brought round,вҖқ said RostГіv wishing to avoid TelyГЎnin, and he went out to give the order. In the passage DenГӯsov, with a pipe, was squatting on the threshold facing the quartermaster who was reporting to him. On seeing RostГіv, DenГӯsov screwed up his face and pointing over his shoulder with his thumb to the room where TelyГЎnin was sitting, he frowned and gave a shudder of disgust. вҖңUgh! I donвҖҷt like that fellow,вҖқ he said, regardless of the quartermasterвҖҷs presence. RostГіv shrugged his shoulders as much as to say: вҖңNor do I, but whatвҖҷs one to do?вҖқ and, having given his order, he returned to TelyГЎnin. TelyГЎnin was sitting in the same indolent pose in which RostГіv had left him, rubbing his small white hands. вҖңWell there certainly are disgusting people,вҖқ thought RostГіv as he entered. вҖңHave you told them to bring the horse?вҖқ asked TelyГЎnin, getting up and looking carelessly about him. вҖңI have.вҖқ вҖңLet us go ourselves. I only came round to ask DenГӯsov about yesterdayвҖҷs order. Have you got it, DenГӯsov?вҖқ вҖңNot yet. But where are you off to?вҖқ вҖңI want to teach this young man how to shoe a horse,вҖқ said TelyГЎnin. They went through the porch and into the stable. The lieutenant explained how to rivet the hoof and went away to his own quarters. When RostГіv went back there was a bottle of vodka and a sausage on the table. DenГӯsov was sitting there scratching with his pen on a sheet of paper. He looked gloomily in RostГіvвҖҷs face and said: вҖңI am witing to her.вҖқ He leaned his elbows on the table with his pen in his hand and, evidently glad of a chance to say quicker in words what he wanted to write, told RostГіv the contents of his letter. вҖңYou see, my fwiend,вҖқ he said, вҖңwe sleep when we donвҖҷt love. We are childwen of the dustвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but one falls in love and one is a God, one is puaвҖҷ as on the fihst day of cweationвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ WhoвҖҷs that now? Send him to the devil, IвҖҷm busy!вҖқ he shouted to LavrГәshka, who went up to him not in the least abashed. вҖңWho should it be? You yourself told him to come. ItвҖҷs the quartermaster for the money.вҖқ DenГӯsov frowned and was about to shout some reply but stopped. вҖңWetched business,вҖқ he muttered to himself. вҖңHow much is left in the puhse?вҖқ he asked, turning to RostГіv. вҖңSeven new and three old imperials.вҖқ вҖңOh, itвҖҷs wetched! Well, what are you standing there for, you scaвҖҷcwow? Call the quahtehmasteh,вҖқ he shouted to LavrГәshka. вҖңPlease, DenГӯsov, let me lend you some: I have some, you know,вҖқ said RostГіv, blushing. вҖңDonвҖҷt like bowwowing from my own fellows, I donвҖҷt,вҖқ growled DenГӯsov. вҖңBut if you wonвҖҷt accept money from me like a comrade, you will offend me. Really I have some,вҖқ RostГіv repeated. вҖңNo, I tell you.вҖқ And DenГӯsov went to the bed to get the purse from under the pillow. вҖңWhere have you put it, WostГіv?вҖқ вҖңUnder the lower pillow.вҖқ вҖңItвҖҷs not there.вҖқ DenГӯsov threw both pillows on the floor. The purse was not there. вҖңThatвҖҷs a miwacle.вҖқ вҖңWait, havenвҖҷt you dropped it?вҖқ said RostГіv, picking up the pillows one at a time and shaking them. He pulled off the quilt and shook it. The purse was not there. вҖңDear me, can I have forgotten? No, I remember thinking that you kept it under your head like a treasure,вҖқ said RostГіv. вҖңI put it just here. Where is it?вҖқ he asked, turning to LavrГәshka. вҖңI havenвҖҷt been in the room. It must be where you put it.вҖқ вҖңBut it isnвҖҷt?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYouвҖҷre always like that; you thwow a thing down anywhere and forget it. Feel in your pockets.вҖқ вҖңNo, if I hadnвҖҷt thought of it being a treasure,вҖқ said RostГіv, вҖңbut I remember putting it there.вҖқ LavrГәshka turned all the bedding over, looked under the bed and under the table, searched everywhere, and stood still in the middle of the room. DenГӯsov silently watched LavrГәshkaвҖҷs movements, and when the latter threw up his arms in surprise saying it was nowhere to be found DenГӯsov glanced at RostГіv. вҖңWostГіv, youвҖҷve not been playing schoolboy twicksвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ RostГіv felt DenГӯsovвҖҷs gaze fixed on him, raised his eyes, and instantly dropped them again. All the blood which had seemed congested somewhere below his throat rushed to his face and eyes. He could not draw breath. вҖңAnd there hasnвҖҷt been anyone in the room except the lieutenant and yourselves. It must be here somewhere,вҖқ said LavrГәshka. вҖңNow then, you devilвҖҷs puppet, look alive and hunt for it!вҖқ shouted DenГӯsov, suddenly, turning purple and rushing at the man with a threatening gesture. вҖңIf the purse isnвҖҷt found IвҖҷll flog you, IвҖҷll flog you all.вҖқ RostГіv, his eyes avoiding DenГӯsov, began buttoning his coat, buckled on his saber, and put on his cap. вҖңI must have that purse, I tell you,вҖқ shouted DenГӯsov, shaking his orderly by the shoulders and knocking him against the wall. вҖңDenГӯsov, let him alone, I know who has taken it,вҖқ said RostГіv, going toward the door without raising his eyes. DenГӯsov paused, thought a moment, and, evidently understanding what RostГіv hinted at, seized his arm. вҖңNonsense!вҖқ he cried, and the veins on his forehead and neck stood out like cords. вҖңYou are mad, I tell you. I wonвҖҷt allow it. The purse is here! IвҖҷll flay this scoundwel alive, and it will be found.вҖқ вҖңI know who has taken it,вҖқ repeated RostГіv in an unsteady voice, and went to the door. вҖңAnd I tell you, donвҖҷt you dahe to do it!вҖқ shouted DenГӯsov, rushing at the cadet to restrain him. But RostГіv pulled away his arm and, with as much anger as though DenГӯsov were his worst enemy, firmly fixed his eyes directly on his face. вҖңDo you understand what youвҖҷre saying?вҖқ he said in a trembling voice. вҖңThere was no one else in the room except myself. So that if it is not so, thenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He could not finish, and ran out of the room. вҖңAh, may the devil take you and evewybody,вҖқ were the last words RostГіv heard. RostГіv went to TelyГЎninвҖҷs quarters. вҖңThe master is not in, heвҖҷs gone to headquarters,вҖқ said TelyГЎninвҖҷs orderly. вҖңHas something happened?вҖқ he added, surprised at the cadetвҖҷs troubled face. вҖңNo, nothing.вҖқ вҖңYouвҖҷve only just missed him,вҖқ said the orderly. The headquarters were situated two miles away from Salzeneck, and RostГіv, without returning home, took a horse and rode there. There was an inn in the village which the officers frequented. RostГіv rode up to it and saw TelyГЎninвҖҷs horse at the porch. In the second room of the inn the lieutenant was sitting over a dish of sausages and a bottle of wine. вҖңAh, youвҖҷve come here too, young man!вҖқ he said, smiling and raising his eyebrows. вҖңYes,вҖқ said RostГіv as if it cost him a great deal to utter the word; and he sat down at the nearest table. Both were silent. There were two Germans and a Russian officer in the room. No one spoke and the only sounds heard were the clatter of knives and the munching of the lieutenant. When TelyГЎnin had finished his lunch he took out of his pocket a double purse and, drawing its rings aside with his small, white, turned-up fingers, drew out a gold imperial, and lifting his eyebrows gave it to the waiter. вҖңPlease be quick,вҖқ he said. The coin was a new one. RostГіv rose and went up to TelyГЎnin. вҖңAllow me to look at your purse,вҖқ he said in a low, almost inaudible, voice. With shifting eyes but eyebrows still raised, TelyГЎnin handed him the purse. вҖңYes, itвҖҷs a nice purse. Yes, yes,вҖқ he said, growing suddenly pale, and added, вҖңLook at it, young man.вҖқ RostГіv took the purse in his hand, examined it and the money in it, and looked at TelyГЎnin. The lieutenant was looking about in his usual way and suddenly seemed to grow very merry. вҖңIf we get to Vienna IвҖҷll get rid of it there but in these wretched little towns thereвҖҷs nowhere to spend it,вҖқ said he. вҖңWell, let me have it, young man, IвҖҷm going.вҖқ RostГіv did not speak. вҖңAnd you? Are you going to have lunch too? They feed you quite decently here,вҖқ continued TelyГЎnin. вҖңNow then, let me have it.вҖқ He stretched out his hand to take hold of the purse. RostГіv let go of it. TelyГЎnin took the purse and began carelessly slipping it into the pocket of his riding breeches, with his eyebrows lifted and his mouth slightly open, as if to say, вҖңYes, yes, I am putting my purse in my pocket and thatвҖҷs quite simple and is no one elseвҖҷs business.вҖқ вҖңWell, young man?вҖқ he said with a sigh, and from under his lifted brows he glanced into RostГіvвҖҷs eyes. Some flash as of an electric spark shot from TelyГЎninвҖҷs eyes to RostГіvвҖҷs and back, and back again and again in an instant. вҖңCome here,вҖқ said RostГіv, catching hold of TelyГЎninвҖҷs arm and almost dragging him to the window. вҖңThat money is DenГӯsovвҖҷs; you took itвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he whispered just above TelyГЎninвҖҷs ear. вҖңWhat? What? How dare you? What?вҖқ said TelyГЎnin. But these words came like a piteous, despairing cry and an entreaty for pardon. As soon as RostГіv heard them, an enormous load of doubt fell from him. He was glad, and at the same instant began to pity the miserable man who stood before him, but the task he had begun had to be completed. вҖңHeaven only knows what the people here may imagine,вҖқ muttered TelyГЎnin, taking up his cap and moving toward a small empty room. вҖңWe must have an explanationвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI know it and shall prove it,вҖқ said RostГіv. вҖңIвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Every muscle of TelyГЎninвҖҷs pale, terrified face began to quiver, his eyes still shifted from side to side but with a downward look not rising to RostГіvвҖҷs face, and his sobs were audible. вҖңCount!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ DonвҖҷt ruin a young fellowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ here is this wretched money, take itвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He threw it on the table. вҖңI have an old father and mother!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ RostГіv took the money, avoiding TelyГЎninвҖҷs eyes, and went out of the room without a word. But at the door he stopped and then retraced his steps. вҖңO God,вҖқ he said with tears in his eyes, вҖңhow could you do it?вҖқ вҖңCountвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said TelyГЎnin drawing nearer to him. вҖңDonвҖҷt touch me,вҖқ said RostГіv, drawing back. вҖңIf you need it, take the money,вҖқ and he threw the purse to him and ran out of the inn. V V That same evening there was an animated discussion among the squadronвҖҷs officers in DenГӯsovвҖҷs quarters. вҖңAnd I tell you, RostГіv, that you must apologize to the colonel!вҖқ said a tall, grizzly-haired staff captain, with enormous mustaches and many wrinkles on his large features, to RostГіv who was crimson with excitement. The staff captain, KГӯrsten, had twice been reduced to the ranks for affairs of honor and had twice regained his commission. вҖңI will allow no one to call me a liar!вҖқ cried RostГіv. вҖңHe told me I lied, and I told him he lied. And there it rests. He may keep me on duty every day, or may place me under arrest, but no one can make me apologize, because if he, as commander of this regiment, thinks it beneath his dignity to give me satisfaction, thenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYou just wait a moment, my dear fellow, and listen,вҖқ interrupted the staff captain in his deep bass, calmly stroking his long mustache. вҖңYou tell the colonel in the presence of other officers that an officer has stolenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңIвҖҷm not to blame that the conversation began in the presence of other officers. Perhaps I ought not to have spoken before them, but I am not a diplomatist. ThatвҖҷs why I joined the hussars, thinking that here one would not need finesse; and he tells me that I am lyingвҒ вҖ”so let him give me satisfactionвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңThatвҖҷs all right. No one thinks you a coward, but thatвҖҷs not the point. Ask DenГӯsov whether it is not out of the question for a cadet to demand satisfaction of his regimental commander?вҖқ DenГӯsov sat gloomily biting his mustache and listening to the conversation, evidently with no wish to take part in it. He answered the staff captainвҖҷs question by a disapproving shake of his head. вҖңYou speak to the colonel about this nasty business before other officers,вҖқ continued the staff captain, вҖңand BogdГЎnichвҖқ (the colonel was called BogdГЎnich) вҖңshuts you up.вҖқ вҖңHe did not shut me up, he said I was telling an untruth.вҖқ вҖңWell, have it so, and you talked a lot of nonsense to him and must apologize.вҖқ вҖңNot on any account!вҖқ exclaimed RostГіv. вҖңI did not expect this of you,вҖқ said the staff captain seriously and severely. вҖңYou donвҖҷt wish to apologize, but, man, itвҖҷs not only to him but to the whole regimentвҒ вҖ”all of usвҒ вҖ”youвҖҷre to blame all round. The case is this: you ought to have thought the matter over and taken advice; but no, you go and blurt it all straight out before the officers. Now what was the colonel to do? Have the officer tried and disgrace the whole regiment? Disgrace the whole regiment because of one scoundrel? Is that how you look at it? We donвҖҷt see it like that. And BogdГЎnich was a brick: he told you you were saying what was not true. ItвҖҷs not pleasant, but whatвҖҷs to be done, my dear fellow? You landed yourself in it. And now, when one wants to smooth the thing over, some conceit prevents your apologizing, and you wish to make the whole affair public. You are offended at being put on duty a bit, but why not apologize to an old and honorable officer? Whatever BogdГЎnich may be, anyway he is an honorable and brave old colonel! YouвҖҷre quick at taking offense, but you donвҖҷt mind disgracing the whole regiment!вҖқ The staff captainвҖҷs voice began to tremble. вҖңYou have been in the regiment next to no time, my lad, youвҖҷre here today and tomorrow youвҖҷll be appointed adjutant somewhere and can snap your fingers when it is said вҖҳThere are thieves among the PГЎvlograd officers!вҖҷ But itвҖҷs not all the same to us! Am I not right, DenГӯsov? ItвҖҷs not the same!вҖқ DenГӯsov remained silent and did not move, but occasionally looked with his glittering black eyes at RostГіv. вҖңYou value your own pride and donвҖҷt wish to apologize,вҖқ continued the staff captain, вҖңbut we old fellows, who have grown up in and, God willing, are going to die in the regiment, we prize the honor of the regiment, and BogdГЎnich knows it. Oh, we do prize it, old fellow! And all this is not right, itвҖҷs not right! You may take offense or not but I always stick to mother truth. ItвҖҷs not right!вҖқ And the staff captain rose and turned away from RostГіv. вҖңThatвҖҷs twue, devil take it!вҖқ shouted DenГӯsov, jumping up. вҖңNow then, WostГіv, now then!вҖқ RostГіv, growing red and pale alternately, looked first at one officer and then at the other. вҖңNo, gentlemen, noвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ you mustnвҖҷt thinkвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I quite understand. YouвҖҷre wrong to think that of meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ for meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ for the honor of the regiment IвҖҷdвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Ah well, IвҖҷll show that in action, and for me the honor of the flagвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, never mind, itвҖҷs true IвҖҷm to blame, to blame all round. Well, what else do you want?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңCome, thatвҖҷs right, Count!вҖқ cried the staff captain, turning round and clapping RostГіv on the shoulder with his big hand. вҖңI tell you,вҖқ shouted DenГӯsov, вҖңheвҖҷs a fine fellow.вҖқ вҖңThatвҖҷs better, Count,вҖқ said the staff captain, beginning to address RostГіv by his title, as if in recognition of his confession. вҖңGo and apologize, your excellency. Yes, go!вҖқ вҖңGentlemen, IвҖҷll do anything. No one shall hear a word from me,вҖқ said RostГіv in an imploring voice, вҖңbut I canвҖҷt apologize, by God I canвҖҷt, do what you will! How can I go and apologize like a little boy asking forgiveness?вҖқ DenГӯsov began to laugh. вҖңItвҖҷll be worse for you. BogdГЎnich is vindictive and youвҖҷll pay for your obstinacy,вҖқ said KГӯrsten. вҖңNo, on my word itвҖҷs not obstinacy! I canвҖҷt describe the feeling. I canвҖҷtвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWell, itвҖҷs as you like,вҖқ said the staff captain. вҖңAnd what has become of that scoundrel?вҖқ he asked DenГӯsov. вҖңHe has weported himself sick, heвҖҷs to be stwuck off the list tomowwow,вҖқ muttered DenГӯsov. вҖңIt is an illness, thereвҖҷs no other way of explaining it,вҖқ said the staff captain. вҖңIllness or not, heвҖҷd better not cwoss my path. IвҖҷd kill him!вҖқ shouted DenГӯsov in a bloodthirsty tone. Just then ZherkГіv entered the room. вҖңWhat brings you here?вҖқ cried the officers turning to the newcomer. вҖңWeвҖҷre to go into action, gentlemen! Mack has surrendered with his whole army.вҖқ вҖңItвҖҷs not true!вҖқ вҖңIвҖҷve seen him myself!вҖқ вҖңWhat? Saw the real Mack? With hands and feet?вҖқ вҖңInto action! Into action! Bring him a bottle for such news! But how did you come here?вҖқ вҖңIвҖҷve been sent back to the regiment all on account of that devil, Mack. An Austrian general complained of me. I congratulated him on MackвҖҷs arrivalвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ WhatвҖҷs the matter, RostГіv? You look as if youвҖҷd just come out of a hot bath.вҖқ вҖңOh, my dear fellow, weвҖҷre in such a stew here these last two days.вҖқ The regimental adjutant came in and confirmed the news brought by ZherkГіv. They were under orders to advance next day. вҖңWeвҖҷre going into action, gentlemen!вҖқ вҖңWell, thank God! WeвҖҷve been sitting here too long!вҖқ VI VI KutГәzov fell back toward Vienna, destroying behind him the bridges over the rivers Inn (at Braunau) and Traun (near Linz). On October 23 the Russian troops were crossing the river Enns. At midday the Russian baggage train, the artillery, and columns of troops were defiling through the town of Enns on both sides of the bridge. It was a warm, rainy, autumnal day. The wide expanse that opened out before the heights on which the Russian batteries stood guarding the bridge was at times veiled by a diaphanous curtain of slanting rain, and then, suddenly spread out in the sunlight, far-distant objects could be clearly seen glittering as though freshly varnished. Down below, the little town could be seen with its white, red-roofed houses, its cathedral, and its bridge, on both sides of which streamed jostling masses of Russian troops. At the bend of the Danube, vessels, an island, and a castle with a park surrounded by the waters of the confluence of the Enns and the Danube became visible, and the rocky left bank of the Danube covered with pine forests, with a mystic background of green treetops and bluish gorges. The turrets of a convent stood out beyond a wild virgin pine forest, and far away on the other side of the Enns the enemyвҖҷs horse patrols could be discerned. Among the field guns on the brow of the hill the general in command of the rearguard stood with a staff officer, scanning the country through his fieldglass. A little behind them NesvГӯtski, who had been sent to the rearguard by the commander in chief, was sitting on the trail of a gun carriage. A Cossack who accompanied him had handed him a knapsack and a flask, and NesvГӯtski was treating some officers to pies and real doppelkГјmmel. The officers gladly gathered round him, some on their knees, some squatting Turkish fashion on the wet grass. вҖңYes, the Austrian prince who built that castle was no fool. ItвҖҷs a fine place! Why are you not eating anything, gentlemen?вҖқ NesvГӯtski was saying. вҖңThank you very much, Prince,вҖқ answered one of the officers, pleased to be talking to a staff officer of such importance. вҖңItвҖҷs a lovely place! We passed close to the park and saw two deerвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and what a splendid house!вҖқ вҖңLook, Prince,вҖқ said another, who would have dearly liked to take another pie but felt shy, and therefore pretended to be examining the countrysideвҒ вҖ”вҖңSee, our infantrymen have already got there. Look there in the meadow behind the village, three of them are dragging something. TheyвҖҷll ransack that castle,вҖқ he remarked with evident approval. вҖңSo they will,вҖқ said NesvГӯtski. вҖңNo, but what I should like,вҖқ added he, munching a pie in his moist-lipped handsome mouth, вҖңwould be to slip in over there.вҖқ He pointed with a smile to a turreted nunnery, and his eyes narrowed and gleamed. вҖңThat would be fine, gentlemen!вҖқ The officers laughed. вҖңJust to flutter the nuns a bit. They say there are Italian girls among them. On my word IвҖҷd give five years of my life for it!вҖқ вҖңThey must be feeling dull, too,вҖқ said one of the bolder officers, laughing. Meanwhile the staff officer standing in front pointed out something to the general, who looked through his field glass. вҖңYes, so it is, so it is,вҖқ said the general angrily, lowering the field glass and shrugging his shoulders, вҖңso it is! TheyвҖҷll be fired on at the crossing. And why are they dawdling there?вҖқ On the opposite side the enemy could be seen by the naked eye, and from their battery a milk-white cloud arose. Then came the distant report of a shot, and our troops could be seen hurrying to the crossing. NesvГӯtski rose, puffing, and went up to the general, smiling. вҖңWould not your excellency like a little refreshment?вҖқ he said. вҖңItвҖҷs a bad business,вҖқ said the general without answering him, вҖңour men have been wasting time.вҖқ вҖңHadnвҖҷt I better ride over, your excellency?вҖқ asked NesvГӯtski. вҖңYes, please do,вҖқ answered the general, and he repeated the order that had already once been given in detail: вҖңand tell the hussars that they are to cross last and to fire the bridge as I ordered; and the inflammable material on the bridge must be reinspected.вҖқ вҖңVery good,вҖқ answered NesvГӯtski. He called the Cossack with his horse, told him to put away the knapsack and flask, and swung his heavy person easily into the saddle. вҖңIвҖҷll really call in on the nuns,вҖқ he said to the officers who watched him smilingly, and he rode off by the winding path down the hill. вҖңNow then, letвҖҷs see how far it will carry, Captain. Just try!вҖқ said the general, turning to an artillery officer. вҖңHave a little fun to pass the time.вҖқ вҖңCrew, to your guns!вҖқ commanded the officer. In a moment the men came running gaily from their campfires and began loading. вҖңOne!вҖқ came the command. Number one jumped briskly aside. The gun rang out with a deafening metallic roar, and a whistling grenade flew above the heads of our troops below the hill and fell far short of the enemy, a little smoke showing the spot where it burst. The faces of officers and men brightened up at the sound. Everyone got up and began watching the movements of our troops below, as plainly visible as if but a stoneвҖҷs throw away, and the movements of the approaching enemy farther off. At the same instant the sun came fully out from behind the clouds, and the clear sound of the solitary shot and the brilliance of the bright sunshine merged in a single joyous and spirited impression. VII VII Two of the enemyвҖҷs shots had already flown across the bridge, where there was a crush. Halfway across stood Prince NesvГӯtski, who had alighted from his horse and whose big body was jammed against the railings. He looked back laughing to the Cossack who stood a few steps behind him holding two horses by their bridles. Each time Prince NesvГӯtski tried to move on, soldiers and carts pushed him back again and pressed him against the railings, and all he could do was to smile. вҖңWhat a fine fellow you are, friend!вҖқ said the Cossack to a convoy soldier with a wagon, who was pressing onto the infantrymen who were crowded together close to his wheels and his horses. вҖңWhat a fellow! You canвҖҷt wait a moment! DonвҖҷt you see the general wants to pass?вҖқ But the convoyman took no notice of the word вҖңgeneralвҖқ and shouted at the soldiers who were blocking his way. вҖңHi there, boys! Keep to the left! Wait a bit.вҖқ But the soldiers, crowded together shoulder to shoulder, their bayonets interlocking, moved over the bridge in a dense mass. Looking down over the rails Prince NesvГӯtski saw the rapid, noisy little waves of the Enns, which rippling and eddying round the piles of the bridge chased each other along.
