On the narrow Augesd Dam where for so many years the old miller had been accustomed to sit in his tasseled cap peacefully angling, while his grandson, with shirt sleeves rolled up, handled the floundering silvery fish in the watering can, on that dam over which for so many years Moravians in shaggy caps and blue jackets had peacefully driven their two-horse carts loaded with wheat and had returned dusty with flour whitening their cartsвҒ вҖ”on that narrow dam amid the wagons and the cannon, under the horsesвҖҷ hoofs and between the wagon wheels, men disfigured by fear of death now crowded together, crushing one another, dying, stepping over the dying and killing one another, only to move on a few steps and be killed themselves in the same way. Every ten seconds a cannon ball flew compressing the air around, or a shell burst in the midst of that dense throng, killing some and splashing with blood those near them. DГіlokhovвҒ вҖ”now an officerвҒ вҖ”wounded in the arm, and on foot, with the regimental commander on horseback and some ten men of his company, represented all that was left of that whole regiment. Impelled by the crowd, they had got wedged in at the approach to the dam and, jammed in on all sides, had stopped because a horse in front had fallen under a cannon and the crowd were dragging it out. A cannon ball killed someone behind them, another fell in front and splashed DГіlokhov with blood. The crowd, pushing forward desperately, squeezed together, moved a few steps, and again stopped. вҖңMove on a hundred yards and we are certainly saved, remain here another two minutes and it is certain death,вҖқ thought each one. DГіlokhov who was in the midst of the crowd forced his way to the edge of the dam, throwing two soldiers off their feet, and ran onto the slippery ice that covered the millpool. вҖңTurn this way!вҖқ he shouted, jumping over the ice which creaked under him; вҖңturn this way!вҖқ he shouted to those with the gun. вҖңIt bears!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The ice bore him but it swayed and creaked, and it was plain that it would give way not only under a cannon or a crowd, but very soon even under his weight alone. The men looked at him and pressed to the bank, hesitating to step onto the ice. The general on horseback at the entrance to the dam raised his hand and opened his mouth to address DГіlokhov. Suddenly a cannon ball hissed so low above the crowd that everyone ducked. It flopped into something moist, and the general fell from his horse in a pool of blood. Nobody gave him a look or thought of raising him. вҖңGet onto the ice, over the ice! Go on! Turn! DonвҖҷt you hear? Go on!вҖқ innumerable voices suddenly shouted after the ball had struck the general, the men themselves not knowing what, or why, they were shouting. One of the hindmost guns that was going onto the dam turned off onto the ice. Crowds of soldiers from the dam began running onto the frozen pond. The ice gave way under one of the foremost soldiers, and one leg slipped into the water. He tried to right himself but fell in up to his waist. The nearest soldiers shrank back, the gun driver stopped his horse, but from behind still came the shouts: вҖңOnto the ice, why do you stop? Go on! Go on!вҖқ And cries of horror were heard in the crowd. The soldiers near the gun waved their arms and beat the horses to make them turn and move on. The horses moved off the bank. The ice, that had held under those on foot, collapsed in a great mass, and some forty men who were on it dashed, some forward and some back, drowning one another. Still the cannon balls continued regularly to whistle and flop onto the ice and into the water and oftenest of all among the crowd that covered the dam, the pond, and the bank. XIX XIX On the Pratzen Heights, where he had fallen with the flagstaff in his hand, lay Prince AndrГ©y BolkГіnski bleeding profusely and unconsciously uttering a gentle, piteous, and childlike moan. Toward evening he ceased moaning and became quite still. He did not know how long his unconsciousness lasted. Suddenly he again felt that he was alive and suffering from a burning, lacerating pain in his head. вҖңWhere is it, that lofty sky that I did not know till now, but saw today?вҖқ was his first thought. вҖңAnd I did not know this suffering either,вҖқ he thought. вҖңYes, I did not know anything, anything at all till now. But where am I?вҖқ He listened and heard the sound of approaching horses, and voices speaking French. He opened his eyes. Above him again was the same lofty sky with clouds that had risen and were floating still higher, and between them gleamed blue infinity. He did not turn his head and did not see those who, judging by the sound of hoofs and voices, had ridden up and stopped near him. It was Napoleon accompanied by two aides-de-camp. Bonaparte riding over the battlefield had given final orders to strengthen the batteries firing at the Augesd Dam and was looking at the killed and wounded left on the field. вҖңFine men!вҖқ remarked Napoleon, looking at a dead Russian grenadier, who, with his face buried in the ground and a blackened nape, lay on his stomach with an already stiffened arm flung wide. вҖңThe ammunition for the guns in position is exhausted, Your Majesty,вҖқ said an adjutant who had come from the batteries that were firing at Augesd. вҖңHave some brought from the reserve,вҖқ said Napoleon, and having gone on a few steps he stopped before Prince AndrГ©y, who lay on his back with the flagstaff that had been dropped beside him. (The flag had already been taken by the French as a trophy.) вҖңThatвҖҷs a fine death!вҖқ said Napoleon as he gazed at BolkГіnski. Prince AndrГ©y understood that this was said of him and that it was Napoleon who said it. He heard the speaker addressed as Sire. But he heard the words as he might have heard the buzzing of a fly. Not only did they not interest him, but he took no notice of them and at once forgot them. His head was burning, he felt himself bleeding to death, and he saw above him the remote, lofty, and everlasting sky. He knew it was NapoleonвҒ вҖ”his heroвҒ вҖ”but at that moment Napoleon seemed to him such a small, insignificant creature compared with what was passing now between himself and that lofty infinite sky with the clouds flying over it. At that moment it meant nothing to him who might be standing over him, or what was said of him; he was only glad that people were standing near him and only wished that they would help him and bring him back to life, which seemed to him so beautiful now that he had today learned to understand it so differently. He collected all his strength, to stir and utter a sound. He feebly moved his leg and uttered a weak, sickly groan which aroused his own pity. вҖңAh! He is alive,вҖқ said Napoleon. вҖңLift this young man up and carry him to the dressing station.вҖқ Having said this, Napoleon rode on to meet Marshal Lannes, who, hat in hand, rode up smiling to the Emperor to congratulate him on the victory. Prince AndrГ©y remembered nothing more: he lost consciousness from the terrible pain of being lifted onto the stretcher, the jolting while being moved, and the probing of his wound at the dressing station. He did not regain consciousness till late in the day, when with other wounded and captured Russian officers he was carried to the hospital. During this transfer he felt a little stronger and was able to look about him and even speak. The first words he heard on coming to his senses were those of a French convoy officer, who said rapidly: вҖңWe must halt here: the Emperor will pass here immediately; it will please him to see these gentlemen prisoners.вҖқ вҖңThere are so many prisoners today, nearly the whole Russian army, that he is probably tired of them,вҖқ said another officer. вҖңAll the same! They say this one is the commander of all the Emperor AlexanderвҖҷs Guards,вҖқ said the first one, indicating a Russian officer in the white uniform of the Horse Guards. BolkГіnski recognized Prince RepnГӯn whom he had met in Petersburg society. Beside him stood a lad of nineteen, also a wounded officer of the Horse Guards. Bonaparte, having come up at a gallop, stopped his horse. вҖңWhich is the senior?вҖқ he asked, on seeing the prisoners. They named the colonel, Prince RepnГӯn. вҖңYou are the commander of the Emperor AlexanderвҖҷs regiment of Horse Guards?вҖқ asked Napoleon. вҖңI commanded a squadron,вҖқ replied RepnГӯn. вҖңYour regiment fulfilled its duty honorably,вҖқ said Napoleon. вҖңThe praise of a great commander is a soldierвҖҷs highest reward,вҖқ said RepnГӯn. вҖңI bestow it with pleasure,вҖқ said Napoleon. вҖңAnd who is that young man beside you?вҖқ Prince RepnГӯn named Lieutenant SukhtГ©len. After looking at him Napoleon smiled. вҖңHeвҖҷs very young to come to meddle with us.вҖқ вҖңYouth is no hindrance to courage,вҖқ muttered SukhtГ©len in a failing voice. вҖңA splendid reply!вҖқ said Napoleon. вҖңYoung man, you will go far!вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y, who had also been brought forward before the EmperorвҖҷs eyes to complete the show of prisoners, could not fail to attract his attention. Napoleon apparently remembered seeing him on the battlefield and, addressing him, again used the epithet вҖңyoung manвҖқ that was connected in his memory with Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңWell, and you, young man,вҖқ said he. вҖңHow do you feel, mon brave?вҖқ Though five minutes before, Prince AndrГ©y had been able to say a few words to the soldiers who were carrying him, now with his eyes fixed straight on Napoleon, he was silent.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ So insignificant at that moment seemed to him all the interests that engrossed Napoleon, so mean did his hero himself with his paltry vanity and joy in victory appear, compared to the lofty, equitable, and kindly sky which he had seen and understood, that he could not answer him. Everything seemed so futile and insignificant in comparison with the stern and solemn train of thought that weakness from loss of blood, suffering, and the nearness of death aroused in him. Looking into NapoleonвҖҷs eyes Prince AndrГ©y thought of the insignificance of greatness, the unimportance of life which no one could understand, and the still greater unimportance of death, the meaning of which no one alive could understand or explain. The Emperor without waiting for an answer turned away and said to one of the officers as he went: вҖңHave these gentlemen attended to and taken to my bivouac; let my doctor, Larrey, examine their wounds. Au revoir, Prince RepnГӯn!вҖқ and he spurred his horse and galloped away. His face shone with self-satisfaction and pleasure. The soldiers who had carried Prince AndrГ©y had noticed and taken the little gold icon Princess MГЎrya had hung round her brotherвҖҷs neck, but seeing the favor the Emperor showed the prisoners, they now hastened to return the holy image. Prince AndrГ©y did not see how and by whom it was replaced, but the little icon with its thin gold chain suddenly appeared upon his chest outside his uniform. вҖңIt would be good,вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y, glancing at the icon his sister had hung round his neck with such emotion and reverence, вҖңit would be good if everything were as clear and simple as it seems to MГЎrya. How good it would be to know where to seek for help in this life, and what to expect after it beyond the grave! How happy and calm I should be if I could now say: вҖҳLord, have mercy on me!вҖҷвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But to whom should I say that? Either to a Power indefinable, incomprehensible, which I not only cannot address but which I cannot even express in wordsвҒ вҖ”the Great All or NothingвҒ вҖ”вҖқ said he to himself, вҖңor to that God who has been sewn into this amulet by MГЎrya! There is nothing certain, nothing at all except the unimportance of everything I understand, and the greatness of something incomprehensible but all-important.вҖқ The stretchers moved on. At every jolt he again felt unendurable pain; his feverishness increased and he grew delirious. Visions of his father, wife, sister, and future son, and the tenderness he had felt the night before the battle, the figure of the insignificant little Napoleon, and above all this the lofty sky, formed the chief subjects of his delirious fancies. The quiet home life and peaceful happiness of Bald Hills presented itself to him. He was already enjoying that happiness when that little Napoleon had suddenly appeared with his unsympathizing look of shortsighted delight at the misery of others, and doubts and torments had followed, and only the heavens promised peace. Toward morning all these dreams melted and merged into the chaos and darkness of unconciousness and oblivion which in the opinion of NapoleonвҖҷs doctor, Larrey, was much more likely to end in death than in convalescence. вҖңHe is a nervous, bilious subject,вҖқ said Larrey, вҖңand will not recover.вҖқ And Prince AndrГ©y, with others fatally wounded, was left to the care of the inhabitants of the district. II II In November, 1805, Prince VasГӯli had to go on a tour of inspection in four different provinces. He had arranged this for himself so as to visit his neglected estates at the same time and pick up his son Anatole where his regiment was stationed, and take him to visit Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich BolkГіnski in order to arrange a match for him with the daughter of that rich old man. But before leaving home and undertaking these new affairs, Prince VasГӯli had to settle matters with Pierre, who, it is true, had latterly spent whole days at home, that is, in Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs house where he was staying, and had been absurd, excited, and foolish in ElГЁnвҖҷs presence (as a lover should be), but had not yet proposed to her. вҖңThis is all very fine, but things must be settled,вҖқ said Prince VasГӯli to himself, with a sorrowful sigh, one morning, feeling that Pierre who was under such obligations to him (вҖңBut never mind thatвҖқ) was not behaving very well in this matter. вҖңYouth, frivolityвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ well, God be with him,вҖқ thought he, relishing his own goodness of heart, вҖңbut it must be brought to a head. The day after tomorrow will be LГ«lyaвҖҷs name day. I will invite two or three people, and if he does not understand what he ought to do then it will be my affairвҒ вҖ”yes, my affair. I am her father.вҖқ Six weeks after Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs вҖңAt HomeвҖқ and after the sleepless night when he had decided that to marry ElГЁn would be a calamity and that he ought to avoid her and go away, Pierre, despite that decision, had not left Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs and felt with terror that in peopleвҖҷs eyes he was every day more and more connected with her, that it was impossible for him to return to his former conception of her, that he could not break away from her, and that though it would be a terrible thing he would have to unite his fate with hers. He might perhaps have been able to free himself but that Prince VasГӯli (who had rarely before given receptions) now hardly let a day go by without having an evening party at which Pierre had to be present unless he wished to spoil the general pleasure and disappoint everyoneвҖҷs expectation. Prince VasГӯli, in the rare moments when he was at home, would take PierreвҖҷs hand in passing and draw it downwards, or absentmindedly hold out his wrinkled, clean-shaven cheek for Pierre to kiss and would say: вҖңTill tomorrow,вҖқ or, вҖңBe in to dinner or I shall not see you,вҖқ or, вҖңI am staying in for your sake,вҖқ and so on. And though Prince VasГӯli, when he stayed in (as he said) for PierreвҖҷs sake, hardly exchanged a couple of words with him, Pierre felt unable to disappoint him. Every day he said to himself one and the same thing: вҖңIt is time I understood her and made up my mind what she really is. Was I mistaken before, or am I mistaken now? No, she is not stupid, she is an excellent girl,вҖқ he sometimes said to himself вҖңshe never makes a mistake, never says anything stupid. She says little, but what she does say is always clear and simple, so she is not stupid. She never was abashed and is not abashed now, so she cannot be a bad woman!вҖқ He had often begun to make reflections or think aloud in her company, and she had always answered him either by a brief but appropriate remarkвҒ вҖ”showing that it did not interest herвҒ вҖ”or by a silent look and smile which more palpably than anything else showed Pierre her superiority. She was right in regarding all arguments as nonsense in comparison with that smile. She always addressed him with a radiantly confiding smile meant for him alone, in which there was something more significant than in the general smile that usually brightened her face. Pierre knew that everyone was waiting for him to say a word and cross a certain line, and he knew that sooner or later he would step across it, but an incomprehensible terror seized him at the thought of that dreadful step. A thousand times during that month and a half while he felt himself drawn nearer and nearer to that dreadful abyss, Pierre said to himself: вҖңWhat am I doing? I need resolution. Can it be that I have none?вҖқ He wished to take a decision, but felt with dismay that in this matter he lacked that strength of will which he had known in himself and really possessed. Pierre was one of those who are only strong when they feel themselves quite innocent, and since that day when he was overpowered by a feeling of desire while stooping over the snuffbox at Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs, an unacknowledged sense of the guilt of that desire paralyzed his will. On ElГЁnвҖҷs name day, a small party of just their own peopleвҒ вҖ”as his wife saidвҒ вҖ”met for supper at Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs. All these friends and relations had been given to understand that the fate of the young girl would be decided that evening. The visitors were seated at supper. Princess KurГЎgina, a portly imposing woman who had once been handsome, was sitting at the head of the table. On either side of her sat the more important guestsвҒ вҖ”an old general and his wife, and Anna PГЎvlovna SchГ©rer. At the other end sat the younger and less important guests, and there too sat the members of the family, and Pierre and ElГЁn, side by side. Prince VasГӯli was not having any supper: he went round the table in a merry mood, sitting down now by one, now by another, of the guests. To each of them he made some careless and agreeable remark except to Pierre and ElГЁn, whose presence he seemed not to notice. He enlivened the whole party. The wax candles burned brightly, the silver and crystal gleamed, so did the ladiesвҖҷ toilets and the gold and silver of the menвҖҷs epaulets; servants in scarlet liveries moved round the table, the clatter of plates, knives, and glasses mingled with the animated hum of several conversations. At one end of the table, the old chamberlain was heard assuring an old baroness that he loved her passionately, at which she laughed; at the other could be heard the story of the misfortunes of some MГЎrya VГӯktorovna or other. At the center of the table, Prince VasГӯli attracted everybodyвҖҷs attention. With a facetious smile on his face, he was telling the ladies about last WednesdayвҖҷs meeting of the Imperial Council, at which SergГ©y KuzmГӯch VyazmГӯtinov, the new military governor general of Petersburg, had received and read the then famous rescript of the Emperor Alexander from the army to SergГ©y KuzmГӯch, in which the Emperor said that he was receiving from all sides declarations of the peopleвҖҷs loyalty, that the declaration from Petersburg gave him particular pleasure, and that he was proud to be at the head of such a nation and would endeavor to be worthy of it. This rescript began with the words: вҖңSergГ©y KuzmГӯch, From all sides reports reach me,вҖқ etc. вҖңWell, and so he never got farther than: вҖҳSergГ©y KuzmГӯchвҖҷ?вҖқ asked one of the ladies. вҖңExactly, not a hairвҖҷs breadth farther,вҖқ answered Prince VasГӯli, laughing, вҖңвҖҠвҖҳSergГ©y KuzmГӯchвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ From all sidesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ From all sidesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ SergГ©y KuzmГӯchвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҷ Poor VyazmГӯtinov could not get any farther! He began the rescript again and again, but as soon as he uttered вҖҳSergГ©yвҖҷ he sobbed, вҖҳKuz-mГӯ-ch,вҖҷ tears, and вҖҳFrom all sidesвҖҷ was smothered in sobs and he could get no farther. And again his handkerchief, and again: вҖҳSergГ©y KuzmГӯch, From all sides,вҖҷвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and tears, till at last somebody else was asked to read it.вҖқ вҖңKuzmГӯchвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ From all sidesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and then tears,вҖқ someone repeated laughing. вҖңDonвҖҷt be unkind,вҖқ cried Anna PГЎvlovna from her end of the table holding up a threatening finger. вҖңHe is such a worthy and excellent man, our dear Viasmitinoff.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Everybody laughed a great deal. At the head of the table, where the honored guests sat, everyone seemed to be in high spirits and under the influence of a variety of exciting sensations. Only Pierre and ElГЁn sat silently side by side almost at the bottom of the table, a suppressed smile brightening both their faces, a smile that had nothing to do with SergГ©y KuzmГӯchвҒ вҖ”a smile of bashfulness at their own feelings. But much as all the rest laughed, talked, and joked, much as they enjoyed their Rhine wine, sautГ©, and ices, and however they avoided looking at the young couple, and heedless and unobservant as they seemed of them, one could feel by the occasional glances they gave that the story about SergГ©y KuzmГӯch, the laughter, and the food were all a pretense, and that the whole attention of that company was directed toвҒ вҖ”Pierre and ElГЁn. Prince VasГӯli mimicked the sobbing of SergГ©y KuzmГӯch and at the same time his eyes glanced toward his daughter, and while he laughed the expression on his face clearly said: вҖңYesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ itвҖҷs getting on, it will all be settled today.вҖқ Anna PГЎvlovna threatened him on behalf of вҖңour dear Viasmitinoff,вҖқ and in her eyes, which, for an instant, glanced at Pierre, Prince VasГӯli read a congratulation on his future son-in-law and on his daughterвҖҷs happiness. The old princess sighed sadly as she offered some wine to the old lady next to her and glanced angrily at her daughter, and her sigh seemed to say: вҖңYes, thereвҖҷs nothing left for you and me but to sip sweet wine, my dear, now that the time has come for these young ones to be thus boldly, provocatively happy.вҖқ вҖңAnd what nonsense all this is that I am saying!вҖқ thought a diplomatist, glancing at the happy faces of the lovers. вҖңThatвҖҷs happiness!вҖқ Into the insignificant, trifling, and artificial interests uniting that society had entered the simple feeling of the attraction of a healthy and handsome young man and woman for one another. And this human feeling dominated everything else and soared above all their affected chatter. Jests fell flat, news was not interesting, and the animation was evidently forced. Not only the guests but even the footmen waiting at table seemed to feel this, and they forgot their duties as they looked at the beautiful ElГЁn with her radiant face and at the red, broad, and happy though uneasy face of Pierre. It seemed as if the very light of the candles was focused on those two happy faces alone. Pierre felt that he was the center of it all, and this both pleased and embarrassed him. He was like a man entirely absorbed in some occupation. He did not see, hear, or understand anything clearly. Only now and then detached ideas and impressions from the world of reality shot unexpectedly through his mind. вҖңSo it is all finished!вҖқ he thought. вҖңAnd how has it all happened? How quickly! Now I know that not because of her alone, nor of myself alone, but because of everyone, it must inevitably come about. They are all expecting it, they are so sure that it will happen that I cannot, I cannot, disappoint them. But how will it be? I do not know, but it will certainly happen!вҖқ thought Pierre, glancing at those dazzling shoulders close to his eyes. Or he would suddenly feel ashamed of he knew not what. He felt it awkward to attract everyoneвҖҷs attention and to be considered a lucky man and, with his plain face, to be looked on as a sort of Paris possessed of a Helen. вҖңBut no doubt it always is and must be so!вҖқ he consoled himself. вҖңAnd besides, what have I done to bring it about? How did it begin? I traveled from Moscow with Prince VasГӯli. Then there was nothing. So why should I not stay at his house? Then I played cards with her and picked up her reticule and drove out with her. How did it begin, when did it all come about?вҖқ And here he was sitting by her side as her betrothed, seeing, hearing, feeling her nearness, her breathing, her movements, her beauty. Then it would suddenly seem to him that it was not she but he was so unusually beautiful, and that that was why they all looked so at him, and flattered by this general admiration he would expand his chest, raise his head, and rejoice at his good fortune. Suddenly he heard a familiar voice repeating something to him a second time. But Pierre was so absorbed that he did not understand what was said. вҖңI am asking you when you last heard from BolkГіnski,вҖқ repeated Prince VasГӯli a third time. вҖңHow absentminded you are, my dear fellow.вҖқ Prince VasГӯli smiled, and Pierre noticed that everyone was smiling at him and ElГЁn. вҖңWell, what of it, if you all know it?вҖқ thought Pierre. вҖңWhat of it? ItвҖҷs the truth!вҖқ and he himself smiled his gentle childlike smile, and ElГЁn smiled too. вҖңWhen did you get the letter? Was it from OlmГјtz?вҖқ repeated Prince VasГӯli, who pretended to want to know this in order to settle a dispute. вҖңHow can one talk or think of such trifles?вҖқ thought Pierre. вҖңYes, from OlmГјtz,вҖқ he answered, with a sigh. After supper Pierre with his partner followed the others into the drawing room. The guests began to disperse, some without taking leave of ElГЁn. Some, as if unwilling to distract her from an important occupation, came up to her for a moment and made haste to go away, refusing to let her see them off. The diplomatist preserved a mournful silence as he left the drawing room. He pictured the vanity of his diplomatic career in comparison with PierreвҖҷs happiness. The old general grumbled at his wife when she asked how his leg was. вҖңOh, the old fool,вҖқ he thought. вҖңThat ElГЁna VasГӯlievna will be beautiful still when sheвҖҷs fifty.вҖқ вҖңI think I may congratulate you,вҖқ whispered Anna PГЎvlovna to the old princess, kissing her soundly. вҖңIf I hadnвҖҷt this headache IвҖҷd have stayed longer.вҖқ The old princess did not reply, she was tormented by jealousy of her daughterвҖҷs happiness. While the guests were taking their leave Pierre remained for a long time alone with ElГЁn in the little drawing room where they were sitting. He had often before, during the last six weeks, remained alone with her, but had never spoken to her of love. Now he felt that it was inevitable, but he could not make up his mind to take the final step. He felt ashamed; he felt that he was occupying someone elseвҖҷs place here beside ElГЁn. вҖңThis happiness is not for you,вҖқ some inner voice whispered to him. вҖңThis happiness is for those who have not in them what there is in you.вҖқ But, as he had to say something, he began by asking her whether she was satisfied with the party. She replied in her usual simple manner that this name day of hers had been one of the pleasantest she had ever had. Some of the nearest relatives had not yet left. They were sitting in the large drawing room. Prince VasГӯli came up to Pierre with languid footsteps. Pierre rose and said it was getting late. Prince VasГӯli gave him a look of stern inquiry, as though what Pierre had just said was so strange that one could not take it in. But then the expression of severity changed, and he drew PierreвҖҷs hand downwards, made him sit down, and smiled affectionately. вҖңWell, LГ«lya?вҖқ he asked, turning instantly to his daughter and addressing her with the careless tone of habitual tenderness natural to parents who have petted their children from babyhood, but which Prince VasГӯli had only acquired by imitating other parents. And he again turned to Pierre. вҖңSergГ©y KuzmГӯchвҒ вҖ”From all sidesвҒ вҖ”вҖқ he said, unbuttoning the top button of his waistcoat. Pierre smiled, but his smile showed that he knew it was not the story about SergГ©y KuzmГӯch that interested Prince VasГӯli just then, and Prince VasГӯli saw that Pierre knew this. He suddenly muttered something and went away. It seemed to Pierre that even the prince was disconcerted. The sight of the discomposure of that old man of the world touched Pierre: he looked at ElГЁn and she too seemed disconcerted, and her look seemed to say: вҖңWell, it is your own fault.вҖқ вҖңThe step must be taken but I cannot, I cannot!вҖқ thought Pierre, and he again began speaking about indifferent matters, about SergГ©y KuzmГӯch, asking what the point of the story was as he had not heard it properly. ElГЁn answered with a smile that she too had missed it. When Prince VasГӯli returned to the drawing room, the princess, his wife, was talking in low tones to the elderly lady about Pierre. вҖңOf course, it is a very brilliant match, but happiness, my dearвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңMarriages are made in heaven,вҖқ replied the elderly lady. Prince VasГӯli passed by, seeming not to hear the ladies, and sat down on a sofa in a far corner of the room. He closed his eyes and seemed to be dozing. His head sank forward and then he roused himself. вҖңAline,вҖқ he said to his wife, вҖңgo and see what they are about.вҖқ The princess went up to the door, passed by it with a dignified and indifferent air, and glanced into the little drawing room. Pierre and ElГЁn still sat talking just as before. вҖңStill the same,вҖқ she said to her husband. Prince VasГӯli frowned, twisting his mouth, his cheeks quivered and his face assumed the coarse, unpleasant expression peculiar to him. Shaking himself, he rose, threw back his head, and with resolute steps went past the ladies into the little drawing room. With quick steps he went joyfully up to Pierre. His face was so unusually triumphant that Pierre rose in alarm on seeing it. вҖңThank God!вҖқ said Prince VasГӯli. вҖңMy wife has told me everything!вҖқ (He put one arm around Pierre and the other around his daughter.)вҒ вҖ”вҖңMy dear boyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ LГ«lyaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I am very pleased.вҖқ (His voice trembled.) вҖңI loved your fatherвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and she will make you a good wifeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ God bless you!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He embraced his daughter, and then again Pierre, and kissed him with his malodorous mouth. Tears actually moistened his cheeks. вҖңPrincess, come here!вҖқ he shouted. The old princess came in and also wept. The elderly lady was using her handkerchief too. Pierre was kissed, and he kissed the beautiful ElГЁnвҖҷs hand several times. After a while they were left alone again. вҖңAll this had to be and could not be otherwise,вҖқ thought Pierre, вҖңso it is useless to ask whether it is good or bad. It is good because itвҖҷs definite and one is rid of the old tormenting doubt.вҖқ Pierre held the hand of his betrothed in silence, looking at her beautiful bosom as it rose and fell. вҖңElГЁn!вҖқ he said aloud and paused. вҖңSomething special is always said in such cases,вҖқ he thought, but could not remember what it was that people say. He looked at her face. She drew nearer to him. Her face flushed. вҖңOh, take those offвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ thoseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she said, pointing to his spectacles. Pierre took them off, and his eyes, besides the strange look eyes have from which spectacles have just been removed, had also a frightened and inquiring look. He was about to stoop over her hand and kiss it, but with a rapid, almost brutal movement of her head, she intercepted his lips and met them with her own. Her face struck Pierre, by its altered, unpleasantly excited expression. вҖңIt is too late now, itвҖҷs done; besides I love her,вҖқ thought Pierre. вҖңJe vous aime!вҖқ38 he said, remembering what has to be said at such moments: but his words sounded so weak that he felt ashamed of himself. Six weeks later he was married, and settled in Count BezГәkhovвҖҷs large, newly furnished Petersburg house, the happy possessor, as people said, of a wife who was a celebrated beauty and of millions of money. III III Old Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich BolkГіnski received a letter from Prince VasГӯli in November, 1805, announcing that he and his son would be paying him a visit. вҖңI am starting on a journey of inspection, and of course I shall think nothing of an extra seventy miles to come and see you at the same time, my honored benefactor,вҖқ wrote Prince VasГӯli. вҖңMy son Anatole is accompanying me on his way to the army, so I hope you will allow him personally to express the deep respect that, emulating his father, he feels for you.вҖқ вҖңIt seems that there will be no need to bring MГЎrya out, suitors are coming to us of their own accord,вҖқ incautiously remarked the little princess on hearing the news. Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich frowned, but said nothing. A fortnight after the letter Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs servants came one evening in advance of him, and he and his son arrived next day. Old BolkГіnski had always had a poor opinion of Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs character, but more so recently, since in the new reigns of Paul and Alexander Prince VasГӯli had risen to high position and honors. And now, from the hints contained in his letter and given by the little princess, he saw which way the wind was blowing, and his low opinion changed into a feeling of contemptuous ill will. He snorted whenever he mentioned him. On the day of Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs arrival, Prince BolkГіnski was particularly discontented and out of temper. Whether he was in a bad temper because Prince VasГӯli was coming, or whether his being in a bad temper made him specially annoyed at Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs visit, he was in a bad temper, and in the morning TГӯkhon had already advised the architect not to go to the prince with his report. вҖңDo you hear how heвҖҷs walking?вҖқ said TГӯkhon, drawing the architectвҖҷs attention to the sound of the princeвҖҷs footsteps. вҖңStepping flat on his heelsвҒ вҖ”we know what that means.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ However, at nine oвҖҷclock the prince, in his velvet coat with a sable collar and cap, went out for his usual walk. It had snowed the day before and the path to the hothouse, along which the prince was in the habit of walking, had been swept: the marks of the broom were still visible in the snow and a shovel had been left sticking in one of the soft snowbanks that bordered both sides of the path. The prince went through the conservatories, the serfsвҖҷ quarters, and the outbuildings, frowning and silent. вҖңCan a sleigh pass?вҖқ he asked his overseer, a venerable man, resembling his master in manners and looks, who was accompanying him back to the house. вҖңThe snow is deep. I am having the avenue swept, your honor.вҖқ The prince bowed his head and went up to the porch. вҖңGod be thanked,вҖқ thought the overseer, вҖңthe storm has blown over!вҖқ вҖңIt would have been hard to drive up, your honor,вҖқ he added. вҖңI heard, your honor, that a minister is coming to visit your honor.вҖқ The prince turned round to the overseer and fixed his eyes on him, frowning. вҖңWhat? A minister? What minister? Who gave orders?вҖқ he said in his shrill, harsh voice. вҖңThe road is not swept for the princess my daughter, but for a minister! For me, there are no ministers!вҖқ вҖңYour honor, I thoughtвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYou thought!вҖқ shouted the prince, his words coming more and more rapidly and indistinctly. вҖңYou thought!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Rascals! Blackguards!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ IвҖҷll teach you to think!вҖқ and lifting his stick he swung it and would have hit AlpГЎtych, the overseer, had not the latter instinctively avoided the blow. вҖңThoughtвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ BlackguardsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ shouted the prince rapidly. But although AlpГЎtych, frightened at his own temerity in avoiding the stroke, came up to the prince, bowing his bald head resignedly before him, or perhaps for that very reason, the prince, though he continued to shout: вҖңBlackguards!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Throw the snow back on the road!вҖқ did not lift his stick again but hurried into the house. Before dinner, Princess MГЎrya and Mademoiselle Bourienne, who knew that the prince was in a bad humor, stood awaiting him; Mademoiselle Bourienne with a radiant face that said: вҖңI know nothing, I am the same as usual,вҖқ and Princess MГЎrya pale, frightened, and with downcast eyes. What she found hardest to bear was to know that on such occasions she ought to behave like Mademoiselle Bourienne, but could not. She thought: вҖңIf I seem not to notice he will think that I do not sympathize with him; if I seem sad and out of spirits myself, he will say (as he has done before) that IвҖҷm in the dumps.вҖқ The prince looked at his daughterвҖҷs frightened face and snorted. вҖңFoolвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ or dummy!вҖқ he muttered. вҖңAnd the other one is not here. TheyвҖҷve been telling tales,вҖқ he thoughtвҒ вҖ”referring to the little princess who was not in the dining room. вҖңWhere is the princess?вҖқ he asked. вҖңHiding?вҖқ вҖңShe is not very well,вҖқ answered Mademoiselle Bourienne with a bright smile, вҖңso she wonвҖҷt come down. It is natural in her state.вҖқ вҖңHm! Hm!вҖқ muttered the prince, sitting down. His plate seemed to him not quite clean, and pointing to a spot he flung it away. TГӯkhon caught it and handed it to a footman. The little princess was not unwell, but had such an overpowering fear of the prince that, hearing he was in a bad humor, she had decided not to appear. вҖңI am afraid for the baby,вҖқ she said to Mademoiselle Bourienne: вҖңHeaven knows what a fright might do.вҖқ In general at Bald Hills the little princess lived in constant fear, and with a sense of antipathy to the old prince which she did not realize because the fear was so much the stronger feeling. The prince reciprocated this antipathy, but it was overpowered by his contempt for her. When the little princess had grown accustomed to life at Bald Hills, she took a special fancy to Mademoiselle Bourienne, spent whole days with her, asked her to sleep in her room, and often talked with her about the old prince and criticized him. вҖңSo we are to have visitors, mon prince?вҖқ remarked Mademoiselle Bourienne, unfolding her white napkin with her rosy fingers. вҖңHis Excellency Prince Kouraguine and his son, I understand?вҖқ she said inquiringly. вҖңHm!вҒ вҖ”his excellency is a puppy.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I got him his appointment in the service,вҖқ said the prince disdainfully. вҖңWhy his son is coming I donвҖҷt understand. Perhaps Princess LizavГ©ta KarlГіvna and Princess MГЎrya know. I donвҖҷt want him.вҖқ (He looked at his blushing daughter.) вҖңAre you unwell today? Eh? Afraid of the вҖҳministerвҖҷ as that idiot AlpГЎtych called him this morning?вҖқ вҖңNo, mon pГЁre.вҖқ Though Mademoiselle Bourienne had been so unsuccessful in her choice of a subject, she did not stop talking, but chattered about the conservatories and the beauty of a flower that had just opened, and after the soup the prince became more genial. After dinner, he went to see his daughter-in-law. The little princess was sitting at a small table, chattering with MГЎsha, her maid. She grew pale on seeing her father-in-law. She was much altered. She was now plain rather than pretty. Her cheeks had sunk, her lip was drawn up, and her eyes drawn down. вҖңYes, I feel a kind of oppression,вҖқ she said in reply to the princeвҖҷs question as to how she felt. вҖңDo you want anything?вҖқ вҖңNo, merci, mon pГЁre.вҖқ вҖңWell, all right, all right.вҖқ He left the room and went to the waiting room where AlpГЎtych stood with bowed head. вҖңHas the snow been shoveled back?вҖқ вҖңYes, your excellency. Forgive me for heavenвҖҷs sakeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ It was only my stupidity.вҖқ вҖңAll right, all right,вҖқ interrupted the prince, and laughing his unnatural way, he stretched out his hand for AlpГЎtych to kiss, and then proceeded to his study. Prince VasГӯli arrived that evening. He was met in the avenue by coachmen and footmen, who, with loud shouts, dragged his sleighs up to one of the lodges over the road purposely laden with snow. Prince VasГӯli and Anatole had separate rooms assigned to them. Anatole, having taken off his overcoat, sat with arms akimbo before a table on a corner of which he smilingly and absentmindedly fixed his large and handsome eyes. He regarded his whole life as a continual round of amusement which someone for some reason had to provide for him. And he looked on this visit to a churlish old man and a rich and ugly heiress in the same way. All this might, he thought, turn out very well and amusingly. вҖңAnd why not marry her if she really has so much money? That never does any harm,вҖқ thought Anatole. He shaved and scented himself with the care and elegance which had become habitual to him and, his handsome head held high, entered his fatherвҖҷs room with the good-humored and victorious air natural to him. Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs two valets were busy dressing him, and he looked round with much animation and cheerfully nodded to his son as the latter entered, as if to say: вҖңYes, thatвҖҷs how I want you to look.вҖқ вҖңI say, Father, joking apart, is she very hideous?вҖқ Anatole asked, as if continuing a conversation the subject of which had often been mentioned during the journey. вҖңEnough! What nonsense! Above all, try to be respectful and cautious with the old prince.вҖқ вҖңIf he starts a row IвҖҷll go away,вҖқ said Prince Anatole. вҖңI canвҖҷt bear those old men! Eh?вҖқ вҖңRemember, for you everything depends on this.вҖқ In the meantime, not only was it known in the maidservantsвҖҷ rooms that the minister and his son had arrived, but the appearance of both had been minutely described. Princess MГЎrya was sitting alone in her room, vainly trying to master her agitation. вҖңWhy did they write, why did Liza tell me about it? It can never happen!вҖқ she said, looking at herself in the glass. вҖңHow shall I enter the drawing room? Even if I like him I canвҖҷt now be myself with him.вҖқ The mere thought of her fatherвҖҷs look filled her with terror. The little princess and Mademoiselle Bourienne had already received from MГЎsha, the ladyвҖҷs maid, the necessary report of how handsome the ministerвҖҷs son was, with his rosy cheeks and dark eyebrows, and with what difficulty the father had dragged his legs upstairs while the son had followed him like an eagle, three steps at a time. Having received this information, the little princess and Mademoiselle Bourienne, whose chattering voices had reached her from the corridor, went into Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs room. вҖңYou know theyвҖҷve come, Marie?вҖқ said the little princess, waddling in, and sinking heavily into an armchair. She was no longer in the loose gown she generally wore in the morning, but had on one of her best dresses. Her hair was carefully done and her face was animated, which, however, did not conceal its sunken and faded outlines. Dressed as she used to be in Petersburg society, it was still more noticeable how much plainer she had become. Some unobtrusive touch had been added to Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs toilet which rendered her fresh and pretty face yet more attractive. вҖңWhat! Are you going to remain as you are, dear princess?вҖқ she began. вҖңTheyвҖҷll be announcing that the gentlemen are in the drawing room and we shall have to go down, and you have not smartened yourself up at all!вҖқ The little princess got up, rang for the maid, and hurriedly and merrily began to devise and carry out a plan of how Princess MГЎrya should be dressed. Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs self-esteem was wounded by the fact that the arrival of a suitor agitated her, and still more so by both her companionsвҖҷ not having the least conception that it could be otherwise. To tell them that she felt ashamed for herself and for them would be to betray her agitation, while to decline their offers to dress her would prolong their banter and insistence. She flushed, her beautiful eyes grew dim, red blotches came on her face, and it took on the unattractive martyrlike expression it so often wore, as she submitted herself to Mademoiselle Bourienne and Liza. Both these women quite sincerely tried to make her look pretty. She was so plain that neither of them could think of her as a rival, so they began dressing her with perfect sincerity, and with the naive and firm conviction women have that dress can make a face pretty. вҖңNo really, my dear, this dress is not pretty,вҖқ said Liza, looking sideways at Princess MГЎrya from a little distance. вҖңYou have a maroon dress, have it fetched. Really! You know the fate of your whole life may be at stake. But this one is too light, itвҖҷs not becoming!вҖқ It was not the dress, but the face and whole figure of Princess MГЎrya that was not pretty, but neither Mademoiselle Bourienne nor the little princess felt this; they still thought that if a blue ribbon were placed in the hair, the hair combed up, and the blue scarf arranged lower on the best maroon dress, and so on, all would be well. They forgot that the frightened face and the figure could not be altered, and that however they might change the setting and adornment of that face, it would still remain piteous and plain. After two or three changes to which Princess MГЎrya meekly submitted, just as her hair had been arranged on the top of her head (a style that quite altered and spoiled her looks) and she had put on a maroon dress with a pale-blue scarf, the little princess walked twice round her, now adjusting a fold of the dress with her little hand, now arranging the scarf and looking at her with her head bent first on one side and then on the other. вҖңNo, it will not do,вҖқ she said decidedly, clasping her hands. вҖңNo, Marie, really this dress does not suit you. I prefer you in your little gray everyday dress. Now please, do it for my sake. Katya,вҖқ she said to the maid, вҖңbring the princess her gray dress, and youвҖҷll see, Mademoiselle Bourienne, how I shall arrange it,вҖқ she added, smiling with a foretaste of artistic pleasure. But when Katya brought the required dress, Princess MГЎrya remained sitting motionless before the glass, looking at her face, and saw in the mirror her eyes full of tears and her mouth quivering, ready to burst into sobs. вҖңCome, dear princess,вҖқ said Mademoiselle Bourienne, вҖңjust one more little effort.вҖқ The little princess, taking the dress from the maid, came up to Princess MГЎrya. вҖңWell, now weвҖҷll arrange something quite simple and becoming,вҖқ she said. The three voices, hers, Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs, and KatyaвҖҷs, who was laughing at something, mingled in a merry sound, like the chirping of birds. вҖңNo, leave me alone,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. Her voice sounded so serious and so sad that the chirping of the birds was silenced at once. They looked at the beautiful, large, thoughtful eyes full of tears and of thoughts, gazing shiningly and imploringly at them, and understood that it was useless and even cruel to insist. вҖңAt least, change your coiffure,вҖқ said the little princess. вҖңDidnвҖҷt I tell you,вҖқ she went on, turning reproachfully to Mademoiselle Bourienne, вҖңMarieвҖҷs is a face which such a coiffure does not suit in the least. Not in the least! Please change it.вҖқ вҖңLeave me alone, please leave me alone! It is all quite the same to me,вҖқ answered a voice struggling with tears. Mademoiselle Bourienne and the little princess had to own to themselves that Princess MГЎrya in this guise looked very plain, worse than usual, but it was too late. She was looking at them with an expression they both knew, an expression thoughtful and sad. This expression in Princess MГЎrya did not frighten them (she never inspired fear in anyone), but they knew that when it appeared on her face, she became mute and was not to be shaken in her determination. вҖңYou will change it, wonвҖҷt you?вҖқ said Liza. And as Princess MГЎrya gave no answer, she left the room. Princess MГЎrya was left alone. She did not comply with LizaвҖҷs request, she not only left her hair as it was, but did not even look in her glass. Letting her arms fall helplessly, she sat with downcast eyes and pondered. A husband, a man, a strong dominant and strangely attractive being rose in her imagination, and carried her into a totally different happy world of his own. She fancied a child, her ownвҒ вҖ”such as she had seen the day before in the arms of her nurseвҖҷs daughterвҒ вҖ”at her own breast, the husband standing by and gazing tenderly at her and the child. вҖңBut no, it is impossible, I am too ugly,вҖқ she thought. вҖңPlease come to tea. The prince will be out in a moment,вҖқ came the maidвҖҷs voice at the door. She roused herself, and felt appalled at what she had been thinking, and before going down she went into the room where the icons hung and, her eyes fixed on the dark face of a large icon of the Saviour lit by a lamp, she stood before it with folded hands for a few moments. A painful doubt filled her soul. Could the joy of love, of earthly love for a man, be for her? In her thoughts of marriage Princess MГЎrya dreamed of happiness and of children, but her strongest, most deeply hidden longing was for earthly love. The more she tried to hide this feeling from others and even from herself, the stronger it grew. вҖңO God,вҖқ she said, вҖңhow am I to stifle in my heart these temptations of the devil? How am I to renounce forever these vile fancies, so as peacefully to fulfill Thy will?вҖқ And scarcely had she put that question than God gave her the answer in her own heart. вҖңDesire nothing for thyself, seek nothing, be not anxious or envious. ManвҖҷs future and thy own fate must remain hidden from thee, but live so that thou mayest be ready for anything. If it be GodвҖҷs will to prove thee in the duties of marriage, be ready to fulfill His will.вҖқ With this consoling thought (but yet with a hope for the fulfillment of her forbidden earthly longing) Princess MГЎrya sighed, and having crossed herself went down, thinking neither of her gown and coiffure nor of how she would go in nor of what she would say. What could all that matter in comparison with the will of God, without Whose care not a hair of manвҖҷs head can fall? IV IV When Princess MГЎrya came down, Prince VasГӯli and his son were already in the drawing room, talking to the little princess and Mademoiselle Bourienne. When she entered with her heavy step, treading on her heels, the gentlemen and Mademoiselle Bourienne rose and the little princess, indicating her to the gentlemen, said: вҖңVoilГ  Marie!вҖқ Princess MГЎrya saw them all and saw them in detail. She saw Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs face, serious for an instant at the sight of her, but immediately smiling again, and the little princess curiously noting the impression вҖңMarieвҖқ produced on the visitors. And she saw Mademoiselle Bourienne, with her ribbon and pretty face, and her unusually animated look which was fixed on him, but him she could not see, she only saw something large, brilliant, and handsome moving toward her as she entered the room. Prince VasГӯli approached first, and she kissed the bold forehead that bent over her hand and answered his question by saying that, on the contrary, she remembered him quite well. Then Anatole came up to her. She still could not see him. She only felt a soft hand taking hers firmly, and she touched with her lips a white forehead, over which was beautiful light-brown hair smelling of pomade. When she looked up at him she was struck by his beauty. Anatole stood with his right thumb under a button of his uniform, his chest expanded and his back drawn in, slightly swinging one foot, and, with his head a little bent, looked with beaming face at the princess without speaking and evidently not thinking about her at all. Anatole was not quick-witted, nor ready or eloquent in conversation, but he had the faculty, so invaluable in society, of composure and imperturbable self-possession. If a man lacking in self-confidence remains dumb on a first introduction and betrays a consciousness of the impropriety of such silence and an anxiety to find something to say, the effect is bad. But Anatole was dumb, swung his foot, and smilingly examined the princessвҖҷ hair. It was evident that he could be silent in this way for a very long time. вҖңIf anyone finds this silence inconvenient, let him talk, but I donвҖҷt want to,вҖқ he seemed to say. Besides this, in his behavior to women Anatole had a manner which particularly inspires in them curiosity, awe, and even loveвҒ вҖ”a supercilious consciousness of his own superiority. It was as if he said to them: вҖңI know you, I know you, but why should I bother about you? YouвҖҷd be only too glad, of course.вҖқ Perhaps he did not really think this when he met womenвҒ вҖ”even probably he did not, for in general he thought very littleвҒ вҖ”but his looks and manner gave that impression. The princess felt this, and as if wishing to show him that she did not even dare expect to interest him, she turned to his father. The conversation was general and animated, thanks to Princess LizaвҖҷs voice and little downy lip that lifted over her white teeth. She met Prince VasГӯli with that playful manner often employed by lively chatty people, and consisting in the assumption that between the person they so address and themselves there are some semiprivate, long-established jokes and amusing reminiscences, though no such reminiscences really existвҒ вҖ”just as none existed in this case. Prince VasГӯli readily adopted her tone and the little princess also drew Anatole, whom she hardly knew, into these amusing recollections of things that had never occurred. Mademoiselle Bourienne also shared them and even Princess MГЎrya felt herself pleasantly made to share in these merry reminiscences. вҖңHere at least we shall have the benefit of your company all to ourselves, dear prince,вҖқ said the little princess (of course, in French) to Prince VasГӯli. вҖңItвҖҷs not as at AnnetteвҖҷs39 receptions where you always ran away; you remember cette chГЁre Annette!вҖқ вҖңAh, but you wonвҖҷt talk politics to me like Annette!вҖқ вҖңAnd our little tea table?вҖқ вҖңOh, yes!вҖқ вҖңWhy is it you were never at AnnetteвҖҷs?вҖқ the little princess asked Anatole. вҖңAh, I know, I know,вҖқ she said with a sly glance, вҖңyour brother Ippolit told me about your goings on. Oh!вҖқ and she shook her finger at him, вҖңI have even heard of your doings in Paris!вҖқ вҖңAnd didnвҖҷt Ippolit tell you?вҖқ asked Prince VasГӯli, turning to his son and seizing the little princessвҖҷ arm as if she would have run away and he had just managed to catch her, вҖңdidnвҖҷt he tell you how he himself was pining for the dear princess, and how she showed him the door? Oh, she is a pearl among women, Princess,вҖқ he added, turning to Princess MГЎrya. When Paris was mentioned, Mademoiselle Bourienne for her part seized the opportunity of joining in the general current of recollections. She took the liberty of inquiring whether it was long since Anatole had left Paris and how he had liked that city. Anatole answered the Frenchwoman very readily and, looking at her with a smile, talked to her about her native land. When he saw the pretty little Bourienne, Anatole came to the conclusion that he would not find Bald Hills dull either. вҖңNot at all bad!вҖқ he thought, examining her, вҖңnot at all bad, that little companion! I hope she will bring her along with her when weвҖҷre married, la petite est gentille.вҖқ40 The old prince dressed leisurely in his study, frowning and considering what he was to do. The coming of these visitors annoyed him. вҖңWhat are Prince VasГӯli and that son of his to me? Prince VasГӯli is a shallow braggart and his son, no doubt, is a fine specimen,вҖқ he grumbled to himself. What angered him was that the coming of these visitors revived in his mind an unsettled question he always tried to stifle, one about which he always deceived himself. The question was whether he could ever bring himself to part from his daughter and give her to a husband. The prince never directly asked himself that question, knowing beforehand that he would have to answer it justly, and justice clashed not only with his feelings but with the very possibility of life. Life without Princess MГЎrya, little as he seemed to value her, was unthinkable to him. вҖңAnd why should she marry?вҖқ he thought. вҖңTo be unhappy for certain. ThereвҖҷs Liza, married to AndrГ©yвҒ вҖ”a better husband one would think could hardly be found nowadaysвҒ вҖ”but is she contented with her lot? And who would marry MГЎrya for love? Plain and awkward! TheyвҖҷll take her for her connections and wealth. Are there no women living unmarried, and even the happier for it?вҖқ So thought Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich while dressing, and yet the question he was always putting off demanded an immediate answer. Prince VasГӯli had brought his son with the evident intention of proposing, and today or tomorrow he would probably ask for an answer. His birth and position in society were not bad. вҖңWell, IвҖҷve nothing against it,вҖқ the prince said to himself, вҖңbut he must be worthy of her. And that is what we shall see.вҖқ вҖңThat is what we shall see! That is what we shall see!вҖқ he added aloud. He entered the drawing room with his usual alert step, glancing rapidly round the company. He noticed the change in the little princessвҖҷ dress, Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs ribbon, Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs unbecoming coiffure, Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs and AnatoleвҖҷs smiles, and the loneliness of his daughter amid the general conversation. вҖңGot herself up like a fool!вҖқ he thought, looking irritably at her. вҖңShe is shameless, and he ignores her!вҖқ He went straight up to Prince VasГӯli. вҖңWell! How dвҖҷye do? How dвҖҷye do? Glad to see you!вҖқ вҖңFriendship laughs at distance,вҖқ began Prince VasГӯli in his usual rapid, self-confident, familiar tone. вҖңHere is my second son; please love and befriend him.вҖқ Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich surveyed Anatole. вҖңFine young fellow! Fine young fellow!вҖқ he said. вҖңWell, come and kiss me,вҖқ and he offered his cheek. Anatole kissed the old man, and looked at him with curiosity and perfect composure, waiting for a display of the eccentricities his father had told him to expect. Prince BolkГіnski sat down in his usual place in the corner of the sofa and, drawing up an armchair for Prince VasГӯli, pointed to it and began questioning him about political affairs and news. He seemed to listen attentively to what Prince VasГӯli said, but kept glancing at Princess MГЎrya. вҖңAnd so they are writing from Potsdam already?вҖқ he said, repeating Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs last words. Then rising, he suddenly went up to his daughter. вҖңIs it for visitors youвҖҷve got yourself up like that, eh?вҖқ said he. вҖңFine, very fine! You have done up your hair in this new way for the visitors, and before the visitors I tell you that in future you are never to dare to change your way of dress without my consent.вҖқ вҖңIt was my fault, mon pГЁre,вҖқ interceded the little princess, with a blush. вҖңYou must do as you please,вҖқ said Prince BolkГіnski, bowing to his daughter-in-law, вҖңbut she need not make a fool of herself, sheвҖҷs plain enough as it is.вҖқ And he sat down again, paying no more attention to his daughter, who was reduced to tears. вҖңOn the contrary, that coiffure suits the princess very well,вҖқ said Prince VasГӯli. вҖңNow you, young prince, whatвҖҷs your name?вҖқ said Prince BolkГіnski, turning to Anatole, вҖңcome here, let us talk and get acquainted.вҖқ вҖңNow the fun begins,вҖқ thought Anatole, sitting down with a smile beside the old prince. вҖңWell, my dear boy, I hear youвҖҷve been educated abroad, not taught to read and write by the deacon, like your father and me. Now tell me, my dear boy, are you serving in the Horse Guards?вҖқ asked the old man, scrutinizing Anatole closely and intently. вҖңNo, I have been transferred to the line,вҖқ said Anatole, hardly able to restrain his laughter. вҖңAh! ThatвҖҷs a good thing. So, my dear boy, you wish to serve the Tsar and the country? It is wartime. Such a fine fellow must serve. Well, are you off to the front?вҖқ вҖңNo, Prince, our regiment has gone to the front, but I am attachedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ what is it I am attached to, Papa?вҖқ said Anatole, turning to his father with a laugh. вҖңA splendid soldier, splendid! вҖҳWhat am I attached to!вҖҷ Ha, ha, ha!вҖқ laughed Prince BolkГіnski, and Anatole laughed still louder. Suddenly Prince BolkГіnski frowned. вҖңYou may go,вҖқ he said to Anatole. Anatole returned smiling to the ladies. вҖңAnd so youвҖҷve had him educated abroad, Prince VasГӯli, havenвҖҷt you?вҖқ said the old prince to Prince VasГӯli. вҖңI have done my best for him, and I can assure you the education there is much better than ours.вҖқ вҖңYes, everything is different nowadays, everything is changed. The ladвҖҷs a fine fellow, a fine fellow! Well, come with me now.вҖқ He took Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs arm and led him to his study. As soon as they were alone together, Prince VasГӯli announced his hopes and wishes to the old prince. вҖңWell, do you think I shall prevent her, that I canвҖҷt part from her?вҖқ said the old prince angrily. вҖңWhat an idea! IвҖҷm ready for it tomorrow! Only let me tell you, I want to know my son-in-law better. You know my principlesвҒ вҖ”everything aboveboard! I will ask her tomorrow in your presence; if she is willing, then he can stay on. He can stay and IвҖҷll see.вҖқ The old prince snorted. вҖңLet her marry, itвҖҷs all the same to me!вҖқ he screamed in the same piercing tone as when parting from his son. вҖңI will tell you frankly,вҖқ said Prince VasГӯli in the tone of a crafty man convinced of the futility of being cunning with so keen-sighted a companion. вҖңYou know, you see right through people. Anatole is no genius, but he is an honest, goodhearted lad; an excellent son or kinsman.вҖқ вҖңAll right, all right, weвҖҷll see!вҖқ As always happens when women lead lonely lives for any length of time without male society, on AnatoleвҖҷs appearance all the three women of Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evichвҖҷs household felt that their life had not been real till then. Their powers of reasoning, feeling, and observing immediately increased tenfold, and their life, which seemed to have been passed in darkness, was suddenly lit up by a new brightness, full of significance. Princess MГЎrya grew quite unconscious of her face and coiffure. The handsome open face of the man who might perhaps be her husband absorbed all her attention. He seemed to her kind, brave, determined, manly, and magnanimous. She felt convinced of that. Thousands of dreams of a future family life continually rose in her imagination. She drove them away and tried to conceal them. вҖңBut am I not too cold with him?вҖқ thought the princess. вҖңI try to be reserved because in the depth of my soul I feel too near to him already, but then he cannot know what I think of him and may imagine that I do not like him.вҖқ And Princess MГЎrya tried, but could not manage, to be cordial to her new guest. вҖңPoor girl, sheвҖҷs devilish ugly!вҖқ thought Anatole. Mademoiselle Bourienne, also roused to great excitement by AnatoleвҖҷs arrival, thought in another way. Of course, she, a handsome young woman without any definite position, without relations or even a country, did not intend to devote her life to serving Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich, to reading aloud to him and being friends with Princess MГЎrya. Mademoiselle Bourienne had long been waiting for a Russian prince who, able to appreciate at a glance her superiority to the plain, badly dressed, ungainly Russian princesses, would fall in love with her and carry her off; and here at last was a Russian prince. Mademoiselle Bourienne knew a story, heard from her aunt but finished in her own way, which she liked to repeat to herself. It was the story of a girl who had been seduced, and to whom her poor mother (sa pauvre mГЁre) appeared, and reproached her for yielding to a man without being married. Mademoiselle Bourienne was often touched to tears as in imagination she told this story to him, her seducer. And now he, a real Russian prince, had appeared. He would carry her away and then sa pauvre mГЁre would appear and he would marry her. So her future shaped itself in Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs head at the very time she was talking to Anatole about Paris. It was not calculation that guided her (she did not even for a moment consider what she should do), but all this had long been familiar to her, and now that Anatole had appeared it just grouped itself around him and she wished and tried to please him as much as possible. The little princess, like an old war horse that hears the trumpet, unconsciously and quite forgetting her condition, prepared for the familiar gallop of coquetry, without any ulterior motive or any struggle, but with naive and lighthearted gaiety. Although in female society Anatole usually assumed the role of a man tired of being run after by women, his vanity was flattered by the spectacle of his power over these three women. Besides that, he was beginning to feel for the pretty and provocative Mademoiselle Bourienne that passionate animal feeling which was apt to master him with great suddenness and prompt him to the coarsest and most reckless actions. After tea, the company went into the sitting room and Princess MГЎrya was asked to play on the clavichord. Anatole, laughing and in high spirits, came and leaned on his elbows, facing her and beside Mademoiselle Bourienne. Princess MГЎrya felt his look with a painfully joyous emotion. Her favorite sonata bore her into a most intimately poetic world and the look she felt upon her made that world still more poetic. But AnatoleвҖҷs expression, though his eyes were fixed on her, referred not to her but to the movements of Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs little foot, which he was then touching with his own under the clavichord. Mademoiselle Bourienne was also looking at Princess MГЎrya, and in her lovely eyes there was a look of fearful joy and hope that was also new to the princess. вҖңHow she loves me!вҖқ thought Princess MГЎrya. вҖңHow happy I am now, and how happy I may be with such a friend and such a husband! Husband? Can it be possible?вҖқ she thought, not daring to look at his face, but still feeling his eyes gazing at her. In the evening, after supper, when all were about to retire, Anatole kissed Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs hand. She did not know how she found the courage, but she looked straight into his handsome face as it came near to her shortsighted eyes. Turning from Princess MГЎrya he went up and kissed Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs hand. (This was not etiquette, but then he did everything so simply and with such assurance!) Mademoiselle Bourienne flushed, and gave the princess a frightened look. вҖңWhat delicacy!вҖқ thought the princess. вҖңIs it possible that AmГ©lieвҖқ (Mademoiselle Bourienne) вҖңthinks I could be jealous of her, and not value her pure affection and devotion to me?вҖқ She went up to her and kissed her warmly. Anatole went up to kiss the little princessвҖҷ hand. вҖңNo! No! No! When your father writes to tell me that you are behaving well I will give you my hand to kiss. Not till then!вҖқ she said. And smilingly raising a finger at him, she left the room. V V They all separated, but, except Anatole who fell asleep as soon as he got into bed, all kept awake a long time that night. вҖңIs he really to be my husband, this stranger who is so kindвҒ вҖ”yes, kind, that is the chief thing,вҖқ thought Princess MГЎrya; and fear, which she had seldom experienced, came upon her. She feared to look round, it seemed to her that someone was there standing behind the screen in the dark corner. And this someone was heвҒ вҖ”the devilвҒ вҖ”and he was also this man with the white forehead, black eyebrows, and red lips. She rang for her maid and asked her to sleep in her room. Mademoiselle Bourienne walked up and down the conservatory for a long time that evening, vainly expecting someone, now smiling at someone, now working herself up to tears with the imaginary words of her pauvre mГЁre rebuking her for her fall. The little princess grumbled to her maid that her bed was badly made. She could not lie either on her face or on her side. Every position was awkward and uncomfortable, and her burden oppressed her now more than ever because AnatoleвҖҷs presence had vividly recalled to her the time when she was not like that and when everything was light and gay. She sat in an armchair in her dressing jacket and nightcap and Katya, sleepy and disheveled, beat and turned the heavy feather bed for the third time, muttering to herself. вҖңI told you it was all lumps and holes!вҖқ the little princess repeated. вҖңI should be glad enough to fall asleep, so itвҖҷs not my fault!вҖқ and her voice quivered like that of a child about to cry. The old prince did not sleep either. TГӯkhon, half asleep, heard him pacing angrily about and snorting. The old prince felt as though he had been insulted through his daughter. The insult was the more pointed because it concerned not himself but another, his daughter, whom he loved more than himself. He kept telling himself that he would consider the whole matter and decide what was right and how he should act, but instead of that he only excited himself more and more. вҖңThe first man that turns upвҒ вҖ”she forgets her father and everything else, runs upstairs and does up her hair and wags her tail and is unlike herself! Glad to throw her father over! And she knew I should notice it. FrвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ frвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ fr! And donвҖҷt I see that that idiot had eyes only for BourienneвҒ вҖ”I shall have to get rid of her. And how is it she has not pride enough to see it? If she has no pride for herself she might at least have some for my sake! She must be shown that the blockhead thinks nothing of her and looks only at Bourienne. No, she has no prideвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but IвҖҷll let her see.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The old prince knew that if he told his daughter she was making a mistake and that Anatole meant to flirt with Mademoiselle Bourienne, Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs self-esteem would be wounded and his point (not to be parted from her) would be gained, so pacifying himself with this thought, he called TГӯkhon and began to undress. вҖңWhat devil brought them here?вҖқ thought he, while TГӯkhon was putting the nightshirt over his dried-up old body and gray-haired chest. вҖңI never invited them. They came to disturb my lifeвҒ вҖ”and there is not much of it left.вҖқ вҖңDevil take вҖҷem!вҖқ he muttered, while his head was still covered by the shirt. TГӯkhon knew his masterвҖҷs habit of sometimes thinking aloud, and therefore met with unaltered looks the angrily inquisitive expression of the face that emerged from the shirt. вҖңGone to bed?вҖқ asked the prince. TГӯkhon, like all good valets, instinctively knew the direction of his masterвҖҷs thoughts. He guessed that the question referred to Prince VasГӯli and his son. вҖңThey have gone to bed and put out their lights, your excellency.вҖқ вҖңNo goodвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ no goodвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said the prince rapidly, and thrusting his feet into his slippers and his arms into the sleeves of his dressing gown, he went to the couch on which he slept. Though no words had passed between Anatole and Mademoiselle Bourienne, they quite understood one another as to the first part of their romance, up to the appearance of the pauvre mГЁre; they understood that they had much to say to one another in private and so they had been seeking an opportunity since morning to meet one another alone. When Princess MГЎrya went to her fatherвҖҷs room at the usual hour, Mademoiselle Bourienne and Anatole met in the conservatory. Princess MГЎrya went to the door of the study with special trepidation. It seemed to her that not only did everybody know that her fate would be decided that day, but that they also knew what she thought about it. She read this in TГӯkhonвҖҷs face and in that of Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs valet, who made her a low bow when she met him in the corridor carrying hot water. The old prince was very affectionate and careful in his treatment of his daughter that morning. Princess MГЎrya well knew this painstaking expression of her fatherвҖҷs. His face wore that expression when his dry hands clenched with vexation at her not understanding a sum in arithmetic, when rising from his chair he would walk away from her, repeating in a low voice the same words several times over. He came to the point at once, treating her ceremoniously. вҖңI have had a proposition made me concerning you,вҖқ he said with an unnatural smile. вҖңI expect you have guessed that Prince VasГӯli has not come and brought his pupil with himвҖқ (for some reason Prince BolkГіnski referred to Anatole as a вҖңpupilвҖқ) вҖңfor the sake of my beautiful eyes. Last night a proposition was made me on your account and, as you know my principles, I refer it to you.вҖқ вҖңHow am I to understand you, mon pГЁre?вҖқ said the princess, growing pale and then blushing. вҖңHow understand me!вҖқ cried her father angrily. вҖңPrince VasГӯli finds you to his taste as a daughter-in-law and makes a proposal to you on his pupilвҖҷs behalf. ThatвҖҷs how itвҖҷs to be understood! вҖҳHow understand itвҖҷ!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And I ask you!вҖқ вҖңI do not know what you think, Father,вҖқ whispered the princess. вҖңI? I? What of me? Leave me out of the question. IвҖҷm not going to get married. What about you? ThatвҖҷs what I want to know.вҖқ The princess saw that her father regarded the matter with disapproval, but at that moment the thought occurred to her that her fate would be decided now or never. She lowered her eyes so as not to see the gaze under which she felt that she could not think, but would only be able to submit from habit, and she said: вҖңI wish only to do your will, but if I had to express my own desireвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ She had no time to finish. The old prince interrupted her. вҖңThatвҖҷs admirable!вҖқ he shouted. вҖңHe will take you with your dowry and take Mademoiselle Bourienne into the bargain. SheвҖҷll be the wife, while youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The prince stopped. He saw the effect these words had produced on his daughter. She lowered her head and was ready to burst into tears. вҖңNow then, now then, IвҖҷm only joking!вҖқ he said. вҖңRemember this, Princess, I hold to the principle that a maiden has a full right to choose. I give you freedom. Only remember that your lifeвҖҷs happiness depends on your decision. Never mind me!вҖқ вҖңBut I do not know, Father!вҖқ вҖңThereвҖҷs no need to talk! He receives his orders and will marry you or anybody; but you are free to choose.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Go to your room, think it over, and come back in an hour and tell me in his presence: yes or no. I know you will pray over it. Well, pray if you like, but you had better think it over. Go! Yes or no, yes or no, yes or no!вҖқ he still shouted when the princess, as if lost in a fog, had already staggered out of the study. Her fate was decided and happily decided. But what her father had said about Mademoiselle Bourienne was dreadful. It was untrue to be sure, but still it was terrible, and she could not help thinking of it. She was going straight on through the conservatory, neither seeing nor hearing anything, when suddenly the well-known whispering of Mademoiselle Bourienne aroused her. She raised her eyes, and two steps away saw Anatole embracing the Frenchwoman and whispering something to her. With a horrified expression on his handsome face, Anatole looked at Princess MГЎrya, but did not at once take his arm from the waist of Mademoiselle Bourienne who had not yet seen her. вҖңWhoвҖҷs that? Why? Wait a moment!вҖқ AnatoleвҖҷs face seemed to say. Princess MГЎrya looked at them in silence. She could not understand it. At last Mademoiselle Bourienne gave a scream and ran away. Anatole bowed to Princess MГЎrya with a gay smile, as if inviting her to join in a laugh at this strange incident, and then shrugging his shoulders went to the door that led to his own apartments. An hour later, TГӯkhon came to call Princess MГЎrya to the old prince; he added that Prince VasГӯli was also there. When TГӯkhon came to her Princess MГЎrya was sitting on the sofa in her room, holding the weeping Mademoiselle Bourienne in her arms and gently stroking her hair. The princessвҖҷ beautiful eyes with all their former calm radiance were looking with tender affection and pity at Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs pretty face. вҖңNo, Princess, I have lost your affection forever!вҖқ said Mademoiselle Bourienne. вҖңWhy? I love you more than ever,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya, вҖңand I will try to do all I can for your happiness.вҖқ вҖңBut you despise me. You who are so pure can never understand being so carried away by passion. Oh, only my poor motherвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI quite understand,вҖқ answered Princess MГЎrya, with a sad smile. вҖңCalm yourself, my dear. I will go to my father,вҖқ she said, and went out. Prince VasГӯli, with one leg thrown high over the other and a snuffbox in his hand, was sitting there with a smile of deep emotion on his face, as if stirred to his heartвҖҷs core and himself regretting and laughing at his own sensibility, when Princess MГЎrya entered. He hurriedly took a pinch of snuff. вҖңAh, my dear, my dear!вҖқ he began, rising and taking her by both hands. Then, sighing, he added: вҖңMy sonвҖҷs fate is in your hands. Decide, my dear, good, gentle Marie, whom I have always loved as a daughter!вҖқ He drew back and a real tear appeared in his eye. вҖңFrвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ frвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ snorted Prince BolkГіnski. вҖңThe prince is making a proposition to you in his pupilвҖҷsвҒ вҖ”I mean, his sonвҖҷsвҒ вҖ”name. Do you wish or not to be Prince Anatole KurГЎginвҖҷs wife? Reply: yes or no,вҖқ he shouted, вҖңand then I shall reserve the right to state my opinion also. Yes, my opinion, and only my opinion,вҖқ added Prince BolkГіnski, turning to Prince VasГӯli and answering his imploring look. вҖңYes, or no?вҖқ вҖңMy desire is never to leave you, Father, never to separate my life from yours. I donвҖҷt wish to marry,вҖқ she answered positively, glancing at Prince VasГӯli and at her father with her beautiful eyes. вҖңHumbug! Nonsense! Humbug, humbug, humbug!вҖқ cried Prince BolkГіnski, frowning and taking his daughterвҖҷs hand; he did not kiss her, but only bending his forehead to hers just touched it, and pressed her hand so that she winced and uttered a cry. Prince VasГӯli rose. вҖңMy dear, I must tell you that this is a moment I shall never, never forget. But, my dear, will you not give us a little hope of touching this heart, so kind and generous? Say вҖҳperhapsвҖҷвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The future is so long. Say вҖҳperhaps.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ вҖңPrince, what I have said is all there is in my heart. I thank you for the honor, but I shall never be your sonвҖҷs wife.вҖқ вҖңWell, so thatвҖҷs finished, my dear fellow! I am very glad to have seen you. Very glad! Go back to your rooms, Princess. Go!вҖқ said the old prince. вҖңVery, very glad to have seen you,вҖқ repeated he, embracing Prince VasГӯli. вҖңMy vocation is a different one,вҖқ thought Princess MГЎrya. вҖңMy vocation is to be happy with another kind of happiness, the happiness of love and self-sacrifice. And cost what it may, I will arrange poor AmГ©lieвҖҷs happiness, she loves him so passionately, and so passionately repents. I will do all I can to arrange the match between them.
