If he is not rich I will give her the means; I will ask my father and AndrГ©y. I shall be so happy when she is his wife. She is so unfortunate, a stranger, alone, helpless! And, oh God, how passionately she must love him if she could so far forget herself! Perhaps I might have done the same!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ thought Princess MГЎrya. VI VI It was long since the RostГіvs had news of NikolГәshka. Not till midwinter was the count at last handed a letter addressed in his sonвҖҷs handwriting. On receiving it, he ran on tiptoe to his study in alarm and haste, trying to escape notice, closed the door, and began to read the letter. Anna MikhГЎylovna, who always knew everything that passed in the house, on hearing of the arrival of the letter went softly into the room and found the count with it in his hand, sobbing and laughing at the same time. Anna MikhГЎylovna, though her circumstances had improved, was still living with the RostГіvs. вҖңMy dear friend?вҖқ said she, in a tone of pathetic inquiry, prepared to sympathize in any way. The count sobbed yet more. вҖңNikolГәshkaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ a letterвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ waвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ aвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ sвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ woundedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ my darling boyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the countessвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ promoted to be an officerвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ thank GodвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ How tell the little countess!вҖқ Anna MikhГЎylovna sat down beside him, with her own handkerchief wiped the tears from his eyes and from the letter, then having dried her own eyes she comforted the count, and decided that at dinner and till teatime she would prepare the countess, and after tea, with GodвҖҷs help, would inform her. At dinner Anna MikhГЎylovna talked the whole time about the war news and about NikolГәshka, twice asked when the last letter had been received from him, though she knew that already, and remarked that they might very likely be getting a letter from him that day. Each time that these hints began to make the countess anxious and she glanced uneasily at the count and at Anna MikhГЎylovna, the latter very adroitly turned the conversation to insignificant matters. NatГЎsha, who, of the whole family, was the most gifted with a capacity to feel any shades of intonation, look, and expression, pricked up her ears from the beginning of the meal and was certain that there was some secret between her father and Anna MikhГЎylovna, that it had something to do with her brother, and that Anna MikhГЎylovna was preparing them for it. Bold as she was, NatГЎsha, who knew how sensitive her mother was to anything relating to NikolГәshka, did not venture to ask any questions at dinner, but she was too excited to eat anything and kept wriggling about on her chair regardless of her governessвҖҷ remarks. After dinner, she rushed headlong after Anna MikhГЎylovna and, dashing at her, flung herself on her neck as soon as she overtook her in the sitting room. вҖңAuntie, darling, do tell me what it is!вҖқ вҖңNothing, my dear.вҖқ вҖңNo, dearest, sweet one, honey, I wonвҖҷt give upвҒ вҖ”I know you know something.вҖқ Anna MikhГЎylovna shook her head. вҖңYou are a little slyboots,вҖқ she said. вҖңA letter from NikГіlenka! IвҖҷm sure of it!вҖқ exclaimed NatГЎsha, reading confirmation in Anna MikhГЎylovnaвҖҷs face. вҖңBut for GodвҖҷs sake, be careful, you know how it may affect your mamma.вҖқ вҖңI will, I will, only tell me! You wonвҖҷt? Then I will go and tell at once.вҖқ Anna MikhГЎylovna, in a few words, told her the contents of the letter, on condition that she should tell no one. вҖңNo, on my true word of honor,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, crossing herself, вҖңI wonвҖҷt tell anyone!вҖқ and she ran off at once to SГіnya. вҖңNikГіlenkaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ woundedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ a letter,вҖқ she announced in gleeful triumph. вҖңNicolas!вҖқ was all SГіnya said, instantly turning white. NatГЎsha, seeing the impression the news of her brotherвҖҷs wound produced on SГіnya, felt for the first time the sorrowful side of the news. She rushed to SГіnya, hugged her, and began to cry. вҖңA little wound, but he has been made an officer; he is well now, he wrote himself,вҖқ said she through her tears. вҖңThere now! ItвҖҷs true that all you women are crybabies,вҖқ remarked PГ©tya, pacing the room with large, resolute strides. вҖңNow IвҖҷm very glad, very glad indeed, that my brother has distinguished himself so. You are all blubberers and understand nothing.вҖқ NatГЎsha smiled through her tears. вҖңYou havenвҖҷt read the letter?вҖқ asked SГіnya. вҖңNo, but she said that it was all over and that heвҖҷs now an officer.вҖқ вҖңThank God!вҖқ said SГіnya, crossing herself. вҖңBut perhaps she deceived you. Let us go to Mamma.вҖқ PГ©tya paced the room in silence for a time. вҖңIf IвҖҷd been in NikolГәshkaвҖҷs place I would have killed even more of those Frenchmen,вҖқ he said. вҖңWhat nasty brutes they are! IвҖҷd have killed so many that thereвҖҷd have been a heap of them.вҖқ вҖңHold your tongue, PГ©tya, what a goose you are!вҖқ вҖңIвҖҷm not a goose, but they are who cry about trifles,вҖқ said PГ©tya. вҖңDo you remember him?вҖқ NatГЎsha suddenly asked, after a momentвҖҷs silence. SГіnya smiled. вҖңDo I remember Nicolas?вҖқ вҖңNo, SГіnya, but do you remember so that you remember him perfectly, remember everything?вҖқ said NatГЎsha, with an expressive gesture, evidently wishing to give her words a very definite meaning. вҖңI remember NikГіlenka too, I remember him well,вҖқ she said. вҖңBut I donвҖҷt remember BorГӯs. I donвҖҷt remember him a bit.вҖқ вҖңWhat! You donвҖҷt remember BorГӯs?вҖқ asked SГіnya in surprise. вҖңItвҖҷs not that I donвҖҷt rememberвҒ вҖ”I know what he is like, but not as I remember NikГіlenka. HimвҒ вҖ”I just shut my eyes and remember, but BorГӯsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No!вҖқ (She shut her eyes.) вҖңNo! thereвҖҷs nothing at all.вҖқ вҖңOh, NatГЎsha!вҖқ said SГіnya, looking ecstatically and earnestly at her friend as if she did not consider her worthy to hear what she meant to say and as if she were saying it to someone else, with whom joking was out of the question, вҖңI am in love with your brother once for all and, whatever may happen to him or to me, shall never cease to love him as long as I live.вҖқ NatГЎsha looked at SГіnya with wondering and inquisitive eyes, and said nothing. She felt that SГіnya was speaking the truth, that there was such love as SГіnya was speaking of. But NatГЎsha had not yet felt anything like it. She believed it could be, but did not understand it. вҖңShall you write to him?вҖқ she asked. SГіnya became thoughtful. The question of how to write to Nicolas, and whether she ought to write, tormented her. Now that he was already an officer and a wounded hero, would it be right to remind him of herself and, as it might seem, of the obligations to her he had taken on himself? вҖңI donвҖҷt know. I think if he writes, I will write too,вҖқ she said, blushing. вҖңAnd you wonвҖҷt feel ashamed to write to him?вҖқ SГіnya smiled. вҖңNo.вҖқ вҖңAnd I should be ashamed to write to BorГӯs. IвҖҷm not going to.вҖқ вҖңWhy should you be ashamed?вҖқ вҖңWell, I donвҖҷt know. ItвҖҷs awkward and would make me ashamed.вҖқ вҖңAnd I know why sheвҖҷd be ashamed,вҖқ said PГ©tya, offended by NatГЎshaвҖҷs previous remark. вҖңItвҖҷs because she was in love with that fat one in spectaclesвҖқ (that was how PГ©tya described his namesake, the new Count BezГәkhov) вҖңand now sheвҖҷs in love with that singerвҖқ (he meant NatГЎshaвҖҷs Italian singing master), вҖңthatвҖҷs why sheвҖҷs ashamed!вҖқ вҖңPГ©tya, youвҖҷre stupid!вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңNot more stupid than you, madam,вҖқ said the nine-year-old PГ©tya, with the air of an old brigadier. The countess had been prepared by Anna MikhГЎylovnaвҖҷs hints at dinner. On retiring to her own room, she sat in an armchair, her eyes fixed on a miniature portrait of her son on the lid of a snuffbox, while the tears kept coming into her eyes. Anna MikhГЎylovna, with the letter, came on tiptoe to the countessвҖҷ door and paused. вҖңDonвҖҷt come in,вҖқ she said to the old count who was following her. вҖңCome later.вҖқ And she went in, closing the door behind her. The count put his ear to the keyhole and listened. At first he heard the sound of indifferent voices, then Anna MikhГЎylovnaвҖҷs voice alone in a long speech, then a cry, then silence, then both voices together with glad intonations, and then footsteps. Anna MikhГЎylovna opened the door. Her face wore the proud expression of a surgeon who has just performed a difficult operation and admits the public to appreciate his skill. вҖңIt is done!вҖқ she said to the count, pointing triumphantly to the countess, who sat holding in one hand the snuffbox with its portrait and in the other the letter, and pressing them alternately to her lips. When she saw the count, she stretched out her arms to him, embraced his bald head, over which she again looked at the letter and the portrait, and in order to press them again to her lips, she slightly pushed away the bald head. VГ©ra, NatГЎsha, SГіnya, and PГ©tya now entered the room, and the reading of the letter began. After a brief description of the campaign and the two battles in which he had taken part, and his promotion, NikolГәshka said that he kissed his fatherвҖҷs and motherвҖҷs hands asking for their blessing, and that he kissed VГ©ra, NatГЎsha, and PГ©tya. Besides that, he sent greetings to Monsieur Schelling, Madame Schoss, and his old nurse, and asked them to kiss for him вҖңdear SГіnya, whom he loved and thought of just the same as ever.вҖқ When she heard this SГіnya blushed so that tears came into her eyes and, unable to bear the looks turned upon her, ran away into the dancing hall, whirled round it at full speed with her dress puffed out like a balloon, and, flushed and smiling, plumped down on the floor. The countess was crying. вҖңWhy are you crying, Mamma?вҖқ asked VГ©ra. вҖңFrom all he says one should be glad and not cry.вҖқ This was quite true, but the count, the countess, and NatГЎsha looked at her reproachfully. вҖңAnd who is it she takes after?вҖқ thought the countess. NikolГәshkaвҖҷs letter was read over hundreds of times, and those who were considered worthy to hear it had to come to the countess, for she did not let it out of her hands. The tutors came, and the nurses, and MГӯtenka, and several acquaintances, and the countess reread the letter each time with fresh pleasure and each time discovered in it fresh proofs of NikolГәshkaвҖҷs virtues. How strange, how extraordinary, how joyful it seemed, that her son, the scarcely perceptible motion of whose tiny limbs she had felt twenty years ago within her, that son about whom she used to have quarrels with the too indulgent count, that son who had first learned to say вҖңpearвҖқ and then вҖңgranny,вҖқ that this son should now be away in a foreign land amid strange surroundings, a manly warrior doing some kind of manвҖҷs work of his own, without help or guidance. The universal experience of ages, showing that children do grow imperceptibly from the cradle to manhood, did not exist for the countess. Her sonвҖҷs growth toward manhood, at each of its stages, had seemed as extraordinary to her as if there had never existed the millions of human beings who grew up in the same way. As twenty years before, it seemed impossible that the little creature who lived somewhere under her heart would ever cry, suck her breast, and begin to speak, so now she could not believe that that little creature could be this strong, brave man, this model son and officer that, judging by this letter, he now was. вҖңWhat a style! How charmingly he describes!вҖқ said she, reading the descriptive part of the letter. вҖңAnd what a soul! Not a word about himself.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Not a word! About some DenГӯsov or other, though he himself, I dare say, is braver than any of them. He says nothing about his sufferings. What a heart! How like him it is! And how he has remembered everybody! Not forgetting anyone. I always said when he was only so highвҒ вҖ”I always saidвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ For more than a week preparations were being made, rough drafts of letters to NikolГәshka from all the household were written and copied out, while under the supervision of the countess and the solicitude of the count, money and all things necessary for the uniform and equipment of the newly commissioned officer were collected. Anna MikhГЎylovna, practical woman that she was, had even managed by favor with army authorities to secure advantageous means of communication for herself and her son. She had opportunities of sending her letters to the Grand Duke Constantine PГЎvlovich, who commanded the Guards. The RostГіvs supposed that вҖңThe Russian Guards, Abroad,вҖқ was quite a definite address, and that if a letter reached the Grand Duke in command of the Guards there was no reason why it should not reach the PГЎvlograd regiment, which was presumably somewhere in the same neighborhood. And so it was decided to send the letters and money by the Grand DukeвҖҷs courier to BorГӯs and BorГӯs was to forward them to NikolГәshka. The letters were from the old count, the countess, PГ©tya, VГ©ra, NatГЎsha, and SГіnya, and finally there were six thousand rubles for his outfit and various other things the old count sent to his son. VII VII On the twelfth of November, KutГәzovвҖҷs active army, in camp before OlmГјtz, was preparing to be reviewed next day by the two EmperorsвҒ вҖ”the Russian and the Austrian. The Guards, just arrived from Russia, spent the night ten miles from OlmГјtz and next morning were to come straight to the review, reaching the field at OlmГјtz by ten oвҖҷclock. That day NikolГЎy RostГіv received a letter from BorГӯs, telling him that the IsmГЎylov regiment was quartered for the night ten miles from OlmГјtz and that he wanted to see him as he had a letter and money for him. RostГіv was particularly in need of money now that the troops, after their active service, were stationed near OlmГјtz and the camp swarmed with well-provisioned sutlers and Austrian Jews offering all sorts of tempting wares. The PГЎvlograds held feast after feast, celebrating awards they had received for the campaign, and made expeditions to OlmГјtz to visit a certain Caroline the Hungarian, who had recently opened a restaurant there with girls as waitresses. RostГіv, who had just celebrated his promotion to a cornetcy and bought DenГӯsovвҖҷs horse, Bedouin, was in debt all round, to his comrades and the sutlers. On receiving BorГӯsвҖҷ letter he rode with a fellow officer to OlmГјtz, dined there, drank a bottle of wine, and then set off alone to the GuardsвҖҷ camp to find his old playmate. RostГіv had not yet had time to get his uniform. He had on a shabby cadet jacket, decorated with a soldierвҖҷs cross, equally shabby cadetвҖҷs riding breeches lined with worn leather, and an officerвҖҷs saber with a sword knot. The Don horse he was riding was one he had bought from a Cossack during the campaign, and he wore a crumpled hussar cap stuck jauntily back on one side of his head. As he rode up to the camp he thought how he would impress BorГӯs and all his comrades of the Guards by his appearanceвҒ вҖ”that of a fighting hussar who had been under fire. The Guards had made their whole march as if on a pleasure trip, parading their cleanliness and discipline. They had come by easy stages, their knapsacks conveyed on carts, and the Austrian authorities had provided excellent dinners for the officers at every halting place. The regiments had entered and left the town with their bands playing, and by the Grand DukeвҖҷs orders the men had marched all the way in step (a practice on which the Guards prided themselves), the officers on foot and at their proper posts. BorГӯs had been quartered, and had marched all the way, with Berg who was already in command of a company. Berg, who had obtained his captaincy during the campaign, had gained the confidence of his superiors by his promptitude and accuracy and had arranged his money matters very satisfactorily. BorГӯs, during the campaign, had made the acquaintance of many persons who might prove useful to him, and by a letter of recommendation he had brought from Pierre had become acquainted with Prince AndrГ©y BolkГіnski, through whom he hoped to obtain a post on the commander in chiefвҖҷs staff. Berg and BorГӯs, having rested after yesterdayвҖҷs march, were sitting, clean and neatly dressed, at a round table in the clean quarters allotted to them, playing chess. Berg held a smoking pipe between his knees. BorГӯs, in the accurate way characteristic of him, was building a little pyramid of chessmen with his delicate white fingers while awaiting BergвҖҷs move, and watched his opponentвҖҷs face, evidently thinking about the game as he always thought only of whatever he was engaged on. вҖңWell, how are you going to get out of that?вҖқ he remarked. вҖңWeвҖҷll try to,вҖқ replied Berg, touching a pawn and then removing his hand. At that moment the door opened. вҖңHere he is at last!вҖқ shouted RostГіv. вҖңAnd Berg too! Oh, you petisenfans, allay cushay dormir!вҖқ he exclaimed, imitating his Russian nurseвҖҷs French, at which he and BorГӯs used to laugh long ago. вҖңDear me, how you have changed!вҖқ BorГӯs rose to meet RostГіv, but in doing so did not omit to steady and replace some chessmen that were falling. He was about to embrace his friend, but NikolГЎy avoided him. With that peculiar feeling of youth, that dread of beaten tracks, and wish to express itself in a manner different from that of its elders which is often insincere, NikolГЎy wished to do something special on meeting his friend. He wanted to pinch him, push him, do anything but kiss himвҒ вҖ”a thing everybody did. But notwithstanding this, BorГӯs embraced him in a quiet, friendly way and kissed him three times. They had not met for nearly half a year and, being at the age when young men take their first steps on lifeвҖҷs road, each saw immense changes in the other, quite a new reflection of the society in which they had taken those first steps. Both had changed greatly since they last met and both were in a hurry to show the changes that had taken place in them. вҖңOh, you damned dandies! Clean and fresh as if youвҖҷd been to a fГӘte, not like us sinners of the line,вҖқ cried RostГіv, with martial swagger and with baritone notes in his voice, new to BorГӯs, pointing to his own mud-bespattered breeches. The German landlady, hearing RostГіvвҖҷs loud voice, popped her head in at the door. вҖңEh, is she pretty?вҖқ he asked with a wink. вҖңWhy do you shout so? YouвҖҷll frighten them!вҖқ said BorГӯs. вҖңI did not expect you today,вҖқ he added. вҖңI only sent you the note yesterday by BolkГіnskiвҒ вҖ”an adjutant of KutГәzovвҖҷs, whoвҖҷs a friend of mine. I did not think he would get it to you so quickly.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, how are you? Been under fire already?вҖқ asked BorГӯs. Without answering, RostГіv shook the soldierвҖҷs Cross of St.В George fastened to the cording of his uniform and, indicating a bandaged arm, glanced at Berg with a smile. вҖңAs you see,вҖқ he said. вҖңIndeed? Yes, yes!вҖқ said BorГӯs, with a smile. вҖңAnd we too have had a splendid march. You know, of course, that His Imperial Highness rode with our regiment all the time, so that we had every comfort and every advantage. What receptions we had in Poland! What dinners and balls! I canвҖҷt tell you. And the TsarГ©vich was very gracious to all our officers.вҖқ And the two friends told each other of their doings, the one of his hussar revels and life in the fighting line, the other of the pleasures and advantages of service under members of the Imperial family. вҖңOh, you Guards!вҖқ said RostГіv. вҖңI say, send for some wine.вҖқ BorГӯs made a grimace. вҖңIf you really want it,вҖқ said he. He went to his bed, drew a purse from under the clean pillow, and sent for wine. вҖңYes, and I have some money and a letter to give you,вҖқ he added. RostГіv took the letter and, throwing the money on the sofa, put both arms on the table and began to read. After reading a few lines, he glanced angrily at Berg, then, meeting his eyes, hid his face behind the letter. вҖңWell, theyвҖҷve sent you a tidy sum,вҖқ said Berg, eying the heavy purse that sank into the sofa. вҖңAs for us, Count, we get along on our pay. I can tell you for myselfвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI say, Berg, my dear fellow,вҖқ said RostГіv, вҖңwhen you get a letter from home and meet one of your own people whom you want to talk everything over with, and I happen to be there, IвҖҷll go at once, to be out of your way! Do go somewhere, anywhereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ to the devil!вҖқ he exclaimed, and immediately seizing him by the shoulder and looking amiably into his face, evidently wishing to soften the rudeness of his words, he added, вҖңDonвҖҷt be hurt, my dear fellow; you know I speak from my heart as to an old acquaintance.вҖқ вҖңOh, donвҖҷt mention it, Count! I quite understand,вҖқ said Berg, getting up and speaking in a muffled and guttural voice. вҖңGo across to our hosts: they invited you,вҖқ added BorГӯs. Berg put on the cleanest of coats, without a spot or speck of dust, stood before a looking glass and brushed the hair on his temples upwards, in the way affected by the Emperor Alexander, and, having assured himself from the way RostГіv looked at it that his coat had been noticed, left the room with a pleasant smile. вҖңOh dear, what a beast I am!вҖқ muttered RostГіv, as he read the letter. вҖңWhy?вҖқ вҖңOh, what a pig I am, not to have written and to have given them such a fright! Oh, what a pig I am!вҖқ he repeated, flushing suddenly. вҖңWell, have you sent GavrГӯl for some wine? All right letвҖҷs have some!вҖқ In the letter from his parents was enclosed a letter of recommendation to BagratiГіn which the old countess at Anna MikhГЎylovnaвҖҷs advice had obtained through an acquaintance and sent to her son, asking him to take it to its destination and make use of it. вҖңWhat nonsense! Much I need it!вҖқ said RostГіv, throwing the letter under the table. вҖңWhy have you thrown that away?вҖқ asked BorГӯs. вҖңIt is some letter of recommendationвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ what the devil do I want it for!вҖқ вҖңWhy вҖҳWhat the devilвҖҷ?вҖқ said BorГӯs, picking it up and reading the address. вҖңThis letter would be of great use to you.вҖқ вҖңI want nothing, and I wonвҖҷt be anyoneвҖҷs adjutant.вҖқ вҖңWhy not?вҖқ inquired BorГӯs. вҖңItвҖҷs a lackeyвҖҷs job!вҖқ вҖңYou are still the same dreamer, I see,вҖқ remarked BorГӯs, shaking his head. вҖңAnd youвҖҷre still the same diplomatist! But thatвҖҷs not the pointвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Come, how are you?вҖқ asked RostГіv. вҖңWell, as you see. So far everythingвҖҷs all right, but I confess I should much like to be an adjutant and not remain at the front.вҖқ вҖңWhy?вҖқ вҖңBecause when once a man starts on military service, he should try to make as successful a career of it as possible.вҖқ вҖңOh, thatвҖҷs it!вҖқ said RostГіv, evidently thinking of something else. He looked intently and inquiringly into his friendвҖҷs eyes, evidently trying in vain to find the answer to some question. Old GavrГӯl brought in the wine. вҖңShouldnвҖҷt we now send for Alphonse KГЎrlovich?вҖқ asked BorГӯs. вҖңHe would drink with you. I canвҖҷt.вҖқ вҖңWell, send for himвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and how do you get on with that German?вҖқ asked RostГіv, with a contemptuous smile. вҖңHe is a very, very nice, honest, and pleasant fellow,вҖқ answered BorГӯs. Again RostГіv looked intently into BorГӯsвҖҷ eyes and sighed. Berg returned, and over the bottle of wine conversation between the three officers became animated. The Guardsmen told RostГіv of their march and how they had been made much of in Russia, Poland, and abroad. They spoke of the sayings and doings of their commander, the Grand Duke, and told stories of his kindness and irascibility. Berg, as usual, kept silent when the subject did not relate to himself, but in connection with the stories of the Grand DukeвҖҷs quick temper he related with gusto how in Galicia he had managed to deal with the Grand Duke when the latter made a tour of the regiments and was annoyed at the irregularity of a movement. With a pleasant smile Berg related how the Grand Duke had ridden up to him in a violent passion, shouting: вҖңArnauts!вҖқ (вҖңArnautsвҖқ was the TsarГ©vichвҖҷs favorite expression when he was in a rage) and called for the company commander. вҖңWould you believe it, Count, I was not at all alarmed, because I knew I was right. Without boasting, you know, I may say that I know the Army Orders by heart and know the Regulations as well as I do the LordвҖҷs Prayer. So, Count, there never is any negligence in my company, and so my conscience was at ease. I came forward.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ (Berg stood up and showed how he presented himself, with his hand to his cap, and really it would have been difficult for a face to express greater respect and self-complacency than his did.) вҖңWell, he stormed at me, as the saying is, stormed and stormed and stormed! It was not a matter of life but rather of death, as the saying is. вҖҳAlbanians!вҖҷ and вҖҳdevils!вҖҷ and вҖҳTo Siberia!вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ said Berg with a sagacious smile. вҖңI knew I was in the right so I kept silent; was not that best, Count?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖҳHey, are you dumb?вҖҷ he shouted. Still I remained silent. And what do you think, Count? The next day it was not even mentioned in the Orders of the Day. ThatвҖҷs what keeping oneвҖҷs head means. ThatвҖҷs the way, Count,вҖқ said Berg, lighting his pipe and emitting rings of smoke. вҖңYes, that was fine,вҖқ said RostГіv, smiling. But BorГӯs noticed that he was preparing to make fun of Berg, and skillfully changed the subject. He asked him to tell them how and where he got his wound. This pleased RostГіv and he began talking about it, and as he went on became more and more animated. He told them of his SchГ¶n Grabern affair, just as those who have taken part in a battle generally do describe it, that is, as they would like it to have been, as they have heard it described by others, and as sounds well, but not at all as it really was. RostГіv was a truthful young man and would on no account have told a deliberate lie. He began his story meaning to tell everything just as it happened, but imperceptibly, involuntarily, and inevitably he lapsed into falsehood. If he had told the truth to his hearersвҒ вҖ”who like himself had often heard stories of attacks and had formed a definite idea of what an attack was and were expecting to hear just such a storyвҒ вҖ”they would either not have believed him or, still worse, would have thought that RostГіv was himself to blame since what generally happens to the narrators of cavalry attacks had not happened to him. He could not tell them simply that everyone went at a trot and that he fell off his horse and sprained his arm and then ran as hard as he could from a Frenchman into the wood. Besides, to tell everything as it really happened, it would have been necessary to make an effort of will to tell only what happened. It is very difficult to tell the truth, and young people are rarely capable of it. His hearers expected a story of how beside himself and all aflame with excitement, he had flown like a storm at the square, cut his way in, slashed right and left, how his saber had tasted flesh and he had fallen exhausted, and so on. And so he told them all that. In the middle of his story, just as he was saying: вҖңYou cannot imagine what a strange frenzy one experiences during an attack,вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y, whom BorГӯs was expecting, entered the room. Prince AndrГ©y, who liked to help young men, was flattered by being asked for his assistance and being well disposed toward BorГӯs, who had managed to please him the day before, he wished to do what the young man wanted. Having been sent with papers from KutГәzov to the TsarГ©vich, he looked in on BorГӯs, hoping to find him alone. When he came in and saw an hussar of the line recounting his military exploits (Prince AndrГ©y could not endure that sort of man), he gave BorГӯs a pleasant smile, frowned as with half-closed eyes he looked at RostГіv, bowed slightly and wearily, and sat down languidly on the sofa: he felt it unpleasant to have dropped in on bad company. RostГіv flushed up on noticing this, but he did not care, this was a mere stranger. Glancing, however, at BorГӯs, he saw that he too seemed ashamed of the hussar of the line. In spite of Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs disagreeable, ironical tone, in spite of the contempt with which RostГіv, from his fighting army point of view, regarded all these little adjutants on the staff of whom the newcomer was evidently one, RostГіv felt confused, blushed, and became silent. BorГӯs inquired what news there might be on the staff, and what, without indiscretion, one might ask about our plans. вҖңWe shall probably advance,вҖқ replied BolkГіnski, evidently reluctant to say more in the presence of a stranger. Berg took the opportunity to ask, with great politeness, whether, as was rumored, the allowance of forage money to captains of companies would be doubled. To this Prince AndrГ©y answered with a smile that he could give no opinion on such an important government order, and Berg laughed gaily. вҖңAs to your business,вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y continued, addressing BorГӯs, вҖңwe will talk of it laterвҖқ (and he looked round at RostГіv). вҖңCome to me after the review and we will do what is possible.вҖқ And, having glanced round the room, Prince AndrГ©y turned to RostГіv, whose state of unconquerable childish embarrassment now changing to anger he did not condescend to notice, and said: вҖңI think you were talking of the SchГ¶n Grabern affair? Were you there?вҖқ вҖңI was there,вҖқ said RostГіv angrily, as if intending to insult the aide-de-camp. BolkГіnski noticed the hussarвҖҷs state of mind, and it amused him. With a slightly contemptuous smile, he said: вҖңYes, there are many stories now told about that affair!вҖқ вҖңYes, stories!вҖқ repeated RostГіv loudly, looking with eyes suddenly grown furious, now at BorГӯs, now at BolkГіnski. вҖңYes, many stories! But our stories are the stories of men who have been under the enemyвҖҷs fire! Our stories have some weight, not like the stories of those fellows on the staff who get rewards without doing anything!вҖқ вҖңOf whom you imagine me to be one?вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, with a quiet and particularly amiable smile. A strange feeling of exasperation and yet of respect for this manвҖҷs self-possession mingled at that moment in RostГіvвҖҷs soul. вҖңI am not talking about you,вҖқ he said, вҖңI donвҖҷt know you and, frankly, I donвҖҷt want to. I am speaking of the staff in general.вҖқ вҖңAnd I will tell you this,вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y interrupted in a tone of quiet authority, вҖңyou wish to insult me, and I am ready to agree with you that it would be very easy to do so if you havenвҖҷt sufficient self-respect, but admit that the time and place are very badly chosen. In a day or two we shall all have to take part in a greater and more serious duel, and besides, DrubetskГіy, who says he is an old friend of yours, is not at all to blame that my face has the misfortune to displease you. However,вҖқ he added rising, вҖңyou know my name and where to find me, but donвҖҷt forget that I do not regard either myself or you as having been at all insulted, and as a man older than you, my advice is to let the matter drop. Well then, on Friday after the review I shall expect you, DrubetskГіy. Au revoir!вҖқ exclaimed Prince AndrГ©y, and with a bow to them both he went out. Only when Prince AndrГ©y was gone did RostГіv think of what he ought to have said. And he was still more angry at having omitted to say it. He ordered his horse at once and, coldly taking leave of BorГӯs, rode home. Should he go to headquarters next day and challenge that affected adjutant, or really let the matter drop, was the question that worried him all the way. He thought angrily of the pleasure he would have at seeing the fright of that small and frail but proud man when covered by his pistol, and then he felt with surprise that of all the men he knew there was none he would so much like to have for a friend as that very adjutant whom he so hated. VIII VIII The day after RostГіv had been to see BorГӯs, a review was held of the Austrian and Russian troops, both those freshly arrived from Russia and those who had been campaigning under KutГәzov. The two Emperors, the Russian with his heir the TsarГ©vich, and the Austrian with the Archduke, inspected the allied army of eighty thousand men. From early morning the smart clean troops were on the move, forming up on the field before the fortress. Now thousands of feet and bayonets moved and halted at the officersвҖҷ command, turned with banners flying, formed up at intervals, and wheeled round other similar masses of infantry in different uniforms; now was heard the rhythmic beat of hoofs and the jingling of showy cavalry in blue, red, and green braided uniforms, with smartly dressed bandsmen in front mounted on black, roan, or gray horses; then again, spreading out with the brazen clatter of the polished shining cannon that quivered on the gun carriages and with the smell of linstocks, came the artillery which crawled between the infantry and cavalry and took up its appointed position. Not only the generals in full parade uniforms, with their thin or thick waists drawn in to the utmost, their red necks squeezed into their stiff collars, and wearing scarves and all their decorations, not only the elegant, pomaded officers, but every soldier with his freshly washed and shaven face and his weapons clean and polished to the utmost, and every horse groomed till its coat shone like satin and every hair of its wetted mane lay smoothвҒ вҖ”felt that no small matter was happening, but an important and solemn affair. Every general and every soldier was conscious of his own insignificance, aware of being but a drop in that ocean of men, and yet at the same time was conscious of his strength as a part of that enormous whole. From early morning strenuous activities and efforts had begun and by ten oвҖҷclock all had been brought into due order. The ranks were drawn up on the vast field. The whole army was extended in three lines: the cavalry in front, behind it the artillery, and behind that again the infantry. A space like a street was left between each two lines of troops. The three parts of that army were sharply distinguished: KutГәzovвҖҷs fighting army (with the PГЎvlograds on the right flank of the front); those recently arrived from Russia, both Guards and regiments of the line; and the Austrian troops. But they all stood in the same lines, under one command, and in a like order. Like wind over leaves ran an excited whisper: вҖңTheyвҖҷre coming! TheyвҖҷre coming!вҖқ Alarmed voices were heard, and a stir of final preparation swept over all the troops. From the direction of OlmГјtz in front of them, a group was seen approaching. And at that moment, though the day was still, a light gust of wind blowing over the army slightly stirred the streamers on the lances and the unfolded standards fluttered against their staffs. It looked as if by that slight motion the army itself was expressing its joy at the approach of the Emperors. One voice was heard shouting: вҖңEyes front!вҖқ Then, like the crowing of cocks at sunrise, this was repeated by others from various sides and all became silent. In the deathlike stillness only the tramp of horses was heard. This was the EmperorsвҖҷ suites. The Emperors rode up to the flank, and the trumpets of the first cavalry regiment played the general march. It seemed as though not the trumpeters were playing, but as if the army itself, rejoicing at the EmperorsвҖҷ approach, had naturally burst into music. Amid these sounds, only the youthful kindly voice of the Emperor Alexander was clearly heard. He gave the words of greeting, and the first regiment roared вҖңHurrah!вҖқ so deafeningly, continuously, and joyfully that the men themselves were awed by their multitude and the immensity of the power they constituted. RostГіv, standing in the front lines of KutГәzovвҖҷs army which the Tsar approached first, experienced the same feeling as every other man in that army: a feeling of self-forgetfulness, a proud consciousness of might, and a passionate attraction to him who was the cause of this triumph. He felt that at a single word from that man all this vast mass (and he himself an insignificant atom in it) would go through fire and water, commit crime, die, or perform deeds of highest heroism, and so he could not but tremble and his heart stand still at the imminence of that word. вҖңHurrah! Hurrah! Hurrah!вҖқ thundered from all sides, one regiment after another greeting the Tsar with the strains of the march, and then вҖңHurrah!вҖқвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Then the general march, and again вҖңHurrah! Hurrah!вҖқ growing ever stronger and fuller and merging into a deafening roar. Till the Tsar reached it, each regiment in its silence and immobility seemed like a lifeless body, but as soon as he came up it became alive, its thunder joining the roar of the whole line along which he had already passed. Through the terrible and deafening roar of those voices, amid the square masses of troops standing motionless as if turned to stone, hundreds of riders composing the suites moved carelessly but symmetrically and above all freely, and in front of them two menвҒ вҖ”the Emperors. Upon them the undivided, tensely passionate attention of that whole mass of men was concentrated. The handsome young Emperor Alexander, in the uniform of the Horse Guards, wearing a cocked hat with its peaks front and back, with his pleasant face and resonant though not loud voice, attracted everyoneвҖҷs attention. RostГіv was not far from the trumpeters, and with his keen sight had recognized the Tsar and watched his approach. When he was within twenty paces, and NikolГЎy could clearly distinguish every detail of his handsome, happy young face, he experienced a feeling of tenderness and ecstasy such as he had never before known. Every trait and every movement of the TsarвҖҷs seemed to him enchanting. Stopping in front of the PГЎvlograds, the Tsar said something in French to the Austrian Emperor and smiled. Seeing that smile, RostГіv involuntarily smiled himself and felt a still stronger flow of love for his sovereign. He longed to show that love in some way and knowing that this was impossible was ready to cry. The Tsar called the colonel of the regiment and said a few words to him. вҖңOh God, what would happen to me if the Emperor spoke to me?вҖқ thought RostГіv. вҖңI should die of happiness!вҖқ The Tsar addressed the officers also: вҖңI thank you all, gentlemen, I thank you with my whole heart.вҖқ To RostГіv every word sounded like a voice from heaven. How gladly would he have died at once for his Tsar! вҖңYou have earned the St.В GeorgeвҖҷs standards and will be worthy of them.вҖқ вҖңOh, to die, to die for him,вҖқ thought RostГіv. The Tsar said something more which RostГіv did not hear, and the soldiers, straining their lungs, shouted вҖңHurrah!вҖқ RostГіv too, bending over his saddle, shouted вҖңHurrah!вҖқ with all his might, feeling that he would like to injure himself by that shout, if only to express his rapture fully. The Tsar stopped a few minutes in front of the hussars as if undecided. вҖңHow can the Emperor be undecided?вҖқ thought RostГіv, but then even this indecision appeared to him majestic and enchanting, like everything else the Tsar did. That hesitation lasted only an instant. The TsarвҖҷs foot, in the narrow pointed boot then fashionable, touched the groin of the bobtailed bay mare he rode, his hand in a white glove gathered up the reins, and he moved off accompanied by an irregularly swaying sea of aides-de-camp. Farther and farther he rode away, stopping at other regiments, till at last only his white plumes were visible to RostГіv from amid the suites that surrounded the Emperors. Among the gentlemen of the suite, RostГіv noticed BolkГіnski, sitting his horse indolently and carelessly. RostГіv recalled their quarrel of yesterday and the question presented itself whether he ought or ought not to challenge BolkГіnski. вҖңOf course not!вҖқ he now thought. вҖңIs it worth thinking or speaking of it at such a moment? At a time of such love, such rapture, and such self-sacrifice, what do any of our quarrels and affronts matter? I love and forgive everybody now.вҖқ When the Emperor had passed nearly all the regiments, the troops began a ceremonial march past him, and RostГіv on Bedouin, recently purchased from DenГӯsov, rode past too, at the rear of his squadronвҒ вҖ”that is, alone and in full view of the Emperor. Before he reached him, RostГіv, who was a splendid horseman, spurred Bedouin twice and successfully put him to the showy trot in which the animal went when excited. Bending his foaming muzzle to his chest, his tail extended, Bedouin, as if also conscious of the EmperorвҖҷs eye upon him, passed splendidly, lifting his feet with a high and graceful action, as if flying through the air without touching the ground. RostГіv himself, his legs well back and his stomach drawn in and feeling himself one with his horse, rode past the Emperor with a frowning but blissful face вҖңlike a vewy devil,вҖқ as DenГӯsov expressed it. вҖңFine fellows, the PГЎvlograds!вҖқ remarked the Emperor. вҖңMy God, how happy I should be if he ordered me to leap into the fire this instant!вҖқ thought RostГіv. When the review was over, the newly arrived officers, and also KutГәzovвҖҷs, collected in groups and began to talk about the awards, about the Austrians and their uniforms, about their lines, about Bonaparte, and how badly the latter would fare now, especially if the Essen corps arrived and Prussia took our side. But the talk in every group was chiefly about the Emperor Alexander. His every word and movement was described with ecstasy. They all had but one wish: to advance as soon as possible against the enemy under the EmperorвҖҷs command. Commanded by the Emperor himself they could not fail to vanquish anyone, be it whom it might: so thought RostГіv and most of the officers after the review. All were then more confident of victory than the winning of two battles would have made them. IX IX The day after the review, BorГӯs DrubetskГіy, in his best uniform and with his comrade BergвҖҷs best wishes for success, rode to OlmГјtz to see BolkГіnski, wishing to profit by his friendliness and obtain for himself the best post he couldвҒ вҖ”preferably that of adjutant to some important personage, a position in the army which seemed to him most attractive. вҖңIt is all very well for RostГіv, whose father sends him ten thousand rubles at a time, to talk about not wishing to cringe to anybody and not be anyoneвҖҷs lackey, but I who have nothing but my brains have to make a career and must not miss opportunities, but must avail myself of them!вҖқ he reflected. He did not find Prince AndrГ©y in OlmГјtz that day, but the appearance of the town where the headquarters and the diplomatic corps were stationed and the two Emperors were living with their suites, households, and courts only strengthened his desire to belong to that higher world. He knew no one, and despite his smart GuardsmanвҖҷs uniform, all these exalted personages passing in the streets in their elegant carriages with their plumes, ribbons, and medals, both courtiers and military men, seemed so immeasurably above him, an insignificant officer of the Guards, that they not only did not wish to, but simply could not, be aware of his existence. At the quarters of the commander in chief, KutГәzov, where he inquired for BolkГіnski, all the adjutants and even the orderlies looked at him as if they wished to impress on him that a great many officers like him were always coming there and that everybody was heartily sick of them. In spite of this, or rather because of it, next day, November 15, after dinner he again went to OlmГјtz and, entering the house occupied by KutГәzov, asked for BolkГіnski. Prince AndrГ©y was in and BorГӯs was shown into a large hall probably formerly used for dancing, but in which five beds now stood, and furniture of various kinds: a table, chairs, and a clavichord. One adjutant, nearest the door, was sitting at the table in a Persian dressing gown, writing. Another, the red, stout NesvГӯtski, lay on a bed with his arms under his head, laughing with an officer who had sat down beside him. A third was playing a Viennese waltz on the clavichord, while a fourth, lying on the clavichord, sang the tune. BolkГіnski was not there. None of these gentlemen changed his position on seeing BorГӯs. The one who was writing and whom BorГӯs addressed turned round crossly and told him BolkГіnski was on duty and that he should go through the door on the left into the reception room if he wished to see him. BorГӯs thanked him and went to the reception room, where he found some ten officers and generals. When he entered, Prince AndrГ©y, his eyes drooping contemptuously (with that peculiar expression of polite weariness which plainly says, вҖңIf it were not my duty I would not talk to you for a momentвҖқ), was listening to an old Russian general with decorations, who stood very erect, almost on tiptoe, with a soldierвҖҷs obsequious expression on his purple face, reporting something. вҖңVery well, then, be so good as to wait,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y to the general, in Russian, speaking with the French intonation he affected when he wished to speak contemptuously, and noticing BorГӯs, Prince AndrГ©y, paying no more heed to the general who ran after him imploring him to hear something more, nodded and turned to him with a cheerful smile. At that moment BorГӯs clearly realized what he had before surmised, that in the army, besides the subordination and discipline prescribed in the military code, which he and the others knew in the regiment, there was another, more important, subordination, which made this tight-laced, purple-faced general wait respectfully while Captain Prince AndrГ©y, for his own pleasure, chose to chat with Lieutenant DrubetskГіy. More than ever was BorГӯs resolved to serve in future not according to the written code, but under this unwritten law. He felt now that merely by having been recommended to Prince AndrГ©y he had already risen above the general who at the front had the power to annihilate him, a lieutenant of the Guards. Prince AndrГ©y came up to him and took his hand. вҖңI am very sorry you did not find me in yesterday. I was fussing about with Germans all day. We went with Weyrother to survey the dispositions. When Germans start being accurate, thereвҖҷs no end to it!вҖқ BorГӯs smiled, as if he understood what Prince AndrГ©y was alluding to as something generally known. But it was the first time he had heard WeyrotherвҖҷs name, or even the term вҖңdispositions.вҖқ вҖңWell, my dear fellow, so you still want to be an adjutant? I have been thinking about you.вҖқ вҖңYes, I was thinkingвҖқвҒ вҖ”for some reason BorГӯs could not help blushingвҒ вҖ”вҖңof asking the commander in chief. He has had a letter from Prince KurГЎgin about me. I only wanted to ask because I fear the Guards wonвҖҷt be in action,вҖқ he added as if in apology. вҖңAll right, all right. WeвҖҷll talk it over,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңOnly let me report this gentlemanвҖҷs business, and I shall be at your disposal.вҖқ While Prince AndrГ©y went to report about the purple-faced general, that gentlemanвҒ вҖ”evidently not sharing BorГӯsвҖҷ conception of the advantages of the unwritten code of subordinationвҒ вҖ”looked so fixedly at the presumptuous lieutenant who had prevented his finishing what he had to say to the adjutant that BorГӯs felt uncomfortable. He turned away and waited impatiently for Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs return from the commander in chiefвҖҷs room. вҖңYou see, my dear fellow, I have been thinking about you,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y when they had gone into the large room where the clavichord was. вҖңItвҖҷs no use your going to the commander in chief. He would say a lot of pleasant things, ask you to dinnerвҖқ (вҖңThat would not be bad as regards the unwritten code,вҖқ thought BorГӯs), вҖңbut nothing more would come of it. There will soon be a battalion of us aides-de-camp and adjutants! But this is what weвҖҷll do: I have a good friend, an adjutant general and an excellent fellow, Prince DolgorГәkov; and though you may not know it, the fact is that now KutГәzov with his staff and all of us count for nothing. Everything is now centered round the Emperor. So we will go to DolgorГәkov; I have to go there anyhow and I have already spoken to him about you. We shall see whether he cannot attach you to himself or find a place for you somewhere nearer the sun.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y always became specially keen when he had to guide a young man and help him to worldly success. Under cover of obtaining help of this kind for another, which from pride he would never accept for himself, he kept in touch with the circle which confers success and which attracted him. He very readily took up BorГӯsвҖҷ cause and went with him to DolgorГәkov. It was late in the evening when they entered the palace at OlmГјtz occupied by the Emperors and their retinues. That same day a council of war had been held in which all the members of the Hofkriegsrath and both Emperors took part. At that council, contrary to the views of the old generals KutГәzov and Prince Schwartzenberg, it had been decided to advance immediately and give battle to Bonaparte. The council of war was just over when Prince AndrГ©y accompanied by BorГӯs arrived at the palace to find DolgorГәkov. Everyone at headquarters was still under the spell of the dayвҖҷs council, at which the party of the young had triumphed. The voices of those who counseled delay and advised waiting for something else before advancing had been so completely silenced and their arguments confuted by such conclusive evidence of the advantages of attacking that what had been discussed at the councilвҒ вҖ”the coming battle and the victory that would certainly result from itвҒ вҖ”no longer seemed to be in the future but in the past. All the advantages were on our side. Our enormous forces, undoubtedly superior to NapoleonвҖҷs, were concentrated in one place, the troops inspired by the EmperorsвҖҷ presence were eager for action. The strategic position where the operations would take place was familiar in all its details to the Austrian General Weyrother: a lucky accident had ordained that the Austrian army should maneuver the previous year on the very fields where the French had now to be fought; the adjacent locality was known and shown in every detail on the maps, and Bonaparte, evidently weakened, was undertaking nothing. DolgorГәkov, one of the warmest advocates of an attack, had just returned from the council, tired and exhausted but eager and proud of the victory that had been gained. Prince AndrГ©y introduced his protГ©gГ©, but Prince DolgorГәkov politely and firmly pressing his hand said nothing to BorГӯs and, evidently unable to suppress the thoughts which were uppermost in his mind at that moment, addressed Prince AndrГ©y in French. вҖңAh, my dear fellow, what a battle we have gained! God grant that the one that will result from it will be as victorious! However, dear fellow,вҖқ he said abruptly and eagerly, вҖңI must confess to having been unjust to the Austrians and especially to Weyrother. What exactitude, what minuteness, what knowledge of the locality, what foresight for every eventuality, every possibility even to the smallest detail! No, my dear fellow, no conditions better than our present ones could have been devised. This combination of Austrian precision with Russian valorвҒ вҖ”what more could be wished for?вҖқ вҖңSo the attack is definitely resolved on?вҖқ asked BolkГіnski. вҖңAnd do you know, my dear fellow, it seems to me that Buonaparte has decidedly lost bearings, you know that a letter was received from him today for the Emperor.вҖқ DolgorГәkov smiled significantly. вҖңIs that so? And what did he say?вҖқ inquired BolkГіnski. вҖңWhat can he say? Tra-di-ri-di-ra and so onвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ merely to gain time. I tell you he is in our hands, thatвҖҷs certain! But what was most amusing,вҖқ he continued, with a sudden, good-natured laugh, вҖңwas that we could not think how to address the reply! If not as вҖҳConsulвҖҷ and of course not as вҖҳEmperor,вҖҷ it seemed to me it should be to вҖҳGeneral Buonaparte.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ вҖңBut between not recognizing him as Emperor and calling him General Buonaparte, there is a difference,вҖқ remarked BolkГіnski. вҖңThatвҖҷs just it,вҖқ interrupted DolgorГәkov quickly, laughing. вҖңYou know BilГӯbinвҒ вҖ”heвҖҷs a very clever fellow. He suggested addressing him as вҖҳUsurper and Enemy of Mankind.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ DolgorГәkov laughed merrily. вҖңOnly that?вҖқ said BolkГіnski. вҖңAll the same, it was BilГӯbin who found a suitable form for the address. He is a wise and clever fellow.вҖқ вҖңWhat was it?вҖқ вҖңTo the Head of the French GovernmentвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Au chef du gouvernement franГ§ais,вҖқ said DolgorГәkov, with grave satisfaction. вҖңGood, wasnвҖҷt it?вҖқ вҖңYes, but he will dislike it extremely,вҖқ said BolkГіnski. вҖңOh yes, very much! My brother knows him, heвҖҷs dined with himвҒ вҖ”the present EmperorвҒ вҖ”more than once in Paris, and tells me he never met a more cunning or subtle diplomatistвҒ вҖ”you know, a combination of French adroitness and Italian playacting! Do you know the tale about him and Count MarkГіv? Count MarkГіv was the only man who knew how to handle him. You know the story of the handkerchief? It is delightful!вҖқ And the talkative DolgorГәkov, turning now to BorГӯs, now to Prince AndrГ©y, told how Bonaparte wishing to test MarkГіv, our ambassador, purposely dropped a handkerchief in front of him and stood looking at MarkГіv, probably expecting MarkГіv to pick it up for him, and how MarkГіv immediately dropped his own beside it and picked it up without touching BonaparteвҖҷs. вҖңDelightful!вҖқ said BolkГіnski. вҖңBut I have come to you, Prince, as a petitioner on behalf of this young man. You seeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ but before Prince AndrГ©y could finish, an aide-de-camp came in to summon DolgorГәkov to the Emperor. вҖңOh, what a nuisance,вҖқ said DolgorГәkov, getting up hurriedly and pressing the hands of Prince AndrГ©y and BorГӯs. вҖңYou know I should be very glad to do all in my power both for you and for this dear young man.вҖқ Again he pressed the hand of the latter with an expression of good-natured, sincere, and animated levity. вҖңBut you seeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ another time!вҖқ BorГӯs was excited by the thought of being so close to the higher powers as he felt himself to be at that moment. He was conscious that here he was in contact with the springs that set in motion the enormous movements of the mass of which in his regiment he felt himself a tiny, obedient, and insignificant atom. They followed Prince DolgorГәkov out into the corridor and metвҒ вҖ”coming out of the door of the EmperorвҖҷs room by which DolgorГәkov had enteredвҒ вҖ”a short man in civilian clothes with a clever face and sharply projecting jaw which, without spoiling his face, gave him a peculiar vivacity and shiftiness of expression. This short man nodded to DolgorГәkov as to an intimate friend and stared at Prince AndrГ©y with cool intensity, walking straight toward him and evidently expecting him to bow or to step out of his way. Prince AndrГ©y did neither: a look of animosity appeared on his face and the other turned away and went down the side of the corridor. вҖңWho was that?вҖқ asked BorГӯs. вҖңHe is one of the most remarkable, but to me most unpleasant of menвҒ вҖ”the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Prince Adam CzartorГҪski.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ It is such men as he who decide the fate of nations,вҖқ added BolkГіnski with a sigh he could not suppress, as they passed out of the palace. Next day, the army began its campaign, and up to the very battle of Austerlitz, BorГӯs was unable to see either Prince AndrГ©y or DolgorГәkov again and remained for a while with the IsmГЎylov regiment. I I Early in the year 1806 NikolГЎy RostГіv returned home on leave. DenГӯsov was going home to VorГіnezh and RostГіv persuaded him to travel with him as far as Moscow and to stay with him there. Meeting a comrade at the last post station but one before Moscow, DenГӯsov had drunk three bottles of wine with him and, despite the jolting ruts across the snow-covered road, did not once wake up on the way to Moscow, but lay at the bottom of the sleigh beside RostГіv, who grew more and more impatient the nearer they got to Moscow. вҖңHow much longer? How much longer? Oh, these insufferable streets, shops, bakersвҖҷ signboards, street lamps, and sleighs!вҖқ thought RostГіv, when their leave permits had been passed at the town gate and they had entered Moscow. вҖңDenГӯsov! WeвҖҷre here! HeвҖҷs asleep,вҖқ he added, leaning forward with his whole body as if in that position he hoped to hasten the speed of the sleigh. DenГӯsov gave no answer. вҖңThereвҖҷs the corner at the crossroads, where the cabman, ZakhГЎr, has his stand, and thereвҖҷs ZakhГЎr himself and still the same horse! And hereвҖҷs the little shop where we used to buy gingerbread! CanвҖҷt you hurry up? Now then!вҖқ вҖңWhich house is it?вҖқ asked the driver. вҖңWhy, that one, right at the end, the big one. DonвҖҷt you see? ThatвҖҷs our house,вҖқ said RostГіv. вҖңOf course, itвҖҷs our house! DenГӯsov, DenГӯsov! WeвҖҷre almost there!вҖқ DenГӯsov raised his head, coughed, and made no answer. вҖңDmГӯtri,вҖқ said RostГіv to his valet on the box, вҖңthose lights are in our house, arenвҖҷt they?вҖқ вҖңYes, sir, and thereвҖҷs a light in your fatherвҖҷs study.вҖқ вҖңThen theyвҖҷve not gone to bed yet? What do you think? Mind now, donвҖҷt forget to put out my new coat,вҖқ added RostГіv, fingering his new mustache. вҖңNow then, get on,вҖқ he shouted to the driver. вҖңDo wake up, VГЎska!вҖқ he went on, turning to DenГӯsov, whose head was again nodding. вҖңCome, get on! You shall have three rubles for vodkaвҒ вҖ”get on!вҖқ RostГіv shouted, when the sleigh was only three houses from his door. It seemed to him the horses were not moving at all. At last the sleigh bore to the right, drew up at an entrance, and RostГіv saw overhead the old familiar cornice with a bit of plaster broken off, the porch, and the post by the side of the pavement. He sprang out before the sleigh stopped, and ran into the hall. The house stood cold and silent, as if quite regardless of who had come to it. There was no one in the hall. вҖңOh God! Is everyone all right?вҖқ he thought, stopping for a moment with a sinking heart, and then immediately starting to run along the hall and up the warped steps of the familiar staircase. The well-known old door handle, which always angered the countess when it was not properly cleaned, turned as loosely as ever. A solitary tallow candle burned in the anteroom. Old MikhГЎilo was asleep on the chest. ProkГіfy, the footman, who was so strong that he could lift the back of the carriage from behind, sat plaiting slippers out of cloth selvedges. He looked up at the opening door and his expression of sleepy indifference suddenly changed to one of delighted amazement. вҖңGracious heavens! The young count!вҖқ he cried, recognizing his young master. вҖңCan it be? My treasure!вҖқ and ProkГіfy, trembling with excitement, rushed toward the drawing room door, probably in order to announce him, but, changing his mind, came back and stooped to kiss the young manвҖҷs shoulder. вҖңAll well?вҖқ asked RostГіv, drawing away his arm. вҖңYes, God be thanked! Yes! TheyвҖҷve just finished supper. Let me have a look at you, your excellency.вҖқ вҖңIs everything quite all right?вҖқ вҖңThe Lord be thanked, yes!вҖқ RostГіv, who had completely forgotten DenГӯsov, not wishing anyone to forestall him, threw off his fur coat and ran on tiptoe through the large dark ballroom. All was the same: there were the same old card tables and the same chandelier with a cover over it; but someone had already seen the young master, and, before he had reached the drawing room, something flew out from a side door like a tornado and began hugging and kissing him. Another and yet another creature of the same kind sprang from a second door and a third; more hugging, more kissing, more outcries, and tears of joy. He could not distinguish which was Papa, which NatГЎsha, and which PГ©tya. Everyone shouted, talked, and kissed him at the same time. Only his mother was not there, he noticed that. вҖңAnd I did not knowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ NikolГәshkaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ My darling!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңHere he isвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ our ownвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ KГіlya,43 dear fellowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ How he has changed!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Where are the candles?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Tea!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAnd me, kiss me!вҖқ вҖңDearestвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and me!вҖқ SГіnya, NatГЎsha, PГ©tya, Anna MikhГЎylovna, VГ©ra, and the old count were all hugging him, and the serfs, men and maids, flocked into the room, exclaiming and oh-ing and ah-ing. PГ©tya, clinging to his legs, kept shouting, вҖңAnd me too!вҖқ NatГЎsha, after she had pulled him down toward her and covered his face with kisses, holding him tight by the skirt of his coat, sprang away and pranced up and down in one place like a goat and shrieked piercingly. All around were loving eyes glistening with tears of joy, and all around were lips seeking a kiss. SГіnya too, all rosy red, clung to his arm and, radiant with bliss, looked eagerly toward his eyes, waiting for the look for which she longed. SГіnya now was sixteen and she was very pretty, especially at this moment of happy, rapturous excitement. She gazed at him, not taking her eyes off him, and smiling and holding her breath. He gave her a grateful look, but was still expectant and looking for someone. The old countess had not yet come. But now steps were heard at the door, steps so rapid that they could hardly be his motherвҖҷs. Yet it was she, dressed in a new gown which he did not know, made since he had left. All the others let him go, and he ran to her. When they met, she fell on his breast, sobbing. She could not lift her face, but only pressed it to the cold braiding of his hussarвҖҷs jacket. DenГӯsov, who had come into the room unnoticed by anyone, stood there and wiped his eyes at the sight. вҖңVasГӯli DenГӯsov, your sonвҖҷs friend,вҖқ he said, introducing himself to the count, who was looking inquiringly at him. вҖңYou are most welcome! I know, I know,вҖқ said the count, kissing and embracing DenГӯsov. вҖңNikolГәshka wrote usвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ NatГЎsha, VГ©ra, look! Here is DenГӯsov!вҖқ The same happy, rapturous faces turned to the shaggy figure of DenГӯsov. вҖңDarling DenГӯsov!вҖқ screamed NatГЎsha, beside herself with rapture, springing to him, putting her arms round him, and kissing him. This escapade made everybody feel confused. DenГӯsov blushed too, but smiled and, taking NatГЎshaвҖҷs hand, kissed it. DenГӯsov was shown to the room prepared for him, and the RostГіvs all gathered round NikolГәshka in the sitting room. The old countess, not letting go of his hand and kissing it every moment, sat beside him: the rest, crowding round him, watched every movement, word, or look of his, never taking their blissfully adoring eyes off him. His brother and sisters struggled for the places nearest to him and disputed with one another who should bring him his tea, handkerchief, and pipe. RostГіv was very happy in the love they showed him; but the first moment of meeting had been so beatific that his present joy seemed insufficient, and he kept expecting something more, more and yet more. Next morning, after the fatigues of their journey, the travelers slept till ten oвҖҷclock. In the room next to their bedroom there was a confusion of sabers, satchels, sabretaches, open portmanteaus, and dirty boots. Two freshly cleaned pairs with spurs had just been placed by the wall. The servants were bringing in jugs and basins, hot water for shaving, and their well-brushed clothes. There was a masculine odor and a smell of tobacco. вҖңHallo, GwГӯskaвҒ вҖ”my pipe!вҖқ came VГЎska DenГӯsovвҖҷs husky voice. вҖңWostГіv, get up!вҖқ RostГіv, rubbing his eyes that seemed glued together, raised his disheveled head from the hot pillow. вҖңWhy, is it late?вҖқ вҖңLate! ItвҖҷs nearly ten oвҖҷclock,вҖқ answered NatГЎshaвҖҷs voice. A rustle of starched petticoats and the whispering and laughter of girlsвҖҷ voices came from the adjoining room. The door was opened a crack and there was a glimpse of something blue, of ribbons, black hair, and merry faces. It was NatГЎsha, SГіnya, and PГ©tya, who had come to see whether they were getting up. вҖңNikГіlenka! Get up!вҖқ NatГЎshaвҖҷs voice was again heard at the door. вҖңDirectly!вҖқ Meanwhile, PГ©tya, having found and seized the sabers in the outer room, with the delight boys feel at the sight of a military elder brother, and forgetting that it was unbecoming for the girls to see men undressed, opened the bedroom door. вҖңIs this your saber?вҖқ he shouted. The girls sprang aside. DenГӯsov hid his hairy legs under the blanket, looking with a scared face at his comrade for help. The door, having let PГ©tya in, closed again. A sound of laughter came from behind it. вҖңNikГіlenka! Come out in your dressing gown!вҖқ said NatГЎshaвҖҷs voice. вҖңIs this your saber?вҖқ asked PГ©tya. вҖңOr is it yours?вҖқ he said, addressing the black-mustached DenГӯsov with servile deference. RostГіv hurriedly put something on his feet, drew on his dressing gown, and went out. NatГЎsha had put on one spurred boot and was just getting her foot into the other. SГіnya, when he came in, was twirling round and was about to expand her dresses into a balloon and sit down. They were dressed alike, in new pale-blue frocks, and were both fresh, rosy, and bright. SГіnya ran away, but NatГЎsha, taking her brotherвҖҷs arm, led him into the sitting room, where they began talking. They hardly gave one another time to ask questions and give replies concerning a thousand little matters which could not interest anyone but themselves. NatГЎsha laughed at every word he said or that she said herself, not because what they were saying was amusing, but because she felt happy and was unable to control her joy which expressed itself by laughter. вҖңOh, how nice, how splendid!вҖқ she said to everything. RostГіv felt that, under the influence of the warm rays of love, that childlike smile which had not once appeared on his face since he left home now for the first time after eighteen months again brightened his soul and his face. вҖңNo, but listen,вҖқ she said, вҖңnow you are quite a man, arenвҖҷt you? IвҖҷm awfully glad youвҖҷre my brother.вҖқ She touched his mustache. вҖңI want to know what you men are like. Are you the same as we? No?вҖқ вҖңWhy did SГіnya run away?вҖқ asked RostГіv. вҖңAh, yes! ThatвҖҷs a whole long story! How are you going to speak to herвҒ вҖ”thou or you?вҖқ вҖңAs may happen,вҖқ said RostГіv. вҖңNo, call her you, please! IвҖҷll tell you all about it some other time. No, IвҖҷll tell you now. You know SГіnyaвҖҷs my dearest friend. Such a friend that I burned my arm for her sake. Look here!вҖқ She pulled up her muslin sleeve and showed him a red scar on her long, slender, delicate arm, high above the elbow on that part that is covered even by a ball dress. вҖңI burned this to prove my love for her. I just heated a ruler in the fire and pressed it there!вҖқ Sitting on the sofa with the little cushions on its arms, in what used to be his old schoolroom, and looking into NatГЎshaвҖҷs wildly bright eyes, RostГіv reentered that world of home and childhood which had no meaning for anyone else, but gave him some of the best joys of his life; and the burning of an arm with a ruler as a proof of love did not seem to him senseless, he understood and was not surprised at it. вҖңWell, and is that all?вҖқ he asked. вҖңWe are such friends, such friends! All that ruler business was just nonsense, but we are friends forever. She, if she loves anyone, does it for life, but I donвҖҷt understand that, I forget quickly.вҖқ вҖңWell, what then?вҖқ вҖңWell, she loves me and you like that.вҖқ NatГЎsha suddenly flushed. вҖңWhy, you remember before you went away?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, she says you are to forget all that.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She says: вҖҳI shall love him always, but let him be free.вҖҷ IsnвҖҷt that lovely and noble! Yes, very noble? IsnвҖҷt it?вҖқ asked NatГЎsha, so seriously and excitedly that it was evident that what she was now saying she had talked of before, with tears. RostГіv became thoughtful. вҖңI never go back on my word,вҖқ he said. вҖңBesides, SГіnya is so charming that only a fool would renounce such happiness.вҖқ вҖңNo, no!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha, вҖңshe and I have already talked it over. We knew youвҖҷd say so. But it wonвҖҷt do, because you see, if you say thatвҒ вҖ”if you consider yourself bound by your promiseвҒ вҖ”it will seem as if she had not meant it seriously. It makes it as if you were marrying her because you must, and that wouldnвҖҷt do at all.вҖқ RostГіv saw that it had been well considered by them. SГіnya had already struck him by her beauty on the preceding day. Today, when he had caught a glimpse of her, she seemed still more lovely. She was a charming girl of sixteen, evidently passionately in love with him (he did not doubt that for an instant). Why should he not love her now, and even marry her, RostГіv thought, but just now there were so many other pleasures and interests before him! вҖңYes, they have taken a wise decision,вҖқ he thought, вҖңI must remain free.вҖқ вҖңWell then, thatвҖҷs excellent,вҖқ said he. вҖңWeвҖҷll talk it over later on. Oh, how glad I am to have you!вҖқ вҖңWell, and are you still true to BorГӯs?вҖқ he continued. вҖңOh, what nonsense!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha, laughing. вҖңI donвҖҷt think about him or anyone else, and I donвҖҷt want anything of the kind.вҖқ вҖңDear me! Then what are you up to now?вҖқ вҖңNow?вҖқ repeated NatГЎsha, and a happy smile lit up her face. вҖңHave you seen Duport?вҖқ вҖңNo.вҖқ вҖңNot seen DuportвҒ вҖ”the famous dancer? Well then, you wonвҖҷt understand. ThatвҖҷs what IвҖҷm up to.вҖқ Curving her arms, NatГЎsha held out her skirts as dancers do, ran back a few steps, turned, cut a caper, brought her little feet sharply together, and made some steps on the very tips of her toes. вҖңSee, IвҖҷm standing! See!вҖқ she said, but could not maintain herself on her toes any longer. вҖңSo thatвҖҷs what IвҖҷm up to! IвҖҷll never marry anyone, but will be a dancer. Only donвҖҷt tell anyone.вҖқ RostГіv laughed so loud and merrily that DenГӯsov, in his bedroom, felt envious and NatГЎsha could not help joining in. вҖңNo, but donвҖҷt you think itвҖҷs nice?вҖқ she kept repeating. вҖңNice! And so you no longer wish to marry BorГӯs?вҖқ NatГЎsha flared up. вҖңI donвҖҷt want to marry anyone. And IвҖҷll tell him so when I see him!вҖқ вҖңDear me!вҖқ said RostГіv. вҖңBut thatвҖҷs all rubbish,вҖқ NatГЎsha chattered on. вҖңAnd is DenГӯsov nice?вҖқ she asked. вҖңYes, indeed!вҖқ вҖңOh, well then, goodbye: go and dress. Is he very terrible, DenГӯsov?вҖқ вҖңWhy terrible?вҖқ asked Nicolas. вҖңNo, VГЎska is a splendid fellow.вҖқ вҖңYou call him VГЎska? ThatвҖҷs funny! And is he very nice?вҖқ вҖңVery.вҖқ вҖңWell then, be quick. WeвҖҷll all have breakfast together.вҖқ And NatГЎsha rose and went out of the room on tiptoe, like a ballet dancer, but smiling as only happy girls of fifteen can smile. When RostГіv met SГіnya in the drawing room, he reddened. He did not know how to behave with her. The evening before, in the first happy moment of meeting, they had kissed each other, but today they felt it could not be done; he felt that everybody, including his mother and sisters, was looking inquiringly at him and watching to see how he would behave with her. He kissed her hand and addressed her not as thou but as youвҒ вҖ”SГіnya. But their eyes met and said thou, and exchanged tender kisses. Her looks asked him to forgive her for having dared, by NatГЎshaвҖҷs intermediacy, to remind him of his promise, and then thanked him for his love. His looks thanked her for offering him his freedom and told her that one way or another he would never cease to love her, for that would be impossible. вҖңHow strange it is,вҖқ said VГ©ra, selecting a moment when all were silent, вҖңthat SГіnya and NikГіlenka now say you to one another and meet like strangers.вҖқ VГ©raвҖҷs remark was correct, as her remarks always were, but, like most of her observations, it made everyone feel uncomfortable, not only SГіnya, NikolГЎy, and NatГЎsha, but even the old countess, whoвҒ вҖ”dreading this love affair which might hinder NikolГЎy from making a brilliant matchвҒ вҖ”blushed like a girl. DenГӯsov, to RostГіvвҖҷs surprise, appeared in the drawing room with pomaded hair, perfumed, and in a new uniform, looking just as smart as he made himself when going into battle, and he was more amiable to the ladies and gentlemen than RostГіv had ever expected to see him. X X RostГіvвҖҷs share in DГіlokhovвҖҷs duel with BezГәkhov was hushed up by the efforts of the old count, and instead of being degraded to the ranks as he expected he was appointed an adjutant to the governor general of Moscow. As a result he could not go to the country with the rest of the family, but was kept all summer in Moscow by his new duties. DГіlokhov recovered, and RostГіv became very friendly with him during his convalescence. DГіlokhov lay ill at his motherвҖҷs who loved him passionately and tenderly, and old MГЎrya IvГЎnovna, who had grown fond of RostГіv for his friendship to her FГ©dya, often talked to him about her son. вҖңYes, Count,вҖқ she would say, вҖңhe is too noble and pure-souled for our present, depraved world. No one now loves virtue; it seems like a reproach to everyone. Now tell me, Count, was it right, was it honorable, of BezГәkhov? And FГ©dya, with his noble spirit, loved him and even now never says a word against him. Those pranks in Petersburg when they played some tricks on a policeman, didnвҖҷt they do it together? And there! BezГәkhov got off scotfree, while FГ©dya had to bear the whole burden on his shoulders. Fancy what he had to go through! ItвҖҷs true he has been reinstated, but how could they fail to do that? I think there were not many such gallant sons of the fatherland out there as he. And nowвҒ вҖ”this duel! Have these people no feeling, or honor? Knowing him to be an only son, to challenge him and shoot so straight! ItвҖҷs well God had mercy on us. And what was it for? Who doesnвҖҷt have intrigues nowadays? Why, if he was so jealous, as I see things he should have shown it sooner, but he lets it go on for months. And then to call him out, reckoning on FГ©dya not fighting because he owed him money! What baseness! What meanness! I know you understand FГ©dya, my dear count; that, believe me, is why I am so fond of you. Few people do understand him. He is such a lofty, heavenly soul!вҖқ DГіlokhov himself during his convalescence spoke to RostГіv in a way no one would have expected of him. вҖңI know people consider me a bad man!вҖқ he said. вҖңLet them! I donвҖҷt care a straw about anyone but those I love; but those I love, I love so that I would give my life for them, and the others IвҖҷd throttle if they stood in my way. I have an adored, a priceless mother, and two or three friendsвҒ вҖ”you among themвҒ вҖ”and as for the rest I only care about them in so far as they are harmful or useful. And most of them are harmful, especially the women. Yes, dear boy,вҖқ he continued, вҖңI have met loving, noble, high-minded men, but I have not yet met any womenвҒ вҖ”countesses or cooksвҒ вҖ”who were not venal. I have not yet met that divine purity and devotion I look for in women. If I found such a one IвҖҷd give my life for her! But those!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and he made a gesture of contempt. вҖңAnd believe me, if I still value my life it is only because I still hope to meet such a divine creature, who will regenerate, purify, and elevate me. But you donвҖҷt understand it.вҖқ вҖңOh, yes, I quite understand,вҖқ answered RostГіv, who was under his new friendвҖҷs influence. In the autumn the RostГіvs returned to Moscow. Early in the winter DenГӯsov also came back and stayed with them. The first half of the winter of 1806, which NikolГЎy RostГіv spent in Moscow, was one of the happiest, merriest times for him and the whole family. NikolГЎy brought many young men to his parentsвҖҷ house. VГ©ra was a handsome girl of twenty; SГіnya a girl of sixteen with all the charm of an opening flower; NatГЎsha, half grown up and half child, was now childishly amusing, now girlishly enchanting. At that time in the RostГіvsвҖҷ house there prevailed an amorous atmosphere characteristic of homes where there are very young and very charming girls. Every young man who came to the houseвҒ вҖ”seeing those impressionable, smiling young faces (smiling probably at their own happiness), feeling the eager bustle around him, and hearing the fitful bursts of song and music and the inconsequent but friendly prattle of young girls ready for anything and full of hopeвҒ вҖ”experienced the same feeling; sharing with the young folk of the RostГіvsвҖҷ household a readiness to fall in love and an expectation of happiness. Among the young men introduced by RostГіv one of the first was DГіlokhov, whom everyone in the house liked except NatГЎsha. She almost quarreled with her brother about him. She insisted that he was a bad man, and that in the duel with BezГәkhov, Pierre was right and DГіlokhov wrong, and further that he was disagreeable and unnatural. вҖңThereвҖҷs nothing for me to understand,вҖқ she cried out with resolute self-will, вҖңhe is wicked and heartless. There now, I like your DenГӯsov though he is a rake and all that, still I like him; so you see I do understand. I donвҖҷt know how to put itвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ with this one everything is calculated, and I donвҖҷt like that. But DenГӯsovвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңOh, DenГӯsov is quite different,вҖқ replied NikolГЎy, implying that even DenГӯsov was nothing compared to DГіlokhovвҒ вҖ”вҖңyou must understand what a soul there is in DГіlokhov, you should see him with his mother. What a heart!вҖқ вҖңWell, I donвҖҷt know about that, but I am uncomfortable with him. And do you know he has fallen in love with SГіnya?вҖқ вҖңWhat nonsenseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңIвҖҷm certain of it; youвҖҷll see.вҖқ NatГЎshaвҖҷs prediction proved true. DГіlokhov, who did not usually care for the society of ladies, began to come often to the house, and the question for whose sake he came (though no one spoke of it) was soon settled. He came because of SГіnya. And SГіnya, though she would never have dared to say so, knew it and blushed scarlet every time DГіlokhov appeared. DГіlokhov often dined at the RostГіvsвҖҷ, never missed a performance at which they were present, and went to IogelвҖҷs balls for young people which the RostГіvs always attended. He was pointedly attentive to SГіnya and looked at her in such a way that not only could she not bear his glances without coloring, but even the old countess and NatГЎsha blushed when they saw his looks. It was evident that this strange, strong man was under the irresistible influence of the dark, graceful girl who loved another. RostГіv noticed something new in DГіlokhovвҖҷs relations with SГіnya, but he did not explain to himself what these new relations were. вҖңTheyвҖҷre always in love with someone,вҖқ he thought of SГіnya and NatГЎsha. But he was not as much at ease with SГіnya and DГіlokhov as before and was less frequently at home. In the autumn of 1806 everybody had again begun talking of the war with Napoleon with even greater warmth than the year before.
