Orders were given to raise recruits, ten men in every thousand for the regular army, and besides this, nine men in every thousand for the militia. Everywhere Bonaparte was anathematized and in Moscow nothing but the coming war was talked of. For the RostГіv family the whole interest of these preparations for war lay in the fact that NikolГәshka would not hear of remaining in Moscow, and only awaited the termination of DenГӯsovвҖҷs furlough after Christmas to return with him to their regiment. His approaching departure did not prevent his amusing himself, but rather gave zest to his pleasures. He spent the greater part of his time away from home, at dinners, parties, and balls. XI XI On the third day after Christmas NikolГЎy dined at home, a thing he had rarely done of late. It was a grand farewell dinner, as he and DenГӯsov were leaving to join their regiment after Epiphany. About twenty people were present, including DГіlokhov and DenГӯsov. Never had love been so much in the air, and never had the amorous atmosphere made itself so strongly felt in the RostГіvsвҖҷ house as at this holiday time. вҖңSeize the moments of happiness, love and be loved! That is the only reality in the world, all else is folly. It is the one thing we are interested in here,вҖқ said the spirit of the place. NikolГЎy, having as usual exhausted two pairs of horses, without visiting all the places he meant to go to and where he had been invited, returned home just before dinner. As soon as he entered he noticed and felt the tension of the amorous air in the house, and also noticed a curious embarrassment among some of those present. SГіnya, DГіlokhov, and the old countess were especially disturbed, and to a lesser degree NatГЎsha. NikolГЎy understood that something must have happened between SГіnya and DГіlokhov before dinner, and with the kindly sensitiveness natural to him was very gentle and wary with them both at dinner. On that same evening there was to be one of the balls that Iogel (the dancing master) gave for his pupils during the holidays. вҖңNikГіlenka, will you come to IogelвҖҷs? Please do!вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңHe asked you, and VasГӯli DmГӯtrich49 is also going.вҖқ вҖңWhere would I not go at the countessвҖҷ command!вҖқ said DenГӯsov, who at the RostГіvsвҖҷ had jocularly assumed the role of NatГЎshaвҖҷs knight. вҖңIвҖҷm even weady to dance the pas de chГўle.вҖқ вҖңIf I have time,вҖқ answered NikolГЎy. вҖңBut I promised the ArkhГЎrovs; they have a party.вҖқ вҖңAnd you?вҖқ he asked DГіlokhov, but as soon as he had asked the question he noticed that it should not have been put. вҖңPerhaps,вҖқ coldly and angrily replied DГіlokhov, glancing at SГіnya, and, scowling, he gave NikolГЎy just such a look as he had given Pierre at the club dinner. вҖңThere is something up,вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, and he was further confirmed in this conclusion by the fact that DГіlokhov left immediately after dinner. He called NatГЎsha and asked her what was the matter. вҖңAnd I was looking for you,вҖқ said NatГЎsha running out to him. вҖңI told you, but you would not believe it,вҖқ she said triumphantly. вҖңHe has proposed to SГіnya!вҖқ Little as NikolГЎy had occupied himself with SГіnya of late, something seemed to give way within him at this news. DГіlokhov was a suitable and in some respects a brilliant match for the dowerless, orphan girl. From the point of view of the old countess and of society it was out of the question for her to refuse him. And therefore NikolГЎyвҖҷs first feeling on hearing the news was one of anger with SГіnya.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ He tried to say, вҖңThatвҖҷs capital; of course sheвҖҷll forget her childish promises and accept the offer,вҖқ but before he had time to say it NatГЎsha began again. вҖңAnd fancy! she refused him quite definitely!вҖқ adding, after a pause, вҖңshe told him she loved another.вҖқ вҖңYes, my SГіnya could not have done otherwise!вҖқ thought NikolГЎy. вҖңMuch as Mamma pressed her, she refused, and I know she wonвҖҷt change once she has saidвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAnd Mamma pressed her!вҖқ said NikolГЎy reproachfully. вҖңYes,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңDo you know, NikГіlenkaвҒ вҖ”donвҖҷt be angryвҒ вҖ”but I know you will not marry her. I know, heaven knows how, but I know for certain that you wonвҖҷt marry her.вҖқ вҖңNow you donвҖҷt know that at all!вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңBut I must talk to her. What a darling SГіnya is!вҖқ he added with a smile. вҖңAh, she is indeed a darling! IвҖҷll send her to you.вҖқ And NatГЎsha kissed her brother and ran away. A minute later SГіnya came in with a frightened, guilty, and scared look. NikolГЎy went up to her and kissed her hand. This was the first time since his return that they had talked alone and about their love. вҖңSophie,вҖқ he began, timidly at first and then more and more boldly, вҖңif you wish to refuse one who is not only a brilliant and advantageous match but a splendid, noble fellowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ he is my friendвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ SГіnya interrupted him. вҖңI have already refused,вҖқ she said hurriedly. вҖңIf you are refusing for my sake, I am afraid that IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ SГіnya again interrupted. She gave him an imploring, frightened look. вҖңNicolas, donвҖҷt tell me that!вҖқ she said. вҖңNo, but I must. It may be arrogant of me, but still it is best to say it. If you refuse him on my account, I must tell you the whole truth. I love you, and I think I love you more than anyone else.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңThat is enough for me,вҖқ said SГіnya, blushing. вҖңNo, but I have been in love a thousand times and shall fall in love again, though for no one have I such a feeling of friendship, confidence, and love as I have for you. Then I am young. Mamma does not wish it. In a word, I make no promise. And I beg you to consider DГіlokhovвҖҷs offer,вҖқ he said, articulating his friendвҖҷs name with difficulty. вҖңDonвҖҷt say that to me! I want nothing. I love you as a brother and always shall, and I want nothing more.вҖқ вҖңYou are an angel: I am not worthy of you, but I am afraid of misleading you.вҖқ And NikolГЎy again kissed her hand. XII XII IogelвҖҷs were the most enjoyable balls in Moscow. So said the mothers as they watched their young people executing their newly learned steps, and so said the youths and maidens themselves as they danced till they were ready to drop, and so said the grown-up young men and women who came to these balls with an air of condescension and found them most enjoyable. That year two marriages had come of these balls. The two pretty young Princesses GorchakГіv met suitors there and were married and so further increased the fame of these dances. What distinguished them from others was the absence of host or hostess and the presence of the good-natured Iogel, flying about like a feather and bowing according to the rules of his art, as he collected the tickets from all his visitors. There was the fact that only those came who wished to dance and amuse themselves as girls of thirteen and fourteen do who are wearing long dresses for the first time. With scarcely any exceptions they all were, or seemed to be, prettyвҒ вҖ”so rapturous were their smiles and so sparkling their eyes. Sometimes the best of the pupils, of whom NatГЎsha, who was exceptionally graceful, was first, even danced the pas de chГўle, but at this last ball only the Г©cossaise, the anglaise, and the mazurka, which was just coming into fashion, were danced. Iogel had taken a ballroom in BezГәkhovвҖҷs house, and the ball, as everyone said, was a great success. There were many pretty girls and the RostГіv girls were among the prettiest. They were both particularly happy and gay. That evening, proud of DГіlokhovвҖҷs proposal, her refusal, and her explanation with NikolГЎy, SГіnya twirled about before she left home so that the maid could hardly get her hair plaited, and she was transparently radiant with impulsive joy. NatГЎsha no less proud of her first long dress and of being at a real ball was even happier. They were both dressed in white muslin with pink ribbons. NatГЎsha fell in love the very moment she entered the ballroom. She was not in love with anyone in particular, but with everyone. Whatever person she happened to look at she was in love with for that moment. вҖңOh, how delightful it is!вҖқ she kept saying, running up to SГіnya. NikolГЎy and DenГӯsov were walking up and down, looking with kindly patronage at the dancers. вҖңHow sweet she isвҒ вҖ”she will be a weal beauty!вҖқ said DenГӯsov. вҖңWho?вҖқ вҖңCountess NatГЎsha,вҖқ answered DenГӯsov. вҖңAnd how she dances! What gwace!вҖқ he said again after a pause. вҖңWho are you talking about?вҖқ вҖңAbout your sister,вҖқ ejaculated DenГӯsov testily. RostГіv smiled. вҖңMy dear count, you were one of my best pupilsвҒ вҖ”you must dance,вҖқ said little Iogel coming up to NikolГЎy. вҖңLook how many charming young ladiesвҒ вҖ”вҖқ He turned with the same request to DenГӯsov who was also a former pupil of his. вҖңNo, my dear fellow, IвҖҷll be a wallflower,вҖқ said DenГӯsov. вҖңDonвҖҷt you wecollect what bad use I made of your lessons?вҖқ вҖңOh no!вҖқ said Iogel, hastening to reassure him. вҖңYou were only inattentive, but you had talentвҒ вҖ”oh yes, you had talent!вҖқ The band struck up the newly introduced mazurka. NikolГЎy could not refuse Iogel and asked SГіnya to dance. DenГӯsov sat down by the old ladies and, leaning on his saber and beating time with his foot, told them something funny and kept them amused, while he watched the young people dancing, Iogel with NatГЎsha, his pride and his best pupil, were the first couple. Noiselessly, skillfully stepping with his little feet in low shoes, Iogel flew first across the hall with NatГЎsha, who, though shy, went on carefully executing her steps. DenГӯsov did not take his eyes off her and beat time with his saber in a way that clearly indicated that if he was not dancing it was because he would not and not because he could not. In the middle of a figure he beckoned to RostГіv who was passing: вҖңThis is not at all the thing,вҖқ he said. вҖңWhat sort of Polish mazuwka is this? But she does dance splendidly.вҖқ Knowing that DenГӯsov had a reputation even in Poland for the masterly way in which he danced the mazurka, NikolГЎy ran up to NatГЎsha: вҖңGo and choose DenГӯsov. He is a real dancer, a wonder!вҖқ he said. When it came to NatГЎshaвҖҷs turn to choose a partner, she rose and, tripping rapidly across in her little shoes trimmed with bows, ran timidly to the corner where DenГӯsov sat. She saw that everybody was looking at her and waiting. NikolГЎy saw that DenГӯsov was refusing though he smiled delightedly. He ran up to them. вҖңPlease, VasГӯli DmГӯtrich,вҖқ NatГЎsha was saying, вҖңdo come!вҖқ вҖңOh no, let me off, Countess,вҖқ DenГӯsov replied. вҖңNow then, VГЎska,вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңThey coax me as if I were VГЎska the cat!вҖқ said DenГӯsov jokingly. вҖңIвҖҷll sing for you a whole evening,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңOh, the faiwy! She can do anything with me!вҖқ said DenГӯsov, and he unhooked his saber. He came out from behind the chairs, clasped his partnerвҖҷs hand firmly, threw back his head, and advanced his foot, waiting for the beat. Only on horse back and in the mazurka was DenГӯsovвҖҷs short stature not noticeable and he looked the fine fellow he felt himself to be. At the right beat of the music he looked sideways at his partner with a merry and triumphant air, suddenly stamped with one foot, bounded from the floor like a ball, and flew round the room taking his partner with him. He glided silently on one foot half across the room, and seeming not to notice the chairs was dashing straight at them, when suddenly, clinking his spurs and spreading out his legs, he stopped short on his heels, stood so a second, stamped on the spot clanking his spurs, whirled rapidly round, and, striking his left heel against his right, flew round again in a circle. NatГЎsha guessed what he meant to do, and abandoning herself to him followed his lead hardly knowing how. First he spun her round, holding her now with his left, now with his right hand, then falling on one knee he twirled her round him, and again jumping up, dashed so impetuously forward that it seemed as if he would rush through the whole suite of rooms without drawing breath, and then he suddenly stopped and performed some new and unexpected steps. When at last, smartly whirling his partner round in front of her chair, he drew up with a click of his spurs and bowed to her, NatГЎsha did not even make him a curtsy. She fixed her eyes on him in amazement, smiling as if she did not recognize him. вҖңWhat does this mean?вҖқ she brought out. Although Iogel did not acknowledge this to be the real mazurka, everyone was delighted with DenГӯsovвҖҷs skill, he was asked again and again as a partner, and the old men began smilingly to talk about Poland and the good old days. DenГӯsov, flushed after the mazurka and mopping himself with his handkerchief, sat down by NatГЎsha and did not leave her for the rest of the evening. XIII XIII For two days after that RostГіv did not see DГіlokhov at his own or at DГіlokhovвҖҷs home: on the third day he received a note from him: As I do not intend to be at your house again for reasons you know of, and am going to rejoin my regiment, I am giving a farewell supper tonight to my friendsвҒ вҖ”come to the English Hotel. About ten oвҖҷclock RostГіv went to the English Hotel straight from the theater, where he had been with his family and DenГӯsov. He was at once shown to the best room, which DГіlokhov had taken for that evening. Some twenty men were gathered round a table at which DГіlokhov sat between two candles. On the table was a pile of gold and paper money, and he was keeping the bank. RostГіv had not seen him since his proposal and SГіnyaвҖҷs refusal and felt uncomfortable at the thought of how they would meet. DГіlokhovвҖҷs clear, cold glance met RostГіv as soon as he entered the door, as though he had long expected him. вҖңItвҖҷs a long time since we met,вҖқ he said. вҖңThanks for coming. IвҖҷll just finish dealing, and then IlyГәshka will come with his chorus.вҖқ вҖңI called once or twice at your house,вҖқ said RostГіv, reddening. DГіlokhov made no reply. вҖңYou may punt,вҖқ he said. RostГіv recalled at that moment a strange conversation he had once had with DГіlokhov. вҖңNone but fools trust to luck in play,вҖқ DГіlokhov had then said. вҖңOr are you afraid to play with me?вҖқ DГіlokhov now asked as if guessing RostГіvвҖҷs thought. Beneath his smile RostГіv saw in him the mood he had shown at the club dinner and at other times, when as if tired of everyday life he had felt a need to escape from it by some strange, and usually cruel, action. RostГіv felt ill at ease. He tried, but failed, to find some joke with which to reply to DГіlokhovвҖҷs words. But before he had thought of anything, DГіlokhov, looking straight in his face, said slowly and deliberately so that everyone could hear: вҖңDo you remember we had a talk about cardsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖҳHeвҖҷs a fool who trusts to luck, one should make certain,вҖҷ and I want to try.вҖқ вҖңTo try his luck or the certainty?вҖқ RostГіv asked himself. вҖңWell, youвҖҷd better not play,вҖқ DГіlokhov added, and springing a new pack of cards said: вҖңBank, gentlemen!вҖқ Moving the money forward he prepared to deal. RostГіv sat down by his side and at first did not play. DГіlokhov kept glancing at him. вҖңWhy donвҖҷt you play?вҖқ he asked. And strange to say NikolГЎy felt that he could not help taking up a card, putting a small stake on it, and beginning to play. вҖңI have no money with me,вҖқ he said. вҖңIвҖҷll trust you.вҖқ RostГіv staked five rubles on a card and lost, staked again, and again lost. DГіlokhov вҖңkilled,вҖқ that is, beat, ten cards of RostГіvвҖҷs running. вҖңGentlemen,вҖқ said DГіlokhov after he had dealt for some time. вҖңPlease place your money on the cards or I may get muddled in the reckoning.вҖқ One of the players said he hoped he might be trusted. вҖңYes, you might, but I am afraid of getting the accounts mixed. So I ask you to put the money on your cards,вҖқ replied DГіlokhov. вҖңDonвҖҷt stint yourself, weвҖҷll settle afterwards,вҖқ he added, turning to RostГіv. The game continued; a waiter kept handing round champagne. All RostГіvвҖҷs cards were beaten and he had eight hundred rubles scored up against him. He wrote вҖң800 rublesвҖқ on a card, but while the waiter filled his glass he changed his mind and altered it to his usual stake of twenty rubles. вҖңLeave it,вҖқ said DГіlokhov, though he did not seem to be even looking at RostГіv, вҖңyouвҖҷll win it back all the sooner. I lose to the others but win from you. Or are you afraid of me?вҖқ he asked again. RostГіv submitted. He let the eight hundred remain and laid down a seven of hearts with a torn corner, which he had picked up from the floor. He well remembered that seven afterwards. He laid down the seven of hearts, on which with a broken bit of chalk he had written вҖң800 rublesвҖқ in clear upright figures; he emptied the glass of warm champagne that was handed him, smiled at DГіlokhovвҖҷs words, and with a sinking heart, waiting for a seven to turn up, gazed at DГіlokhovвҖҷs hands which held the pack. Much depended on RostГіvвҖҷs winning or losing on that seven of hearts. On the previous Sunday the old count had given his son two thousand rubles, and though he always disliked speaking of money difficulties had told NikolГЎy that this was all he could let him have till May, and asked him to be more economical this time. NikolГЎy had replied that it would be more than enough for him and that he gave his word of honor not to take anything more till the spring. Now only twelve hundred rubles was left of that money, so that this seven of hearts meant for him not only the loss of sixteen hundred rubles, but the necessity of going back on his word. With a sinking heart he watched DГіlokhovвҖҷs hands and thought, вҖңNow then, make haste and let me have this card and IвҖҷll take my cap and drive home to supper with DenГӯsov, NatГЎsha, and SГіnya, and will certainly never touch a card again.вҖқ At that moment his home life, jokes with PГ©tya, talks with SГіnya, duets with NatГЎsha, piquet with his father, and even his comfortable bed in the house on the PovarskГЎya rose before him with such vividness, clearness, and charm that it seemed as if it were all a lost and unappreciated bliss, long past. He could not conceive that a stupid chance, letting the seven be dealt to the right rather than to the left, might deprive him of all this happiness, newly appreciated and newly illumined, and plunge him into the depths of unknown and undefined misery. That could not be, yet he awaited with a sinking heart the movement of DГіlokhovвҖҷs hands. Those broad, reddish hands, with hairy wrists visible from under the shirt cuffs, laid down the pack and took up a glass and a pipe that were handed him. вҖңSo you are not afraid to play with me?вҖқ repeated DГіlokhov, and as if about to tell a good story he put down the cards, leaned back in his chair, and began deliberately with a smile: вҖңYes, gentlemen, IвҖҷve been told thereвҖҷs a rumor going about Moscow that IвҖҷm a sharper, so I advise you to be careful.вҖқ вҖңCome now, deal!вҖқ exclaimed RostГіv. вҖңOh, those Moscow gossips!вҖқ said DГіlokhov, and he took up the cards with a smile. вҖңAah!вҖқ RostГіv almost screamed lifting both hands to his head. The seven he needed was lying uppermost, the first card in the pack. He had lost more than he could pay. вҖңStill, donвҖҷt ruin yourself!вҖқ said DГіlokhov with a side glance at RostГіv as he continued to deal. XIV XIV An hour and a half later most of the players were but little interested in their own play. The whole interest was concentrated on RostГіv. Instead of sixteen hundred rubles he had a long column of figures scored against him, which he had reckoned up to ten thousand, but that now, as he vaguely supposed, must have risen to fifteen thousand. In reality it already exceeded twenty thousand rubles. DГіlokhov was no longer listening to stories or telling them, but followed every movement of RostГіvвҖҷs hands and occasionally ran his eyes over the score against him. He had decided to play until that score reached forty-three thousand. He had fixed on that number because forty-three was the sum of his and SГіnyaвҖҷs joint ages. RostГіv, leaning his head on both hands, sat at the table which was scrawled over with figures, wet with spilled wine, and littered with cards. One tormenting impression did not leave him: that those broad-boned reddish hands with hairy wrists visible from under the shirt sleeves, those hands which he loved and hated, held him in their power. вҖңSix hundred rubles, ace, a corner, a nineвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ winning it backвҖҷs impossibleвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Oh, how pleasant it was at home!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The knave, double or quitsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ it canвҖҷt be!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And why is he doing this to me?вҖқ RostГіv pondered. Sometimes he staked a large sum, but DГіlokhov refused to accept it and fixed the stake himself. NikolГЎy submitted to him, and at one moment prayed to God as he had done on the battlefield at the bridge over the Enns, and then guessed that the card that came first to hand from the crumpled heap under the table would save him, now counted the cords on his coat and took a card with that number and tried staking the total of his losses on it, then he looked round for aid from the other players, or peered at the now cold face of DГіlokhov and tried to read what was passing in his mind. вҖңHe knows of course what this loss means to me. He canвҖҷt want my ruin. WasnвҖҷt he my friend? WasnвҖҷt I fond of him? But itвҖҷs not his fault. WhatвҖҷs he to do if he has such luck?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And itвҖҷs not my fault either,вҖқ he thought to himself, вҖңI have done nothing wrong. Have I killed anyone, or insulted or wished harm to anyone? Why such a terrible misfortune? And when did it begin? Such a little while ago I came to this table with the thought of winning a hundred rubles to buy that casket for MammaвҖҷs name day and then going home. I was so happy, so free, so lighthearted! And I did not realize how happy I was! When did that end and when did this new, terrible state of things begin? What marked the change? I sat all the time in this same place at this table, chose and placed cards, and watched those broad-boned agile hands in the same way. When did it happen and what has happened? I am well and strong and still the same and in the same place. No, it canвҖҷt be! Surely it will all end in nothing!вҖқ He was flushed and bathed in perspiration, though the room was not hot. His face was terrible and piteous to see, especially from its helpless efforts to seem calm. The score against him reached the fateful sum of forty-three thousand. RostГіv had just prepared a card, by bending the corner of which he meant to double the three thousand just put down to his score, when DГіlokhov, slamming down the pack of cards, put it aside and began rapidly adding up the total of RostГіvвҖҷs debt, breaking the chalk as he marked the figures in his clear, bold hand. вҖңSupper, itвҖҷs time for supper! And here are the gypsies!вҖқ Some swarthy men and women were really entering from the cold outside and saying something in their gypsy accents. NikolГЎy understood that it was all over; but he said in an indifferent tone: вҖңWell, wonвҖҷt you go on? I had a splendid card all ready,вҖқ as if it were the fun of the game which interested him most. вҖңItвҖҷs all up! IвҖҷm lost!вҖқ thought he. вҖңNow a bullet through my brainвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs all thatвҖҷs left me!вҖқ And at the same time he said in a cheerful voice: вҖңCome now, just this one more little card!вҖқ вҖңAll right!вҖқ said DГіlokhov, having finished the addition. вҖңAll right! Twenty-one rubles,вҖқ he said, pointing to the figure twenty-one by which the total exceeded the round sum of forty-three thousand; and taking up a pack he prepared to deal. RostГіv submissively unbent the corner of his card and, instead of the six thousand he had intended, carefully wrote twenty-one. вҖңItвҖҷs all the same to me,вҖқ he said. вҖңI only want to see whether you will let me win this ten, or beat it.вҖқ DГіlokhov began to deal seriously. Oh, how RostГіv detested at that moment those hands with their short reddish fingers and hairy wrists, which held him in their power.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The ten fell to him. вҖңYou owe forty-three thousand, Count,вҖқ said DГіlokhov, and stretching himself he rose from the table. вҖңOne does get tired sitting so long,вҖқ he added. вҖңYes, IвҖҷm tired too,вҖқ said RostГіv. DГіlokhov cut him short, as if to remind him that it was not for him to jest. вҖңWhen am I to receive the money, Count?вҖқ RostГіv, flushing, drew DГіlokhov into the next room. вҖңI cannot pay it all immediately. Will you take an I.O.U.?вҖқ he said. вҖңI say, RostГіv,вҖқ said DГіlokhov clearly, smiling and looking NikolГЎy straight in the eyes, вҖңyou know the saying, вҖҳLucky in love, unlucky at cards.вҖҷ Your cousin is in love with you, I know.вҖқ вҖңOh, itвҖҷs terrible to feel oneself so in this manвҖҷs power,вҖқ thought RostГіv. He knew what a shock he would inflict on his father and mother by the news of this loss, he knew what a relief it would be to escape it all, and felt that DГіlokhov knew that he could save him from all this shame and sorrow, but wanted now to play with him as a cat does with a mouse. вҖңYour cousinвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ DГіlokhov started to say, but NikolГЎy interrupted him. вҖңMy cousin has nothing to do with this and itвҖҷs not necessary to mention her!вҖқ he exclaimed fiercely. вҖңThen when am I to have it?вҖқ вҖңTomorrow,вҖқ replied RostГіv and left the room. XV XV To say вҖңtomorrowвҖқ and keep up a dignified tone was not difficult, but to go home alone, see his sisters, brother, mother, and father, confess and ask for money he had no right to after giving his word of honor, was terrible. At home, they had not yet gone to bed. The young people, after returning from the theater, had had supper and were grouped round the clavichord. As soon as NikolГЎy entered, he was enfolded in that poetic atmosphere of love which pervaded the RostГіv household that winter and, now after DГіlokhovвҖҷs proposal and IogelвҖҷs ball, seemed to have grown thicker round SГіnya and NatГЎsha as the air does before a thunderstorm. SГіnya and NatГЎsha, in the light-blue dresses they had worn at the theater, looking pretty and conscious of it, were standing by the clavichord, happy and smiling. VГ©ra was playing chess with ShinshГӯn in the drawing room. The old countess, waiting for the return of her husband and son, sat playing patience with the old gentlewoman who lived in their house. DenГӯsov, with sparkling eyes and ruffled hair, sat at the clavichord striking chords with his short fingers, his legs thrown back and his eyes rolling as he sang, with his small, husky, but true voice, some verses called вҖңEnchantress,вҖқ which he had composed, and to which he was trying to fit music: Enchantress, say, to my forsaken lyre What magic power is this recalls me still? What spark has set my inmost soul on fire, What is this bliss that makes my fingers thrill? He was singing in passionate tones, gazing with his sparkling black-agate eyes at the frightened and happy NatГЎsha. вҖңSplendid! Excellent!вҖқ exclaimed NatГЎsha. вҖңAnother verse,вҖқ she said, without noticing NikolГЎy. вҖңEverythingвҖҷs still the same with them,вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, glancing into the drawing room, where he saw VГ©ra and his mother with the old lady. вҖңAh, and hereвҖҷs NikГіlenka!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha, running up to him. вҖңIs Papa at home?вҖқ he asked. вҖңI am so glad youвҖҷve come!вҖқ said NatГЎsha, without answering him. вҖңWe are enjoying ourselves! VasГӯli DmГӯtrich is staying a day longer for my sake! Did you know?вҖқ вҖңNo, Papa is not back yet,вҖқ said SГіnya. вҖңNikolГЎy, have you come? Come here, dear!вҖқ called the old countess from the drawing room. NikolГЎy went to her, kissed her hand, and sitting down silently at her table began to watch her hands arranging the cards. From the dancing room, they still heard the laughter and merry voices trying to persuade NatГЎsha to sing. вҖңAll wight! All wight!вҖқ shouted DenГӯsov. вҖңItвҖҷs no good making excuses now! ItвҖҷs your turn to sing the baвҖҷcawollaвҒ вҖ”I entweat you!вҖқ The countess glanced at her silent son. вҖңWhat is the matter?вҖқ she asked. вҖңOh, nothing,вҖқ said he, as if weary of being continually asked the same question. вҖңWill Papa be back soon?вҖқ вҖңI expect so.вҖқ вҖңEverythingвҖҷs the same with them. They know nothing about it! Where am I to go?вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, and went again into the dancing room where the clavichord stood. SГіnya was sitting at the clavichord, playing the prelude to DenГӯsovвҖҷs favorite barcarolle. NatГЎsha was preparing to sing. DenГӯsov was looking at her with enraptured eyes. NikolГЎy began pacing up and down the room. вҖңWhy do they want to make her sing? How can she sing? ThereвҖҷs nothing to be happy about!вҖқ thought he. SГіnya struck the first chord of the prelude. вҖңMy God, IвҖҷm a ruined and dishonored man! A bullet through my brain is the only thing left meвҒ вҖ”not singing!вҖқ his thoughts ran on. вҖңGo away? But where to? ItвҖҷs oneвҒ вҖ”let them sing!вҖқ He continued to pace the room, looking gloomily at DenГӯsov and the girls and avoiding their eyes. вҖңNikГіlenka, what is the matter?вҖқ SГіnyaвҖҷs eyes fixed on him seemed to ask. She noticed at once that something had happened to him. NikolГЎy turned away from her. NatГЎsha too, with her quick instinct, had instantly noticed her brotherвҖҷs condition. But, though she noticed it, she was herself in such high spirits at that moment, so far from sorrow, sadness, or self-reproach, that she purposely deceived herself as young people often do. вҖңNo, I am too happy now to spoil my enjoyment by sympathy with anyoneвҖҷs sorrow,вҖқ she felt, and she said to herself: вҖңNo, I must be mistaken, he must be feeling happy, just as I am.вҖқ вҖңNow, SГіnya!вҖқ she said, going to the very middle of the room, where she considered the resonance was best. Having lifted her head and let her arms droop lifelessly, as ballet dancers do, NatГЎsha, rising energetically from her heels to her toes, stepped to the middle of the room and stood still. вҖңYes, thatвҖҷs me!вҖқ she seemed to say, answering the rapt gaze with which DenГӯsov followed her. вҖңAnd what is she so pleased about?вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, looking at his sister. вҖңWhy isnвҖҷt she dull and ashamed?вҖқ NatГЎsha took the first note, her throat swelled, her chest rose, her eyes became serious. At that moment she was oblivious of her surroundings, and from her smiling lips flowed sounds which anyone may produce at the same intervals and hold for the same time, but which leave you cold a thousand times and the thousand and first time thrill you and make you weep. NatГЎsha, that winter, had for the first time begun to sing seriously, mainly because DenГӯsov so delighted in her singing. She no longer sang as a child, there was no longer in her singing that comical, childish, painstaking effect that had been in it before; but she did not yet sing well, as all the connoisseurs who heard her said: вҖңIt is not trained, but it is a beautiful voice that must be trained.вҖқ Only they generally said this some time after she had finished singing. While that untrained voice, with its incorrect breathing and labored transitions, was sounding, even the connoisseurs said nothing, but only delighted in it and wished to hear it again. In her voice there was a virginal freshness, an unconsciousness of her own powers, and an as yet untrained velvety softness, which so mingled with her lack of art in singing that it seemed as if nothing in that voice could be altered without spoiling it. вҖңWhat is this?вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, listening to her with widely opened eyes. вҖңWhat has happened to her? How she is singing today!вҖқ And suddenly the whole world centered for him on anticipation of the next note, the next phrase, and everything in the world was divided into three beats: вҖңOh mio crudele affetto.вҖқвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ One, two, threeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ one, two, threeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ OneвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңOh mio crudele affetto.вҖқвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ One, two, threeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ One. вҖңOh, this senseless life of ours!вҖқ thought NikolГЎy. вҖңAll this misery, and money, and DГіlokhov, and anger, and honorвҒ вҖ”itвҖҷs all nonsenseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but this is real.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Now then, NatГЎsha, now then, dearest! Now then, darling! How will she take that si? SheвҖҷs taken it! Thank God!вҖқ And without noticing that he was singing, to strengthen the si he sung a second, a third below the high note. вҖңAh, God! How fine! Did I really take it? How fortunate!вҖқ he thought. Oh, how that chord vibrated, and how moved was something that was finest in RostГіvвҖҷs soul! And this something was apart from everything else in the world and above everything in the world. вҖңWhat were losses, and DГіlokhov, and words of honor?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ All nonsense! One might kill and rob and yet be happy.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ XVI XVI It was long since RostГіv had felt such enjoyment from music as he did that day. But no sooner had NatГЎsha finished her barcarolle than reality again presented itself. He got up without saying a word and went downstairs to his own room. A quarter of an hour later the old count came in from his club, cheerful and contented. NikolГЎy, hearing him drive up, went to meet him. вҖңWellвҒ вҖ”had a good time?вҖқ said the old count, smiling gaily and proudly at his son. NikolГЎy tried to say вҖңYes,вҖқ but could not: and he nearly burst into sobs. The count was lighting his pipe and did not notice his sonвҖҷs condition. вҖңAh, it canвҖҷt be avoided!вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, for the first and last time. And suddenly, in the most casual tone, which made him feel ashamed of himself, he said, as if merely asking his father to let him have the carriage to drive to town: вҖңPapa, I have come on a matter of business. I was nearly forgetting. I need some money.вҖқ вҖңDear me!вҖқ said his father, who was in a specially good humor. вҖңI told you it would not be enough. How much?вҖқ вҖңVery much,вҖқ said NikolГЎy flushing, and with a stupid careless smile, for which he was long unable to forgive himself, вҖңI have lost a little, I mean a good deal, a great dealвҒ вҖ”forty three thousand.вҖқ вҖңWhat! To whom?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Nonsense!вҖқ cried the count, suddenly reddening with an apoplectic flush over neck and nape as old people do. вҖңI promised to pay tomorrow,вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңWell!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said the old count, spreading out his arms and sinking helplessly on the sofa. вҖңIt canвҖҷt be helped! It happens to everyone!вҖқ said the son, with a bold, free, and easy tone, while in his soul he regarded himself as a worthless scoundrel whose whole life could not atone for his crime. He longed to kiss his fatherвҖҷs hands and kneel to beg his forgiveness, but said, in a careless and even rude voice, that it happens to everyone! The old count cast down his eyes on hearing his sonвҖҷs words and began bustlingly searching for something. вҖңYes, yes,вҖқ he muttered, вҖңit will be difficult, I fear, difficult to raiseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ happens to everybody! Yes, who has not done it?вҖқ And with a furtive glance at his sonвҖҷs face, the count went out of the room.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ NikolГЎy had been prepared for resistance, but had not at all expected this. вҖңPapa! Papa!вҖқ he called after him, sobbing, вҖңforgive me!вҖқ And seizing his fatherвҖҷs hand, he pressed it to his lips and burst into tears. While father and son were having their explanation, the mother and daughter were having one not less important. NatГЎsha came running to her mother, quite excited. вҖңMamma!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Mamma!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ He has made meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңMade what?вҖқ вҖңMade, made me an offer, Mamma! Mamma!вҖқ she exclaimed. The countess did not believe her ears. DenГӯsov had proposed. To whom? To this chit of a girl, NatГЎsha, who not so long ago was playing with dolls and who was still having lessons. вҖңDonвҖҷt, NatГЎsha! What nonsense!вҖқ she said, hoping it was a joke. вҖңNonsense, indeed! I am telling you the fact,вҖқ said NatГЎsha indignantly. вҖңI come to ask you what to do, and you call it вҖҳnonsense!вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ The countess shrugged her shoulders. вҖңIf it is true that Monsieur DenГӯsov has made you a proposal, tell him he is a fool, thatвҖҷs all!вҖқ вҖңNo, heвҖҷs not a fool!вҖқ replied NatГЎsha indignantly and seriously. вҖңWell then, what do you want? YouвҖҷre all in love nowadays. Well, if you are in love, marry him!вҖқ said the countess, with a laugh of annoyance. вҖңGood luck to you!вҖқ вҖңNo, Mamma, IвҖҷm not in love with him, I suppose IвҖҷm not in love with him.вҖқ вҖңWell then, tell him so.вҖқ вҖңMamma, are you cross? DonвҖҷt be cross, dear! Is it my fault?вҖқ вҖңNo, but what is it, my dear? Do you want me to go and tell him?вҖқ said the countess smiling. вҖңNo, I will do it myself, only tell me what to say. ItвҖҷs all very well for you,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, with a responsive smile. вҖңYou should have seen how he said it! I know he did not mean to say it, but it came out accidently.вҖқ вҖңWell, all the same, you must refuse him.вҖқ вҖңNo, I mustnвҖҷt. I am so sorry for him! HeвҖҷs so nice.вҖқ вҖңWell then, accept his offer. ItвҖҷs high time for you to be married,вҖқ answered the countess sharply and sarcastically. вҖңNo, Mamma, but IвҖҷm so sorry for him. I donвҖҷt know how IвҖҷm to say it.вҖқ вҖңAnd thereвҖҷs nothing for you to say. I shall speak to him myself,вҖқ said the countess, indignant that they should have dared to treat this little NatГЎsha as grown up. вҖңNo, not on any account! I will tell him myself, and youвҖҷll listen at the door,вҖқ and NatГЎsha ran across the drawing room to the dancing hall, where DenГӯsov was sitting on the same chair by the clavichord with his face in his hands. He jumped up at the sound of her light step. вҖңNatГЎli,вҖқ he said, moving with rapid steps toward her, вҖңdecide my fate. It is in your hands.вҖқ вҖңVasГӯli DmГӯtrich, IвҖҷm so sorry for you!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No, but you are so niceвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but it wonвҖҷt doвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ not thatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but as a friend, I shall always love you.вҖқ DenГӯsov bent over her hand and she heard strange sounds she did not understand. She kissed his rough curly black head. At this instant, they heard the quick rustle of the countessвҖҷ dress. She came up to them. вҖңVasГӯli DmГӯtrich, I thank you for the honor,вҖқ she said, with an embarrassed voice, though it sounded severe to DenГӯsovвҒ вҖ”вҖңbut my daughter is so young, and I thought that, as my sonвҖҷs friend, you would have addressed yourself first to me. In that case you would not have obliged me to give this refusal.вҖқ вҖңCountessвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said DenГӯsov, with downcast eyes and a guilty face. He tried to say more, but faltered. NatГЎsha could not remain calm, seeing him in such a plight. She began to sob aloud. вҖңCountess, I have done wвҖҷong,вҖқ DenГӯsov went on in an unsteady voice, вҖңbut believe me, I so adore your daughter and all your family that I would give my life twice overвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He looked at the countess, and seeing her severe face said: вҖңWell, goodbye, Countess,вҖқ and kissing her hand, he left the room with quick resolute strides, without looking at NatГЎsha. Next day RostГіv saw DenГӯsov off. He did not wish to stay another day in Moscow. All DenГӯsovвҖҷs Moscow friends gave him a farewell entertainment at the gypsiesвҖҷ, with the result that he had no recollection of how he was put in the sleigh or of the first three stages of his journey. After DenГӯsovвҖҷs departure, RostГіv spent another fortnight in Moscow, without going out of the house, waiting for the money his father could not at once raise, and he spent most of his time in the girlsвҖҷ room. SГіnya was more tender and devoted to him than ever. It was as if she wanted to show him that his losses were an achievement that made her love him all the more, but NikolГЎy now considered himself unworthy of her. He filled the girlsвҖҷ albums with verses and music, and having at last sent DГіlokhov the whole forty-three thousand rubles and received his receipt, he left at the end of November, without taking leave of any of his acquaintances, to overtake his regiment which was already in Poland. II II On his return to Moscow from the army, NikolГЎy RostГіv was welcomed by his home circle as the best of sons, a hero, and their darling NikolГәshka; by his relations as a charming, attractive, and polite young man; by his acquaintances as a handsome lieutenant of hussars, a good dancer, and one of the best matches in the city. The RostГіvs knew everybody in Moscow. The old count had money enough that year, as all his estates had been remortgaged, and so NikolГәshka, acquiring a trotter of his own, very stylish riding breeches of the latest cut, such as no one else yet had in Moscow, and boots of the latest fashion, with extremely pointed toes and small silver spurs, passed his time very gaily. After a short period of adapting himself to the old conditions of life, NikolГЎy found it very pleasant to be at home again. He felt that he had grown up and matured very much. His despair at failing in a Scripture examination, his borrowing money from GavrГӯl to pay a sleigh driver, his kissing SГіnya on the slyвҒ вҖ”he now recalled all this as childishness he had left immeasurably behind. Now he was a lieutenant of hussars, in a jacket laced with silver, and wearing the Cross of St.В George, awarded to soldiers for bravery in action, and in the company of well-known, elderly, and respected racing men was training a trotter of his own for a race. He knew a lady on one of the boulevards whom he visited of an evening. He led the mazurka at the ArkhГЎrovsвҖҷ ball, talked about the war with Field Marshal KГЎmenski, visited the English Club, and was on intimate terms with a colonel of forty to whom DenГӯsov had introduced him. His passion for the Emperor had cooled somewhat in Moscow. But still, as he did not see him and had no opportunity of seeing him, he often spoke about him and about his love for him, letting it be understood that he had not told all and that there was something in his feelings for the Emperor not everyone could understand, and with his whole soul he shared the adoration then common in Moscow for the Emperor, who was spoken of as the вҖңangel incarnate.вҖқ During RostГіvвҖҷs short stay in Moscow, before rejoining the army, he did not draw closer to SГіnya, but rather drifted away from her. She was very pretty and sweet, and evidently deeply in love with him, but he was at the period of youth when there seems so much to do that there is no time for that sort of thing and a young man fears to bind himself and prizes his freedom which he needs for so many other things. When he thought of SГіnya, during this stay in Moscow, he said to himself, вҖңAh, there will be, and there are, many more such girls somewhere whom I do not yet know. There will be time enough to think about love when I want to, but now I have no time.вҖқ Besides, it seemed to him that the society of women was rather derogatory to his manhood. He went to balls and into ladiesвҖҷ society with an affectation of doing so against his will. The races, the English Club, sprees with DenГӯsov, and visits to a certain houseвҒ вҖ”that was another matter and quite the thing for a dashing young hussar! At the beginning of March, old Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich RostГіv was very busy arranging a dinner in honor of Prince BagratiГіn at the English Club. The count walked up and down the hall in his dressing gown, giving orders to the club steward and to the famous FeoktГӯst, the clubвҖҷs head cook, about asparagus, fresh cucumbers, strawberries, veal, and fish for this dinner. The count had been a member and on the committee of the club from the day it was founded. To him the club entrusted the arrangement of the festival in honor of BagratiГіn, for few men knew so well how to arrange a feast on an openhanded, hospitable scale, and still fewer men would be so well able and willing to make up out of their own resources what might be needed for the success of the fГӘte. The club cook and the steward listened to the countвҖҷs orders with pleased faces, for they knew that under no other management could they so easily extract a good profit for themselves from a dinner costing several thousand rubles. вҖңWell then, mind and have cocksвҖҷ comb in the turtle soup, you know!вҖқ вҖңShall we have three cold dishes then?вҖқ asked the cook. The count considered. вҖңWe canвҖҷt have lessвҒ вҖ”yes, threeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the mayonnaise, thatвҖҷs one,вҖқ said he, bending down a finger. вҖңThen am I to order those large sterlets?вҖқ asked the steward. вҖңYes, it canвҖҷt be helped if they wonвҖҷt take less. Ah, dear me! I was forgetting. We must have another entrГ©e. Ah, goodness gracious!вҖқ he clutched at his head. вҖңWho is going to get me the flowers? MГӯtenka! Eh, MГӯtenka! Gallop off to our Moscow estate,вҖқ he said to the factotum who appeared at his call. вҖңHurry off and tell MaksГӯmka, the gardener, to set the serfs to work. Say that everything out of the hothouses must be brought here well wrapped up in felt. I must have two hundred pots here on Friday.вҖқ Having given several more orders, he was about to go to his вҖңlittle countessвҖқ to have a rest, but remembering something else of importance, he returned again, called back the cook and the club steward, and again began giving orders. A light footstep and the clinking of spurs were heard at the door, and the young count, handsome, rosy, with a dark little mustache, evidently rested and made sleeker by his easy life in Moscow, entered the room. вҖңAh, my boy, my headвҖҷs in a whirl!вҖқ said the old man with a smile, as if he felt a little confused before his son. вҖңNow, if you would only help a bit! I must have singers too. I shall have my own orchestra, but shouldnвҖҷt we get the gypsy singers as well? You military men like that sort of thing.вҖқ вҖңReally, Papa, I believe Prince BagratiГіn worried himself less before the battle of SchГ¶n Grabern than you do now,вҖқ said his son with a smile. The old count pretended to be angry. вҖңYes, you talk, but try it yourself!вҖқ And the count turned to the cook, who, with a shrewd and respectful expression, looked observantly and sympathetically at the father and son. вҖңWhat have the young people come to nowadays, eh, FeoktГӯst?вҖқ said he. вҖңLaughing at us old fellows!вҖқ вҖңThatвҖҷs so, your excellency, all they have to do is to eat a good dinner, but providing it and serving it all up, thatвҖҷs not their business!вҖқ вҖңThatвҖҷs it, thatвҖҷs it!вҖқ exclaimed the count, and gaily seizing his son by both hands, he cried, вҖңNow IвҖҷve got you, so take the sleigh and pair at once, and go to BezГәkhovвҖҷs, and tell him вҖҳCount IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich has sent you to ask for strawberries and fresh pineapples.вҖҷ We canвҖҷt get them from anyone else. HeвҖҷs not there himself, so youвҖҷll have to go in and ask the princesses; and from there go on to the RasgulyГЎyвҒ вҖ”the coachman IpГЎtka knowsвҒ вҖ”and look up the gypsy IlyГәshka, the one who danced at Count OrlГіvвҖҷs, you remember, in a white Cossack coat, and bring him along to me.вҖқ вҖңAnd am I to bring the gypsy girls along with him?вҖқ asked NikolГЎy, laughing. вҖңDear, dear!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ At that moment, with noiseless footsteps and with the businesslike, preoccupied, yet meekly Christian look which never left her face, Anna MikhГЎylovna entered the hall. Though she came upon the count in his dressing gown every day, he invariably became confused and begged her to excuse his costume. вҖңNo matter at all, my dear count,вҖқ she said, meekly closing her eyes. вҖңBut IвҖҷll go to BezГәkhovвҖҷs myself. Pierre has arrived, and now we shall get anything we want from his hothouses. I have to see him in any case. He has forwarded me a letter from BorГӯs. Thank God, BorГӯs is now on the staff.вҖқ The count was delighted at Anna MikhГЎylovnaвҖҷs taking upon herself one of his commissions and ordered the small closed carriage for her. вҖңTell BezГәkhov to come. IвҖҷll put his name down. Is his wife with him?вҖқ he asked. Anna MikhГЎylovna turned up her eyes, and profound sadness was depicted on her face. вҖңAh, my dear friend, he is very unfortunate,вҖқ she said. вҖңIf what we hear is true, it is dreadful. How little we dreamed of such a thing when we were rejoicing at his happiness! And such a lofty angelic soul as young BezГәkhov! Yes, I pity him from my heart, and shall try to give him what consolation I can.вҖқ вҖңWh-what is the matter?вҖқ asked both the young and old RostГіv. Anna MikhГЎylovna sighed deeply. вҖңDГіlokhov, MГЎrya IvГЎnovnaвҖҷs son,вҖқ she said in a mysterious whisper, вҖңhas compromised her completely, they say. Pierre took him up, invited him to his house in Petersburg, and nowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ she has come here and that daredevil after her!вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna, wishing to show her sympathy for Pierre, but by involuntary intonations and a half smile betraying her sympathy for the вҖңdaredevil,вҖқ as she called DГіlokhov. вҖңThey say Pierre is quite broken by his misfortune.вҖқ вҖңDear, dear! But still tell him to come to the clubвҒ вҖ”it will all blow over. It will be a tremendous banquet.вҖқ Next day, the third of March, soon after one oвҖҷclock, two hundred and fifty members of the English Club and fifty guests were awaiting the guest of honor and hero of the Austrian campaign, Prince BagratiГіn, to dinner. On the first arrival of the news of the battle of Austerlitz, Moscow had been bewildered. At that time, the Russians were so used to victories that on receiving news of the defeat some would simply not believe it, while others sought some extraordinary explanation of so strange an event. In the English Club, where all who were distinguished, important, and well informed foregathered when the news began to arrive in December, nothing was said about the war and the last battle, as though all were in a conspiracy of silence. The men who set the tone in conversationвҒ вҖ”Count RostopchГӯn, Prince YГәri VladГӯmirovich DolgorГәkov, ValГәev, Count MarkГіv, and Prince VyГЎzemskiвҒ вҖ”did not show themselves at the club, but met in private houses in intimate circles, and the Moscovites who took their opinions from othersвҒ вҖ”Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich RostГіv among themвҒ вҖ”remained for a while without any definite opinion on the subject of the war and without leaders. The Moscovites felt that something was wrong and that to discuss the bad news was difficult, and so it was best to be silent. But after a while, just as a jury comes out of its room, the bigwigs who guided the clubвҖҷs opinion reappeared, and everybody began speaking clearly and definitely. Reasons were found for the incredible, unheard-of, and impossible event of a Russian defeat, everything became clear, and in all corners of Moscow the same things began to be said. These reasons were the treachery of the Austrians, a defective commissariat, the treachery of the Pole PrzebyszГ©wski and of the Frenchman Langeron, KutГәzovвҖҷs incapacity, and (it was whispered) the youth and inexperience of the sovereign, who had trusted worthless and insignificant people. But the army, the Russian army, everyone declared, was extraordinary and had achieved miracles of valor. The soldiers, officers, and generals were heroes. But the hero of heroes was Prince BagratiГіn, distinguished by his SchГ¶n Grabern affair and by the retreat from Austerlitz, where he alone had withdrawn his column unbroken and had all day beaten back an enemy force twice as numerous as his own. What also conduced to BagratiГіnвҖҷs being selected as MoscowвҖҷs hero was the fact that he had no connections in the city and was a stranger there. In his person, honor was shown to a simple fighting Russian soldier without connections and intrigues, and to one who was associated by memories of the Italian campaign with the name of SuvГіrov. Moreover, paying such honor to BagratiГіn was the best way of expressing disapproval and dislike of KutГәzov. вҖңHad there been no BagratiГіn, it would have been necessary to invent him,вҖқ said the wit ShinshГӯn, parodying the words of Voltaire. KutГәzov no one spoke of, except some who abused him in whispers, calling him a court weathercock and an old satyr. All Moscow repeated Prince DolgorГәkovвҖҷs saying: вҖңIf you go on modeling and modeling you must get smeared with clay,вҖқ suggesting consolation for our defeat by the memory of former victories; and the words of RostopchГӯn, that French soldiers have to be incited to battle by highfalutin words, and Germans by logical arguments to show them that it is more dangerous to run away than to advance, but that Russian soldiers only need to be restrained and held back! On all sides, new and fresh anecdotes were heard of individual examples of heroism shown by our officers and men at Austerlitz. One had saved a standard, another had killed five Frenchmen, a third had loaded five cannon singlehanded. Berg was mentioned, by those who did not know him, as having, when wounded in the right hand, taken his sword in the left, and gone forward. Of BolkГіnski, nothing was said, and only those who knew him intimately regretted that he had died so young, leaving a pregnant wife with his eccentric father. III III On that third of March, all the rooms in the English Club were filled with a hum of conversation, like the hum of bees swarming in springtime. The members and guests of the club wandered hither and thither, sat, stood, met, and separated, some in uniform and some in evening dress, and a few here and there with powdered hair and in Russian kaftГЎns. Powdered footmen, in livery with buckled shoes and smart stockings, stood at every door anxiously noting visitorsвҖҷ every movement in order to offer their services. Most of those present were elderly, respected men with broad, self-confident faces, fat fingers, and resolute gestures and voices. This class of guests and members sat in certain habitual places and met in certain habitual groups. A minority of those present were casual guestsвҒ вҖ”chiefly young men, among whom were DenГӯsov, RostГіv, and DГіlokhovвҒ вҖ”who was now again an officer in the SemГ«nov regiment. The faces of these young people, especially those who were military men, bore that expression of condescending respect for their elders which seems to say to the older generation, вҖңWe are prepared to respect and honor you, but all the same remember that the future belongs to us.вҖқ NesvГӯtski was there as an old member of the club. Pierre, who at his wifeвҖҷs command had let his hair grow and abandoned his spectacles, went about the rooms fashionably dressed but looking sad and dull. Here, as elsewhere, he was surrounded by an atmosphere of subservience to his wealth, and being in the habit of lording it over these people, he treated them with absentminded contempt. By his age he should have belonged to the younger men, but by his wealth and connections he belonged to the groups of old and honored guests, and so he went from one group to another. Some of the most important old men were the center of groups which even strangers approached respectfully to hear the voices of well-known men. The largest circles formed round Count RostopchГӯn, ValГәev, and NarГҪshkin. RostopchГӯn was describing how the Russians had been overwhelmed by flying Austrians and had had to force their way through them with bayonets. ValГәev was confidentially telling that UvГЎrov had been sent from Petersburg to ascertain what Moscow was thinking about Austerlitz. In the third circle, NarГҪshkin was speaking of the meeting of the Austrian Council of War at which SuvГіrov crowed like a cock in reply to the nonsense talked by the Austrian generals. ShinshГӯn, standing close by, tried to make a joke, saying that KutГәzov had evidently failed to learn from SuvГіrov even so simple a thing as the art of crowing like a cock, but the elder members glanced severely at the wit, making him feel that in that place and on that day, it was improper to speak so of KutГәzov. Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich RostГіv, hurried and preoccupied, went about in his soft boots between the dining and drawing rooms, hastily greeting the important and unimportant, all of whom he knew, as if they were all equals, while his eyes occasionally sought out his fine well-set-up young son, resting on him and winking joyfully at him. Young RostГіv stood at a window with DГіlokhov, whose acquaintance he had lately made and highly valued. The old count came up to them and pressed DГіlokhovвҖҷs hand. вҖңPlease come and visit usвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ you know my brave boyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ been together out thereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ both playing the heroвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Ah, VasГӯli IgnГЎtovichвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ How dвҖҷye do, old fellow?вҖқ he said, turning to an old man who was passing, but before he had finished his greeting there was a general stir, and a footman who had run in announced, with a frightened face: вҖңHeвҖҷs arrived!вҖқ Bells rang, the stewards rushed forward, andвҒ вҖ”like rye shaken together in a shovelвҒ вҖ”the guests who had been scattered about in different rooms came together and crowded in the large drawing room by the door of the ballroom. BagratiГіn appeared in the doorway of the anteroom without hat or sword, which, in accord with the club custom, he had given up to the hall porter. He had no lambskin cap on his head, nor had he a loaded whip over his shoulder, as when RostГіv had seen him on the eve of the battle of Austerlitz, but wore a tight new uniform with Russian and foreign Orders, and the Star of St.В George on his left breast. Evidently just before coming to the dinner he had had his hair and whiskers trimmed, which changed his appearance for the worse. There was something naively festive in his air, which, in conjunction with his firm and virile features, gave him a rather comical expression. BekleshГ«v and FГ«dor PetrГіvich UvГЎrov, who had arrived with him, paused at the doorway to allow him, as the guest of honor, to enter first. BagratiГіn was embarrassed, not wishing to avail himself of their courtesy, and this caused some delay at the doors, but after all he did at last enter first. He walked shyly and awkwardly over the parquet floor of the reception room, not knowing what to do with his hands; he was more accustomed to walk over a plowed field under fire, as he had done at the head of the Kursk regiment at SchГ¶n GrabernвҒ вҖ”and he would have found that easier. The committeemen met him at the first door and, expressing their delight at seeing such a highly honored guest, took possession of him as it were, without waiting for his reply, surrounded him, and led him to the drawing room. It was at first impossible to enter the drawing room door for the crowd of members and guests jostling one another and trying to get a good look at BagratiГіn over each otherвҖҷs shoulders, as if he were some rare animal. Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich, laughing and repeating the words, вҖңMake way, dear boy! Make way, make way!вҖқ pushed through the crowd more energetically than anyone, led the guests into the drawing room, and seated them on the center sofa. The bigwigs, the most respected members of the club, beset the new arrivals. Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich, again thrusting his way through the crowd, went out of the drawing room and reappeared a minute later with another committeeman, carrying a large silver salver which he presented to Prince BagratiГіn. On the salver lay some verses composed and printed in the heroвҖҷs honor. BagratiГіn, on seeing the salver, glanced around in dismay, as though seeking help. But all eyes demanded that he should submit. Feeling himself in their power, he resolutely took the salver with both hands and looked sternly and reproachfully at the count who had presented it to him. Someone obligingly took the dish from BagratiГіn (or he would, it seemed, have held it till evening and have gone in to dinner with it) and drew his attention to the verses. вҖңWell, I will read them, then!вҖқ BagratiГіn seemed to say, and, fixing his weary eyes on the paper, began to read them with a fixed and serious expression. But the author himself took the verses and began reading them aloud. BagratiГіn bowed his head and listened: Bring glory then to AlexanderвҖҷs reign And on the throne our Titus shield. A dreaded foe be thou, kindhearted as a man, A Rhipheus at home, a Caesar in the field! EвҖҷen fortunate Napoleon Knows by experience, now, BagratiГіn, And dare not Herculean Russians troubleвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But before he had finished reading, a stentorian majordomo announced that dinner was ready! The door opened, and from the dining room came the resounding strains of the polonaise: ConquestвҖҷs joyful thunder waken, Triumph, valiant Russians, now!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich, glancing angrily at the author who went on reading his verses, bowed to BagratiГіn. Everyone rose, feeling that dinner was more important than verses, and BagratiГіn, again preceding all the rest, went in to dinner. He was seated in the place of honor between two AlexandersвҒ вҖ”BekleshГ«v and NarГҪshkinвҒ вҖ”which was a significant allusion to the name of the sovereign. Three hundred persons took their seats in the dining room, according to their rank and importance: the more important nearer to the honored guest, as naturally as water flows deepest where the land lies lowest. Just before dinner, Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich presented his son to BagratiГіn, who recognized him and said a few words to him, disjointed and awkward, as were all the words he spoke that day, and Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich looked joyfully and proudly around while BagratiГіn spoke to his son. NikolГЎy RostГіv, with DenГӯsov and his new acquaintance, DГіlokhov, sat almost at the middle of the table. Facing them sat Pierre, beside Prince NesvГӯtski. Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich with the other members of the committee sat facing BagratiГіn and, as the very personification of Moscow hospitality, did the honors to the prince. His efforts had not been in vain. The dinner, both the Lenten and the other fare, was splendid, yet he could not feel quite at ease till the end of the meal. He winked at the butler, whispered directions to the footmen, and awaited each expected dish with some anxiety. Everything was excellent. With the second course, a gigantic sterlet (at sight of which IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich blushed with self-conscious pleasure), the footmen began popping corks and filling the champagne glasses. After the fish, which made a certain sensation, the count exchanged glances with the other committeemen. вҖңThere will be many toasts, itвҖҷs time to begin,вҖқ he whispered, and taking up his glass, he rose. All were silent, waiting for what he would say. вҖңTo the health of our Sovereign, the Emperor!вҖқ he cried, and at the same moment his kindly eyes grew moist with tears of joy and enthusiasm. The band immediately struck up вҖңConquestвҖҷs joyful thunder wakenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ All rose and cried вҖңHurrah!вҖқ BagratiГіn also rose and shouted вҖңHurrah!вҖқ in exactly the same voice in which he had shouted it on the field at SchГ¶n Grabern. Young RostГіvвҖҷs ecstatic voice could be heard above the three hundred others. He nearly wept. вҖңTo the health of our Sovereign, the Emperor!вҖқ he roared, вҖңHurrah!вҖқ and emptying his glass at one gulp he dashed it to the floor. Many followed his example, and the loud shouting continued for a long time. When the voices subsided, the footmen cleared away the broken glass and everybody sat down again, smiling at the noise they had made and exchanging remarks. The old count rose once more, glanced at a note lying beside his plate, and proposed a toast, вҖңTo the health of the hero of our last campaign, Prince Pyotr IvГЎnovich BagratiГіn!вҖқ and again his blue eyes grew moist. вҖңHurrah!вҖқ cried the three hundred voices again, but instead of the band a choir began singing a cantata composed by PГЎvel IvГЎnovich KutГәzov: Russians! OвҖҷer all barriers on! Courage conquest guarantees; Have we not BagratiГіn? He brings foemen to their knees,вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ etc. As soon as the singing was over, another and another toast was proposed and Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich became more and more moved, more glass was smashed, and the shouting grew louder. They drank to BekleshГ«v, NarГҪshkin, UvГЎrov, DolgorГәkov, AprГЎksin, ValГәev, to the committee, to all the club members and to all the club guests, and finally to Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich separately, as the organizer of the banquet. At that toast, the count took out his handkerchief and, covering his face, wept outright. IV IV Pierre sat opposite DГіlokhov and NikolГЎy RostГіv. As usual, he ate and drank much, and eagerly. But those who knew him intimately noticed that some great change had come over him that day. He was silent all through dinner and looked about, blinking and scowling, or, with fixed eyes and a look of complete absentmindedness, kept rubbing the bridge of his nose. His face was depressed and gloomy. He seemed to see and hear nothing of what was going on around him and to be absorbed by some depressing and unsolved problem. The unsolved problem that tormented him was caused by hints given by the princess, his cousin, at Moscow, concerning DГіlokhovвҖҷs intimacy with his wife, and by an anonymous letter he had received that morning, which in the mean jocular way common to anonymous letters said that he saw badly through his spectacles, but that his wifeвҖҷs connection with DГіlokhov was a secret to no one but himself. Pierre absolutely disbelieved both the princessвҖҷ hints and the letter, but he feared now to look at DГіlokhov, who was sitting opposite him. Every time he chanced to meet DГіlokhovвҖҷs handsome insolent eyes, Pierre felt something terrible and monstrous rising in his soul and turned quickly away. Involuntarily recalling his wifeвҖҷs past and her relations with DГіlokhov, Pierre saw clearly that what was said in the letter might be true, or might at least seem to be true had it not referred to his wife. He involuntarily remembered how DГіlokhov, who had fully recovered his former position after the campaign, had returned to Petersburg and come to him. Availing himself of his friendly relations with Pierre as a boon companion, DГіlokhov had come straight to his house, and Pierre had put him up and lent him money. Pierre recalled how ElГЁn had smilingly expressed disapproval of DГіlokhovвҖҷs living at their house, and how cynically DГіlokhov had praised his wifeвҖҷs beauty to him and from that time till they came to Moscow had not left them for a day. вҖңYes, he is very handsome,вҖқ thought Pierre, вҖңand I know him. It would be particularly pleasant to him to dishonor my name and ridicule me, just because I have exerted myself on his behalf, befriended him, and helped him. I know and understand what a spice that would add to the pleasure of deceiving me, if it really were true. Yes, if it were true, but I do not believe it. I have no right to, and canвҖҷt, believe it.вҖқ He remembered the expression DГіlokhovвҖҷs face assumed in his moments of cruelty, as when tying the policeman to the bear and dropping them into the water, or when he challenged a man to a duel without any reason, or shot a postboyвҖҷs horse with a pistol. That expression was often on DГіlokhovвҖҷs face when looking at him. вҖңYes, he is a bully,вҖқ thought Pierre, вҖңto kill a man means nothing to him. It must seem to him that everyone is afraid of him, and that must please him. He must think that I, too, am afraid of himвҒ вҖ”and in fact I am afraid of him,вҖқ he thought, and again he felt something terrible and monstrous rising in his soul. DГіlokhov, DenГӯsov, and RostГіv were now sitting opposite Pierre and seemed very gay. RostГіv was talking merrily to his two friends, one of whom was a dashing hussar and the other a notorious duelist and rake, and every now and then he glanced ironically at Pierre, whose preoccupied, absentminded, and massive figure was a very noticeable one at the dinner. RostГіv looked inimically at Pierre, first because Pierre appeared to his hussar eyes as a rich civilian, the husband of a beauty, and in a wordвҒ вҖ”an old woman; and secondly because Pierre in his preoccupation and absentmindedness had not recognized RostГіv and had not responded to his greeting. When the EmperorвҖҷs health was drunk, Pierre, lost in thought, did not rise or lift his glass. вҖңWhat are you about?вҖқ shouted RostГіv, looking at him in an ecstasy of exasperation. вҖңDonвҖҷt you hear itвҖҷs His Majesty the EmperorвҖҷs health?вҖқ Pierre sighed, rose submissively, emptied his glass, and, waiting till all were seated again, turned with his kindly smile to RostГіv. вҖңWhy, I didnвҖҷt recognize you!вҖқ he said. But RostГіv was otherwise engaged; he was shouting вҖңHurrah!вҖқ вҖңWhy donвҖҷt you renew the acquaintance?вҖқ said DГіlokhov to RostГіv. вҖңConfound him, heвҖҷs a fool!вҖқ said RostГіv. вҖңOne should make up to the husbands of pretty women,вҖқ said DenГӯsov. Pierre did not catch what they were saying, but knew they were talking about him. He reddened and turned away. вҖңWell, now to the health of handsome women!вҖқ said DГіlokhov, and with a serious expression, but with a smile lurking at the corners of his mouth, he turned with his glass to Pierre. вҖңHereвҖҷs to the health of lovely women, PГ©trushaвҒ вҖ”and their lovers!вҖқ he added. Pierre, with downcast eyes, drank out of his glass without looking at DГіlokhov or answering him. The footman, who was distributing leaflets with KutГәzovвҖҷs cantata, laid one before Pierre as one of the principal guests. He was just going to take it when DГіlokhov, leaning across, snatched it from his hand and began reading it. Pierre looked at DГіlokhov and his eyes dropped, the something terrible and monstrous that had tormented him all dinnertime rose and took possession of him. He leaned his whole massive body across the table. вҖңHow dare you take it?вҖқ he shouted. Hearing that cry and seeing to whom it was addressed, NesvГӯtski and the neighbor on his right quickly turned in alarm to BezГәkhov. вҖңDonвҖҷt! DonвҖҷt! What are you about?вҖқ whispered their frightened voices. DГіlokhov looked at Pierre with clear, mirthful, cruel eyes, and that smile of his which seemed to say, вҖңAh! This is what I like!вҖқ вҖңYou shanвҖҷt have it!вҖқ he said distinctly. Pale, with quivering lips, Pierre snatched the copy. вҖңYouвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ! youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ scoundrel! I challenge you!вҖқ he ejaculated, and, pushing back his chair, he rose from the table. At the very instant he did this and uttered those words, Pierre felt that the question of his wifeвҖҷs guilt which had been tormenting him the whole day was finally and indubitably answered in the affirmative. He hated her and was forever sundered from her. Despite DenГӯsovвҖҷs request that he would take no part in the matter, RostГіv agreed to be DГіlokhovвҖҷs second, and after dinner he discussed the arrangements for the duel with NesvГӯtski, BezГәkhovвҖҷs second. Pierre went home, but RostГіv with DГіlokhov and DenГӯsov stayed on at the club till late, listening to the gypsies and other singers. вҖңWell then, till tomorrow at SokГіlniki,вҖқ said DГіlokhov, as he took leave of RostГіv in the club porch. вҖңAnd do you feel quite calm?вҖқ RostГіv asked. DГіlokhov paused. вҖңWell, you see, IвҖҷll tell you the whole secret of dueling in two words. If you are going to fight a duel, and you make a will and write affectionate letters to your parents, and if you think you may be killed, you are a fool and are lost for certain. But go with the firm intention of killing your man as quickly and surely as possible, and then all will be right, as our bear huntsman at KostromГЎ used to tell me. вҖҳEveryone fears a bear,вҖҷ he says, вҖҳbut when you see one your fearвҖҷs all gone, and your only thought is not to let him get away!вҖҷ And thatвҖҷs how it is with me. ГҖ demain, mon cher.вҖқ44 Next day, at eight in the morning, Pierre and NesvГӯtski drove to the SokГіlniki forest and found DГіlokhov, DenГӯsov, and RostГіv already there. Pierre had the air of a man preoccupied with considerations which had no connection with the matter in hand. His haggard face was yellow. He had evidently not slept that night. He looked about distractedly and screwed up his eyes as if dazzled by the sun. He was entirely absorbed by two considerations: his wifeвҖҷs guilt, of which after his sleepless night he had not the slightest doubt, and the guiltlessness of DГіlokhov, who had no reason to preserve the honor of a man who was nothing to him.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңI should perhaps have done the same thing in his place,вҖқ thought Pierre. вҖңItвҖҷs even certain that I should have done the same, then why this duel, this murder? Either I shall kill him, or he will hit me in the head, or elbow, or knee. CanвҖҷt I go away from here, run away, bury myself somewhere?вҖқ passed through his mind. But just at moments when such thoughts occurred to him, he would ask in a particularly calm and absentminded way, which inspired the respect of the onlookers, вҖңWill it be long? Are things ready?вҖқ When all was ready, the sabers stuck in the snow to mark the barriers, and the pistols loaded, NesvГӯtski went up to Pierre. вҖңI should not be doing my duty, Count,вҖқ he said in timid tones, вҖңand should not justify your confidence and the honor you have done me in choosing me for your second, if at this grave, this very grave, moment I did not tell you the whole truth. I think there is no sufficient ground for this affair, or for blood to be shed over it.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You were not right, not quite in the right, you were impetuousвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңOh yes, it is horribly stupid,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңThen allow me to express your regrets, and I am sure your opponent will accept them,вҖқ said NesvГӯtski (who like the others concerned in the affair, and like everyone in similar cases, did not yet believe that the affair had come to an actual duel). вҖңYou know, Count, it is much more honorable to admit oneвҖҷs mistake than to let matters become irreparable. There was no insult on either side. Allow me to conveyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңNo! What is there to talk about?вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңItвҖҷs all the same.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Is everything ready?вҖқ he added. вҖңOnly tell me where to go and where to shoot,вҖқ he said with an unnaturally gentle smile. He took the pistol in his hand and began asking about the working of the trigger, as he had not before held a pistol in his handвҒ вҖ”a fact that he did not wish to confess. вҖңOh yes, like that, I know, I only forgot,вҖқ said he. вҖңNo apologies, none whatever,вҖқ said DГіlokhov to DenГӯsov (who on his side had been attempting a reconciliation), and he also went up to the appointed place. The spot chosen for the duel was some eighty paces from the road, where the sleighs had been left, in a small clearing in the pine forest covered with melting snow, the frost having begun to break up during the last few days. The antagonists stood forty paces apart at the farther edge of the clearing. The seconds, measuring the paces, left tracks in the deep wet snow between the place where they had been standing and NesvГӯtskiвҖҷs and DГіlokhovвҖҷs sabers, which were stuck into the ground ten paces apart to mark the barrier. It was thawing and misty; at forty pacesвҖҷ distance nothing could be seen. For three minutes all had been ready, but they still delayed and all were silent. V V вҖңWell, begin!вҖқ said DГіlokhov. вҖңAll right,вҖқ said Pierre, still smiling in the same way. A feeling of dread was in the air. It was evident that the affair so lightly begun could no longer be averted but was taking its course independently of menвҖҷs will. DenГӯsov first went to the barrier and announced: вҖңAs the adveвҖҷsawies have wefused a weconciliation, please pwoceed. Take your pistols, and at the word thwee begin to advance. вҖңO-ne! T-wo! Thwee!вҖқ he shouted angrily and stepped aside. The combatants advanced along the trodden tracks, nearer and nearer to one another, beginning to see one another through the mist. They had the right to fire when they liked as they approached the barrier. DГіlokhov walked slowly without raising his pistol, looking intently with his bright, sparkling blue eyes into his antagonistвҖҷs face. His mouth wore its usual semblance of a smile. вҖңSo I can fire when I like!вҖқ said Pierre, and at the word вҖңthree,вҖқ he went quickly forward, missing the trodden path and stepping into the deep snow. He held the pistol in his right hand at armвҖҷs length, apparently afraid of shooting himself with it. His left hand he held carefully back, because he wished to support his right hand with it and knew he must not do so. Having advanced six paces and strayed off the track into the snow, Pierre looked down at his feet, then quickly glanced at DГіlokhov and, bending his finger as he had been shown, fired. Not at all expecting so loud a report, Pierre shuddered at the sound and then, smiling at his own sensations, stood still. The smoke, rendered denser by the mist, prevented him from seeing anything for an instant, but there was no second report as he had expected. He only heard DГіlokhovвҖҷs hurried steps, and his figure came in view through the smoke. He was pressing one hand to his left side, while the other clutched his drooping pistol. His face was pale. RostГіv ran toward him and said something. вҖңNo-o-o!вҖқ muttered DГіlokhov through his teeth, вҖңno, itвҖҷs not over.вҖқ And after stumbling a few staggering steps right up to the saber, he sank on the snow beside it. His left hand was bloody; he wiped it on his coat and supported himself with it. His frowning face was pallid and quivered. вҖңPleaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ began DГіlokhov, but could not at first pronounce the word. вҖңPlease,вҖқ he uttered with an effort. Pierre, hardly restraining his sobs, began running toward DГіlokhov and was about to cross the space between the barriers, when DГіlokhov cried: вҖңTo your barrier!вҖқ and Pierre, grasping what was meant, stopped by his saber. Only ten paces divided them. DГіlokhov lowered his head to the snow, greedily bit at it, again raised his head, adjusted himself, drew in his legs and sat up, seeking a firm center of gravity. He sucked and swallowed the cold snow, his lips quivered but his eyes, still smiling, glittered with effort and exasperation as he mustered his remaining strength. He raised his pistol and aimed. вҖңSideways! Cover yourself with your pistol!вҖқ ejaculated NesvГӯtski. вҖңCover yourself!вҖқ even DenГӯsov cried to his adversary. Pierre, with a gentle smile of pity and remorse, his arms and legs helplessly spread out, stood with his broad chest directly facing DГіlokhov and looked sorrowfully at him. DenГӯsov, RostГіv, and NesvГӯtski closed their eyes. At the same instant they heard a report and DГіlokhovвҖҷs angry cry. вҖңMissed!вҖқ shouted DГіlokhov, and he lay helplessly, face downwards on the snow. Pierre clutched his temples, and turning round went into the forest, trampling through the deep snow, and muttering incoherent words: вҖңFollyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ folly! DeathвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ liesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he repeated, puckering his face. NesvГӯtski stopped him and took him home. RostГіv and DenГӯsov drove away with the wounded DГіlokhov. The latter lay silent in the sleigh with closed eyes and did not answer a word to the questions addressed to him.
