And now IвҖҷm from KolyГЎzin, master, where a great and wonderful blessing has been revealed.вҖқ вҖңAnd was IvГЎnushka with you?вҖқ вҖңI go by myself, benefactor,вҖқ said IvГЎnushka, trying to speak in a bass voice. вҖңI only came across PelagГ©yushka in YГәkhnovo.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ PelagГ©yushka interrupted her companion; she evidently wished to tell what she had seen. вҖңIn KolyГЎzin, master, a wonderful blessing has been revealed.вҖқ вҖңWhat is it? Some new relics?вҖқ asked Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңAndrГ©y, do leave off,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңDonвҖҷt tell him, PelagГ©yushka.вҖқ вҖңNoвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ why not, my dear, why shouldnвҖҷt I? I like him. He is kind, he is one of GodвҖҷs chosen, heвҖҷs a benefactor, he once gave me ten rubles, I remember. When I was in Kiev, Crazy KiryГәsha says to me (heвҖҷs one of GodвҖҷs own and goes barefoot summer and winter), he says, вҖҳWhy are you not going to the right place? Go to KolyГЎzin where a wonder-working icon of the Holy Mother of God has been revealed.вҖҷ On hearing those words I said goodbye to the holy folk and went.вҖқ All were silent, only the pilgrim woman went on in measured tones, drawing in her breath. вҖңSo I come, master, and the people say to me: вҖҳA great blessing has been revealed, holy oil trickles from the cheeks of our blessed Mother, the Holy Virgin Mother of God.вҖҷвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAll right, all right, you can tell us afterwards,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya, flushing. вҖңLet me ask her,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңDid you see it yourselves?вҖқ he inquired. вҖңOh, yes, master, I was found worthy. Such a brightness on the face like the light of heaven, and from the blessed MotherвҖҷs cheek it drops and drops.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңBut, dear me, that must be a fraud!вҖқ said Pierre, naively, who had listened attentively to the pilgrim. вҖңOh, master, what are you saying?вҖқ exclaimed the horrified PelagГ©yushka, turning to Princess MГЎrya for support. вҖңThey impose on the people,вҖқ he repeated. вҖңLord Jesus Christ!вҖқ exclaimed the pilgrim woman, crossing herself. вҖңOh, donвҖҷt speak so, master! There was a general who did not believe, and said, вҖҳThe monks cheat,вҖҷ and as soon as heвҖҷd said it he went blind. And he dreamed that the Holy Virgin Mother of the Kiev catacombs came to him and said, вҖҳBelieve in me and I will make you whole.вҖҷ So he begged: вҖҳTake me to her, take me to her.вҖҷ ItвҖҷs the real truth IвҖҷm telling you, I saw it myself. So he was brought, quite blind, straight to her, and he goes up to her and falls down and says, вҖҳMake me whole,вҖҷ says he, вҖҳand IвҖҷll give thee what the Tsar bestowed on me.вҖҷ I saw it myself, master, the star is fixed into the icon. Well, and what do you think? He received his sight! ItвҖҷs a sin to speak so. God will punish you,вҖқ she said admonishingly, turning to Pierre. вҖңHow did the star get into the icon?вҖқ Pierre asked. вҖңAnd was the Holy Mother promoted to the rank of general?вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, with a smile. PelagГ©yushka suddenly grew quite pale and clasped her hands. вҖңOh, master, master, what a sin! And you who have a son!вҖқ she began, her pallor suddenly turning to a vivid red. вҖңMaster, what have you said? God forgive you!вҖқ And she crossed herself. вҖңLord forgive him! My dear, what does it mean?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she asked, turning to Princess MГЎrya. She got up and, almost crying, began to arrange her wallet. She evidently felt frightened and ashamed to have accepted charity in a house where such things could be said, and was at the same time sorry to have now to forgo the charity of this house. вҖңNow, why need you do it?вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңWhy did you come to me?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңCome, PelagГ©yushka, I was joking,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңPrincesse, ma parole, je nвҖҷai pas voulu lвҖҷoffenser.57 I did not mean anything, I was only joking,вҖқ he said, smiling shyly and trying to efface his offense. вҖңIt was all my fault, and AndrГ©y was only joking.вҖқ PelagГ©yushka stopped doubtfully, but in PierreвҖҷs face there was such a look of sincere penitence, and Prince AndrГ©y glanced so meekly now at her and now at Pierre, that she was gradually reassured. XIV XIV The pilgrim woman was appeased and, being encouraged to talk, gave a long account of Father Amphilochus, who led so holy a life that his hands smelled of incense, and how on her last visit to Kiev some monks she knew let her have the keys of the catacombs, and how she, taking some dried bread with her, had spent two days in the catacombs with the saints. вҖңIвҖҷd pray awhile to one, ponder awhile, then go on to another. IвҖҷd sleep a bit and then again go and kiss the relics, and there was such peace all around, such blessedness, that one donвҖҷt want to come out, even into the light of heaven again.вҖқ Pierre listened to her attentively and seriously. Prince AndrГ©y went out of the room, and then, leaving вҖңGodвҖҷs folkвҖқ to finish their tea, Princess MГЎrya took Pierre into the drawing room. вҖңYou are very kind,вҖқ she said to him. вҖңOh, I really did not mean to hurt her feelings. I understand them so well and have the greatest respect for them.вҖқ Princess MГЎrya looked at him silently and smiled affectionately. вҖңI have known you a long time, you see, and am as fond of you as of a brother,вҖқ she said. вҖңHow do you find AndrГ©y?вҖқ she added hurriedly, not giving him time to reply to her affectionate words. вҖңI am very anxious about him. His health was better in the winter, but last spring his wound reopened and the doctor said he ought to go away for a cure. And I am also very much afraid for him spiritually. He has not a character like us women who, when we suffer, can weep away our sorrows. He keeps it all within him. Today he is cheerful and in good spirits, but that is the effect of your visitвҒ вҖ”he is not often like that. If you could persuade him to go abroad. He needs activity, and this quiet regular life is very bad for him. Others donвҖҷt notice it, but I see it.вҖқ Toward ten oвҖҷclock the men servants rushed to the front door, hearing the bells of the old princeвҖҷs carriage approaching. Prince AndrГ©y and Pierre also went out into the porch. вҖңWhoвҖҷs that?вҖқ asked the old prince, noticing Pierre as he got out of the carriage. вҖңAh! Very glad! Kiss me,вҖқ he said, having learned who the young stranger was. The old prince was in a good temper and very gracious to Pierre. Before supper, Prince AndrГ©y, coming back to his fatherвҖҷs study, found him disputing hotly with his visitor. Pierre was maintaining that a time would come when there would be no more wars. The old prince disputed it chaffingly, but without getting angry. вҖңDrain the blood from menвҖҷs veins and put in water instead, then there will be no more war! Old womenвҖҷs nonsenseвҒ вҖ”old womenвҖҷs nonsense!вҖқ he repeated, but still he patted Pierre affectionately on the shoulder, and then went up to the table where Prince AndrГ©y, evidently not wishing to join in the conversation, was looking over the papers his father had brought from town. The old prince went up to him and began to talk business. вҖңThe marshal, a Count RostГіv, hasnвҖҷt sent half his contingent. He came to town and wanted to invite me to dinnerвҒ вҖ”I gave him a pretty dinner!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And there, look at this.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, my boy,вҖқ the old prince went on, addressing his son and patting Pierre on the shoulder. вҖңA fine fellowвҒ вҖ”your friendвҒ вҖ”I like him! He stirs me up. Another says clever things and one doesnвҖҷt care to listen, but this one talks rubbish yet stirs an old fellow up. Well, go! Get along! Perhaps IвҖҷll come and sit with you at supper. WeвҖҷll have another dispute. Make friends with my little fool, Princess MГЎrya,вҖқ he shouted after Pierre, through the door. Only now, on his visit to Bald Hills, did Pierre fully realize the strength and charm of his friendship with Prince AndrГ©y. That charm was not expressed so much in his relations with him as with all his family and with the household. With the stern old prince and the gentle, timid Princess MГЎrya, though he had scarcely known them, Pierre at once felt like an old friend. They were all fond of him already. Not only Princess MГЎrya, who had been won by his gentleness with the pilgrims, gave him her most radiant looks, but even the one-year-old вҖңPrince NikolГЎyвҖқ (as his grandfather called him) smiled at Pierre and let himself be taken in his arms, and MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich and Mademoiselle Bourienne looked at him with pleasant smiles when he talked to the old prince. The old prince came in to supper; this was evidently on PierreвҖҷs account. And during the two days of the young manвҖҷs visit he was extremely kind to him and told him to visit them again. When Pierre had gone and the members of the household met together, they began to express their opinions of him as people always do after a new acquaintance has left, but as seldom happens, no one said anything but what was good of him. XV XV When returning from his leave, RostГіv felt, for the first time, how close was the bond that united him to DenГӯsov and the whole regiment. On approaching it, RostГіv felt as he had done when approaching his home in Moscow. When he saw the first hussar with the unbuttoned uniform of his regiment, when he recognized red-haired DemГ©ntyev and saw the picket ropes of the roan horses, when LavrГәshka gleefully shouted to his master, вҖңThe count has come!вҖқ and DenГӯsov, who had been asleep on his bed, ran all disheveled out of the mud hut to embrace him, and the officers collected round to greet the new arrival, RostГіv experienced the same feeling as when his mother, his father, and his sister had embraced him, and tears of joy choked him so that he could not speak. The regiment was also a home, and as unalterably dear and precious as his parentsвҖҷ house. When he had reported himself to the commander of the regiment and had been reassigned to his former squadron, had been on duty and had gone out foraging, when he had again entered into all the little interests of the regiment and felt himself deprived of liberty and bound in one narrow, unchanging frame, he experienced the same sense of peace, of moral support, and the same sense of being at home here in his own place, as he had felt under the parental roof. But here was none of all that turmoil of the world at large, where he did not know his right place and took mistaken decisions; here was no SГіnya with whom he ought, or ought not, to have an explanation; here was no possibility of going there or not going there; here there were not twenty-four hours in the day which could be spent in such a variety of ways; there was not that innumerable crowd of people of whom not one was nearer to him or farther from him than another; there were none of those uncertain and undefined money relations with his father, and nothing to recall that terrible loss to DГіlokhov. Here, in the regiment, all was clear and simple. The whole world was divided into two unequal parts: one, our PГЎvlograd regiment; the other, all the rest. And the rest was no concern of his. In the regiment, everything was definite: who was lieutenant, who captain, who was a good fellow, who a bad one, and most of all, who was a comrade. The canteenkeeper gave one credit, oneвҖҷs pay came every four months, there was nothing to think out or decide, you had only to do nothing that was considered bad in the PГЎvlograd regiment and, when given an order, to do what was clearly, distinctly, and definitely orderedвҒ вҖ”and all would be well. Having once more entered into the definite conditions of this regimental life, RostГіv felt the joy and relief a tired man feels on lying down to rest. Life in the regiment, during this campaign, was all the pleasanter for him, because, after his loss to DГіlokhov (for which, in spite of all his familyвҖҷs efforts to console him, he could not forgive himself), he had made up his mind to atone for his fault by serving, not as he had done before, but really well, and by being a perfectly first-rate comrade and officerвҒ вҖ”in a word, a splendid man altogether, a thing which seemed so difficult out in the world, but so possible in the regiment. After his losses, he had determined to pay back his debt to his parents in five years. He received ten thousand rubles a year, but now resolved to take only two thousand and leave the rest to repay the debt to his parents. Our army, after repeated retreats and advances and battles at PultГәsk and Preussisch-Eylau, was concentrated near Bartenstein. It was awaiting the EmperorвҖҷs arrival and the beginning of a new campaign. The PГЎvlograd regiment, belonging to that part of the army which had served in the 1805 campaign, had been recruiting up to strength in Russia, and arrived too late to take part in the first actions of the campaign. It had been neither at PultГәsk nor at Preussisch-Eylau and, when it joined the army in the field in the second half of the campaign, was attached to PlГЎtovвҖҷs division. PlГЎtovвҖҷs division was acting independently of the main army. Several times parts of the PГЎvlograd regiment had exchanged shots with the enemy, had taken prisoners, and once had even captured Marshal OudinotвҖҷs carriages. In April the PГЎvlograds were stationed immovably for some weeks near a totally ruined and deserted German village. A thaw had set in, it was muddy and cold, the ice on the river broke, and the roads became impassable. For days neither provisions for the men nor fodder for the horses had been issued. As no transports could arrive, the men dispersed about the abandoned and deserted villages, searching for potatoes, but found few even of these. Everything had been eaten up and the inhabitants had all fledвҒ вҖ”if any remained, they were worse than beggars and nothing more could be taken from them; even the soldiers, usually pitiless enough, instead of taking anything from them, often gave them the last of their rations. The PГЎvlograd regiment had had only two men wounded in action, but had lost nearly half its men from hunger and sickness. In the hospitals, death was so certain that soldiers suffering from fever, or the swelling that came from bad food, preferred to remain on duty, and hardly able to drag their legs went to the front rather than to the hospitals. When spring came on, the soldiers found a plant just showing out of the ground that looked like asparagus, which, for some reason, they called вҖңMГЎshkaвҖҷs sweet root.вҖқ It was very bitter, but they wandered about the fields seeking it and dug it out with their sabers and ate it, though they were ordered not to do so, as it was a noxious plant. That spring a new disease broke out among the soldiers, a swelling of the arms, legs, and face, which the doctors attributed to eating this root. But in spite of all this, the soldiers of DenГӯsovвҖҷs squadron fed chiefly on вҖңMГЎshkaвҖҷs sweet root,вҖқ because it was the second week that the last of the biscuits were being doled out at the rate of half a pound a man and the last potatoes received had sprouted and frozen. The horses also had been fed for a fortnight on straw from the thatched roofs and had become terribly thin, though still covered with tufts of felty winter hair. Despite this destitution, the soldiers and officers went on living just as usual. Despite their pale swollen faces and tattered uniforms, the hussars formed line for roll call, kept things in order, groomed their horses, polished their arms, brought in straw from the thatched roofs in place of fodder, and sat down to dine round the cauldrons from which they rose up hungry, joking about their nasty food and their hunger. As usual, in their spare time, they lit bonfires, steamed themselves before them naked; smoked, picked out and baked sprouting rotten potatoes, told and listened to stories of PotГ«mkinвҖҷs and SuvГіrovвҖҷs campaigns, or to legends of AlГ«sha the Sly, or the priestвҖҷs laborer MikГіlka. The officers, as usual, lived in twos and threes in the roofless, half-ruined houses. The seniors tried to collect straw and potatoes and, in general, food for the men. The younger ones occupied themselves as before, some playing cards (there was plenty of money, though there was no food), some with more innocent games, such as quoits and skittles. The general trend of the campaign was rarely spoken of, partly because nothing certain was known about it, partly because there was a vague feeling that in the main it was going badly. RostГіv lived, as before, with DenГӯsov, and since their furlough they had become more friendly than ever. DenГӯsov never spoke of RostГіvвҖҷs family, but by the tender friendship his commander showed him, RostГіv felt that the elder hussarвҖҷs luckless love for NatГЎsha played a part in strengthening their friendship. DenГӯsov evidently tried to expose RostГіv to danger as seldom as possible, and after an action greeted his safe return with evident joy. On one of his foraging expeditions, in a deserted and ruined village to which he had come in search of provisions, RostГіv found a family consisting of an old Pole and his daughter with an infant in arms. They were half clad, hungry, too weak to get away on foot and had no means of obtaining a conveyance. RostГіv brought them to his quarters, placed them in his own lodging, and kept them for some weeks while the old man was recovering. One of his comrades, talking of women, began chaffing RostГіv, saying that he was more wily than any of them and that it would not be a bad thing if he introduced to them the pretty Polish girl he had saved. RostГіv took the joke as an insult, flared up, and said such unpleasant things to the officer that it was all DenГӯsov could do to prevent a duel. When the officer had gone away, DenГӯsov, who did not himself know what RostГіvвҖҷs relations with the Polish girl might be, began to upbraid him for his quickness of temper, and RostГіv replied: вҖңSay what you like.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She is like a sister to me, and I canвҖҷt tell you how it offended meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ well, for that reason.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ DenГӯsov patted him on the shoulder and began rapidly pacing the room without looking at RostГіv, as was his way at moments of deep feeling. вҖңAh, what a mad bweed you WostГіvs are!вҖқ he muttered, and RostГіv noticed tears in his eyes. XVI XVI In April the troops were enlivened by news of the EmperorвҖҷs arrival, but RostГіv had no chance of being present at the review he held at Bartenstein, as the PГЎvlograds were at the outposts far beyond that place. They were bivouacking. DenГӯsov and RostГіv were living in an earth hut, dug out for them by the soldiers and roofed with branches and turf. The hut was made in the following manner, which had then come into vogue. A trench was dug three and a half feet wide, four feet eight inches deep, and eight feet long. At one end of the trench, steps were cut out and these formed the entrance and vestibule. The trench itself was the room, in which the lucky ones, such as the squadron commander, had a board, lying on piles at the end opposite the entrance, to serve as a table. On each side of the trench, the earth was cut out to a breadth of about two and a half feet, and this did duty for bedsteads and couches. The roof was so constructed that one could stand up in the middle of the trench and could even sit up on the beds if one drew close to the table. DenГӯsov, who was living luxuriously because the soldiers of his squadron liked him, had also a board in the roof at the farther end, with a piece of (broken but mended) glass in it for a window. When it was very cold, embers from the soldiersвҖҷ campfire were placed on a bent sheet of iron on the steps in the вҖңreception roomвҖқвҒ вҖ”as DenГӯsov called that part of the hutвҒ вҖ”and it was then so warm that the officers, of whom there were always some with DenГӯsov and RostГіv, sat in their shirt sleeves. In April, RostГіv was on orderly duty. One morning, between seven and eight, returning after a sleepless night, he sent for embers, changed his rain-soaked underclothes, said his prayers, drank tea, got warm, then tidied up the things on the table and in his own corner, and, his face glowing from exposure to the wind and with nothing on but his shirt, lay down on his back, putting his arms under his head. He was pleasantly considering the probability of being promoted in a few days for his last reconnoitering expedition, and was awaiting DenГӯsov, who had gone out somewhere and with whom he wanted a talk. Suddenly he heard DenГӯsov shouting in a vibrating voice behind the hut, evidently much excited. RostГіv moved to the window to see whom he was speaking to, and saw the quartermaster, TopchГ©enko. вҖңI ordered you not to let them eat that MГЎshka woot stuff!вҖқ DenГӯsov was shouting. вҖңAnd I saw with my own eyes how LazarchГәk bwought some fwom the fields.вҖқ вҖңI have given the order again and again, your honor, but they donвҖҷt obey,вҖқ answered the quartermaster. RostГіv lay down again on his bed and thought complacently: вҖңLet him fuss and bustle now, my jobвҖҷs done and IвҖҷm lying downвҒ вҖ”capitally!вҖқ He could hear that LavrГәshkaвҒ вҖ”that sly, bold orderly of DenГӯsovвҖҷsвҒ вҖ”was talking, as well as the quartermaster. LavrГәshka was saying something about loaded wagons, biscuits, and oxen he had seen when he had gone out for provisions. Then DenГӯsovвҖҷs voice was heard shouting farther and farther away. вҖңSaddle! Second platoon!вҖқ вҖңWhere are they off to now?вҖқ thought RostГіv. Five minutes later, DenГӯsov came into the hut, climbed with muddy boots on the bed, lit his pipe, furiously scattered his things about, took his leaded whip, buckled on his saber, and went out again. In answer to RostГіvвҖҷs inquiry where he was going, he answered vaguely and crossly that he had some business. вҖңLet God and our gweat monarch judge me afterwards!вҖқ said DenГӯsov going out, and RostГіv heard the hoofs of several horses splashing through the mud. He did not even trouble to find out where DenГӯsov had gone. Having got warm in his corner, he fell asleep and did not leave the hut till toward evening. DenГӯsov had not yet returned. The weather had cleared up, and near the next hut two officers and a cadet were playing svГЎyka, laughing as they threw their missiles which buried themselves in the soft mud. RostГіv joined them. In the middle of the game, the officers saw some wagons approaching with fifteen hussars on their skinny horses behind them. The wagons escorted by the hussars drew up to the picket ropes and a crowd of hussars surrounded them. вҖңThere now, DenГӯsov has been worrying,вҖқ said RostГіv, вҖңand here are the provisions.вҖқ вҖңSo they are!вҖқ said the officers. вҖңWonвҖҷt the soldiers be glad!вҖқ A little behind the hussars came DenГӯsov, accompanied by two infantry officers with whom he was talking. RostГіv went to meet them. вҖңI warn you, Captain,вҖқ one of the officers, a short thin man, evidently very angry, was saying. вҖңHavenвҖҷt I told you I wonвҖҷt give them up?вҖқ replied DenГӯsov. вҖңYou will answer for it, Captain. It is mutinyвҒ вҖ”seizing the transport of oneвҖҷs own army. Our men have had nothing to eat for two days.вҖқ вҖңAnd mine have had nothing for two weeks,вҖқ said DenГӯsov. вҖңIt is robbery! YouвҖҷll answer for it, sir!вҖқ said the infantry officer, raising his voice. вҖңNow, what are you pestewing me for?вҖқ cried DenГӯsov, suddenly losing his temper. вҖңI shall answer for it and not you, and youвҖҷd better not buzz about here till you get hurt. Be off! Go!вҖқ he shouted at the officers. вҖңVery well, then!вҖқ shouted the little officer, undaunted and not riding away. вҖңIf you are determined to rob, IвҖҷllвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңGo to the devil! quick maвҖҷch, while youвҖҷre safe and sound!вҖқ and DenГӯsov turned his horse on the officer. вҖңVery well, very well!вҖқ muttered the officer, threateningly, and turning his horse he trotted away, jolting in his saddle. вҖңA dog astwide a fence! A weal dog astwide a fence!вҖқ shouted DenГӯsov after him (the most insulting expression a cavalryman can address to a mounted infantryman) and riding up to RostГіv, he burst out laughing. вҖңIвҖҷve taken twansports from the infantwy by force!вҖқ he said. вҖңAfter all, canвҖҷt let our men starve.вҖқ The wagons that had reached the hussars had been consigned to an infantry regiment, but learning from LavrГәshka that the transport was unescorted, DenГӯsov with his hussars had seized it by force. The soldiers had biscuits dealt out to them freely, and they even shared them with the other squadrons. The next day the regimental commander sent for DenГӯsov, and holding his fingers spread out before his eyes said: вҖңThis is how I look at this affair: I know nothing about it and wonвҖҷt begin proceedings, but I advise you to ride over to the staff and settle the business there in the commissariat department and if possible sign a receipt for such and such stores received. If not, as the demand was booked against an infantry regiment, there will be a row and the affair may end badly.вҖқ From the regimental commanderвҖҷs, DenГӯsov rode straight to the staff with a sincere desire to act on this advice. In the evening he came back to his dugout in a state such as RostГіv had never yet seen him in. DenГӯsov could not speak and gasped for breath. When RostГіv asked what was the matter, he only uttered some incoherent oaths and threats in a hoarse, feeble voice. Alarmed at DenГӯsovвҖҷs condition, RostГіv suggested that he should undress, drink some water, and send for the doctor. вҖңTwy me for wobbewyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ oh! Some more waterвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Let them twy me, but IвҖҷll always thwash scoundwelsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and IвҖҷll tell the EmpewoвҖҷвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ IceвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he muttered. The regimental doctor, when he came, said it was absolutely necessary to bleed DenГӯsov. A deep saucer of black blood was taken from his hairy arm and only then was he able to relate what had happened to him. вҖңI get there,вҖқ began DenГӯsov. вҖңвҖҠвҖҳNow then, whereвҖҷs your chiefвҖҷs quarters?вҖҷ They were pointed out. вҖҳPlease to wait.вҖҷ вҖҳIвҖҷve widden twenty miles and have duties to attend to and no time to wait. Announce me.вҖҷ Vewy well, so out comes their head chiefвҒ вҖ”also took it into his head to lecture me: вҖҳItвҖҷs wobbewy!вҖҷвҒ вҖ”вҖҳWobbewy,вҖҷ I say, вҖҳis not done by man who seizes pwovisions to feed his soldiers, but by him who takes them to fill his own pockets!вҖҷ вҖҳWill you please be silent?вҖҷ вҖҳVewy good!вҖҷ Then he says: вҖҳGo and give a weceipt to the commissioner, but your affair will be passed on to headquarters.вҖҷ I go to the commissioner. I enter, and at the tableвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ who do you think? No, but wait a bit!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Who is it thatвҖҷs starving us?вҖқ shouted DenГӯsov, hitting the table with the fist of his newly bled arm so violently that the table nearly broke down and the tumblers on it jumped about. вҖңTelyГЎnin! вҖҳWhat? So itвҖҷs you whoвҖҷs starving us to death! Is it? Take this and this!вҖҷ and I hit him so pat, stwaight on his snoutвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖҳAh, what aвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ what aвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ!вҖҷ and I staвҖҷted fwashing himвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, IвҖҷve had a bit of fun I can tell you!вҖқ cried DenГӯsov, gleeful and yet angry, his white teeth showing under his black mustache. вҖңIвҖҷd have killed him if they hadnвҖҷt taken him away!вҖқ вҖңBut what are you shouting for? Calm yourself,вҖқ said RostГіv. вҖңYouвҖҷve set your arm bleeding afresh. Wait, we must tie it up again.вҖқ DenГӯsov was bandaged up again and put to bed. Next day he woke calm and cheerful. But at noon the adjutant of the regiment came into RostГіvвҖҷs and DenГӯsovвҖҷs dugout with a grave and serious face and regretfully showed them a paper addressed to Major DenГӯsov from the regimental commander in which inquiries were made about yesterdayвҖҷs occurrence. The adjutant told them that the affair was likely to take a very bad turn: that a court-martial had been appointed, and that in view of the severity with which marauding and insubordination were now regarded, degradation to the ranks would be the best that could be hoped for. The case, as represented by the offended parties, was that, after seizing the transports, Major DenГӯsov, being drunk, went to the chief quartermaster and without any provocation called him a thief, threatened to strike him, and on being led out had rushed into the office and given two officials a thrashing, and dislocated the arm of one of them. In answer to RostГіvвҖҷs renewed questions, DenГӯsov said, laughing, that he thought he remembered that some other fellow had got mixed up in it, but that it was all nonsense and rubbish, and he did not in the least fear any kind of trial, and that if those scoundrels dared attack him he would give them an answer that they would not easily forget. DenГӯsov spoke contemptuously of the whole matter, but RostГіv knew him too well not to detect that (while hiding it from others) at heart he feared a court-martial and was worried over the affair, which was evidently taking a bad turn. Every day, letters of inquiry and notices from the court arrived, and on the first of May, DenГӯsov was ordered to hand the squadron over to the next in seniority and appear before the staff of his division to explain his violence at the commissariat office. On the previous day PlГЎtov reconnoitered with two Cossack regiments and two squadrons of hussars. DenГӯsov, as was his wont, rode out in front of the outposts, parading his courage. A bullet fired by a French sharpshooter hit him in the fleshy part of his leg. Perhaps at another time DenГӯsov would not have left the regiment for so slight a wound, but now he took advantage of it to excuse himself from appearing at the staff and went into hospital. XVII XVII In June the battle of Friedland was fought, in which the PГЎvlograds did not take part, and after that an armistice was proclaimed. RostГіv, who felt his friendвҖҷs absence very much, having no news of him since he left and feeling very anxious about his wound and the progress of his affairs, took advantage of the armistice to get leave to visit DenГӯsov in hospital. The hospital was in a small Prussian town that had been twice devastated by Russian and French troops. Because it was summer, when it is so beautiful out in the fields, the little town presented a particularly dismal appearance with its broken roofs and fences, its foul streets, tattered inhabitants, and the sick and drunken soldiers wandering about. The hospital was in a brick building with some of the window frames and panes broken and a courtyard surrounded by the remains of a wooden fence that had been pulled to pieces. Several bandaged soldiers, with pale swollen faces, were sitting or walking about in the sunshine in the yard. Directly RostГіv entered the door he was enveloped by a smell of putrefaction and hospital air. On the stairs he met a Russian army doctor smoking a cigar. The doctor was followed by a Russian assistant. вҖңI canвҖҷt tear myself to pieces,вҖқ the doctor was saying. вҖңCome to MakГЎr AlexГ©evich in the evening. I shall be there.вҖқ The assistant asked some further questions. вҖңOh, do the best you can! IsnвҖҷt it all the same?вҖқ The doctor noticed RostГіv coming upstairs. вҖңWhat do you want, sir?вҖқ said the doctor. вҖңWhat do you want? The bullets having spared you, do you want to try typhus? This is a pesthouse, sir.вҖқ вҖңHow so?вҖқ asked RostГіv. вҖңTyphus, sir. ItвҖҷs death to go in. Only we two, MakГ©ev and IвҖқ (he pointed to the assistant), вҖңkeep on here. Some five of us doctors have died in this place.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ When a new one comes he is done for in a week,вҖқ said the doctor with evident satisfaction. вҖңPrussian doctors have been invited here, but our allies donвҖҷt like it at all.вҖқ RostГіv explained that he wanted to see Major DenГӯsov of the hussars, who was wounded. вҖңI donвҖҷt know. I canвҖҷt tell you, sir. Only think! I am alone in charge of three hospitals with more than four hundred patients! ItвҖҷs well that the charitable Prussian ladies send us two pounds of coffee and some lint each month or we should be lost!вҖқ he laughed. вҖңFour hundred, sir, and theyвҖҷre always sending me fresh ones. There are four hundred? Eh?вҖқ he asked, turning to the assistant. The assistant looked fagged out. He was evidently vexed and impatient for the talkative doctor to go. вҖңMajor DenГӯsov,вҖқ RostГіv said again. вҖңHe was wounded at Molliten.вҖқ вҖңDead, I fancy. Eh, MakГ©ev?вҖқ queried the doctor, in a tone of indifference. The assistant, however, did not confirm the doctorвҖҷs words. вҖңIs he tall and with reddish hair?вҖқ asked the doctor. RostГіv described DenГӯsovвҖҷs appearance. вҖңThere was one like that,вҖқ said the doctor, as if pleased. вҖңThat one is dead, I fancy. However, IвҖҷll look up our list. We had a list. Have you got it, MakГ©ev?вҖқ вҖңMakГЎr AlexГ©evich has the list,вҖқ answered the assistant. вҖңBut if youвҖҷll step into the officersвҖҷ wards youвҖҷll see for yourself,вҖқ he added, turning to RostГіv. вҖңAh, youвҖҷd better not go, sir,вҖқ said the doctor, вҖңor you may have to stay here yourself.вҖқ But RostГіv bowed himself away from the doctor and asked the assistant to show him the way. вҖңOnly donвҖҷt blame me!вҖқ the doctor shouted up after him. RostГіv and the assistant went into the dark corridor. The smell was so strong there that RostГіv held his nose and had to pause and collect his strength before he could go on. A door opened to the right, and an emaciated sallow man on crutches, barefoot and in underclothing, limped out and, leaning against the doorpost, looked with glittering envious eyes at those who were passing. Glancing in at the door, RostГіv saw that the sick and wounded were lying on the floor on straw and overcoats. вҖңMay I go in and look?вҖқ вҖңWhat is there to see?вҖқ said the assistant. But, just because the assistant evidently did not want him to go in, RostГіv entered the soldiersвҖҷ ward. The foul air, to which he had already begun to get used in the corridor, was still stronger here. It was a little different, more pungent, and one felt that this was where it originated. In the long room, brightly lit up by the sun through the large windows, the sick and wounded lay in two rows with their heads to the walls, and leaving a passage in the middle. Most of them were unconscious and paid no attention to the newcomers. Those who were conscious raised themselves or lifted their thin yellow faces, and all looked intently at RostГіv with the same expression of hope, of relief, reproach, and envy of anotherвҖҷs health. RostГіv went to the middle of the room and looking through the open doors into the two adjoining rooms saw the same thing there. He stood still, looking silently around. He had not at all expected such a sight. Just before him, almost across the middle of the passage on the bare floor, lay a sick man, probably a Cossack to judge by the cut of his hair. The man lay on his back, his huge arms and legs outstretched. His face was purple, his eyes were rolled back so that only the whites were seen, and on his bare legs and arms which were still red, the veins stood out like cords. He was knocking the back of his head against the floor, hoarsely uttering some word which he kept repeating. RostГіv listened and made out the word. It was вҖңdrink, drink, a drink!вҖқ RostГіv glanced round, looking for someone who would put this man back in his place and bring him water. вҖңWho looks after the sick here?вҖқ he asked the assistant. Just then a commissariat soldier, a hospital orderly, came in from the next room, marching stiffly, and drew up in front of RostГіv. вҖңGood day, your honor!вҖқ he shouted, rolling his eyes at RostГіv and evidently mistaking him for one of the hospital authorities. вҖңGet him to his place and give him some water,вҖқ said RostГіv, pointing to the Cossack. вҖңYes, your honor,вҖқ the soldier replied complacently, and rolling his eyes more than ever he drew himself up still straighter, but did not move. вҖңNo, itвҖҷs impossible to do anything here,вҖқ thought RostГіv, lowering his eyes, and he was going out, but became aware of an intense look fixed on him on his right, and he turned. Close to the corner, on an overcoat, sat an old, unshaven, gray-bearded soldier as thin as a skeleton, with a stern sallow face and eyes intently fixed on RostГіv. The manвҖҷs neighbor on one side whispered something to him, pointing at RostГіv, who noticed that the old man wanted to speak to him. He drew nearer and saw that the old man had only one leg bent under him, the other had been amputated above the knee. His neighbor on the other side, who lay motionless some distance from him with his head thrown back, was a young soldier with a snub nose. His pale waxen face was still freckled and his eyes were rolled back. RostГіv looked at the young soldier and a cold chill ran down his back. вҖңWhy, this one seemsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he began, turning to the assistant. вҖңAnd how weвҖҷve been begging, your honor,вҖқ said the old soldier, his jaw quivering. вҖңHeвҖҷs been dead since morning. After all weвҖҷre men, not dogs.вҖқ вҖңIвҖҷll send someone at once. He shall be taken awayвҒ вҖ”taken away at once,вҖқ said the assistant hurriedly. вҖңLet us go, your honor.вҖқ вҖңYes, yes, let us go,вҖқ said RostГіv hastily, and lowering his eyes and shrinking, he tried to pass unnoticed between the rows of reproachful envious eyes that were fixed upon him, and went out of the room. XVIII XVIII Going along the corridor, the assistant led RostГіv to the officersвҖҷ wards, consisting of three rooms, the doors of which stood open. There were beds in these rooms and the sick and wounded officers were lying or sitting on them. Some were walking about the rooms in hospital dressing gowns. The first person RostГіv met in the officersвҖҷ ward was a thin little man with one arm, who was walking about the first room in a nightcap and hospital dressing gown, with a pipe between his teeth. RostГіv looked at him, trying to remember where he had seen him before. вҖңSee where weвҖҷve met again!вҖқ said the little man. вҖңTГәshin, TГәshin, donвҖҷt you remember, who gave you a lift at SchГ¶n Grabern? And IвҖҷve had a bit cut off, you seeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he went on with a smile, pointing to the empty sleeve of his dressing gown. вҖңLooking for VasГӯli DmГӯtrich DenГӯsov? My neighbor,вҖқ he added, when he heard who RostГіv wanted. вҖңHere, here,вҖқ and TГәshin led him into the next room, from whence came sounds of several laughing voices. вҖңHow can they laugh, or even live at all here?вҖқ thought RostГіv, still aware of that smell of decomposing flesh that had been so strong in the soldiersвҖҷ ward, and still seeming to see fixed on him those envious looks which had followed him out from both sides, and the face of that young soldier with eyes rolled back. DenГӯsov lay asleep on his bed with his head under the blanket, though it was nearly noon. вҖңAh, WostГіv? How are you, how are you?вҖқ he called out, still in the same voice as in the regiment, but RostГіv noticed sadly that under this habitual ease and animation some new, sinister, hidden feeling showed itself in the expression of DenГӯsovвҖҷs face and the intonations of his voice. His wound, though a slight one, had not yet healed even now, six weeks after he had been hit. His face had the same swollen pallor as the faces of the other hospital patients, but it was not this that struck RostГіv. What struck him was that DenГӯsov did not seem glad to see him, and smiled at him unnaturally. He did not ask about the regiment, nor about the general state of affairs, and when RostГіv spoke of these matters did not listen. RostГіv even noticed that DenГӯsov did not like to be reminded of the regiment, or in general of that other free life which was going on outside the hospital. He seemed to try to forget that old life and was only interested in the affair with the commissariat officers. On RostГіvвҖҷs inquiry as to how the matter stood, he at once produced from under his pillow a paper he had received from the commission and the rough draft of his answer to it. He became animated when he began reading his paper and specially drew RostГіvвҖҷs attention to the stinging rejoinders he made to his enemies. His hospital companions, who had gathered round RostГіvвҒ вҖ”a fresh arrival from the world outsideвҒ вҖ”gradually began to disperse as soon as DenГӯsov began reading his answer. RostГіv noticed by their faces that all those gentlemen had already heard that story more than once and were tired of it. Only the man who had the next bed, a stout Uhlan, continued to sit on his bed, gloomily frowning and smoking a pipe, and little one-armed TГәshin still listened, shaking his head disapprovingly. In the middle of the reading, the Uhlan interrupted DenГӯsov. вҖңBut what I say is,вҖқ he said, turning to RostГіv, вҖңit would be best simply to petition the Emperor for pardon. They say great rewards will now be distributed, and surely a pardon would be granted.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңMe petition the EmpewoвҖҷ!вҖқ exclaimed DenГӯsov, in a voice to which he tried hard to give the old energy and fire, but which sounded like an expression of irritable impotence. вҖңWhat for? If I were a wobber I would ask mercy, but IвҖҷm being court-martialed for bwinging wobbers to book. Let them twy me, IвҖҷm not afwaid of anyone. IвҖҷve served the Tsar and my countwy honowably and have not stolen! And am I to be degwaded?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Listen, IвҖҷm wвҖҷiting to them stwaight. This is what I say: вҖҳIf I had wobbed the TweasuwyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҷвҖҠвҖқ вҖңItвҖҷs certainly well written,вҖқ said TГәshin, вҖңbut thatвҖҷs not the point, VasГӯli DmГӯtrich,вҖқ and he also turned to RostГіv. вҖңOne has to submit, and VasГӯli DmГӯtrich doesnвҖҷt want to. You know the auditor told you it was a bad business.вҖқ вҖңWell, let it be bad,вҖқ said DenГӯsov. вҖңThe auditor wrote out a petition for you,вҖқ continued TГәshin, вҖңand you ought to sign it and ask this gentleman to take it. No doubt heвҖқ (indicating RostГіv) вҖңhas connections on the staff. You wonвҖҷt find a better opportunity.вҖқ вҖңHavenвҖҷt I said IвҖҷm not going to gwovel?вҖқ DenГӯsov interrupted him, went on reading his paper. RostГіv had not the courage to persuade DenГӯsov, though he instinctively felt that the way advised by TГәshin and the other officers was the safest, and though he would have been glad to be of service to DenГӯsov. He knew his stubborn will and straightforward hasty temper. When the reading of DenГӯsovвҖҷs virulent reply, which took more than an hour, was over, RostГіv said nothing, and he spent the rest of the day in a most dejected state of mind amid DenГӯsovвҖҷs hospital comrades, who had gathered round him, telling them what he knew and listening to their stories. DenГӯsov was moodily silent all the evening. Late in the evening, when RostГіv was about to leave, he asked DenГӯsov whether he had no commission for him. вҖңYes, wait a bit,вҖқ said DenГӯsov, glancing round at the officers, and taking his papers from under his pillow he went to the window, where he had an inkpot, and sat down to write. вҖңIt seems itвҖҷs no use knocking oneвҖҷs head against a wall!вҖқ he said, coming from the window and giving RostГіv a large envelope. In it was the petition to the Emperor drawn up by the auditor, in which DenГӯsov, without alluding to the offenses of the commissariat officials, simply asked for pardon. вҖңHand it in. It seemsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He did not finish, but gave a painfully unnatural smile. XIX XIX Having returned to the regiment and told the commander the state of DenГӯsovвҖҷs affairs, RostГіv rode to Tilsit with the letter to the Emperor. On the thirteenth of June the French and Russian Emperors arrived in Tilsit. BorГӯs DrubetskГіy had asked the important personage on whom he was in attendance, to include him in the suite appointed for the stay at Tilsit. вҖңI should like to see the great man,вҖқ he said, alluding to Napoleon, whom hitherto he, like everyone else, had always called Buonaparte. вҖңYou are speaking of Buonaparte?вҖқ asked the general, smiling. BorГӯs looked at his general inquiringly and immediately saw that he was being tested. вҖңI am speaking, Prince, of the Emperor Napoleon,вҖқ he replied. The general patted him on the shoulder, with a smile. вҖңYou will go far,вҖқ he said, and took him to Tilsit with him. BorГӯs was among the few present at the Niemen on the day the two Emperors met. He saw the raft, decorated with monograms, saw Napoleon pass before the French Guards on the farther bank of the river, saw the pensive face of the Emperor Alexander as he sat in silence in a tavern on the bank of the Niemen awaiting NapoleonвҖҷs arrival, saw both Emperors get into boats, and saw how NapoleonвҒ вҖ”reaching the raft firstвҒ вҖ”stepped quickly forward to meet Alexander and held out his hand to him, and how they both retired into the pavilion. Since he had begun to move in the highest circles BorГӯs had made it his habit to watch attentively all that went on around him and to note it down. At the time of the meeting at Tilsit he asked the names of those who had come with Napoleon and about the uniforms they wore, and listened attentively to words spoken by important personages. At the moment the Emperors went into the pavilion he looked at his watch, and did not forget to look at it again when Alexander came out. The interview had lasted an hour and fifty-three minutes. He noted this down that same evening, among other facts he felt to be of historic importance. As the EmperorвҖҷs suite was a very small one, it was a matter of great importance, for a man who valued his success in the service, to be at Tilsit on the occasion of this interview between the two Emperors, and having succeeded in this, BorГӯs felt that henceforth his position was fully assured. He had not only become known, but people had grown accustomed to him and accepted him. Twice he had executed commissions to the Emperor himself, so that the latter knew his face, and all those at court, far from cold-shouldering him as at first when they considered him a newcomer, would now have been surprised had he been absent. BorГӯs lodged with another adjutant, the Polish Count ZhilГӯnski. ZhilГӯnski, a Pole brought up in Paris, was rich, and passionately fond of the French, and almost every day of the stay at Tilsit, French officers of the Guard and from French headquarters were dining and lunching with him and BorГӯs. On the evening of the twenty-fourth of June, Count ZhilГӯnski arranged a supper for his French friends. The guest of honor was an aide-de-camp of NapoleonвҖҷs, there were also several French officers of the Guard, and a page of NapoleonвҖҷs, a young lad of an old aristocratic French family. That same day, RostГіv, profiting by the darkness to avoid being recognized in civilian dress, came to Tilsit and went to the lodging occupied by BorГӯs and ZhilГӯnski. RostГіv, in common with the whole army from which he came, was far from having experienced the change of feeling toward Napoleon and the FrenchвҒ вҖ”who from being foes had suddenly become friendsвҒ вҖ”that had taken place at headquarters and in BorГӯs. In the army, Bonaparte and the French were still regarded with mingled feelings of anger, contempt, and fear. Only recently, talking with one of PlГЎtovвҖҷs Cossack officers, RostГіv had argued that if Napoleon were taken prisoner he would be treated not as a sovereign, but as a criminal. Quite lately, happening to meet a wounded French colonel on the road, RostГіv had maintained with heat that peace was impossible between a legitimate sovereign and the criminal Bonaparte. RostГіv was therefore unpleasantly struck by the presence of French officers in BorГӯsвҖҷ lodging, dressed in uniforms he had been accustomed to see from quite a different point of view from the outposts of the flank. As soon as he noticed a French officer, who thrust his head out of the door, that warlike feeling of hostility which he always experienced at the sight of the enemy suddenly seized him. He stopped at the threshold and asked in Russian whether DrubetskГіy lived there. BorГӯs, hearing a strange voice in the anteroom, came out to meet him. An expression of annoyance showed itself for a moment on his face on first recognizing RostГіv. вҖңAh, itвҖҷs you? Very glad, very glad to see you,вҖқ he said, however, coming toward him with a smile. But RostГіv had noticed his first impulse. вҖңIвҖҷve come at a bad time I think. I should not have come, but I have business,вҖқ he said coldly. вҖңNo, I only wonder how you managed to get away from your regiment. Dans un moment je suis Г  vous,вҖқ58 he said, answering someone who called him. вҖңI see IвҖҷm intruding,вҖқ RostГіv repeated. The look of annoyance had already disappeared from BorГӯsвҖҷ face: having evidently reflected and decided how to act, he very quietly took both RostГіvвҖҷs hands and led him into the next room. His eyes, looking serenely and steadily at RostГіv, seemed to be veiled by something, as if screened by blue spectacles of conventionality. So it seemed to RostГіv. вҖңOh, come now! As if you could come at a wrong time!вҖқ said BorГӯs, and he led him into the room where the supper table was laid and introduced him to his guests, explaining that he was not a civilian, but an hussar officer, and an old friend of his. вҖңCount ZhilГӯnskiвҒ вҖ”le Comte N. N.вҒ вҖ”le Capitaine S. S.,вҖқ said he, naming his guests. RostГіv looked frowningly at the Frenchmen, bowed reluctantly, and remained silent. ZhilГӯnski evidently did not receive this new Russian person very willingly into his circle and did not speak to RostГіv. BorГӯs did not appear to notice the constraint the newcomer produced and, with the same pleasant composure and the same veiled look in his eyes with which he had met RostГіv, tried to enliven the conversation. One of the Frenchmen, with the politeness characteristic of his countrymen, addressed the obstinately taciturn RostГіv, saying that the latter had probably come to Tilsit to see the Emperor. вҖңNo, I came on business,вҖқ replied RostГіv, briefly. RostГіv had been out of humor from the moment he noticed the look of dissatisfaction on BorГӯsвҖҷ face, and as always happens to those in a bad humor, it seemed to him that everyone regarded him with aversion and that he was in everybodyвҖҷs way. He really was in their way, for he alone took no part in the conversation which again became general. The looks the visitors cast on him seemed to say: вҖңAnd what is he sitting here for?вҖқ He rose and went up to BorГӯs. вҖңAnyhow, IвҖҷm in your way,вҖқ he said in a low tone. вҖңCome and talk over my business and IвҖҷll go away.вҖқ вҖңOh, no, not at all,вҖқ said BorГӯs. вҖңBut if you are tired, come and lie down in my room and have a rest.вҖқ вҖңYes, reallyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ They went into the little room where BorГӯs slept. RostГіv, without sitting down, began at once, irritably (as if BorГӯs were to blame in some way) telling him about DenГӯsovвҖҷs affair, asking him whether, through his general, he could and would intercede with the Emperor on DenГӯsovвҖҷs behalf and get DenГӯsovвҖҷs petition handed in. When he and BorГӯs were alone, RostГіv felt for the first time that he could not look BorГӯs in the face without a sense of awkwardness. BorГӯs, with one leg crossed over the other and stroking his left hand with the slender fingers of his right, listened to RostГіv as a general listens to the report of a subordinate, now looking aside and now gazing straight into RostГіvвҖҷs eyes with the same veiled look. Each time this happened RostГіv felt uncomfortable and cast down his eyes. вҖңI have heard of such cases and know that His Majesty is very severe in such affairs. I think it would be best not to bring it before the Emperor, but to apply to the commander of the corps.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But in general, I thinkвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңSo you donвҖҷt want to do anything? Well then, say so!вҖқ RostГіv almost shouted, not looking BorГӯs in the face. BorГӯs smiled. вҖңOn the contrary, I will do what I can. Only I thoughtвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ At that moment ZhilГӯnskiвҖҷs voice was heard calling BorГӯs. вҖңWell then, go, go, goвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said RostГіv, and refusing supper and remaining alone in the little room, he walked up and down for a long time, hearing the lighthearted French conversation from the next room. II II вҖңI have the pleasure of addressing Count BezГәkhov, if I am not mistaken,вҖқ said the stranger in a deliberate and loud voice. Pierre looked silently and inquiringly at him over his spectacles. вҖңI have heard of you, my dear sir,вҖқ continued the stranger, вҖңand of your misfortune.вҖқ He seemed to emphasize the last word, as if to sayвҒ вҖ”вҖңYes, misfortune! Call it what you please, I know that what happened to you in Moscow was a misfortune.вҖқвҒ вҖ”вҖңI regret it very much, my dear sir.вҖқ Pierre flushed and, hurriedly putting his legs down from the bed, bent forward toward the old man with a forced and timid smile. вҖңI have not referred to this out of curiosity, my dear sir, but for greater reasons.вҖқ He paused, his gaze still on Pierre, and moved aside on the sofa by way of inviting the other to take a seat beside him. Pierre felt reluctant to enter into conversation with this old man, but, submitting to him involuntarily, came up and sat down beside him. вҖңYou are unhappy, my dear sir,вҖқ the stranger continued. вҖңYou are young and I am old. I should like to help you as far as lies in my power.вҖқ вҖңOh, yes!вҖқ said Pierre, with a forced smile. вҖңI am very grateful to you. Where are you traveling from?вҖқ The strangerвҖҷs face was not genial, it was even cold and severe, but in spite of this, both the face and words of his new acquaintance were irresistibly attractive to Pierre. вҖңBut if for any reason you donвҖҷt feel inclined to talk to me,вҖқ said the old man, вҖңsay so, my dear sir.вҖқ And he suddenly smiled, in an unexpected and tenderly paternal way. вҖңOh no, not at all! On the contrary, I am very glad to make your acquaintance,вҖқ said Pierre. And again, glancing at the strangerвҖҷs hands, he looked more closely at the ring, with its skullвҒ вҖ”a Masonic sign. вҖңAllow me to ask,вҖқ he said, вҖңare you a Mason?вҖқ вҖңYes, I belong to the Brotherhood of the Freemasons,вҖқ said the stranger, looking deeper and deeper into PierreвҖҷs eyes. вҖңAnd in their name and my own I hold out a brotherly hand to you.вҖқ вҖңI am afraid,вҖқ said Pierre, smiling, and wavering between the confidence the personality of the Freemason inspired in him and his own habit of ridiculing the Masonic beliefsвҒ вҖ”вҖңI am afraid I am very far from understandingвҒ вҖ”how am I to put it?вҒ вҖ”I am afraid my way of looking at the world is so opposed to yours that we shall not understand one another.вҖқ вҖңI know your outlook,вҖқ said the Mason, вҖңand the view of life you mention, and which you think is the result of your own mental efforts, is the one held by the majority of people, and is the invariable fruit of pride, indolence, and ignorance. Forgive me, my dear sir, but if I had not known it I should not have addressed you. Your view of life is a regrettable delusion.вҖқ вҖңJust as I may suppose you to be deluded,вҖқ said Pierre, with a faint smile. вҖңI should never dare to say that I know the truth,вҖқ said the Mason, whose words struck Pierre more and more by their precision and firmness. вҖңNo one can attain to truth by himself. Only by laying stone on stone with the cooperation of all, by the millions of generations from our forefather Adam to our own times, is that temple reared which is to be a worthy dwelling place of the Great God,вҖқ he added, and closed his eyes. вҖңI ought to tell you that I do not believeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ do not believe in God,вҖқ said Pierre, regretfully and with an effort, feeling it essential to speak the whole truth. The Mason looked intently at Pierre and smiled as a rich man with millions in hand might smile at a poor fellow who told him that he, poor man, had not the five rubles that would make him happy. вҖңYes, you do not know Him, my dear sir,вҖқ said the Mason. вҖңYou cannot know Him. You do not know Him and that is why you are unhappy.вҖқ вҖңYes, yes, I am unhappy,вҖқ assented Pierre. вҖңBut what am I to do?вҖқ вҖңYou know Him not, my dear sir, and so you are very unhappy. You do not know Him, but He is here, He is in me, He is in my words, He is in thee, and even in those blasphemous words thou hast just uttered!вҖқ pronounced the Mason in a stern and tremulous voice. He paused and sighed, evidently trying to calm himself. вҖңIf He were not,вҖқ he said quietly, вҖңyou and I would not be speaking of Him, my dear sir. Of what, of whom, are we speaking? Whom hast thou denied?вҖқ he suddenly asked with exulting austerity and authority in his voice. вҖңWho invented Him, if He did not exist? Whence came thy conception of the existence of such an incomprehensible Being? didst thou, and why did the whole world, conceive the idea of the existence of such an incomprehensible Being, a Being all-powerful, eternal, and infinite in all His attributes?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He stopped and remained silent for a long time. Pierre could not and did not wish to break this silence. вҖңHe exists, but to understand Him is hard,вҖқ the Mason began again, looking not at Pierre but straight before him, and turning the leaves of his book with his old hands which from excitement he could not keep still. вҖңIf it were a man whose existence thou didst doubt I could bring him to thee, could take him by the hand and show him to thee. But how can I, an insignificant mortal, show His omnipotence, His infinity, and all His mercy to one who is blind, or who shuts his eyes that he may not see or understand Him and may not see or understand his own vileness and sinfulness?вҖқ He paused again. вҖңWho art thou? Thou dreamest that thou art wise because thou couldst utter those blasphemous words,вҖқ he went on, with a somber and scornful smile. вҖңAnd thou art more foolish and unreasonable than a little child, who, playing with the parts of a skillfully made watch, dares to say that, as he does not understand its use, he does not believe in the master who made it. To know Him is hard.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ For ages, from our forefather Adam to our own day, we labor to attain that knowledge and are still infinitely far from our aim; but in our lack of understanding we see only our weakness and His greatness.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre listened with swelling heart, gazing into the MasonвҖҷs face with shining eyes, not interrupting or questioning him, but believing with his whole soul what the stranger said. Whether he accepted the wise reasoning contained in the MasonвҖҷs words, or believed as a child believes, in the speakerвҖҷs tone of conviction and earnestness, or the tremor of the speakerвҖҷs voiceвҒ вҖ”which sometimes almost brokeвҒ вҖ”or those brilliant aged eyes grown old in this conviction, or the calm firmness and certainty of his vocation, which radiated from his whole being (and which struck Pierre especially by contrast with his own dejection and hopelessness)вҒ вҖ”at any rate, Pierre longed with his whole soul to believe and he did believe, and felt a joyful sense of comfort, regeneration, and return to life. вҖңHe is not to be apprehended by reason, but by life,вҖқ said the Mason. вҖңI do not understand,вҖқ said Pierre, feeling with dismay doubts reawakening. He was afraid of any want of clearness, any weakness, in the MasonвҖҷs arguments; he dreaded not to be able to believe in him. вҖңI donвҖҷt understand,вҖқ he said, вҖңhow it is that the mind of man cannot attain the knowledge of which you speak.вҖқ The Mason smiled with his gentle fatherly smile. вҖңThe highest wisdom and truth are like the purest liquid we may wish to imbibe,вҖқ he said. вҖңCan I receive that pure liquid into an impure vessel and judge of its purity? Only by the inner purification of myself can I retain in some degree of purity the liquid I receive.вҖқ вҖңYes, yes, that is so,вҖқ said Pierre joyfully. вҖңThe highest wisdom is not founded on reason alone, not on those worldly sciences of physics, history, chemistry, and the like, into which intellectual knowledge is divided. The highest wisdom is one. The highest wisdom has but one scienceвҒ вҖ”the science of the wholeвҒ вҖ”the science explaining the whole creation and manвҖҷs place in it. To receive that science it is necessary to purify and renew oneвҖҷs inner self, and so before one can know, it is necessary to believe and to perfect oneвҖҷs self. And to attain this end, we have the light called conscience that God has implanted in our souls.вҖқ вҖңYes, yes,вҖқ assented Pierre. вҖңLook then at thy inner self with the eyes of the spirit, and ask thyself whether thou art content with thyself. What hast thou attained relying on reason only? What art thou? You are young, you are rich, you are clever, you are well educated. And what have you done with all these good gifts? Are you content with yourself and with your life?вҖқ вҖңNo, I hate my life,вҖқ Pierre muttered, wincing. вҖңThou hatest it. Then change it, purify thyself; and as thou art purified, thou wilt gain wisdom. Look at your life, my dear sir. How have you spent it? In riotous orgies and debauchery, receiving everything from society and giving nothing in return. You have become the possessor of wealth. How have you used it? What have you done for your neighbor? Have you ever thought of your tens of thousands of slaves? Have you helped them physically and morally? No! You have profited by their toil to lead a profligate life. That is what you have done. Have you chosen a post in which you might be of service to your neighbor? No! You have spent your life in idleness. Then you married, my dear sirвҒ вҖ”took on yourself responsibility for the guidance of a young woman; and what have you done? You have not helped her to find the way of truth, my dear sir, but have thrust her into an abyss of deceit and misery. A man offended you and you shot him, and you say you do not know God and hate your life. There is nothing strange in that, my dear sir!вҖқ After these words, the Mason, as if tired by his long discourse, again leaned his arms on the back of the sofa and closed his eyes. Pierre looked at that aged, stern, motionless, almost lifeless face and moved his lips without uttering a sound. He wished to say, вҖңYes, a vile, idle, vicious life!вҖқ but dared not break the silence. The Mason cleared his throat huskily, as old men do, and called his servant. вҖңHow about the horses?вҖқ he asked, without looking at Pierre. вҖңThe exchange horses have just come,вҖқ answered the servant. вҖңWill you not rest here?вҖқ вҖңNo, tell them to harness.вҖқ вҖңCan he really be going away leaving me alone without having told me all, and without promising to help me?вҖқ thought Pierre, rising with downcast head; and he began to pace the room, glancing occasionally at the Mason. вҖңYes, I never thought of it, but I have led a contemptible and profligate life, though I did not like it and did not want to,вҖқ thought Pierre. вҖңBut this man knows the truth and, if he wished to, could disclose it to me.вҖқ Pierre wished to say this to the Mason, but did not dare to. The traveler, having packed his things with his practiced hands, began fastening his coat. When he had finished, he turned to BezГәkhov, and said in a tone of indifferent politeness: вҖңWhere are you going to now, my dear sir?вҖқ вҖңI?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ IвҖҷm going to Petersburg,вҖқ answered Pierre, in a childlike, hesitating voice. вҖңI thank you. I agree with all you have said. But do not suppose me to be so bad. With my whole soul I wish to be what you would have me be, but I have never had help from anyone.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But it is I, above all, who am to blame for everything. Help me, teach me, and perhaps I mayвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre could not go on. He gulped and turned away. The Mason remained silent for a long time, evidently considering. вҖңHelp comes from God alone,вҖқ he said, вҖңbut such measure of help as our Order can bestow it will render you, my dear sir. You are going to Petersburg. Hand this to Count WillarskiвҖқ (he took out his notebook and wrote a few words on a large sheet of paper folded in four). вҖңAllow me to give you a piece of advice. When you reach the capital, first of all devote some time to solitude and self-examination and do not resume your former way of life. And now I wish you a good journey, my dear sir,вҖқ he added, seeing that his servant had enteredвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңand success.вҖқ The traveler was Osip AlexГ©evich BazdГ©ev, as Pierre saw from the postmasterвҖҷs book. BazdГ©ev had been one of the best-known Freemasons and Martinists, even in NovГӯkovвҖҷs time. For a long while after he had gone, Pierre did not go to bed or order horses but paced up and down the room, pondering over his vicious past, and with a rapturous sense of beginning anew pictured to himself the blissful, irreproachable, virtuous future that seemed to him so easy. It seemed to him that he had been vicious only because he had somehow forgotten how good it is to be virtuous. Not a trace of his former doubts remained in his soul. He firmly believed in the possibility of the brotherhood of men united in the aim of supporting one another in the path of virtue, and that is how Freemasonry presented itself to him. XX XX RostГіv had come to Tilsit the day least suitable for a petition on DenГӯsovвҖҷs behalf. He could not himself go to the general in attendance as he was in mufti and had come to Tilsit without permission to do so, and BorГӯs, even had he wished to, could not have done so on the following day. On that day, June 27, the preliminaries of peace were signed. The Emperors exchanged decorations: Alexander received the Cross of the Legion of Honor and Napoleon the Order of St.В AndrГ©y of the First Degree, and a dinner had been arranged for the evening, given by a battalion of the French Guards to the PreobrazhГ©nsk battalion. The Emperors were to be present at that banquet. RostГіv felt so ill at ease and uncomfortable with BorГӯs that, when the latter looked in after supper, he pretended to be asleep, and early next morning went away, avoiding BorГӯs. In his civilian clothes and a round hat, he wandered about the town, staring at the French and their uniforms and at the streets and houses where the Russian and French Emperors were staying. In a square he saw tables being set up and preparations made for the dinner; he saw the Russian and French colors draped from side to side of the streets, with huge monograms A and N. In the windows of the houses also flags and bunting were displayed. вҖңBorГӯs doesnвҖҷt want to help me and I donвҖҷt want to ask him. ThatвҖҷs settled,вҖқ thought NikolГЎy. вҖңAll is over between us, but I wonвҖҷt leave here without having done all I can for DenГӯsov and certainly not without getting his letter to the Emperor. The Emperor!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ He is here!вҖқ thought RostГіv, who had unconsciously returned to the house where Alexander lodged. Saddled horses were standing before the house and the suite were assembling, evidently preparing for the Emperor to come out. вҖңI may see him at any moment,вҖқ thought RostГіv. вҖңIf only I were to hand the letter direct to him and tell him allвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ could they really arrest me for my civilian clothes? Surely not! He would understand on whose side justice lies. He understands everything, knows everything. Who can be more just, more magnanimous than he? And even if they did arrest me for being here, what would it matter?вҖқ thought he, looking at an officer who was entering the house the Emperor occupied. вҖңAfter all, people do go in.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ItвҖҷs all nonsense! IвҖҷll go in and hand the letter to the Emperor myself so much the worse for DrubetskГіy who drives me to it!вҖқ And suddenly with a determination he himself did not expect, RostГіv felt for the letter in his pocket and went straight to the house. вҖңNo, I wonвҖҷt miss my opportunity now, as I did after Austerlitz,вҖқ he thought, expecting every moment to meet the monarch, and conscious of the blood that rushed to his heart at the thought. вҖңI will fall at his feet and beseech him. He will lift me up, will listen, and will even thank me. вҖҳI am happy when I can do good, but to remedy injustice is the greatest happiness,вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ RostГіv fancied the sovereign saying. And passing people who looked after him with curiosity, he entered the porch of the EmperorвҖҷs house. A broad staircase led straight up from the entry, and to the right he saw a closed door. Below, under the staircase, was a door leading to the lower floor. вҖңWhom do you want?вҖқ someone inquired. вҖңTo hand in a letter, a petition, to His Majesty,вҖқ said NikolГЎy, with a tremor in his voice. вҖңA petition? This way, to the officer on dutyвҖқ (he was shown the door leading downstairs), вҖңonly it wonвҖҷt be accepted.вҖқ On hearing this indifferent voice, RostГіv grew frightened at what he was doing; the thought of meeting the Emperor at any moment was so fascinating and consequently so alarming that he was ready to run away, but the official who had questioned him opened the door, and RostГіv entered. A short stout man of about thirty, in white breeches and high boots and a batiste shirt that he had evidently only just put on, standing in that room, and his valet was buttoning on to the back of his breeches a new pair of handsome silk-embroidered braces that, for some reason, attracted RostГіvвҖҷs attention. This man was speaking to someone in the adjoining room. вҖңA good figure and in her first bloom,вҖқ he was saying, but on seeing RostГіv, he stopped short and frowned. вҖңWhat is it? A petition?вҖқ вҖңWhat is it?вҖқ asked the person in the other room. вҖңAnother petitioner,вҖқ answered the man with the braces. вҖңTell him to come later. HeвҖҷll be coming out directly, we must go.вҖқ вҖңLaterвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ later! Tomorrow. ItвҖҷs too lateвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ RostГіv turned and was about to go, but the man in the braces stopped him. вҖңWhom have you come from? Who are you?вҖқ вҖңI come from Major DenГӯsov,вҖқ answered RostГіv. вҖңAre you an officer?вҖқ вҖңLieutenant Count RostГіv.вҖқ вҖңWhat audacity! Hand it in through your commander. And go along with youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ go,вҖқ and he continued to put on the uniform the valet handed him. RostГіv went back into the hall and noticed that in the porch there were many officers and generals in full parade uniform, whom he had to pass. Cursing his temerity, his heart sinking at the thought of finding himself at any moment face to face with the Emperor and being put to shame and arrested in his presence, fully alive now to the impropriety of his conduct and repenting of it, RostГіv, with downcast eyes, was making his way out of the house through the brilliant suite when a familiar voice called him and a hand detained him. вҖңWhat are you doing here, sir, in civilian dress?вҖқ asked a deep voice. It was a cavalry general who had obtained the EmperorвҖҷs special favor during this campaign, and who had formerly commanded the division in which RostГіv was serving. RostГіv, in dismay, began justifying himself, but seeing the kindly, jocular face of the general, he took him aside and in an excited voice told him the whole affair, asking him to intercede for DenГӯsov, whom the general knew. Having heard RostГіv to the end, the general shook his head gravely. вҖңIвҖҷm sorry, sorry for that fine fellow. Give me the letter.вҖқ Hardly had RostГіv handed him the letter and finished explaining DenГӯsovвҖҷs case, when hasty steps and the jingling of spurs were heard on the stairs, and the general, leaving him, went to the porch. The gentlemen of the EmperorвҖҷs suite ran down the stairs and went to their horses. Hayne, the same groom who had been at Austerlitz, led up the EmperorвҖҷs horse, and the faint creak of a footstep RostГіv knew at once was heard on the stairs. Forgetting the danger of being recognized, RostГіv went close to the porch, together with some inquisitive civilians, and again, after two years, saw those features he adored: that same face and same look and step, and the same union of majesty and mildness.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And the feeling of enthusiasm and love for his sovereign rose again in RostГіvвҖҷs soul in all its old force. In the uniform of the PreobrazhГ©nsk regimentвҒ вҖ”white chamois-leather breeches and high bootsвҒ вҖ”and wearing a star RostГіv did not know (it was that of the LГ©gion dвҖҷhonneur), the monarch came out into the porch, putting on his gloves and carrying his hat under his arm. He stopped and looked about him, brightening everything around by his glance. He spoke a few words to some of the generals, and, recognizing the former commander of RostГіvвҖҷs division, smiled and beckoned to him. All the suite drew back and RostГіv saw the general talking for some time to the Emperor. The Emperor said a few words to him and took a step toward his horse. Again the crowd of members of the suite and street gazers (among whom was RostГіv) moved nearer to the Emperor. Stopping beside his horse, with his hand on the saddle, the Emperor turned to the cavalry general and said in a loud voice, evidently wishing to be heard by all: вҖңI cannot do it, General. I cannot, because the law is stronger than I,вҖқ and he raised his foot to the stirrup. The general bowed his head respectfully, and the monarch mounted and rode down the street at a gallop. Beside himself with enthusiasm, RostГіv ran after him with the crowd. XXI XXI The Emperor rode to the square where, facing one another, a battalion of the PreobrazhГ©nsk regiment stood on the right and a battalion of the French Guards in their bearskin caps on the left. As the Tsar rode up to one flank of the battalions, which presented arms, another group of horsemen galloped up to the opposite flank, and at the head of them RostГіv recognized Napoleon. It could be no one else. He came at a gallop, wearing a small hat, a blue uniform open over a white vest, and the St.В AndrГ©y ribbon over his shoulder. He was riding a very fine thoroughbred gray Arab horse with a crimson gold-embroidered saddlecloth. On approaching Alexander he raised his hat, and as he did so, RostГіv, with his cavalrymanвҖҷs eye, could not help noticing that Napoleon did not sit well or firmly in the saddle. The battalions shouted вҖңHurrah!вҖқ and вҖңVive lвҖҷEmpereur!вҖқ Napoleon said something to Alexander, and both Emperors dismounted and took each otherвҖҷs hands. NapoleonвҖҷs face wore an unpleasant and artificial smile. Alexander was saying something affable to him. In spite of the trampling of the French gendarmesвҖҷ horses, which were pushing back the crowd, RostГіv kept his eyes on every movement of Alexander and Bonaparte. It struck him as a surprise that Alexander treated Bonaparte as an equal and that the latter was quite at ease with the Tsar, as if such relations with an Emperor were an everyday matter to him. Alexander and Napoleon, with the long train of their suites, approached the right flank of the PreobrazhГ©nsk battalion and came straight up to the crowd standing there. The crowd unexpectedly found itself so close to the Emperors that RostГіv, standing in the front row, was afraid he might be recognized. вҖңSire, I ask your permission to present the Legion of Honor to the bravest of your soldiers,вҖқ said a sharp, precise voice, articulating every letter. This was said by the undersized Napoleon, looking up straight into AlexanderвҖҷs eyes. Alexander listened attentively to what was said to him and, bending his head, smiled pleasantly. вҖңTo him who has borne himself most bravely in this last war,вҖқ added Napoleon, accentuating each syllable, as with a composure and assurance exasperating to RostГіv, he ran his eyes over the Russian ranks drawn up before him, who all presented arms with their eyes fixed on their Emperor. вҖңWill Your Majesty allow me to consult the colonel?вҖқ said Alexander and took a few hasty steps toward Prince KozlГіvski, the commander of the battalion. Bonaparte meanwhile began taking the glove off his small white hand, tore it in doing so, and threw it away. An aide-de-camp behind him rushed forward and picked it up. вҖңTo whom shall it be given?вҖқ the Emperor Alexander asked KozlГіvski, in Russian in a low voice. вҖңTo whomever Your Majesty commands.вҖқ The Emperor knit his brows with dissatisfaction and, glancing back, remarked: вҖңBut we must give him an answer.вҖқ KozlГіvski scanned the ranks resolutely and included RostГіv in his scrutiny. вҖңCan it be me?вҖқ thought RostГіv. вҖңLГЎzarev!вҖқ the colonel called, with a frown, and LГЎzarev, the first soldier in the rank, stepped briskly forward. вҖңWhere are you off to? Stop here!вҖқ voices whispered to LГЎzarev who did not know where to go. LГЎzarev stopped, casting a sidelong look at his colonel in alarm. His face twitched, as often happens to soldiers called before the ranks. Napoleon slightly turned his head, and put his plump little hand out behind him as if to take something. The members of his suite, guessing at once what he wanted, moved about and whispered as they passed something from one to another, and a pageвҒ вҖ”the same one RostГіv had seen the previous evening at BorГӯsвҖҷвҒ вҖ”ran forward and, bowing respectfully over the outstretched hand and not keeping it waiting a moment, laid in it an Order on a red ribbon. Napoleon, without looking, pressed two fingers together and the badge was between them. Then he approached LГЎzarev (who rolled his eyes and persistently gazed at his own monarch), looked round at the Emperor Alexander to imply that what he was now doing was done for the sake of his ally, and the small white hand holding the Order touched one of LГЎzarevвҖҷs buttons. It was as if Napoleon knew that it was only necessary for his hand to deign to touch that soldierвҖҷs breast for the soldier to be forever happy, rewarded, and distinguished from everyone else in the world. Napoleon merely laid the cross on LГЎzarevвҖҷs breast and, dropping his hand, turned toward Alexander as though sure that the cross would adhere there. And it really did. Officious hands, Russian and French, immediately seized the cross and fastened it to the uniform. LГЎzarev glanced morosely at the little man with white hands who was doing something to him and, still standing motionless presenting arms, looked again straight into AlexanderвҖҷs eyes, as if asking whether he should stand there, or go away, or do something else. But receiving no orders, he remained for some time in that rigid position. The Emperors remounted and rode away. The PreobrazhГ©nsk battalion, breaking rank, mingled with the French Guards and sat down at the tables prepared for them. LГЎzarev sat in the place of honor. Russian and French officers embraced him, congratulated him, and pressed his hands. Crowds of officers and civilians drew near merely to see him. A rumble of Russian and French voices and laughter filled the air round the tables in the square. Two officers with flushed faces, looking cheerful and happy, passed by RostГіv. вҖңWhat dвҖҷyou think of the treat? All on silver plate,вҖқ one of them was saying. вҖңHave you seen LГЎzarev?вҖқ вҖңI have.вҖқ вҖңTomorrow, I hear, the PreobrazhГ©nskis will give them a dinner.вҖқ вҖңYes, but what luck for LГЎzarev! Twelve hundred francsвҖҷ pension for life.вҖқ вҖңHereвҖҷs a cap, lads!вҖқ shouted a PreobrazhГ©nsk soldier, donning a shaggy French cap. вҖңItвҖҷs a fine thing!
