I felt abashed and asked whether what he had been saying did not concern me; but he did not reply, gave me a kind look, and then we suddenly found ourselves in my bedroom where there is a double bed. He lay down on the edge of it and I burned with longing to caress him and lie down too. And he said, вҖңTell me frankly what is your chief temptation? Do you know it? I think you know it already.вҖқ Abashed by this question, I replied that sloth was my chief temptation. He shook his head incredulously; and even more abashed, I said that though I was living with my wife as he advised, I was not living with her as her husband. To this he replied that one should not deprive a wife of oneвҖҷs embraces and gave me to understand that that was my duty. But I replied that I should be ashamed to do it, and suddenly everything vanished. And I awoke and found in my mind the text from the Gospel: вҖңThe life was the light of men. And the light shineth in darkness; and the darkness comprehended it not.вҖқ Osip AlexГ©evichвҖҷs face had looked young and bright. That day I received a letter from my benefactor in which he wrote about вҖңconjugal duties.вҖқ 9th December I had a dream from which I awoke with a throbbing heart. I saw that I was in Moscow in my house, in the big sitting room, and Osip AlexГ©evich came in from the drawing room. I seemed to know at once that the process of regeneration had already taken place in him, and I rushed to meet him. I embraced him and kissed his hands, and he said, вҖңHast thou noticed that my face is different?вҖқ I looked at him, still holding him in my arms, and saw that his face was young, but that he had no hair on his head and his features were quite changed. And I said, вҖңI should have known you had I met you by chance,вҖқ and I thought to myself, вҖңAm I telling the truth?вҖқ And suddenly I saw him lying like a dead body; then he gradually recovered and went with me into my study carrying a large book of sheets of drawing paper; I said, вҖңI drew that,вҖқ and he answered by bowing his head. I opened the book, and on all the pages there were excellent drawings. And in my dream I knew that these drawings represented the love adventures of the soul with its beloved. And on its pages I saw a beautiful representation of a maiden in transparent garments and with a transparent body, flying up to the clouds. And I seemed to know that this maiden was nothing else than a representation of the Song of Songs. And looking at those drawings I dreamed I felt that I was doing wrong, but could not tear myself away from them. Lord, help me! My God, if Thy forsaking me is Thy doing, Thy will be done; but if I am myself the cause, teach me what I should do! I shall perish of my debauchery if Thou utterly desertest me! XI XI The RostГіvsвҖҷ monetary affairs had not improved during the two years they had spent in the country. Though NikolГЎy RostГіv had kept firmly to his resolution and was still serving modestly in an obscure regiment, spending comparatively little, the way of life at OtrГЎdnoeвҒ вҖ”MГӯtenkaвҖҷs management of affairs, in particularвҒ вҖ”was such that the debts inevitably increased every year. The only resource obviously presenting itself to the old count was to apply for an official post, so he had come to Petersburg to look for one and also, as he said, to let the lassies enjoy themselves for the last time. Soon after their arrival in Petersburg Berg proposed to VГ©ra and was accepted. Though in Moscow the RostГіvs belonged to the best society without themselves giving it a thought, yet in Petersburg their circle of acquaintances was a mixed and indefinite one. In Petersburg they were provincials, and the very people they had entertained in Moscow without inquiring to what set they belonged, here looked down on them. The RostГіvs lived in the same hospitable way in Petersburg as in Moscow, and the most diverse people met at their suppers. Country neighbors from OtrГЎdnoe, impoverished old squires and their daughters, PerГіnskaya a maid of honor, Pierre BezГәkhov, and the son of their district postmaster who had obtained a post in Petersburg. Among the men who very soon became frequent visitors at the RostГіvsвҖҷ house in Petersburg were BorГӯs, Pierre whom the count had met in the street and dragged home with him, and Berg who spent whole days at the RostГіvsвҖҷ and paid the eldest daughter, Countess VГ©ra, the attentions a young man pays when he intends to propose. Not in vain had Berg shown everybody his right hand wounded at Austerlitz and held a perfectly unnecessary sword in his left. He narrated that episode so persistently and with so important an air that everyone believed in the merit and usefulness of his deed, and he had obtained two decorations for Austerlitz. In the Finnish war he also managed to distinguish himself. He had picked up the scrap of a grenade that had killed an aide-de-camp standing near the commander in chief and had taken it to his commander. Just as he had done after Austerlitz, he related this occurrence at such length and so insistently that everyone again believed it had been necessary to do this, and he received two decorations for the Finnish war also. In 1809 he was a captain in the Guards, wore medals, and held some special lucrative posts in Petersburg. Though some skeptics smiled when told of BergвҖҷs merits, it could not be denied that he was a painstaking and brave officer, on excellent terms with his superiors, and a moral young man with a brilliant career before him and an assured position in society. Four years before, meeting a German comrade in the stalls of a Moscow theater, Berg had pointed out VГ©ra RostГіva to him and had said in German, вҖңdas soll mein Weib werden,вҖқ64 and from that moment had made up his mind to marry her. Now in Petersburg, having considered the RostГіvsвҖҷ position and his own, he decided that the time had come to propose. BergвҖҷs proposal was at first received with a perplexity that was not flattering to him. At first it seemed strange that the son of an obscure Livonian gentleman should propose marriage to a Countess RostГіva; but BergвҖҷs chief characteristic was such a naive and good natured egotism that the RostГіvs involuntarily came to think it would be a good thing, since he himself was so firmly convinced that it was good, indeed excellent. Moreover, the RostГіvsвҖҷ affairs were seriously embarrassed, as the suitor could not but know; and above all, VГ©ra was twenty-four, had been taken out everywhere, and though she was certainly good-looking and sensible, no one up to now had proposed to her. So they gave their consent. вҖңYou see,вҖқ said Berg to his comrade, whom he called вҖңfriendвҖқ only because he knew that everyone has friends, вҖңyou see, I have considered it all, and should not marry if I had not thought it all out or if it were in any way unsuitable. But on the contrary, my papa and mamma are now provided forвҒ вҖ”I have arranged that rent for them in the Baltic ProvincesвҒ вҖ”and I can live in Petersburg on my pay, and with her fortune and my good management we can get along nicely. I am not marrying for moneyвҒ вҖ”I consider that dishonorableвҒ вҖ”but a wife should bring her share and a husband his. I have my position in the service, she has connections and some means. In our times that is worth something, isnвҖҷt it? But above all, she is a handsome, estimable girl, and she loves me.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Berg blushed and smiled. вҖңAnd I love her, because her character is sensible and very good. Now the other sister, though they are the same family, is quite differentвҒ вҖ”an unpleasant character and has not the same intelligence. She is soвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ you know?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ UnpleasantвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But my fiancГ©e!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, you will be coming,вҖқ he was going to say, вҖңto dine,вҖқ but changed his mind and said вҖңto take tea with us,вҖқ and quickly doubling up his tongue he blew a small round ring of tobacco smoke, perfectly embodying his dream of happiness. After the first feeling of perplexity aroused in the parents by BergвҖҷs proposal, the holiday tone of joyousness usual at such times took possession of the family, but the rejoicing was external and insincere. In the familyвҖҷs feeling toward this wedding a certain awkwardness and constraint was evident, as if they were ashamed of not having loved VГ©ra sufficiently and of being so ready to get her off their hands. The old count felt this most. He would probably have been unable to state the cause of his embarrassment, but it resulted from the state of his affairs. He did not know at all how much he had, what his debts amounted to, or what dowry he could give VГ©ra. When his daughters were born he had assigned to each of them, for her dowry, an estate with three hundred serfs; but one of these estates had already been sold, and the other was mortgaged and the interest so much in arrears that it would have to be sold, so that it was impossible to give it to VГ©ra. Nor had he any money. Berg had already been engaged a month, and only a week remained before the wedding, but the count had not yet decided in his own mind the question of the dowry, nor spoken to his wife about it. At one time the count thought of giving her the RyazГЎn estate or of selling a forest, at another time of borrowing money on a note of hand. A few days before the wedding Berg entered the countвҖҷs study early one morning and, with a pleasant smile, respectfully asked his future father-in-law to let him know what VГ©raвҖҷs dowry would be. The count was so disconcerted by this long-foreseen inquiry that without consideration he gave the first reply that came into his head. вҖңI like your being businesslike about it.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I like it. You shall be satisfied.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ And patting Berg on the shoulder he got up, wishing to end the conversation. But Berg, smiling pleasantly, explained that if he did not know for certain how much VГ©ra would have and did not receive at least part of the dowry in advance, he would have to break matters off. вҖңBecause, consider, CountвҒ вҖ”if I allowed myself to marry now without having definite means to maintain my wife, I should be acting badly.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The conversation ended by the count, who wished to be generous and to avoid further importunity, saying that he would give a note of hand for eighty thousand rubles. Berg smiled meekly, kissed the count on the shoulder, and said that he was very grateful, but that it was impossible for him to arrange his new life without receiving thirty thousand in ready money. вҖңOr at least twenty thousand, Count,вҖқ he added, вҖңand then a note of hand for only sixty thousand.вҖқ вҖңYes, yes, all right!вҖқ said the count hurriedly. вҖңOnly excuse me, my dear fellow, IвҖҷll give you twenty thousand and a note of hand for eighty thousand as well. Yes, yes! Kiss me.вҖқ XII XII NatГЎsha was sixteen and it was the year 1809, the very year to which she had counted on her fingers with BorГӯs after they had kissed four years ago. Since then she had not seen him. Before SГіnya and her mother, if BorГӯs happened to be mentioned, she spoke quite freely of that episode as of some childish, long-forgotten matter that was not worth mentioning. But in the secret depths of her soul the question whether her engagement to BorГӯs was a jest or an important, binding promise tormented her. Since BorГӯs left Moscow in 1805 to join the army he had not seen the RostГіvs. He had been in Moscow several times, and had passed near OtrГЎdnoe, but had never been to see them. Sometimes it occurred to NatГЎsha that he did not wish to see her, and this conjecture was confirmed by the sad tone in which her elders spoke of him. вҖңNowadays old friends are not remembered,вҖқ the countess would say when BorГӯs was mentioned. Anna MikhГЎylovna also had of late visited them less frequently, seemed to hold herself with particular dignity, and always spoke rapturously and gratefully of the merits of her son and the brilliant career on which he had entered. When the RostГіvs came to Petersburg BorГӯs called on them. He drove to their house in some agitation. The memory of NatГЎsha was his most poetic recollection. But he went with the firm intention of letting her and her parents feel that the childish relations between himself and NatГЎsha could not be binding either on her or on him. He had a brilliant position in society thanks to his intimacy with Countess BezГәkhova, a brilliant position in the service thanks to the patronage of an important personage whose complete confidence he enjoyed, and he was beginning to make plans for marrying one of the richest heiresses in Petersburg, plans which might very easily be realized. When he entered the RostГіvsвҖҷ drawing room NatГЎsha was in her own room. When she heard of his arrival she almost ran into the drawing room, flushed and beaming with a more than cordial smile. BorГӯs remembered NatГЎsha in a short dress, with dark eyes shining from under her curls and boisterous, childish laughter, as he had known her four years before; and so he was taken aback when quite a different NatГЎsha entered, and his face expressed rapturous astonishment. This expression on his face pleased NatГЎsha. вҖңWell, do you recognize your little madcap playmate?вҖқ asked the countess. BorГӯs kissed NatГЎshaвҖҷs hand and said that he was astonished at the change in her. вҖңHow handsome you have grown!вҖқ вҖңI should think so!вҖқ replied NatГЎshaвҖҷs laughing eyes. вҖңAnd is Papa older?вҖқ she asked. NatГЎsha sat down and, without joining in BorГӯsвҖҷ conversation with the countess, silently and minutely studied her childhoodвҖҷs suitor. He felt the weight of that resolute and affectionate scrutiny and glanced at her occasionally. BorГӯsвҖҷ uniform, spurs, tie, and the way his hair was brushed were all comme il faut and in the latest fashion. This NatГЎsha noticed at once. He sat rather sideways in the armchair next to the countess, arranging with his right hand the cleanest of gloves that fitted his left hand like a skin, and he spoke with a particularly refined compression of his lips about the amusements of the highest Petersburg society, recalling with mild irony old times in Moscow and Moscow acquaintances. It was not accidentally, NatГЎsha felt, that he alluded, when speaking of the highest aristocracy, to an ambassadorвҖҷs ball he had attended, and to invitations he had received from N. N. and S. S. All this time NatГЎsha sat silent, glancing up at him from under her brows. This gaze disturbed and confused BorГӯs more and more. He looked round more frequently toward her, and broke off in what he was saying. He did not stay more than ten minutes, then rose and took his leave. The same inquisitive, challenging, and rather mocking eyes still looked at him. After his first visit BorГӯs said to himself that NatГЎsha attracted him just as much as ever, but that he must not yield to that feeling, because to marry her, a girl almost without fortune, would mean ruin to his career, while to renew their former relations without intending to marry her would be dishonorable. BorГӯs made up his mind to avoid meeting NatГЎsha, but despite that resolution he called again a few days later and began calling often and spending whole days at the RostГіvsвҖҷ. It seemed to him that he ought to have an explanation with NatГЎsha and tell her that the old times must be forgotten, that in spite of everythingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ she could not be his wife, that he had no means, and they would never let her marry him. But he failed to do so and felt awkward about entering on such an explanation. From day to day he became more and more entangled. It seemed to her mother and SГіnya that NatГЎsha was in love with BorГӯs as of old. She sang him his favorite songs, showed him her album, making him write in it, did not allow him to allude to the past, letting it be understood how delightful was the present; and every day he went away in a fog, without having said what he meant to, and not knowing what he was doing or why he came, or how it would all end. He left off visiting ElГЁn and received reproachful notes from her every day, and yet he continued to spend whole days with the RostГіvs. XIII XIII One night when the old countess, in nightcap and dressing jacket, without her false curls, and with her poor little knob of hair showing under her white cotton cap, knelt sighing and groaning on a rug and bowing to the ground in prayer, her door creaked and NatГЎsha, also in a dressing jacket with slippers on her bare feet and her hair in curlpapers, ran in. The countessвҒ вҖ”her prayerful mood dispelledвҒ вҖ”looked round and frowned. She was finishing her last prayer: вҖңCan it be that this couch will be my grave?вҖқ NatГЎsha, flushed and eager, seeing her mother in prayer, suddenly checked her rush, half sat down, and unconsciously put out her tongue as if chiding herself. Seeing that her mother was still praying she ran on tiptoe to the bed and, rapidly slipping one little foot against the other, pushed off her slippers and jumped onto the bed the countess had feared might become her grave. This couch was high, with a feather bed and five pillows each smaller than the one below. NatГЎsha jumped on it, sank into the feather bed, rolled over to the wall, and began snuggling up the bedclothes as she settled down, raising her knees to her chin, kicking out and laughing almost inaudibly, now covering herself up head and all, and now peeping at her mother. The countess finished her prayers and came to the bed with a stern face, but seeing that NatГЎshaвҖҷs head was covered, she smiled in her kind, weak way. вҖңNow then, now then!вҖқ said she. вҖңMamma, can we have a talk? Yes?вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңNow, just one on your throat and anotherвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ thatвҖҷll do!вҖқ And seizing her mother round the neck, she kissed her on the throat. In her behavior to her mother NatГЎsha seemed rough, but she was so sensitive and tactful that however she clasped her mother she always managed to do it without hurting her or making her feel uncomfortable or displeased. вҖңWell, what is it tonight?вҖқ said the mother, having arranged her pillows and waited until NatГЎsha, after turning over a couple of times, had settled down beside her under the quilt, spread out her arms, and assumed a serious expression. These visits of NatГЎshaвҖҷs at night before the count returned from his club were one of the greatest pleasures of both mother, and daughter. вҖңWhat is it tonight?вҒ вҖ”But I have to tell youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ NatГЎsha put her hand on her motherвҖҷs mouth. вҖңAbout BorГӯsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I know,вҖқ she said seriously; вҖңthatвҖҷs what I have come about. DonвҖҷt say itвҒ вҖ”I know. No, do tell me!вҖқ and she removed her hand. вҖңTell me, Mamma! HeвҖҷs nice?вҖқ вҖңNatГЎsha, you are sixteen. At your age I was married. You say BorГӯs is nice. He is very nice, and I love him like a son. But what then?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What are you thinking about? You have quite turned his head, I can see that.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ As she said this the countess looked round at her daughter. NatГЎsha was lying looking steadily straight before her at one of the mahogany sphinxes carved on the corners of the bedstead, so that the countess only saw her daughterвҖҷs face in profile. That face struck her by its peculiarly serious and concentrated expression. NatГЎsha was listening and considering. вҖңWell, what then?вҖқ said she. вҖңYou have quite turned his head, and why? What do you want of him? You know you canвҖҷt marry him.вҖқ вҖңWhy not?вҖқ said NatГЎsha, without changing her position. вҖңBecause he is young, because he is poor, because he is a relationвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and because you yourself donвҖҷt love him.вҖқ вҖңHow do you know?вҖқ вҖңI know. It is not right, darling!вҖқ вҖңBut if I want toвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңLeave off talking nonsense,вҖқ said the countess. вҖңBut if I want toвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңNatГЎsha, I am in earnestвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ NatГЎsha did not let her finish. She drew the countessвҖҷ large hand to her, kissed it on the back and then on the palm, then again turned it over and began kissing first one knuckle, then the space between the knuckles, then the next knuckle, whispering, вҖңJanuary, February, March, April, May. Speak, Mamma, why donвҖҷt you say anything? Speak!вҖқ said she, turning to her mother, who was tenderly gazing at her daughter and in that contemplation seemed to have forgotten all she had wished to say. вҖңIt wonвҖҷt do, my love! Not everyone will understand this friendship dating from your childish days, and to see him so intimate with you may injure you in the eyes of other young men who visit us, and above all it torments him for nothing. He may already have found a suitable and wealthy match, and now heвҖҷs half crazy.вҖқ вҖңCrazy?вҖқ repeated NatГЎsha. вҖңIвҖҷll tell you some things about myself. I had a cousinвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI know! KirГӯla MatvГ©ichвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but he is old.вҖқ вҖңHe was not always old. But this is what IвҖҷll do, NatГЎsha, IвҖҷll have a talk with BorГӯs. He need not come so often.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhy not, if he likes to?вҖқ вҖңBecause I know it will end in nothing.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңHow can you know? No, Mamma, donвҖҷt speak to him! What nonsense!вҖқ said NatГЎsha in the tone of one being deprived of her property. вҖңWell, I wonвҖҷt marry, but let him come if he enjoys it and I enjoy it.вҖқ NatГЎsha smiled and looked at her mother. вҖңNot to marry, but just so,вҖқ she added. вҖңHow so, my pet?вҖқ вҖңJust so. ThereвҖҷs no need for me to marry him. ButвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ just so.вҖқ вҖңJust so, just so,вҖқ repeated the countess, and shaking all over, she went off into a good humored, unexpected, elderly laugh. вҖңDonвҖҷt laugh, stop!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha. вҖңYouвҖҷre shaking the whole bed! YouвҖҷre awfully like me, just such another giggler.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ WaitвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and she seized the countessвҖҷ hands and kissed a knuckle of the little finger, saying, вҖңJune,вҖқ and continued, kissing, вҖңJuly, August,вҖқ on the other hand. вҖңBut, Mamma, is he very much in love? What do you think? Was anybody ever so much in love with you? And heвҖҷs very nice, very, very nice. Only not quite my tasteвҒ вҖ”he is so narrow, like the dining-room clock.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ DonвҖҷt you understand? Narrow, you knowвҒ вҖ”gray, light grayвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhat rubbish youвҖҷre talking!вҖқ said the countess. NatГЎsha continued: вҖңDonвҖҷt you really understand? NikГіlenka would understand.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ BezГәkhov, now, is blue, dark-blue and red, and he is square.вҖқ вҖңYou flirt with him too,вҖқ said the countess, laughing. вҖңNo, he is a Freemason, I have found out. He is fine, dark-blue and red.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ How can I explain it to you?вҖқ вҖңLittle countess!вҖқ the countвҖҷs voice called from behind the door. вҖңYouвҖҷre not asleep?вҖқ NatГЎsha jumped up, snatched up her slippers, and ran barefoot to her own room. It was a long time before she could sleep. She kept thinking that no one could understand all that she understood and all there was in her. вҖңSГіnya?вҖқ she thought, glancing at that curled-up, sleeping little kitten with her enormous plait of hair. вҖңNo, how could she? SheвҖҷs virtuous. She fell in love with NikГіlenka and does not wish to know anything more. Even Mamma does not understand. It is wonderful how clever I am and howвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ charming she is,вҖқ she went on, speaking of herself in the third person, and imagining it was some very wise manвҒ вҖ”the wisest and best of menвҒ вҖ”who was saying it of her. вҖңThere is everything, everything in her,вҖқ continued this man. вҖңShe is unusually intelligent, charmingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and then she is pretty, uncommonly pretty, and agileвҒ вҖ”she swims and rides splendidlyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and her voice! One can really say itвҖҷs a wonderful voice!вҖқ She hummed a scrap from her favorite opera by Cherubini, threw herself on her bed, laughed at the pleasant thought that she would immediately fall asleep, called DunyГЎsha the maid to put out the candle, and before DunyГЎsha had left the room had already passed into yet another happier world of dreams, where everything was as light and beautiful as in reality, and even more so because it was different. Next day the countess called BorГӯs aside and had a talk with him, after which he ceased coming to the RostГіvsвҖҷ. XIV XIV On the thirty-first of December, New YearвҖҷs Eve, 1809вҒ вҖ“вҒ 10 an old grandee of CatherineвҖҷs day was giving a ball and midnight supper. The diplomatic corps and the Emperor himself were to be present. The grandeeвҖҷs well-known mansion on the English Quay glittered with innumerable lights. Police were stationed at the brightly lit entrance which was carpeted with red baize, and not only gendarmes but dozens of police officers and even the police master himself stood at the porch. Carriages kept driving away and fresh ones arriving, with red-liveried footmen and footmen in plumed hats. From the carriages emerged men wearing uniforms, stars, and ribbons, while ladies in satin and ermine cautiously descended the carriage steps which were let down for them with a clatter, and then walked hurriedly and noiselessly over the baize at the entrance. Almost every time a new carriage drove up a whisper ran through the crowd and caps were doffed. вҖңThe Emperor?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No, a ministerвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ princeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ambassador. DonвҖҷt you see the plumes?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ was whispered among the crowd. One person, better dressed than the rest, seemed to know everyone and mentioned by name the greatest dignitaries of the day. A third of the visitors had already arrived, but the RostГіvs, who were to be present, were still hurrying to get dressed. There had been many discussions and preparations for this ball in the RostГіv family, many fears that the invitation would not arrive, that the dresses would not be ready, or that something would not be arranged as it should be. MГЎrya IgnГЎtevna PerГіnskaya, a thin and shallow maid of honor at the court of the Dowager Empress, who was a friend and relation of the countess and piloted the provincial RostГіvs in Petersburg high society, was to accompany them to the ball. They were to call for her at her house in the Taurida Gardens at ten oвҖҷclock, but it was already five minutes to ten, and the girls were not yet dressed. NatГЎsha was going to her first grand ball. She had got up at eight that morning and had been in a fever of excitement and activity all day. All her powers since morning had been concentrated on ensuring that they allвҒ вҖ”she herself, Mamma, and SГіnyaвҒ вҖ”should be as well dressed as possible. SГіnya and her mother put themselves entirely in her hands. The countess was to wear a claret-colored velvet dress, and the two girls white gauze over pink silk slips, with roses on their bodices and their hair dressed Г  la grecque. Everything essential had already been done; feet, hands, necks, and ears washed, perfumed, and powdered, as befits a ball; the openwork silk stockings and white satin shoes with ribbons were already on; the hairdressing was almost done. SГіnya was finishing dressing and so was the countess, but NatГЎsha, who had bustled about helping them all, was behindhand. She was still sitting before a looking-glass with a dressing jacket thrown over her slender shoulders. SГіnya stood ready dressed in the middle of the room and, pressing the head of a pin till it hurt her dainty finger, was fixing on a last ribbon that squeaked as the pin went through it. вҖңThatвҖҷs not the way, thatвҖҷs not the way, SГіnya!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha turning her head and clutching with both hands at her hair which the maid who was dressing it had not time to release. вҖңThat bow is not right. Come here!вҖқ SГіnya sat down and NatГЎsha pinned the ribbon on differently. вҖңAllow me, Miss! I canвҖҷt do it like that,вҖқ said the maid who was holding NatГЎshaвҖҷs hair. вҖңOh, dear! Well then, wait. ThatвҖҷs right, SГіnya.вҖқ вҖңArenвҖҷt you ready? It is nearly ten,вҖқ came the countessвҖҷ voice. вҖңDirectly! Directly! And you, Mamma?вҖқ вҖңI have only my cap to pin on.вҖқ вҖңDonвҖҷt do it without me!вҖқ called NatГЎsha. вҖңYou wonвҖҷt do it right.вҖқ вҖңBut itвҖҷs already ten.вҖқ They had decided to be at the ball by half-past ten, and NatГЎsha had still to get dressed and they had to call at the Taurida Gardens. When her hair was done, NatГЎsha, in her short petticoat from under which her dancing shoes showed, and in her motherвҖҷs dressing jacket, ran up to SГіnya, scrutinized her, and then ran to her mother. Turning her motherвҖҷs head this way and that, she fastened on the cap and, hurriedly kissing her gray hair, ran back to the maids who were turning up the hem of her skirt. The cause of the delay was NatГЎshaвҖҷs skirt, which was too long. Two maids were turning up the hem and hurriedly biting off the ends of thread. A third with pins in her mouth was running about between the countess and SГіnya, and a fourth held the whole of the gossamer garment up high on one uplifted hand. вҖңMГЎvrusha, quicker, darling!вҖқ вҖңGive me my thimble, Miss, from thereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhenever will you be ready?вҖқ asked the count coming to the door. вҖңHere is some scent. PerГіnskaya must be tired of waiting.вҖқ вҖңItвҖҷs ready, Miss,вҖқ said the maid, holding up the shortened gauze dress with two fingers, and blowing and shaking something off it, as if by this to express a consciousness of the airiness and purity of what she held. NatГЎsha began putting on the dress. вҖңIn a minute! In a minute! DonвҖҷt come in, Papa!вҖқ she cried to her father as he opened the doorвҒ вҖ”speaking from under the filmy skirt which still covered her whole face. SГіnya slammed the door to. A minute later they let the count in. He was wearing a blue swallowtail coat, shoes and stockings, and was perfumed and his hair pomaded. вҖңOh, Papa! how nice you look! Charming!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha, as she stood in the middle of the room smoothing out the folds of the gauze. вҖңIf you please, Miss! allow me,вҖқ said the maid, who on her knees was pulling the skirt straight and shifting the pins from one side of her mouth to the other with her tongue. вҖңSay what you like,вҖқ exclaimed SГіnya, in a despairing voice as she looked at NatГЎsha, вҖңsay what you like, itвҖҷs still too long.вҖқ NatГЎsha stepped back to look at herself in the pier glass. The dress was too long. вҖңReally, madam, it is not at all too long,вҖқ said MГЎvrusha, crawling on her knees after her young lady. вҖңWell, if itвҖҷs too long weвҖҷll tack it upвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ weвҖҷll tack it up in one minute,вҖқ said the resolute DunyГЎsha taking a needle that was stuck on the front of her little shawl and, still kneeling on the floor, set to work once more. At that moment, with soft steps, the countess came in shyly, in her cap and velvet gown. вҖңOo-oo, my beauty!вҖқ exclaimed the count, вҖңshe looks better than any of you!вҖқ He would have embraced her but, blushing, she stepped aside fearing to be rumpled. вҖңMamma, your cap, more to this side,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңIвҖҷll arrange it,вҖқ and she rushed forward so that the maids who were tacking up her skirt could not move fast enough and a piece of gauze was torn off. вҖңOh goodness! What has happened? Really it was not my fault!вҖқ вҖңNever mind, IвҖҷll run it up, it wonвҖҷt show,вҖқ said DunyГЎsha. вҖңWhat a beautyвҒ вҖ”a very queen!вҖқ said the nurse as she came to the door. вҖңAnd SonyГәshka! They are lovely!вҖқ At a quarter past ten they at last got into their carriages and started. But they had still to call at the Taurida Gardens. PerГіnskaya was quite ready. In spite of her age and plainness she had gone through the same process as the RostГіvs, but with less flurryвҒ вҖ”for to her it was a matter of routine. Her ugly old body was washed, perfumed, and powdered in just the same way. She had washed behind her ears just as carefully, and when she entered her drawing room in her yellow dress, wearing her badge as maid of honor, her old ladyвҖҷs maid was as full of rapturous admiration as the RostГіvsвҖҷ servants had been. She praised the RostГіvsвҖҷ toilets. They praised her taste and toilet, and at eleven oвҖҷclock, careful of their coiffures and dresses, they settled themselves in their carriages and drove off. XV XV NatГЎsha had not had a moment free since early morning and had not once had time to think of what lay before her. In the damp chill air and crowded closeness of the swaying carriage, she for the first time vividly imagined what was in store for her there at the ball, in those brightly lighted roomsвҒ вҖ”with music, flowers, dances, the Emperor, and all the brilliant young people of Petersburg. The prospect was so splendid that she hardly believed it would come true, so out of keeping was it with the chill darkness and closeness of the carriage. She understood all that awaited her only when, after stepping over the red baize at the entrance, she entered the hall, took off her fur cloak, and, beside SГіnya and in front of her mother, mounted the brightly illuminated stairs between the flowers. Only then did she remember how she must behave at a ball, and tried to assume the majestic air she considered indispensable for a girl on such an occasion. But, fortunately for her, she felt her eyes growing misty, she saw nothing clearly, her pulse beat a hundred to the minute, and the blood throbbed at her heart. She could not assume that pose, which would have made her ridiculous, and she moved on almost fainting from excitement and trying with all her might to conceal it. And this was the very attitude that became her best. Before and behind them other visitors were entering, also talking in low tones and wearing ball dresses. The mirrors on the landing reflected ladies in white, pale-blue, and pink dresses, with diamonds and pearls on their bare necks and arms. NatГЎsha looked in the mirrors and could not distinguish her reflection from the others. All was blended into one brilliant procession. On entering the ballroom the regular hum of voices, footsteps, and greetings deafened NatГЎsha, and the light and glitter dazzled her still more. The host and hostess, who had already been standing at the door for half an hour repeating the same words to the various arrivals, вҖңCharmГ© de vous voir,вҖқ65 greeted the RostГіvs and PerГіnskaya in the same manner. The two girls in their white dresses, each with a rose in her black hair, both curtsied in the same way, but the hostessвҖҷ eye involuntarily rested longer on the slim NatГЎsha. She looked at her and gave her alone a special smile in addition to her usual smile as hostess. Looking at her she may have recalled the golden, irrecoverable days of her own girlhood and her own first ball. The host also followed NatГЎsha with his eyes and asked the count which was his daughter. вҖңCharming!вҖқ said he, kissing the tips of his fingers. In the ballroom guests stood crowding at the entrance doors awaiting the Emperor. The countess took up a position in one of the front rows of that crowd. NatГЎsha heard and felt that several people were asking about her and looking at her. She realized that those noticing her liked her, and this observation helped to calm her. вҖңThere are some like ourselves and some worse,вҖқ she thought. PerГіnskaya was pointing out to the countess the most important people at the ball. вҖңThat is the Dutch ambassador, do you see? That gray-haired man,вҖқ she said, indicating an old man with a profusion of silver-gray curly hair, who was surrounded by ladies laughing at something he said. вҖңAh, here she is, the Queen of Petersburg, Countess BezГәkhova,вҖқ said PerГіnskaya, indicating ElГЁn who had just entered. вҖңHow lovely! She is quite equal to MГЎrya AntГіnovna. See how the men, young and old, pay court to her. Beautiful and cleverвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ they say Prince вё» is quite mad about her. But see, those two, though not good-looking, are even more run after.вҖқ She pointed to a lady who was crossing the room followed by a very plain daughter. вҖңShe is a splendid match, a millionairess,вҖқ said PerГіnskaya. вҖңAnd look, here come her suitors.вҖқ вҖңThat is BezГәkhovaвҖҷs brother, Anatole KurГЎgin,вҖқ she said, indicating a handsome officer of the Horse Guards who passed by them with head erect, looking at something over the heads of the ladies. вҖңHeвҖҷs handsome, isnвҖҷt he? I hear they will marry him to that rich girl. But your cousin, DrubetskГіy, is also very attentive to her. They say she has millions. Oh yes, thatвҖҷs the French ambassador himself!вҖқ she replied to the countessвҖҷ inquiry about Caulaincourt. вҖңLooks as if he were a king! All the same, the French are charming, very charming. No one more charming in society. Ah, here she is! Yes, she is still the most beautiful of them all, our MГЎrya AntГіnovna! And how simply she is dressed! Lovely! And that stout one in spectacles is the universal Freemason,вҖқ she went on, indicating Pierre. вҖңPut him beside his wife and he looks a regular buffoon!вҖқ Pierre, swaying his stout body, advanced, making way through the crowd and nodding to right and left as casually and good-naturedly as if he were passing through a crowd at a fair. He pushed through, evidently looking for someone. NatГЎsha looked joyfully at the familiar face of Pierre, вҖңthe buffoon,вҖқ as PerГіnskaya had called him, and knew he was looking for them, and for her in particular. He had promised to be at the ball and introduce partners to her. But before he reached them Pierre stopped beside a very handsome, dark man of middle height, and in a white uniform, who stood by a window talking to a tall man wearing stars and a ribbon. NatГЎsha at once recognized the shorter and younger man in the white uniform: it was BolkГіnski, who seemed to her to have grown much younger, happier, and better-looking. вҖңThereвҖҷs someone else we knowвҒ вҖ”BolkГіnski, do you see, Mamma?вҖқ said NatГЎsha, pointing out Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңYou remember, he stayed a night with us at OtrГЎdnoe.вҖқ вҖңOh, you know him?вҖқ said PerГіnskaya. вҖңI canвҖҷt bear him. Il fait Г  prГ©sent la pluie et le beau temps.66 HeвҖҷs too proud for anything. Takes after his father. And heвҖҷs hand in glove with SperГЎnski, writing some project or other. Just look how he treats the ladies! ThereвҖҷs one talking to him and he has turned away,вҖқ she said, pointing at him. вҖңIвҖҷd give it to him if he treated me as he does those ladies.вҖқ XVI XVI Suddenly everybody stirred, began talking, and pressed forward and then back, and between the two rows, which separated, the Emperor entered to the sounds of music that had immediately struck up. Behind him walked his host and hostess. He walked in rapidly, bowing to right and left as if anxious to get the first moments of the reception over. The band played the polonaise in vogue at that time on account of the words that had been set to it, beginning: вҖңAlexander, Elisaveta, all our hearts you ravish quiteвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The Emperor passed on to the drawing room, the crowd made a rush for the doors, and several persons with excited faces hurried there and back again. Then the crowd hastily retired from the drawing room door, at which the Emperor reappeared talking to the hostess. A young man, looking distraught, pounced down on the ladies, asking them to move aside. Some ladies, with faces betraying complete forgetfulness of all the rules of decorum, pushed forward to the detriment of their toilets. The men began to choose partners and take their places for the polonaise. Everyone moved back, and the Emperor came smiling out of the drawing room leading his hostess by the hand but not keeping time to the music. The host followed with MГЎrya AntГіnovna NarГҪshkina; then came ambassadors, ministers, and various generals, whom PerГіnskaya diligently named. More than half the ladies already had partners and were taking up, or preparing to take up, their positions for the polonaise. NatГЎsha felt that she would be left with her mother and SГіnya among a minority of women who crowded near the wall, not having been invited to dance. She stood with her slender arms hanging down, her scarcely defined bosom rising and falling regularly, and with bated breath and glittering, frightened eyes gazed straight before her, evidently prepared for the height of joy or misery. She was not concerned about the Emperor or any of those great people whom PerГіnskaya was pointing outвҒ вҖ”she had but one thought: вҖңIs it possible no one will ask me, that I shall not be among the first to dance? Is it possible that not one of all these men will notice me? They do not even seem to see me, or if they do they look as if they were saying, вҖҳAh, sheвҖҷs not the one IвҖҷm after, so itвҖҷs not worth looking at her!вҖҷ No, itвҖҷs impossible,вҖқ she thought. вҖңThey must know how I long to dance, how splendidly I dance, and how they would enjoy dancing with me.вҖқ The strains of the polonaise, which had continued for a considerable time, had begun to sound like a sad reminiscence to NatГЎshaвҖҷs ears. She wanted to cry. PerГіnskaya had left them. The count was at the other end of the room. She and the countess and SГіnya were standing by themselves as in the depths of a forest amid that crowd of strangers, with no one interested in them and not wanted by anyone. Prince AndrГ©y with a lady passed by, evidently not recognizing them. The handsome Anatole was smilingly talking to a partner on his arm and looked at NatГЎsha as one looks at a wall. BorГӯs passed them twice and each time turned away. Berg and his wife, who were not dancing, came up to them. This family gathering seemed humiliating to NatГЎshaвҒ вҖ”as if there were nowhere else for the family to talk but here at the ball. She did not listen to or look at VГ©ra, who was telling her something about her own green dress. At last the Emperor stopped beside his last partner (he had danced with three) and the music ceased. A worried aide-de-camp ran up to the RostГіvs requesting them to stand farther back, though as it was they were already close to the wall, and from the gallery resounded the distinct, precise, enticingly rhythmical strains of a waltz. The Emperor looked smilingly down the room. A minute passed but no one had yet begun dancing. An aide-de-camp, the Master of Ceremonies, went up to Countess BezГәkhova and asked her to dance. She smilingly raised her hand and laid it on his shoulder without looking at him. The aide-de-camp, an adept in his art, grasping his partner firmly round her waist, with confident deliberation started smoothly, gliding first round the edge of the circle, then at the corner of the room he caught ElГЁnвҖҷs left hand and turned her, the only sound audible, apart from the ever-quickening music, being the rhythmic click of the spurs on his rapid, agile feet, while at every third beat his partnerвҖҷs velvet dress spread out and seemed to flash as she whirled round. NatГЎsha gazed at them and was ready to cry because it was not she who was dancing that first turn of the waltz. Prince AndrГ©y, in the white uniform of a cavalry colonel, wearing stockings and dancing shoes, stood looking animated and bright in the front row of the circle not far from the RostГіvs. Baron Firhoff was talking to him about the first sitting of the Council of State to be held next day. Prince AndrГ©y, as one closely connected with SperГЎnski and participating in the work of the legislative commission, could give reliable information about that sitting, concerning which various rumors were current. But not listening to what Firhoff was saying, he was gazing now at the sovereign and now at the men intending to dance who had not yet gathered courage to enter the circle. Prince AndrГ©y was watching these men abashed by the EmperorвҖҷs presence, and the women who were breathlessly longing to be asked to dance. Pierre came up to him and caught him by the arm. вҖңYou always dance. I have a protГ©gГ©e, the young RostГіva, here. Ask her,вҖқ he said. вҖңWhere is she?вҖқ asked BolkГіnski. вҖңExcuse me!вҖқ he added, turning to the baron, вҖңwe will finish this conversation elsewhereвҒ вҖ”at a ball one must dance.вҖқ He stepped forward in the direction Pierre indicated. The despairing, dejected expression of NatГЎshaвҖҷs face caught his eye. He recognized her, guessed her feelings, saw that it was her dГ©but, remembered her conversation at the window, and with an expression of pleasure on his face approached Countess RostГіva. вҖңAllow me to introduce you to my daughter,вҖқ said the countess, with heightened color. вҖңI have the pleasure of being already acquainted, if the countess remembers me,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y with a low and courteous bow quite belying PerГіnskayaвҖҷs remarks about his rudeness, and approaching NatГЎsha he held out his arm to grasp her waist before he had completed his invitation. He asked her to waltz. That tremulous expression on NatГЎshaвҖҷs face, prepared either for despair or rapture, suddenly brightened into a happy, grateful, childlike smile. вҖңI have long been waiting for you,вҖқ that frightened happy little girl seemed to say by the smile that replaced the threatened tears, as she raised her hand to Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs shoulder. They were the second couple to enter the circle. Prince AndrГ©y was one of the best dancers of his day and NatГЎsha danced exquisitely. Her little feet in their white satin dancing shoes did their work swiftly, lightly, and independently of herself, while her face beamed with ecstatic happiness. Her slender bare arms and neck were not beautifulвҒ вҖ”compared to ElГЁnвҖҷs her shoulders looked thin and her bosom undeveloped. But ElГЁn seemed, as it were, hardened by a varnish left by the thousands of looks that had scanned her person, while NatГЎsha was like a girl exposed for the first time, who would have felt very much ashamed had she not been assured that this was absolutely necessary. Prince AndrГ©y liked dancing, and wishing to escape as quickly as possible from the political and clever talk which everyone addressed to him, wishing also to break up the circle of restraint he disliked, caused by the EmperorвҖҷs presence, he danced, and had chosen NatГЎsha because Pierre pointed her out to him and because she was the first pretty girl who caught his eye; but scarcely had he embraced that slender supple figure and felt her stirring so close to him and smiling so near him than the wine of her charm rose to his head, and he felt himself revived and rejuvenated when after leaving her he stood breathing deeply and watching the other dancers. XVII XVII After Prince AndrГ©y, BorГӯs came up to ask NatГЎsha for a dance, and then the aide-de-camp who had opened the ball, and several other young men, so that, flushed and happy, and passing on her superfluous partners to SГіnya, she did not cease dancing all the evening. She noticed and saw nothing of what occupied everyone else. Not only did she fail to notice that the Emperor talked a long time with the French ambassador, and how particularly gracious he was to a certain lady, or that Prince So-and-so and So-and-so did and said this and that, and that ElГЁn had great success and was honored by the special attention of So-and-so, but she did not even see the Emperor, and only noticed that he had gone because the ball became livelier after his departure. For one of the merry cotillions before supper Prince AndrГ©y was again her partner. He reminded her of their first encounter in the OtrГЎdnoe avenue, and how she had been unable to sleep that moonlight night, and told her how he had involuntarily overheard her. NatГЎsha blushed at that recollection and tried to excuse herself, as if there had been something to be ashamed of in what Prince AndrГ©y had overheard. Like all men who have grown up in society, Prince AndrГ©y liked meeting someone there not of the conventional society stamp. And such was NatГЎsha, with her surprise, her delight, her shyness, and even her mistakes in speaking French. With her he behaved with special care and tenderness, sitting beside her and talking of the simplest and most unimportant matters; he admired her shy grace. In the middle of the cotillion, having completed one of the figures, NatГЎsha, still out of breath, was returning to her seat when another dancer chose her. She was tired and panting and evidently thought of declining, but immediately put her hand gaily on the manвҖҷs shoulder, smiling at Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңIвҖҷd be glad to sit beside you and rest: IвҖҷm tired; but you see how they keep asking me, and IвҖҷm glad of it, IвҖҷm happy and I love everybody, and you and I understand it all,вҖқ and much, much more was said in her smile. When her partner left her NatГЎsha ran across the room to choose two ladies for the figure. вҖңIf she goes to her cousin first and then to another lady, she will be my wife,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y to himself quite to his own surprise, as he watched her. She did go first to her cousin. вҖңWhat rubbish sometimes enters oneвҖҷs head!вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңbut what is certain is that that girl is so charming, so original, that she wonвҖҷt be dancing here a month before she will be married.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Such as she are rare here,вҖқ he thought, as NatГЎsha, readjusting a rose that was slipping on her bodice, settled herself beside him. When the cotillion was over the old count in his blue coat came up to the dancers. He invited Prince AndrГ©y to come and see them, and asked his daughter whether she was enjoying herself. NatГЎsha did not answer at once but only looked up with a smile that said reproachfully: вҖңHow can you ask such a question?вҖқ вҖңI have never enjoyed myself so much before!вҖқ she said, and Prince AndrГ©y noticed how her thin arms rose quickly as if to embrace her father and instantly dropped again. NatГЎsha was happier than she had ever been in her life. She was at that height of bliss when one becomes completely kind and good and does not believe in the possibility of evil, unhappiness, or sorrow. At that ball Pierre for the first time felt humiliated by the position his wife occupied in court circles. He was gloomy and absentminded. A deep furrow ran across his forehead, and standing by a window he stared over his spectacles seeing no one. On her way to supper NatГЎsha passed him. PierreвҖҷs gloomy, unhappy look struck her. She stopped in front of him. She wished to help him, to bestow on him the superabundance of her own happiness. вҖңHow delightful it is, Count!вҖқ said she. вҖңIsnвҖҷt it?вҖқ Pierre smiled absentmindedly, evidently not grasping what she said. вҖңYes, I am very glad,вҖқ he said. вҖңHow can people be dissatisfied with anything?вҖқ thought NatГЎsha. вҖңEspecially such a capital fellow as BezГәkhov!вҖқ In NatГЎshaвҖҷs eyes all the people at the ball alike were good, kind, and splendid people, loving one another; none of them capable of injuring anotherвҒ вҖ”and so they ought all to be happy. XVIII XVIII Next day Prince AndrГ©y thought of the ball, but his mind did not dwell on it long. вҖңYes, it was a very brilliant ball,вҖқ and thenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңYes, that little RostГіva is very charming. ThereвҖҷs something fresh, original, un-Petersburg-like about her that distinguishes her.вҖқ That was all he thought about yesterdayвҖҷs ball, and after his morning tea he set to work. But either from fatigue or want of sleep he was ill-disposed for work and could get nothing done. He kept criticizing his own work, as he often did, and was glad when he heard someone coming. The visitor was BГӯtski, who served on various committees, frequented all the societies in Petersburg, and was a passionate devotee of the new ideas and of SperГЎnski, and a diligent Petersburg newsmongerвҒ вҖ”one of those men who choose their opinions like their clothes according to the fashion, but who for that very reason appear to be the warmest partisans. Hardly had he got rid of his hat before he ran into Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs room with a preoccupied air and at once began talking. He had just heard particulars of that morningвҖҷs sitting of the Council of State opened by the Emperor, and he spoke of it enthusiastically. The EmperorвҖҷs speech had been extraordinary. It had been a speech such as only constitutional monarchs deliver. вҖңThe Sovereign plainly said that the Council and Senate are estates of the realm, he said that the government must rest not on authority but on secure bases. The Emperor said that the fiscal system must be reorganized and the accounts published,вҖқ recounted BГӯtski, emphasizing certain words and opening his eyes significantly. вҖңAh, yes! TodayвҖҷs events mark an epoch, the greatest epoch in our history,вҖқ he concluded. Prince AndrГ©y listened to the account of the opening of the Council of State, which he had so impatiently awaited and to which he had attached such importance, and was surprised that this event, now that it had taken place, did not affect him, and even seemed quite insignificant. He listened with quiet irony to BГӯtskiвҖҷs enthusiastic account of it. A very simple thought occurred to him: вҖңWhat does it matter to me or to BГӯtski what the Emperor was pleased to say at the Council? Can all that make me any happier or better?вҖқ And this simple reflection suddenly destroyed all the interest Prince AndrГ©y had felt in the impending reforms. He was going to dine that evening at SperГЎnskiвҖҷs, вҖңwith only a few friends,вҖқ as the host had said when inviting him. The prospect of that dinner in the intimate home circle of the man he so admired had greatly interested Prince AndrГ©y, especially as he had not yet seen SperГЎnski in his domestic surroundings, but now he felt disinclined to go to it. At the appointed hour, however, he entered the modest house SperГЎnski owned in the Taurida Gardens. In the parqueted dining room of this small house, remarkable for its extreme cleanliness (suggesting that of a monastery), Prince AndrГ©y, who was rather late, found the friendly gathering of SperГЎnskiвҖҷs intimate acquaintances already assembled at five oвҖҷclock. There were no ladies present except SperГЎnskiвҖҷs little daughter (long-faced like her father) and her governess. The other guests were Gervais, MagnГӯtski, and StolГҪpin. While still in the anteroom Prince AndrГ©y heard loud voices and a ringing staccato laughвҒ вҖ”a laugh such as one hears on the stage. SomeoneвҒ вҖ”it sounded like SperГЎnskiвҒ вҖ”was distinctly ejaculating ha-ha-ha. Prince AndrГ©y had never before heard SperГЎnskiвҖҷs famous laugh, and this ringing, high-pitched laughter from a statesman made a strange impression on him. He entered the dining room. The whole company were standing between two windows at a small table laid with hors-dвҖҷoeuvres. SperГЎnski, wearing a gray swallowtail coat with a star on the breast, and evidently still the same waistcoat and high white stock he had worn at the meeting of the Council of State, stood at the table with a beaming countenance. His guests surrounded him. MagnГӯtski, addressing himself to SperГЎnski, was relating an anecdote, and SperГЎnski was laughing in advance at what MagnГӯtski was going to say. When Prince AndrГ©y entered the room MagnГӯtskiвҖҷs words were again crowned by laughter. StolГҪpin gave a deep bass guffaw as he munched a piece of bread and cheese. Gervais laughed softly with a hissing chuckle, and SperГЎnski in a high-pitched staccato manner. Still laughing, SperГЎnski held out his soft white hand to Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңVery pleased to see you, Prince,вҖқ he said. вҖңOne momentвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he went on, turning to MagnГӯtski and interrupting his story. вҖңWe have agreed that this is a dinner for recreation, with not a word about business!вҖқ and turning again to the narrator he began to laugh afresh. Prince AndrГ©y looked at the laughing SperГЎnski with astonishment, regret, and disillusionment. It seemed to him that this was not SperГЎnski but someone else. Everything that had formerly appeared mysterious and fascinating in SperГЎnski suddenly became plain and unattractive. At dinner the conversation did not cease for a moment and seemed to consist of the contents of a book of funny anecdotes. Before MagnГӯtski had finished his story someone else was anxious to relate something still funnier. Most of the anecdotes, if not relating to the state service, related to people in the service. It seemed that in this company the insignificance of those people was so definitely accepted that the only possible attitude toward them was one of good humored ridicule. SperГЎnski related how at the Council that morning a deaf dignitary, when asked his opinion, replied that he thought so too. Gervais gave a long account of an official revision, remarkable for the stupidity of everybody concerned. StolГҪpin, stuttering, broke into the conversation and began excitedly talking of the abuses that existed under the former order of thingsвҒ вҖ”threatening to give a serious turn to the conversation. MagnГӯtski starting quizzing StolГҪpin about his vehemence. Gervais intervened with a joke, and the talk reverted to its former lively tone. Evidently SperГЎnski liked to rest after his labors and find amusement in a circle of friends, and his guests, understanding his wish, tried to enliven him and amuse themselves. But their gaiety seemed to Prince AndrГ©y mirthless and tiresome. SperГЎnskiвҖҷs high-pitched voice struck him unpleasantly, and the incessant laughter grated on him like a false note. Prince AndrГ©y did not laugh and feared that he would be a damper on the spirits of the company, but no one took any notice of his being out of harmony with the general mood. They all seemed very gay. He tried several times to join in the conversation, but his remarks were tossed aside each time like a cork thrown out of the water, and he could not jest with them. There was nothing wrong or unseemly in what they said, it was witty and might have been funny, but it lacked just that something which is the salt of mirth, and they were not even aware that such a thing existed. After dinner SperГЎnskiвҖҷs daughter and her governess rose. He patted the little girl with his white hand and kissed her. And that gesture, too, seemed unnatural to Prince AndrГ©y. The men remained at table over their portвҒ вҖ”English fashion. In the midst of a conversation that was started about NapoleonвҖҷs Spanish affairs, which they all agreed in approving, Prince AndrГ©y began to express a contrary opinion. SperГЎnski smiled and, with an evident wish to prevent the conversation from taking an unpleasant course, told a story that had no connection with the previous conversation. For a few moments all were silent. Having sat some time at table, SperГЎnski corked a bottle of wine and, remarking, вҖңNowadays good wine rides in a carriage and pair,вҖқ passed it to the servant and got up. All rose and continuing to talk loudly went into the drawing room. Two letters brought by a courier were handed to SperГЎnski and he took them to his study. As soon as he had left the room the general merriment stopped and the guests began to converse sensibly and quietly with one another. вҖңNow for the recitation!вҖқ said SperГЎnski on returning from his study. вҖңA wonderful talent!вҖқ he said to Prince AndrГ©y, and MagnГӯtski immediately assumed a pose and began reciting some humorous verses in French which he had composed about various well-known Petersburg people. He was interrupted several times by applause. When the verses were finished Prince AndrГ©y went up to SperГЎnski and took his leave. вҖңWhere are you off to so early?вҖқ asked SperГЎnski. вҖңI promised to go to a reception.вҖқ They said no more. Prince AndrГ©y looked closely into those mirrorlike, impenetrable eyes, and felt that it had been ridiculous of him to have expected anything from SperГЎnski and from any of his own activities connected with him, or ever to have attributed importance to what SperГЎnski was doing. That precise, mirthless laughter rang in Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs ears long after he had left the house. When he reached home Prince AndrГ©y began thinking of his life in Petersburg during those last four months as if it were something new. He recalled his exertions and solicitations, and the history of his project of army reform, which had been accepted for consideration and which they were trying to pass over in silence simply because another, a very poor one, had already been prepared and submitted to the Emperor. He thought of the meetings of a committee of which Berg was a member. He remembered how carefully and at what length everything relating to form and procedure was discussed at those meetings, and how sedulously and promptly all that related to the gist of the business was evaded. He recalled his labors on the Legal Code, and how painstakingly he had translated the articles of the Roman and French codes into Russian, and he felt ashamed of himself. Then he vividly pictured to himself BoguchГЎrovo, his occupations in the country, his journey to RyazГЎn; he remembered the peasants and Dron the village elder, and mentally applying to them the Personal Rights he had divided into paragraphs, he felt astonished that he could have spent so much time on such useless work. XIX XIX Next day Prince AndrГ©y called at a few houses he had not visited before, and among them at the RostГіvsвҖҷ with whom he had renewed acquaintance at the ball. Apart from considerations of politeness which demanded the call, he wanted to see that original, eager girl who had left such a pleasant impression on his mind, in her own home. NatГЎsha was one of the first to meet him. She was wearing a dark-blue house dress in which Prince AndrГ©y thought her even prettier than in her ball dress. She and all the RostГіv family welcomed him as an old friend, simply and cordially. The whole family, whom he had formerly judged severely, now seemed to him to consist of excellent, simple, and kindly people. The old countвҖҷs hospitality and good nature, which struck one especially in Petersburg as a pleasant surprise, were such that Prince AndrГ©y could not refuse to stay to dinner. вҖңYes,вҖқ he thought, вҖңthey are capital people, who of course have not the slightest idea what a treasure they possess in NatГЎsha; but they are kindly folk and form the best possible setting for this strikingly poetic, charming girl, overflowing with life!вҖқ In NatГЎsha Prince AndrГ©y was conscious of a strange world completely alien to him and brimful of joys unknown to him, a different world, that in the OtrГЎdnoe avenue and at the window that moonlight night had already begun to disconcert him. Now this world disconcerted him no longer and was no longer alien to him, but he himself having entered it found in it a new enjoyment. After dinner NatГЎsha, at Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs request, went to the clavichord and began singing. Prince AndrГ©y stood by a window talking to the ladies and listened to her. In the midst of a phrase he ceased speaking and suddenly felt tears choking him, a thing he had thought impossible for him. He looked at NatГЎsha as she sang, and something new and joyful stirred in his soul. He felt happy and at the same time sad. He had absolutely nothing to weep about yet he was ready to weep. What about? His former love? The little princess? His disillusionments?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ His hopes for the future?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Yes and no. The chief reason was a sudden, vivid sense of the terrible contrast between something infinitely great and illimitable within him and that limited and material something that he, and even she, was. This contrast weighed on and yet cheered him while she sang. As soon as NatГЎsha had finished she went up to him and asked how he liked her voice. She asked this and then became confused, feeling that she ought not to have asked it. He smiled, looking at her, and said he liked her singing as he liked everything she did. Prince AndrГ©y left the RostГіvsвҖҷ late in the evening. He went to bed from habit, but soon realized that he could not sleep. Having lit his candle he sat up in bed, then got up, then lay down again not at all troubled by his sleeplessness: his soul was as fresh and joyful as if he had stepped out of a stuffy room into GodвҖҷs own fresh air. It did not enter his head that he was in love with NatГЎsha; he was not thinking about her, but only picturing her to himself, and in consequence all life appeared in a new light. вҖңWhy do I strive, why do I toil in this narrow, confined frame, when life, all life with all its joys, is open to me?вҖқ said he to himself. And for the first time for a very long while he began making happy plans for the future. He decided that he must attend to his sonвҖҷs education by finding a tutor and putting the boy in his charge, then he ought to retire from the service and go abroad, and see England, Switzerland and Italy. вҖңI must use my freedom while I feel so much strength and youth in me,вҖқ he said to himself. вҖңPierre was right when he said one must believe in the possibility of happiness in order to be happy, and now I do believe in it. Let the dead bury their dead, but while one has life one must live and be happy!вҖқ thought he. II II Prince AndrГ©y had to see the Marshal of the Nobility for the district in connection with the affairs of the RyazГЎn estate of which he was trustee. This Marshal was Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich RostГіv, and in the middle of May Prince AndrГ©y went to visit him. It was now hot spring weather. The whole forest was already clothed in green. It was dusty and so hot that on passing near water one longed to bathe. Prince AndrГ©y, depressed and preoccupied with the business about which he had to speak to the Marshal, was driving up the avenue in the grounds of the RostГіvsвҖҷ house at OtrГЎdnoe. He heard merry girlish cries behind some trees on the right and saw a group of girls running to cross the path of his calГЁche. Ahead of the rest and nearer to him ran a dark-haired, remarkably slim, pretty girl in a yellow chintz dress, with a white handkerchief on her head from under which loose locks of hair escaped. The girl was shouting something but, seeing that he was a stranger, ran back laughing without looking at him. Suddenly, he did not know why, he felt a pang. The day was so beautiful, the sun so bright, everything around so gay, but that slim pretty girl did not know, or wish to know, of his existence and was contented and cheerful in her own separateвҒ вҖ”probably foolishвҒ вҖ”but bright and happy life. вҖңWhat is she so glad about? What is she thinking of? Not of the military regulations or of the arrangement of the RyazГЎn serfsвҖҷ quitrents. Of what is she thinking? Why is she so happy?вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y asked himself with instinctive curiosity. In 1809 Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich was living at OtrГЎdnoe just as he had done in former years, that is, entertaining almost the whole province with hunts, theatricals, dinners, and music. He was glad to see Prince AndrГ©y, as he was to see any new visitor, and insisted on his staying the night. During the dull day, in the course of which he was entertained by his elderly hosts and by the more important of the visitors (the old countвҖҷs house was crowded on account of an approaching name day), Prince AndrГ©y repeatedly glanced at NatГЎsha, gay and laughing among the younger members of the company, and asked himself each time, вҖңWhat is she thinking about? Why is she so glad?вҖқ That night, alone in new surroundings, he was long unable to sleep. He read awhile and then put out his candle, but relit it. It was hot in the room, the inside shutters of which were closed. He was cross with the stupid old man (as he called RostГіv), who had made him stay by assuring him that some necessary documents had not yet arrived from town, and he was vexed with himself for having stayed. He got up and went to the window to open it. As soon as he opened the shutters the moonlight, as if it had long been watching for this, burst into the room. He opened the casement. The night was fresh, bright, and very still. Just before the window was a row of pollard trees, looking black on one side and with a silvery light on the other. Beneath the trees grew some kind of lush, wet, bushy vegetation with silver-lit leaves and stems here and there. Farther back beyond the dark trees a roof glittered with dew, to the right was a leafy tree with brilliantly white trunk and branches, and above it shone the moon, nearly at its full, in a pale, almost starless, spring sky. Prince AndrГ©y leaned his elbows on the window ledge and his eyes rested on that sky. His room was on the first floor. Those in the rooms above were also awake. He heard female voices overhead. вҖңJust once more,вҖқ said a girlish voice above him which Prince AndrГ©y recognized at once. вҖңBut when are you coming to bed?вҖқ replied another voice. вҖңI wonвҖҷt, I canвҖҷt sleep, whatвҖҷs the use? Come now for the last time.вҖқ Two girlish voices sang a musical passageвҒ вҖ”the end of some song. вҖңOh, how lovely! Now go to sleep, and thereвҖҷs an end of it.вҖқ вҖңYou go to sleep, but I canвҖҷt,вҖқ said the first voice, coming nearer to the window. She was evidently leaning right out, for the rustle of her dress and even her breathing could be heard. Everything was stone-still, like the moon and its light and the shadows. Prince AndrГ©y, too, dared not stir, for fear of betraying his unintentional presence. вҖңSГіnya! SГіnya!вҖқ he again heard the first speaker. вҖңOh, how can you sleep? Only look how glorious it is! Ah, how glorious! Do wake up, SГіnya!вҖқ she said almost with tears in her voice. вҖңThere never, never was such a lovely night before!вҖқ SГіnya made some reluctant reply. вҖңDo just come and see what a moon!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Oh, how lovely! Come here.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Darling, sweetheart, come here! There, you see? I feel like sitting down on my heels, putting my arms round my knees like this, straining tight, as tight as possible, and flying away! Like this.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңTake care, youвҖҷll fall out.вҖқ He heard the sound of a scuffle and SГіnyaвҖҷs disapproving voice: вҖңItвҖҷs past one oвҖҷclock.вҖқ вҖңOh, you only spoil things for me. All right, go, go!вҖқ Again all was silent, but Prince AndrГ©y knew she was still sitting there. From time to time he heard a soft rustle and at times a sigh. вҖңO God, O God! What does it mean?вҖқ she suddenly exclaimed. вҖңTo bed then, if it must be!вҖқ and she slammed the casement. вҖңFor her I might as well not exist!вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y while he listened to her voice, for some reason expecting yet fearing that she might say something about him. вҖңThere she is again! As if it were on purpose,вҖқ thought he. In his soul there suddenly arose such an unexpected turmoil of youthful thoughts and hopes, contrary to the whole tenor of his life, that unable to explain his condition to himself he lay down and fell asleep at once. XX XX One morning Colonel Berg, whom Pierre knew as he knew everybody in Moscow and Petersburg, came to see him. Berg arrived in an immaculate brand-new uniform, with his hair pomaded and brushed forward over his temples as the Emperor Alexander wore his hair. вҖңI have just been to see the countess, your wife. Unfortunately she could not grant my request, but I hope, Count, I shall be more fortunate with you,вҖқ he said with a smile. вҖңWhat is it you wish, Colonel? I am at your service.вҖқ вҖңI have now quite settled in my new rooms, CountвҖқ (Berg said this with perfect conviction that this information could not but be agreeable), вҖңand so I wish to arrange just a small party for my own and my wifeвҖҷs friends.вҖқ (He smiled still more pleasantly.) вҖңI wished to ask the countess and you to do me the honor of coming to tea and to supper.вҖқ Only Countess ElГЁna VasГӯlievna, considering the society of such people as the Bergs beneath her, could be cruel enough to refuse such an invitation. Berg explained so clearly why he wanted to collect at his house a small but select company, and why this would give him pleasure, and why though he grudged spending money on cards or anything harmful, he was prepared to run into some expense for the sake of good societyвҒ вҖ”that Pierre could not refuse, and promised to come. вҖңBut donвҖҷt be late, Count, if I may venture to ask; about ten minutes to eight, please. We shall make up a rubber. Our general is coming. He is very good to me. We shall have supper, Count. So you will do me the favor.вҖқ Contrary to his habit of being late, Pierre on that day arrived at the BergsвҖҷ house, not at ten but at fifteen minutes to eight. Having prepared everything necessary for the party, the Bergs were ready for their guestsвҖҷ arrival. In their new, clean, and light study with its small busts and pictures and new furniture sat Berg and his wife. Berg, closely buttoned up in his new uniform, sat beside his wife explaining to her that one always could and should be acquainted with people above one, because only then does one get satisfaction from acquaintances. вҖңYou can get to know something, you can ask for something. See how I managed from my first promotion.вҖқ (Berg measured his life not by years but by promotions.) вҖңMy comrades are still nobodies, while I am only waiting for a vacancy to command a regiment, and have the happiness to be your husband.вҖқ (He rose and kissed VГ©raвҖҷs hand, and on the way to her straightened out a turned-up corner of the carpet.) вҖңAnd how have I obtained all this? Chiefly by knowing how to choose my aquaintances. It goes without saying that one must be conscientious and methodical.вҖқ Berg smiled with a sense of his superiority over a weak woman, and paused, reflecting that this dear wife of his was after all but a weak woman who could not understand all that constitutes a manвҖҷs dignity, what it was ein Mann zu sein.67 VГ©ra at the same time smiling with a sense of superiority over her good, conscientious husband, who all the same understood life wrongly, as according to VГ©ra all men did. Berg, judging by his wife, thought all women weak and foolish. VГ©ra, judging only by her husband and generalizing from that observation, supposed that all men, though they understand nothing and are conceited and selfish, ascribe common sense to themselves alone. Berg rose and embraced his wife carefully, so as not to crush her lace fichu for which he had paid a good price, kissing her straight on the lips. вҖңThe only thing is, we mustnвҖҷt have children too soon,вҖқ he continued, following an unconscious sequence of ideas. вҖңYes,вҖқ answered VГ©ra, вҖңI donвҖҷt at all want that. We must live for society.вҖқ вҖңPrincess YusГәpova wore one exactly like this,вҖқ said Berg, pointing to the fichu with a happy and kindly smile. Just then Count BezГәkhov was announced. Husband and wife glanced at one another, both smiling with self-satisfaction, and each mentally claiming the honor of this visit. вҖңThis is what comes of knowing how to make acquaintances,вҖқ thought Berg. вҖңThis is what comes of knowing how to conduct oneself.вҖқ вҖңBut please donвҖҷt interrupt me when I am entertaining the guests,вҖқ said VГ©ra, вҖңbecause I know what interests each of them and what to say to different people.вҖқ Berg smiled again. вҖңIt canвҖҷt be helped: men must sometimes have masculine conversation,вҖқ said he. They received Pierre in their small, new drawing room, where it was impossible to sit down anywhere without disturbing its symmetry, neatness, and order; so it was quite comprehensible and not strange that Berg, having generously offered to disturb the symmetry of an armchair or of the sofa for his dear guest, but being apparently painfully undecided on the matter himself, eventually left the visitor to settle the question of selection. Pierre disturbed the symmetry by moving a chair for himself, and Berg and VГ©ra immediately began their evening party, interrupting each other in their efforts to entertain their guest. VГ©ra, having decided in her own mind that Pierre ought to be entertained with conversation about the French embassy, at once began accordingly. Berg, having decided that masculine conversation was required, interrupted his wifeвҖҷs remarks and touched on the question of the war with Austria, and unconsciously jumped from the general subject to personal considerations as to the proposals made him to take part in the Austrian campaign and the reasons why he had declined them. Though the conversation was very incoherent and VГ©ra was angry at the intrusion of the masculine element, both husband and wife felt with satisfaction that, even if only one guest was present, their evening had begun very well and was as like as two peas to every other evening party with its talk, tea, and lighted candles. Before long BorГӯs, BergвҖҷs old comrade, arrived. There was a shade of condescension and patronage in his treatment of Berg and VГ©ra. After BorГӯs came a lady with the colonel, then the general himself, then the RostГіvs, and the party became unquestionably exactly like all other evening parties. Berg and VГ©ra could not repress their smiles of satisfaction at the sight of all this movement in their drawing room, at the sound of the disconnected talk, the rustling of dresses, and the bowing and scraping. Everything was just as everybody always has it, especially so the general, who admired the apartment, patted Berg on the shoulder, and with parental authority superintended the setting out of the table for boston. The general sat down by Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich, who was next to himself the most important guest. The old people sat with the old, the young with the young, and the hostess at the tea table, on which stood exactly the same kind of cakes in a silver cake basket as the Panins had at their party. Everything was just as it was everywhere else. XXI XXI Pierre, as one of the principal guests, had to sit down to boston with Count RostГіv, the general, and the colonel. At the card table he happened to be directly facing NatГЎsha, and was struck by a curious change that had come over her since the ball. She was silent, and not only less pretty than at the ball, but only redeemed from plainness by her look of gentle indifference to everything around. вҖңWhatвҖҷs the matter with her?вҖқ thought Pierre, glancing at her. She was sitting by her sister at the tea table, and reluctantly, without looking at him, made some reply to BorГӯs who sat down beside her. After playing out a whole suit and to his partnerвҖҷs delight taking five tricks, Pierre, hearing greetings and the steps of someone who had entered the room while he was picking up his tricks, glanced again at NatГЎsha. вҖңWhat has happened to her?вҖқ he asked himself with still greater surprise. Prince AndrГ©y was standing before her, saying something to her with a look of tender solicitude. She, having raised her head, was looking up at him, flushed and evidently trying to master her rapid breathing. And the bright glow of some inner fire that had been suppressed was again alight in her. She was completely transformed and from a plain girl had again become what she had been at the ball. Prince AndrГ©y went up to Pierre, and the latter noticed a new and youthful expression in his friendвҖҷs face. Pierre changed places several times during the game, sitting now with his back to NatГЎsha and now facing her, but during the whole of the six rubbers he watched her and his friend. вҖңSomething very important is happening between them,вҖқ thought Pierre, and a feeling that was both joyful and painful agitated him and made him neglect the game.
