After six rubbers the general got up, saying that it was no use playing like that, and Pierre was released. NatГЎsha on one side was talking with SГіnya and BorГӯs, and VГ©ra with a subtle smile was saying something to Prince AndrГ©y. Pierre went up to his friend and, asking whether they were talking secrets, sat down beside them. VГ©ra, having noticed Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs attentions to NatГЎsha, decided that at a party, a real evening party, subtle allusions to the tender passion were absolutely necessary and, seizing a moment when Prince AndrГ©y was alone, began a conversation with him about feelings in general and about her sister. With so intellectual a guest as she considered Prince AndrГ©y to be, she felt that she had to employ her diplomatic tact. When Pierre went up to them he noticed that VГ©ra was being carried away by her self-satisfied talk, but that Prince AndrГ©y seemed embarrassed, a thing that rarely happened with him. вҖңWhat do you think?вҖқ VГ©ra was saying with an arch smile. вҖңYou are so discerning, Prince, and understand peopleвҖҷs characters so well at a glance. What do you think of NatГЎli? Could she be constant in her attachments? Could she, like other womenвҖқ (VГ©ra meant herself), вҖңlove a man once for all and remain true to him forever? That is what I consider true love. What do you think, Prince?вҖқ вҖңI know your sister too little,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y, with a sarcastic smile under which he wished to hide his embarrassment, вҖңto be able to solve so delicate a question, and then I have noticed that the less attractive a woman is the more constant she is likely to be,вҖқ he added, and looked up at Pierre who was just approaching them. вҖңYes, that is true, Prince. In our days,вҖқ continued VГ©raвҒ вҖ”mentioning вҖңour daysвҖқ as people of limited intelligence are fond of doing, imagining that they have discovered and appraised the peculiarities of вҖңour daysвҖқ and that human characteristics change with the timesвҒ вҖ”вҖңin our days a girl has so much freedom that the pleasure of being courted often stifles real feeling in her. And it must be confessed that Nathalie is very susceptible.вҖқ This return to the subject of NatГЎli caused Prince AndrГ©y to knit his brows with discomfort: he was about to rise, but VГ©ra continued with a still more subtle smile: вҖңI think no one has been more courted than she,вҖқ she went on, вҖңbut till quite lately she never cared seriously for anyone. Now you know, Count,вҖқ she said to Pierre, вҖңeven our dear cousin BorГӯs, who, between ourselves, was very far gone in the land of tendernessвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ (alluding to a map of love much in vogue at that time). Prince AndrГ©y frowned and remained silent. вҖңYou are friendly with BorГӯs, arenвҖҷt you?вҖқ asked VГ©ra. вҖңYes, I know him.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI expect he has told you of his childish love for NatГЎsha?вҖқ вҖңOh, there was childish love?вҖқ suddenly asked Prince AndrГ©y, blushing unexpectedly. вҖңYes, you know between cousins intimacy often leads to love. Le cousinage est un dangereux voisinage.68 DonвҖҷt you think so?вҖқ вҖңOh, undoubtedly!вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, and with sudden and unnatural liveliness he began chaffing Pierre about the need to be very careful with his fifty-year-old Moscow cousins, and in the midst of these jesting remarks he rose, taking Pierre by the arm, and drew him aside. вҖңWell?вҖқ asked Pierre, seeing his friendвҖҷs strange animation with surprise, and noticing the glance he turned on NatГЎsha as he rose. вҖңI mustвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I must have a talk with you,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңYou know that pair of womenвҖҷs gloves?вҖқ (He referred to the Masonic gloves given to a newly initiated Brother to present to the woman he loved.) вҖңIвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but no, I will talk to you later on,вҖқ and with a strange light in his eyes and restlessness in his movements, Prince AndrГ©y approached NatГЎsha and sat down beside her. Pierre saw how Prince AndrГ©y asked her something and how she flushed as she replied. But at that moment Berg came to Pierre and began insisting that he should take part in an argument between the general and the colonel on the affairs in Spain. Berg was satisfied and happy. The smile of pleasure never left his face. The party was very successful and quite like other parties he had seen. Everything was similar: the ladiesвҖҷ subtle talk, the cards, the general raising his voice at the card table, and the samovar and the tea cakes; only one thing was lacking that he had always seen at the evening parties he wished to imitate. They had not yet had a loud conversation among the men and a dispute about something important and clever. Now the general had begun such a discussion and so Berg drew Pierre to it. XXII XXII Next day, having been invited by the count, Prince AndrГ©y dined with the RostГіvs and spent the rest of the day there. Everyone in the house realized for whose sake Prince AndrГ©y came, and without concealing it he tried to be with NatГЎsha all day. Not only in the soul of the frightened yet happy and enraptured NatГЎsha, but in the whole house, there was a feeling of awe at something important that was bound to happen. The countess looked with sad and sternly serious eyes at Prince AndrГ©y when he talked to NatГЎsha and timidly started some artificial conversation about trifles as soon as he looked her way. SГіnya was afraid to leave NatГЎsha and afraid of being in the way when she was with them. NatГЎsha grew pale, in a panic of expectation, when she remained alone with him for a moment. Prince AndrГ©y surprised her by his timidity. She felt that he wanted to say something to her but could not bring himself to do so. In the evening, when Prince AndrГ©y had left, the countess went up to NatГЎsha and whispered: вҖңWell, what?вҖқ вҖңMamma! For heavenвҖҷs sake donвҖҷt ask me anything now! One canвҖҷt talk about that,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. But all the same that night NatГЎsha, now agitated and now frightened, lay a long time in her motherвҖҷs bed gazing straight before her. She told her how he had complimented her, how he told her he was going abroad, asked her where they were going to spend the summer, and then how he had asked her about BorГӯs. вҖңBut such aвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ such aвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ never happened to me before!вҖқ she said. вҖңOnly I feel afraid in his presence. I am always afraid when IвҖҷm with him. What does that mean? Does it mean that itвҖҷs the real thing? Yes? Mamma, are you asleep?вҖқ вҖңNo, my love; I am frightened myself,вҖқ answered her mother. вҖңNow go!вҖқ вҖңAll the same I shanвҖҷt sleep. What silliness, to sleep! Mummy! Mummy! such a thing never happened to me before,вҖқ she said, surprised and alarmed at the feeling she was aware of in herself. вҖңAnd could we ever have thought!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ It seemed to NatГЎsha that even at the time she first saw Prince AndrГ©y at OtrГЎdnoe she had fallen in love with him. It was as if she feared this strange, unexpected happiness of meeting again the very man she had then chosen (she was firmly convinced she had done so) and of finding him, as it seemed, not indifferent to her. вҖңAnd it had to happen that he should come specially to Petersburg while we are here. And it had to happen that we should meet at that ball. It is fate. Clearly it is fate that everything led up to this! Already then, directly I saw him I felt something peculiar.вҖқ вҖңWhat else did he say to you? What are those verses? Read themвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said her mother, thoughtfully, referring to some verses Prince AndrГ©y had written in NatГЎshaвҖҷs album. вҖңMamma, one need not be ashamed of his being a widower?вҖқ вҖңDonвҖҷt, NatГЎsha! Pray to God. вҖҳMarriages are made in heaven,вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ said her mother. вҖңDarling Mummy, how I love you! How happy I am!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha, shedding tears of joy and excitement and embracing her mother. At that very time Prince AndrГ©y was sitting with Pierre and telling him of his love for NatГЎsha and his firm resolve to make her his wife. That day Countess ElГЁna VasГӯlievna had a reception at her house. The French ambassador was there, and a foreign prince of the blood who had of late become a frequent visitor of hers, and many brilliant ladies and gentlemen. Pierre, who had come downstairs, walked through the rooms and struck everyone by his preoccupied, absentminded, and morose air. Since the ball he had felt the approach of a fit of nervous depression and had made desperate efforts to combat it. Since the intimacy of his wife with the royal prince, Pierre had unexpectedly been made a gentleman of the bedchamber, and from that time he had begun to feel oppressed and ashamed in court society, and dark thoughts of the vanity of all things human came to him oftener than before. At the same time the feeling he had noticed between his protГ©gГ©e NatГЎsha and Prince AndrГ©y accentuated his gloom by the contrast between his own position and his friendвҖҷs. He tried equally to avoid thinking about his wife, and about NatГЎsha and Prince AndrГ©y; and again everything seemed to him insignificant in comparison with eternity; again the question: for what? presented itself; and he forced himself to work day and night at Masonic labors, hoping to drive away the evil spirit that threatened him. Toward midnight, after he had left the countessвҖҷ apartments, he was sitting upstairs in a shabby dressing gown, copying out the original transaction of the Scottish lodge of Freemasons at a table in his low room cloudy with tobacco smoke, when someone came in. It was Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңAh, itвҖҷs you!вҖқ said Pierre with a preoccupied, dissatisfied air. вҖңAnd I, you see, am hard at it.вҖқ He pointed to his manuscript book with that air of escaping from the ills of life with which unhappy people look at their work. Prince AndrГ©y, with a beaming, ecstatic expression of renewed life on his face, paused in front of Pierre and, not noticing his sad look, smiled at him with the egotism of joy. вҖңWell, dear heart,вҖқ said he, вҖңI wanted to tell you about it yesterday and I have come to do so today. I never experienced anything like it before. I am in love, my friend!вҖқ Suddenly Pierre heaved a deep sigh and dumped his heavy person down on the sofa beside Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңWith NatГЎsha RostГіva, yes?вҖқ said he. вҖңYes, yes! Who else should it be? I should never have believed it, but the feeling is stronger than I. Yesterday I tormented myself and suffered, but I would not exchange even that torment for anything in the world, I have not lived till now. At last I live, but I canвҖҷt live without her! But can she love me?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I am too old for her.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Why donвҖҷt you speak?вҖқ вҖңI? I? What did I tell you?вҖқ said Pierre suddenly, rising and beginning to pace up and down the room. вҖңI always thought it.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ That girl is such a treasureвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ she is a rare girl.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ My dear friend, I entreat you, donвҖҷt philosophize, donвҖҷt doubt, marry, marry, marry.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And I am sure there will not be a happier man than you.вҖқ вҖңBut what of her?вҖқ вҖңShe loves you.вҖқ вҖңDonвҖҷt talk rubbishвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, smiling and looking into PierreвҖҷs eyes. вҖңShe does, I know,вҖқ Pierre cried fiercely. вҖңBut do listen,вҖқ returned Prince AndrГ©y, holding him by the arm. вҖңDo you know the condition I am in? I must talk about it to someone.вҖқ вҖңWell, go on, go on. I am very glad,вҖқ said Pierre, and his face really changed, his brow became smooth, and he listened gladly to Prince AndrГ©y. Prince AndrГ©y seemed, and really was, quite a different, quite a new man. Where was his spleen, his contempt for life, his disillusionment? Pierre was the only person to whom he made up his mind to speak openly; and to him he told all that was in his soul. Now he boldly and lightly made plans for an extended future, said he could not sacrifice his own happiness to his fatherвҖҷs caprice, and spoke of how he would either make his father consent to this marriage and love her, or would do without his consent; then he marveled at the feeling that had mastered him as at something strange, apart from and independent of himself. вҖңI should not have believed anyone who told me that I was capable of such love,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңIt is not at all the same feeling that I knew in the past. The whole world is now for me divided into two halves: one half is she, and there all is joy, hope, light: the other half is everything where she is not, and there is all gloom and darkness.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңDarkness and gloom,вҖқ reiterated Pierre: вҖңyes, yes, I understand that.вҖқ вҖңI cannot help loving the light, it is not my fault. And I am very happy! You understand me? I know you are glad for my sake.вҖқ вҖңYes, yes,вҖқ Pierre assented, looking at his friend with a touched and sad expression in his eyes. The brighter Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs lot appeared to him, the gloomier seemed his own. XXIII XXIII Prince AndrГ©y needed his fatherвҖҷs consent to his marriage, and to obtain this he started for the country next day. His father received his sonвҖҷs communication with external composure, but inward wrath. He could not comprehend how anyone could wish to alter his life or introduce anything new into it, when his own life was already ending. вҖңIf only they would let me end my days as I want to,вҖқ thought the old man, вҖңthen they might do as they please.вҖқ With his son, however, he employed the diplomacy he reserved for important occasions and, adopting a quiet tone, discussed the whole matter. In the first place the marriage was not a brilliant one as regards birth, wealth, or rank. Secondly, Prince AndrГ©y was no longer as young as he had been and his health was poor (the old man laid special stress on this), while she was very young. Thirdly, he had a son whom it would be a pity to entrust to a chit of a girl. вҖңFourthly and finally,вҖқ the father said, looking ironically at his son, вҖңI beg you to put it off for a year: go abroad, take a cure, look out as you wanted to for a German tutor for Prince NikolГЎy. Then if your love or passion or obstinacyвҒ вҖ”as you pleaseвҒ вҖ”is still as great, marry! And thatвҖҷs my last word on it. Mind, the lastвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ concluded the prince, in a tone which showed that nothing would make him alter his decision. Prince AndrГ©y saw clearly that the old man hoped that his feelings, or his fiancГ©eвҖҷs, would not stand a yearвҖҷs test, or that he (the old prince himself) would die before then, and he decided to conform to his fatherвҖҷs wishвҒ вҖ”to propose, and postpone the wedding for a year. Three weeks after the last evening he had spent with the RostГіvs, Prince AndrГ©y returned to Petersburg. Next day after her talk with her mother NatГЎsha expected BolkГіnski all day, but he did not come. On the second and third day it was the same. Pierre did not come either and NatГЎsha, not knowing that Prince AndrГ©y had gone to see his father, could not explain his absence to herself. Three weeks passed in this way. NatГЎsha had no desire to go out anywhere and wandered from room to room like a shadow, idle and listless; she wept secretly at night and did not go to her mother in the evenings. She blushed continually and was irritable. It seemed to her that everybody knew about her disappointment and was laughing at her and pitying her. Strong as was her inward grief, this wound to her vanity intensified her misery. Once she came to her mother, tried to say something, and suddenly began to cry. Her tears were those of an offended child who does not know why it is being punished. The countess began to soothe NatГЎsha, who after first listening to her motherвҖҷs words, suddenly interrupted her: вҖңLeave off, Mamma! I donвҖҷt think, and donвҖҷt want to think about it! He just came and then left off, left off.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Her voice trembled, and she again nearly cried, but recovered and went on quietly: вҖңAnd I donвҖҷt at all want to get married. And I am afraid of him; I have now become quite calm, quite calm.вҖқ The day after this conversation NatГЎsha put on the old dress which she knew had the peculiar property of conducing to cheerfulness in the mornings, and that day she returned to the old way of life which she had abandoned since the ball. Having finished her morning tea she went to the ballroom, which she particularly liked for its loud resonance, and began singing her solfeggio. When she had finished her first exercise she stood still in the middle of the room and sang a musical phrase that particularly pleased her. She listened joyfully (as though she had not expected it) to the charm of the notes reverberating, filling the whole empty ballroom, and slowly dying away; and all at once she felt cheerful. вҖңWhatвҖҷs the good of making so much of it? Things are nice as it is,вҖқ she said to herself, and she began walking up and down the room, not stepping simply on the resounding parquet but treading with each step from the heel to the toe (she had on a new and favorite pair of shoes) and listening to the regular tap of the heel and creak of the toe as gladly as she had to the sounds of her own voice. Passing a mirror she glanced into it. вҖңThere, thatвҖҷs me!вҖқ the expression of her face seemed to say as she caught sight of herself. вҖңWell, and very nice too! I need nobody.вҖқ A footman wanted to come in to clear away something in the room but she would not let him, and having closed the door behind him continued her walk. That morning she had returned to her favorite moodвҒ вҖ”love of, and delight in, herself. вҖңHow charming that NatГЎsha is!вҖқ she said again, speaking as some third, collective, male person. вҖңPretty, a good voice, young, and in nobodyвҖҷs way if only they leave her in peace.вҖқ But however much they left her in peace she could not now be at peace, and immediately felt this. In the hall the porch door opened, and someone asked, вҖңAt home?вҖқ and then footsteps were heard. NatГЎsha was looking at the mirror, but did not see herself. She listened to the sounds in the hall. When she saw herself, her face was pale. It was he. She knew this for certain, though she hardly heard his voice through the closed doors. Pale and agitated, NatГЎsha ran into the drawing room. вҖңMamma! BolkГіnski has come!вҖқ she said. вҖңMamma, it is awful, it is unbearable! I donвҖҷt wantвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ to be tormented? What am I to do?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Before the countess could answer, Prince AndrГ©y entered the room with an agitated and serious face. As soon as he saw NatГЎsha his face brightened. He kissed the countessвҖҷ hand and NatГЎshaвҖҷs, and sat down beside the sofa. вҖңIt is long since we had the pleasureвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ began the countess, but Prince AndrГ©y interrupted her by answering her intended question, obviously in haste to say what he had to. вҖңI have not been to see you all this time because I have been at my fatherвҖҷs. I had to talk over a very important matter with him. I only got back last night,вҖқ he said glancing at NatГЎsha; вҖңI want to have a talk with you, Countess,вҖқ he added after a momentвҖҷs pause. The countess lowered her eyes, sighing deeply. вҖңI am at your disposal,вҖқ she murmured. NatГЎsha knew that she ought to go away, but was unable to do so: something gripped her throat, and regardless of manners she stared straight at Prince AndrГ©y with wide-open eyes. вҖңAt once? This instant!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No, it canвҖҷt be!вҖқ she thought. Again he glanced at her, and that glance convinced her that she was not mistaken. Yes, at once, that very instant, her fate would be decided. вҖңGo, NatГЎsha! I will call you,вҖқ said the countess in a whisper. NatГЎsha glanced with frightened imploring eyes at Prince AndrГ©y and at her mother and went out. вҖңI have come, Countess, to ask for your daughterвҖҷs hand,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. The countessвҖҷ face flushed hotly, but she said nothing. вҖңYour offerвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she began at last sedately. He remained silent, looking into her eyes. вҖңYour offerвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ (she grew confused) вҖңis agreeable to us, and I accept your offer. I am glad. And my husbandвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I hopeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but it will depend on her.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI will speak to her when I have your consent.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Do you give it to me?вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңYes,вҖқ replied the countess. She held out her hand to him, and with a mixed feeling of estrangement and tenderness pressed her lips to his forehead as he stooped to kiss her hand. She wished to love him as a son, but felt that to her he was a stranger and a terrifying man. вҖңI am sure my husband will consent,вҖқ said the countess, вҖңbut your fatherвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңMy father, to whom I have told my plans, has made it an express condition of his consent that the wedding is not to take place for a year. And I wished to tell you of that,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңIt is true that NatГЎsha is still young, butвҒ вҖ”so long as that?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңIt is unavoidable,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y with a sigh. вҖңI will send her to you,вҖқ said the countess, and left the room. вҖңLord have mercy upon us!вҖқ she repeated while seeking her daughter. SГіnya said that NatГЎsha was in her bedroom. NatГЎsha was sitting on the bed, pale and dry-eyed, and was gazing at the icons and whispering something as she rapidly crossed herself. Seeing her mother she jumped up and flew to her. вҖңWell, Mamma?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңGo, go to him. He is asking for your hand,вҖқ said the countess, coldly it seemed to NatГЎsha. вҖңGoвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ go,вҖқ said the mother, sadly and reproachfully, with a deep sigh, as her daughter ran away. NatГЎsha never remembered how she entered the drawing room. When she came in and saw him she paused. вҖңIs it possible that this stranger has now become everything to me?вҖқ she asked herself, and immediately answered, вҖңYes, everything! He alone is now dearer to me than everything in the world.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y came up to her with downcast eyes. вҖңI have loved you from the very first moment I saw you. May I hope?вҖқ He looked at her and was struck by the serious impassioned expression of her face. Her face said: вҖңWhy ask? Why doubt what you cannot but know? Why speak, when words cannot express what one feels?вҖқ She drew near to him and stopped. He took her hand and kissed it. вҖңDo you love me?вҖқ вҖңYes, yes!вҖқ NatГЎsha murmured as if in vexation. Then she sighed loudly and, catching her breath more and more quickly, began to sob. вҖңWhat is it? WhatвҖҷs the matter?вҖқ вҖңOh, I am so happy!вҖқ she replied, smiled through her tears, bent over closer to him, paused for an instant as if asking herself whether she might, and then kissed him. Prince AndrГ©y held her hands, looked into her eyes, and did not find in his heart his former love for her. Something in him had suddenly changed; there was no longer the former poetic and mystic charm of desire, but there was pity for her feminine and childish weakness, fear at her devotion and trustfulness, and an oppressive yet joyful sense of the duty that now bound him to her forever. The present feeling, though not so bright and poetic as the former, was stronger and more serious. вҖңDid your mother tell you that it cannot be for a year?вҖқ asked Prince AndrГ©y, still looking into her eyes. вҖңIs it possible that IвҒ вҖ”the вҖҳchit of a girl,вҖҷ as everybody called me,вҖқ thought NatГЎshaвҒ вҖ”вҖңis it possible that I am now to be the wife and the equal of this strange, dear, clever man whom even my father looks up to? Can it be true? Can it be true that there can be no more playing with life, that now I am grown up, that on me now lies a responsibility for my every word and deed? Yes, but what did he ask me?вҖқ вҖңNo,вҖқ she replied, but she had not understood his question. вҖңForgive me!вҖқ he said. вҖңBut you are so young, and I have already been through so much in life. I am afraid for you, you do not yet know yourself.вҖқ NatГЎsha listened with concentrated attention, trying but failing to take in the meaning of his words. вҖңHard as this year which delays my happiness will be,вҖқ continued Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңit will give you time to be sure of yourself. I ask you to make me happy in a year, but you are free: our engagement shall remain a secret, and should you find that you do not love me, or should you come to loveвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y with an unnatural smile. вҖңWhy do you say that?вҖқ NatГЎsha interrupted him. вҖңYou know that from the very day you first came to OtrГЎdnoe I have loved you,вҖқ she cried, quite convinced that she spoke the truth. вҖңIn a year you will learn to know yourself.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңA whole year!вҖқ NatГЎsha repeated suddenly, only now realizing that the marriage was to be postponed for a year. вҖңBut why a year? Why a year?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Prince AndrГ©y began to explain to her the reasons for this delay. NatГЎsha did not hear him. вҖңAnd canвҖҷt it be helped?вҖқ she asked. Prince AndrГ©y did not reply, but his face expressed the impossibility of altering that decision. вҖңItвҖҷs awful! Oh, itвҖҷs awful! awful!вҖқ NatГЎsha suddenly cried, and again burst into sobs. вҖңI shall die, waiting a year: itвҖҷs impossible, itвҖҷs awful!вҖқ She looked into her loverвҖҷs face and saw in it a look of commiseration and perplexity. вҖңNo, no! IвҖҷll do anything!вҖқ she said, suddenly checking her tears. вҖңI am so happy.вҖқ The father and mother came into the room and gave the betrothed couple their blessing. From that day Prince AndrГ©y began to frequent the RostГіvsвҖҷ as NatГЎshaвҖҷs affianced lover. XXIV XXIV No betrothal ceremony took place and NatГЎshaвҖҷs engagement to BolkГіnski was not announced; Prince AndrГ©y insisted on that. He said that as he was responsible for the delay he ought to bear the whole burden of it; that he had given his word and bound himself forever, but that he did not wish to bind NatГЎsha and gave her perfect freedom. If after six months she felt that she did not love him she would have full right to reject him. Naturally neither NatГЎsha nor her parents wished to hear of this, but Prince AndrГ©y was firm. He came every day to the RostГіvsвҖҷ, but did not behave to NatГЎsha as an affianced lover: he did not use the familiar thou, but said you to her, and kissed only her hand. After their engagement, quite different, intimate, and natural relations sprang up between them. It was as if they had not known each other till now. Both liked to recall how they had regarded each other when as yet they were nothing to one another; they felt themselves now quite different beings: then they were artificial, now natural and sincere. At first the family felt some constraint in intercourse with Prince AndrГ©y; he seemed a man from another world, and for a long time NatГЎsha trained the family to get used to him, proudly assuring them all that he only appeared to be different, but was really just like all of them, and that she was not afraid of him and no one else ought to be. After a few days they grew accustomed to him, and without restraint in his presence pursued their usual way of life, in which he took his part. He could talk about rural economy with the count, fashions with the countess and NatГЎsha, and about albums and fancywork with SГіnya. Sometimes the household both among themselves and in his presence expressed their wonder at how it had all happened, and at the evident omens there had been of it: Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs coming to OtrГЎdnoe and their coming to Petersburg, and the likeness between NatГЎsha and Prince AndrГ©y which her nurse had noticed on his first visit, and AndrГ©yвҖҷs encounter with NikolГЎy in 1805, and many other incidents betokening that it had to be. In the house that poetic dullness and quiet reigned which always accompanies the presence of a betrothed couple. Often when all sitting together everyone kept silent. Sometimes the others would get up and go away and the couple, left alone, still remained silent. They rarely spoke of their future life. Prince AndrГ©y was afraid and ashamed to speak of it. NatГЎsha shared this as she did all his feelings, which she constantly divined. Once she began questioning him about his son. Prince AndrГ©y blushed, as he often did nowвҒ вҖ”NatГЎsha particularly liked it in himвҒ вҖ”and said that his son would not live with them. вҖңWhy not?вҖқ asked NatГЎsha in a frightened tone. вҖңI cannot take him away from his grandfather, and besidesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңHow I should have loved him!вҖқ said NatГЎsha, immediately guessing his thought; вҖңbut I know you wish to avoid any pretext for finding fault with us.вҖқ Sometimes the old count would come up, kiss Prince AndrГ©y, and ask his advice about PГ©tyaвҖҷs education or NikolГЎyвҖҷs service. The old countess sighed as she looked at them; SГіnya was always getting frightened lest she should be in the way and tried to find excuses for leaving them alone, even when they did not wish it. When Prince AndrГ©y spoke (he could tell a story very well), NatГЎsha listened to him with pride; when she spoke she noticed with fear and joy that he gazed attentively and scrutinizingly at her. She asked herself in perplexity: вҖңWhat does he look for in me? He is trying to discover something by looking at me! What if what he seeks in me is not there?вҖқ Sometimes she fell into one of the mad, merry moods characteristic of her, and then she particularly loved to hear and see how Prince AndrГ©y laughed. He seldom laughed, but when he did he abandoned himself entirely to his laughter, and after such a laugh she always felt nearer to him. NatГЎsha would have been completely happy if the thought of the separation awaiting her and drawing near had not terrified her, just as the mere thought of it made him turn pale and cold. On the eve of his departure from Petersburg Prince AndrГ©y brought with him Pierre, who had not been to the RostГіvsвҖҷ once since the ball. Pierre seemed disconcerted and embarrassed. He was talking to the countess, and NatГЎsha sat down beside a little chess table with SГіnya, thereby inviting Prince AndrГ©y to come too. He did so. вҖңYou have known BezГәkhov a long time?вҖқ he asked. вҖңDo you like him?вҖқ вҖңYes, heвҖҷs a dear, but very absurd.вҖқ And as usual when speaking of Pierre, she began to tell anecdotes of his absentmindedness, some of which had even been invented about him. вҖңDo you know I have entrusted him with our secret? I have known him from childhood. He has a heart of gold. I beg you, NatГЎli,вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y said with sudden seriousnessвҒ вҖ”вҖңI am going away and heaven knows what may happen. You may cease toвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ all right, I know I am not to say that. Only this, then: whatever may happen to you when I am not hereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhat can happen?вҖқ вҖңWhatever trouble may come,вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y continued, вҖңI beg you, Mademoiselle Sophie, whatever may happen, to turn to him alone for advice and help! He is a most absentminded and absurd fellow, but he has a heart of gold.вҖқ Neither her father, nor her mother, nor SГіnya, nor Prince AndrГ©y himself could have foreseen how the separation from her lover would act on NatГЎsha. Flushed and agitated she went about the house all that day, dry-eyed, occupied with most trivial matters as if not understanding what awaited her. She did not even cry when, on taking leave, he kissed her hand for the last time. вҖңDonвҖҷt go!вҖқ she said in a tone that made him wonder whether he really ought not to stay and which he remembered long afterwards. Nor did she cry when he was gone; but for several days she sat in her room dry-eyed, taking no interest in anything and only saying now and then, вҖңOh, why did he go away?вҖқ But a fortnight after his departure, to the surprise of those around her, she recovered from her mental sickness just as suddenly and became her old self again, but with a change in her moral physiognomy, as a child gets up after a long illness with a changed expression of face. XXV XXV During that year after his sonвҖҷs departure, Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich BolkГіnskiвҖҷs health and temper became much worse. He grew still more irritable, and it was Princess MГЎrya who generally bore the brunt of his frequent fits of unprovoked anger. He seemed carefully to seek out her tender spots so as to torture her mentally as harshly as possible. Princess MГЎrya had two passions and consequently two joysвҒ вҖ”her nephew, NikolГәshka, and religionвҒ вҖ”and these were the favorite subjects of the princeвҖҷs attacks and ridicule. Whatever was spoken of he would bring round to the superstitiousness of old maids, or the petting and spoiling of children. вҖңYou want to make himвҖқвҒ вҖ”NikГіlenkaвҒ вҖ”вҖңinto an old maid like yourself! A pity! Prince AndrГ©y wants a son and not an old maid,вҖқ he would say. Or, turning to Mademoiselle Bourienne, he would ask her in Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs presence how she liked our village priests and icons and would joke about them. He continually hurt Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs feelings and tormented her, but it cost her no effort to forgive him. Could he be to blame toward her, or could her father, whom she knew loved her in spite of it all, be unjust? And what is justice? The princess never thought of that proud word вҖңjustice.вҖқ All the complex laws of man centered for her in one clear and simple lawвҒ вҖ”the law of love and self-sacrifice taught us by Him who lovingly suffered for mankind though He Himself was God. What had she to do with the justice or injustice of other people? She had to endure and love, and that she did. During the winter Prince AndrГ©y had come to Bald Hills and had been gay, gentle, and more affectionate than Princess MГЎrya had known him for a long time past. She felt that something had happened to him, but he said nothing to her about his love. Before he left he had a long talk with his father about something, and Princess MГЎrya noticed that before his departure they were dissatisfied with one another. Soon after Prince AndrГ©y had gone, Princess MГЎrya wrote to her friend Julie KarГЎgina in Petersburg, whom she had dreamed (as all girls dream) of marrying to her brother, and who was at that time in mourning for her own brother, killed in Turkey. Sorrow, it seems, is our common lot, my dear, tender friend Julie. Your loss is so terrible that I can only explain it to myself as a special providence of God who, loving you, wishes to try you and your excellent mother. Oh, my friend! Religion, and religion alone, canвҒ вҖ”I will not say comfort usвҒ вҖ”but save us from despair. Religion alone can explain to us what without its help man cannot comprehend: why, for what cause, kind and noble beings able to find happiness in lifeвҒ вҖ”not merely harming no one but necessary to the happiness of othersвҒ вҖ”are called away to God, while cruel, useless, harmful persons, or such as are a burden to themselves and to others, are left living. The first death I saw, and one I shall never forgetвҒ вҖ”that of my dear sister-in-lawвҒ вҖ”left that impression on me. Just as you ask destiny why your splendid brother had to die, so I asked why that angel Liza, who not only never wronged anyone, but in whose soul there were never any unkind thoughts, had to die. And what do you think, dear friend? Five years have passed since then, and already I, with my petty understanding, begin to see clearly why she had to die, and in what way that death was but an expression of the infinite goodness of the Creator, whose every action, though generally incomprehensible to us, is but a manifestation of His infinite love for His creatures. Perhaps, I often think, she was too angelically innocent to have the strength to perform all a motherвҖҷs duties. As a young wife she was irreproachable; perhaps she could not have been so as a mother. As it is, not only has she left us, and particularly Prince AndrГ©y, with the purest regrets and memories, but probably she will there receive a place I dare not hope for myself. But not to speak of her alone, that early and terrible death has had the most beneficent influence on me and on my brother in spite of all our grief. Then, at the moment of our loss, these thoughts could not occur to me; I should then have dismissed them with horror, but now they are very clear and certain. I write all this to you, dear friend, only to convince you of the Gospel truth which has become for me a principle of life: not a single hair of our heads will fall without His will. And His will is governed only by infinite love for us, and so whatever befalls us is for our good. You ask whether we shall spend next winter in Moscow. In spite of my wish to see you, I do not think so and do not want to do so. You will be surprised to hear that the reason for this is Buonaparte! The case is this: my fatherвҖҷs health is growing noticeably worse, he cannot stand any contradiction and is becoming irritable. This irritability is, as you know, chiefly directed to political questions. He cannot endure the notion that Buonaparte is negotiating on equal terms with all the sovereigns of Europe and particularly with our own, the grandson of the Great Catherine! As you know, I am quite indifferent to politics, but from my fatherвҖҷs remarks and his talks with MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich I know all that goes on in the world and especially about the honors conferred on Buonaparte, who only at Bald Hills in the whole world, it seems, is not accepted as a great man, still less as Emperor of France. And my father cannot stand this. It seems to me that it is chiefly because of his political views that my father is reluctant to speak of going to Moscow; for he foresees the encounters that would result from his way of expressing his views regardless of anybody. All the benefit he might derive from a course of treatment he would lose as a result of the disputes about Buonaparte which would be inevitable. In any case it will be decided very shortly. Our family life goes on in the old way except for my brother AndrГ©yвҖҷs absence. He, as I wrote you before, has changed very much of late. After his sorrow he only this year quite recovered his spirits. He has again become as I used to know him when a child: kind, affectionate, with that heart of gold to which I know no equal. He has realized, it seems to me, that life is not over for him. But together with this mental change he has grown physically much weaker. He has become thinner and more nervous. I am anxious about him and glad he is taking this trip abroad which the doctors recommended long ago. I hope it will cure him. You write that in Petersburg he is spoken of as one of the most active, cultivated, and capable of the young men. Forgive my vanity as a relation, but I never doubted it. The good he has done to everybody here, from his peasants up to the gentry, is incalculable. On his arrival in Petersburg he received only his due. I always wonder at the way rumors fly from Petersburg to Moscow, especially such false ones as that you write aboutвҒ вҖ”I mean the report of my brotherвҖҷs betrothal to the little RostГіva. I do not think my brother will ever marry again, and certainly not her; and this is why: first, I know that though he rarely speaks about the wife he has lost, the grief of that loss has gone too deep in his heart for him ever to decide to give her a successor and our little angel a stepmother. Secondly because, as far as I know, that girl is not the kind of girl who could please Prince AndrГ©y. I do not think he would choose her for a wife, and frankly I do not wish it. But I am running on too long and am at the end of my second sheet. Goodbye, my dear friend. May God keep you in His holy and mighty care. My dear friend, Mademoiselle Bourienne, sends you kisses. MГЎrya XXVI XXVI In the middle of the summer Princess MГЎrya received an unexpected letter from Prince AndrГ©y in Switzerland in which he gave her strange and surprising news. He informed her of his engagement to NatГЎsha RostГіva. The whole letter breathed loving rapture for his betrothed and tender and confiding affection for his sister. He wrote that he had never loved as he did now and that only now did he understand and know what life was. He asked his sister to forgive him for not having told her of his resolve when he had last visited Bald Hills, though he had spoken of it to his father. He had not done so for fear Princess MГЎrya should ask her father to give his consent, irritating him and having to bear the brunt of his displeasure without attaining her object. вҖңBesides,вҖқ he wrote, вҖңthe matter was not then so definitely settled as it is now. My father then insisted on a delay of a year and now already six months, half of that period, have passed, and my resolution is firmer than ever. If the doctors did not keep me here at the spas I should be back in Russia, but as it is I have to postpone my return for three months. You know me and my relations with Father. I want nothing from him. I have been and always shall be independent; but to go against his will and arouse his anger, now that he may perhaps remain with us such a short time, would destroy half my happiness. I am now writing to him about the same question, and beg you to choose a good moment to hand him the letter and to let me know how he looks at the whole matter and whether there is hope that he may consent to reduce the term by four months.вҖқ After long hesitations, doubts, and prayers, Princess MГЎrya gave the letter to her father. The next day the old prince said to her quietly: вҖңWrite and tell your brother to wait till I am dead.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ It wonвҖҷt be longвҒ вҖ”I shall soon set him free.вҖқ The princess was about to reply, but her father would not let her speak and, raising his voice more and more, cried: вҖңMarry, marry, my boy!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ A good family!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Clever people, eh? Rich, eh? Yes, a nice stepmother NikolГәshka will have! Write and tell him that he may marry tomorrow if he likes. She will be NikolГәshkaвҖҷs stepmother and IвҖҷll marry Bourienne!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Ha, ha, ha! He mustnвҖҷt be without a stepmother either! Only one thing, no more women are wanted in my houseвҒ вҖ”let him marry and live by himself. Perhaps you will go and live with him too?вҖқ he added, turning to Princess MГЎrya. вҖңGo in heavenвҖҷs name! Go out into the frostвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the frostвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the frost!вҖқ After this outburst the prince did not speak any more about the matter. But repressed vexation at his sonвҖҷs poor-spirited behavior found expression in his treatment of his daughter. To his former pretexts for irony a fresh one was now addedвҒ вҖ”allusions to stepmothers and amiabilities to Mademoiselle Bourienne. вҖңWhy shouldnвҖҷt I marry her?вҖқ he asked his daughter. вҖңSheвҖҷll make a splendid princess!вҖқ And latterly, to her surprise and bewilderment, Princess MГЎrya noticed that her father was really associating more and more with the Frenchwoman. She wrote to Prince AndrГ©y about the reception of his letter, but comforted him with hopes of reconciling their father to the idea. NikolГәshka and his education, her brother AndrГ©, and religion were Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs joys and consolations; but besides that, since everyone must have personal hopes, Princess MГЎrya in the profoundest depths of her heart had a hidden dream and hope that supplied the chief consolation of her life. This comforting dream and hope were given her by GodвҖҷs folkвҒ вҖ”the half-witted and other pilgrims who visited her without the princeвҖҷs knowledge. The longer she lived, the more experience and observation she had of life, the greater was her wonder at the shortsightedness of men who seek enjoyment and happiness here on earth: toiling, suffering, struggling, and harming one another, to obtain that impossible, visionary, sinful happiness. Prince AndrГ©y had loved his wife, she died, but that was not enough: he wanted to bind his happiness to another woman. Her father objected to this because he wanted a more distinguished and wealthier match for AndrГ©y. And they all struggled and suffered and tormented one another and injured their souls, their eternal souls, for the attainment of benefits which endure but for an instant. Not only do we know this ourselves, but Christ, the Son of God, came down to earth and told us that this life is but for a moment and is a probation; yet we cling to it and think to find happiness in it. вҖңHow is it that no one realizes this?вҖқ thought Princess MГЎrya. вҖңNo one except these despised GodвҖҷs folk who, wallet on back, come to me by the back door, afraid of being seen by the prince, not for fear of ill-usage by him but for fear of causing him to sin. To leave family, home, and all the cares of worldly welfare, in order without clinging to anything to wander in hempen rags from place to place under an assumed name, doing no one any harm but praying for allвҒ вҖ”for those who drive one away as well as for those who protect one: higher than that life and truth there is no life or truth!вҖқ There was one pilgrim, a quiet pockmarked little woman of fifty called FГ«dosyushka, who for over thirty years had gone about barefoot and worn heavy chains. Princess MГЎrya was particularly fond of her. Once, when in a room with a lamp dimly lit before the icon FГ«dosyushka was talking of her life, the thought that FГ«dosyushka alone had found the true path of life suddenly came to Princess MГЎrya with such force that she resolved to become a pilgrim herself. When FГ«dosyushka had gone to sleep Princess MГЎrya thought about this for a long time, and at last made up her mind that, strange as it might seem, she must go on a pilgrimage. She disclosed this thought to no one but to her confessor, Father AkГӯnfi, the monk, and he approved of her intention. Under guise of a present for the pilgrims, Princess MГЎrya prepared a pilgrimвҖҷs complete costume for herself: a coarse smock, bast shoes, a rough coat, and a black kerchief. Often, approaching the chest of drawers containing this secret treasure, Princess MГЎrya paused, uncertain whether the time had not already come to put her project into execution. Often, listening to the pilgrimsвҖҷ tales, she was so stimulated by their simple speech, mechanical to them but to her so full of deep meaning, that several times she was on the point of abandoning everything and running away from home. In imagination she already pictured herself by FГ«dosyushkaвҖҷs side, dressed in coarse rags, walking with a staff, a wallet on her back, along the dusty road, directing her wanderings from one saintвҖҷs shrine to another, free from envy, earthly love, or desire, and reaching at last the place where there is no more sorrow or sighing, but eternal joy and bliss. вҖңI shall come to a place and pray there, and before having time to get used to it or getting to love it, I shall go farther. I will go on till my legs fail, and IвҖҷll lie down and die somewhere, and shall at last reach that eternal, quiet haven, where there is neither sorrow nor sighingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ thought Princess MГЎrya. But afterwards, when she saw her father and especially little Koko (NikolГәshka), her resolve weakened. She wept quietly, and felt that she was a sinner who loved her father and little nephew more than God. III III Next morning, having taken leave of no one but the count, and not waiting for the ladies to appear, Prince AndrГ©y set off for home. It was already the beginning of June when on his return journey he drove into the birch forest where the gnarled old oak had made so strange and memorable an impression on him. In the forest the harness bells sounded yet more muffled than they had done six weeks before, for now all was thick, shady, and dense, and the young firs dotted about in the forest did not jar on the general beauty but, lending themselves to the mood around, were delicately green with fluffy young shoots. The whole day had been hot. Somewhere a storm was gathering, but only a small cloud had scattered some raindrops lightly, sprinkling the road and the sappy leaves. The left side of the forest was dark in the shade, the right side glittered in the sunlight, wet and shiny and scarcely swayed by the breeze. Everything was in blossom, the nightingales trilled, and their voices reverberated now near, now far away. вҖңYes, here in this forest was that oak with which I agreed,вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңBut where is it?вҖқ he again wondered, gazing at the left side of the road, and without recognizing it he looked with admiration at the very oak he sought. The old oak, quite transfigured, spreading out a canopy of sappy dark-green foliage, stood rapt and slightly trembling in the rays of the evening sun. Neither gnarled fingers nor old scars nor old doubts and sorrows were any of them in evidence now. Through the hard century-old bark, even where there were no twigs, leaves had sprouted such as one could hardly believe the old veteran could have produced. вҖңYes, it is the same oak,вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y, and all at once he was seized by an unreasoning springtime feeling of joy and renewal. All the best moments of his life suddenly rose to his memory. Austerlitz with the lofty heavens, his wifeвҖҷs dead reproachful face, Pierre at the ferry, that girl thrilled by the beauty of the night, and that night itself and the moon, andвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ all this rushed suddenly to his mind. вҖңNo, life is not over at thirty-one!вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y suddenly decided finally and decisively. вҖңIt is not enough for me to know what I have in meвҒ вҖ”everyone must know it: Pierre, and that young girl who wanted to fly away into the sky, everyone must know me, so that my life may not be lived for myself alone while others live so apart from it, but so that it may be reflected in them all, and they and I may live in harmony!вҖқ On reaching home Prince AndrГ©y decided to go to Petersburg that autumn and found all sorts of reasons for this decision. A whole series of sensible and logical considerations showing it to be essential for him to go to Petersburg, and even to reenter the service, kept springing up in his mind. He could not now understand how he could ever even have doubted the necessity of taking an active share in life, just as a month before he had not understood how the idea of leaving the quiet country could ever enter his head. It now seemed clear to him that all his experience of life must be senselessly wasted unless he applied it to some kind of work and again played an active part in life. He did not even remember how formerly, on the strength of similar wretched logical arguments, it had seemed obvious that he would be degrading himself if he now, after the lessons he had had in life, allowed himself to believe in the possibility of being useful and in the possibility of happiness or love. Now reason suggested quite the opposite. After that journey to RyazГЎn he found the country dull; his former pursuits no longer interested him, and often when sitting alone in his study he got up, went to the mirror, and gazed a long time at his own face. Then he would turn away to the portrait of his dead Liza, who with hair curled Г  la grecque looked tenderly and gaily at him out of the gilt frame. She did not now say those former terrible words to him, but looked simply, merrily, and inquisitively at him. And Prince AndrГ©y, crossing his arms behind him, long paced the room, now frowning, now smiling, as he reflected on those irrational, inexpressible thoughts, secret as a crime, which altered his whole life and were connected with Pierre, with fame, with the girl at the window, the oak, and womanвҖҷs beauty and love. And if anyone came into his room at such moments he was particularly cold, stern, and above all unpleasantly logical. вҖңMy dear,вҖқ Princess MГЎrya entering at such a moment would say, вҖңNikolГәshka canвҖҷt go out today, itвҖҷs very cold.вҖқ вҖңIf it were hot,вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y would reply at such times very dryly to his sister, вҖңhe could go out in his smock, but as it is cold he must wear warm clothes, which were designed for that purpose. That is what follows from the fact that it is cold; and not that a child who needs fresh air should remain at home,вҖқ he would add with extreme logic, as if punishing someone for those secret illogical emotions that stirred within him. At such moments Princess MГЎrya would think how intellectual work dries men up. IV IV Prince AndrГ©y arrived in Petersburg in August, 1809. It was the time when the youthful SperГЎnski was at the zenith of his fame and his reforms were being pushed forward with the greatest energy. That same August the Emperor was thrown from his calГЁche, injured his leg, and remained three weeks at Peterhof, receiving SperГЎnski every day and no one else. At that time the two famous decrees were being prepared that so agitated societyвҒ вҖ”abolishing court ranks and introducing examinations to qualify for the grades of Collegiate Assessor and State CouncilorвҒ вҖ”and not merely these but a whole state constitution, intended to change the existing order of government in Russia: legal, administrative, and financial, from the Council of State down to the district tribunals. Now those vague liberal dreams with which the Emperor Alexander had ascended the throne, and which he had tried to put into effect with the aid of his associates, CzartorГҪski, NovosГӯltsev, KochubГ©y, and StrГіgonovвҒ вҖ”whom he himself in jest had called his ComitГ© de salut publicвҒ вҖ”were taking shape and being realized. Now all these men were replaced by SperГЎnski on the civil side, and ArakchГ©ev on the military. Soon after his arrival Prince AndrГ©y, as a gentleman of the chamber, presented himself at court and at a levee. The Emperor, though he met him twice, did not favor him with a single word. It had always seemed to Prince AndrГ©y before that he was antipathetic to the Emperor and that the latter disliked his face and personality generally, and in the cold, repellent glance the Emperor gave him, he now found further confirmation of this surmise. The courtiers explained the EmperorвҖҷs neglect of him by His MajestyвҖҷs displeasure at BolkГіnskiвҖҷs not having served since 1805. вҖңI know myself that one cannot help oneвҖҷs sympathies and antipathies,вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңso it will not do to present my proposal for the reform of the army regulations to the Emperor personally, but the project will speak for itself.вҖқ He mentioned what he had written to an old field marshal, a friend of his fatherвҖҷs. The field marshal made an appointment to see him, received him graciously, and promised to inform the Emperor. A few days later Prince AndrГ©y received notice that he was to go to see the Minister of War, Count ArakchГ©ev. On the appointed day Prince AndrГ©y entered Count ArakchГ©evвҖҷs waiting room at nine in the morning. He did not know ArakchГ©ev personally, had never seen him, and all he had heard of him inspired him with but little respect for the man. вҖңHe is Minister of War, a man trusted by the Emperor, and I need not concern myself about his personal qualities: he has been commissioned to consider my project, so he alone can get it adopted,вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y as he waited among a number of important and unimportant people in Count ArakchГ©evвҖҷs waiting room. During his service, chiefly as an adjutant, Prince AndrГ©y had seen the anterooms of many important men, and the different types of such rooms were well known to him. Count ArakchГ©evвҖҷs anteroom had quite a special character. The faces of the unimportant people awaiting their turn for an audience showed embarrassment and servility; the faces of those of higher rank expressed a common feeling of awkwardness, covered by a mask of unconcern and ridicule of themselves, their situation, and the person for whom they were waiting. Some walked thoughtfully up and down, others whispered and laughed. Prince AndrГ©y heard the nickname вҖңSГӯla AndrГ©evichвҖқ and the words, вҖңUncle will give it to us hot,вҖқ in reference to Count ArakchГ©ev. One general (an important personage), evidently feeling offended at having to wait so long, sat crossing and uncrossing his legs and smiling contemptuously to himself. But the moment the door opened one feeling alone appeared on all facesвҒ вҖ”that of fear. Prince AndrГ©y for the second time asked the adjutant on duty to take in his name, but received an ironical look and was told that his turn would come in due course. After some others had been shown in and out of the ministerвҖҷs room by the adjutant on duty, an officer who struck Prince AndrГ©y by his humiliated and frightened air was admitted at that terrible door. This officerвҖҷs audience lasted a long time. Then suddenly the grating sound of a harsh voice was heard from the other side of the door, and the officerвҒ вҖ”with pale face and trembling lipsвҒ вҖ”came out and passed through the waiting room, clutching his head. After this Prince AndrГ©y was conducted to the door and the officer on duty said in a whisper, вҖңTo the right, at the window.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y entered a plain tidy room and saw at the table a man of forty with a long waist, a long closely cropped head, deep wrinkles, scowling brows above dull greenish-hazel eyes and an overhanging red nose. ArakchГ©ev turned his head toward him without looking at him. вҖңWhat is your petition?вҖқ asked ArakchГ©ev. вҖңI am not petitioning, your excellency,вҖқ returned Prince AndrГ©y quietly. ArakchГ©evвҖҷs eyes turned toward him. вҖңSit down,вҖқ said he. вҖңPrince BolkГіnski?вҖқ вҖңI am not petitioning about anything. His Majesty the Emperor has deigned to send your excellency a project submitted by meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYou see, my dear sir, I have read your project,вҖқ interrupted ArakchГ©ev, uttering only the first words amiably and thenвҒ вҖ”again without looking at Prince AndrГ©yвҒ вҖ”relapsing gradually into a tone of grumbling contempt. вҖңYou are proposing new military laws? There are many laws but no one to carry out the old ones. Nowadays everybody designs laws, it is easier writing than doing.вҖқ вҖңI came at His Majesty the EmperorвҖҷs wish to learn from your excellency how you propose to deal with the memorandum I have presented,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y politely. вҖңI have endorsed a resolution on your memorandum and sent it to the committee. I do not approve of it,вҖқ said ArakchГ©ev, rising and taking a paper from his writing table. вҖңHere!вҖқ and he handed it to Prince AndrГ©y. Across the paper was scrawled in pencil, without capital letters, misspelled, and without punctuation: вҖңUnsoundly constructed because resembles an imitation of the French military code and from the Articles of War needlessly deviating.вҖқ вҖңTo what committee has the memorandum been referred?вҖқ inquired Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңTo the Committee on Army Regulations, and I have recommended that your honor should be appointed a member, but without a salary.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y smiled. вҖңI donвҖҷt want one.вҖқ вҖңA member without salary,вҖқ repeated ArakchГ©ev. вҖңI have the honorвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Eh! Call the next one! Who else is there?вҖқ he shouted, bowing to Prince AndrГ©y. V V While waiting for the announcement of his appointment to the committee Prince AndrГ©y looked up his former acquaintances, particularly those he knew to be in power and whose aid he might need. In Petersburg he now experienced the same feeling he had had on the eve of a battle, when troubled by anxious curiosity and irresistibly attracted to the ruling circles where the future, on which the fate of millions depended, was being shaped. From the irritation of the older men, the curiosity of the uninitiated, the reserve of the initiated, the hurry and preoccupation of everyone, and the innumerable committees and commissions of whose existence he learned every day, he felt that now, in 1809, here in Petersburg a vast civil conflict was in preparation, the commander in chief of which was a mysterious person he did not know, but who was supposed to be a man of geniusвҒ вҖ”SperГЎnski. And this movement of reconstruction of which Prince AndrГ©y had a vague idea, and SperГЎnski its chief promoter, began to interest him so keenly that the question of the army regulations quickly receded to a secondary place in his consciousness. Prince AndrГ©y was most favorably placed to secure good reception in the highest and most diverse Petersburg circles of the day. The reforming party cordially welcomed and courted him, in the first place because he was reputed to be clever and very well read, and secondly because by liberating his serfs he had obtained the reputation of being a liberal. The party of the old and dissatisfied, who censured the innovations, turned to him expecting his sympathy in their disapproval of the reforms, simply because he was the son of his father. The feminine society world welcomed him gladly, because he was rich, distinguished, a good match, and almost a newcomer, with a halo of romance on account of his supposed death and the tragic loss of his wife. Besides this the general opinion of all who had known him previously was that he had greatly improved during these last five years, having softened and grown more manly, lost his former affectation, pride, and contemptuous irony, and acquired the serenity that comes with years. People talked about him, were interested in him, and wanted to meet him. The day after his interview with Count ArakchГ©ev, Prince AndrГ©y spent the evening at Count KochubГ©yвҖҷs. He told the count of his interview with SГӯla AndrГ©evich (KochubГ©y spoke of ArakchГ©ev by that nickname with the same vague irony Prince AndrГ©y had noticed in the Minister of WarвҖҷs anteroom). вҖңMon cher, even in this case you canвҖҷt do without MikhГЎil MikhГЎilovich. He manages everything. IвҖҷll speak to him. He has promised to come this evening.вҖқ вҖңWhat has SperГЎnski to do with the army regulations?вҖқ asked Prince AndrГ©y. KochubГ©y shook his head smilingly, as if surprised at BolkГіnskiвҖҷs simplicity. вҖңWe were talking to him about you a few days ago,вҖқ KochubГ©y continued, вҖңand about your freed plowmen.вҖқ вҖңOh, is it you, Prince, who have freed your serfs?вҖқ said an old man of CatherineвҖҷs day, turning contemptuously toward BolkГіnski. вҖңIt was a small estate that brought in no profit,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y, trying to extenuate his action so as not to irritate the old man uselessly. вҖңAfraid of being lateвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said the old man, looking at KochubГ©y. вҖңThereвҖҷs one thing I donвҖҷt understand,вҖқ he continued. вҖңWho will plow the land if they are set free? It is easy to write laws, but difficult to rule.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Just the same as nowвҒ вҖ”I ask you, CountвҒ вҖ”who will be heads of the departments when everybody has to pass examinations?вҖқ вҖңThose who pass the examinations, I suppose,вҖқ replied KochubГ©y, crossing his legs and glancing round. вҖңWell, I have PryГЎnichnikov serving under me, a splendid man, a priceless man, but heвҖҷs sixty. Is he to go up for examination?вҖқ вҖңYes, thatвҖҷs a difficulty, as education is not at all general, butвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Count KochubГ©y did not finish. He rose, took Prince AndrГ©y by the arm, and went to meet a tall, bald, fair man of about forty with a large open forehead and a long face of unusual and peculiar whiteness, who was just entering. The newcomer wore a blue swallowtail coat with a cross suspended from his neck and a star on his left breast. It was SperГЎnski. Prince AndrГ©y recognized him at once, and felt a throb within him, as happens at critical moments of life. Whether it was from respect, envy, or anticipation, he did not know. SperГЎnskiвҖҷs whole figure was of a peculiar type that made him easily recognizable. In the society in which Prince AndrГ©y lived he had never seen anyone who together with awkward and clumsy gestures possessed such calmness and self-assurance; he had never seen so resolute yet gentle an expression as that in those half-closed, rather humid eyes, or so firm a smile that expressed nothing; nor had he heard such a refined, smooth, soft voice; above all he had never seen such delicate whiteness of face or handsвҒ вҖ”hands which were broad, but very plump, soft, and white. Such whiteness and softness Prince AndrГ©y had only seen on the faces of soldiers who had been long in hospital. This was SperГЎnski, Secretary of State, reporter to the Emperor and his companion at Erfurt, where he had more than once met and talked with Napoleon. SperГЎnski did not shift his eyes from one face to another as people involuntarily do on entering a large company and was in no hurry to speak. He spoke slowly, with assurance that he would be listened to, and he looked only at the person with whom he was conversing. Prince AndrГ©y followed SperГЎnskiвҖҷs every word and movement with particular attention. As happens to some people, especially to men who judge those near to them severely, he always on meeting anyone newвҒ вҖ”especially anyone whom, like SperГЎnski, he knew by reputationвҒ вҖ”expected to discover in him the perfection of human qualities. SperГЎnski told KochubГ©y he was sorry he had been unable to come sooner as he had been detained at the palace. He did not say that the Emperor had kept him, and Prince AndrГ©y noticed this affectation of modesty. When KochubГ©y introduced Prince AndrГ©y, SperГЎnski slowly turned his eyes to BolkГіnski with his customary smile and looked at him in silence. вҖңI am very glad to make your acquaintance. I had heard of you, as everyone has,вҖқ he said after a pause. KochubГ©y said a few words about the reception ArakchГ©ev had given BolkГіnski. SperГЎnski smiled more markedly. вҖңThe chairman of the Committee on Army Regulations is my good friend Monsieur MagnГӯtski,вҖқ he said, fully articulating every word and syllable, вҖңand if you like I can put you in touch with him.вҖқ He paused at the full stop. вҖңI hope you will find him sympathetic and ready to cooperate in promoting all that is reasonable.вҖқ A circle soon formed round SperГЎnski, and the old man who had talked about his subordinate PryГЎnichnikov addressed a question to him. Prince AndrГ©y without joining in the conversation watched every movement of SperГЎnskiвҖҷs: this man, not long since an insignificant divinity student, who now, BolkГіnski thought, held in his handsвҒ вҖ”those plump white handsвҒ вҖ”the fate of Russia. Prince AndrГ©y was struck by the extraordinarily disdainful composure with which SperГЎnski answered the old man. He appeared to address condescending words to him from an immeasurable height. When the old man began to speak too loud, SperГЎnski smiled and said he could not judge of the advantage or disadvantage of what pleased the sovereign. Having talked for a little while in the general circle, SperГЎnski rose and coming up to Prince AndrГ©y took him along to the other end of the room. It was clear that he thought it necessary to interest himself in BolkГіnski. вҖңI had no chance to talk with you, Prince, during the animated conversation in which that venerable gentleman involved me,вҖқ he said with a mildly contemptuous smile, as if intimating by that smile that he and Prince AndrГ©y understood the insignificance of the people with whom he had just been talking. This flattered Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңI have known of you for a long time: first from your action with regard to your serfs, a first example, of which it is very desirable that there should be more imitators; and secondly because you are one of those gentlemen of the chamber who have not considered themselves offended by the new decree concerning the ranks allotted to courtiers, which is causing so much gossip and tittle-tattle.вҖқ вҖңNo,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңmy father did not wish me to take advantage of the privilege. I began the service from the lower grade.вҖқ вҖңYour father, a man of the last century, evidently stands above our contemporaries who so condemn this measure which merely reestablishes natural justice.вҖқ вҖңI think, however, that these condemnations have some ground,вҖқ returned Prince AndrГ©y, trying to resist SperГЎnskiвҖҷs influence, of which he began to be conscious. He did not like to agree with him in everything and felt a wish to contradict. Though he usually spoke easily and well, he felt a difficulty in expressing himself now while talking with SperГЎnski. He was too much absorbed in observing the famous manвҖҷs personality. вҖңGrounds of personal ambition maybe,вҖқ SperГЎnski put in quietly. вҖңAnd of state interest to some extent,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңWhat do you mean?вҖқ asked SperГЎnski quietly, lowering his eyes. вҖңI am an admirer of Montesquieu,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңand his idea that le principe des monarchies est lвҖҷhonneur me paraГ®t incontestable. Certains droits et privilГЁges de la noblesse me paraissent ГӘtre des moyens de soutenir ce sentiment.вҖқ59 The smile vanished from SperГЎnskiвҖҷs white face, which was much improved by the change. Probably Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs thought interested him. вҖңSi vous envisagez la question sous ce point de vue,вҖқ60 he began, pronouncing French with evident difficulty, and speaking even slower than in Russian but quite calmly. SperГЎnski went on to say that honor, lвҖҷhonneur, cannot be upheld by privileges harmful to the service; that honor, lвҖҷhonneur, is either a negative concept of not doing what is blameworthy or it is a source of emulation in pursuit of commendation and rewards, which recognize it. His arguments were concise, simple, and clear. вҖңAn institution upholding honor, the source of emulation, is one similar to the LГ©gion dвҖҷhonneur of the great Emperor Napoleon, not harmful but helpful to the success of the service, but not a class or court privilege.вҖқ вҖңI do not dispute that, but it cannot be denied that court privileges have attained the same end,вҖқ returned Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңEvery courtier considers himself bound to maintain his position worthily.вҖқ вҖңYet you do not care to avail yourself of the privilege, Prince,вҖқ said SperГЎnski, indicating by a smile that he wished to finish amiably an argument which was embarrassing for his companion. вҖңIf you will do me the honor of calling on me on Wednesday,вҖқ he added, вҖңI will, after talking with MagnГӯtski, let you know what may interest you, and shall also have the pleasure of a more detailed chat with you.вҖқ Closing his eyes, he bowed Г  la franГ§aise, without taking leave, and trying to attract as little attention as possible, he left the room. VI VI During the first weeks of his stay in Petersburg Prince AndrГ©y felt the whole trend of thought he had formed during his life of seclusion quite overshadowed by the trifling cares that engrossed him in that city. On returning home in the evening he would jot down in his notebook four or five necessary calls or appointments for certain hours. The mechanism of life, the arrangement of the day so as to be in time everywhere, absorbed the greater part of his vital energy. He did nothing, did not even think or find time to think, but only talked, and talked successfully, of what he had thought while in the country. He sometimes noticed with dissatisfaction that he repeated the same remark on the same day in different circles. But he was so busy for whole days together that he had no time to notice that he was thinking of nothing. As he had done on their first meeting at KochubГ©yвҖҷs, SperГЎnski produced a strong impression on Prince AndrГ©y on the Wednesday, when he received him tГӘte-Г -tГӘte at his own house and talked to him long and confidentially. To BolkГіnski so many people appeared contemptible and insignificant creatures, and he so longed to find in someone the living ideal of that perfection toward which he strove, that he readily believed that in SperГЎnski he had found this ideal of a perfectly rational and virtuous man. Had SperГЎnski sprung from the same class as himself and possessed the same breeding and traditions, BolkГіnski would soon have discovered his weak, human, unheroic sides; but as it was, SperГЎnskiвҖҷs strange and logical turn of mind inspired him with respect all the more because he did not quite understand him. Moreover, SperГЎnski, either because he appreciated the otherвҖҷs capacity or because he considered it necessary to win him to his side, showed off his dispassionate calm reasonableness before Prince AndrГ©y and flattered him with that subtle flattery which goes hand in hand with self-assurance and consists in a tacit assumption that oneвҖҷs companion is the only man besides oneself capable of understanding the folly of the rest of mankind and the reasonableness and profundity of oneвҖҷs own ideas. During their long conversation on Wednesday evening, SperГЎnski more than once remarked: вҖңWe regard everything that is above the common level of rooted customвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ or, with a smile: вҖңBut we want the wolves to be fed and the sheep to be safeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ or: вҖңThey cannot understand thisвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and all in a way that seemed to say: вҖңWe, you and I, understand what they are and who we are.вҖқ This first long conversation with SperГЎnski only strengthened in Prince AndrГ©y the feeling he had experienced toward him at their first meeting. He saw in him a remarkable, clear-thinking man of vast intellect who by his energy and persistence had attained power, which he was using solely for the welfare of Russia. In Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs eyes SperГЎnski was the man he would himself have wished to beвҒ вҖ”one who explained all the facts of life reasonably, considered important only what was rational, and was capable of applying the standard of reason to everything. Everything seemed so simple and clear in SperГЎnskiвҖҷs exposition that Prince AndrГ©y involuntarily agreed with him about everything. If he replied and argued, it was only because he wished to maintain his independence and not submit to SperГЎnskiвҖҷs opinions entirely. Everything was right and everything was as it should be: only one thing disconcerted Prince AndrГ©y. This was SperГЎnskiвҖҷs cold, mirrorlike look, which did not allow one to penetrate to his soul, and his delicate white hands, which Prince AndrГ©y involuntarily watched as one does watch the hands of those who possess power. This mirrorlike gaze and those delicate hands irritated Prince AndrГ©y, he knew not why. He was unpleasantly struck, too, by the excessive contempt for others that he observed in SperГЎnski, and by the diversity of lines of argument he used to support his opinions. He made use of every kind of mental device, except analogy, and passed too boldly, it seemed to Prince AndrГ©y, from one to another. Now he would take up the position of a practical man and condemn dreamers; now that of a satirist, and laugh ironically at his opponents; now grow severely logical, or suddenly rise to the realm of metaphysics. (This last resource was one he very frequently employed.) He would transfer a question to metaphysical heights, pass on to definitions of space, time, and thought, and, having deduced the refutation he needed, would again descend to the level of the original discussion. In general the trait of SperГЎnskiвҖҷs mentality which struck Prince AndrГ©y most was his absolute and unshakable belief in the power and authority of reason. It was evident that the thought could never occur to him which to Prince AndrГ©y seemed so natural, namely, that it is after all impossible to express all one thinks; and that he had never felt the doubt, вҖңIs not all I think and believe nonsense?вҖқ And it was just this peculiarity of SperГЎnskiвҖҷs mind that particularly attracted Prince AndrГ©y. During the first period of their acquaintance BolkГіnski felt a passionate admiration for him similar to that which he had once felt for Bonaparte. The fact that SperГЎnski was the son of a village priest, and that stupid people might meanly despise him on account of his humble origin (as in fact many did), caused Prince AndrГ©y to cherish his sentiment for him the more, and unconsciously to strengthen it. On that first evening BolkГіnski spent with him, having mentioned the Commission for the Revision of the Code of Laws, SperГЎnski told him sarcastically that the Commission had existed for a hundred and fifty years, had cost millions, and had done nothing except that Rosenkampf had stuck labels on the corresponding paragraphs of the different codes. вҖңAnd that is all the state has for the millions it has spent,вҖқ said he. вҖңWe want to give the Senate new juridical powers, but we have no laws. That is why it is a sin for men like you, Prince, not to serve in these times!вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y said that for that work an education in jurisprudence was needed which he did not possess. вҖңBut nobody possesses it, so what would you have? It is a vicious circle from which we must break a way out.вҖқ A week later Prince AndrГ©y was a member of the Committee on Army Regulations andвҒ вҖ”what he had not at all expectedвҒ вҖ”was chairman of a section of the committee for the revision of the laws. At SperГЎnskiвҖҷs request he took the first part of the Civil Code that was being drawn up and, with the aid of the Code NapolГ©on and the Institutes of Justinian, he worked at formulating the section on Personal Rights. VII VII Nearly two years before this, in 1808, Pierre on returning to Petersburg after visiting his estates had involuntarily found himself in a leading position among the Petersburg Freemasons. He arranged dining and funeral lodge meetings, enrolled new members, and busied himself uniting various lodges and acquiring authentic charters. He gave money for the erection of temples and supplemented as far as he could the collection of alms, in regard to which the majority of members were stingy and irregular. He supported almost singlehanded a poorhouse the order had founded in Petersburg. His life meanwhile continued as before, with the same infatuations and dissipations. He liked to dine and drink well, and though he considered it immoral and humiliating could not resist the temptations of the bachelor circles in which he moved. Amid the turmoil of his activities and distractions, however, Pierre at the end of a year began to feel that the more firmly he tried to rest upon it, the more Masonic ground on which he stood gave way under him. At the same time he felt that the deeper the ground sank under him the closer bound he involuntarily became to the order. When he had joined the Freemasons he had experienced the feeling of one who confidently steps onto the smooth surface of a bog. When he put his foot down it sank in. To make quite sure of the firmness of the ground, he put his other foot down and sank deeper still, became stuck in it, and involuntarily waded knee-deep in the bog. Osip AlexГ©evich was not in PetersburgвҒ вҖ”he had of late stood aside from the affairs of the Petersburg lodges, and lived almost entirely in Moscow. All the members of the lodges were men Pierre knew in ordinary life, and it was difficult for him to regard them merely as Brothers in Freemasonry and not as Prince B. or IvГЎn VasГӯlevich D., whom he knew in society mostly as weak and insignificant men. Under the Masonic aprons and insignia he saw the uniforms and decorations at which they aimed in ordinary life. Often after collecting alms, and reckoning up twenty to thirty rubles received for the most part in promises from a dozen members, of whom half were as well able to pay as himself, Pierre remembered the Masonic vow in which each Brother promised to devote all his belongings to his neighbor, and doubts on which he tried not to dwell arose in his soul.
