He divided the Brothers he knew into four categories. In the first he put those who did not take an active part in the affairs of the lodges or in human affairs, but were exclusively occupied with the mystical science of the order: with questions of the threefold designation of God, the three primordial elementsвҒ вҖ”sulphur, mercury, and saltвҒ вҖ”or the meaning of the square and all the various figures of the temple of Solomon. Pierre respected this class of Brothers to which the elder ones chiefly belonged, including, Pierre thought, Osip AlexГ©evich himself, but he did not share their interests. His heart was not in the mystical aspect of Freemasonry. In the second category Pierre reckoned himself and others like him, seeking and vacillating, who had not yet found in Freemasonry a straight and comprehensible path, but hoped to do so. In the third category he included those Brothers (the majority) who saw nothing in Freemasonry but the external forms and ceremonies, and prized the strict performance of these forms without troubling about their purport or significance. Such were Willarski and even the Grand Master of the principal lodge. Finally, to the fourth category also a great many Brothers belonged, particularly those who had lately joined. These according to PierreвҖҷs observations were men who had no belief in anything, nor desire for anything, but joined the Freemasons merely to associate with the wealthy young Brothers who were influential through their connections or rank, and of whom there were very many in the lodge. Pierre began to feel dissatisfied with what he was doing. Freemasonry, at any rate as he saw it here, sometimes seemed to him based merely on externals. He did not think of doubting Freemasonry itself, but suspected that Russian Masonry had taken a wrong path and deviated from its original principles. And so toward the end of the year he went abroad to be initiated into the higher secrets of the order. In the summer of 1809 Pierre returned to Petersburg. Our Freemasons knew from correspondence with those abroad that BezГәkhov had obtained the confidence of many highly placed persons, had been initiated into many mysteries, had been raised to a higher grade, and was bringing back with him much that might conduce to the advantage of the Masonic cause in Russia. The Petersburg Freemasons all came to see him, tried to ingratiate themselves with him, and it seemed to them all that he was preparing something for them and concealing it. A solemn meeting of the lodge of the second degree was convened, at which Pierre promised to communicate to the Petersburg Brothers what he had to deliver to them from the highest leaders of their order. The meeting was a full one. After the usual ceremonies Pierre rose and began his address. вҖңDear Brothers,вҖқ he began, blushing and stammering, with a written speech in his hand, вҖңit is not sufficient to observe our mysteries in the seclusion of our lodgeвҒ вҖ”we must actвҒ вҖ”act! We are drowsing, but we must act.вҖқ Pierre raised his notebook and began to read. вҖңFor the dissemination of pure truth and to secure the triumph of virtue,вҖқ he read, вҖңwe must cleanse men from prejudice, diffuse principles in harmony with the spirit of the times, undertake the education of the young, unite ourselves in indissoluble bonds with the wisest men, boldly yet prudently overcome superstitions, infidelity, and folly, and form of those devoted to us a body linked together by unity of purpose and possessed of authority and power. вҖңTo attain this end we must secure a preponderance of virtue over vice and must endeavor to secure that the honest man may, even in this world, receive a lasting reward for his virtue. But in these great endeavors we are gravely hampered by the political institutions of today. What is to be done in these circumstances? To favor revolutions, overthrow everything, repel force by force?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No! We are very far from that. Every violent reform deserves censure, for it quite fails to remedy evil while men remain what they are, and also because wisdom needs no violence. вҖңThe whole plan of our order should be based on the idea of preparing men of firmness and virtue bound together by unity of convictionвҒ вҖ”aiming at the punishment of vice and folly, and patronizing talent and virtue: raising worthy men from the dust and attaching them to our Brotherhood. Only then will our order have the power unobtrusively to bind the hands of the protectors of disorder and to control them without their being aware of it. In a word, we must found a form of government holding universal sway, which should be diffused over the whole world without destroying the bonds of citizenship, and beside which all other governments can continue in their customary course and do everything except what impedes the great aim of our order, which is to obtain for virtue the victory over vice. This aim was that of Christianity itself. It taught men to be wise and good and for their own benefit to follow the example and instruction of the best and wisest men. вҖңAt that time, when everything was plunged in darkness, preaching alone was of course sufficient. The novelty of Truth endowed her with special strength, but now we need much more powerful methods. It is now necessary that man, governed by his senses, should find in virtue a charm palpable to those senses. It is impossible to eradicate the passions; but we must strive to direct them to a noble aim, and it is therefore necessary that everyone should be able to satisfy his passions within the limits of virtue. Our order should provide means to that end. вҖңAs soon as we have a certain number of worthy men in every state, each of them again training two others and all being closely united, everything will be possible for our order, which has already in secret accomplished much for the welfare of mankind.вҖқ This speech not only made a strong impression, but created excitement in the lodge. The majority of the Brothers, seeing in it dangerous designs of Illuminism,61 met it with a coldness that surprised Pierre. The Grand Master began answering him, and Pierre began developing his views with more and more warmth. It was long since there had been so stormy a meeting. Parties were formed, some accusing Pierre of Illuminism, others supporting him. At that meeting he was struck for the first time by the endless variety of menвҖҷs minds, which prevents a truth from ever presenting itself identically to two persons. Even those members who seemed to be on his side understood him in their own way with limitations and alterations he could not agree to, as what he always wanted most was to convey his thought to others just as he himself understood it. At the end of the meeting the Grand Master with irony and ill-will reproved BezГәkhov for his vehemence and said it was not love of virtue alone, but also a love of strife that had moved him in the dispute. Pierre did not answer him and asked briefly whether his proposal would be accepted. He was told that it would not, and without waiting for the usual formalities he left the lodge and went home. VIII VIII Again Pierre was overtaken by the depression he so dreaded. For three days after the delivery of his speech at the lodge he lay on a sofa at home receiving no one and going nowhere. It was just then that he received a letter from his wife, who implored him to see her, telling him how grieved she was about him and how she wished to devote her whole life to him. At the end of the letter she informed him that in a few days she would return to Petersburg from abroad. Following this letter one of the Masonic Brothers whom Pierre respected less than the others forced his way in to see him and, turning the conversation upon PierreвҖҷs matrimonial affairs, by way of fraternal advice expressed the opinion that his severity to his wife was wrong and that he was neglecting one of the first rules of Freemasonry by not forgiving the penitent. At the same time his mother-in-law, Prince VasГӯliвҖҷs wife, sent to him imploring him to come if only for a few minutes to discuss a most important matter. Pierre saw that there was a conspiracy against him and that they wanted to reunite him with his wife, and in the mood he then was, this was not even unpleasant to him. Nothing mattered to him. Nothing in life seemed to him of much importance, and under the influence of the depression that possessed him he valued neither his liberty nor his resolution to punish his wife. вҖңNo one is right and no one is to blame; so she too is not to blame,вҖқ he thought. If he did not at once give his consent to a reunion with his wife, it was only because in his state of depression he did not feel able to take any step. Had his wife come to him, he would not have turned her away. Compared to what preoccupied him, was it not a matter of indifference whether he lived with his wife or not? Without replying either to his wife or his mother-in-law, Pierre late one night prepared for a journey and started for Moscow to see Osip AlexГ©evich. This is what he noted in his diary: Moscow, 17th November I have just returned from my benefactor, and hasten to write down what I have experienced. Osip AlexГ©evich is living poorly and has for three years been suffering from a painful disease of the bladder. No one has ever heard him utter a groan or a word of complaint. From morning till late at night, except when he eats his very plain food, he is working at science. He received me graciously and made me sit down on the bed on which he lay. I made the sign of the Knights of the East and of Jerusalem, and he responded in the same manner, asking me with a mild smile what I had learned and gained in the Prussian and Scottish lodges. I told him everything as best I could, and told him what I had proposed to our Petersburg lodge, of the bad reception I had encountered, and of my rupture with the Brothers. Osip AlexГ©evich, having remained silent and thoughtful for a good while, told me his view of the matter, which at once lit up for me my whole past and the future path I should follow. He surprised me by asking whether I remembered the threefold aim of the order: (1) The preservation and study of the mystery. (2) The purification and reformation of oneself for its reception, and (3) The improvement of the human race by striving for such purification. Which is the principal aim of these three? Certainly self-reformation and self-purification. Only to this aim can we always strive independently of circumstances. But at the same time just this aim demands the greatest efforts of us; and so, led astray by pride, losing sight of this aim, we occupy ourselves either with the mystery which in our impurity we are unworthy to receive, or seek the reformation of the human race while ourselves setting an example of baseness and profligacy. Illuminism is not a pure doctrine, just because it is attracted by social activity and puffed up by pride. On this ground Osip AlexГ©evich condemned my speech and my whole activity, and in the depth of my soul I agreed with him. Talking of my family affairs he said to me, вҖңthe chief duty of a true Mason, as I have told you, lies in perfecting himself. We often think that by removing all the difficulties of our life we shall more quickly reach our aim, but on the contrary, my dear sir, it is only in the midst of worldly cares that we can attain our three chief aims: (1) Self-knowledgeвҒ вҖ”for man can only know himself by comparison, (2) Self-perfecting, which can only be attained by conflict, and (3) The attainment of the chief virtueвҒ вҖ”love of death. Only the vicissitudes of life can show us its vanity and develop our innate love of death or of rebirth to a new life.вҖқ These words are all the more remarkable because, in spite of his great physical sufferings, Osip AlexГ©evich is never weary of life though he loves death, for whichвҒ вҖ”in spite of the purity and loftiness of his inner manвҒ вҖ”he does not yet feel himself sufficiently prepared. My benefactor then explained to me fully the meaning of the Great Square of creation and pointed out to me that the numbers three and seven are the basis of everything. He advised me not to avoid intercourse with the Petersburg Brothers, but to take up only second-grade posts in the lodge, to try, while diverting the Brothers from pride, to turn them toward the true path self-knowledge and self-perfecting. Besides this he advised me for myself personally above all to keep a watch over myself, and to that end he gave me a notebook, the one I am now writing in and in which I will in future note down all my actions. Petersburg, 23rd November I am again living with my wife. My mother-in-law came to me in tears and said that ElГЁn was here and that she implored me to hear her; that she was innocent and unhappy at my desertion, and much more. I knew that if I once let myself see her I should not have strength to go on refusing what she wanted. In my perplexity I did not know whose aid and advice to seek. Had my benefactor been here he would have told me what to do. I went to my room and reread Osip AlexГ©evichвҖҷs letters and recalled my conversations with him, and deduced from it all that I ought not to refuse a supplicant, and ought to reach a helping hand to everyoneвҒ вҖ”especially to one so closely bound to meвҒ вҖ”and that I must bear my cross. But if I forgive her for the sake of doing right, then let union with her have only a spiritual aim. That is what I decided, and what I wrote to Osip AlexГ©evich. I told my wife that I begged her to forget the past, to forgive me whatever wrong I may have done her, and that I had nothing to forgive. It gave me joy to tell her this. She need not know how hard it was for me to see her again. I have settled on the upper floor of this big house and am experiencing a happy feeling of regeneration. IX IX At that time, as always happens, the highest society that met at court and at the grand balls was divided into several circles, each with its own particular tone. The largest of these was the French circle of the Napoleonic alliance, the circle of Count RumyГЎntsev and Caulaincourt. In this group ElГЁn, as soon as she had settled in Petersburg with her husband, took a very prominent place. She was visited by the members of the French embassy and by many belonging to that circle and noted for their intellect and polished manners. ElГЁn had been at Erfurt during the famous meeting of the Emperors and had brought from there these connections with the Napoleonic notabilities. At Erfurt her success had been brilliant. Napoleon himself had noticed her in the theater and said of her: вҖңCвҖҷest un superbe animal.вҖқ62 Her success as a beautiful and elegant woman did not surprise Pierre, for she had become even handsomer than before. What did surprise him was that during these last two years his wife had succeeded in gaining the reputation вҖңdвҖҷ une femme charmante, aussi spirituelle que belle.вҖқ63 The distinguished Prince de Ligne wrote her eight-page letters. BilГӯbin saved up his epigrams to produce them in Countess BezГәkhovaвҖҷs presence. To be received in the Countess BezГәkhovaвҖҷs salon was regarded as a diploma of intellect. Young men read books before attending ElГЁnвҖҷs evenings, to have something to say in her salon, and secretaries of the embassy, and even ambassadors, confided diplomatic secrets to her, so that in a way ElГЁn was a power. Pierre, who knew she was very stupid, sometimes attended, with a strange feeling of perplexity and fear, her evenings and dinner parties, where politics, poetry, and philosophy were discussed. At these parties his feelings were like those of a conjuror who always expects his trick to be found out at any moment. But whether because stupidity was just what was needed to run such a salon, or because those who were deceived found pleasure in the deception, at any rate it remained unexposed and ElГЁna VasГӯlievna BezГәkhovaвҖҷs reputation as a lovely and clever woman became so firmly established that she could say the emptiest and stupidest things and everybody would go into raptures over every word of hers and look for a profound meaning in it of which she herself had no conception. Pierre was just the husband needed for a brilliant society woman. He was that absentminded crank, a grand seigneur husband who was in no oneвҖҷs way, and far from spoiling the high tone and general impression of the drawing room, he served, by the contrast he presented to her, as an advantageous background to his elegant and tactful wife. Pierre during the last two years, as a result of his continual absorption in abstract interests and his sincere contempt for all else, had acquired in his wifeвҖҷs circle, which did not interest him, that air of unconcern, indifference, and benevolence toward all, which cannot be acquired artificially and therefore inspires involuntary respect. He entered his wifeвҖҷs drawing room as one enters a theater, was acquainted with everybody, equally pleased to see everyone, and equally indifferent to them all. Sometimes he joined in a conversation which interested him and, regardless of whether any вҖңgentlemen of the embassyвҖқ were present or not, lispingly expressed his views, which were sometimes not at all in accord with the accepted tone of the moment. But the general opinion concerning the queer husband of вҖңthe most distinguished woman in PetersburgвҖқ was so well established that no one took his freaks seriously. Among the many young men who frequented her house every day, BorГӯs DrubetskГіy, who had already achieved great success in the service, was the most intimate friend of the BezГәkhov household since ElГЁnвҖҷs return from Erfurt. ElГЁn spoke of him as вҖңmon pageвҖқ and treated him like a child. Her smile for him was the same as for everybody, but sometimes that smile made Pierre uncomfortable. Toward him BorГӯs behaved with a particularly dignified and sad deference. This shade of deference also disturbed Pierre. He had suffered so painfully three years before from the mortification to which his wife had subjected him that he now protected himself from the danger of its repetition, first by not being a husband to his wife, and secondly by not allowing himself to suspect. вҖңNo, now that she has become a bluestocking she has finally renounced her former infatuations,вҖқ he told himself. вҖңThere has never been an instance of a bluestocking being carried away by affairs of the heartвҖқвҒ вҖ”a statement which, though gathered from an unknown source, he believed implicitly. Yet strange to say BorГӯsвҖҷ presence in his wifeвҖҷs drawing room (and he was almost always there) had a physical effect upon Pierre; it constricted his limbs and destroyed the unconsciousness and freedom of his movements. вҖңWhat a strange antipathy,вҖқ thought Pierre, вҖңyet I used to like him very much.вҖқ In the eyes of the world Pierre was a great gentleman, the rather blind and absurd husband of a distinguished wife, a clever crank who did nothing but harmed nobody and was a first-rate, good-natured fellow. But a complex and difficult process of internal development was taking place all this time in PierreвҖҷs soul, revealing much to him and causing him many spiritual doubts and joys. I I The Bible legend tells us that the absence of laborвҒ вҖ”idlenessвҒ вҖ”was a condition of the first manвҖҷs blessedness before the Fall. Fallen man has retained a love of idleness, but the curse weighs on the race not only because we have to seek our bread in the sweat of our brows, but because our moral nature is such that we cannot be both idle and at ease. An inner voice tells us we are in the wrong if we are idle. If man could find a state in which he felt that though idle he was fulfilling his duty, he would have found one of the conditions of manвҖҷs primitive blessedness. And such a state of obligatory and irreproachable idleness is the lot of a whole classвҒ вҖ”the military. The chief attraction of military service has consisted and will consist in this compulsory and irreproachable idleness. NikolГЎy RostГіv experienced this blissful condition to the full when, after 1807, he continued to serve in the PГЎvlograd regiment, in which he already commanded the squadron he had taken over from DenГӯsov. RostГіv had become a bluff, good-natured fellow, whom his Moscow acquaintances would have considered rather bad form, but who was liked and respected by his comrades, subordinates, and superiors, and was well contented with his life. Of late, in 1809, he found in letters from home more frequent complaints from his mother that their affairs were falling into greater and greater disorder, and that it was time for him to come back to gladden and comfort his old parents. Reading these letters, NikolГЎy felt a dread of their wanting to take him away from surroundings in which, protected from all the entanglements of life, he was living so calmly and quietly. He felt that sooner or later he would have to reenter that whirlpool of life, with its embarrassments and affairs to be straightened out, its accounts with stewards, quarrels, and intrigues, its ties, society, and with SГіnyaвҖҷs love and his promise to her. It was all dreadfully difficult and complicated; and he replied to his mother in cold, formal letters in French, beginning: вҖңMy dear Mamma,вҖқ and ending: вҖңYour obedient son,вҖқ which said nothing of when he would return. In 1810 he received letters from his parents, in which they told him of NatГЎshaвҖҷs engagement to BolkГіnski, and that the wedding would be in a yearвҖҷs time because the old prince made difficulties. This letter grieved and mortified NikolГЎy. In the first place he was sorry that NatГЎsha, for whom he cared more than for anyone else in the family, should be lost to the home; and secondly, from his hussar point of view, he regretted not to have been there to show that fellow BolkГіnski that connection with him was no such great honor after all, and that if he loved NatГЎsha he might dispense with permission from his dotard father. For a moment he hesitated whether he should not apply for leave in order to see NatГЎsha before she was married, but then came the maneuvers, and considerations about SГіnya and about the confusion of their affairs, and NikolГЎy again put it off. But in the spring of that year, he received a letter from his mother, written without his fatherвҖҷs knowledge, and that letter persuaded him to return. She wrote that if he did not come and take matters in hand, their whole property would be sold by auction and they would all have to go begging. The count was so weak, and trusted MГӯtenka so much, and was so good-natured, that everybody took advantage of him and things were going from bad to worse. вҖңFor GodвҖҷs sake, I implore you, come at once if you do not wish to make me and the whole family wretched,вҖқ wrote the countess. This letter touched NikolГЎy. He had that common sense of a matter-of-fact man which showed him what he ought to do. The right thing now was, if not to retire from the service, at any rate to go home on leave. Why he had to go he did not know; but after his after-dinner nap he gave orders to saddle Mars, an extremely vicious gray stallion that had not been ridden for a long time, and when he returned with the horse all in a lather, he informed LavrГәshka (DenГӯsovвҖҷs servant who had remained with him) and his comrades who turned up in the evening that he was applying for leave and was going home. Difficult and strange as it was for him to reflect that he would go away without having heard from the staffвҒ вҖ”and this interested him extremelyвҒ вҖ”whether he was promoted to a captaincy or would receive the Order of St.В Anne for the last maneuvers; strange as it was to think that he would go away without having sold his three roans to the Polish Count Golukhovski, who was bargaining for the horses RostГіv had betted he would sell for two thousand rubles; incomprehensible as it seemed that the ball the hussars were giving in honor of the Polish Mademoiselle Przazdziecka (out of rivalry to the Uhlans who had given one in honor of their Polish Mademoiselle Borzozowska) would take place without himвҒ вҖ”he knew he must go away from this good, bright world to somewhere where everything was stupid and confused. A week later he obtained his leave. His hussar comradesвҒ вҖ”not only those of his own regiment, but the whole brigadeвҒ вҖ”gave RostГіv a dinner to which the subscription was fifteen rubles a head, and at which there were two bands and two choirs of singers. RostГіv danced the TrepГЎk with Major BГЎsov; the tipsy officers tossed, embraced, and dropped RostГіv; the soldiers of the third squadron tossed him too, and shouted вҖңhurrah!вҖқ and then they put him in his sleigh and escorted him as far as the first post station. During the first half of the journeyвҒ вҖ”from KremenchГәg to KievвҒ вҖ”all RostГіvвҖҷs thoughts, as is usual in such cases, were behind him, with the squadron; but when he had gone more than halfway he began to forget his three roans and DozhoyvГ©yko, his quartermaster, and to wonder anxiously how things would be at OtrГЎdnoe and what he would find there. Thoughts of home grew stronger the nearer he approached itвҒ вҖ”far stronger, as though this feeling of his was subject to the law by which the force of attraction is in inverse proportion to the square of the distance. At the last post station before OtrГЎdnoe he gave the driver a three-ruble tip, and on arriving he ran breathlessly, like a boy, up the steps of his home. After the rapture of meeting, and after that odd feeling of unsatisfied expectationвҒ вҖ”the feeling that вҖңeverything is just the same, so why did I hurry?вҖқвҒ вҖ”NikolГЎy began to settle down in his old home world. His father and mother were much the same, only a little older. What was new in them was a certain uneasiness and occasional discord, which there used not to be, and which, as NikolГЎy soon found out, was due to the bad state of their affairs. SГіnya was nearly twenty; she had stopped growing prettier and promised nothing more than she was already, but that was enough. She exhaled happiness and love from the time NikolГЎy returned, and the faithful, unalterable love of this girl had a gladdening effect on him. PГ©tya and NatГЎsha surprised NikolГЎy most. PГ©tya was a big handsome boy of thirteen, merry, witty, and mischievous, with a voice that was already breaking. As for NatГЎsha, for a long while NikolГЎy wondered and laughed whenever he looked at her. вҖңYouвҖҷre not the same at all,вҖқ he said. вҖңHow? Am I uglier?вҖқ вҖңOn the contrary, but what dignity? A princess!вҖқ he whispered to her. вҖңYes, yes, yes!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha, joyfully. She told him about her romance with Prince AndrГ©y and of his visit to OtrГЎdnoe and showed him his last letter. вҖңWell, are you glad?вҖқ NatГЎsha asked. вҖңI am so tranquil and happy now.вҖқ вҖңVery glad,вҖқ answered NikolГЎy. вҖңHe is an excellent fellow.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And are you very much in love?вҖқ вҖңHow shall I put it?вҖқ replied NatГЎsha. вҖңI was in love with BorГӯs, with my teacher, and with DenГӯsov, but this is quite different. I feel at peace and settled. I know that no better man than he exists, and I am calm and contented now. Not at all as before.вҖқ NikolГЎy expressed his disapproval of the postponement of the marriage for a year; but NatГЎsha attacked her brother with exasperation, proving to him that it could not be otherwise, and that it would be a bad thing to enter a family against the fatherвҖҷs will, and that she herself wished it so. вҖңYou donвҖҷt at all understand,вҖқ she said. NikolГЎy was silent and agreed with her. Her brother often wondered as he looked at her. She did not seem at all like a girl in love and parted from her affianced husband. She was even-tempered and calm and quite as cheerful as of old. This amazed NikolГЎy and even made him regard BolkГіnskiвҖҷs courtship skeptically. He could not believe that her fate was sealed, especially as he had not seen her with Prince AndrГ©y. It always seemed to him that there was something not quite right about this intended marriage. вҖңWhy this delay? Why no betrothal?вҖқ he thought. Once, when he had touched on this topic with his mother, he discovered, to his surprise and somewhat to his satisfaction, that in the depth of her soul she too had doubts about this marriage. вҖңYou see he writes,вҖқ said she, showing her son a letter of Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs, with that latent grudge a mother always has in regard to a daughterвҖҷs future married happiness, вҖңhe writes that he wonвҖҷt come before December. What can be keeping him? Illness, probably! His health is very delicate. DonвҖҷt tell NatГЎsha. And donвҖҷt attach importance to her being so bright: thatвҖҷs because sheвҖҷs living through the last days of her girlhood, but I know what she is like every time we receive a letter from him! However, God grant that everything turns out well!вҖқ (She always ended with these words.) вҖңHe is an excellent man!вҖқ X X вҖңDoes it ever happen to you,вҖқ said NatГЎsha to her brother, when they settled down in the sitting room, вҖңdoes it ever happen to you to feel as if there were nothing more to comeвҒ вҖ”nothing; that everything good is past? And to feel not exactly dull, but sad?вҖқ вҖңI should think so!вҖқ he replied. вҖңI have felt like that when everything was all right and everyone was cheerful. The thought has come into my mind that I was already tired of it all, and that we must all die. Once in the regiment I had not gone to some merrymaking where there was musicвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and suddenly I felt so depressedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңOh yes, I know, I know, I know!вҖқ NatГЎsha interrupted him. вҖңWhen I was quite little that used to be so with me. Do you remember when I was punished once about some plums? You were all dancing, and I sat sobbing in the schoolroom? I shall never forget it: I felt sad and sorry for everyone, for myself, and for everyone. And I was innocentвҒ вҖ”that was the chief thing,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңDo you remember?вҖқ вҖңI remember,вҖқ answered NikolГЎy. вҖңI remember that I came to you afterwards and wanted to comfort you, but do you know, I felt ashamed to. We were terribly absurd. I had a funny doll then and wanted to give it to you. Do you remember?вҖқ вҖңAnd do you remember,вҖқ NatГЎsha asked with a pensive smile, вҖңhow once, long, long ago, when we were quite little, Uncle called us into the studyвҒ вҖ”that was in the old houseвҒ вҖ”and it was darkвҒ вҖ”we went in and suddenly there stoodвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңA Negro,вҖқ chimed in NikolГЎy with a smile of delight. вҖңOf course I remember. Even now I donвҖҷt know whether there really was a Negro, or if we only dreamed it or were told about him.вҖқ вҖңHe was gray, you remember, and had white teeth, and stood and looked at us.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңSГіnya, do you remember?вҖқ asked NikolГЎy. вҖңYes, yes, I do remember something too,вҖқ SГіnya answered timidly. вҖңYou know I have asked Papa and Mamma about that Negro,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, вҖңand they say there was no Negro at all. But you see, you remember!вҖқ вҖңOf course I do, I remember his teeth as if I had just seen them.вҖқ вҖңHow strange it is! ItвҖҷs as if it were a dream! I like that.вҖқ вҖңAnd do you remember how we rolled hard-boiled eggs in the ballroom, and suddenly two old women began spinning round on the carpet? Was that real or not? Do you remember what fun it was?вҖқ вҖңYes, and you remember how Papa in his blue overcoat fired a gun in the porch?вҖқ So they went through their memories, smiling with pleasure: not the sad memories of old age, but poetic, youthful onesвҒ вҖ”those impressions of oneвҖҷs most distant past in which dreams and realities blendвҒ вҖ”and they laughed with quiet enjoyment. SГіnya, as always, did not quite keep pace with them, though they shared the same reminiscences. Much that they remembered had slipped from her mind, and what she recalled did not arouse the same poetic feeling as they experienced. She simply enjoyed their pleasure and tried to fit in with it. She only really took part when they recalled SГіnyaвҖҷs first arrival. She told them how afraid she had been of NikolГЎy because he had on a corded jacket and her nurse had told her that she, too, would be sewn up with cords. вҖңAnd I remember their telling me that you had been born under a cabbage,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, вҖңand I remember that I dared not disbelieve it then, but knew that it was not true, and I felt so uncomfortable.вҖқ While they were talking a maid thrust her head in at the other door of the sitting room. вҖңThey have brought the cock, Miss,вҖқ she said in a whisper. вҖңIt isnвҖҷt wanted, PГіlya. Tell them to take it away,вҖқ replied NatГЎsha. In the middle of their talk in the sitting room, Dimmler came in and went up to the harp that stood there in a corner. He took off its cloth covering, and the harp gave out a jarring sound. вҖңEduГЎrd KarlГҪch, please play my favorite nocturne by Field,вҖқ came the old countessвҖҷ voice from the drawing room. Dimmler struck a chord and, turning to NatГЎsha, NikolГЎy, and SГіnya, remarked: вҖңHow quiet you young people are!вҖқ вҖңYes, weвҖҷre philosophizing,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, glancing round for a moment and then continuing the conversation. They were now discussing dreams. Dimmler began to play; NatГЎsha went on tiptoe noiselessly to the table, took up a candle, carried it out, and returned, seating herself quietly in her former place. It was dark in the room especially where they were sitting on the sofa, but through the big windows the silvery light of the full moon fell on the floor. Dimmler had finished the piece but still sat softly running his fingers over the strings, evidently uncertain whether to stop or to play something else. вҖңDo you know,вҖқ said NatГЎsha in a whisper, moving closer to NikolГЎy and SГіnya, вҖңthat when one goes on and on recalling memories, one at last begins to remember what happened before one was in the world.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңThat is metempsychosis,вҖқ said SГіnya, who had always learned well, and remembered everything. вҖңThe Egyptians believed that our souls have lived in animals, and will go back into animals again.вҖқ вҖңNo, I donвҖҷt believe we ever were in animals,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, still in a whisper though the music had ceased. вҖңBut I am certain that we were angels somewhere there, and have been here, and that is why we remember.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңMay I join you?вҖқ said Dimmler who had come up quietly, and he sat down by them. вҖңIf we have been angels, why have we fallen lower?вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңNo, that canвҖҷt be!вҖқ вҖңNot lower, who said we were lower?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ How do I know what I was before?вҖқ NatГЎsha rejoined with conviction. вҖңThe soul is immortalвҒ вҖ”well then, if I shall always live I must have lived before, lived for a whole eternity.вҖқ вҖңYes, but it is hard for us to imagine eternity,вҖқ remarked Dimmler, who had joined the young folk with a mildly condescending smile but now spoke as quietly and seriously as they. вҖңWhy is it hard to imagine eternity?вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңIt is now today, and it will be tomorrow, and always; and there was yesterday, and the day before.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңNatГЎsha! Now itвҖҷs your turn. Sing me something,вҖқ they heard the countess say. вҖңWhy are you sitting there like conspirators?вҖқ вҖңMamma, I donвҖҷt at all want to,вҖқ replied NatГЎsha, but all the same she rose. None of them, not even the middle-aged Dimmler, wanted to break off their conversation and quit that corner in the sitting room, but NatГЎsha got up and NikolГЎy sat down at the clavichord. Standing as usual in the middle of the hall and choosing the place where the resonance was best, NatГЎsha began to sing her motherвҖҷs favorite song. She had said she did not want to sing, but it was long since she had sung, and long before she again sang, as she did that evening. The count, from his study where he was talking to MГӯtenka, heard her and, like a schoolboy in a hurry to run out to play, blundered in his talk while giving orders to the steward, and at last stopped, while MГӯtenka stood in front of him also listening and smiling. NikolГЎy did not take his eyes off his sister and drew breath in time with her. SГіnya, as she listened, thought of the immense difference there was between herself and her friend, and how impossible it was for her to be anything like as bewitching as her cousin. The old countess sat with a blissful yet sad smile and with tears in her eyes, occasionally shaking her head. She thought of NatГЎsha and of her own youth, and of how there was something unnatural and dreadful in this impending marriage of NatГЎsha and Prince AndrГ©y. Dimmler, who had seated himself beside the countess, listened with closed eyes. вҖңAh, Countess,вҖқ he said at last, вҖңthatвҖҷs a European talent, she has nothing to learnвҒ вҖ”what softness, tenderness, and strength.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAh, how afraid I am for her, how afraid I am!вҖқ said the countess, not realizing to whom she was speaking. Her maternal instinct told her that NatГЎsha had too much of something, and that because of this she would not be happy. Before NatГЎsha had finished singing, fourteen-year-old PГ©tya rushed in delightedly, to say that some mummers had arrived. NatГЎsha stopped abruptly. вҖңIdiot!вҖқ she screamed at her brother and, running to a chair, threw herself on it, sobbing so violently that she could not stop for a long time. вҖңItвҖҷs nothing, Mamma, really itвҖҷs nothing; only PГ©tya startled me,вҖқ she said, trying to smile, but her tears still flowed and sobs still choked her. The mummers (some of the house serfs) dressed up as bears, Turks, innkeepers, and ladiesвҒ вҖ”frightening and funnyвҒ вҖ”bringing in with them the cold from outside and a feeling of gaiety, crowded, at first timidly, into the anteroom, then hiding behind one another they pushed into the ballroom where, shyly at first and then more and more merrily and heartily, they started singing, dancing, and playing Christmas games. The countess, when she had identified them and laughed at their costumes, went into the drawing room. The count sat in the ballroom, smiling radiantly and applauding the players. The young people had disappeared. Half an hour later there appeared among the other mummers in the ballroom an old lady in a hooped skirtвҒ вҖ”this was NikolГЎy. A Turkish girl was PГ©tya. A clown was Dimmler. An hussar was NatГЎsha, and a Circassian was SГіnya with burnt-cork mustache and eyebrows. After the condescending surprise, non-recognition, and praise, from those who were not themselves dressed up, the young people decided that their costumes were so good that they ought to be shown elsewhere. NikolГЎy, who, as the roads were in splendid condition, wanted to take them all for a drive in his troyka, proposed to take with them about a dozen of the serf mummers and drive to вҖңUncleвҖҷs.вҖқ вҖңNo, why disturb the old fellow?вҖқ said the countess. вҖңBesides, you wouldnвҖҷt have room to turn round there. If you must go, go to the MelyukГіvsвҖҷ.вҖқ MelyukГіva was a widow, who, with her family and their tutors and governesses, lived three miles from the RostГіvs. вҖңThatвҖҷs right, my dear,вҖқ chimed in the old count, thoroughly aroused. вҖңIвҖҷll dress up at once and go with them. IвҖҷll make Pashette open her eyes.вҖқ But the countess would not agree to his going; he had had a bad leg all these last days. It was decided that the count must not go, but that if LuГӯza IvГЎnovna (Madame Schoss) would go with them, the young ladies might go to the MelyukГіvsвҖҷ, SГіnya, generally so timid and shy, more urgently than anyone begging LuГӯza IvГЎnovna not to refuse. SГіnyaвҖҷs costume was the best of all. Her mustache and eyebrows were extraordinarily becoming. Everyone told her she looked very handsome, and she was in a spirited and energetic mood unusual with her. Some inner voice told her that now or never her fate would be decided, and in her male attire she seemed quite a different person. LuГӯza IvГЎnovna consented to go, and in half an hour four troyka sleighs with large and small bells, their runners squeaking and whistling over the frozen snow, drove up to the porch. NatГЎsha was foremost in setting a merry holiday tone, which, passing from one to another, grew stronger and reached its climax when they all came out into the frost and got into the sleighs, talking, calling to one another, laughing, and shouting. Two of the troykas were the usual household sleighs, the third was the old countвҖҷs with a trotter from the OrlГіv stud as shaft horse, the fourth was NikolГЎyвҖҷs own with a short shaggy black shaft horse. NikolГЎy, in his old ladyвҖҷs dress over which he had belted his hussar overcoat, stood in the middle of the sleigh, reins in hand. It was so light that he could see the moonlight reflected from the metal harness disks and from the eyes of the horses, who looked round in alarm at the noisy party under the shadow of the porch roof. NatГЎsha, SГіnya, Madame Schoss, and two maids got into NikolГЎyвҖҷs sleigh; Dimmler, his wife, and PГ©tya, into the old countвҖҷs, and the rest of the mummers seated themselves in the other two sleighs. вҖңYou go ahead, ZakhГЎr!вҖқ shouted NikolГЎy to his fatherвҖҷs coachman, wishing for a chance to race past him. The old countвҖҷs troyka, with Dimmler and his party, started forward, squeaking on its runners as though freezing to the snow, its deep-toned bell clanging. The side horses, pressing against the shafts of the middle horse, sank in the snow, which was dry and glittered like sugar, and threw it up. NikolГЎy set off, following the first sleigh; behind him the others moved noisily, their runners squeaking. At first they drove at a steady trot along the narrow road. While they drove past the garden the shadows of the bare trees often fell across the road and hid the brilliant moonlight, but as soon as they were past the fence, the snowy plain bathed in moonlight and motionless spread out before them glittering like diamonds and dappled with bluish shadows. Bang, bang! went the first sleigh over a cradle hole in the snow of the road, and each of the other sleighs jolted in the same way, and rudely breaking the frost-bound stillness, the troykas began to speed along the road, one after the other. вҖңA hareвҖҷs track, a lot of tracks!вҖқ rang out NatГЎshaвҖҷs voice through the frost-bound air. вҖңHow light it is, Nicolas!вҖқ came SГіnyaвҖҷs voice. NikolГЎy glanced round at SГіnya, and bent down to see her face closer. Quite a new, sweet face with black eyebrows and mustaches peeped up at him from her sable fursвҒ вҖ”so close and yet so distantвҒ вҖ”in the moonlight. вҖңThat used to be SГіnya,вҖқ thought he, and looked at her closer and smiled. вҖңWhat is it, Nicolas?вҖқ вҖңNothing,вҖқ said he and turned again to the horses. When they came out onto the beaten high roadвҒ вҖ”polished by sleigh runners and cut up by roughshod hoofs, the marks of which were visible in the moonlightвҒ вҖ”the horses began to tug at the reins of their own accord and increased their pace. The near side horse, arching his head and breaking into a short canter, tugged at his traces. The shaft horse swayed from side to side, moving his ears as if asking: вҖңIsnвҖҷt it time to begin now?вҖқ In front, already far ahead the deep bell of the sleigh ringing farther and farther off, the black horses driven by ZakhГЎr could be clearly seen against the white snow. From that sleigh one could hear the shouts, laughter, and voices of the mummers. вҖңGee up, my darlings!вҖқ shouted NikolГЎy, pulling the reins to one side and flourishing the whip. It was only by the keener wind that met them and the jerks given by the side horses who pulled harderвҒ вҖ”ever increasing their gallopвҒ вҖ”that one noticed how fast the troyka was flying. NikolГЎy looked back. With screams, squeals, and waving of whips that caused even the shaft horses to gallopвҒ вҖ”the other sleighs followed. The shaft horse swung steadily beneath the bow over its head, with no thought of slackening pace and ready to put on speed when required. NikolГЎy overtook the first sleigh. They were driving downhill and coming out upon a broad trodden track across a meadow, near a river. вҖңWhere are we?вҖқ thought he. вҖңItвҖҷs the KosГіy meadow, I suppose. But noвҒ вҖ”this is something new IвҖҷve never seen before. This isnвҖҷt the KosГіy meadow nor the DГ«mkin hill, and heaven only knows what it is! It is something new and enchanted. Well, whatever it may beвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ And shouting to his horses, he began to pass the first sleigh. ZakhГЎr held back his horses and turned his face, which was already covered with hoarfrost to his eyebrows. NikolГЎy gave the horses the rein, and ZakhГЎr, stretching out his arms, clucked his tongue and let his horses go. вҖңNow, look out, master!вҖқ he cried. Faster still the two troykas flew side by side, and faster moved the feet of the galloping side horses. NikolГЎy began to draw ahead. ZakhГЎr, while still keeping his arms extended, raised one hand with the reins. вҖңNo you wonвҖҷt, master!вҖқ he shouted. NikolГЎy put all his horses to a gallop and passed ZakhГЎr. The horses showered the fine dry snow on the faces of those in the sleighвҒ вҖ”beside them sounded quick ringing bells and they caught confused glimpses of swiftly moving legs and the shadows of the troyka they were passing. The whistling sound of the runners on the snow and the voices of girls shrieking were heard from different sides. Again checking his horses, NikolГЎy looked around him. They were still surrounded by the magic plain bathed in moonlight and spangled with stars. вҖңZakhГЎr is shouting that I should turn to the left, but why to the left?вҖқ thought NikolГЎy. вҖңAre we getting to the MelyukГіvsвҖҷ? Is this MelyukГіvka? Heaven only knows where we are going, and heaven knows what is happening to usвҒ вҖ”but it is very strange and pleasant whatever it is.вҖқ And he looked round in the sleigh. вҖңLook, his mustache and eyelashes are all white!вҖқ said one of the strange, pretty, unfamiliar peopleвҒ вҖ”the one with fine eyebrows and mustache. вҖңI think this used to be NatГЎsha,вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, вҖңand that was Madame Schoss, but perhaps itвҖҷs not, and this Circassian with the mustache I donвҖҷt know, but I love her.вҖқ вҖңArenвҖҷt you cold?вҖқ he asked. They did not answer but began to laugh. Dimmler from the sleigh behind shouted somethingвҒ вҖ”probably something funnyвҒ вҖ”but they could not make out what he said. вҖңYes, yes!вҖқ some voices answered, laughing. вҖңBut here was a fairy forest with black moving shadows, and a glitter of diamonds and a flight of marble steps and the silver roofs of fairy buildings and the shrill yells of some animals. And if this is really MelyukГіvka, it is still stranger that we drove heaven knows where and have come to MelyukГіvka,вҖқ thought NikolГЎy. It really was MelyukГіvka, and maids and footmen with merry faces came running, out to the porch carrying candles. вҖңWho is it?вҖқ asked someone in the porch. вҖңThe mummers from the countвҖҷs. I know by the horses,вҖқ replied some voices. XI XI PelagГ©ya DanГӯlovna MelyukГіva, a broadly built, energetic woman wearing spectacles, sat in the drawing room in a loose dress, surrounded by her daughters whom she was trying to keep from feeling dull. They were quietly dropping melted wax into snow and looking at the shadows the wax figures would throw on the wall, when they heard the steps and voices of new arrivals in the vestibule. Hussars, ladies, witches, clowns, and bears, after clearing their throats and wiping the hoarfrost from their faces in the vestibule, came into the ballroom where candles were hurriedly lighted. The clownвҒ вҖ”DimmlerвҒ вҖ”and the ladyвҒ вҖ”NikolГЎyвҒ вҖ”started a dance. Surrounded by the screaming children the mummers, covering their faces and disguising their voices, bowed to their hostess and arranged themselves about the room. вҖңDear me! thereвҖҷs no recognizing them! And NatГЎsha! See whom she looks like! She really reminds me of somebody. But EduГЎrd KarlГҪchвҒ вҖ”isnвҖҷt he good! I didnвҖҷt know him! And how he dances. Dear me, thereвҖҷs a Circassian. Really, how becoming it is to dear SonyГәshka. And who is that? Well, you have cheered us up! NikГӯta and VanyaвҒ вҖ”clear away the tables! And we were sitting so quietly. Ha, ha, ha!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The hussar, the hussar! Just like a boy! And the legs!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I canвҖҷt look at himвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ different voices were saying. NatГЎsha, the young MelyukГіvsвҖҷ favorite, disappeared with them into the back rooms where a cork and various dressing gowns and male garments were called for and received from the footman by bare girlish arms from behind the door. Ten minutes later, all the young MelyukГіvs joined the mummers. PelagГ©ya DanГӯlovna, having given orders to clear the rooms for the visitors and arranged about refreshments for the gentry and the serfs, went about among the mummers without removing her spectacles, peering into their faces with a suppressed smile and failing to recognize any of them. It was not merely Dimmler and the RostГіvs she failed to recognize, she did not even recognize her own daughters, or her late husbandвҖҷs dressing gowns and uniforms, which they had put on. вҖңAnd who is this?вҖқ she asked her governess, peering into the face of her own daughter dressed up as a KazГЎn-Tartar. вҖңI suppose it is one of the RostГіvs! Well, Mr.В Hussar, and what regiment do you serve in?вҖқ she asked NatГЎsha. вҖңHere, hand some fruit jelly to the Turk!вҖқ she ordered the butler who was handing things round. вҖңThatвҖҷs not forbidden by his law.вҖқ Sometimes, as she looked at the strange but amusing capers cut by the dancers, whoвҒ вҖ”having decided once for all that being disguised, no one would recognize themвҒ вҖ”were not at all shy, PelagГ©ya DanГӯlovna hid her face in her handkerchief, and her whole stout body shook with irrepressible, kindly, elderly laughter. вҖңMy little SГЎsha! Look at SГЎsha!вҖқ she said. After Russian country dances and chorus dances, PelagГ©ya DanГӯlovna made the serfs and gentry join in one large circle: a ring, a string, and a silver ruble were fetched and they all played games together. In an hour, all the costumes were crumpled and disordered. The corked eyebrows and mustaches were smeared over the perspiring, flushed, and merry faces. PelagГ©ya DanГӯlovna began to recognize the mummers, admired their cleverly contrived costumes, and particularly how they suited the young ladies, and she thanked them all for having entertained her so well. The visitors were invited to supper in the drawing room, and the serfs had something served to them in the ballroom. вҖңNow to tell oneвҖҷs fortune in the empty bathhouse is frightening!вҖқ said an old maid who lived with the MelyukГіvs, during supper. вҖңWhy?вҖқ said the eldest MelyukГіv girl. вҖңYou wouldnвҖҷt go, it takes courage.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңIвҖҷll go,вҖқ said SГіnya. вҖңTell what happened to the young lady!вҖқ said the second MelyukГіv girl. вҖңWell,вҖқ began the old maid, вҖңa young lady once went out, took a cock, laid the table for two, all properly, and sat down. After sitting a while, she suddenly hears someone comingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ a sleigh drives up with harness bells; she hears him coming! He comes in, just in the shape of a man, like an officerвҒ вҖ”comes in and sits down to table with her.вҖқ вҖңAh! ah!вҖқ screamed NatГЎsha, rolling her eyes with horror. вҖңYes? And howвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ did he speak?вҖқ вҖңYes, like a man. Everything quite all right, and he began persuading her; and she should have kept him talking till cockcrow, but she got frightened, just got frightened and hid her face in her hands. Then he caught her up. It was lucky the maids ran in just then.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңNow, why frighten them?вҖқ said PelagГ©ya DanГӯlovna. вҖңMamma, you used to try your fate yourselfвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said her daughter. вҖңAnd how does one do it in a barn?вҖқ inquired SГіnya. вҖңWell, say you went to the barn now, and listened. It depends on what you hear; hammering and knockingвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs bad; but a sound of shifting grain is good and one sometimes hears that, too.вҖқ вҖңMamma, tell us what happened to you in the barn.вҖқ PelagГ©ya DanГӯlovna smiled. вҖңOh, IвҖҷve forgottenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she replied. вҖңBut none of you would go?вҖқ вҖңYes, I will; PelagГ©ya DanГӯlovna, let me! IвҖҷll go,вҖқ said SГіnya. вҖңWell, why not, if youвҖҷre not afraid?вҖқ вҖңLuГӯza IvГЎnovna, may I?вҖқ asked SГіnya. Whether they were playing the ring and string game or the ruble game or talking as now, NikolГЎy did not leave SГіnyaвҖҷs side, and gazed at her with quite new eyes. It seemed to him that it was only today, thanks to that burnt-cork mustache, that he had fully learned to know her. And really, that evening, SГіnya was brighter, more animated, and prettier than NikolГЎy had ever seen her before. вҖңSo thatвҖҷs what she is like; what a fool I have been!вҖқ he thought gazing at her sparkling eyes, and under the mustache a happy rapturous smile dimpled her cheeks, a smile he had never seen before. вҖңIвҖҷm not afraid of anything,вҖқ said SГіnya. вҖңMay I go at once?вҖқ She got up. They told her where the barn was and how she should stand and listen, and they handed her a fur cloak. She threw this over her head and shoulders and glanced at NikolГЎy. вҖңWhat a darling that girl is!вҖқ thought he. вҖңAnd what have I been thinking of till now?вҖқ SГіnya went out into the passage to go to the barn. NikolГЎy went hastily to the front porch, saying he felt too hot. The crowd of people really had made the house stuffy. Outside, there was the same cold stillness and the same moon, but even brighter than before. The light was so strong and the snow sparkled with so many stars that one did not wish to look up at the sky and the real stars were unnoticed. The sky was black and dreary, while the earth was gay. вҖңI am a fool, a fool! what have I been waiting for?вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, and running out from the porch he went round the corner of the house and along the path that led to the back porch. He knew SГіnya would pass that way. Halfway lay some snow-covered piles of firewood and across and along them a network of shadows from the bare old lime trees fell on the snow and on the path. This path led to the barn. The log walls of the barn and its snow-covered roof, that looked as if hewn out of some precious stone, sparkled in the moonlight. A tree in the garden snapped with the frost, and then all was again perfectly silent. His bosom seemed to inhale not air but the strength of eternal youth and gladness. From the back porch came the sound of feet descending the steps, the bottom step upon which snow had fallen gave a ringing creak and he heard the voice of an old maidservant saying, вҖңStraight, straight, along the path, Miss. Only, donвҖҷt look back.вҖқ вҖңI am not afraid,вҖқ answered SГіnyaвҖҷs voice, and along the path toward NikolГЎy came the crunching, whistling sound of SГіnyaвҖҷs feet in her thin shoes. SГіnya came along, wrapped in her cloak. She was only a couple of paces away when she saw him, and to her too he was not the NikolГЎy she had known and always slightly feared. He was in a womanвҖҷs dress, with tousled hair and a happy smile new to SГіnya. She ran rapidly toward him. вҖңQuite different and yet the same,вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, looking at her face all lit up by the moonlight. He slipped his arms under the cloak that covered her head, embraced her, pressed her to him, and kissed her on the lips that wore a mustache and had a smell of burnt cork. SГіnya kissed him full on the lips, and disengaging her little hands pressed them to his cheeks. вҖңSГіnya!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Nicolas!вҖқвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ was all they said. They ran to the barn and then back again, reentering, he by the front and she by the back porch. XII XII When they all drove back from PelagГ©ya DanГӯlovnaвҖҷs, NatГЎsha, who always saw and noticed everything, arranged that she and LuГӯza IvГЎnovna should go back in the sleigh with Dimmler, and SГіnya with NikolГЎy and the maids. On the way back NikolГЎy drove at a steady pace instead of racing and kept peering by that fantastic all-transforming light into SГіnyaвҖҷs face and searching beneath the eyebrows and mustache for his former and his present SГіnya from whom he had resolved never to be parted again. He looked and recognizing in her both the old and the new SГіnya, and being reminded by the smell of burnt cork of the sensation of her kiss, inhaled the frosty air with a full breast and, looking at the ground flying beneath him and at the sparkling sky, felt himself again in fairyland. вҖңSГіnya, is it well with thee?вҖқ he asked from time to time. вҖңYes!вҖқ she replied. вҖңAnd with thee?вҖқ When halfway home NikolГЎy handed the reins to the coachman and ran for a moment to NatГЎshaвҖҷs sleigh and stood on its wing. вҖңNatГЎsha!вҖқ he whispered in French, вҖңdo you know I have made up my mind about SГіnya?вҖқ вҖңHave you told her?вҖқ asked NatГЎsha, suddenly beaming all over with joy. вҖңOh, how strange you are with that mustache and those eyebrows!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ NatГЎshaвҒ вҖ”are you glad?вҖқ вҖңI am so glad, so glad! I was beginning to be vexed with you. I did not tell you, but you have been treating her badly. What a heart she has, Nicolas! I am horrid sometimes, but I was ashamed to be happy while SГіnya was not,вҖқ continued NatГЎsha. вҖңNow I am so glad! Well, run back to her.вҖқ вҖңNo, wait a bit.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Oh, how funny you look!вҖқ cried NikolГЎy, peering into her face and finding in his sister too something new, unusual, and bewitchingly tender that he had not seen in her before. вҖңNatГЎsha, itвҖҷs magical, isnвҖҷt it?вҖқ вҖңYes,вҖқ she replied. вҖңYou have done splendidly.вҖқ вҖңHad I seen her before as she is now,вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, вҖңI should long ago have asked her what to do and have done whatever she told me, and all would have been well.вҖқ вҖңSo you are glad and I have done right?вҖқ вҖңOh, quite right! I had a quarrel with Mamma some time ago about it. Mamma said she was angling for you. How could she say such a thing! I nearly stormed at Mamma. I will never let anyone say anything bad of SГіnya, for there is nothing but good in her.вҖқ вҖңThen itвҖҷs all right?вҖқ said NikolГЎy, again scrutinizing the expression of his sisterвҖҷs face to see if she was in earnest. Then he jumped down and, his boots scrunching the snow, ran back to his sleigh. The same happy, smiling Circassian, with mustache and beaming eyes looking up from under a sable hood, was still sitting there, and that Circassian was SГіnya, and that SГіnya was certainly his future happy and loving wife. When they reached home and had told their mother how they had spent the evening at the MelyukГіvsвҖҷ, the girls went to their bedroom. When they had undressed, but without washing off the cork mustaches, they sat a long time talking of their happiness. They talked of how they would live when they were married, how their husbands would be friends, and how happy they would be. On NatГЎshaвҖҷs table stood two looking glasses which DunyГЎsha had prepared beforehand. вҖңOnly when will all that be? I am afraid never.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ It would be too good!вҖқ said NatГЎsha, rising and going to the looking glasses. вҖңSit down, NatГЎsha; perhaps youвҖҷll see him,вҖқ said SГіnya. NatГЎsha lit the candles, one on each side of one of the looking glasses, and sat down. вҖңI see someone with a mustache,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, seeing her own face. вҖңYou mustnвҖҷt laugh, Miss,вҖқ said DunyГЎsha. With SГіnyaвҖҷs help and the maidвҖҷs, NatГЎsha got the glass she held into the right position opposite the other; her face assumed a serious expression and she sat silent. She sat a long time looking at the receding line of candles reflected in the glasses and expecting (from tales she had heard) to see a coffin, or him, Prince AndrГ©y, in that last dim, indistinctly outlined square. But ready as she was to take the smallest speck for the image of a man or of a coffin, she saw nothing. She began blinking rapidly and moved away from the looking glasses. вҖңWhy is it others see things and I donвҖҷt?вҖқ she said. вҖңYou sit down now, SГіnya. You absolutely must, tonight! Do it for me.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Today I feel so frightened!вҖқ SГіnya sat down before the glasses, got the right position, and began looking. вҖңNow, SГіfya AlexГЎndrovna is sure to see something,вҖқ whispered DunyГЎsha; вҖңwhile you do nothing but laugh.вҖқ SГіnya heard this and NatГЎshaвҖҷs whisper: вҖңI know she will. She saw something last year.вҖқ For about three minutes all were silent. вҖңOf course she will!вҖқ whispered NatГЎsha, but did not finishвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ suddenly SГіnya pushed away the glass she was holding and covered her eyes with her hand. вҖңOh, NatГЎsha!вҖқ she cried. вҖңDid you see? Did you? What was it?вҖқ exclaimed NatГЎsha, holding up the looking glass. SГіnya had not seen anything, she was just wanting to blink and to get up when she heard NatГЎsha say, вҖңOf course she will!вҖқ She did not wish to disappoint either DunyГЎsha or NatГЎsha, but it was hard to sit still. She did not herself know how or why the exclamation escaped her when she covered her eyes. вҖңYou saw him?вҖқ urged NatГЎsha, seizing her hand. вҖңYes. Wait a bitвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ saw him,вҖқ SГіnya could not help saying, not yet knowing whom NatГЎsha meant by him, NikolГЎy or Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңBut why shouldnвҖҷt I say I saw something? Others do see! Besides who can tell whether I saw anything or not?вҖқ flashed through SГіnyaвҖҷs mind. вҖңYes, I saw him,вҖқ she said. вҖңHow? Standing or lying?вҖқ вҖңNo, I sawвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ At first there was nothing, then I saw him lying down.вҖқ вҖңAndrГ©y lying? Is he ill?вҖқ asked NatГЎsha, her frightened eyes fixed on her friend. вҖңNo, on the contrary, on the contrary! His face was cheerful, and he turned to me.вҖқ And when saying this she herself fancied she had really seen what she described. вҖңWell, and then, SГіnya?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAfter that, I could not make out what there was; something blue and red.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңSГіnya! When will he come back? When shall I see him! O, God, how afraid I am for him and for myself and about everything!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ NatГЎsha began, and without replying to SГіnyaвҖҷs words of comfort she got into bed, and long after her candle was out lay open-eyed and motionless, gazing at the moonlight through the frosty windowpanes. XIII XIII Soon after the Christmas holidays NikolГЎy told his mother of his love for SГіnya and of his firm resolve to marry her. The countess, who had long noticed what was going on between them and was expecting this declaration, listened to him in silence and then told her son that he might marry whom he pleased, but that neither she nor his father would give their blessing to such a marriage. NikolГЎy, for the first time, felt that his mother was displeased with him and that, despite her love for him, she would not give way. Coldly, without looking at her son, she sent for her husband and, when he came, tried briefly and coldly to inform him of the facts, in her sonвҖҷs presence, but unable to restrain herself she burst into tears of vexation and left the room. The old count began irresolutely to admonish NikolГЎy and beg him to abandon his purpose. NikolГЎy replied that he could not go back on his word, and his father, sighing and evidently disconcerted, very soon became silent and went in to the countess. In all his encounters with his son, the count was always conscious of his own guilt toward him for having wasted the family fortune, and so he could not be angry with him for refusing to marry an heiress and choosing the dowerless SГіnya. On this occasion, he was only more vividly conscious of the fact that if his affairs had not been in disorder, no better wife for NikolГЎy than SГіnya could have been wished for, and that no one but himself with his MГӯtenka and his uncomfortable habits was to blame for the condition of the family finances. The father and mother did not speak of the matter to their son again, but a few days later the countess sent for SГіnya and, with a cruelty neither of them expected, reproached her niece for trying to catch NikolГЎy and for ingratitude. SГіnya listened silently with downcast eyes to the countessвҖҷ cruel words, without understanding what was required of her. She was ready to sacrifice everything for her benefactors. Self-sacrifice was her most cherished idea but in this case she could not see what she ought to sacrifice, or for whom. She could not help loving the countess and the whole RostГіv family, but neither could she help loving NikolГЎy and knowing that his happiness depended on that love. She was silent and sad and did not reply. NikolГЎy felt the situation to be intolerable and went to have an explanation with his mother. He first implored her to forgive him and SГіnya and consent to their marriage, then he threatened that if she molested SГіnya he would at once marry her secretly. The countess, with a coldness her son had never seen in her before, replied that he was of age, that Prince AndrГ©y was marrying without his fatherвҖҷs consent, and he could do the same, but that she would never receive that intriguer as her daughter. Exploding at the word intriguer, NikolГЎy, raising his voice, told his mother he had never expected her to try to force him to sell his feelings, but if that were so, he would say for the last time.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But he had no time to utter the decisive word which the expression of his face caused his mother to await with terror, and which would perhaps have forever remained a cruel memory to them both. He had not time to say it, for NatГЎsha, with a pale and set face, entered the room from the door at which she had been listening. вҖңNikГіlenka, you are talking nonsense! Be quiet, be quiet, be quiet, I tell you!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she almost screamed, so as to drown his voice. вҖңMamma darling, itвҖҷs not at all soвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ my poor, sweet darling,вҖқ she said to her mother, who conscious that they had been on the brink of a rupture gazed at her son with terror, but in the obstinacy and excitement of the conflict could not and would not give way. вҖңNikГіlenka, IвҖҷll explain to you. Go away! Listen, Mamma darling,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. Her words were incoherent, but they attained the purpose at which she was aiming. The countess, sobbing heavily, hid her face on her daughterвҖҷs breast, while NikolГЎy rose, clutching his head, and left the room. NatГЎsha set to work to effect a reconciliation, and so far succeeded that NikolГЎy received a promise from his mother that SГіnya should not be troubled, while he on his side promised not to undertake anything without his parentsвҖҷ knowledge. Firmly resolved, after putting his affairs in order in the regiment, to retire from the army and return and marry SГіnya, NikolГЎy, serious, sorrowful, and at variance with his parents, but, as it seemed to him, passionately in love, left at the beginning of January to rejoin his regiment. After NikolГЎy had gone things in the RostГіv household were more depressing than ever, and the countess fell ill from mental agitation. SГіnya was unhappy at the separation from NikolГЎy and still more so on account of the hostile tone the countess could not help adopting toward her. The count was more perturbed than ever by the condition of his affairs, which called for some decisive action. Their town house and estate near Moscow had inevitably to be sold, and for this they had to go to Moscow. But the countessвҖҷ health obliged them to delay their departure from day to day. NatГЎsha, who had borne the first period of separation from her betrothed lightly and even cheerfully, now grew more agitated and impatient every day. The thought that her best days, which she would have employed in loving him, were being vainly wasted, with no advantage to anyone, tormented her incessantly. His letters for the most part irritated her. It hurt her to think that while she lived only in the thought of him, he was living a real life, seeing new places and new people that interested him. The more interesting his letters were the more vexed she felt. Her letters to him, far from giving her any comfort, seemed to her a wearisome and artificial obligation. She could not write, because she could not conceive the possibility of expressing sincerely in a letter even a thousandth part of what she expressed by voice, smile, and glance. She wrote to him formal, monotonous, and dry letters, to which she attached no importance herself, and in the rough copies of which the countess corrected her mistakes in spelling. There was still no improvement in the countessвҖҷ health, but it was impossible to defer the journey to Moscow any longer. NatГЎshaвҖҷs trousseau had to be ordered and the house sold. Moreover, Prince AndrГ©y was expected in Moscow, where old Prince BolkГіnski was spending the winter, and NatГЎsha felt sure he had already arrived. So the countess remained in the country, and the count, taking SГіnya and NatГЎsha with him, went to Moscow at the end of January. II II After reaching home NikolГЎy was at first serious and even dull. He was worried by the impending necessity of interfering in the stupid business matters for which his mother had called him home. To throw off this burden as quickly as possible, on the third day after his arrival he went, angry and scowling and without answering questions as to where he was going, to MГӯtenkaвҖҷs lodge and demanded an account of everything. But what an account of everything might be NikolГЎy knew even less than the frightened and bewildered MГӯtenka. The conversation and the examination of the accounts with MГӯtenka did not last long. The village elder, a peasant delegate, and the village clerk, who were waiting in the passage, heard with fear and delight first the young countвҖҷs voice roaring and snapping and rising louder and louder, and then words of abuse, dreadful words, ejaculated one after the other. вҖңRobber!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Ungrateful wretch!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ IвҖҷll hack the dog to pieces! IвҖҷm not my father!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Robbing us!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and so on. Then with no less fear and delight they saw how the young count, red in the face and with bloodshot eyes, dragged MГӯtenka out by the scruff of the neck and applied his foot and knee to his behind with great agility at convenient moments between the words, shouting, вҖңBe off! Never let me see your face here again, you villain!вҖқ MГӯtenka flew headlong down the six steps and ran away into the shrubbery. (This shrubbery was a well-known haven of refuge for culprits at OtrГЎdnoe. MГӯtenka himself, returning tipsy from the town, used to hide there, and many of the residents at OtrГЎdnoe, hiding from MГӯtenka, knew of its protective qualities.) MГӯtenkaвҖҷs wife and sisters-in-law thrust their heads and frightened faces out of the door of a room where a bright samovar was boiling and where the stewardвҖҷs high bedstead stood with its patchwork quilt. The young count paid no heed to them, but, breathing hard, passed by with resolute strides and went into the house. The countess, who heard at once from the maids what had happened at the lodge, was calmed by the thought that now their affairs would certainly improve, but on the other hand felt anxious as to the effect this excitement might have on her son. She went several times to his door on tiptoe and listened, as he lighted one pipe after another. Next day the old count called his son aside and, with an embarrassed smile, said to him: вҖңBut you know, my dear boy, itвҖҷs a pity you got excited! MГӯtenka has told me all about it.вҖқ вҖңI knew,вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, вҖңthat I should never understand anything in this crazy world.вҖқ вҖңYou were angry that he had not entered those 700 rubles. But they were carried forwardвҒ вҖ”and you did not look at the other page.вҖқ вҖңPapa, he is a blackguard and a thief! I know he is! And what I have done, I have done; but, if you like, I wonвҖҷt speak to him again.вҖқ вҖңNo, my dear boyвҖқ (the count, too, felt embarrassed. He knew he had mismanaged his wifeвҖҷs property and was to blame toward his children, but he did not know how to remedy it). вҖңNo, I beg you to attend to the business. I am old. IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңNo, Papa. Forgive me if I have caused you unpleasantness. I understand it all less than you do.вҖқ вҖңDevil take all these peasants, and money matters, and carryings forward from page to page,вҖқ he thought. вҖңI used to understand what a вҖҳcornerвҖҷ and the stakes at cards meant, but carrying forward to another page I donвҖҷt understand at all,вҖқ said he to himself, and after that he did not meddle in business affairs. But once the countess called her son and informed him that she had a promissory note from Anna MikhГЎylovna for two thousand rubles, and asked him what he thought of doing with it. вҖңThis,вҖқ answered NikolГЎy. вҖңYou say it rests with me. Well, I donвҖҷt like Anna MikhГЎylovna and I donвҖҷt like BorГӯs, but they were our friends and poor. Well then, this!вҖқ and he tore up the note, and by so doing caused the old countess to weep tears of joy. After that, young RostГіv took no further part in any business affairs, but devoted himself with passionate enthusiasm to what was to him a new pursuitвҒ вҖ”the chaseвҒ вҖ”for which his father kept a large establishment. III III The weather was already growing wintry and morning frosts congealed an earth saturated by autumn rains. The verdure had thickened and its bright green stood out sharply against the brownish strips of winter rye trodden down by the cattle, and against the pale-yellow stubble of the spring buckwheat. The wooded ravines and the copses, which at the end of August had still been green islands amid black fields and stubble, had become golden and bright-red islands amid the green winter rye. The hares had already half changed their summer coats, the fox cubs were beginning to scatter, and the young wolves were bigger than dogs. It was the best time of the year for the chase. The hounds of that ardent young sportsman RostГіv had not merely reached hard winter condition, but were so jaded that at a meeting of the huntsmen it was decided to give them a three daysвҖҷ rest and then, on the sixteenth of September, to go on a distant expedition, starting from the oak grove where there was an undisturbed litter of wolf cubs. All that day the hounds remained at home. It was frosty and the air was sharp, but toward evening the sky became overcast and it began to thaw. On the fifteenth, when young RostГіv, in his dressing gown, looked out of the window, he saw it was an unsurpassable morning for hunting: it was as if the sky were melting and sinking to the earth without any wind. The only motion in the air was that of the dripping, microscopic particles of drizzling mist. The bare twigs in the garden were hung with transparent drops which fell on the freshly fallen leaves. The earth in the kitchen garden looked wet and black and glistened like poppy seed and at a short distance merged into the dull, moist veil of mist. NikolГЎy went out into the wet and muddy porch. There was a smell of decaying leaves and of dog. MГӯlka, a black-spotted, broad-haunched bitch with prominent black eyes, got up on seeing her master, stretched her hind legs, lay down like a hare, and then suddenly jumped up and licked him right on his nose and mustache. Another borzoi, a dog, catching sight of his master from the garden path, arched his back and, rushing headlong toward the porch with lifted tail, began rubbing himself against his legs. вҖңO-hoy!вҖқ came at that moment, that inimitable huntsmanвҖҷs call which unites the deepest bass with the shrillest tenor, and round the corner came DanГӯlo the head huntsman and head kennelman, a gray, wrinkled old man with hair cut straight over his forehead, Ukrainian fashion, a long bent whip in his hand, and that look of independence and scorn of everything that is only seen in huntsmen. He doffed his Circassian cap to his master and looked at him scornfully. This scorn was not offensive to his master. NikolГЎy knew that this DanГӯlo, disdainful of everybody and who considered himself above them, was all the same his serf and huntsman. вҖңDanГӯlo!вҖқ NikolГЎy said timidly, conscious at the sight of the weather, the hounds, and the huntsman that he was being carried away by that irresistible passion for sport which makes a man forget all his previous resolutions, as a lover forgets in the presence of his mistress. вҖңWhat orders, your excellency?вҖқ said the huntsman in his deep bass, deep as a proto-deaconвҖҷs and hoarse with hallooingвҒ вҖ”and two flashing black eyes gazed from under his brows at his master, who was silent. вҖңCan you resist it?вҖқ those eyes seemed to be asking. вҖңItвҖҷs a good day, eh? For a hunt and a gallop, eh?вҖқ asked NikolГЎy, scratching MГӯlka behind the ears. DanГӯlo did not answer, but winked instead. вҖңI sent UvГЎrka at dawn to listen,вҖқ his bass boomed out after a minuteвҖҷs pause. вҖңHe says sheвҖҷs moved them into the OtrГЎdnoe enclosure. They were howling there.вҖқ (This meant that the she-wolf, about whom they both knew, had moved with her cubs to the OtrГЎdnoe copse, a small place a mile and a half from the house.) вҖңWe ought to go, donвҖҷt you think so?вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңCome to me with UvГЎrka.вҖқ вҖңAs you please.вҖқ вҖңThen put off feeding them.вҖқ вҖңYes, sir.вҖқ Five minutes later DanГӯlo and UvГЎrka were standing in NikolГЎyвҖҷs big study. Though DanГӯlo was not a big man, to see him in a room was like seeing a horse or a bear on the floor among the furniture and surroundings of human life. DanГӯlo himself felt this, and as usual stood just inside the door, trying to speak softly and not move, for fear of breaking something in the masterвҖҷs apartment, and he hastened to say all that was necessary so as to get from under that ceiling, out into the open under the sky once more. Having finished his inquiries and extorted from DanГӯlo an opinion that the hounds were fit (DanГӯlo himself wished to go hunting), NikolГЎy ordered the horses to be saddled. But just as DanГӯlo was about to go NatГЎsha came in with rapid steps, not having done up her hair or finished dressing and with her old nurseвҖҷs big shawl wrapped round her. PГ©tya ran in at the same time. вҖңYou are going?вҖқ asked NatГЎsha. вҖңI knew you would! SГіnya said you wouldnвҖҷt go, but I knew that today is the sort of day when you couldnвҖҷt help going.вҖқ вҖңYes, we are going,вҖқ replied NikolГЎy reluctantly, for today, as he intended to hunt seriously, he did not want to take NatГЎsha and PГ©tya. вҖңWe are going, but only wolf hunting: it would be dull for you.вҖқ вҖңYou know it is my greatest pleasure,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңItвҖҷs not fair; you are going by yourself, are having the horses saddled and said nothing to us about it.вҖқ вҖңвҖҠвҖҳNo barrier bars a RussianвҖҷs pathвҖҷвҒ вҖ”weвҖҷll go!вҖқ shouted PГ©tya. вҖңBut you canвҖҷt. Mamma said you mustnвҖҷt,вҖқ said NikolГЎy to NatГЎsha. вҖңYes, IвҖҷll go. I shall certainly go,вҖқ said NatГЎsha decisively. вҖңDanГӯlo, tell them to saddle for us, and MikhГЎilo must come with my dogs,вҖқ she added to the huntsman. It seemed to DanГӯlo irksome and improper to be in a room at all, but to have anything to do with a young lady seemed to him impossible.
