He cast down his eyes and hurried out as if it were none of his business, careful as he went not to inflict any accidental injury on the young lady. IV IV The old count, who had always kept up an enormous hunting establishment but had now handed it all completely over to his sonвҖҷs care, being in very good spirits on this fifteenth of September, prepared to go out with the others. In an hourвҖҷs time the whole hunting party was at the porch. NikolГЎy, with a stern and serious air which showed that now was no time for attending to trifles, went past NatГЎsha and PГ©tya who were trying to tell him something. He had a look at all the details of the hunt, sent a pack of hounds and huntsmen on ahead to find the quarry, mounted his chestnut DonГ©ts, and whistling to his own leash of borzois, set off across the threshing ground to a field leading to the OtrГЎdnoe wood. The old countвҖҷs horse, a sorrel gelding called ViflyГЎnka, was led by the groom in attendance on him, while the count himself was to drive in a small trap straight to a spot reserved for him. They were taking fifty-four hounds, with six hunt attendants and whippers-in. Besides the family, there were eight borzoi kennelmen and more than forty borzois, so that, with the borzois on the leash belonging to members of the family, there were about a hundred and thirty dogs and twenty horsemen. Each dog knew its master and its call. Each man in the hunt knew his business, his place, what he had to do. As soon as they had passed the fence they all spread out evenly and quietly, without noise or talk, along the road and field leading to the OtrГЎdnoe covert. The horses stepped over the field as over a thick carpet, now and then splashing into puddles as they crossed a road. The misty sky still seemed to descend evenly and imperceptibly toward the earth, the air was still, warm, and silent. Occasionally the whistle of a huntsman, the snort of a horse, the crack of a whip, or the whine of a straggling hound could be heard. When they had gone a little less than a mile, five more riders with dogs appeared out of the mist, approaching the RostГіvs. In front rode a fresh-looking, handsome old man with a large gray mustache. вҖңGood morning, Uncle!вҖқ said NikolГЎy, when the old man drew near. вҖңThatвҖҷs it. Come on!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I was sure of it,вҖқ began вҖңUncle.вҖқ (He was a distant relative of the RostГіvsвҖҷ, a man of small means, and their neighbor.) вҖңI knew you wouldnвҖҷt be able to resist it and itвҖҷs a good thing youвҖҷre going. ThatвҖҷs it! Come on!вҖқ (This was вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ favorite expression.) вҖңTake the covert at once, for my GГӯrchik says the IlГЎgins are at KornikГӯ with their hounds. ThatвҖҷs it. Come on!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ TheyвҖҷll take the cubs from under your very nose.вҖқ вҖңThatвҖҷs where IвҖҷm going. Shall we join up our packs?вҖқ asked NikolГЎy. The hounds were joined into one pack, and вҖңUncleвҖқ and NikolГЎy rode on side by side. NatГЎsha, muffled up in shawls which did not hide her eager face and shining eyes, galloped up to them. She was followed by PГ©tya who always kept close to her, by MikhГЎilo, a huntsman, and by a groom appointed to look after her. PГ©tya, who was laughing, whipped and pulled at his horse. NatГЎsha sat easily and confidently on her black ArГЎbchik and reined him in without effort with a firm hand. вҖңUncleвҖқ looked round disapprovingly at PГ©tya and NatГЎsha. He did not like to combine frivolity with the serious business of hunting. вҖңGood morning, Uncle! We are going too!вҖқ shouted PГ©tya. вҖңGood morning, good morning! But donвҖҷt go overriding the hounds,вҖқ said вҖңUncleвҖқ sternly. вҖңNikГіlenka, what a fine dog TrunГӯla is! He knew me,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, referring to her favorite hound. вҖңIn the first place, TrunГӯla is not a вҖҳdog,вҖҷ but a harrier,вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, and looked sternly at his sister, trying to make her feel the distance that ought to separate them at that moment. NatГЎsha understood it. вҖңYou mustnвҖҷt think weвҖҷll be in anyoneвҖҷs way, Uncle,вҖқ she said. вҖңWeвҖҷll go to our places and wonвҖҷt budge.вҖқ вҖңA good thing too, little countess,вҖқ said вҖңUncle,вҖқ вҖңonly mind you donвҖҷt fall off your horse,вҖқ he added, вҖңbecauseвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs it, come on!вҒ вҖ”youвҖҷve nothing to hold on to.вҖқ The oasis of the OtrГЎdnoe covert came in sight a few hundred yards off, the huntsmen were already nearing it. RostГіv, having finally settled with вҖңUncleвҖқ where they should set on the hounds, and having shown NatГЎsha where she was to standвҒ вҖ”a spot where nothing could possibly run outвҒ вҖ”went round above the ravine. вҖңWell, nephew, youвҖҷre going for a big wolf,вҖқ said вҖңUncle.вҖқ вҖңMind and donвҖҷt let her slip!вҖқ вҖңThatвҖҷs as may happen,вҖқ answered RostГіv. вҖңKarГЎy, here!вҖқ he shouted, answering вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ remark by this call to his borzoi. KarГЎy was a shaggy old dog with a hanging jowl, famous for having tackled a big wolf unaided. They all took up their places. The old count, knowing his sonвҖҷs ardor in the hunt, hurried so as not to be late, and the huntsmen had not yet reached their places when Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich, cheerful, flushed, and with quivering cheeks, drove up with his black horses over the winter rye to the place reserved for him, where a wolf might come out. Having straightened his coat and fastened on his hunting knives and horn, he mounted his good, sleek, well-fed, and comfortable horse, ViflyГЎnka, which was turning gray, like himself. His horses and trap were sent home. Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich, though not at heart a keen sportsman, knew the rules of the hunt well, and rode to the bushy edge of the road where he was to stand, arranged his reins, settled himself in the saddle, and, feeling that he was ready, looked about with a smile. Beside him was SemГ«n ChekmГЎr, his personal attendant, an old horseman now somewhat stiff in the saddle. ChekmГЎr held in leash three formidable wolfhounds, who had, however, grown fat like their master and his horse. Two wise old dogs lay down unleashed. Some hundred paces farther along the edge of the wood stood MГӯtka, the countвҖҷs other groom, a daring horseman and keen rider to hounds. Before the hunt, by old custom, the count had drunk a silver cupful of mulled brandy, taken a snack, and washed it down with half a bottle of his favorite Bordeaux. He was somewhat flushed with the wine and the drive. His eyes were rather moist and glittered more than usual, and as he sat in his saddle, wrapped up in his fur coat, he looked like a child taken out for an outing. The thin, hollow-cheeked ChekmГЎr, having got everything ready, kept glancing at his master with whom he had lived on the best of terms for thirty years, and understanding the mood he was in expected a pleasant chat. A third person rode up circumspectly through the wood (it was plain that he had had a lesson) and stopped behind the count. This person was a gray-bearded old man in a womanвҖҷs cloak, with a tall peaked cap on his head. He was the buffoon, who went by a womanвҖҷs name, NastГЎsya IvГЎnovna. вҖңWell, NastГЎsya IvГЎnovna!вҖқ whispered the count, winking at him. вҖңIf you scare away the beast, DanГӯloвҖҷll give it you!вҖқ вҖңI know a thing or two myself!вҖқ said NastГЎsya IvГЎnovna. вҖңHush!вҖқ whispered the count and turned to SemГ«n. вҖңHave you seen the young countess?вҖқ he asked. вҖңWhere is she?вҖқ вҖңWith Pyotr IlГҪnich, by the ZhГЎrov rank grass,вҖқ answered SemГ«n, smiling. вҖңThough sheвҖҷs a lady, sheвҖҷs very fond of hunting.вҖқ вҖңAnd youвҖҷre surprised at the way she rides, SemГ«n, eh?вҖқ said the count. вҖңSheвҖҷs as good as many a man!вҖқ вҖңOf course! ItвҖҷs marvelous. So bold, so easy!вҖқ вҖңAnd NikolГЎsha? Where is he? By the LyГЎdov upland, isnвҖҷt he?вҖқ вҖңYes, sir. He knows where to stand. He understands the matter so well that DanГӯlo and I are often quite astounded,вҖқ said SemГ«n, well knowing what would please his master. вҖңRides well, eh? And how well he looks on his horse, eh?вҖқ вҖңA perfect picture! How he chased a fox out of the rank grass by the ZavГЎrzinsk thicket the other day! Leaped a fearful place; what a sight when they rushed from the covertвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the horse worth a thousand rubles and the rider beyond all price! Yes, one would have to search far to find another as smart.вҖқ вҖңTo search farвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ repeated the count, evidently sorry SemГ«n had not said more. вҖңTo search far,вҖқ he said, turning back the skirt of his coat to get at his snuffbox. вҖңThe other day when he came out from Mass in full uniform, MikhГЎil SidГіrychвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ SemГ«n did not finish, for on the still air he had distinctly caught the music of the hunt with only two or three hounds giving tongue. He bent down his head and listened, shaking a warning finger at his master. вҖңThey are on the scent of the cubsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he whispered, вҖңstraight to the LyГЎdov uplands.вҖқ The count, forgetting to smooth out the smile on his face, looked into the distance straight before him, down the narrow open space, holding the snuffbox in his hand but not taking any. After the cry of the hounds came the deep tones of the wolf call from DanГӯloвҖҷs hunting horn; the pack joined the first three hounds and they could be heard in full cry, with that peculiar lift in the note that indicates that they are after a wolf. The whippers-in no longer set on the hounds, but changed to the cry of ulyulyu, and above the others rose DanГӯloвҖҷs voice, now a deep bass, now piercingly shrill. His voice seemed to fill the whole wood and carried far beyond out into the open field. After listening a few moments in silence, the count and his attendant convinced themselves that the hounds had separated into two packs: the sound of the larger pack, eagerly giving tongue, began to die away in the distance, the other pack rushed by the wood past the count, and it was with this that DanГӯloвҖҷs voice was heard calling ulyulyu. The sounds of both packs mingled and broke apart again, but both were becoming more distant. SemГ«n sighed and stooped to straighten the leash a young borzoi had entangled; the count too sighed and, noticing the snuffbox in his hand, opened it and took a pinch. вҖңBack!вҖқ cried SemГ«n to a borzoi that was pushing forward out of the wood. The count started and dropped the snuffbox. NastГЎsya IvГЎnovna dismounted to pick it up. The count and SemГ«n were looking at him. Then, unexpectedly, as often happens, the sound of the hunt suddenly approached, as if the hounds in full cry and DanГӯlo ulyulyuing were just in front of them. The count turned and saw on his right MГӯtka staring at him with eyes starting out of his head, raising his cap and pointing before him to the other side. вҖңLook out!вҖқ he shouted, in a voice plainly showing that he had long fretted to utter that word, and letting the borzois slip he galloped toward the count. The count and SemГ«n galloped out of the wood and saw on their left a wolf which, softly swaying from side to side, was coming at a quiet lope farther to the left to the very place where they were standing. The angry borzois whined and getting free of the leash rushed past the horsesвҖҷ feet at the wolf. The wolf paused, turned its heavy forehead toward the dogs awkwardly, like a man suffering from the quinsy, and, still slightly swaying from side to side, gave a couple of leaps and with a swish of its tail disappeared into the skirt of the wood. At the same instant, with a cry like a wail, first one hound, then another, and then another, sprang helter-skelter from the wood opposite and the whole pack rushed across the field toward the very spot where the wolf had disappeared. The hazel bushes parted behind the hounds and DanГӯloвҖҷs chestnut horse appeared, dark with sweat. On its long back sat DanГӯlo, hunched forward, capless, his disheveled gray hair hanging over his flushed, perspiring face. вҖңUlyulyulyu! ulyulyu!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he cried. When he caught sight of the count his eyes flashed lightning. вҖңBlast you!вҖқ he shouted, holding up his whip threateningly at the count. вҖңYouвҖҷve let the wolf go!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What sportsmen!вҖқ and as if scorning to say more to the frightened and shamefaced count, he lashed the heaving flanks of his sweating chestnut gelding with all the anger the count had aroused and flew off after the hounds. The count, like a punished schoolboy, looked round, trying by a smile to win SemГ«nвҖҷs sympathy for his plight. But SemГ«n was no longer there. He was galloping round by the bushes while the field was coming up on both sides, all trying to head the wolf, but it vanished into the wood before they could do so. V V NikolГЎy RostГіv meanwhile remained at his post, waiting for the wolf. By the way the hunt approached and receded, by the cries of the dogs whose notes were familiar to him, by the way the voices of the huntsmen approached, receded, and rose, he realized what was happening at the copse. He knew that young and old wolves were there, that the hounds had separated into two packs, that somewhere a wolf was being chased, and that something had gone wrong. He expected the wolf to come his way any moment. He made thousands of different conjectures as to where and from what side the beast would come and how he would set upon it. Hope alternated with despair. Several times he addressed a prayer to God that the wolf should come his way. He prayed with that passionate and shamefaced feeling with which men pray at moments of great excitement arising from trivial causes. вҖңWhat would it be to Thee to do this for me?вҖқ he said to God. вҖңI know Thou art great, and that it is a sin to ask this of Thee, but for GodвҖҷs sake do let the old wolf come my way and let KarГЎy spring at itвҒ вҖ”in sight of вҖҳUncleвҖҷ who is watching from over thereвҒ вҖ”and seize it by the throat in a death grip!вҖқ A thousand times during that half-hour RostГіv cast eager and restless glances over the edge of the wood, with the two scraggy oaks rising above the aspen undergrowth and the gully with its water-worn side and вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ cap just visible above the bush on his right. вҖңNo, I shanвҖҷt have such luck,вҖқ thought RostГіv, вҖңyet what wouldnвҖҷt it be worth! It is not to be! Everywhere, at cards and in war, I am always unlucky.вҖқ Memories of Austerlitz and of DГіlokhov flashed rapidly and clearly through his mind. вҖңOnly once in my life to get an old wolf, I want only that!вҖқ thought he, straining eyes and ears and looking to the left and then to the right and listening to the slightest variation of note in the cries of the dogs. Again he looked to the right and saw something running toward him across the deserted field. вҖңNo, it canвҖҷt be!вҖқ thought RostГіv, taking a deep breath, as a man does at the coming of something long hoped for. The height of happiness was reachedвҒ вҖ”and so simply, without warning, or noise, or display, that RostГіv could not believe his eyes and remained in doubt for over a second. The wolf ran forward and jumped heavily over a gully that lay in her path. She was an old animal with a gray back and big reddish belly. She ran without hurry, evidently feeling sure that no one saw her. RostГіv, holding his breath, looked round at the borzois. They stood or lay not seeing the wolf or understanding the situation. Old KarГЎy had turned his head and was angrily searching for fleas, baring his yellow teeth and snapping at his hind legs. вҖңUlyulyulyu!вҖқ whispered RostГіv, pouting his lips. The borzois jumped up, jerking the rings of the leashes and pricking their ears. KarГЎy finished scratching his hindquarters and, cocking his ears, got up with quivering tail from which tufts of matted hair hung down. вҖңShall I loose them or not?вҖқ NikolГЎy asked himself as the wolf approached him coming from the copse. Suddenly the wolfвҖҷs whole physiognomy changed: she shuddered, seeing what she had probably never seen beforeвҒ вҖ”human eyes fixed upon herвҒ вҖ”and turning her head a little toward RostГіv, she paused. вҖңBack or forward? Eh, no matter, forwardвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ the wolf seemed to say to herself, and she moved forward without again looking round and with a quiet, long, easy yet resolute lope. вҖңUlyulyu!вҖқ cried NikolГЎy, in a voice not his own, and of its own accord his good horse darted headlong downhill, leaping over gullies to head off the wolf, and the borzois passed it, running faster still. NikolГЎy did not hear his own cry nor feel that he was galloping, nor see the borzois, nor the ground over which he went: he saw only the wolf, who, increasing her speed, bounded on in the same direction along the hollow. The first to come into view was MГӯlka, with her black markings and powerful quarters, gaining upon the wolf. Nearer and nearerвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ now she was ahead of it; but the wolf turned its head to face her, and instead of putting on speed as she usually did MГӯlka suddenly raised her tail and stiffened her forelegs. вҖңUlyulyulyulyu!вҖқ shouted NikolГЎy. The reddish LyubГӯm rushed forward from behind MГӯlka, sprang impetuously at the wolf, and seized it by its hindquarters, but immediately jumped aside in terror. The wolf crouched, gnashed her teeth, and again rose and bounded forward, followed at the distance of a couple of feet by all the borzois, who did not get any closer to her. вҖңSheвҖҷll get away! No, itвҖҷs impossible!вҖқ thought NikolГЎy, still shouting with a hoarse voice. вҖңKarГЎy, ulyulyu!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he shouted, looking round for the old borzoi who was now his only hope. KarГЎy, with all the strength age had left him, stretched himself to the utmost and, watching the wolf, galloped heavily aside to intercept it. But the quickness of the wolfвҖҷs lope and the borzoiвҖҷs slower pace made it plain that KarГЎy had miscalculated. NikolГЎy could already see not far in front of him the wood where the wolf would certainly escape should she reach it. But, coming toward him, he saw hounds and a huntsman galloping almost straight at the wolf. There was still hope. A long, yellowish young borzoi, one NikolГЎy did not know, from another leash, rushed impetuously at the wolf from in front and almost knocked her over. But the wolf jumped up more quickly than anyone could have expected and, gnashing her teeth, flew at the yellowish borzoi, which, with a piercing yelp, fell with its head on the ground, bleeding from a gash in its side. вҖңKarГЎy? Old fellow!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ wailed NikolГЎy. Thanks to the delay caused by this crossing of the wolfвҖҷs path, the old dog with its felted hair hanging from its thigh was within five paces of it. As if aware of her danger, the wolf turned her eyes on KarГЎy, tucked her tail yet further between her legs, and increased her speed. But here NikolГЎy only saw that something happened to KarГЎyвҒ вҖ”the borzoi was suddenly on the wolf, and they rolled together down into a gully just in front of them. That instant, when NikolГЎy saw the wolf struggling in the gully with the dogs, while from under them could be seen her gray hair and outstretched hind leg and her frightened choking head, with her ears laid back (KarГЎy was pinning her by the throat), was the happiest moment of his life. With his hand on his saddlebow, he was ready to dismount and stab the wolf, when she suddenly thrust her head up from among that mass of dogs, and then her forepaws were on the edge of the gully. She clicked her teeth (KarГЎy no longer had her by the throat), leaped with a movement of her hind legs out of the gully, and having disengaged herself from the dogs, with tail tucked in again, went forward. KarГЎy, his hair bristling, and probably bruised or wounded, climbed with difficulty out of the gully. вҖңOh my God! Why?вҖқ NikolГЎy cried in despair. вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ huntsman was galloping from the other side across the wolfвҖҷs path and his borzois once more stopped the animalвҖҷs advance. She was again hemmed in. NikolГЎy and his attendant, with вҖңUncleвҖқ and his huntsman, were all riding round the wolf, crying вҖңulyulyu!вҖқ shouting and preparing to dismount each moment that the wolf crouched back, and starting forward again every time she shook herself and moved toward the wood where she would be safe. Already, at the beginning of this chase, DanГӯlo, hearing the ulyulyuing, had rushed out from the wood. He saw KarГЎy seize the wolf, and checked his horse, supposing the affair to be over. But when he saw that the horsemen did not dismount and that the wolf shook herself and ran for safety, DanГӯlo set his chestnut galloping, not at the wolf but straight toward the wood, just as KarГЎy had run to cut the animal off. As a result of this, he galloped up to the wolf just when she had been stopped a second time by вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ borzois. DanГӯlo galloped up silently, holding a naked dagger in his left hand and thrashing the laboring sides of his chestnut horse with his whip as if it were a flail. NikolГЎy neither saw nor heard DanГӯlo until the chestnut, breathing heavily, panted past him, and he heard the fall of a body and saw DanГӯlo lying on the wolfвҖҷs back among the dogs, trying to seize her by the ears. It was evident to the dogs, the hunters, and to the wolf herself that all was now over. The terrified wolf pressed back her ears and tried to rise, but the borzois stuck to her. DanГӯlo rose a little, took a step, and with his whole weight, as if lying down to rest, fell on the wolf, seizing her by the ears. NikolГЎy was about to stab her, but DanГӯlo whispered, вҖңDonвҖҷt! WeвҖҷll gag her!вҖқ and, changing his position, set his foot on the wolfвҖҷs neck. A stick was thrust between her jaws and she was fastened with a leash, as if bridled, her legs were bound together, and DanГӯlo rolled her over once or twice from side to side. With happy, exhausted faces, they laid the old wolf, alive, on a shying and snorting horse and, accompanied by the dogs yelping at her, took her to the place where they were all to meet. The hounds had killed two of the cubs and the borzois three. The huntsmen assembled with their booty and their stories, and all came to look at the wolf, which, with her broad-browed head hanging down and the bitten stick between her jaws, gazed with great glassy eyes at this crowd of dogs and men surrounding her. When she was touched, she jerked her bound legs and looked wildly yet simply at everybody. Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich also rode up and touched the wolf. вҖңOh, what a formidable one!вҖқ said he. вҖңA formidable one, eh?вҖқ he asked DanГӯlo, who was standing near. вҖңYes, your excellency,вҖқ answered DanГӯlo, quickly doffing his cap. The count remembered the wolf he had let slip and his encounter with DanГӯlo. вҖңAh, but you are a crusty fellow, friend!вҖқ said the count. For sole reply DanГӯlo gave him a shy, childlike, meek, and amiable smile. VI VI The old count went home, and NatГЎsha and PГ©tya promised to return very soon, but as it was still early the hunt went farther. At midday they put the hounds into a ravine thickly overgrown with young trees. NikolГЎy standing in a fallow field could see all his whips. Facing him lay a field of winter rye, there his own huntsman stood alone in a hollow behind a hazel bush. The hounds had scarcely been loosed before NikolГЎy heard one he knew, VoltГіrn, giving tongue at intervals; other hounds joined in, now pausing and now again giving tongue. A moment later he heard a cry from the wooded ravine that a fox had been found, and the whole pack, joining together, rushed along the ravine toward the ryefield and away from NikolГЎy. He saw the whips in their red caps galloping along the edge of the ravine, he even saw the hounds, and was expecting a fox to show itself at any moment on the ryefield opposite. The huntsman standing in the hollow moved and loosed his borzois, and NikolГЎy saw a queer, short-legged red fox with a fine brush going hard across the field. The borzois bore down on it.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Now they drew close to the fox which began to dodge between the field in sharper and sharper curves, trailing its brush, when suddenly a strange white borzoi dashed in followed by a black one, and everything was in confusion; the borzois formed a star-shaped figure, scarcely swaying their bodies and with tails turned away from the center of the group. Two huntsmen galloped up to the dogs; one in a red cap, the other, a stranger, in a green coat. вҖңWhatвҖҷs this?вҖқ thought NikolГЎy. вҖңWhereвҖҷs that huntsman from? He is not вҖҳUncleвҖҷsвҖҷ man.вҖқ The huntsmen got the fox, but stayed there a long time without strapping it to the saddle. Their horses, bridled and with high saddles, stood near them and there too the dogs were lying. The huntsmen waved their arms and did something to the fox. Then from that spot came the sound of a horn, with the signal agreed on in case of a fight. вҖңThatвҖҷs IlГЎginвҖҷs huntsman having a row with our IvГЎn,вҖқ said NikolГЎyвҖҷs groom. NikolГЎy sent the man to call NatГЎsha and PГ©tya to him, and rode at a footpace to the place where the whips were getting the hounds together. Several of the field galloped to the spot where the fight was going on. NikolГЎy dismounted, and with NatГЎsha and PГ©tya, who had ridden up, stopped near the hounds, waiting to see how the matter would end. Out of the bushes came the huntsman who had been fighting and rode toward his young master, with the fox tied to his crupper. While still at a distance he took off his cap and tried to speak respectfully, but he was pale and breathless and his face was angry. One of his eyes was black, but he probably was not even aware of it. вҖңWhat has happened?вҖқ asked NikolГЎy. вҖңA likely thing, killing a fox our dogs had hunted! And it was my gray bitch that caught it! Go to law, indeed!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ He snatches at the fox! I gave him one with the fox. Here it is on my saddle! Do you want a taste of this?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said the huntsman, pointing to his dagger and probably imagining himself still speaking to his foe. NikolГЎy, not stopping to talk to the man, asked his sister and PГ©tya to wait for him and rode to the spot where the enemyвҖҷs, IlГЎginвҖҷs, hunting party was. The victorious huntsman rode off to join the field, and there, surrounded by inquiring sympathizers, recounted his exploits. The facts were that IlГЎgin, with whom the RostГіvs had a quarrel and were at law, hunted over places that belonged by custom to the RostГіvs, and had now, as if purposely, sent his men to the very woods the RostГіvs were hunting and let his man snatch a fox their dogs had chased. NikolГЎy, though he had never seen IlГЎgin, with his usual absence of moderation in judgment, hated him cordially from reports of his arbitrariness and violence, and regarded him as his bitterest foe. He rode in angry agitation toward him, firmly grasping his whip and fully prepared to take the most resolute and desperate steps to punish his enemy. Hardly had he passed an angle of the wood before a stout gentleman in a beaver cap came riding toward him on a handsome raven-black horse, accompanied by two hunt servants. Instead of an enemy, NikolГЎy found in IlГЎgin a stately and courteous gentleman who was particularly anxious to make the young countвҖҷs acquaintance. Having ridden up to NikolГЎy, IlГЎgin raised his beaver cap and said he much regretted what had occurred and would have the man punished who had allowed himself to seize a fox hunted by someone elseвҖҷs borzois. He hoped to become better acquainted with the count and invited him to draw his covert. NatГЎsha, afraid that her brother would do something dreadful, had followed him in some excitement. Seeing the enemies exchanging friendly greetings, she rode up to them. IlГЎgin lifted his beaver cap still higher to NatГЎsha and said, with a pleasant smile, that the young countess resembled Diana in her passion for the chase as well as in her beauty, of which he had heard much. To expiate his huntsmanвҖҷs offense, IlГЎgin pressed the RostГіvs to come to an upland of his about a mile away which he usually kept for himself and which, he said, swarmed with hares. NikolГЎy agreed, and the hunt, now doubled, moved on. The way to IliginвҖҷs upland was across the fields. The hunt servants fell into line. The masters rode together. вҖңUncle,вҖқ RostГіv, and IlГЎgin kept stealthily glancing at one anotherвҖҷs dogs, trying not to be observed by their companions and searching uneasily for rivals to their own borzois. RostГіv was particularly struck by the beauty of a small, purebred, red-spotted bitch on IlГЎginвҖҷs leash, slender but with muscles like steel, a delicate muzzle, and prominent black eyes. He had heard of the swiftness of IlГЎginвҖҷs borzois, and in that beautiful bitch saw a rival to his own MГӯlka. In the middle of a sober conversation begun by IlГЎgin about the yearвҖҷs harvest, NikolГЎy pointed to the red-spotted bitch. вҖңA fine little bitch, that!вҖқ said he in a careless tone. вҖңIs she swift?вҖқ вҖңThat one? Yes, sheвҖҷs a good dog, gets what sheвҖҷs after,вҖқ answered IlГЎgin indifferently, of the red-spotted bitch ErzГЎ, for which, a year before, he had given a neighbor three families of house serfs. вҖңSo in your parts, too, the harvest is nothing to boast of, Count?вҖқ he went on, continuing the conversation they had begun. And considering it polite to return the young countвҖҷs compliment, IlГЎgin looked at his borzois and picked out MГӯlka who attracted his attention by her breadth. вҖңThat black-spotted one of yours is fineвҒ вҖ”well shaped!вҖқ said he. вҖңYes, sheвҖҷs fast enough,вҖқ replied NikolГЎy, and thought: вҖңIf only a full-grown hare would cross the field now IвҖҷd show you what sort of borzoi she is,вҖқ and turning to his groom, he said he would give a ruble to anyone who found a hare. вҖңI donвҖҷt understand,вҖқ continued IlГЎgin, вҖңhow some sportsmen can be so jealous about game and dogs. For myself, I can tell you, Count, I enjoy riding in company such as thisвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ what could be better?вҖқ (he again raised his cap to NatГЎsha) вҖңbut as for counting skins and what one takes, I donвҖҷt care about that.вҖқ вҖңOf course not!вҖқ вҖңOr being upset because someone elseвҖҷs borzoi and not mine catches something. All I care about is to enjoy seeing the chase, is it not so, Count? For I consider thatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңA-tu!вҖқ came the long-drawn cry of one of the borzoi whippers-in, who had halted. He stood on a knoll in the stubble, holding his whip aloft, and again repeated his long-drawn cry, вҖңA-tu!вҖқ (This call and the uplifted whip meant that he saw a sitting hare.) вҖңAh, he has found one, I think,вҖқ said IlГЎgin carelessly. вҖңYes, we must ride up.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Shall we both course it?вҖқ answered NikolГЎy, seeing in ErzГЎ and вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ red RugГЎy two rivals he had never yet had a chance of pitting against his own borzois. вҖңAnd suppose they outdo my MГӯlka at once!вҖқ he thought as he rode with вҖңUncleвҖқ and IlГЎgin toward the hare. вҖңA full-grown one?вҖқ asked IlГЎgin as he approached the whip who had sighted the hareвҒ вҖ”and not without agitation he looked round and whistled to ErzГЎ. вҖңAnd you, MikhГЎil NikanГіrovich?вҖқ he said, addressing вҖңUncle.вҖқ The latter was riding with a sullen expression on his face. вҖңHow can I join in? Why, youвҖҷve given a village for each of your borzois! ThatвҖҷs it, come on! Yours are worth thousands. Try yours against one another, you two, and IвҖҷll look on!вҖқ вҖңRugГЎy, hey, hey!вҖқ he shouted. вҖңRugГЎyushka!вҖқ he added, involuntarily by this diminutive expressing his affection and the hopes he placed on this red borzoi. NatГЎsha saw and felt the agitation the two elderly men and her brother were trying to conceal, and was herself excited by it. The huntsman stood halfway up the knoll holding up his whip and the gentlefolk rode up to him at a footpace; the hounds that were far off on the horizon turned away from the hare, and the whips, but not the gentlefolk, also moved away. All were moving slowly and sedately. вҖңHow is it pointing?вҖқ asked NikolГЎy, riding a hundred paces toward the whip who had sighted the hare. But before the whip could reply, the hare, scenting the frost coming next morning, was unable to rest and leaped up. The pack on leash rushed downhill in full cry after the hare, and from all sides the borzois that were not on leash darted after the hounds and the hare. All the hunt, who had been moving slowly, shouted, вҖңStop!вҖқ calling in the hounds, while the borzoi whips, with a cry of вҖңA-tu!вҖқ galloped across the field setting the borzois on the hare. The tranquil IlГЎgin, NikolГЎy, NatГЎsha, and вҖңUncleвҖқ flew, reckless of where and how they went, seeing only the borzois and the hare and fearing only to lose sight even for an instant of the chase. The hare they had started was a strong and swift one. When he jumped up he did not run at once, but pricked his ears listening to the shouting and trampling that resounded from all sides at once. He took a dozen bounds, not very quickly, letting the borzois gain on him, and, finally having chosen his direction and realized his danger, laid back his ears and rushed off headlong. He had been lying in the stubble, but in front of him was the autumn sowing where the ground was soft. The two borzois of the huntsman who had sighted him, having been the nearest, were the first to see and pursue him, but they had not gone far before IlГЎginвҖҷs red-spotted ErzГЎ passed them, got within a length, flew at the hare with terrible swiftness aiming at his scut, and, thinking she had seized him, rolled over like a ball. The hare arched his back and bounded off yet more swiftly. From behind ErzГЎ rushed the broad-haunched, black-spotted MГӯlka and began rapidly gaining on the hare. вҖңMilГЎshka, dear!вҖқ rose NikolГЎyвҖҷs triumphant cry. It looked as if MГӯlka would immediately pounce on the hare, but she overtook him and flew past. The hare had squatted. Again the beautiful ErzГЎ reached him, but when close to the hareвҖҷs scut paused as if measuring the distance, so as not to make a mistake this time but seize his hind leg. вҖңErzГЎ, darling!вҖқ IlГЎgin wailed in a voice unlike his own. ErzГЎ did not hearken to his appeal. At the very moment when she would have seized her prey, the hare moved and darted along the balk between the winter rye and the stubble. Again ErzГЎ and MГӯlka were abreast, running like a pair of carriage horses, and began to overtake the hare, but it was easier for the hare to run on the balk and the borzois did not overtake him so quickly. вҖңRugГЎy, RugГЎyushka! ThatвҖҷs it, come on!вҖқ came a third voice just then, and вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ red borzoi, straining and curving its back, caught up with the two foremost borzois, pushed ahead of them regardless of the terrible strain, put on speed close to the hare, knocked it off the balk onto the ryefield, again put on speed still more viciously, sinking to his knees in the muddy field, and all one could see was how, muddying his back, he rolled over with the hare. A ring of borzois surrounded him. A moment later everyone had drawn up round the crowd of dogs. Only the delighted вҖңUncleвҖқ dismounted, and cut off a pad, shaking the hare for the blood to drip off, and anxiously glancing round with restless eyes while his arms and legs twitched. He spoke without himself knowing whom to or what about. вҖңThatвҖҷs it, come on! ThatвҖҷs a dog!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ There, it has beaten them all, the thousand-ruble as well as the one-ruble borzois. ThatвҖҷs it, come on!вҖқ said he, panting and looking wrathfully around as if he were abusing someone, as if they were all his enemies and had insulted him, and only now had he at last succeeded in justifying himself. вҖңThere are your thousand-ruble ones.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ThatвҖҷs it, come on!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңRugГЎy, hereвҖҷs a pad for you!вҖқ he said, throwing down the hareвҖҷs muddy pad. вҖңYouвҖҷve deserved it, thatвҖҷs it, come on!вҖқ вҖңSheвҖҷd tired herself out, sheвҖҷd run it down three times by herself,вҖқ said NikolГЎy, also not listening to anyone and regardless of whether he were heard or not. вҖңBut what is there in running across it like that?вҖқ said IlГЎginвҖҷs groom. вҖңOnce she had missed it and turned it away, any mongrel could take it,вҖқ IlГЎgin was saying at the same time, breathless from his gallop and his excitement. At the same moment NatГЎsha, without drawing breath, screamed joyously, ecstatically, and so piercingly that it set everyoneвҖҷs ear tingling. By that shriek she expressed what the others expressed by all talking at once, and it was so strange that she must herself have been ashamed of so wild a cry and everyone else would have been amazed at it at any other time. вҖңUncleвҖқ himself twisted up the hare, threw it neatly and smartly across his horseвҖҷs back as if by that gesture he meant to rebuke everybody, and, with an air of not wishing to speak to anyone, mounted his bay and rode off. The others all followed, dispirited and shamefaced, and only much later were they able to regain their former affectation of indifference. For a long time they continued to look at red RugГЎy who, his arched back spattered with mud and clanking the ring of his leash, walked along just behind вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ horse with the serene air of a conqueror. вҖңWell, I am like any other dog as long as itвҖҷs not a question of coursing. But when it is, then look out!вҖқ his appearance seemed to NikolГЎy to be saying. When, much later, вҖңUncleвҖқ rode up to NikolГЎy and began talking to him, he felt flattered that, after what had happened, вҖңUncleвҖқ deigned to speak to him. VII VII Toward evening IlГЎgin took leave of NikolГЎy, who found that they were so far from home that he accepted вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ offer that the hunting party should spend the night in his little village of MikhГЎylovna. вҖңAnd if you put up at my house that will be better still. ThatвҖҷs it, come on!вҖқ said вҖңUncle.вҖқ вҖңYou see itвҖҷs damp weather, and you could rest, and the little countess could be driven home in a trap.вҖқ вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ offer was accepted. A huntsman was sent to OtrГЎdnoe for a trap, while NikolГЎy rode with NatГЎsha and PГ©tya to вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ house. Some five male domestic serfs, big and little, rushed out to the front porch to meet their master. A score of women serfs, old and young, as well as children, popped out from the back entrance to have a look at the hunters who were arriving. The presence of NatГЎshaвҒ вҖ”a woman, a lady, and on horsebackвҒ вҖ”raised the curiosity of the serfs to such a degree that many of them came up to her, stared her in the face, and unabashed by her presence made remarks about her as though she were some prodigy on show and not a human being able to hear or understand what was said about her. вҖңArГӯnka! Look, she sits sideways! There she sits and her skirt dangles.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ See, sheвҖҷs got a little hunting horn!вҖқ вҖңGoodness gracious! See her knife?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңIsnвҖҷt she a Tartar!вҖқ вҖңHow is it you didnвҖҷt go head over heels?вҖқ asked the boldest of all, addressing NatГЎsha directly. вҖңUncleвҖқ dismounted at the porch of his little wooden house which stood in the midst of an overgrown garden and, after a glance at his retainers, shouted authoritatively that the superfluous ones should take themselves off and that all necessary preparations should be made to receive the guests and the visitors. The serfs all dispersed. вҖңUncleвҖқ lifted NatГЎsha off her horse and taking her hand led her up the rickety wooden steps of the porch. The house, with its bare, unplastered log walls, was not overcleanвҒ вҖ”it did not seem that those living in it aimed at keeping it spotlessвҒ вҖ”but neither was it noticeably neglected. In the entry there was a smell of fresh apples, and wolf and fox skins hung about. вҖңUncleвҖқ led the visitors through the anteroom into a small hall with a folding table and red chairs, then into the drawing room with a round birchwood table and a sofa, and finally into his private room where there was a tattered sofa, a worn carpet, and portraits of SuvГіrov, of the hostвҖҷs father and mother, and of himself in military uniform. The study smelt strongly of tobacco and dogs. вҖңUncleвҖқ asked his visitors to sit down and make themselves at home, and then went out of the room. RugГЎy, his back still muddy, came into the room and lay down on the sofa, cleaning himself with his tongue and teeth. Leading from the study was a passage in which a partition with ragged curtains could be seen. From behind this came womenвҖҷs laughter and whispers. NatГЎsha, NikolГЎy, and PГ©tya took off their wraps and sat down on the sofa. PГ©tya, leaning on his elbow, fell asleep at once. NatГЎsha and NikolГЎy were silent. Their faces glowed, they were hungry and very cheerful. They looked at one another (now that the hunt was over and they were in the house, NikolГЎy no longer considered it necessary to show his manly superiority over his sister), NatГЎsha gave him a wink, and neither refrained long from bursting into a peal of ringing laughter even before they had a pretext ready to account for it. After a while вҖңUncleвҖқ came in, in a Cossack coat, blue trousers, and small top boots. And NatГЎsha felt that this costume, the very one she had regarded with surprise and amusement at OtrГЎdnoe, was just the right thing and not at all worse than a swallowtail or frock coat. вҖңUncleвҖқ too was in high spirits and far from being offended by the brotherвҖҷs and sisterвҖҷs laughter (it could never enter his head that they might be laughing at his way of life) he himself joined in the merriment. вҖңThatвҖҷs right, young countess, thatвҖҷs it, come on! I never saw anyone like her!вҖқ said he, offering NikolГЎy a pipe with a long stem and, with a practiced motion of three fingers, taking down another that had been cut short. вҖңSheвҖҷs ridden all day like a man, and is as fresh as ever!вҖқ Soon after вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ reappearance the door was opened, evidently from the sound by a barefooted girl, and a stout, rosy, good-looking woman of about forty, with a double chin and full red lips, entered carrying a large loaded tray. With hospitable dignity and cordiality in her glance and in every motion, she looked at the visitors and, with a pleasant smile, bowed respectfully. In spite of her exceptional stoutness, which caused her to protrude her chest and stomach and throw back her head, this woman (who was вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ housekeeper) trod very lightly. She went to the table, set down the tray, and with her plump white hands deftly took from it the bottles and various hors dвҖҷoeuvres and dishes and arranged them on the table. When she had finished, she stepped aside and stopped at the door with a smile on her face. вҖңHere I am. I am she! Now do you understand вҖҳUncleвҖҷ?вҖқ her expression said to RostГіv. How could one help understanding? Not only NikolГЎy, but even NatГЎsha understood the meaning of his puckered brow and the happy complacent smile that slightly puckered his lips when AnГӯsya FГ«dorovna entered. On the tray was a bottle of herb wine, different kinds of vodka, pickled mushrooms, rye cakes made with buttermilk, honey in the comb, still mead and sparkling mead, apples, nuts (raw and roasted), and nut-and-honey sweets. Afterwards she brought a freshly roasted chicken, ham, preserves made with honey, and preserves made with sugar. All this was the fruit of AnГӯsya FГ«dorovnaвҖҷs housekeeping, gathered and prepared by her. The smell and taste of it all had a smack of AnГӯsya FГ«dorovna herself: a savor of juiciness, cleanliness, whiteness, and pleasant smiles. вҖңTake this, little Lady-Countess!вҖқ she kept saying, as she offered NatГЎsha first one thing and then another. NatГЎsha ate of everything and thought she had never seen or eaten such buttermilk cakes, such aromatic jam, such honey-and-nut sweets, or such a chicken anywhere. AnГӯsya FГ«dorovna left the room. After supper, over their cherry brandy, RostГіv and вҖңUncleвҖқ talked of past and future hunts, of RugГЎy and IlГЎginвҖҷs dogs, while NatГЎsha sat upright on the sofa and listened with sparkling eyes. She tried several times to wake PГ©tya that he might eat something, but he only muttered incoherent words without waking up. NatГЎsha felt so lighthearted and happy in these novel surroundings that she only feared the trap would come for her too soon. After a casual pause, such as often occurs when receiving friends for the first time in oneвҖҷs own house, вҖңUncle,вҖқ answering a thought that was in his visitorsвҖҷ minds, said: вҖңThis, you see, is how I am finishing my daysвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Death will come. ThatвҖҷs it, come on! Nothing will remain. Then why harm anyone?вҖқ вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ face was very significant and even handsome as he said this. Involuntarily RostГіv recalled all the good he had heard about him from his father and the neighbors. Throughout the whole province вҖңUncleвҖқ had the reputation of being the most honorable and disinterested of cranks. They called him in to decide family disputes, chose him as executor, confided secrets to him, elected him to be a justice and to other posts; but he always persistently refused public appointments, passing the autumn and spring in the fields on his bay gelding, sitting at home in winter, and lying in his overgrown garden in summer. вҖңWhy donвҖҷt you enter the service, Uncle?вҖқ вҖңI did once, but gave it up. I am not fit for it. ThatвҖҷs it, come on! I canвҖҷt make head or tail of it. ThatвҖҷs for youвҒ вҖ”I havenвҖҷt brains enough. Now, hunting is another matterвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs it, come on! Open the door, there!вҖқ he shouted. вҖңWhy have you shut it?вҖқ The door at the end of the passage led to the huntsmenвҖҷs room, as they called the room for the hunt servants. There was a rapid patter of bare feet, and an unseen hand opened the door into the huntsmenвҖҷs room, from which came the clear sounds of a balalГЎyka on which someone, who was evidently a master of the art, was playing. NatГЎsha had been listening to those strains for some time and now went out into the passage to hear better. вҖңThatвҖҷs MГӯtka, my coachman.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I have got him a good balalГЎyka. IвҖҷm fond of it,вҖқ said вҖңUncle.вҖқ It was the custom for MГӯtka to play the balalГЎyka in the huntsmenвҖҷs room when вҖңUncleвҖқ returned from the chase. вҖңUncleвҖқ was fond of such music. вҖңHow good! Really very good!вҖқ said NikolГЎy with some unintentional superciliousness, as if ashamed to confess that the sounds pleased him very much. вҖңVery good?вҖқ said NatГЎsha reproachfully, noticing her brotherвҖҷs tone. вҖңNot вҖҳvery goodвҖҷвҒ вҖ”itвҖҷs simply delicious!вҖқ Just as вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ pickled mushrooms, honey, and cherry brandy had seemed to her the best in the world, so also that song, at that moment, seemed to her the acme of musical delight. вҖңMore, please, more!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha at the door as soon as the balalГЎyka ceased. MГӯtka tuned up afresh, and recommenced thrumming the balalГЎyka to the air of вҖңMy Lady,вҖқ with trills and variations. вҖңUncleвҖқ sat listening, slightly smiling, with his head on one side. The air was repeated a hundred times. The balalГЎyka was retuned several times and the same notes were thrummed again, but the listeners did not grow weary of it and wished to hear it again and again. AnГӯsya FГ«dorovna came in and leaned her portly person against the doorpost. вҖңYou like listening?вҖқ she said to NatГЎsha, with a smile extremely like вҖңUncleвҖҷs.вҖқ вҖңThatвҖҷs a good player of ours,вҖқ she added. вҖңHe doesnвҖҷt play that part right!вҖқ said вҖңUncleвҖқ suddenly, with an energetic gesture. вҖңHere he ought to burst outвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs it, come on!вҒ вҖ”ought to burst out.вҖқ вҖңDo you play then?вҖқ asked NatГЎsha. вҖңUncleвҖқ did not answer, but smiled. вҖңAnГӯsyushka, go and see if the strings of my guitar are all right. I havenвҖҷt touched it for a long time. ThatвҖҷs itвҒ вҖ”come on! IвҖҷve given it up.вҖқ AnГӯsya FГ«dorovna, with her light step, willingly went to fulfill her errand and brought back the guitar. Without looking at anyone, вҖңUncleвҖқ blew the dust off it and, tapping the case with his bony fingers, tuned the guitar and settled himself in his armchair. He took the guitar a little above the fingerboard, arching his left elbow with a somewhat theatrical gesture, and, with a wink at AnГӯsya FГ«dorovna, struck a single chord, pure and sonorous, and then quietly, smoothly, and confidently began playing in very slow time, not вҖңMy Lady,вҖқ but the well-known song: вҖңCame a maiden down the street.вҖқ The tune, played with precision and in exact time, began to thrill in the hearts of NikolГЎy and NatГЎsha, arousing in them the same kind of sober mirth as radiated from AnГӯsya FГ«dorovnaвҖҷs whole being. AnГӯsya FГ«dorovna flushed, and drawing her kerchief over her face went laughing out of the room. вҖңUncleвҖқ continued to play correctly, carefully, with energetic firmness, looking with a changed and inspired expression at the spot where AnГӯsya FГ«dorovna had just stood. Something seemed to be laughing a little on one side of his face under his gray mustaches, especially as the song grew brisker and the time quicker and when, here and there, as he ran his fingers over the strings, something seemed to snap. вҖңLovely, lovely! Go on, Uncle, go on!вҖқ shouted NatГЎsha as soon as he had finished. She jumped up and hugged and kissed him. вҖңNikГіlenka, NikГіlenka!вҖқ she said, turning to her brother, as if asking him: вҖңWhat is it moves me so?вҖқ NikolГЎy too was greatly pleased by вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ playing, and вҖңUncleвҖқ played the piece over again. AnГӯsya FГ«dorovnaвҖҷs smiling face reappeared in the doorway and behind hers other facesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Fetching water clear and sweet, Stop, dear maiden, I entreatвҒ вҖ” played вҖңUncleвҖқ once more, running his fingers skillfully over the strings, and then he stopped short and jerked his shoulders. вҖңGo on, Uncle dear,вҖқ NatГЎsha wailed in an imploring tone as if her life depended on it. вҖңUncleвҖқ rose, and it was as if there were two men in him: one of them smiled seriously at the merry fellow, while the merry fellow struck a naive and precise attitude preparatory to a folk dance. вҖңNow then, niece!вҖқ he exclaimed, waving to NatГЎsha the hand that had just struck a chord. NatГЎsha threw off the shawl from her shoulders, ran forward to face вҖңUncle,вҖқ and setting her arms akimbo also made a motion with her shoulders and struck an attitude. Where, how, and when had this young countess, educated by an Г©migrГ© French governess, imbibed from the Russian air she breathed that spirit and obtained that manner which the pas de chГўle69 would, one would have supposed, long ago have effaced? But the spirit and the movements were those inimitable and unteachable Russian ones that вҖңUncleвҖқ had expected of her. As soon as she had struck her pose, and smiled triumphantly, proudly, and with sly merriment, the fear that had at first seized NikolГЎy and the others that she might not do the right thing was at an end, and they were already admiring her. She did the right thing with such precision, such complete precision, that AnГӯsya FГ«dorovna, who had at once handed her the handkerchief she needed for the dance, had tears in her eyes, though she laughed as she watched this slim, graceful countess, reared in silks and velvets and so different from herself, who yet was able to understand all that was in AnГӯsya and in AnГӯsyaвҖҷs father and mother and aunt, and in every Russian man and woman. вҖңWell, little countess; thatвҖҷs itвҒ вҖ”come on!вҖқ cried вҖңUncle,вҖқ with a joyous laugh, having finished the dance. вҖңWell done, niece! Now a fine young fellow must be found as husband for you. ThatвҖҷs itвҒ вҖ”come on!вҖқ вҖңHeвҖҷs chosen already,вҖқ said NikolГЎy smiling. вҖңOh?вҖқ said вҖңUncleвҖқ in surprise, looking inquiringly at NatГЎsha, who nodded her head with a happy smile. вҖңAnd such a one!вҖқ she said. But as soon as she had said it a new train of thoughts and feelings arose in her. вҖңWhat did NikolГЎyвҖҷs smile mean when he said вҖҳchosen alreadyвҖҷ? Is he glad of it or not? It is as if he thought my BolkГіnski would not approve of or understand our gaiety. But he would understand it all. Where is he now?вҖқ she thought, and her face suddenly became serious. But this lasted only a second. вҖңDonвҖҷt dare to think about it,вҖқ she said to herself, and sat down again smilingly beside вҖңUncle,вҖқ begging him to play something more. вҖңUncleвҖқ played another song and a valse; then after a pause he cleared his throat and sang his favorite hunting song: As вҖҷtwas growing dark last night Fell the snow so soft and lightвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңUncleвҖқ sang as peasants sing, with full and naive conviction that the whole meaning of a song lies in the words and that the tune comes of itself, and that apart from the words there is no tune, which exists only to give measure to the words. As a result of this the unconsidered tune, like the song of a bird, was extraordinarily good. NatГЎsha was in ecstasies over вҖңUncleвҖҷsвҖқ singing. She resolved to give up learning the harp and to play only the guitar. She asked вҖңUncleвҖқ for his guitar and at once found the chords of the song. After nine oвҖҷclock two traps and three mounted men, who had been sent to look for them, arrived to fetch NatГЎsha and PГ©tya. The count and countess did not know where they were and were very anxious, said one of the men. PГ©tya was carried out like a log and laid in the larger of the two traps. NatГЎsha and NikolГЎy got into the other. вҖңUncleвҖқ wrapped NatГЎsha up warmly and took leave of her with quite a new tenderness. He accompanied them on foot as far as the bridge that could not be crossed, so that they had to go round by the ford, and he sent huntsmen to ride in front with lanterns. вҖңGoodbye, dear niece,вҖқ his voice called out of the darknessвҒ вҖ”not the voice NatГЎsha had known previously, but the one that had sung вҖңAs вҖҷtwas growing dark last night.вҖқ In the village through which they passed there were red lights and a cheerful smell of smoke. вҖңWhat a darling Uncle is!вҖқ said NatГЎsha, when they had come out onto the high road. вҖңYes,вҖқ returned NikolГЎy. вҖңYouвҖҷre not cold?вҖқ вҖңNo. IвҖҷm quite, quite all right. I feel so comfortable!вҖқ answered NatГЎsha, almost perplexed by her feelings. They remained silent a long while. The night was dark and damp. They could not see the horses, but only heard them splashing through the unseen mud. What was passing in that receptive childlike soul that so eagerly caught and assimilated all the diverse impressions of life? How did they all find place in her? But she was very happy. As they were nearing home she suddenly struck up the air of вҖңAs вҖҷtwas growing dark last nightвҖқвҒ вҖ”the tune of which she had all the way been trying to get and had at last caught. вҖңGot it?вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңWhat were you thinking about just now, NikolГЎy?вҖқ inquired NatГЎsha. They were fond of asking one another that question. вҖңI?вҖқ said NikolГЎy, trying to remember. вҖңWell, you see, first I thought that RugГЎy, the red hound, was like Uncle, and that if he were a man he would always keep Uncle near him, if not for his riding, then for his manner. What a good fellow Uncle is! DonвҖҷt you think so?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, and you?вҖқ вҖңI? Wait a bit, wait.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Yes, first I thought that we are driving along and imagining that we are going home, but that heaven knows where we are really going in the darkness, and that we shall arrive and suddenly find that we are not in OtrГЎdnoe, but in Fairyland. And then I thoughtвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No, nothing else.вҖқ вҖңI know, I expect you thought of him,вҖқ said NikolГЎy, smiling as NatГЎsha knew by the sound of his voice. вҖңNo,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, though she had in reality been thinking about Prince AndrГ©y at the same time as of the rest, and of how he would have liked вҖңUncle.вҖқ вҖңAnd then I was saying to myself all the way, вҖҳHow well AnГӯsyushka carried herself, how well!вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ And NikolГЎy heard her spontaneous, happy, ringing laughter. вҖңAnd do you know,вҖқ she suddenly said, вҖңI know that I shall never again be as happy and tranquil as I am now.вҖқ вҖңRubbish, nonsense, humbug!вҖқ exclaimed NikolГЎy, and he thought: вҖңHow charming this NatГЎsha of mine is! I have no other friend like her and never shall have. Why should she marry? We might always drive about together!вҖқ вҖңWhat a darling this NikolГЎy of mine is!вҖқ thought NatГЎsha. вҖңAh, there are still lights in the drawing room!вҖқ she said, pointing to the windows of the house that gleamed invitingly in the moist velvety darkness of the night. VIII VIII Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich had resigned the position of Marshal of the Nobility because it involved him in too much expense, but still his affairs did not improve. NatГЎsha and NikolГЎy often noticed their parents conferring together anxiously and privately and heard suggestions of selling the fine ancestral RostГіv house and estate near Moscow. It was not necessary to entertain so freely as when the count had been Marshal, and life at OtrГЎdnoe was quieter than in former years, but still the enormous house and its lodges were full of people and more than twenty sat down to table every day. These were all their own people who had settled down in the house almost as members of the family, or persons who were, it seemed, obliged to live in the countвҖҷs house. Such were Dimmler the musician and his wife, Vogel the dancing master and his family, BelГіva, an old maiden lady, an inmate of the house, and many others such as PГ©tyaвҖҷs tutors, the girlsвҖҷ former governess, and other people who simply found it preferable and more advantageous to live in the countвҖҷs house than at home. They had not as many visitors as before, but the old habits of life without which the count and countess could not conceive of existence remained unchanged. There was still the hunting establishment which NikolГЎy had even enlarged, the same fifty horses and fifteen grooms in the stables, the same expensive presents and dinner parties to the whole district on name days; there were still the countвҖҷs games of whist and boston, at whichвҒ вҖ”spreading out his cards so that everybody could see themвҒ вҖ”he let himself be plundered of hundreds of rubles every day by his neighbors, who looked upon an opportunity to play a rubber with Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich as a most profitable source of income. The count moved in his affairs as in a huge net, trying not to believe that he was entangled but becoming more and more so at every step, and feeling too feeble to break the meshes or to set to work carefully and patiently to disentangle them. The countess, with her loving heart, felt that her children were being ruined, that it was not the countвҖҷs fault for he could not help being what he wasвҒ вҖ”that (though he tried to hide it) he himself suffered from the consciousness of his own and his childrenвҖҷs ruin, and she tried to find means of remedying the position. From her feminine point of view she could see only one solution, namely, for NikolГЎy to marry a rich heiress. She felt this to be their last hope and that if NikolГЎy refused the match she had found for him, she would have to abandon the hope of ever getting matters right. This match was with Julie KarГЎgina, the daughter of excellent and virtuous parents, a girl the RostГіvs had known from childhood, and who had now become a wealthy heiress through the death of the last of her brothers. The countess had written direct to JulieвҖҷs mother in Moscow suggesting a marriage between their children and had received a favorable answer from her. KarГЎgina had replied that for her part she was agreeable, and everything depend on her daughterвҖҷs inclination. She invited NikolГЎy to come to Moscow. Several times the countess, with tears in her eyes, told her son that now both her daughters were settled, her only wish was to see him married. She said she could lie down in her grave peacefully if that were accomplished. Then she told him that she knew of a splendid girl and tried to discover what he thought about marriage. At other times she praised Julie to him and advised him to go to Moscow during the holidays to amuse himself. NikolГЎy guessed what his motherвҖҷs remarks were leading to and during one of these conversations induced her to speak quite frankly. She told him that her only hope of getting their affairs disentangled now lay in his marrying Julie KarГЎgina. вҖңBut, Mamma, suppose I loved a girl who has no fortune, would you expect me to sacrifice my feelings and my honor for the sake of money?вҖқ he asked his mother, not realizing the cruelty of his question and only wishing to show his noble-mindedness. вҖңNo, you have not understood me,вҖқ said his mother, not knowing how to justify herself. вҖңYou have not understood me, NikГіlenka. It is your happiness I wish for,вҖқ she added, feeling that she was telling an untruth and was becoming entangled. She began to cry. вҖңMamma, donвҖҷt cry! Only tell me that you wish it, and you know I will give my life, anything, to put you at ease,вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңI would sacrifice anything for youвҒ вҖ”even my feelings.вҖқ But the countess did not want the question put like that: she did not want a sacrifice from her son, she herself wished to make a sacrifice for him. вҖңNo, you have not understood me, donвҖҷt let us talk about it,вҖқ she replied, wiping away her tears. вҖңMaybe I do love a poor girl,вҖқ said NikolГЎy to himself. вҖңAm I to sacrifice my feelings and my honor for money? I wonder how Mamma could speak so to me. Because SГіnya is poor I must not love her,вҖқ he thought, вҖңmust not respond to her faithful, devoted love? Yet I should certainly be happier with her than with some doll-like Julie. I can always sacrifice my feelings for my familyвҖҷs welfare,вҖқ he said to himself, вҖңbut I canвҖҷt coerce my feelings. If I love SГіnya, that feeling is for me stronger and higher than all else.вҖқ NikolГЎy did not go to Moscow, and the countess did not renew the conversation with him about marriage. She saw with sorrow, and sometimes with exasperation, symptoms of a growing attachment between her son and the portionless SГіnya. Though she blamed herself for it, she could not refrain from grumbling at and worrying SГіnya, often pulling her up without reason, addressing her stiffly as вҖңmy dear,вҖқ and using the formal вҖңyouвҖқ instead of the intimate вҖңthouвҖқ in speaking to her. The kindhearted countess was the more vexed with SГіnya because that poor, dark-eyed niece of hers was so meek, so kind, so devotedly grateful to her benefactors, and so faithfully, unchangingly, and unselfishly in love with NikolГЎy, that there were no grounds for finding fault with her. NikolГЎy was spending the last of his leave at home. A fourth letter had come from Prince AndrГ©y, from Rome, in which he wrote that he would have been on his way back to Russia long ago had not his wound unexpectedly reopened in the warm climate, which obliged him to defer his return till the beginning of the new year. NatГЎsha was still as much in love with her betrothed, found the same comfort in that love, and was still as ready to throw herself into all the pleasures of life as before; but at the end of the fourth month of their separation she began to have fits of depression which she could not master. She felt sorry for herself: sorry that she was being wasted all this time and of no use to anyoneвҒ вҖ”while she felt herself so capable of loving and being loved. Things were not cheerful in the RostГіvsвҖҷ home. IX IX Christmas came and except for the ceremonial Mass, the solemn and wearisome Christmas congratulations from neighbors and servants, and the new dresses everyone put on, there were no special festivities, though the calm frost of twenty degrees RГ©aumur, the dazzling sunshine by day, and the starlight of the winter nights seemed to call for some special celebration of the season. On the third day of Christmas week, after the midday dinner, all the inmates of the house dispersed to various rooms. It was the dullest time of the day. NikolГЎy, who had been visiting some neighbors that morning, was asleep on the sitting-room sofa. The old count was resting in his study. SГіnya sat in the drawing room at the round table, copying a design for embroidery. The countess was playing patience. NastГЎsya IvГЎnovna the buffoon sat with a sad face at the window with two old ladies. NatГЎsha came into the room, went up to SГіnya, glanced at what she was doing, and then went up to her mother and stood without speaking. вҖңWhy are you wandering about like an outcast?вҖқ asked her mother. вҖңWhat do you want?вҖқ вҖңHimвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I want himвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ now, this minute! I want him!вҖқ said NatГЎsha, with glittering eyes and no sign of a smile. The countess lifted her head and looked attentively at her daughter. вҖңDonвҖҷt look at me, Mamma! DonвҖҷt look; I shall cry directly.вҖқ вҖңSit down with me a little,вҖқ said the countess. вҖңMamma, I want him. Why should I be wasted like this, Mamma?вҖқ Her voice broke, tears gushed from her eyes, and she turned quickly to hide them and left the room. She passed into the sitting room, stood there thinking awhile, and then went into the maidsвҖҷ room. There an old maidservant was grumbling at a young girl who stood panting, having just run in through the cold from the serfsвҖҷ quarters. вҖңStop playingвҒ вҖ”thereвҖҷs a time for everything,вҖқ said the old woman. вҖңLet her alone, KondrГЎtevna,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңGo, MavrГәshka, go.вҖқ Having released MavrГәshka, NatГЎsha crossed the dancing hall and went to the vestibule. There an old footman and two young ones were playing cards. They broke off and rose as she entered. вҖңWhat can I do with them?вҖқ thought NatГЎsha. вҖңOh, NikГӯta, please goвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ where can I send him?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Yes, go to the yard and fetch a fowl, please, a cock, and you, Misha, bring me some oats.вҖқ вҖңJust a few oats?вҖқ said Misha, cheerfully and readily. вҖңGo, go quickly,вҖқ the old man urged him. вҖңAnd you, FГ«dor, get me a piece of chalk.вҖқ On her way past the butlerвҖҷs pantry she told them to set a samovar, though it was not at all the time for tea. FГіka, the butler, was the most ill-tempered person in the house. NatГЎsha liked to test her power over him. He distrusted the order and asked whether the samovar was really wanted. вҖңOh dear, what a young lady!вҖқ said FГіka, pretending to frown at NatГЎsha. No one in the house sent people about or gave them as much trouble as NatГЎsha did. She could not see people unconcernedly, but had to send them on some errand. She seemed to be trying whether any of them would get angry or sulky with her; but the serfs fulfilled no oneвҖҷs orders so readily as they did hers. вҖңWhat can I do, where can I go?вҖқ thought she, as she went slowly along the passage. вҖңNastГЎsya IvГЎnovna, what sort of children shall I have?вҖқ she asked the buffoon, who was coming toward her in a womanвҖҷs jacket. вҖңWhy, fleas, crickets, grasshoppers,вҖқ answered the buffoon. вҖңO Lord, O Lord, itвҖҷs always the same! Oh, where am I to go? What am I to do with myself?вҖқ And tapping with her heels, she ran quickly upstairs to see Vogel and his wife who lived on the upper story. Two governesses were sitting with the Vogels at a table, on which were plates of raisins, walnuts, and almonds. The governesses were discussing whether it was cheaper to live in Moscow or Odessa. NatГЎsha sat down, listened to their talk with a serious and thoughtful air, and then got up again. вҖңThe island of Madagascar,вҖқ she said, вҖңMaвҖ‘daвҖ‘gasвҖ‘car,вҖқ she repeated, articulating each syllable distinctly, and, not replying to Madame Schoss who asked her what she was saying, she went out of the room. Her brother PГ©tya was upstairs too; with the man in attendance on him he was preparing fireworks to let off that night. вҖңPГ©tya! PГ©tya!вҖқ she called to him. вҖңCarry me downstairs.вҖқ PГ©tya ran up and offered her his back. She jumped on it, putting her arms round his neck, and he pranced along with her. вҖңNo, donвҖҷtвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the island of Madagascar!вҖқ she said, and jumping off his back she went downstairs. Having as it were reviewed her kingdom, tested her power, and made sure that everyone was submissive, but that all the same it was dull, NatГЎsha betook herself to the ballroom, picked up her guitar, sat down in a dark corner behind a bookcase, and began to run her fingers over the strings in the bass, picking out a passage she recalled from an opera she had heard in Petersburg with Prince AndrГ©y. What she drew from the guitar would have had no meaning for other listeners, but in her imagination a whole series of reminiscences arose from those sounds. She sat behind the bookcase with her eyes fixed on a streak of light escaping from the pantry door and listened to herself and pondered. She was in a mood for brooding on the past. SГіnya passed to the pantry with a glass in her hand. NatГЎsha glanced at her and at the crack in the pantry door, and it seemed to her that she remembered the light falling through that crack once before and SГіnya passing with a glass in her hand. вҖңYes it was exactly the same,вҖқ thought NatГЎsha. вҖңSГіnya, what is this?вҖқ she cried, twanging a thick string. вҖңOh, you are there!вҖқ said SГіnya with a start, and came near and listened. вҖңI donвҖҷt know. A storm?вҖқ she ventured timidly, afraid of being wrong. вҖңThere! ThatвҖҷs just how she started and just how she came up smiling timidly when all this happened before,вҖқ thought NatГЎsha, вҖңand in just the same way I thought there was something lacking in her.вҖқ вҖңNo, itвҖҷs the chorus from The Water-Carrier, listen!вҖқ and NatГЎsha sang the air of the chorus so that SГіnya should catch it. вҖңWhere were you going?вҖқ she asked. вҖңTo change the water in this glass. I am just finishing the design.вҖқ вҖңYou always find something to do, but I canвҖҷt,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңAnd whereвҖҷs NikГіlenka?вҖқ вҖңAsleep, I think.вҖқ вҖңSГіnya, go and wake him,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңTell him I want him to come and sing.вҖқ She sat awhile, wondering what the meaning of it all having happened before could be, and without solving this problem, or at all regretting not having done so, she again passed in fancy to the time when she was with him and he was looking at her with a loverвҖҷs eyes. вҖңOh, if only he would come quicker! I am so afraid it will never be! And, worst of all, I am growing oldвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs the thing! There wonвҖҷt then be in me what there is now. But perhaps heвҖҷll come today, will come immediately. Perhaps he has come and is sitting in the drawing room. Perhaps he came yesterday and I have forgotten it.вҖқ She rose, put down the guitar, and went to the drawing room. All the domestic circle, tutors, governesses, and guests, were already at the tea table. The servants stood round the tableвҒ вҖ”but Prince AndrГ©y was not there and life was going on as before. вҖңAh, here she is!вҖқ said the old count, when he saw NatГЎsha enter. вҖңWell, sit down by me.вҖқ But NatГЎsha stayed by her mother and glanced round as if looking for something. вҖңMamma!вҖқ she muttered, вҖңgive him to me, give him, Mamma, quickly, quickly!вҖқ and she again had difficulty in repressing her sobs. She sat down at the table and listened to the conversation between the elders and NikolГЎy, who had also come to the table. вҖңMy God, my God! The same faces, the same talk, Papa holding his cup and blowing in the same way!вҖқ thought NatГЎsha, feeling with horror a sense of repulsion rising up in her for the whole household, because they were always the same. After tea, NikolГЎy, SГіnya, and NatГЎsha went to the sitting room, to their favorite corner where their most intimate talks always began. I I After Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs engagement to NatГЎsha, Pierre without any apparent cause suddenly felt it impossible to go on living as before. Firmly convinced as he was of the truths revealed to him by his benefactor, and happy as he had been in perfecting his inner man, to which he had devoted himself with such ardorвҒ вҖ”all the zest of such a life vanished after the engagement of AndrГ©y and NatГЎsha and the death of Osip AlexГ©evich, the news of which reached him almost at the same time. Only the skeleton of life remained: his house, a brilliant wife who now enjoyed the favors of a very important personage, acquaintance with all Petersburg, and his court service with its dull formalities. And this life suddenly seemed to Pierre unexpectedly loathsome. He ceased keeping a diary, avoided the company of the Brothers, began going to the club again, drank a great deal, and came once more in touch with the bachelor sets, leading such a life that the Countess ElГЁna VasГӯlievna thought it necessary to speak severely to him about it. Pierre felt that she was right, and to avoid compromising her went away to Moscow. In Moscow as soon as he entered his huge house in which the faded and fading princesses still lived, with its enormous retinue; as soon as, driving through the town, he saw the Iberian shrine with innumerable tapers burning before the golden covers of the icons, the KrГ©mlin Square with its snow undisturbed by vehicles, the sleigh drivers and hovels of the SГӯvtsev VrazhГіk, those old Moscovites who desired nothing, hurried nowhere, and were ending their days leisurely; when he saw those old Moscow ladies, the Moscow balls, and the English Club, he felt himself at home in a quiet haven. In Moscow he felt at peace, at home, warm and dirty as in an old dressing gown. Moscow society, from the old women down to the children, received Pierre like a long-expected guest whose place was always ready awaiting him. For Moscow society Pierre was the nicest, kindest, most intellectual, merriest, and most magnanimous of cranks, a heedless, genial nobleman of the old Russian type. His purse was always empty because it was open to everyone. Benefit performances, poor pictures, statues, benevolent societies, gypsy choirs, schools, subscription dinners, sprees, Freemasons, churches, and booksвҒ вҖ”no one and nothing met with a refusal from him, and had it not been for two friends who had borrowed large sums from him and taken him under their protection, he would have given everything away. There was never a dinner or soiree at the club without him. As soon as he sank into his place on the sofa after two bottles of Margaux he was surrounded, and talking, disputing, and joking began. When there were quarrels, his kindly smile and well-timed jests reconciled the antagonists. The Masonic dinners were dull and dreary when he was not there. When after a bachelor supper he rose with his amiable and kindly smile, yielding to the entreaties of the festive company to drive off somewhere with them, shouts of delight and triumph arose among the young men. At balls he danced if a partner was needed. Young ladies, married and unmarried, liked him because without making love to any of them, he was equally amiable to all, especially after supper. вҖңIl est charmant; il nвҖҷa pas de sexe,вҖқ70 they said of him. Pierre was one of those retired gentlemen-in-waiting of whom there were hundreds good-humoredly ending their days in Moscow. How horrified he would have been seven years before, when he first arrived from abroad, had he been told that there was no need for him to seek or plan anything, that his rut had long been shaped, eternally predetermined, and that wriggle as he might, he would be what all in his position were. He could not have believed it! Had he not at one time longed with all his heart to establish a republic in Russia; then himself to be a Napoleon; then to be a philosopher; and then a strategist and the conqueror of Napoleon? Had he not seen the possibility of, and passionately desired, the regeneration of the sinful human race, and his own progress to the highest degree of perfection? Had he not established schools and hospitals and liberated his serfs? But instead of all thatвҒ вҖ”here he was, the wealthy husband of an unfaithful wife, a retired gentleman-in-waiting, fond of eating and drinking and, as he unbuttoned his waistcoat, of abusing the government a bit, a member of the Moscow English Club, and a universal favorite in Moscow society. For a long time he could not reconcile himself to the idea that he was one of those same retired Moscow gentlemen-in-waiting he had so despised seven years before. Sometimes he consoled himself with the thought that he was only living this life temporarily; but then he was shocked by the thought of how many, like himself, had entered that life and that club temporarily, with all their teeth and hair, and had only left it when not a single tooth or hair remained. In moments of pride, when he thought of his position it seemed to him that he was quite different and distinct from those other retired gentlemen-in-waiting he had formerly despised: they were empty, stupid, contented fellows, satisfied with their position, вҖңwhile I am still discontented and want to do something for mankind. But perhaps all these comrades of mine struggled just like me and sought something new, a path in life of their own, and like me were brought by force of circumstances, society, and raceвҒ вҖ”by that elemental force against which man is powerlessвҒ вҖ”to the condition I am in,вҖқ said he to himself in moments of humility; and after living some time in Moscow he no longer despised, but began to grow fond of, to respect, and to pity his comrades in destiny, as he pitied himself. Pierre no longer suffered moments of despair, hypochondria, and disgust with life, but the malady that had formerly found expression in such acute attacks was driven inwards and never left him for a moment. вҖңWhat for? Why? What is going on in the world?вҖқ he would ask himself in perplexity several times a day, involuntarily beginning to reflect anew on the meaning of the phenomena of life; but knowing by experience that there were no answers to these questions he made haste to turn away from them, and took up a book, or hurried off to the club or to ApollГіn NikolГЎevichвҖҷs, to exchange the gossip of the town. вҖңElГЁna VasГӯlievna, who has never cared for anything but her own body and is one of the stupidest women in the world,вҖқ thought Pierre, вҖңis regarded by people as the acme of intelligence and refinement, and they pay homage to her. Napoleon Bonaparte was despised by all as long as he was great, but now that he has become a wretched comedian the Emperor Francis wants to offer him his daughter in an illegal marriage. The Spaniards, through the Catholic clergy, offer praise to God for their victory over the French on the fourteenth of June, and the French, also through the Catholic clergy, offer praise because on that same fourteenth of June they defeated the Spaniards. My brother Masons swear by the blood that they are ready to sacrifice everything for their neighbor, but they do not give a ruble each to the collections for the poor, and they intrigue, the Astraea Lodge against the Manna Seekers, and fuss about an authentic Scotch carpet and a charter that nobody needs, and the meaning of which the very man who wrote it does not understand.
