We all profess the Christian law of forgiveness of injuries and love of our neighbors, the law in honor of which we have built in Moscow forty times forty churchesвҒ вҖ”but yesterday a deserter was knouted to death and a minister of that same law of love and forgiveness, a priest, gave the soldier a cross to kiss before his execution.вҖқ So thought Pierre, and the whole of this general deception which everyone accepts, accustomed as he was to it, astonished him each time as if it were something new. вҖңI understand the deception and confusion,вҖқ he thought, вҖңbut how am I to tell them all that I see? I have tried, and have always found that they too in the depths of their souls understand it as I do, and only try not to see it. So it appears that it must be so! But IвҒ вҖ”what is to become of me?вҖқ thought he. He had the unfortunate capacity many men, especially Russians, have of seeing and believing in the possibility of goodness and truth, but of seeing the evil and falsehood of life too clearly to be able to take a serious part in it. Every sphere of work was connected, in his eyes, with evil and deception. Whatever he tried to be, whatever he engaged in, the evil and falsehood of it repulsed him and blocked every path of activity. Yet he had to live and to find occupation. It was too dreadful to be under the burden of these insoluble problems, so he abandoned himself to any distraction in order to forget them. He frequented every kind of society, drank much, bought pictures, engaged in building, and above allвҒ вҖ”read. He read, and read everything that came to hand. On coming home, while his valets were still taking off his things, he picked up a book and began to read. From reading he passed to sleeping, from sleeping to gossip in drawing rooms of the club, from gossip to carousals and women; from carousals back to gossip, reading, and wine. Drinking became more and more a physical and also a moral necessity. Though the doctors warned him that with his corpulence wine was dangerous for him, he drank a great deal. He was only quite at ease when having poured several glasses of wine mechanically into his large mouth he felt a pleasant warmth in his body, an amiability toward all his fellows, and a readiness to respond superficially to every idea without probing it deeply. Only after emptying a bottle or two did he feel dimly that the terribly tangled skein of life which previously had terrified him was not as dreadful as he had thought. He was always conscious of some aspect of that skein, as with a buzzing in his head after dinner or supper he chatted or listened to conversation or read. But under the influence of wine he said to himself: вҖңIt doesnвҖҷt matter. IвҖҷll get it unraveled. I have a solution ready, but have no time nowвҒ вҖ”IвҖҷll think it all out later on!вҖқ But the later on never came. In the morning, on an empty stomach, all the old questions appeared as insoluble and terrible as ever, and Pierre hastily picked up a book, and if anyone came to see him he was glad. Sometimes he remembered how he had heard that soldiers in war when entrenched under the enemyвҖҷs fire, if they have nothing to do, try hard to find some occupation the more easily to bear the danger. To Pierre all men seemed like those soldiers, seeking refuge from life: some in ambition, some in cards, some in framing laws, some in women, some in toys, some in horses, some in politics, some in sport, some in wine, and some in governmental affairs. вҖңNothing is trivial, and nothing is important, itвҖҷs all the sameвҒ вҖ”only to save oneself from it as best one can,вҖқ thought Pierre. вҖңOnly not to see it, that dreadful it!вҖқ X X During the entrвҖҷacte a whiff of cold air came into ElГЁnвҖҷs box, the door opened, and Anatole entered, stooping and trying not to brush against anyone. вҖңLet me introduce my brother to you,вҖқ said ElГЁn, her eyes shifting uneasily from NatГЎsha to Anatole. NatГЎsha turned her pretty little head toward the elegant young officer and smiled at him over her bare shoulder. Anatole, who was as handsome at close quarters as at a distance, sat down beside her and told her he had long wished to have this happinessвҒ вҖ”ever since the NarГҪshkinsвҖҷ ball in fact, at which he had had the well-remembered pleasure of seeing her. KurГЎgin was much more sensible and simple with women than among men. He talked boldly and naturally, and NatГЎsha was strangely and agreeably struck by the fact that there was nothing formidable in this man about whom there was so much talk, but that on the contrary his smile was most naive, cheerful, and good-natured. KurГЎgin asked her opinion of the performance and told her how at a previous performance SemГ«nova had fallen down on the stage. вҖңAnd do you know, Countess,вҖқ he said, suddenly addressing her as an old, familiar acquaintance, вҖңwe are getting up a costume tournament; you ought to take part in it! It will be great fun. We shall all meet at the KarГЎginsвҖҷ! Please come! No! Really, eh?вҖқ said he. While saying this he never removed his smiling eyes from her face, her neck, and her bare arms. NatГЎsha knew for certain that he was enraptured by her. This pleased her, yet his presence made her feel constrained and oppressed. When she was not looking at him she felt that he was looking at her shoulders, and she involuntarily caught his eye so that he should look into hers rather than this. But looking into his eyes she was frightened, realizing that there was not that barrier of modesty she had always felt between herself and other men. She did not know how it was that within five minutes she had come to feel herself terribly near to this man. When she turned away she feared he might seize her from behind by her bare arm and kiss her on the neck. They spoke of most ordinary things, yet she felt that they were closer to one another than she had ever been to any man. NatГЎsha kept turning to ElГЁn and to her father, as if asking what it all meant, but ElГЁn was engaged in conversation with a general and did not answer her look, and her fatherвҖҷs eyes said nothing but what they always said: вҖңHaving a good time? Well, IвҖҷm glad of it!вҖқ During one of these moments of awkward silence when AnatoleвҖҷs prominent eyes were gazing calmly and fixedly at her, NatГЎsha, to break the silence, asked him how he liked Moscow. She asked the question and blushed. She felt all the time that by talking to him she was doing something improper. Anatole smiled as though to encourage her. вҖңAt first I did not like it much, because what makes a town pleasant ce sont les jolies femmes,74 isnвҖҷt that so? But now I like it very much indeed,вҖқ he said, looking at her significantly. вҖңYouвҖҷll come to the costume tournament, Countess? Do come!вҖқ and putting out his hand to her bouquet and dropping his voice, he added, вҖңYou will be the prettiest there. Do come, dear countess, and give me this flower as a pledge!вҖқ NatГЎsha did not understand what he was saying any more than he did himself, but she felt that his incomprehensible words had an improper intention. She did not know what to say and turned away as if she had not heard his remark. But as soon as she had turned away she felt that he was there, behind, so close behind her. вҖңHow is he now? Confused? Angry? Ought I to put it right?вҖқ she asked herself, and she could not refrain from turning round. She looked straight into his eyes, and his nearness, self-assurance, and the good-natured tenderness of his smile vanquished her. She smiled just as he was doing, gazing straight into his eyes. And again she felt with horror that no barrier lay between him and her. The curtain rose again. Anatole left the box, serene and gay. NatГЎsha went back to her father in the other box, now quite submissive to the world she found herself in. All that was going on before her now seemed quite natural, but on the other hand all her previous thoughts of her betrothed, of Princess MГЎrya, or of life in the country did not once recur to her mind and were as if belonging to a remote past. In the fourth act there was some sort of devil who sang waving his arm about, till the boards were withdrawn from under him and he disappeared down below. That was the only part of the fourth act that NatГЎsha saw. She felt agitated and tormented, and the cause of this was KurГЎgin whom she could not help watching. As they were leaving the theater Anatole came up to them, called their carriage, and helped them in. As he was putting NatГЎsha in he pressed her arm above the elbow. Agitated and flushed she turned round. He was looking at her with glittering eyes, smiling tenderly. Only after she had reached home was NatГЎsha able clearly to think over what had happened to her, and suddenly remembering Prince AndrГ©y she was horrified, and at tea to which all had sat down after the opera, she gave a loud exclamation, flushed, and ran out of the room. вҖңO God! I am lost!вҖқ she said to herself. вҖңHow could I let him?вҖқ She sat for a long time hiding her flushed face in her hands trying to realize what had happened to her, but was unable either to understand what had happened or what she felt. Everything seemed dark, obscure, and terrible. There in that enormous, illuminated theater where the bare-legged Duport, in a tinsel-decorated jacket, jumped about to the music on wet boards, and young girls and old men, and the nearly naked ElГЁn with her proud, calm smile, rapturously cried вҖңbravo!вҖқвҒ вҖ”there in the presence of that ElГЁn it had all seemed clear and simple; but now, alone by herself, it was incomprehensible. вҖңWhat is it? What was that terror I felt of him? What is this gnawing of conscience I am feeling now?вҖқ she thought. Only to the old countess at night in bed could NatГЎsha have told all she was feeling. She knew that SГіnya with her severe and simple views would either not understand it at all or would be horrified at such a confession. So NatГЎsha tried to solve what was torturing her by herself. вҖңAm I spoiled for AndrГ©yвҖҷs love or not?вҖқ she asked herself, and with soothing irony replied: вҖңWhat a fool I am to ask that! What did happen to me? Nothing! I have done nothing, I didnвҖҷt lead him on at all. Nobody will know and I shall never see him again,вҖқ she told herself. вҖңSo it is plain that nothing has happened and there is nothing to repent of, and AndrГ©y can love me still. But why вҖҳstill?вҖҷ O God, why isnвҖҷt he here?вҖқ NatГЎsha quieted herself for a moment, but again some instinct told her that though all this was true, and though nothing had happened, yet the former purity of her love for Prince AndrГ©y had perished. And again in imagination she went over her whole conversation with KurГЎgin, and again saw the face, gestures, and tender smile of that bold handsome man when he pressed her arm. XI XI Anatole KurГЎgin was staying in Moscow because his father had sent him away from Petersburg, where he had been spending twenty thousand rubles a year in cash, besides running up debts for as much more, which his creditors demanded from his father. His father announced to him that he would now pay half his debts for the last time, but only on condition that he went to Moscow as adjutant to the commander in chiefвҒ вҖ”a post his father had procured for himвҒ вҖ”and would at last try to make a good match there. He indicated to him Princess MГЎrya and Julie KarГЎgina. Anatole consented and went to Moscow, where he put up at PierreвҖҷs house. Pierre received him unwillingly at first, but got used to him after a while, sometimes even accompanied him on his carousals, and gave him money under the guise of loans. As ShinshГӯn had remarked, from the time of his arrival Anatole had turned the heads of the Moscow ladies, especially by the fact that he slighted them and plainly preferred the gypsy girls and French actressesвҒ вҖ”with the chief of whom, Mademoiselle George, he was said to be on intimate relations. He had never missed a carousal at DanГӯlovвҖҷs or other Moscow revelersвҖҷ, drank whole nights through, outvying everyone else, and was at all the balls and parties of the best society. There was talk of his intrigues with some of the ladies, and he flirted with a few of them at the balls. But he did not run after the unmarried girls, especially the rich heiresses who were most of them plain. There was a special reason for this, as he had got married two years beforeвҒ вҖ”a fact known only to his most intimate friends. At that time while with his regiment in Poland, a Polish landowner of small means had forced him to marry his daughter. Anatole had very soon abandoned his wife and, for a payment which he agreed to send to his father-in-law, had arranged to be free to pass himself off as a bachelor. Anatole was always content with his position, with himself, and with others. He was instinctively and thoroughly convinced that it was impossible for him to live otherwise than as he did and that he had never in his life done anything base. He was incapable of considering how his actions might affect others or what the consequences of this or that action of his might be. He was convinced that, as a duck is so made that it must live in water, so God had made him such that he must spend thirty thousand rubles a year and always occupy a prominent position in society. He believed this so firmly that others, looking at him, were persuaded of it too and did not refuse him either a leading place in society or money, which he borrowed from anyone and everyone and evidently would not repay. He was not a gambler, at any rate he did not care about winning. He was not vain. He did not mind what people thought of him. Still less could he be accused of ambition. More than once he had vexed his father by spoiling his own career, and he laughed at distinctions of all kinds. He was not mean, and did not refuse anyone who asked of him. All he cared about was gaiety and women, and as according to his ideas there was nothing dishonorable in these tastes, and he was incapable of considering what the gratification of his tastes entailed for others, he honestly considered himself irreproachable, sincerely despised rogues and bad people, and with a tranquil conscience carried his head high. Rakes, those male Magdalenes, have a secret feeling of innocence similar to that which female Magdalenes have, based on the same hope of forgiveness. вҖңAll will be forgiven her, for she loved much; and all will be forgiven him, for he enjoyed much.вҖқ DГіlokhov, who had reappeared that year in Moscow after his exile and his Persian adventures, and was leading a life of luxury, gambling, and dissipation, associated with his old Petersburg comrade KurГЎgin and made use of him for his own ends. Anatole was sincerely fond of DГіlokhov for his cleverness and audacity. DГіlokhov, who needed Anatole KurГЎginвҖҷs name, position, and connections as a bait to draw rich young men into his gambling set, made use of him and amused himself at his expense without letting the other feel it. Apart from the advantage he derived from Anatole, the very process of dominating anotherвҖҷs will was in itself a pleasure, a habit, and a necessity to DГіlokhov. NatГЎsha had made a strong impression on KurГЎgin. At supper after the opera he described to DГіlokhov with the air of a connoisseur the attractions of her arms, shoulders, feet, and hair and expressed his intention of making love to her. Anatole had no notion and was incapable of considering what might come of such lovemaking, as he never had any notion of the outcome of any of his actions. вҖңSheвҖҷs first-rate, my dear fellow, but not for us,вҖқ replied DГіlokhov. вҖңI will tell my sister to ask her to dinner,вҖқ said Anatole. вҖңEh?вҖқ вҖңYouвҖҷd better wait till sheвҖҷs married.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYou know, I adore little girls, they lose their heads at once,вҖқ pursued Anatole. вҖңYou have been caught once already by a вҖҳlittle girl,вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ said DГіlokhov who knew of KurГЎginвҖҷs marriage. вҖңTake care!вҖқ вҖңWell, that canвҖҷt happen twice! Eh?вҖқ said Anatole, with a good-humored laugh. XII XII The day after the opera the RostГіvs went nowhere and nobody came to see them. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna talked to the count about something which they concealed from NatГЎsha. NatГЎsha guessed they were talking about the old prince and planning something, and this disquieted and offended her. She was expecting Prince AndrГ©y any moment and twice that day sent a manservant to the VozdvГӯzhenka to ascertain whether he had come. He had not arrived. She suffered more now than during her first days in Moscow. To her impatience and pining for him were now added the unpleasant recollection of her interview with Princess MГЎrya and the old prince, and a fear and anxiety of which she did not understand the cause. She continually fancied that either he would never come or that something would happen to her before he came. She could no longer think of him by herself calmly and continuously as she had done before. As soon as she began to think of him, the recollection of the old prince, of Princess MГЎrya, of the theater, and of KurГЎgin mingled with her thoughts. The question again presented itself whether she was not guilty, whether she had not already broken faith with Prince AndrГ©y, and again she found herself recalling to the minutest detail every word, every gesture, and every shade in the play of expression on the face of the man who had been able to arouse in her such an incomprehensible and terrifying feeling. To the family NatГЎsha seemed livelier than usual, but she was far less tranquil and happy than before. On Sunday morning MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna invited her visitors to Mass at her parish churchвҒ вҖ”the Church of the Assumption built over the graves of victims of the plague. вҖңI donвҖҷt like those fashionable churches,вҖқ she said, evidently priding herself on her independence of thought. вҖңGod is the same everywhere. We have an excellent priest, he conducts the service decently and with dignity, and the deacon is the same. What holiness is there in giving concerts in the choir? I donвҖҷt like it, itвҖҷs just self-indulgence!вҖқ MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna liked Sundays and knew how to keep them. Her whole house was scrubbed and cleaned on Saturdays; neither she nor the servants worked, and they all wore holiday dress and went to church. At her table there were extra dishes at dinner, and the servants had vodka and roast goose or suckling pig. But in nothing in the house was the holiday so noticeable as in MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs broad, stern face, which on that day wore an invariable look of solemn festivity. After Mass, when they had finished their coffee in the dining room where the loose covers had been removed from the furniture, a servant announced that the carriage was ready, and MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna rose with a stern air. She wore her holiday shawl, in which she paid calls, and announced that she was going to see Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich BolkГіnski to have an explanation with him about NatГЎsha. After she had gone, a dressmaker from Madame Suppert-Roguet waited on the RostГіvs, and NatГЎsha, very glad of this diversion, having shut herself into a room adjoining the drawing room, occupied herself trying on the new dresses. Just as she had put on a bodice without sleeves and only tacked together, and was turning her head to see in the glass how the back fitted, she heard in the drawing room the animated sounds of her fatherвҖҷs voice and anotherвҖҷsвҒ вҖ”a womanвҖҷsвҒ вҖ”that made her flush. It was ElГЁn. NatГЎsha had not time to take off the bodice before the door opened and Countess BezГәkhova, dressed in a purple velvet gown with a high collar, came into the room beaming with good-humored amiable smiles. вҖңOh, my enchantress!вҖқ she cried to the blushing NatГЎsha. вҖңCharming! No, this is really beyond anything, my dear count,вҖқ said she to Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich who had followed her in. вҖңHow can you live in Moscow and go nowhere? No, I wonвҖҷt let you off! Mademoiselle George will recite at my house tonight and thereвҖҷll be some people, and if you donвҖҷt bring your lovely girlsвҒ вҖ”who are prettier than Mademoiselle GeorgeвҒ вҖ”I wonвҖҷt know you! My husband is away in Tver or I would send him to fetch you. You must come. You positively must! Between eight and nine.вҖқ She nodded to the dressmaker, whom she knew and who had curtsied respectfully to her, and seated herself in an armchair beside the looking glass, draping the folds of her velvet dress picturesquely. She did not cease chattering good-naturedly and gaily, continually praising NatГЎshaвҖҷs beauty. She looked at NatГЎshaвҖҷs dresses and praised them, as well as a new dress of her own made of вҖңmetallic gauze,вҖқ which she had received from Paris, and advised NatГЎsha to have one like it. вҖңBut anything suits you, my charmer!вҖқ she remarked. A smile of pleasure never left NatГЎshaвҖҷs face. She felt happy and as if she were blossoming under the praise of this dear Countess BezГәkhova who had formerly seemed to her so unapproachable and important and was now so kind to her. NatГЎsha brightened up and felt almost in love with this woman, who was so beautiful and so kind. ElГЁn for her part was sincerely delighted with NatГЎsha and wished to give her a good time. Anatole had asked her to bring him and NatГЎsha together, and she was calling on the RostГіvs for that purpose. The idea of throwing her brother and NatГЎsha together amused her. Though at one time, in Petersburg, she had been annoyed with NatГЎsha for drawing BorГӯs away, she did not think of that now, and in her own way heartily wished NatГЎsha well. As she was leaving the RostГіvs she called her protГ©gГ©e aside. вҖңMy brother dined with me yesterdayвҒ вҖ”we nearly died of laughterвҒ вҖ”he ate nothing and kept sighing for you, my charmer! He is madly, quite madly, in love with you, my dear.вҖқ NatГЎsha blushed scarlet when she heard this. вҖңHow she blushes, how she blushes, my pretty!вҖқ said ElГЁn. вҖңYou must certainly come. If you love somebody, my charmer, that is not a reason to shut yourself up. Even if you are engaged, I am sure your fiancГ© would wish you to go into society rather than be bored to death.вҖқ вҖңSo she knows I am engaged, and she and her husband PierreвҒ вҖ”that good PierreвҒ вҖ”have talked and laughed about this. So itвҖҷs all right.вҖқ And again, under ElГЁnвҖҷs influence, what had seemed terrible now seemed simple and natural. вҖңAnd she is such a grande dame, so kind, and evidently likes me so much. And why not enjoy myself?вҖқ thought NatГЎsha, gazing at ElГЁn with wide-open, wondering eyes. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna came back to dinner taciturn and serious, having evidently suffered a defeat at the old princeвҖҷs. She was still too agitated by the encounter to be able to talk of the affair calmly. In answer to the countвҖҷs inquiries she replied that things were all right and that she would tell about it next day. On hearing of Countess BezГәkhovaвҖҷs visit and the invitation for that evening, MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna remarked: вҖңI donвҖҷt care to have anything to do with BezГәkhova and donвҖҷt advise you to; however, if youвҖҷve promisedвҒ вҖ”go. It will divert your thoughts,вҖқ she added, addressing NatГЎsha. XIII XIII Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich took the girls to Countess BezГәkhovaвҖҷs. There were a good many people there, but nearly all strangers to NatГЎsha. Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich was displeased to see that the company consisted almost entirely of men and women known for the freedom of their conduct. Mademoiselle George was standing in a corner of the drawing room surrounded by young men. There were several Frenchmen present, among them MГ©tivier who from the time ElГЁn reached Moscow had been an intimate in her house. The count decided not to sit down to cards or let his girls out of his sight and to get away as soon as Mademoiselle GeorgeвҖҷs performance was over. Anatole was at the door, evidently on the lookout for the RostГіvs. Immediately after greeting the count he went up to NatГЎsha and followed her. As soon as she saw him she was seized by the same feeling she had had at the operaвҒ вҖ”gratified vanity at his admiration of her and fear at the absence of a moral barrier between them. ElГЁn welcomed NatГЎsha delightedly and was loud in admiration of her beauty and her dress. Soon after their arrival Mademoiselle George went out of the room to change her costume. In the drawing room people began arranging the chairs and taking their seats. Anatole moved a chair for NatГЎsha and was about to sit down beside her, but the count, who never lost sight of her, took the seat himself. Anatole sat down behind her. Mademoiselle George, with her bare, fat, dimpled arms, and a red shawl draped over one shoulder, came into the space left vacant for her, and assumed an unnatural pose. Enthusiastic whispering was audible. Mademoiselle George looked sternly and gloomily at the audience and began reciting some French verses describing her guilty love for her son. In some places she raised her voice, in others she whispered, lifting her head triumphantly; sometimes she paused and uttered hoarse sounds, rolling her eyes. вҖңAdorable! divine! delicious!вҖқ was heard from every side. NatГЎsha looked at the fat actress, but neither saw nor heard nor understood anything of what went on before her. She only felt herself again completely borne away into this strange senseless worldвҒ вҖ”so remote from her old worldвҒ вҖ”a world in which it was impossible to know what was good or bad, reasonable or senseless. Behind her sat Anatole, and conscious of his proximity she experienced a frightened sense of expectancy. After the first monologue the whole company rose and surrounded Mademoiselle George, expressing their enthusiasm. вҖңHow beautiful she is!вҖқ NatГЎsha remarked to her father who had also risen and was moving through the crowd toward the actress. вҖңI donвҖҷt think so when I look at you!вҖқ said Anatole, following NatГЎsha. He said this at a moment when she alone could hear him. вҖңYou are enchantingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ from the moment I saw you I have never ceasedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңCome, come, NatГЎsha!вҖқ said the count, as he turned back for his daughter. вҖңHow beautiful she is!вҖқ NatГЎsha without saying anything stepped up to her father and looked at him with surprised inquiring eyes. After giving several recitations, Mademoiselle George left, and Countess BezГәkhova asked her visitors into the ballroom. The count wished to go home, but ElГЁn entreated him not to spoil her improvised ball, and the RostГіvs stayed on. Anatole asked NatГЎsha for a valse and as they danced he pressed her waist and hand and told her she was bewitching and that he loved her. During the Г©cossaise, which she also danced with him, Anatole said nothing when they happened to be by themselves, but merely gazed at her. NatГЎsha lifted her frightened eyes to him, but there was such confident tenderness in his affectionate look and smile that she could not, whilst looking at him, say what she had to say. She lowered her eyes. вҖңDonвҖҷt say such things to me. I am betrothed and love another,вҖқ she said rapidly.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She glanced at him. Anatole was not upset or pained by what she had said. вҖңDonвҖҷt speak to me of that! What can I do?вҖқ said he. вҖңI tell you I am madly, madly, in love with you! Is it my fault that you are enchanting?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ItвҖҷs our turn to begin.вҖқ NatГЎsha, animated and excited, looked about her with wide-open frightened eyes and seemed merrier than usual. She understood hardly anything that went on that evening. They danced the Г©cossaise and the Grossvater. Her father asked her to come home, but she begged to remain. Wherever she went and whomever she was speaking to, she felt his eyes upon her. Later on she recalled how she had asked her father to let her go to the dressing room to rearrange her dress, that ElГЁn had followed her and spoken laughingly of her brotherвҖҷs love, and that she again met Anatole in the little sitting room. ElГЁn had disappeared leaving them alone, and Anatole had taken her hand and said in a tender voice: вҖңI cannot come to visit you but is it possible that I shall never see you? I love you madly. Can I neverвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ?вҖқ and, blocking her path, he brought his face close to hers. His large, glittering, masculine eyes were so close to hers that she saw nothing but them. вҖңNatГЎli?вҖқ he whispered inquiringly while she felt her hands being painfully pressed. вҖңNatГЎli?вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt understand. I have nothing to say,вҖқ her eyes replied. Burning lips were pressed to hers, and at the same instant she felt herself released, and ElГЁnвҖҷs footsteps and the rustle of her dress were heard in the room. NatГЎsha looked round at her, and then, red and trembling, threw a frightened look of inquiry at Anatole and moved toward the door. вҖңOne word, just one, for GodвҖҷs sake!вҖқ cried Anatole. She paused. She so wanted a word from him that would explain to her what had happened and to which she could find no answer. вҖңNathalie, just a word, only one!вҖқ he kept repeating, evidently not knowing what to say and he repeated it till ElГЁn came up to them. ElГЁn returned with NatГЎsha to the drawing room. The RostГіvs went away without staying for supper. After reaching home NatГЎsha did not sleep all night. She was tormented by the insoluble question whether she loved Anatole or Prince AndrГ©y. She loved Prince AndrГ©yвҒ вҖ”she remembered distinctly how deeply she loved him. But she also loved Anatole, of that there was no doubt. вҖңElse how could all this have happened?вҖқ thought she. вҖңIf, after that, I could return his smile when saying goodbye, if I was able to let it come to that, it means that I loved him from the first. It means that he is kind, noble, and splendid, and I could not help loving him. What am I to do if I love him and the other one too?вҖқ she asked herself, unable to find an answer to these terrible questions. XIV XIV Morning came with its cares and bustle. Everyone got up and began to move about and talk, dressmakers came again. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna appeared, and they were called to breakfast. NatГЎsha kept looking uneasily at everybody with wide-open eyes, as if wishing to intercept every glance directed toward her, and tried to appear the same as usual. After breakfast, which was her best time, MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna sat down in her armchair and called NatГЎsha and the count to her. вҖңWell, friends, I have now thought the whole matter over and this is my advice,вҖқ she began. вҖңYesterday, as you know, I went to see Prince BolkГіnski. Well, I had a talk with him.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ He took it into his head to begin shouting, but I am not one to be shouted down. I said what I had to say!вҖқ вҖңWell, and he?вҖқ asked the count. вҖңHe? HeвҖҷs crazyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ he did not want to listen. But whatвҖҷs the use of talking? As it is we have worn the poor girl out,вҖқ said MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. вҖңMy advice to you is finish your business and go back home to OtrГЎdnoeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and wait there.вҖқ вҖңOh, no!вҖқ exclaimed NatГЎsha. вҖңYes, go back,вҖқ said MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, вҖңand wait there. If your betrothed comes here nowвҒ вҖ”there will be no avoiding a quarrel; but alone with the old man he will talk things over and then come on to you.вҖқ Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich approved of this suggestion, appreciating its reasonableness. If the old man came round it would be all the better to visit him in Moscow or at Bald Hills later on; and if not, the wedding, against his wishes, could only be arranged at OtrГЎdnoe. вҖңThat is perfectly true. And I am sorry I went to see him and took her,вҖқ said the old count. вҖңNo, why be sorry? Being here, you had to pay your respects. But if he wonвҖҷtвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs his affair,вҖқ said MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, looking for something in her reticule. вҖңBesides, the trousseau is ready, so there is nothing to wait for; and what is not ready IвҖҷll send after you. Though I donвҖҷt like letting you go, it is the best way. So go, with GodвҖҷs blessing!вҖқ Having found what she was looking for in the reticule she handed it to NatГЎsha. It was a letter from Princess MГЎrya. вҖңShe has written to you. How she torments herself, poor thing! SheвҖҷs afraid you might think that she does not like you.вҖқ вҖңBut she doesnвҖҷt like me,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңDonвҖҷt talk nonsense!вҖқ cried MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. вҖңI shanвҖҷt believe anyone, I know she doesnвҖҷt like me,вҖқ replied NatГЎsha boldly as she took the letter, and her face expressed a cold and angry resolution that caused MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna to look at her more intently and to frown. вҖңDonвҖҷt answer like that, my good girl!вҖқ she said. вҖңWhat I say is true! Write an answer!вҖқ NatГЎsha did not reply and went to her own room to read Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs letter. Princess MГЎrya wrote that she was in despair at the misunderstanding that had occurred between them. Whatever her fatherвҖҷs feelings might be, she begged NatГЎsha to believe that she could not help loving her as the one chosen by her brother, for whose happiness she was ready to sacrifice everything. вҖңDo not think, however,вҖқ she wrote, вҖңthat my father is ill-disposed toward you. He is an invalid and an old man who must be forgiven; but he is good and magnanimous and will love her who makes his son happy.вҖқ Princess MГЎrya went on to ask NatГЎsha to fix a time when she could see her again. After reading the letter NatГЎsha sat down at the writing table to answer it. вҖңDear Princess,вҖқ she wrote in French quickly and mechanically, and then paused. What more could she write after all that had happened the evening before? вҖңYes, yes! All that has happened, and now all is changed,вҖқ she thought as she sat with the letter she had begun before her. вҖңMust I break off with him? Must I really? ThatвҖҷs awfulвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and to escape from these dreadful thoughts she went to SГіnya and began sorting patterns with her. After dinner NatГЎsha went to her room and again took up Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs letter. вҖңCan it be that it is all over?вҖқ she thought. вҖңCan it be that all this has happened so quickly and has destroyed all that went before?вҖқ She recalled her love for Prince AndrГ©y in all its former strength, and at the same time felt that she loved KurГЎgin. She vividly pictured herself as Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs wife, and the scenes of happiness with him she had so often repeated in her imagination, and at the same time, aglow with excitement, recalled every detail of yesterdayвҖҷs interview with Anatole. вҖңWhy could that not be as well?вҖқ she sometimes asked herself in complete bewilderment. вҖңOnly so could I be completely happy; but now I have to choose, and I canвҖҷt be happy without either of them. Only,вҖқ she thought, вҖңto tell Prince AndrГ©y what has happened or to hide it from him are both equally impossible. But with that one nothing is spoiled. But am I really to abandon forever the joy of Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs love, in which I have lived so long?вҖқ вҖңPlease, Miss!вҖқ whispered a maid entering the room with a mysterious air. вҖңA man told me to give you thisвҒ вҖ”вҖқ and she handed NatГЎsha a letter. вҖңOnly, for ChristвҖҷs sakeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ the girl went on, as NatГЎsha, without thinking, mechanically broke the seal and read a love letter from Anatole, of which, without taking in a word, she understood only that it was a letter from himвҒ вҖ”from the man she loved. Yes, she loved him, or else how could that have happened which had happened? And how could she have a love letter from him in her hand? With trembling hands NatГЎsha held that passionate love letter which DГіlokhov had composed for Anatole, and as she read it she found in it an echo of all that she herself imagined she was feeling. вҖңSince yesterday evening my fate has been sealed; to be loved by you or to die. There is no other way for me,вҖқ the letter began. Then he went on to say that he knew her parents would not give her to himвҒ вҖ”for this there were secret reasons he could reveal only to herвҒ вҖ”but that if she loved him she need only say the word yes, and no human power could hinder their bliss. Love would conquer all. He would steal her away and carry her off to the ends of the earth. вҖңYes, yes! I love him!вҖқ thought NatГЎsha, reading the letter for the twentieth time and finding some peculiarly deep meaning in each word of it. That evening MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna was going to the AkhГЎrovsвҖҷ and proposed to take the girls with her. NatГЎsha, pleading a headache, remained at home. XV XV On returning late in the evening SГіnya went to NatГЎshaвҖҷs room, and to her surprise found her still dressed and asleep on the sofa. Open on the table, beside her lay AnatoleвҖҷs letter. SГіnya picked it up and read it. As she read she glanced at the sleeping NatГЎsha, trying to find in her face an explanation of what she was reading, but did not find it. Her face was calm, gentle, and happy. Clutching her breast to keep herself from choking, SГіnya, pale and trembling with fear and agitation, sat down in an armchair and burst into tears. вҖңHow was it I noticed nothing? How could it go so far? Can she have left off loving Prince AndrГ©y? And how could she let KurГЎgin go to such lengths? He is a deceiver and a villain, thatвҖҷs plain! What will Nicolas, dear noble Nicolas, do when he hears of it? So this is the meaning of her excited, resolute, unnatural look the day before yesterday, yesterday, and today,вҖқ thought SГіnya. вҖңBut it canвҖҷt be that she loves him! She probably opened the letter without knowing who it was from. Probably she is offended by it. She could not do such a thing!вҖқ SГіnya wiped away her tears and went up to NatГЎsha, again scanning her face. вҖңNatГЎsha!вҖқ she said, just audibly. NatГЎsha awoke and saw SГіnya. вҖңAh, youвҖҷre back?вҖқ And with the decision and tenderness that often come at the moment of awakening, she embraced her friend, but noticing SГіnyaвҖҷs look of embarrassment, her own face expressed confusion and suspicion. вҖңSГіnya, youвҖҷve read that letter?вҖқ she demanded. вҖңYes,вҖқ answered SГіnya softly. NatГЎsha smiled rapturously. вҖңNo, SГіnya, I canвҖҷt any longer!вҖқ she said. вҖңI canвҖҷt hide it from you any longer. You know, we love one another! SГіnya, darling, he writesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ SГіnyaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ SГіnya stared open-eyed at NatГЎsha, unable to believe her ears. вҖңAnd BolkГіnski?вҖқ she asked. вҖңAh, SГіnya, if you only knew how happy I am!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha. вҖңYou donвҖҷt know what love is.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңBut, NatГЎsha, can that be all over?вҖқ NatГЎsha looked at SГіnya with wide-open eyes as if she could not grasp the question. вҖңWell, then, are you refusing Prince AndrГ©y?вҖқ said SГіnya. вҖңOh, you donвҖҷt understand anything! DonвҖҷt talk nonsense, just listen!вҖқ said NatГЎsha, with momentary vexation. вҖңBut I canвҖҷt believe it,вҖқ insisted SГіnya. вҖңI donвҖҷt understand. How is it you have loved a man for a whole year and suddenlyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Why, you have only seen him three times! NatГЎsha, I donвҖҷt believe you, youвҖҷre joking! In three days to forget everything and soвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңThree days?вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңIt seems to me IвҖҷve loved him a hundred years. It seems to me that I have never loved anyone before. You canвҖҷt understand it.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ SГіnya, wait a bit, sit here,вҖқ and NatГЎsha embraced and kissed her. вҖңI had heard that it happens like this, and you must have heard it too, but itвҖҷs only now that I feel such love. ItвҖҷs not the same as before. As soon as I saw him I felt he was my master and I his slave, and that I could not help loving him. Yes, his slave! Whatever he orders I shall do. You donвҖҷt understand that. What can I do? What can I do, SГіnya?вҖқ cried NatГЎsha with a happy yet frightened expression. вҖңBut think what you are doing,вҖқ cried SГіnya. вҖңI canвҖҷt leave it like this. This secret correspondenceвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ How could you let him go so far?вҖқ she went on, with a horror and disgust she could hardly conceal. вҖңI told you that I have no will,вҖқ NatГЎsha replied. вҖңWhy canвҖҷt you understand? I love him!вҖқ вҖңThen I wonвҖҷt let it come to thatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I shall tell!вҖқ cried SГіnya, bursting into tears. вҖңWhat do you mean? For GodвҖҷs sakeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ If you tell, you are my enemy!вҖқ declared NatГЎsha. вҖңYou want me to be miserable, you want us to be separated.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ When she saw NatГЎshaвҖҷs fright, SГіnya shed tears of shame and pity for her friend. вҖңBut what has happened between you?вҖқ she asked. вҖңWhat has he said to you? Why doesnвҖҷt he come to the house?вҖқ NatГЎsha did not answer her questions. вҖңFor GodвҖҷs sake, SГіnya, donвҖҷt tell anyone, donвҖҷt torture me,вҖқ NatГЎsha entreated. вҖңRemember no one ought to interfere in such matters! I have confided in you.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңBut why this secrecy? Why doesnвҖҷt he come to the house?вҖқ asked SГіnya. вҖңWhy doesnвҖҷt he openly ask for your hand? You know Prince AndrГ©y gave you complete freedomвҒ вҖ”if it is really so; but I donвҖҷt believe it! NatГЎsha, have you considered what these secret reasons can be?вҖқ NatГЎsha looked at SГіnya with astonishment. Evidently this question presented itself to her mind for the first time and she did not know how to answer it. вҖңI donвҖҷt know what the reasons are. But there must be reasons!вҖқ SГіnya sighed and shook her head incredulously. вҖңIf there were reasonsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she began. But NatГЎsha, guessing her doubts, interrupted her in alarm. вҖңSГіnya, one canвҖҷt doubt him! One canвҖҷt, one canвҖҷt! DonвҖҷt you understand?вҖқ she cried. вҖңDoes he love you?вҖқ вҖңDoes he love me?вҖқ NatГЎsha repeated with a smile of pity at her friendвҖҷs lack of comprehension. вҖңWhy, you have read his letter and you have seen him.вҖқ вҖңBut if he is dishonorable?вҖқ вҖңHe! dishonorable? If you only knew!вҖқ exclaimed NatГЎsha. вҖңIf he is an honorable man he should either declare his intentions or cease seeing you; and if you wonвҖҷt do this, I will. I will write to him, and I will tell Papa!вҖқ said SГіnya resolutely. вҖңBut I canвҖҷt live without him!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha. вҖңNatГЎsha, I donвҖҷt understand you. And what are you saying! Think of your father and of Nicolas.вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt want anyone, I donвҖҷt love anyone but him. How dare you say he is dishonorable? DonвҖҷt you know that I love him?вҖқ screamed NatГЎsha. вҖңGo away, SГіnya! I donвҖҷt want to quarrel with you, but go, for GodвҖҷs sake go! You see how I am suffering!вҖқ NatГЎsha cried angrily, in a voice of despair and repressed irritation. SГіnya burst into sobs and ran from the room. NatГЎsha went to the table and without a momentвҖҷs reflection wrote that answer to Princess MГЎrya which she had been unable to write all the morning. In this letter she said briefly that all their misunderstandings were at an end; that availing herself of the magnanimity of Prince AndrГ©y who when he went abroad had given her her freedom, she begged Princess MГЎrya to forget everything and forgive her if she had been to blame toward her, but that she could not be his wife. At that moment this all seemed quite easy, simple, and clear to NatГЎsha. On Friday the RostГіvs were to return to the country, but on Wednesday the count went with the prospective purchaser to his estate near Moscow. On the day the count left, SГіnya and NatГЎsha were invited to a big dinner party at the KarГЎginsвҖҷ, and MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna took them there. At that party NatГЎsha again met Anatole, and SГіnya noticed that she spoke to him, trying not to be overheard, and that all through dinner she was more agitated than ever. When they got home NatГЎsha was the first to begin the explanation SГіnya expected. вҖңThere, SГіnya, you were talking all sorts of nonsense about him,вҖқ NatГЎsha began in a mild voice such as children use when they wish to be praised. вҖңWe have had an explanation today.вҖқ вҖңWell, what happened? What did he say? NatГЎsha, how glad I am youвҖҷre not angry with me! Tell me everythingвҒ вҖ”the whole truth. What did he say?вҖқ NatГЎsha became thoughtful. вҖңOh, SГіnya, if you knew him as I do! He saidвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ He asked me what I had promised BolkГіnski. He was glad I was free to refuse him.вҖқ SГіnya sighed sorrowfully. вҖңBut you havenвҖҷt refused BolkГіnski?вҖқ said she. вҖңPerhaps I have. Perhaps all is over between me and BolkГіnski. Why do you think so badly of me?вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt think anything, only I donвҖҷt understand thisвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWait a bit, SГіnya, youвҖҷll understand everything. YouвҖҷll see what a man he is! Now donвҖҷt think badly of me or of him. I donвҖҷt think badly of anyone: I love and pity everybody. But what am I to do?вҖқ SГіnya did not succumb to the tender tone NatГЎsha used toward her. The more emotional and ingratiating the expression of NatГЎshaвҖҷs face became, the more serious and stern grew SГіnyaвҖҷs. вҖңNatГЎsha,вҖқ said she, вҖңyou asked me not to speak to you, and I havenвҖҷt spoken, but now you yourself have begun. I donвҖҷt trust him, NatГЎsha. Why this secrecy?вҖқ вҖңAgain, again!вҖқ interrupted NatГЎsha. вҖңNatГЎsha, I am afraid for you!вҖқ вҖңAfraid of what?вҖқ вҖңI am afraid youвҖҷre going to your ruin,вҖқ said SГіnya resolutely, and was herself horrified at what she had said. Anger again showed in NatГЎshaвҖҷs face. вҖңAnd IвҖҷll go to my ruin, I will, as soon as possible! ItвҖҷs not your business! It wonвҖҷt be you, but I, whoвҖҷll suffer. Leave me alone, leave me alone! I hate you!вҖқ вҖңNatГЎsha!вҖқ moaned SГіnya, aghast. вҖңI hate you, I hate you! YouвҖҷre my enemy forever!вҖқ And NatГЎsha ran out of the room. NatГЎsha did not speak to SГіnya again and avoided her. With the same expression of agitated surprise and guilt she went about the house, taking up now one occupation, now another, and at once abandoning them. Hard as it was for SГіnya, she watched her friend and did not let her out of her sight. The day before the count was to return, SГіnya noticed that NatГЎsha sat by the drawing room window all the morning as if expecting something and that she made a sign to an officer who drove past, whom SГіnya took to be Anatole. SГіnya began watching her friend still more attentively and noticed that at dinner and all that evening NatГЎsha was in a strange and unnatural state. She answered questions at random, began sentences she did not finish, and laughed at everything. After tea SГіnya noticed a housemaid at NatГЎshaвҖҷs door timidly waiting to let her pass. She let the girl go in, and then listening at the door learned that another letter had been delivered. Then suddenly it became clear to SГіnya that NatГЎsha had some dreadful plan for that evening. SГіnya knocked at her door. NatГЎsha did not let her in. вҖңShe will run away with him!вҖқ thought SГіnya. вҖңShe is capable of anything. There was something particularly pathetic and resolute in her face today. She cried as she said goodbye to Uncle,вҖқ SГіnya remembered. вҖңYes, thatвҖҷs it, she means to elope with him, but what am I to do?вҖқ thought she, recalling all the signs that clearly indicated that NatГЎsha had some terrible intention. вҖңThe count is away. What am I to do? Write to KurГЎgin demanding an explanation? But what is there to oblige him to reply? Write to Pierre, as Prince AndrГ©y asked me to in case of some misfortune?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But perhaps she really has already refused BolkГіnskiвҒ вҖ”she sent a letter to Princess MГЎrya yesterday. And Uncle is away.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ To tell MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna who had such faith in NatГЎsha seemed to SГіnya terrible. вҖңWell, anyway,вҖқ thought SГіnya as she stood in the dark passage, вҖңnow or never I must prove that I remember the familyвҖҷs goodness to me and that I love Nicolas. Yes! If I donвҖҷt sleep for three nights IвҖҷll not leave this passage and will hold her back by force and will and not let the family be disgraced,вҖқ thought she. XVI XVI Anatole had lately moved to DГіlokhovвҖҷs. The plan for Natalie RostГіvaвҖҷs abduction had been arranged and the preparations made by DГіlokhov a few days before, and on the day that SГіnya, after listening at NatГЎshaвҖҷs door, resolved to safeguard her, it was to have been put into execution. NatГЎsha had promised to come out to KurГЎgin at the back porch at ten that evening. KurГЎgin was to put her into a troyka he would have ready and to drive her forty miles to the village of KГЎmenka, where an unfrocked priest was in readiness to perform a marriage ceremony over them. At KГЎmenka a relay of horses was to wait which would take them to the Warsaw high road, and from there they would hasten abroad with post horses. Anatole had a passport, an order for post horses, ten thousand rubles he had taken from his sister and another ten thousand borrowed with DГіlokhovвҖҷs help. Two witnesses for the mock marriageвҒ вҖ”KhvГіstikov, a retired petty official whom DГіlokhov made use of in his gambling transactions, and MakГЎrin, a retired hussar, a kindly, weak fellow who had an unbounded affection for KurГЎginвҒ вҖ”were sitting at tea in DГіlokhovвҖҷs front room. In his large study, the walls of which were hung to the ceiling with Persian rugs, bearskins, and weapons, sat DГіlokhov in a traveling cloak and high boots, at an open desk on which lay an abacus and some bundles of paper money. Anatole, with uniform unbuttoned, walked to and fro from the room where the witnesses were sitting, through the study to the room behind, where his French valet and others were packing the last of his things. DГіlokhov was counting the money and noting something down. вҖңWell,вҖқ he said, вҖңKhvГіstikov must have two thousand.вҖқ вҖңGive it to him, then,вҖқ said Anatole. вҖңMakГЎrkaвҖқ (their name for MakГЎrin) вҖңwill go through fire and water for you for nothing. So here are our accounts all settled,вҖқ said DГіlokhov, showing him the memorandum. вҖңIs that right?вҖқ вҖңYes, of course,вҖқ returned Anatole, evidently not listening to DГіlokhov and looking straight before him with a smile that did not leave his face. DГіlokhov banged down the lid of his desk and turned to Anatole with an ironic smile: вҖңDo you know? YouвҖҷd really better drop it all. ThereвҖҷs still time!вҖқ вҖңFool,вҖқ retorted Anatole. вҖңDonвҖҷt talk nonsense! If you only knewвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ itвҖҷs the devil knows what!вҖқ вҖңNo, really, give it up!вҖқ said DГіlokhov. вҖңI am speaking seriously. ItвҖҷs no joke, this plot youвҖҷve hatched.вҖқ вҖңWhat, teasing again? Go to the devil! Eh?вҖқ said Anatole, making a grimace. вҖңReally itвҖҷs no time for your stupid jokes,вҖқ and he left the room. DГіlokhov smiled contemptuously and condescendingly when Anatole had gone out. вҖңYou wait a bit,вҖқ he called after him. вҖңIвҖҷm not joking, IвҖҷm talking sense. Come here, come here!вҖқ Anatole returned and looked at DГіlokhov, trying to give him his attention and evidently submitting to him involuntarily. вҖңNow listen to me. IвҖҷm telling you this for the last time. Why should I joke about it? Did I hinder you? Who arranged everything for you? Who found the priest and got the passport? Who raised the money? I did it all.вҖқ вҖңWell, thank you for it. Do you think I am not grateful?вҖқ And Anatole sighed and embraced DГіlokhov. вҖңI helped you, but all the same I must tell you the truth; it is a dangerous business, and if you think about itвҒ вҖ”a stupid business. Well, youвҖҷll carry her offвҒ вҖ”all right! Will they let it stop at that? It will come out that youвҖҷre already married. Why, theyвҖҷll have you in the criminal court.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңOh, nonsense, nonsense!вҖқ Anatole ejaculated and again made a grimace. вҖңDidnвҖҷt I explain to you? What?вҖқ And Anatole, with the partiality dull-witted people have for any conclusion they have reached by their own reasoning, repeated the argument he had already put to DГіlokhov a hundred times. вҖңDidnвҖҷt I explain to you that I have come to this conclusion: if this marriage is invalid,вҖқ he went on, crooking one finger, вҖңthen I have nothing to answer for; but if it is valid, no matter! Abroad no one will know anything about it. IsnвҖҷt that so? And donвҖҷt talk to me, donвҖҷt, donвҖҷt.вҖқ вҖңSeriously, youвҖҷd better drop it! YouвҖҷll only get yourself into a mess!вҖқ вҖңGo to the devil!вҖқ cried Anatole and, clutching his hair, left the room, but returned at once and dropped into an armchair in front of DГіlokhov with his feet turned under him. вҖңItвҖҷs the very devil! What? Feel how it beats!вҖқ He took DГіlokhovвҖҷs hand and put it on his heart. вҖңWhat a foot, my dear fellow! What a glance! A goddess!вҖқ he added in French. вҖңWhat?вҖқ DГіlokhov with a cold smile and a gleam in his handsome insolent eyes looked at himвҒ вҖ”evidently wishing to get some more amusement out of him. вҖңWell and when the moneyвҖҷs gone, what then?вҖқ вҖңWhat then? Eh?вҖқ repeated Anatole, sincerely perplexed by a thought of the future. вҖңWhat then?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Then, I donвҖҷt know.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But why talk nonsense!вҖқ He glanced at his watch. вҖңItвҖҷs time!вҖқ Anatole went into the back room. вҖңNow then! Nearly ready? YouвҖҷre dawdling!вҖқ he shouted to the servants. DГіlokhov put away the money, called a footman whom he ordered to bring something for them to eat and drink before the journey, and went into the room where KhvГіstikov and MakГЎrin were sitting. Anatole lay on the sofa in the study leaning on his elbow and smiling pensively, while his handsome lips muttered tenderly to himself. вҖңCome and eat something. Have a drink!вҖқ DГіlokhov shouted to him from the other room. вҖңI donвҖҷt want to,вҖқ answered Anatole continuing to smile. вҖңCome! BalagГЎ is here.вҖқ Anatole rose and went into the dining room. BalagГЎ was a famous troyka driver who had known DГіlokhov and Anatole some six years and had given them good service with his troykas. More than once when AnatoleвҖҷs regiment was stationed at Tver he had taken him from Tver in the evening, brought him to Moscow by daybreak, and driven him back again the next night. More than once he had enabled DГіlokhov to escape when pursued. More than once he had driven them through the town with gypsies and вҖңladykinsвҖқ as he called the cocottes. More than once in their service he had run over pedestrians and upset vehicles in the streets of Moscow and had always been protected from the consequences by вҖңmy gentlemenвҖқ as he called them. He had ruined more than one horse in their service. More than once they had beaten him, and more than once they had made him drunk on champagne and Madeira, which he loved; and he knew more than one thing about each of them which would long ago have sent an ordinary man to Siberia. They often called BalagГЎ into their orgies and made him drink and dance at the gypsiesвҖҷ, and more than one thousand rubles of their money had passed through his hands. In their service he risked his skin and his life twenty times a year, and in their service had lost more horses than the money he had from them would buy. But he liked them; liked that mad driving at twelve miles an hour, liked upsetting a driver or running down a pedestrian, and flying at full gallop through the Moscow streets. He liked to hear those wild, tipsy shouts behind him: вҖңGet on! Get on!вҖқ when it was impossible to go any faster. He liked giving a painful lash on the neck to some peasant who, more dead than alive, was already hurrying out of his way. вҖңReal gentlemen!вҖқ he considered them. Anatole and DГіlokhov liked BalagГЎ too for his masterly driving and because he liked the things they liked. With others BalagГЎ bargained, charging twenty-five rubles for a two hoursвҖҷ drive, and rarely drove himself, generally letting his young men do so. But with вҖңhis gentlemenвҖқ he always drove himself and never demanded anything for his work. Only a couple of times a yearвҒ вҖ”when he knew from their valets that they had money in handвҒ вҖ”he would turn up of a morning quite sober and with a deep bow would ask them to help him. The gentlemen always made him sit down. вҖңDo help me out, FГ«dor IvГЎnych, sir,вҖқ or вҖңyour excellency,вҖқ he would say. вҖңI am quite out of horses. Let me have what you can to go to the fair.вҖқ And Anatole and DГіlokhov, when they had money, would give him a thousand or a couple of thousand rubles. BalagГЎ was a fair-haired, short, and snub-nosed peasant of about twenty-seven; red-faced, with a particularly red thick neck, glittering little eyes, and a small beard. He wore a fine, dark-blue, silklined cloth coat over a sheepskin. On entering the room now he crossed himself, turning toward the front corner of the room, and went up to DГіlokhov, holding out a small, black hand. вҖңFГ«dor IvГЎnych!вҖқ he said, bowing. вҖңHow dвҖҷyou do, friend? Well, here he is!вҖқ вҖңGood day, your excellency!вҖқ he said, again holding out his hand to Anatole who had just come in. вҖңI say, BalagГЎ,вҖқ said Anatole, putting his hands on the manвҖҷs shoulders, вҖңdo you care for me or not? Eh? Now, do me a service.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What horses have you come with? Eh?вҖқ вҖңAs your messenger ordered, your special beasts,вҖқ replied BalagГЎ. вҖңWell, listen, BalagГЎ! Drive all three to death but get me there in three hours. Eh?вҖқ вҖңWhen they are dead, what shall I drive?вҖқ said BalagГЎ with a wink. вҖңMind, IвҖҷll smash your face in! DonвҖҷt make jokes!вҖқ cried Anatole, suddenly rolling his eyes. вҖңWhy joke?вҖқ said the driver, laughing. вҖңAs if IвҖҷd grudge my gentlemen anything! As fast as ever the horses can gallop, so fast weвҖҷll go!вҖқ вҖңAh!вҖқ said Anatole. вҖңWell, sit down.вҖқ вҖңYes, sit down!вҖқ said DГіlokhov. вҖңIвҖҷll stand, FГ«dor IvГЎnych.вҖқ вҖңSit down; nonsense! Have a drink!вҖқ said Anatole, and filled a large glass of Madeira for him. The driverвҖҷs eyes sparkled at the sight of the wine. After refusing it for mannersвҖҷ sake, he drank it and wiped his mouth with a red silk handkerchief he took out of his cap. вҖңAnd when are we to start, your excellency?вҖқ вҖңWellвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Anatole looked at his watch. вҖңWeвҖҷll start at once. Mind, BalagГЎ! YouвҖҷll get there in time? Eh?вҖқ вҖңThat depends on our luck in starting, else why shouldnвҖҷt we be there in time?вҖқ replied BalagГЎ. вҖңDidnвҖҷt we get you to Tver in seven hours? I think you remember that, your excellency?вҖқ вҖңDo you know, one Christmas I drove from Tver,вҖқ said Anatole, smilingly at the recollection and turning to MakГЎrin who gazed rapturously at him with wide-open eyes. вҖңWill you believe it, MakГЎrka, it took oneвҖҷs breath away, the rate we flew. We came across a train of loaded sleighs and drove right over two of them. Eh?вҖқ вҖңThose were horses!вҖқ BalagГЎ continued the tale. вҖңThat time IвҖҷd harnessed two young side horses with the bay in the shafts,вҖқ he went on, turning to DГіlokhov. вҖңWill you believe it, FГ«dor IvГЎnych, those animals flew forty miles? I couldnвҖҷt hold them in, my hands grew numb in the sharp frost so that I threw down the reinsвҒ вҖ”вҖҳCatch hold yourself, your excellency!вҖҷ says I, and I just tumbled on the bottom of the sleigh and sprawled there. It wasnвҖҷt a case of urging them on, there was no holding them in till we reached the place. The devils took us there in three hours! Only the near one died of it.вҖқ XVII XVII Anatole went out of the room and returned a few minutes later wearing a fur coat girt with a silver belt, and a sable cap jauntily set on one side and very becoming to his handsome face. Having looked in a mirror, and standing before DГіlokhov in the same pose he had assumed before it, he lifted a glass of wine. вҖңWell, goodbye, FГ©dya. Thank you for everything and farewell!вҖқ said Anatole. вҖңWell, comrades and friendsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he considered for a moment вҖңвҖҰВ of my youth, farewell!вҖқ he said, turning to MakГЎrin and the others. Though they were all going with him, Anatole evidently wished to make something touching and solemn out of this address to his comrades. He spoke slowly in a loud voice and throwing out his chest slightly swayed one leg. вҖңAll take glasses; you too, BalagГЎ. Well, comrades and friends of my youth, weвҖҷve had our fling and lived and reveled. Eh? And now, when shall we meet again? I am going abroad. We have had a good timeвҒ вҖ”now farewell, lads! To our health! Hurrah!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he cried, and emptying his glass flung it on the floor. вҖңTo your health!вҖқ said BalagГЎ who also emptied his glass, and wiped his mouth with his handkerchief. MakГЎrin embraced Anatole with tears in his eyes. вҖңAh, Prince, how sorry I am to part from you!вҖқ вҖңLetвҖҷs go. LetвҖҷs go!вҖқ cried Anatole. BalagГЎ was about to leave the room. вҖңNo, stop!вҖқ said Anatole. вҖңShut the door; we have first to sit down. ThatвҖҷs the way.вҖқ They shut the door and all sat down. вҖңNow, quick march, lads!вҖқ said Anatole, rising. Joseph, his valet, handed him his sabretache and saber, and they all went out into the vestibule. вҖңAnd whereвҖҷs the fur cloak?вҖқ asked DГіlokhov. вҖңHey, IgnГЎtka! Go to MatrГ«na MatrГ©vna and ask her for the sable cloak. I have heard what elopements are like,вҖқ continued DГіlokhov with a wink. вҖңWhy, sheвҖҷll rush out more dead than alive just in the things she is wearing; if you delay at all thereвҖҷll be tears and вҖҳPapaвҖҷ and вҖҳMamma,вҖҷ and sheвҖҷs frozen in a minute and must go backвҒ вҖ”but you wrap the fur cloak round her first thing and carry her to the sleigh.вҖқ The valet brought a womanвҖҷs fox-lined cloak. вҖңFool, I told you the sable one! Hey, MatrГ«shka, the sable!вҖқ he shouted so that his voice rang far through the rooms. A handsome, slim, and pale-faced gypsy girl with glittering black eyes and curly blue-black hair, wearing a red shawl, ran out with a sable mantle on her arm. вҖңHere, I donвҖҷt grudge itвҒ вҖ”take it!вҖқ she said, evidently afraid of her master and yet regretful of her cloak. DГіlokhov, without answering, took the cloak, threw it over MatrГ«sha, and wrapped her up in it. вҖңThatвҖҷs the way,вҖқ said DГіlokhov, вҖңand then so!вҖқ and he turned the collar up round her head, leaving only a little of the face uncovered. вҖңAnd then so, do you see?вҖқ and he pushed AnatoleвҖҷs head forward to meet the gap left by the collar, through which MatrГ«shaвҖҷs brilliant smile was seen. вҖңWell, goodbye, MatrГ«sha,вҖқ said Anatole, kissing her. вҖңAh, my revels here are over. Remember me to StГ«shka. There, goodbye! Goodbye, MatrГ«sha, wish me luck!вҖқ вҖңWell, Prince, may God give you great luck!вҖқ said MatrГ«sha in her gypsy accent. Two troykas were standing before the porch and two young drivers were holding the horses. BalagГЎ took his seat in the front one and holding his elbows high arranged the reins deliberately. Anatole and DГіlokhov got in with him. MakГЎrin, KhvГіstikov, and a valet seated themselves in the other sleigh. вҖңWell, are you ready?вҖқ asked BalagГЎ. вҖңGo!вҖқ he cried, twisting the reins round his hands, and the troyka tore down the NikГӯtski Boulevard. вҖңTproo! Get out of the way! Hi!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Tproo!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The shouting of BalagГЎ and of the sturdy young fellow seated on the box was all that could be heard. On the ArbГЎt Square the troyka caught against a carriage; something cracked, shouts were heard, and the troyka flew along the ArbГЎt Street. After taking a turn along the PodnovГӯnski Boulevard, BalagГЎ began to rein in, and turning back drew up at the crossing of the old KonyГәsheny Street. The young fellow on the box jumped down to hold the horses and Anatole and DГіlokhov went along the pavement. When they reached the gate DГіlokhov whistled. The whistle was answered, and a maidservant ran out. вҖңCome into the courtyard or youвҖҷll be seen; sheвҖҷll come out directly,вҖқ said she. DГіlokhov stayed by the gate. Anatole followed the maid into the courtyard, turned the corner, and ran up into the porch. He was met by GavrГӯlo, MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs gigantic footman. вҖңCome to the mistress, please,вҖқ said the footman in his deep bass, intercepting any retreat. вҖңTo what Mistress? Who are you?вҖқ asked Anatole in a breathless whisper. вҖңKindly step in, my orders are to bring you in.вҖқ вҖңKurГЎgin! Come back!вҖқ shouted DГіlokhov. вҖңBetrayed! Back!вҖқ DГіlokhov, after Anatole entered, had remained at the wicket gate and was struggling with the yard porter who was trying to lock it. With a last desperate effort DГіlokhov pushed the porter aside, and when Anatole ran back seized him by the arm, pulled him through the wicket, and ran back with him to the troyka. XVIII XVIII MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, having found SГіnya weeping in the corridor, made her confess everything, and intercepting the note to NatГЎsha she read it and went into NatГЎshaвҖҷs room with it in her hand. вҖңYou shameless good-for-nothing!вҖқ said she. вҖңI wonвҖҷt hear a word.вҖқ Pushing back NatГЎsha who looked at her with astonished but tearless eyes, she locked her in; and having given orders to the yard porter to admit the persons who would be coming that evening, but not to let them out again, and having told the footman to bring them up to her, she seated herself in the drawing room to await the abductors. When GavrГӯlo came to inform her that the men who had come had run away again, she rose frowning, and clasping her hands behind her paced through the rooms a long time considering what she should do. Toward midnight she went to NatГЎshaвҖҷs room fingering the key in her pocket. SГіnya was sitting sobbing in the corridor. вҖңMГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, for GodвҖҷs sake let me in to her!вҖқ she pleaded, but MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna unlocked the door and went in without giving her an answer.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңDisgusting, abominableвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ In my houseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ horrid girl, hussy! IвҖҷm only sorry for her father!вҖқ thought she, trying to restrain her wrath. вҖңHard as it may be, IвҖҷll tell them all to hold their tongues and will hide it from the count.вҖқ She entered the room with resolute steps. NatГЎsha lying on the sofa, her head hidden in her hands, and she did not stir. She was in just the same position in which MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna had left her. вҖңA nice girl! Very nice!вҖқ said MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. вҖңArranging meetings with lovers in my house! ItвҖҷs no use pretending: you listen when I speak to you!вҖқ And MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna touched her arm. вҖңListen when I speak! YouвҖҷve disgraced yourself like the lowest of hussies. IвҖҷd treat you differently, but IвҖҷm sorry for your father, so I will conceal it.вҖқ NatГЎsha did not change her position, but her whole body heaved with noiseless, convulsive sobs which choked her. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna glanced round at SГіnya and seated herself on the sofa beside NatГЎsha. вҖңItвҖҷs lucky for him that he escaped me; but IвҖҷll find him!вҖқ she said in her rough voice. вҖңDo you hear what I am saying or not?вҖқ she added. She put her large hand under NatГЎshaвҖҷs face and turned it toward her. Both MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna and SГіnya were amazed when they saw how NatГЎsha looked. Her eyes were dry and glistening, her lips compressed, her cheeks sunken. вҖңLet me be!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What is it to me?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I shall die!вҖқ she muttered, wrenching herself from MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs hands with a vicious effort and sinking down again into her former position. вҖңNatГЎlya!вҖқ said MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. вҖңI wish for your good. Lie still, stay like that then, I wonвҖҷt touch you. But listen. I wonвҖҷt tell you how guilty you are. You know that yourself. But when your father comes back tomorrow what am I to tell him? Eh?вҖқ Again NatГЎshaвҖҷs body shook with sobs. вҖңSuppose he finds out, and your brother, and your betrothed?вҖқ вҖңI have no betrothed: I have refused him!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha. вҖңThatвҖҷs all the same,вҖқ continued MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. вҖңIf they hear of this, will they let it pass? He, your father, I know himвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ if he challenges him to a duel will that be all right? Eh?вҖқ вҖңOh, let me be! Why have you interfered at all? Why? Why? Who asked you to?вҖқ shouted NatГЎsha, raising herself on the sofa and looking malignantly at MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. вҖңBut what did you want?вҖқ cried MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, growing angry again. вҖңWere you kept under lock and key? Who hindered his coming to the house? Why carry you off as if you were some gypsy singing girl?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, if he had carried you offвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ do you think they wouldnвҖҷt have found him? Your father, or brother, or your betrothed? And heвҖҷs a scoundrel, a wretchвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs a fact!вҖқ вҖңHe is better than any of you!вҖқ exclaimed NatГЎsha getting up. вҖңIf you hadnвҖҷt interferedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Oh, my God! What is it all? What is it? SГіnya, why?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Go away!вҖқ And she burst into sobs with the despairing vehemence with which people bewail disasters they feel they have themselves occasioned. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna was to speak again but NatГЎsha cried out: вҖңGo away! Go away! You all hate and despise me!вҖқ and she threw herself back on the sofa. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna went on admonishing her for some time, enjoining on her that it must all be kept from her father and assuring her that nobody would know anything about it if only NatГЎsha herself would undertake to forget it all and not let anyone see that something had happened. NatГЎsha did not reply, nor did she sob any longer, but she grew cold and had a shivering fit. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna put a pillow under her head, covered her with two quilts, and herself brought her some lime-flower water, but NatГЎsha did not respond to her. вҖңWell, let her sleep,вҖқ said MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna as she went out of the room supposing NatГЎsha to be asleep. But NatГЎsha was not asleep; with pale face and fixed wide-open eyes she looked straight before her. All that night she did not sleep or weep and did not speak to SГіnya who got up and went to her several times. Next day Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich returned from his estate near Moscow in time for lunch as he had promised. He was in very good spirits; the affair with the purchaser was going on satisfactorily, and there was nothing to keep him any longer in Moscow, away from the countess whom he missed. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna met him and told him that NatГЎsha had been very unwell the day before and that they had sent for the doctor, but that she was better now. NatГЎsha had not left her room that morning. With compressed and parched lips and dry fixed eyes, she sat at the window, uneasily watching the people who drove past and hurriedly glancing round at anyone who entered the room. She was evidently expecting news of him and that he would come or would write to her. When the count came to see her she turned anxiously round at the sound of a manвҖҷs footstep, and then her face resumed its cold and malevolent expression. She did not even get up to greet him. вҖңWhat is the matter with you, my angel? Are you ill?вҖқ asked the count. After a momentвҖҷs silence NatГЎsha answered: вҖңYes, ill.вҖқ In reply to the countвҖҷs anxious inquiries as to why she was so dejected and whether anything had happened to her betrothed, she assured him that nothing had happened and asked him not to worry. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna confirmed NatГЎshaвҖҷs assurances that nothing had happened. From the pretense of illness, from his daughterвҖҷs distress, and by the embarrassed faces of SГіnya and MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, the count saw clearly that something had gone wrong during his absence, but it was so terrible for him to think that anything disgraceful had happened to his beloved daughter, and he so prized his own cheerful tranquillity, that he avoided inquiries and tried to assure himself that nothing particularly had happened; and he was only dissatisfied that her indisposition delayed their return to the country. XIX XIX From the day his wife arrived in Moscow Pierre had been intending to go away somewhere, so as not to be near her. Soon after the RostГіvs came to Moscow the effect NatГЎsha had on him made him hasten to carry out his intention. He went to Tver to see Osip AlexГ©evichвҖҷs widow, who had long since promised to hand over to him some papers of her deceased husbandвҖҷs. When he returned to Moscow Pierre was handed a letter from MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna asking him to come and see her on a matter of great importance relating to AndrГ©y BolkГіnski and his betrothed. Pierre had been avoiding NatГЎsha because it seemed to him that his feeling for her was stronger than a married manвҖҷs should be for his friendвҖҷs fiancГ©e. Yet some fate constantly threw them together. вҖңWhat can have happened? And what can they want with me?вҖқ thought he as he dressed to go to MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs. вҖңIf only Prince AndrГ©y would hurry up and come and marry her!вҖқ thought he on his way to the house. On the TverskГіy Boulevard a familiar voice called to him. вҖңPierre! Been back long?вҖқ someone shouted. Pierre raised his head. In a sleigh drawn by two gray trotting-horses that were bespattering the dashboard with snow, Anatole and his constant companion MakГЎrin dashed past. Anatole was sitting upright in the classic pose of military dandies, the lower part of his face hidden by his beaver collar and his head slightly bent. His face was fresh and rosy, his white-plumed hat, tilted to one side, disclosed his curled and pomaded hair besprinkled with powdery snow. вҖңYes, indeed, thatвҖҷs a true sage,вҖқ thought Pierre. вҖңHe sees nothing beyond the pleasure of the moment, nothing troubles him and so he is always cheerful, satisfied, and serene. What wouldnвҖҷt I give to be like him!вҖқ he thought enviously. In MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs anteroom the footman who helped him off with his fur coat said that the mistress asked him to come to her bedroom. When he opened the ballroom door Pierre saw NatГЎsha sitting at the window, with a thin, pale, and spiteful face. She glanced round at him, frowned, and left the room with an expression of cold dignity. вҖңWhat has happened?вҖқ asked Pierre, entering MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs room. вҖңFine doings!вҖқ answered DmГӯtrievna. вҖңFor fifty-eight years have I lived in this world and never known anything so disgraceful!вҖқ And having put him on his honor not to repeat anything she told him, MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna informed him that NatГЎsha had refused Prince AndrГ©y without her parentsвҖҷ knowledge and that the cause of this was Anatole KurГЎgin into whose society PierreвҖҷs wife had thrown her and with whom NatГЎsha had tried to elope during her fatherвҖҷs absence, in order to be married secretly. Pierre raised his shoulders and listened open-mouthed to what was told him, scarcely able to believe his own ears. That Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs deeply loved affianced wifeвҒ вҖ”the same NatГЎsha RostГіva who used to be so charmingвҒ вҖ”should give up BolkГіnski for that fool Anatole who was already secretly married (as Pierre knew), and should be so in love with him as to agree to run away with him, was something Pierre could not conceive and could not imagine. He could not reconcile the charming impression he had of NatГЎsha, whom he had known from a child, with this new conception of her baseness, folly, and cruelty. He thought of his wife. вҖңThey are all alike!вҖқ he said to himself, reflecting that he was not the only man unfortunate enough to be tied to a bad woman. But still he pitied Prince AndrГ©y to the point of tears and sympathized with his wounded pride, and the more he pitied his friend the more did he think with contempt and even with disgust of that NatГЎsha who had just passed him in the ballroom with such a look of cold dignity. He did not know that NatГЎshaвҖҷs soul was overflowing with despair, shame, and humiliation, and that it was not her fault that her face happened to assume an expression of calm dignity and severity. вҖңBut how get married?вҖқ said Pierre, in answer to MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. вҖңHe could not marryвҒ вҖ”he is married!вҖқ вҖңThings get worse from hour to hour!вҖқ ejaculated MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. вҖңA nice youth! What a scoundrel! And sheвҖҷs expecting himвҒ вҖ”expecting him since yesterday. She must be told! Then at least she wonвҖҷt go on expecting him.вҖқ After hearing the details of AnatoleвҖҷs marriage from Pierre, and giving vent to her anger against Anatole in words of abuse, MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna told Pierre why she had sent for him. She was afraid that the count or BolkГіnski, who might arrive at any moment, if they knew of this affair (which she hoped to hide from them) might challenge Anatole to a duel, and she therefore asked Pierre to tell his brother-in-law in her name to leave Moscow and not dare to let her set eyes on him again. PierreвҒ вҖ”only now realizing the danger to the old count, NikolГЎy, and Prince AndrГ©yвҒ вҖ”promised to do as she wished. Having briefly and exactly explained her wishes to him, she let him go to the drawing room. вҖңMind, the count knows nothing. Behave as if you know nothing either,вҖқ she said. вҖңAnd I will go and tell her it is no use expecting him! And stay to dinner if you care to!вҖқ she called after Pierre. Pierre met the old count, who seemed nervous and upset. That morning NatГЎsha had told him that she had rejected BolkГіnski. вҖңTroubles, troubles, my dear fellow!вҖқ he said to Pierre. вҖңWhat troubles one has with these girls without their mother! I do so regret having come here.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I will be frank with you. Have you heard she has broken off her engagement without consulting anybody? ItвҖҷs true this engagement never was much to my liking. Of course he is an excellent man, but still, with his fatherвҖҷs disapproval they wouldnвҖҷt have been happy, and NatГЎsha wonвҖҷt lack suitors. Still, it has been going on so long, and to take such a step without fatherвҖҷs or motherвҖҷs consent! And now sheвҖҷs ill, and God knows what! ItвҖҷs hard, Count, hard to manage daughters in their motherвҖҷs absence.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre saw that the count was much upset and tried to change the subject, but the count returned to his troubles. SГіnya entered the room with an agitated face. вҖңNatГЎsha is not quite well; sheвҖҷs in her room and would like to see you. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna is with her and she too asks you to come.вҖқ вҖңYes, you are a great friend of BolkГіnskiвҖҷs, no doubt she wants to send him a message,вҖқ said the count. вҖңOh dear! Oh dear! How happy it all was!вҖқ And clutching the spare gray locks on his temples the count left the room. When MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna told NatГЎsha that Anatole was married, NatГЎsha did not wish to believe it and insisted on having it confirmed by Pierre himself. SГіnya told Pierre this as she led him along the corridor to NatГЎshaвҖҷs room. NatГЎsha, pale and stern, was sitting beside MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, and her eyes, glittering feverishly, met Pierre with a questioning look the moment he entered. She did not smile or nod, but only gazed fixedly at him, and her look asked only one thing: was he a friend, or like the others an enemy in regard to Anatole?
