As for Pierre, he evidently did not exist for her. вҖңHe knows all about it,вҖқ said MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna pointing to Pierre and addressing NatГЎsha. вҖңLet him tell you whether I have told the truth.вҖқ NatГЎsha looked from one to the other as a hunted and wounded animal looks at the approaching dogs and sportsmen. вҖңNatГЎlya IlynГӯchna,вҖқ Pierre began, dropping his eyes with a feeling of pity for her and loathing for the thing he had to do, вҖңwhether it is true or not should make no difference to you, becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңThen it is not true that heвҖҷs married!вҖқ вҖңYes, it is true.вҖқ вҖңHas he been married long?вҖқ she asked. вҖңOn your honor?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre gave his word of honor. вҖңIs he still here?вҖқ she asked, quickly. вҖңYes, I have just seen him.вҖқ She was evidently unable to speak and made a sign with her hands that they should leave her alone. II II At the beginning of winter Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich BolkГіnski and his daughter moved to Moscow. At that time enthusiasm for the Emperor AlexanderвҖҷs regime had weakened and a patriotic and anti-French tendency prevailed there, and this, together with his past and his intellect and his originality, at once made Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich an object of particular respect to the Moscovites and the center of the Moscow opposition to the government. The prince had aged very much that year. He showed marked signs of senility by a tendency to fall asleep, forgetfulness of quite recent events, remembrance of remote ones, and the childish vanity with which he accepted the role of head of the Moscow opposition. In spite of this the old man inspired in all his visitors alike a feeling of respectful venerationвҒ вҖ”especially of an evening when he came in to tea in his old-fashioned coat and powdered wig and, aroused by anyone, told his abrupt stories of the past, or uttered yet more abrupt and scathing criticisms of the present. For them all, that old-fashioned house with its gigantic mirrors, pre-Revolution furniture, powdered footmen, and the stern shrewd old man (himself a relic of the past century) with his gentle daughter and the pretty Frenchwoman who were reverently devoted to him presented a majestic and agreeable spectacle. But the visitors did not reflect that besides the couple of hours during which they saw their host, there were also twenty-two hours in the day during which the private and intimate life of the house continued. Latterly that private life had become very trying for Princess MГЎrya. There in Moscow she was deprived of her greatest pleasuresвҒ вҖ”talks with the pilgrims and the solitude which refreshed her at Bald HillsвҒ вҖ”and she had none of the advantages and pleasures of city life. She did not go out into society; everyone knew that her father would not let her go anywhere without him, and his failing health prevented his going out himself, so that she was not invited to dinners and evening parties. She had quite abandoned the hope of getting married. She saw the coldness and malevolence with which the old prince received and dismissed the young men, possible suitors, who sometimes appeared at their house. She had no friends: during this visit to Moscow she had been disappointed in the two who had been nearest to her. Mademoiselle Bourienne, with whom she had never been able to be quite frank, had now become unpleasant to her, and for various reasons Princess MГЎrya avoided her. Julie, with whom she had corresponded for the last five years, was in Moscow, but proved to be quite alien to her when they met. Just then Julie, who by the death of her brothers had become one of the richest heiresses in Moscow, was in the full whirl of society pleasures. She was surrounded by young men who, she fancied, had suddenly learned to appreciate her worth. Julie was at that stage in the life of a society woman when she feels that her last chance of marrying has come and that her fate must be decided now or never. On Thursdays Princess MГЎrya remembered with a mournful smile that she now had no one to write to, since JulieвҒ вҖ”whose presence gave her no pleasureвҒ вҖ”was here and they met every week. Like the old Г©migrГ© who declined to marry the lady with whom he had spent his evenings for years, she regretted JulieвҖҷs presence and having no one to write to. In Moscow Princess MГЎrya had no one to talk to, no one to whom to confide her sorrow, and much sorrow fell to her lot just then. The time for Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs return and marriage was approaching, but his request to her to prepare his father for it had not been carried out; in fact, it seemed as if matters were quite hopeless, for at every mention of the young Countess RostГіva the old prince (who apart from that was usually in a bad temper) lost control of himself. Another lately added sorrow arose from the lessons she gave her six year-old nephew. To her consternation she detected in herself in relation to NikolГәshka some symptoms of her fatherвҖҷs irritability. However often she told herself that she must not get irritable when teaching her nephew, almost every time that, pointer in hand, she sat down to show him the French alphabet, she so longed to pour her own knowledge quickly and easily into the childвҒ вҖ”who was already afraid that Auntie might at any moment get angryвҒ вҖ”that at his slightest inattention she trembled, became flustered and heated, raised her voice, and sometimes pulled him by the arm and put him in the corner. Having put him in the corner she would herself begin to cry over her cruel, evil nature, and NikolГәshka, following her example, would sob, and without permission would leave his corner, come to her, pull her wet hands from her face, and comfort her. But what distressed the princess most of all was her fatherвҖҷs irritability, which was always directed against her and had of late amounted to cruelty. Had he forced her to prostrate herself to the ground all night, had he beaten her or made her fetch wood or water, it would never have entered her mind to think her position hard; but this loving despotвҒ вҖ”the more cruel because he loved her and for that reason tormented himself and herвҒ вҖ”knew how not merely to hurt and humiliate her deliberately, but to show her that she was always to blame for everything. Of late he had exhibited a new trait that tormented Princess MГЎrya more than anything else; this was his ever-increasing intimacy with Mademoiselle Bourienne. The idea that at the first moment of receiving the news of his sonвҖҷs intentions had occurred to him in jestвҒ вҖ”that if AndrГ©y got married he himself would marry BourienneвҒ вҖ”had evidently pleased him, and latterly he had persistently, and as it seemed to Princess MГЎrya merely to offend her, shown special endearments to the companion and expressed his dissatisfaction with his daughter by demonstrations of love of Bourienne. One day in Moscow in Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs presence (she thought her father did it purposely when she was there) the old prince kissed Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs hand and, drawing her to him, embraced her affectionately. Princess MГЎrya flushed and ran out of the room. A few minutes later Mademoiselle Bourienne came into Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs room smiling and making cheerful remarks in her agreeable voice. Princess MГЎrya hastily wiped away her tears, went resolutely up to Mademoiselle Bourienne, and evidently unconscious of what she was doing began shouting in angry haste at the Frenchwoman, her voice breaking: вҖңItвҖҷs horrible, vile, inhuman, to take advantage of the weaknessвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ She did not finish. вҖңLeave my room,вҖқ she exclaimed, and burst into sobs. Next day the prince did not say a word to his daughter, but she noticed that at dinner he gave orders that Mademoiselle Bourienne should be served first. After dinner, when the footman handed coffee and from habit began with the princess, the prince suddenly grew furious, threw his stick at Philip, and instantly gave instructions to have him conscripted for the army. вҖңHe doesnвҖҷt obeyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I said it twiceвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and he doesnвҖҷt obey! She is the first person in this house; sheвҖҷs my best friend,вҖқ cried the prince. вҖңAnd if you allow yourself,вҖқ he screamed in a fury, addressing Princess MГЎrya for the first time, вҖңto forget yourself again before her as you dared to do yesterday, I will show you who is master in this house. Go! DonвҖҷt let me set eyes on you; beg her pardon!вҖқ Princess MГЎrya asked AmГЎlya EvgГ©nievnaвҖҷs pardon, and also her fatherвҖҷs pardon for herself and for Philip the footman, who had begged for her intervention. At such moments something like a pride of sacrifice gathered in her soul. And suddenly that father whom she had judged would look for his spectacles in her presence, fumbling near them and not seeing them, or would forget something that had just occurred, or take a false step with his failing legs and turn to see if anyone had noticed his feebleness, or, worst of all, at dinner when there were no visitors to excite him would suddenly fall asleep, letting his napkin drop and his shaking head sink over his plate. вҖңHe is old and feeble, and I dare to condemn him!вҖқ she thought at such moments, with a feeling of revulsion against herself. XX XX Pierre did not stay for dinner, but left the room and went away at once. He drove through the town seeking Anatole KurГЎgin, at the thought of whom now the blood rushed to his heart and he felt a difficulty in breathing. He was not at the ice hills, nor at the gypsiesвҖҷ, nor at KomonenoвҖҷs. Pierre drove to the Club. In the Club all was going on as usual. The members who were assembling for dinner were sitting about in groups; they greeted Pierre and spoke of the town news. The footman having greeted him, knowing his habits and his acquaintances, told him there was a place left for him in the small dining room and that Prince MikhГЎil ZakhГЎrych was in the library, but PГЎvel TimofГ©evich had not yet arrived. One of PierreвҖҷs acquaintances, while they were talking about the weather, asked if he had heard of KurГЎginвҖҷs abduction of RostГіva which was talked of in the town, and was it true? Pierre laughed and said it was nonsense for he had just come from the RostГіvsвҖҷ. He asked everyone about Anatole. One man told him he had not come yet, and another that he was coming to dinner. Pierre felt it strange to see this calm, indifferent crowd of people unaware of what was going on in his soul. He paced through the ballroom, waited till everyone had come, and as Anatole had not turned up did not stay for dinner but drove home. Anatole, for whom Pierre was looking, dined that day with DГіlokhov, consulting him as to how to remedy this unfortunate affair. It seemed to him essential to see NatГЎsha. In the evening he drove to his sisterвҖҷs to discuss with her how to arrange a meeting. When Pierre returned home after vainly hunting all over Moscow, his valet informed him that Prince Anatole was with the countess. The countessвҖҷ drawing room was full of guests. Pierre without greeting his wife whom he had not seen since his returnвҒ вҖ”at that moment she was more repulsive to him than everвҒ вҖ”entered the drawing room and seeing Anatole went up to him. вҖңAh, Pierre,вҖқ said the countess going up to her husband. вҖңYou donвҖҷt know what a plight our AnatoleвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ She stopped, seeing in the forward thrust of her husbandвҖҷs head, in his glowing eyes and his resolute gait, the terrible indications of that rage and strength which she knew and had herself experienced after his duel with DГіlokhov. вҖңWhere you are, there is vice and evil!вҖқ said Pierre to his wife. вҖңAnatole, come with me! I must speak to you,вҖқ he added in French. Anatole glanced round at his sister and rose submissively, ready to follow Pierre. Pierre, taking him by the arm, pulled him toward himself and was leading him from the room. вҖңIf you allow yourself in my drawing roomвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ whispered ElГЁn, but Pierre did not reply and went out of the room. Anatole followed him with his usual jaunty step but his face betrayed anxiety. Having entered his study Pierre closed the door and addressed Anatole without looking at him. вҖңYou promised Countess RostГіva to marry her and were about to elope with her, is that so?вҖқ вҖңMon cher,вҖқ answered Anatole (their whole conversation was in French), вҖңI donвҖҷt consider myself bound to answer questions put to me in that tone.вҖқ PierreвҖҷs face, already pale, became distorted by fury. He seized Anatole by the collar of his uniform with his big hand and shook him from side to side till AnatoleвҖҷs face showed a sufficient degree of terror. вҖңWhen I tell you that I must talk to you!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ repeated Pierre. вҖңCome now, this is stupid. What?вҖқ said Anatole, fingering a button of his collar that had been wrenched loose with a bit of the cloth. вҖңYouвҖҷre a scoundrel and a blackguard, and I donвҖҷt know what deprives me from the pleasure of smashing your head with this!вҖқ said Pierre, expressing himself so artificially because he was talking French. He took a heavy paperweight and lifted it threateningly, but at once put it back in its place. вҖңDid you promise to marry her?вҖқ вҖңIвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I didnвҖҷt think of it. I never promised, becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre interrupted him. вҖңHave you any letters of hers? Any letters?вҖқ he said, moving toward Anatole. Anatole glanced at him and immediately thrust his hand into his pocket and drew out his pocketbook. Pierre took the letter Anatole handed him and, pushing aside a table that stood in his way, threw himself on the sofa. вҖңI shanвҖҷt be violent, donвҖҷt be afraid!вҖқ said Pierre in answer to a frightened gesture of AnatoleвҖҷs. вҖңFirst, the letters,вҖқ said he, as if repeating a lesson to himself. вҖңSecondly,вҖқ he continued after a short pause, again rising and again pacing the room, вҖңtomorrow you must get out of Moscow.вҖқ вҖңBut how can I?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңThirdly,вҖқ Pierre continued without listening to him, вҖңyou must never breathe a word of what has passed between you and Countess RostГіva. I know I canвҖҷt prevent your doing so, but if you have a spark of conscienceвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre paced the room several times in silence. Anatole sat at a table frowning and biting his lips. вҖңAfter all, you must understand that besides your pleasure there is such a thing as other peopleвҖҷs happiness and peace, and that you are ruining a whole life for the sake of amusing yourself! Amuse yourself with women like my wifeвҒ вҖ”with them you are within your rights, for they know what you want of them. They are armed against you by the same experience of debauchery; but to promise a maid to marry herвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ to deceive, to kidnap.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ DonвҖҷt you understand that it is as mean as beating an old man or a child?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre paused and looked at Anatole no longer with an angry but with a questioning look. вҖңI donвҖҷt know about that, eh?вҖқ said Anatole, growing more confident as Pierre mastered his wrath. вҖңI donвҖҷt know that and donвҖҷt want to,вҖқ he said, not looking at Pierre and with a slight tremor of his lower jaw, вҖңbut you have used such words to meвҒ вҖ”вҖҳmeanвҖҷ and so onвҒ вҖ”which as a man of honor I canвҖҷt allow anyone to use.вҖқ Pierre glanced at him with amazement, unable to understand what he wanted. вҖңThough it was tГӘte-Г -tГӘte,вҖқ Anatole continued, вҖңstill I canвҖҷtвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңIs it satisfaction you want?вҖқ said Pierre ironically. вҖңYou could at least take back your words. What? If you want me to do as you wish, eh?вҖқ вҖңI take them back, I take them back!вҖқ said Pierre, вҖңand I ask you to forgive me.вҖқ Pierre involuntarily glanced at the loose button. вҖңAnd if you require money for your journeyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Anatole smiled. The expression of that base and cringing smile, which Pierre knew so well in his wife, revolted him. вҖңOh, vile and heartless brood!вҖқ he exclaimed, and left the room. Next day Anatole left for Petersburg. XXI XXI Pierre drove to MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs to tell her of the fulfillment of her wish that KurГЎgin should be banished from Moscow. The whole house was in a state of alarm and commotion. NatГЎsha was very ill, having, as MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna told him in secret, poisoned herself the night after she had been told that Anatole was married, with some arsenic she had stealthily procured. After swallowing a little she had been so frightened that she woke SГіnya and told her what she had done. The necessary antidotes had been administered in time and she was now out of danger, though still so weak that it was out of the question to move her to the country, and so the countess had been sent for. Pierre saw the distracted count, and SГіnya, who had a tear-stained face, but he could not see NatГЎsha. Pierre dined at the club that day and heard on all sides gossip about the attempted abduction of RostГіva. He resolutely denied these rumors, assuring everyone that nothing had happened except that his brother-in-law had proposed to her and been refused. It seemed to Pierre that it was his duty to conceal the whole affair and reestablish NatГЎshaвҖҷs reputation. He was awaiting Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs return with dread and went every day to the old princeвҖҷs for news of him. Old Prince BolkГіnski heard all the rumors current in the town from Mademoiselle Bourienne and had read the note to Princess MГЎrya in which NatГЎsha had broken off her engagement. He seemed in better spirits than usual and awaited his son with great impatience. Some days after AnatoleвҖҷs departure Pierre received a note from Prince AndrГ©y, informing him of his arrival and asking him to come to see him. As soon as he reached Moscow, Prince AndrГ©y had received from his father NatГЎshaвҖҷs note to Princess MГЎrya breaking off her engagement (Mademoiselle Bourienne had purloined it from Princess MГЎrya and given it to the old prince), and he heard from him the story of NatГЎshaвҖҷs elopement, with additions. Prince AndrГ©y had arrived in the evening and Pierre came to see him next morning. Pierre expected to find Prince AndrГ©y in almost the same state as NatГЎsha and was therefore surprised on entering the drawing room to hear him in the study talking in a loud animated voice about some intrigue going on in Petersburg. The old princeвҖҷs voice and another now and then interrupted him. Princess MГЎrya came out to meet Pierre. She sighed, looking toward the door of the room where Prince AndrГ©y was, evidently intending to express her sympathy with his sorrow, but Pierre saw by her face that she was glad both at what had happened and at the way her brother had taken the news of NatГЎshaвҖҷs faithlessness. вҖңHe says he expected it,вҖқ she remarked. вҖңI know his pride will not let him express his feelings, but still he has taken it better, far better, than I expected. Evidently it had to be.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңBut is it possible that all is really ended?вҖқ asked Pierre. Princess MГЎrya looked at him with astonishment. She did not understand how he could ask such a question. Pierre went into the study. Prince AndrГ©y, greatly changed and plainly in better health, but with a fresh horizontal wrinkle between his brows, stood in civilian dress facing his father and Prince MeshchГ©rski, warmly disputing and vigorously gesticulating. The conversation was about SperГЎnskiвҒ вҖ”the news of whose sudden exile and alleged treachery had just reached Moscow. вҖңNow he is censured and accused by all who were enthusiastic about him a month ago,вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y was saying, вҖңand by those who were unable to understand his aims. To judge a man who is in disfavor and to throw on him all the blame of other menвҖҷs mistakes is very easy, but I maintain that if anything good has been accomplished in this reign it was done by him, by him alone.вҖқ He paused at the sight of Pierre. His face quivered and immediately assumed a vindictive expression. вҖңPosterity will do him justice,вҖқ he concluded, and at once turned to Pierre. вҖңWell, how are you? Still getting stouter?вҖқ he said with animation, but the new wrinkle on his forehead deepened. вҖңYes, I am well,вҖқ he said in answer to PierreвҖҷs question, and smiled. To Pierre that smile said plainly: вҖңI am well, but my health is now of no use to anyone.вҖқ After a few words to Pierre about the awful roads from the Polish frontier, about people he had met in Switzerland who knew Pierre, and about M. Dessalles, whom he had brought from abroad to be his sonвҖҷs tutor, Prince AndrГ©y again joined warmly in the conversation about SperГЎnski which was still going on between the two old men. вҖңIf there were treason, or proofs of secret relations with Napoleon, they would have been made public,вҖқ he said with warmth and haste. вҖңI do not, and never did, like SperГЎnski personally, but I like justice!вҖқ Pierre now recognized in his friend a need with which he was only too familiar, to get excited and to have arguments about extraneous matters in order to stifle thoughts that were too oppressive and too intimate. When Prince MeshchГ©rski had left, Prince AndrГ©y took PierreвҖҷs arm and asked him into the room that had been assigned him. A bed had been made up there, and some open portmanteaus and trunks stood about. Prince AndrГ©y went to one and took out a small casket, from which he drew a packet wrapped in paper. He did it all silently and very quickly. He stood up and coughed. His face was gloomy and his lips compressed. вҖңForgive me for troubling you.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre saw that Prince AndrГ©y was going to speak of NatГЎsha, and his broad face expressed pity and sympathy. This expression irritated Prince AndrГ©y, and in a determined, ringing, and unpleasant tone he continued: вҖңI have received a refusal from Countess RostГіva and have heard reports of your brother-in-law having sought her hand, or something of that kind. Is that true?вҖқ вҖңBoth true and untrue,вҖқ Pierre began; but Prince AndrГ©y interrupted him. вҖңHere are her letters and her portrait,вҖқ said he. He took the packet from the table and handed it to Pierre. вҖңGive this to the countessвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ if you see her.вҖқ вҖңShe is very ill,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңThen she is here still?вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңAnd Prince KurГЎgin?вҖқ he added quickly. вҖңHe left long ago. She has been at deathвҖҷs door.вҖқ вҖңI much regret her illness,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y; and he smiled like his father, coldly, maliciously, and unpleasantly. вҖңSo Monsieur KurГЎgin has not honored Countess RostГіva with his hand?вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y, and he snorted several times. вҖңHe could not marry, for he was married already,вҖқ said Pierre. Prince AndrГ©y laughed disagreeably, again reminding one of his father. вҖңAnd where is your brother-in-law now, if I may ask?вҖқ he said. вҖңHe has gone to PetersвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But I donвҖҷt know,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңWell, it doesnвҖҷt matter,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңTell Countess RostГіva that she was and is perfectly free and that I wish her all that is good.вҖқ Pierre took the packet. Prince AndrГ©y, as if trying to remember whether he had something more to say, or waiting to see if Pierre would say anything, looked fixedly at him. вҖңI say, do you remember our discussion in Petersburg?вҖқ asked Pierre, вҖңaboutвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYes,вҖқ returned Prince AndrГ©y hastily. вҖңI said that a fallen woman should be forgiven, but I didnвҖҷt say I could forgive her. I canвҖҷt.вҖқ вҖңBut can this be comparedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ?вҖқ said Pierre. Prince AndrГ©y interrupted him and cried sharply: вҖңYes, ask her hand again, be magnanimous, and so on?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Yes, that would be very noble, but I am unable to follow in that gentlemanвҖҷs footsteps. If you wish to be my friend never speak to me of thatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ of all that! Well, goodbye. So youвҖҷll give her the packet?вҖқ Pierre left the room and went to the old prince and Princess MГЎrya. The old man seemed livelier than usual. Princess MГЎrya was the same as always, but beneath her sympathy for her brother, Pierre noticed her satisfaction that the engagement had been broken off. Looking at them Pierre realized what contempt and animosity they all felt for the RostГіvs, and that it was impossible in their presence even to mention the name of her who could give up Prince AndrГ©y for anyone else. At dinner the talk turned on the war, the approach of which was becoming evident. Prince AndrГ©y talked incessantly, arguing now with his father, now with the Swiss tutor Dessalles, and showing an unnatural animation, the cause of which Pierre so well understood. XXII XXII That same evening Pierre went to the RostГіvsвҖҷ to fulfill the commission entrusted to him. NatГЎsha was in bed, the count at the club, and Pierre, after giving the letters to SГіnya, went to MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna who was interested to know how Prince AndrГ©y had taken the news. Ten minutes later SГіnya came to MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna. вҖңNatГЎsha insists on seeing Count Pyotr KirГӯlovich,вҖқ said she. вҖңBut how? Are we to take him up to her? The room there has not been tidied up.вҖқ вҖңNo, she has dressed and gone into the drawing room,вҖқ said SГіnya. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna only shrugged her shoulders. вҖңWhen will her mother come? She has worried me to death! Now mind, donвҖҷt tell her everything!вҖқ said she to Pierre. вҖңOne hasnвҖҷt the heart to scold her, she is so much to be pitied, so much to be pitied.вҖқ NatГЎsha was standing in the middle of the drawing room, emaciated, with a pale set face, but not at all shamefaced as Pierre expected to find her. When he appeared at the door she grew flurried, evidently undecided whether to go to meet him or to wait till he came up. Pierre hastened to her. He thought she would give him her hand as usual; but she, stepping up to him, stopped, breathing heavily, her arms hanging lifelessly just in the pose she used to stand in when she went to the middle of the ballroom to sing, but with quite a different expression of face. вҖңPyotr KirГӯlych,вҖқ she began rapidly, вҖңPrince BolkГіnski was your friendвҒ вҖ”is your friend,вҖқ she corrected herself. (It seemed to her that everything that had once been must now be different.) вҖңHe told me once to apply to youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre sniffed as he looked at her, but did not speak. Till then he had reproached her in his heart and tried to despise her, but he now felt so sorry for her that there was no room in his soul for reproach. вҖңHe is here now: tell himвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ to forвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ forgive me!вҖқ She stopped and breathed still more quickly, but did not shed tears. вҖңYesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I will tell him,вҖқ answered Pierre; вҖңbutвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He did not know what to say. NatГЎsha was evidently dismayed at the thought of what he might think she had meant. вҖңNo, I know all is over,вҖқ she said hurriedly. вҖңNo, that can never be. IвҖҷm only tormented by the wrong I have done him. Tell him only that I beg him to forgive, forgive, forgive me for everything.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ She trembled all over and sat down on a chair. A sense of pity he had never before known overflowed PierreвҖҷs heart. вҖңI will tell him, I will tell him everything once more,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңButвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I should like to know one thing.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңKnow what?вҖқ NatГЎshaвҖҷs eyes asked. вҖңI should like to know, did you loveвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre did not know how to refer to Anatole and flushed at the thought of himвҒ вҖ”вҖңdid you love that bad man?вҖқ вҖңDonвҖҷt call him bad!вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңBut I donвҖҷt know, donвҖҷt know at all.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ She began to cry and a still greater sense of pity, tenderness, and love welled up in Pierre. He felt the tears trickle under his spectacles and hoped they would not be noticed. вҖңWe wonвҖҷt speak of it any more, my dear,вҖқ said Pierre, and his gentle, cordial tone suddenly seemed very strange to NatГЎsha. вҖңWe wonвҖҷt speak of it, my dearвҒ вҖ”IвҖҷll tell him everything; but one thing I beg of you, consider me your friend and if you want help, advice, or simply to open your heart to someoneвҒ вҖ”not now, but when your mind is clearerвҒ вҖ”think of me!вҖқ He took her hand and kissed it. вҖңI shall be happy if itвҖҷs in my powerвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre grew confused. вҖңDonвҖҷt speak to me like that. I am not worth it!вҖқ exclaimed NatГЎsha and turned to leave the room, but Pierre held her hand. He knew he had something more to say to her. But when he said it he was amazed at his own words. вҖңStop, stop! You have your whole life before you,вҖқ said he to her. вҖңBefore me? No! All is over for me,вҖқ she replied with shame and self-abasement. вҖңAll over?вҖқ he repeated. вҖңIf I were not myself, but the handsomest, cleverest, and best man in the world, and were free, I would this moment ask on my knees for your hand and your love!вҖқ For the first time for many days NatГЎsha wept tears of gratitude and tenderness, and glancing at Pierre she went out of the room. Pierre too when she had gone almost ran into the anteroom, restraining tears of tenderness and joy that choked him, and without finding the sleeves of his fur cloak threw it on and got into his sleigh. вҖңWhere to now, your excellency?вҖқ asked the coachman. вҖңWhere to?вҖқ Pierre asked himself. вҖңWhere can I go now? Surely not to the Club or to pay calls?вҖқ All men seemed so pitiful, so poor, in comparison with this feeling of tenderness and love he experienced: in comparison with that softened, grateful, last look she had given him through her tears. вҖңHome!вҖқ said Pierre, and despite twenty-two degrees of frost Fahrenheit he threw open the bearskin cloak from his broad chest and inhaled the air with joy. It was clear and frosty. Above the dirty, ill-lit streets, above the black roofs, stretched the dark starry sky. Only looking up at the sky did Pierre cease to feel how sordid and humiliating were all mundane things compared with the heights to which his soul had just been raised. At the entrance to the ArbГЎt Square an immense expanse of dark starry sky presented itself to his eyes. Almost in the center of it, above the PrechГӯstenka Boulevard, surrounded and sprinkled on all sides by stars but distinguished from them all by its nearness to the earth, its white light, and its long uplifted tail, shone the enormous and brilliant comet of 1812вҒ вҖ”the comet which was said to portend all kinds of woes and the end of the world. In Pierre, however, that comet with its long luminous tail aroused no feeling of fear. On the contrary he gazed joyfully, his eyes moist with tears, at this bright comet which, having traveled in its orbit with inconceivable velocity through immeasurable space, seemed suddenlyвҒ вҖ”like an arrow piercing the earthвҒ вҖ”to remain fixed in a chosen spot, vigorously holding its tail erect, shining and displaying its white light amid countless other scintillating stars. It seemed to Pierre that this comet fully responded to what was passing in his own softened and uplifted soul, now blossoming into a new life. III III In 1811 there was living in Moscow a French doctorвҒ вҖ”MГ©tivierвҒ вҖ”who had rapidly become the fashion. He was enormously tall, handsome, amiable as Frenchmen are, and was, as all Moscow said, an extraordinarily clever doctor. He was received in the best houses not merely as a doctor, but as an equal. Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich had always ridiculed medicine, but latterly on Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs advice had allowed this doctor to visit him and had grown accustomed to him. MГ©tivier came to see the prince about twice a week. On December 6вҒ вҖ”St.В NikolГЎyвҖҷs Day and the princeвҖҷs name dayвҒ вҖ”all Moscow came to the princeвҖҷs front door but he gave orders to admit no one and to invite to dinner only a small number, a list of whom he gave to Princess MГЎrya. MГ©tivier, who came in the morning with his felicitations, considered it proper in his quality of doctor de forcer la consigne,71 as he told Princess MГЎrya, and went in to see the prince. It happened that on that morning of his name day the prince was in one of his worst moods. He had been going about the house all the morning finding fault with everyone and pretending not to understand what was said to him and not to be understood himself. Princess MГЎrya well knew this mood of quiet absorbed querulousness, which generally culminated in a burst of rage, and she went about all that morning as though facing a cocked and loaded gun and awaited the inevitable explosion. Until the doctorвҖҷs arrival the morning had passed off safely. After admitting the doctor, Princess MГЎrya sat down with a book in the drawing room near the door through which she could hear all that passed in the study. At first she heard only MГ©tivierвҖҷs voice, then her fatherвҖҷs, then both voices began speaking at the same time, the door was flung open, and on the threshold appeared the handsome figure of the terrified MГ©tivier with his shock of black hair, and the prince in his dressing gown and fez, his face distorted with fury and the pupils of his eyes rolled downwards. вҖңYou donвҖҷt understand?вҖқ shouted the prince, вҖңbut I do! French spy, slave of Bonaparte, spy, get out of my house! Be off, I tell youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and he slammed the door. MГ©tivier, shrugging his shoulders, went up to Mademoiselle Bourienne who at the sound of shouting had run in from an adjoining room. вҖңThe prince is not very well: bile and rush of blood to the head. Keep calm, I will call again tomorrow,вҖқ said MГ©tivier; and putting his fingers to his lips he hastened away. Through the study door came the sound of slippered feet and the cry: вҖңSpies, traitors, traitors everywhere! Not a momentвҖҷs peace in my own house!вҖқ After MГ©tivierвҖҷs departure the old prince called his daughter in, and the whole weight of his wrath fell on her. She was to blame that a spy had been admitted. Had he not told her, yes, told her to make a list, and not to admit anyone who was not on that list? Then why was that scoundrel admitted? She was the cause of it all. With her, he said, he could not have a momentвҖҷs peace and could not die quietly. вҖңNo, maвҖҷam! We must part, we must part! Understand that, understand it! I cannot endure any more,вҖқ he said, and left the room. Then, as if afraid she might find some means of consolation, he returned and trying to appear calm added: вҖңAnd donвҖҷt imagine I have said this in a moment of anger. I am calm. I have thought it over, and it will be carried outвҒ вҖ”we must part; so find some place for yourself.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ But he could not restrain himself and with the virulence of which only one who loves is capable, evidently suffering himself, he shook his fists at her and screamed: вҖңIf only some fool would marry her!вҖқ Then he slammed the door, sent for Mademoiselle Bourienne, and subsided into his study. At two oвҖҷclock the six chosen guests assembled for dinner. These guestsвҒ вҖ”the famous Count RostopchГӯn, Prince LopukhГӯn with his nephew, General ChatrГіv an old war comrade of the princeвҖҷs, and of the younger generation Pierre and BorГӯs DrubetskГіyвҒ вҖ”awaited the prince in the drawing room. BorГӯs, who had come to Moscow on leave a few days before, had been anxious to be presented to Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich, and had contrived to ingratiate himself so well that the old prince in his case made an exception to the rule of not receiving bachelors in his house. The princeвҖҷs house did not belong to what is known as fashionable society, but his little circleвҒ вҖ”though not much talked about in townвҒ вҖ”was one it was more flattering to be received in than any other. BorГӯs had realized this the week before when the commander in chief in his presence invited RostopchГӯn to dinner on St.В NikolГЎyвҖҷs Day, and RostopchГӯn had replied that he could not come: вҖңOn that day I always go to pay my devotions to the relics of Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich.вҖқ вҖңOh, yes, yes!вҖқ replied the commander in chief. вҖңHow is he?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The small group that assembled before dinner in the lofty old-fashioned drawing room with its old furniture resembled the solemn gathering of a court of justice. All were silent or talked in low tones. Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich came in serious and taciturn. Princess MГЎrya seemed even quieter and more diffident than usual. The guests were reluctant to address her, feeling that she was in no mood for their conversation. Count RostopchГӯn alone kept the conversation going, now relating the latest town news, and now the latest political gossip. LopukhГӯn and the old general occasionally took part in the conversation. Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich listened as a presiding judge receives a report, only now and then, silently or by a brief word, showing that he took heed of what was being reported to him. The tone of the conversation was such as indicated that no one approved of what was being done in the political world. Incidents were related evidently confirming the opinion that everything was going from bad to worse, but whether telling a story or giving an opinion the speaker always stopped, or was stopped, at the point beyond which his criticism might touch the sovereign himself. At dinner the talk turned on the latest political news: NapoleonвҖҷs seizure of the Duke of OldenburgвҖҷs territory, and the Russian Note, hostile to Napoleon, which had been sent to all the European courts. вҖңBonaparte treats Europe as a pirate does a captured vessel,вҖқ said Count RostopchГӯn, repeating a phrase he had uttered several times before. вҖңOne only wonders at the long-suffering or blindness of the crowned heads. Now the PopeвҖҷs turn has come and Bonaparte doesnвҖҷt scruple to depose the head of the Catholic ChurchвҒ вҖ”yet all keep silent! Our sovereign alone has protested against the seizure of the Duke of OldenburgвҖҷs territory, and evenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Count RostopchГӯn paused, feeling that he had reached the limit beyond which censure was impossible. вҖңOther territories have been offered in exchange for the Duchy of Oldenburg,вҖқ said Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich. вҖңHe shifts the Dukes about as I might move my serfs from Bald Hills to BoguchГЎrovo or my RyazГЎn estates.вҖқ вҖңThe Duke of Oldenburg bears his misfortunes with admirable strength of character and resignation,вҖқ remarked BorГӯs, joining in respectfully. He said this because on his journey from Petersburg he had had the honor of being presented to the Duke. Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich glanced at the young man as if about to say something in reply, but changed his mind, evidently considering him too young. вҖңI have read our protests about the Oldenburg affair and was surprised how badly the Note was worded,вҖқ remarked Count RostopchГӯn in the casual tone of a man dealing with a subject quite familiar to him. Pierre looked at RostopchГӯn with naive astonishment, not understanding why he should be disturbed by the bad composition of the Note. вҖңDoes it matter, Count, how the Note is worded,вҖқ he asked, вҖңso long as its substance is forcible?вҖқ вҖңMy dear fellow, with our five hundred thousand troops it should be easy to have a good style,вҖқ returned Count RostopchГӯn. Pierre now understood the countвҖҷs dissatisfaction with the wording of the Note. вҖңOne would have thought quill drivers enough had sprung up,вҖқ remarked the old prince. вҖңThere in Petersburg they are always writingвҒ вҖ”not notes only but even new laws. My AndrГәsha there has written a whole volume of laws for Russia. Nowadays they are always writing!вҖқ and he laughed unnaturally. There was a momentary pause in the conversation; the old general cleared his throat to draw attention. вҖңDid you hear of the last event at the review in Petersburg? The figure cut by the new French ambassador.вҖқ вҖңEh? Yes, I heard something: he said something awkward in His MajestyвҖҷs presence.вҖқ вҖңHis Majesty drew attention to the Grenadier division and to the march past,вҖқ continued the general, вҖңand it seems the ambassador took no notice and allowed himself to reply that: вҖҳWe in France pay no attention to such trifles!вҖҷ The Emperor did not condescend to reply. At the next review, they say, the Emperor did not once deign to address him.вҖқ All were silent. On this fact relating to the Emperor personally, it was impossible to pass any judgment. вҖңImpudent fellows!вҖқ said the prince. вҖңYou know MГ©tivier? I turned him out of my house this morning. He was here; they admitted him in spite of my request that they should let no one in,вҖқ he went on, glancing angrily at his daughter. And he narrated his whole conversation with the French doctor and the reasons that convinced him that MГ©tivier was a spy. Though these reasons were very insufficient and obscure, no one made any rejoinder. After the roast, champagne was served. The guests rose to congratulate the old prince. Princess MГЎrya, too, went round to him. He gave her a cold, angry look and offered her his wrinkled, clean-shaven cheek to kiss. The whole expression of his face told her that he had not forgotten the morningвҖҷs talk, that his decision remained in force, and only the presence of visitors hindered his speaking of it to her now. When they went into the drawing room where coffee was served, the old men sat together. Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich grew more animated and expressed his views on the impending war. He said that our wars with Bonaparte would be disastrous so long as we sought alliances with the Germans and thrust ourselves into European affairs, into which we had been drawn by the Peace of Tilsit. вҖңWe ought not to fight either for or against Austria. Our political interests are all in the East, and in regard to Bonaparte the only thing is to have an armed frontier and a firm policy, and he will never dare to cross the Russian frontier, as was the case in 1807!вҖқ вҖңHow can we fight the French, Prince?вҖқ said Count RostopchГӯn. вҖңCan we arm ourselves against our teachers and divinities? Look at our youths, look at our ladies! The French are our Gods: Paris is our Kingdom of Heaven.вҖқ He began speaking louder, evidently to be heard by everyone. вҖңFrench dresses, French ideas, French feelings! There now, you turned MГ©tivier out by the scruff of his neck because he is a Frenchman and a scoundrel, but our ladies crawl after him on their knees. I went to a party last night, and there out of five ladies three were Roman Catholics and had the PopeвҖҷs indulgence for doing woolwork on Sundays. And they themselves sit there nearly naked, like the signboards at our Public Baths if I may say so. Ah, when one looks at our young people, Prince, one would like to take Peter the GreatвҖҷs old cudgel out of the museum and belabor them in the Russian way till all the nonsense jumps out of them.вҖқ All were silent. The old prince looked at RostopchГӯn with a smile and wagged his head approvingly. вҖңWell, goodbye, your excellency, keep well!вҖқ said RostopchГӯn, getting up with characteristic briskness and holding out his hand to the prince. вҖңGoodbye, my dear fellow.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ His words are music, I never tire of hearing him!вҖқ said the old prince, keeping hold of the hand and offering his cheek to be kissed. Following RostopchГӯnвҖҷs example the others also rose. IV IV Princess MГЎrya as she sat listening to the old menвҖҷs talk and faultfinding, understood nothing of what she heard; she only wondered whether the guests had all observed her fatherвҖҷs hostile attitude toward her. She did not even notice the special attentions and amiabilities shown her during dinner by BorГӯs DrubetskГіy, who was visiting them for the third time already. Princess MГЎrya turned with absentminded questioning look to Pierre, who hat in hand and with a smile on his face was the last of the guests to approach her after the old prince had gone out and they were left alone in the drawing room. вҖңMay I stay a little longer?вҖқ he said, letting his stout body sink into an armchair beside her. вҖңOh yes,вҖқ she answered. вҖңYou noticed nothing?вҖқ her look asked. Pierre was in an agreeable after-dinner mood. He looked straight before him and smiled quietly. вҖңHave you known that young man long, Princess?вҖқ he asked. вҖңWho?вҖқ вҖңDrubetskГіy.вҖқ вҖңNo, not long.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңDo you like him?вҖқ вҖңYes, he is an agreeable young man.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Why do you ask me that?вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya, still thinking of that morningвҖҷs conversation with her father. вҖңBecause I have noticed that when a young man comes on leave from Petersburg to Moscow it is usually with the object of marrying an heiress.вҖқ вҖңYou have observed that?вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңYes,вҖқ returned Pierre with a smile, вҖңand this young man now manages matters so that where there is a wealthy heiress there he is too. I can read him like a book. At present he is hesitating whom to lay siege toвҒ вҖ”you or Mademoiselle Julie KarГЎgina. He is very attentive to her.вҖқ вҖңHe visits them?вҖқ вҖңYes, very often. And do you know the new way of courting?вҖқ said Pierre with an amused smile, evidently in that cheerful mood of good humored raillery for which he so often reproached himself in his diary. вҖңNo,вҖқ replied Princess MГЎrya. вҖңTo please Moscow girls nowadays one has to be melancholy. He is very melancholy with Mademoiselle KarГЎgina,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңReally?вҖқ asked Princess MГЎrya, looking into PierreвҖҷs kindly face and still thinking of her own sorrow. вҖңIt would be a relief,вҖқ thought she, вҖңif I ventured to confide what I am feeling to someone. I should like to tell everything to Pierre. He is kind and generous. It would be a relief. He would give me advice.вҖқ вҖңWould you marry him?вҖқ вҖңOh, my God, Count, there are moments when I would marry anybody!вҖқ she cried suddenly to her own surprise and with tears in her voice. вҖңAh, how bitter it is to love someone near to you and to feel thatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she went on in a trembling voice, вҖңthat you can do nothing for him but grieve him, and to know that you cannot alter this. Then there is only one thing leftвҒ вҖ”to go away, but where could I go?вҖқ вҖңWhat is wrong? What is it, Princess?вҖқ But without finishing what she was saying, Princess MГЎrya burst into tears. вҖңI donвҖҷt know what is the matter with me today. DonвҖҷt take any noticeвҒ вҖ”forget what I have said!вҖқ PierreвҖҷs gaiety vanished completely. He anxiously questioned the princess, asked her to speak out fully and confide her grief to him; but she only repeated that she begged him to forget what she had said, that she did not remember what she had said, and that she had no trouble except the one he knew ofвҒ вҖ”that Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs marriage threatened to cause a rupture between father and son. вҖңHave you any news of the RostГіvs?вҖқ she asked, to change the subject. вҖңI was told they are coming soon. I am also expecting AndrГ© any day. I should like them to meet here.вҖқ вҖңAnd how does he now regard the matter?вҖқ asked Pierre, referring to the old prince. Princess MГЎrya shook her head. вҖңWhat is to be done? In a few months the year will be up. The thing is impossible. I only wish I could spare my brother the first moments. I wish they would come sooner. I hope to be friends with her. You have known them a long time,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңTell me honestly the whole truth: what sort of girl is she, and what do you think of her?вҒ вҖ”The real truth, because you know AndrГ©y is risking so much doing this against his fatherвҖҷs will that I should like to know.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ An undefined instinct told Pierre that these explanations, and repeated requests to be told the whole truth, expressed ill-will on the princessвҖҷ part toward her future sister-in-law and a wish that he should disapprove of AndrГ©yвҖҷs choice; but in reply he said what he felt rather than what he thought. вҖңI donвҖҷt know how to answer your question,вҖқ he said, blushing without knowing why. вҖңI really donвҖҷt know what sort of girl she is; I canвҖҷt analyze her at all. She is enchanting, but what makes her so I donвҖҷt know. That is all one can say about her.вҖқ Princess MГЎrya sighed, and the expression on her face said: вҖңYes, thatвҖҷs what I expected and feared.вҖқ вҖңIs she clever?вҖқ she asked. Pierre considered. вҖңI think not,вҖқ he said, вҖңand yetвҒ вҖ”yes. She does not deign to be clever.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Oh no, she is simply enchanting, and that is all.вҖқ Princess MГЎrya again shook her head disapprovingly. вҖңAh, I so long to like her! Tell her so if you see her before I do.вҖқ вҖңI hear they are expected very soon,вҖқ said Pierre. Princess MГЎrya told Pierre of her plan to become intimate with her future sister-in-law as soon as the RostГіvs arrived and to try to accustom the old prince to her. V V BorГӯs had not succeeded in making a wealthy match in Petersburg, so with the same object in view he came to Moscow. There he wavered between the two richest heiresses, Julie and Princess MГЎrya. Though Princess MГЎrya despite her plainness seemed to him more attractive than Julie, he, without knowing why, felt awkward about paying court to her. When they had last met on the old princeвҖҷs name day, she had answered at random all his attempts to talk sentimentally, evidently not listening to what he was saying. Julie on the contrary accepted his attentions readily, though in a manner peculiar to herself. She was twenty-seven. After the death of her brothers she had become very wealthy. She was by now decidedly plain, but thought herself not merely as good-looking as before but even far more attractive. She was confirmed in this delusion by the fact that she had become a very wealthy heiress and also by the fact that the older she grew the less dangerous she became to men, and the more freely they could associate with her and avail themselves of her suppers, soirees, and the animated company that assembled at her house, without incurring any obligation. A man who would have been afraid ten years before of going every day to the house when there was a girl of seventeen there, for fear of compromising her and committing himself, would now go boldly every day and treat her not as a marriageable girl but as a sexless acquaintance. That winter the KarГЎginsвҖҷ house was the most agreeable and hospitable in Moscow. In addition to the formal evening and dinner parties, a large company, chiefly of men, gathered there every day, supping at midnight and staying till three in the morning. Julie never missed a ball, a promenade, or a play. Her dresses were always of the latest fashion. But in spite of that she seemed to be disillusioned about everything and told everyone that she did not believe either in friendship or in love, or any of the joys of life, and expected peace only вҖңyonder.вҖқ She adopted the tone of one who has suffered a great disappointment, like a girl who has either lost the man she loved or been cruelly deceived by him. Though nothing of the kind had happened to her she was regarded in that light, and had even herself come to believe that she had suffered much in life. This melancholy, which did not prevent her amusing herself, did not hinder the young people who came to her house from passing the time pleasantly. Every visitor who came to the house paid his tribute to the melancholy mood of the hostess, and then amused himself with society gossip, dancing, intellectual games, and bouts rimГ©s, which were in vogue at the KarГЎginsвҖҷ. Only a few of these young men, among them BorГӯs, entered more deeply into JulieвҖҷs melancholy, and with these she had prolonged conversations in private on the vanity of all worldly things, and to them she showed her albums filled with mournful sketches, maxims, and verses. To BorГӯs, Julie was particularly gracious: she regretted his early disillusionment with life, offered him such consolation of friendship as she who had herself suffered so much could render, and showed him her album. BorГӯs sketched two trees in the album and wrote: вҖңRustic trees, your dark branches shed gloom and melancholy upon me.вҖқ On another page he drew a tomb, and wrote: La mort est secourable et la mort est tranquille. Ah! contre les douleurs il nвҖҷy a pas dвҖҷautre asile.72 Julie said this was charming. вҖңThere is something so enchanting in the smile of melancholy,вҖқ she said to BorГӯs, repeating word for word a passage she had copied from a book. вҖңIt is a ray of light in the darkness, a shade between sadness and despair, showing the possibility of consolation.вҖқ In reply BorГӯs wrote these lines: Aliment de poison dвҖҷune Гўme trop sensible, Toi, sans qui le bonheur me serait impossible, Tendre mГ©lancholie, ah, viens me consoler, Viens calmer les tourments de ma sombre retraite, Et mГӘle une douceur secrГЁte A ces pleurs que je sens couler.73 For BorГӯs, Julie played most doleful nocturnes on her harp. BorГӯs read Poor Liza aloud to her, and more than once interrupted the reading because of the emotions that choked him. Meeting at large gatherings Julie and BorГӯs looked on one another as the only souls who understood one another in a world of indifferent people. Anna MikhГЎylovna, who often visited the KarГЎgins, while playing cards with the mother made careful inquiries as to JulieвҖҷs dowry (she was to have two estates in PГ©nza and the NizhegГіrod forests). Anna MikhГЎylovna regarded the refined sadness that united her son to the wealthy Julie with emotion, and resignation to the Divine will. вҖңYou are always charming and melancholy, my dear Julie,вҖқ she said to the daughter. вҖңBorГӯs says his soul finds repose at your house. He has suffered so many disappointments and is so sensitive,вҖқ said she to the mother. вҖңAh, my dear, I canвҖҷt tell you how fond I have grown of Julie latterly,вҖқ she said to her son. вҖңBut who could help loving her? She is an angelic being! Ah, BorГӯs, BorГӯs!вҖқвҒ вҖ”she paused. вҖңAnd how I pity her mother,вҖқ she went on; вҖңtoday she showed me her accounts and letters from PГ©nza (they have enormous estates there), and she, poor thing, has no one to help her, and they do cheat her so!вҖқ BorГӯs smiled almost imperceptibly while listening to his mother. He laughed blandly at her naive diplomacy but listened to what she had to say, and sometimes questioned her carefully about the PГ©nza and NizhegГіrod estates. Julie had long been expecting a proposal from her melancholy adorer and was ready to accept it; but some secret feeling of repulsion for her, for her passionate desire to get married, for her artificiality, and a feeling of horror at renouncing the possibility of real love still restrained BorГӯs. His leave was expiring. He spent every day and whole days at the KarГЎginsвҖҷ, and every day on thinking the matter over told himself that he would propose tomorrow. But in JulieвҖҷs presence, looking at her red face and chin (nearly always powdered), her moist eyes, and her expression of continual readiness to pass at once from melancholy to an unnatural rapture of married bliss, BorГӯs could not utter the decisive words, though in imagination he had long regarded himself as the possessor of those PГ©nza and NizhegГіrod estates and had apportioned the use of the income from them. Julie saw BorГӯsвҖҷ indecision, and sometimes the thought occurred to her that she was repulsive to him, but her feminine self-deception immediately supplied her with consolation, and she told herself that he was only shy from love. Her melancholy, however, began to turn to irritability, and not long before BorГӯsвҖҷ departure she formed a definite plan of action. Just as BorГӯsвҖҷ leave of absence was expiring, Anatole KurГЎgin made his appearance in Moscow, and of course in the KarГЎginsвҖҷ drawing room, and Julie, suddenly abandoning her melancholy, became cheerful and very attentive to KurГЎgin. вҖңMy dear,вҖқ said Anna MikhГЎylovna to her son, вҖңI know from a reliable source that Prince Basile has sent his son to Moscow to get him married to Julie. I am so fond of Julie that I should be sorry for her. What do you think of it, my dear?вҖқ The idea of being made a fool of and of having thrown away that whole month of arduous melancholy service to Julie, and of seeing all the revenue from the PГ©nza estates which he had already mentally apportioned and put to proper use fall into the hands of another, and especially into the hands of that idiot Anatole, pained BorГӯs. He drove to the KarГЎginsвҖҷ with the firm intention of proposing. Julie met him in a gay, careless manner, spoke casually of how she had enjoyed yesterdayвҖҷs ball, and asked when he was leaving. Though BorГӯs had come intentionally to speak of his love and therefore meant to be tender, he began speaking irritably of feminine inconstancy, of how easily women can turn from sadness to joy, and how their moods depend solely on who happens to be paying court to them. Julie was offended and replied that it was true that a woman needs variety, and the same thing over and over again would weary anyone. вҖңThen I should advise youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ BorГӯs began, wishing to sting her; but at that instant the galling thought occurred to him that he might have to leave Moscow without having accomplished his aim, and have vainly wasted his effortsвҒ вҖ”which was a thing he never allowed to happen. He checked himself in the middle of the sentence, lowered his eyes to avoid seeing her unpleasantly irritated and irresolute face, and said: вҖңI did not come here at all to quarrel with you. On the contraryвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He glanced at her to make sure that he might go on. Her irritability had suddenly quite vanished, and her anxious, imploring eyes were fixed on him with greedy expectation. вҖңI can always arrange so as not to see her often,вҖқ thought BorГӯs. вҖңThe affair has been begun and must be finished!вҖқ He blushed hotly, raised his eyes to hers, and said: вҖңYou know my feelings for you!вҖқ There was no need to say more: JulieвҖҷs face shone with triumph and self-satisfaction; but she forced BorГӯs to say all that is said on such occasionsвҒ вҖ”that he loved her and had never loved any other woman more than her. She knew that for the PГ©nza estates and NizhegГіrod forests she could demand this, and she received what she demanded. The affianced couple, no longer alluding to trees that shed gloom and melancholy upon them, planned the arrangements of a splendid house in Petersburg, paid calls, and prepared everything for a brilliant wedding. VI VI At the end of January Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich RostГіv went to Moscow with NatГЎsha and SГіnya. The countess was still unwell and unable to travel but it was impossible to wait for her recovery. Prince AndrГ©y was expected in Moscow any day, the trousseau had to be ordered and the estate near Moscow had to be sold, besides which the opportunity of presenting his future daughter-in-law to old Prince BolkГіnski while he was in Moscow could not be missed. The RostГіvsвҖҷ Moscow house had not been heated that winter and, as they had come only for a short time and the countess was not with them, the count decided to stay with MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna AkhrosГӯmova, who had long been pressing her hospitality on them. Late one evening the RostГіvsвҖҷ four sleighs drove into MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs courtyard in the old KonyГәsheny street. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna lived alone. She had already married off her daughter, and her sons were all in the service. She held herself as erect, told everyone her opinion as candidly, loudly, and bluntly as ever, and her whole bearing seemed a reproach to others for any weakness, passion, or temptationвҒ вҖ”the possibility of which she did not admit. From early in the morning, wearing a dressing jacket, she attended to her household affairs, and then she drove out: on holy days to church and after the service to jails and prisons on affairs of which she never spoke to anyone. On ordinary days, after dressing, she received petitioners of various classes, of whom there were always some. Then she had dinner, a substantial and appetizing meal at which there were always three or four guests; after dinner she played a game of boston, and at night she had the newspapers or a new book read to her while she knitted. She rarely made an exception and went out to pay visits, and then only to the most important persons in the town. She had not yet gone to bed when the RostГіvs arrived and the pulley of the hall door squeaked from the cold as it let in the RostГіvs and their servants. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, with her spectacles hanging down on her nose and her head flung back, stood in the hall doorway looking with a stern, grim face at the new arrivals. One might have thought she was angry with the travelers and would immediately turn them out, had she not at the same time been giving careful instructions to the servants for the accommodation of the visitors and their belongings. вҖңThe countвҖҷs things? Bring them here,вҖқ she said, pointing to the portmanteaus and not greeting anyone. вҖңThe young ladiesвҖҷ? There to the left. Now what are you dawdling for?вҖқ she cried to the maids. вҖңGet the samovar ready!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ YouвҖҷve grown plumper and prettier,вҖқ she remarked, drawing NatГЎsha (whose cheeks were glowing from the cold) to her by the hood. вҖңFoo! You are cold! Now take off your things, quick!вҖқ she shouted to the count who was going to kiss her hand. вҖңYouвҖҷre half frozen, IвҖҷm sure! Bring some rum for tea!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Bonjour, SonyГәshka dear!вҖқ she added, turning to SГіnya and indicating by this French greeting her slightly contemptuous though affectionate attitude toward her. When they came in to tea, having taken off their outdoor things and tidied themselves up after their journey, MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna kissed them all in due order. вҖңIвҖҷm heartily glad you have come and are staying with me. It was high time,вҖқ she said, giving NatГЎsha a significant look. вҖңThe old man is here and his sonвҖҷs expected any day. YouвҖҷll have to make his acquaintance. But weвҖҷll speak of that later on,вҖқ she added, glancing at SГіnya with a look that showed she did not want to speak of it in her presence. вҖңNow listen,вҖқ she said to the count. вҖңWhat do you want tomorrow? Whom will you send for? ShinshГӯn?вҖқ she crooked one of her fingers. вҖңThe sniveling Anna MikhГЎylovna? ThatвҖҷs two. SheвҖҷs here with her son. The son is getting married! Then BezГәkhov, eh? He is here too, with his wife. He ran away from her and she came galloping after him. He dined with me on Wednesday. As for themвҖқвҒ вҖ”and she pointed to the girlsвҒ вҖ”вҖңtomorrow IвҖҷll take them first to the Iberian shrine of the Mother of God, and then weвҖҷll drive to the Super-RogueвҖҷs. I suppose youвҖҷll have everything new. DonвҖҷt judge by me: sleeves nowadays are this size! The other day young Princess IrГӯna VasГӯlevna came to see me; she was an awful sightвҒ вҖ”looked as if she had put two barrels on her arms. You know not a day passes now without some new fashion.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ And what have you to do yourself?вҖқ she asked the count sternly. вҖңOne thing has come on top of another: her rags to buy, and now a purchaser has turned up for the Moscow estate and for the house. If you will be so kind, IвҖҷll fix a time and go down to the estate just for a day, and leave my lassies with you.вҖқ вҖңAll right. All right. TheyвҖҷll be safe with me, as safe as in Chancery! IвҖҷll take them where they must go, scold them a bit, and pet them a bit,вҖқ said MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, touching her goddaughter and favorite, NatГЎsha, on the cheek with her large hand. Next morning MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna took the young ladies to the Iberian shrine of the Mother of God and to Madame Suppert-Roguet, who was so afraid of MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna that she always let her have costumes at a loss merely to get rid of her. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna ordered almost the whole trousseau. When they got home she turned everybody out of the room except NatГЎsha, and then called her pet to her armchair. вҖңWell, now weвҖҷll talk. I congratulate you on your betrothed. YouвҖҷve hooked a fine fellow! I am glad for your sake and IвҖҷve known him since he was so high.вҖқ She held her hand a couple of feet from the ground. NatГЎsha blushed happily. вҖңI like him and all his family. Now listen! You know that old Prince NikolГЎy much dislikes his sonвҖҷs marrying. The old fellowвҖҷs crotchety! Of course Prince AndrГ©y is not a child and can shift without him, but itвҖҷs not nice to enter a family against a fatherвҖҷs will. One wants to do it peacefully and lovingly. YouвҖҷre a clever girl and youвҖҷll know how to manage. Be kind, and use your wits. Then all will be well.вҖқ NatГЎsha remained silent, from shyness MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna supposed, but really because she disliked anyone interfering in what touched her love of Prince AndrГ©y, which seemed to her so apart from all human affairs that no one could understand it. She loved and knew Prince AndrГ©y, he loved her only, and was to come one of these days and take her. She wanted nothing more. вҖңYou see I have known him a long time and am also fond of MГЎshenka, your future sister-in-law. вҖҳHusbandsвҖҷ sisters bring up blisters,вҖҷ but this one wouldnвҖҷt hurt a fly. She has asked me to bring you two together. Tomorrow youвҖҷll go with your father to see her. Be very nice and affectionate to her: youвҖҷre younger than she. When he comes, heвҖҷll find you already know his sister and father and are liked by them. Am I right or not? WonвҖҷt that be best?вҖқ вҖңYes, it will,вҖқ NatГЎsha answered reluctantly. VII VII Next day, by MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs advice, Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich took NatГЎsha to call on Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich. The count did not set out cheerfully on this visit, at heart he felt afraid. He well remembered the last interview he had had with the old prince at the time of the enrollment, when in reply to an invitation to dinner he had had to listen to an angry reprimand for not having provided his full quota of men. NatГЎsha, on the other hand, having put on her best gown, was in the highest spirits. вҖңThey canвҖҷt help liking me,вҖқ she thought. вҖңEverybody always has liked me, and I am so willing to do anything they wish, so ready to be fond of himвҒ вҖ”for being his fatherвҒ вҖ”and of herвҒ вҖ”for being his sisterвҒ вҖ”that there is no reason for them not to like me.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ They drove up to the gloomy old house on the VozdvГӯzhenka and entered the vestibule. вҖңWell, the Lord have mercy on us!вҖқ said the count, half in jest, half in earnest; but NatГЎsha noticed that her father was flurried on entering the anteroom and inquired timidly and softly whether the prince and princess were at home. When they had been announced a perturbation was noticeable among the servants. The footman who had gone to announce them was stopped by another in the large hall and they whispered to one another. Then a maidservant ran into the hall and hurriedly said something, mentioning the princess. At last an old, cross looking footman came and announced to the RostГіvs that the prince was not receiving, but that the princess begged them to walk up. The first person who came to meet the visitors was Mademoiselle Bourienne. She greeted the father and daughter with special politeness and showed them to the princessвҖҷ room. The princess, looking excited and nervous, her face flushed in patches, ran in to meet the visitors, treading heavily, and vainly trying to appear cordial and at ease. From the first glance Princess MГЎrya did not like NatГЎsha. She thought her too fashionably dressed, frivolously gay and vain. She did not at all realize that before having seen her future sister-in-law she was prejudiced against her by involuntary envy of her beauty, youth, and happiness, as well as by jealousy of her brotherвҖҷs love for her. Apart from this insuperable antipathy to her, Princess MГЎrya was agitated just then because on the RostГіvsвҖҷ being announced, the old prince had shouted that he did not wish to see them, that Princess MГЎrya might do so if she chose, but they were not to be admitted to him. She had decided to receive them, but feared lest the prince might at any moment indulge in some freak, as he seemed much upset by the RostГіvsвҖҷ visit. вҖңThere, my dear princess, IвҖҷve brought you my songstress,вҖқ said the count, bowing and looking round uneasily as if afraid the old prince might appear. вҖңI am so glad you should get to know one anotherвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ very sorry the prince is still ailing,вҖқ and after a few more commonplace remarks he rose. вҖңIf youвҖҷll allow me to leave my NatГЎsha in your hands for a quarter of an hour, Princess, IвҖҷll drive round to see Anna SemГ«novna, itвҖҷs quite near in the DogsвҖҷ Square, and then IвҖҷll come back for her.вҖқ The count had devised this diplomatic ruse (as he afterwards told his daughter) to give the future sisters-in-law an opportunity to talk to one another freely, but another motive was to avoid the danger of encountering the old prince, of whom he was afraid. He did not mention this to his daughter, but NatГЎsha noticed her fatherвҖҷs nervousness and anxiety and felt mortified by it. She blushed for him, grew still angrier at having blushed, and looked at the princess with a bold and defiant expression which said that she was not afraid of anybody. The princess told the count that she would be delighted, and only begged him to stay longer at Anna SemГ«novnaвҖҷs, and he departed. Despite the uneasy glances thrown at her by Princess MГЎryaвҒ вҖ”who wished to have a tГӘte-Г -tГӘte with NatГЎshaвҒ вҖ”Mademoiselle Bourienne remained in the room and persistently talked about Moscow amusements and theaters. NatГЎsha felt offended by the hesitation she had noticed in the anteroom, by her fatherвҖҷs nervousness, and by the unnatural manner of the princess whoвҒ вҖ”she thoughtвҒ вҖ”was making a favor of receiving her, and so everything displeased her. She did not like Princess MГЎrya, whom she thought very plain, affected, and dry. NatГЎsha suddenly shrank into herself and involuntarily assumed an offhand air which alienated Princess MГЎrya still more. After five minutes of irksome, constrained conversation, they heard the sound of slippered feet rapidly approaching. Princess MГЎrya looked frightened. The door opened and the old prince, in a dressing gown and a white nightcap, came in. вҖңAh, madam!вҖқ he began. вҖңMadam, CountessвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Countess RostГіva, if I am not mistakenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I beg you to excuse me, to excuse meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I did not know, madam. God is my witness, I did not know you had honored us with a visit, and I came in such a costume only to see my daughter. I beg you to excuse meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ God is my witness, I didnвҖҷt knowвҒ вҖ”вҖқ he repeated, stressing the word вҖңGodвҖқ so unnaturally and so unpleasantly that Princess MГЎrya stood with downcast eyes not daring to look either at her father or at NatГЎsha. Nor did the latter, having risen and curtsied, know what to do. Mademoiselle Bourienne alone smiled agreeably. вҖңI beg you to excuse me, excuse me! God is my witness, I did not know,вҖқ muttered the old man, and after looking NatГЎsha over from head to foot he went out. Mademoiselle Bourienne was the first to recover herself after this apparition and began speaking about the princeвҖҷs indisposition. NatГЎsha and Princess MГЎrya looked at one another in silence, and the longer they did so without saying what they wanted to say, the greater grew their antipathy to one another. When the count returned, NatГЎsha was impolitely pleased and hastened to get away: at that moment she hated the stiff, elderly princess, who could place her in such an embarrassing position and had spent half an hour with her without once mentioning Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңI couldnвҖҷt begin talking about him in the presence of that Frenchwoman,вҖқ thought NatГЎsha. The same thought was meanwhile tormenting Princess MГЎrya. She knew what she ought to have said to NatГЎsha, but she had been unable to say it because Mademoiselle Bourienne was in the way, and because, without knowing why, she felt it very difficult to speak of the marriage. When the count was already leaving the room, Princess MГЎrya went up hurriedly to NatГЎsha, took her by the hand, and said with a deep sigh: вҖңWait, I mustвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ NatГЎsha glanced at her ironically without knowing why. вҖңDear NatГЎli,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya, вҖңI want you to know that I am glad my brother has found happiness.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ She paused, feeling that she was not telling the truth. NatГЎsha noticed this and guessed its reason. вҖңI think, Princess, it is not convenient to speak of that now,вҖқ she said with external dignity and coldness, though she felt the tears choking her. вҖңWhat have I said and what have I done?вҖқ thought she, as soon as she was out of the room. They waited a long time for NatГЎsha to come to dinner that day. She sat in her room crying like a child, blowing her nose and sobbing. SГіnya stood beside her, kissing her hair. вҖңNatГЎsha, what is it about?вҖқ she asked. вҖңWhat do they matter to you? It will all pass, NatГЎsha.вҖқ вҖңBut if you only knew how offensive it wasвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ as if IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңDonвҖҷt talk about it, NatГЎsha. It wasnвҖҷt your fault so why should you mind? Kiss me,вҖқ said SГіnya. NatГЎsha raised her head and, kissing her friend on the lips, pressed her wet face against her. вҖңI canвҖҷt tell you, I donвҖҷt know. No oneвҖҷs to blame,вҖқ said NatГЎshaвҒ вҖ”вҖңItвҖҷs my fault. But it all hurts terribly. Oh, why doesnвҖҷt he come?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ She came in to dinner with red eyes. MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna, who knew how the prince had received the RostГіvs, pretended not to notice how upset NatГЎsha was and jested resolutely and loudly at table with the count and the other guests. VIII VIII That evening the RostГіvs went to the Opera, for which MГЎrya DmГӯtrievna had taken a box. NatГЎsha did not want to go, but could not refuse MГЎrya DmГӯtrievnaвҖҷs kind offer which was intended expressly for her. When she came ready dressed into the ballroom to await her father, and looking in the large mirror there saw that she was pretty, very pretty, she felt even more sad, but it was a sweet, tender sadness. вҖңO God, if he were here now I would not behave as I did then, but differently. I would not be silly and afraid of things, I would simply embrace him, cling to him, and make him look at me with those searching inquiring eyes with which he has so often looked at me, and then I would make him laugh as he used to laugh. And his eyesвҒ вҖ”how I see those eyes!вҖқ thought NatГЎsha. вҖңAnd what do his father and sister matter to me? I love him alone, him, him, with that face and those eyes, with his smile, manly and yet childlike.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No, I had better not think of him; not think of him but forget him, quite forget him for the present. I canвҖҷt bear this waiting and I shall cry in a minute!вҖқ and she turned away from the glass, making an effort not to cry. вҖңAnd how can SГіnya love NikГіlenka so calmly and quietly and wait so long and so patiently?вҖқ thought she, looking at SГіnya, who also came in quite ready, with a fan in her hand. вҖңNo, sheвҖҷs altogether different. I canвҖҷt!вҖқ NatГЎsha at that moment felt so softened and tender that it was not enough for her to love and know she was beloved, she wanted now, at once, to embrace the man she loved, to speak and hear from him words of love such as filled her heart. While she sat in the carriage beside her father, pensively watching the lights of the street lamps flickering on the frozen window, she felt still sadder and more in love, and forgot where she was going and with whom. Having fallen into the line of carriages, the RostГіvsвҖҷ carriage drove up to the theater, its wheels squeaking over the snow. NatГЎsha and SГіnya, holding up their dresses, jumped out quickly. The count got out helped by the footmen, and, passing among men and women who were entering and the program sellers, they all three went along the corridor to the first row of boxes. Through the closed doors the music was already audible. вҖңNathalie, your hair!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ whispered SГіnya. An attendant deferentially and quickly slipped before the ladies and opened the door of their box. The music sounded louder and through the door rows of brightly lit boxes in which ladies sat with bare arms and shoulders, and noisy stalls brilliant with uniforms, glittered before their eyes. A lady entering the next box shot a glance of feminine envy at NatГЎsha. The curtain had not yet risen and the overture was being played. NatГЎsha, smoothing her gown, went in with SГіnya and sat down, scanning the brilliant tiers of boxes opposite. A sensation she had not experienced for a long timeвҒ вҖ”that of hundreds of eyes looking at her bare arms and neckвҒ вҖ”suddenly affected her both agreeably and disagreeably and called up a whole crowd of memories, desires and emotions associated with that feeling. The two remarkably pretty girls, NatГЎsha and SГіnya, with Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich who had not been seen in Moscow for a long time, attracted general attention. Moreover, everybody knew vaguely of NatГЎshaвҖҷs engagement to Prince AndrГ©y, and knew that the RostГіvs had lived in the country ever since, and all looked with curiosity at a fiancГ©e who was making one of the best matches in Russia. NatГЎshaвҖҷs looks, as everyone told her, had improved in the country, and that evening thanks to her agitation she was particularly pretty. She struck those who saw her by her fullness of life and beauty, combined with her indifference to everything about her. Her black eyes looked at the crowd without seeking anyone, and her delicate arm, bare to above the elbow, lay on the velvet edge of the box, while, evidently unconsciously, she opened and closed her hand in time to the music, crumpling her program. вҖңLook, thereвҖҷs AlГ©nina,вҖқ said SГіnya, вҖңwith her mother, isnвҖҷt it?вҖқ вҖңDear me, MikhГЎil KirГӯlovich has grown still stouter!вҖқ remarked the count. вҖңLook at our Anna MikhГЎylovnaвҒ вҖ”what a headdress she has on!вҖқ вҖңThe KarГЎgins, JulieвҒ вҖ”and BorГӯs with them. One can see at once that theyвҖҷre engaged.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңDrubetskГіy has proposed?вҖқ вҖңOh yes, I heard it today,вҖқ said ShinshГӯn, coming into the RostГіvsвҖҷ box. NatГЎsha looked in the direction in which her fatherвҖҷs eyes were turned and saw Julie sitting beside her mother with a happy look on her face and a string of pearls round her thick red neckвҒ вҖ”which NatГЎsha knew was covered with powder. Behind them, wearing a smile and leaning over with an ear to JulieвҖҷs mouth, was BorГӯsвҖҷ handsome smoothly brushed head. He looked at the RostГіvs from under his brows and said something, smiling, to his betrothed. вҖңThey are talking about us, about me and him!вҖқ thought NatГЎsha. вҖңAnd he no doubt is calming her jealousy of me. They neednвҖҷt trouble themselves! If only they knew how little I am concerned about any of them.вҖқ Behind them sat Anna MikhГЎylovna wearing a green headdress and with a happy look of resignation to the will of God on her face. Their box was pervaded by that atmosphere of an affianced couple which NatГЎsha knew so well and liked so much. She turned away and suddenly remembered all that had been so humiliating in her morningвҖҷs visit. вҖңWhat right has he not to wish to receive me into his family? Oh, better not think of itвҒ вҖ”not till he comes back!вҖқ she told herself, and began looking at the faces, some strange and some familiar, in the stalls.
