Our Emperor joined the army to encourage it to defend every inch of Russian soil and not to retreat. The enormous Drissa camp was formed on PfuelвҖҷs plan, and there was no intention of retiring farther. The Emperor reproached the commanders in chief for every step they retired. He could not bear the idea of letting the enemy even reach SmolГ©nsk, still less could he contemplate the burning of Moscow, and when our armies did unite he was displeased that SmolГ©nsk was abandoned and burned without a general engagement having been fought under its walls. So thought the Emperor, and the Russian commanders and people were still more provoked at the thought that our forces were retreating into the depths of the country. Napoleon having cut our armies apart advanced far into the country and missed several chances of forcing an engagement. In August he was at SmolГ©nsk and thought only of how to advance farther, though as we now see that advance was evidently ruinous to him. The facts clearly show that Napoleon did not foresee the danger of the advance on Moscow, nor did Alexander and the Russian commanders then think of luring Napoleon on, but quite the contrary. The luring of Napoleon into the depths of the country was not the result of any plan, for no one believed it to be possible; it resulted from a most complex interplay of intrigues, aims, and wishes among those who took part in the war and had no perception whatever of the inevitable, or of the one way of saving Russia. Everything came about fortuitously. The armies were divided at the commencement of the campaign. We tried to unite them, with the evident intention of giving battle and checking the enemyвҖҷs advance, and by this effort to unite them while avoiding battle with a much stronger enemy, and necessarily withdrawing the armies at an acute angleвҒ вҖ”we led the French on to SmolГ©nsk. But we withdrew at an acute angle not only because the French advanced between our two armies; the angle became still more acute and we withdrew still farther, because Barclay de Tolly was an unpopular foreigner disliked by BagratiГіn (who would come under his command), and BagratiГіnвҒ вҖ”being in command of the second armyвҒ вҖ”tried to postpone joining up and coming under BarclayвҖҷs command as long as he could. BagratiГіn was slow in effecting the junctionвҒ вҖ”though that was the chief aim of all at headquartersвҒ вҖ”because, as he alleged, he exposed his army to danger on this march, and it was best for him to retire more to the left and more to the south, worrying the enemy from flank and rear and securing from the Ukraine recruits for his army; and it looks as if he planned this in order not to come under the command of the detested foreigner Barclay, whose rank was inferior to his own. The Emperor was with the army to encourage it, but his presence and ignorance of what steps to take, and the enormous number of advisers and plans, destroyed the first armyвҖҷs energy and it retired. The intention was to make a stand at the Drissa camp, but Paulucci, aiming at becoming commander in chief, unexpectedly employed his energy to influence Alexander, and PfuelвҖҷs whole plan was abandoned and the command entrusted to Barclay. But as Barclay did not inspire confidence his power was limited. The armies were divided, there was no unity of command, and Barclay was unpopular; but from this confusion, division, and the unpopularity of the foreign commander in chief, there resulted on the one hand indecision and the avoidance of a battle (which we could not have refrained from had the armies been united and had someone else, instead of Barclay, been in command) and on the other an ever-increasing indignation against the foreigners and an increase in patriotic zeal. At last the Emperor left the army, and as the most convenient and indeed the only pretext for his departure it was decided that it was necessary for him to inspire the people in the capitals and arouse the nation in general to a patriotic war. And by this visit of the Emperor to Moscow the strength of the Russian army was trebled. He left in order not to obstruct the commander in chiefвҖҷs undivided control of the army, and hoping that more decisive action would then be taken, but the command of the armies became still more confused and enfeebled. Bennigsen, the TsarГ©vich, and a swarm of adjutants general remained with the army to keep the commander in chief under observation and arouse his energy, and Barclay, feeling less free than ever under the observation of all these вҖңeyes of the Emperor,вҖқ became still more cautious of undertaking any decisive action and avoided giving battle. Barclay stood for caution. The TsarГ©vich hinted at treachery and demanded a general engagement. LubomГӯrski, BronnГӯtski, Wlocki, and the others of that group stirred up so much trouble that Barclay, under pretext of sending papers to the Emperor, dispatched these Polish adjutants general to Petersburg and plunged into an open struggle with Bennigsen and the TsarГ©vich. At SmolГ©nsk the armies at last reunited, much as BagratiГіn disliked it. BagratiГіn drove up in a carriage to the house occupied by Barclay. Barclay donned his sash and came out to meet and report to his senior officer BagratiГіn. Despite his seniority in rank BagratiГіn, in this contest of magnanimity, took his orders from Barclay, but, having submitted, agreed with him less than ever. By the EmperorвҖҷs orders BagratiГіn reported direct to him. He wrote to ArakchГ©ev, the EmperorвҖҷs confidant: вҖңIt must be as my sovereign pleases, but I cannot work with the Minister (meaning Barclay). For GodвҖҷs sake send me somewhere else if only in command of a regiment. I cannot stand it here. Headquarters are so full of Germans that a Russian cannot exist and there is no sense in anything. I thought I was really serving my sovereign and the Fatherland, but it turns out that I am serving Barclay. I confess I do not want to.вҖқ The swarm of BronnГӯtskis and Wintzingerodes and their like still further embittered the relations between the commanders in chief, and even less unity resulted. Preparations were made to fight the French before SmolГ©nsk. A general was sent to survey the position. This general, hating Barclay, rode to visit a friend of his own, a corps commander, and, having spent the day with him, returned to Barclay and condemned, as unsuitable from every point of view, the battleground he had not seen. While disputes and intrigues were going on about the future field of battle, and while we were looking for the FrenchвҒ вҖ”having lost touch with themвҒ вҖ”the French stumbled upon NevГ©rovskiвҖҷs division and reached the walls of SmolГ©nsk. It was necessary to fight an unexpected battle at SmolГ©nsk to save our lines of communication. The battle was fought and thousands were killed on both sides. SmolГ©nsk was abandoned contrary to the wishes of the Emperor and of the whole people. But SmolГ©nsk was burned by its own inhabitants who had been misled by their governor. And these ruined inhabitants, setting an example to other Russians, went to Moscow thinking only of their own losses but kindling hatred of the foe. Napoleon advanced farther and we retired, thus arriving at the very result which caused his destruction. X X After her fatherвҖҷs funeral Princess MГЎrya shut herself up in her room and did not admit anyone. A maid came to the door to say that AlpГЎtych was asking for orders about their departure. (This was before his talk with Dron.) Princess MГЎrya raised herself on the sofa on which she had been lying and replied through the closed door that she did not mean to go away and begged to be left in peace. The windows of the room in which she was lying looked westward. She lay on the sofa with her face to the wall, fingering the buttons of the leather cushion and seeing nothing but that cushion, and her confused thoughts were centered on one subjectвҒ вҖ”the irrevocability of death and her own spiritual baseness, which she had not suspected, but which had shown itself during her fatherвҖҷs illness. She wished to pray but did not dare to, dared not in her present state of mind address herself to God. She lay for a long time in that position. The sun had reached the other side of the house, and its slanting rays shone into the open window, lighting up the room and part of the morocco cushion at which Princess MГЎrya was looking. The flow of her thoughts suddenly stopped. Unconsciously she sat up, smoothed her hair, got up, and went to the window, involuntarily inhaling the freshness of the clear but windy evening. вҖңYes, you can well enjoy the evening now! He is gone and no one will hinder you,вҖқ she said to herself, and sinking into a chair she let her head fall on the window sill. Someone spoke her name in a soft and tender voice from the garden and kissed her head. She looked up. It was Mademoiselle Bourienne in a black dress and weepers. She softly approached Princess MГЎrya, sighed, kissed her, and immediately began to cry. The princess looked up at her. All their former disharmony and her own jealousy recurred to her mind. But she remembered too how he had changed of late toward Mademoiselle Bourienne and could not bear to see her, thereby showing how unjust were the reproaches Princess MГЎrya had mentally addressed to her. вҖңBesides, is it for me, for me who desired his death, to condemn anyone?вҖқ she thought. Princess MГЎrya vividly pictured to herself the position of Mademoiselle Bourienne, whom she had of late kept at a distance, but who yet was dependent on her and living in her house. She felt sorry for her and held out her hand with a glance of gentle inquiry. Mademoiselle Bourienne at once began crying again and kissed that hand, speaking of the princessвҖҷ sorrow and making herself a partner in it. She said her only consolation was the fact that the princess allowed her to share her sorrow, that all the old misunderstandings should sink into nothing but this great grief; that she felt herself blameless in regard to everyone, and that he, from above, saw her affection and gratitude. The princess heard her, not heeding her words but occasionally looking up at her and listening to the sound of her voice. вҖңYour position is doubly terrible, dear princess,вҖқ said Mademoiselle Bourienne after a pause. вҖңI understand that you could not, and cannot, think of yourself, but with my love for you I must do so.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Has AlpГЎtych been to you? Has he spoken to you of going away?вҖқ she asked. Princess MГЎrya did not answer. She did not understand who was to go or where to. вҖңIs it possible to plan or think of anything now? Is it not all the same?вҖқ she thought, and did not reply. вҖңYou know, chГЁre Marie,вҖқ said Mademoiselle Bourienne, вҖңthat we are in dangerвҒ вҖ”are surrounded by the French. It would be dangerous to move now. If we go we are almost sure to be taken prisoners, and God knowsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Princess MГЎrya looked at her companion without understanding what she was talking about. вҖңOh, if anyone knew how little anything matters to me now,вҖқ she said. вҖңOf course I would on no account wish to go away from him.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ AlpГЎtych did say something about going.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Speak to him; I can do nothing, nothing, and donвҖҷt want to.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңIвҖҷve spoken to him. He hopes we should be in time to get away tomorrow, but I think it would now be better to stay here,вҖқ said Mademoiselle Bourienne. вҖңBecause, you will agree, chГЁre Marie, to fall into the hands of the soldiers or of riotous peasants would be terrible.вҖқ Mademoiselle Bourienne took from her reticule a proclamation (not printed on ordinary Russian paper) of General RameauвҖҷs, telling people not to leave their homes and that the French authorities would afford them proper protection. She handed this to the princess. вҖңI think it would be best to appeal to that general,вҖқ she continued, вҖңand I am sure that all due respect would be shown you.вҖқ Princess MГЎrya read the paper, and her face began to quiver with stifled sobs. вҖңFrom whom did you get this?вҖқ she asked. вҖңThey probably recognized that I am French, by my name,вҖқ replied Mademoiselle Bourienne blushing. Princess MГЎrya, with the paper in her hand, rose from the window and with a pale face went out of the room and into what had been Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs study. вҖңDunyГЎsha, send AlpГЎtych, or DrГіnushka, or somebody to me!вҖқ she said, вҖңand tell AmГЎlya KarlГіvna not to come to me,вҖқ she added, hearing Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs voice. вҖңWe must go at once, at once!вҖқ she said, appalled at the thought of being left in the hands of the French. вҖңIf Prince AndrГ©y heard that I was in the power of the French! That I, the daughter of Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich BolkГіnski, asked General Rameau for protection and accepted his favor!вҖқ This idea horrified her, made her shudder, blush, and feel such a rush of anger and pride as she had never experienced before. All that was distressing, and especially all that was humiliating, in her position rose vividly to her mind. вҖңThey, the French, would settle in this house: M. le GГ©nГ©ral Rameau would occupy Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs study and amuse himself by looking through and reading his letters and papers. Mademoiselle Bourienne would do the honors of BoguchГЎrovo for him. I should be given a small room as a favor, the soldiers would violate my fatherвҖҷs newly dug grave to steal his crosses and stars, they would tell me of their victories over the Russians, and would pretend to sympathize with my sorrowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ thought Princess MГЎrya, not thinking her own thoughts but feeling bound to think like her father and her brother. For herself she did not care where she remained or what happened to her, but she felt herself the representative of her dead father and of Prince AndrГ©y. Involuntarily she thought their thoughts and felt their feelings. What they would have said and what they would have done she felt bound to say and do. She went into Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs study, trying to enter completely into his ideas, and considered her position. The demands of life, which had seemed to her annihilated by her fatherвҖҷs death, all at once rose before her with a new, previously unknown force and took possession of her. Agitated and flushed she paced the room, sending now for MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich and now for TГӯkhon or Dron. DunyГЎsha, the nurse, and the other maids could not say in how far Mademoiselle BourienneвҖҷs statement was correct. AlpГЎtych was not at home, he had gone to the police. Neither could the architect MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich, who on being sent for came in with sleepy eyes, tell Princess MГЎrya anything. With just the same smile of agreement with which for fifteen years he had been accustomed to answer the old prince without expressing views of his own, he now replied to Princess MГЎrya, so that nothing definite could be got from his answers. The old valet TГӯkhon, with sunken, emaciated face that bore the stamp of inconsolable grief, replied: вҖңYes, PrincessвҖқ to all Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs questions and hardly refrained from sobbing as he looked at her. At length Dron, the village Elder, entered the room and with a deep bow to Princess MГЎrya came to a halt by the doorpost. Princess MГЎrya walked up and down the room and stopped in front of him. вҖңDrГіnushka,вҖқ she said, regarding as a sure friend this DrГіnushka who always used to bring a special kind of gingerbread from his visit to the fair at VyГЎzma every year and smilingly offer it to her, вҖңDrГіnushka, now since our misfortuneвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she began, but could not go on. вҖңWe are all in GodвҖҷs hands,вҖқ said he, with a sigh. They were silent for a while. вҖңDrГіnushka, AlpГЎtych has gone off somewhere and I have no one to turn to. Is it true, as they tell me, that I canвҖҷt even go away?вҖқ вҖңWhy shouldnвҖҷt you go away, your excellency? You can go,вҖқ said Dron. вҖңI was told it would be dangerous because of the enemy. Dear friend, I can do nothing. I understand nothing. I have nobody! I want to go away tonight or early tomorrow morning.вҖқ Dron paused. He looked askance at Princess MГЎrya and said: вҖңThere are no horses; I told YГЎkov AlpГЎtych so.вҖқ вҖңWhy are there none?вҖқ asked the princess. вҖңItвҖҷs all GodвҖҷs scourge,вҖқ said Dron. вҖңWhat horses we had have been taken for the army or have diedвҒ вҖ”this is such a year! ItвҖҷs not a case of feeding horsesвҒ вҖ”we may die of hunger ourselves! As it is, some go three days without eating. WeвҖҷve nothing, weвҖҷve been ruined.вҖқ Princess MГЎrya listened attentively to what he told her. вҖңThe peasants are ruined? They have no bread?вҖқ she asked. вҖңTheyвҖҷre dying of hunger,вҖқ said Dron. вҖңItвҖҷs not a case of carting.вҖқ вҖңBut why didnвҖҷt you tell me, DrГіnushka? IsnвҖҷt it possible to help them? IвҖҷll do all I can.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ To Princess MГЎrya it was strange that now, at a moment when such sorrow was filling her soul, there could be rich people and poor, and the rich could refrain from helping the poor. She had heard vaguely that there was such a thing as вҖңlandlordвҖҷs cornвҖқ which was sometimes given to the peasants. She also knew that neither her father nor her brother would refuse to help the peasants in need, she only feared to make some mistake in speaking about the distribution of the grain she wished to give. She was glad such cares presented themselves, enabling her without scruple to forget her own grief. She began asking Dron about the peasantsвҖҷ needs and what there was in BoguchГЎrovo that belonged to the landlord. вҖңBut we have grain belonging to my brother?вҖқ she said. вҖңThe landlordвҖҷs grain is all safe,вҖқ replied Dron proudly. вҖңOur prince did not order it to be sold.вҖқ вҖңGive it to the peasants, let them have all they need; I give you leave in my brotherвҖҷs name,вҖқ said she. Dron made no answer but sighed deeply. вҖңGive them that corn if there is enough of it. Distribute it all. I give this order in my brotherвҖҷs name; and tell them that what is ours is theirs. We do not grudge them anything. Tell them so.вҖқ Dron looked intently at the princess while she was speaking. вҖңDischarge me, little mother, for GodвҖҷs sake! Order the keys to be taken from me,вҖқ said he. вҖңI have served twenty-three years and have done no wrong. Discharge me, for GodвҖҷs sake!вҖқ Princess MГЎrya did not understand what he wanted of her or why he was asking to be discharged. She replied that she had never doubted his devotion and that she was ready to do anything for him and for the peasants. XI XI An hour later DunyГЎsha came to tell the princess that Dron had come, and all the peasants had assembled at the barn by the princessвҖҷ order and wished to have word with their mistress. вҖңBut I never told them to come,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңI only told Dron to let them have the grain.вҖқ вҖңOnly, for GodвҖҷs sake, Princess dear, have them sent away and donвҖҷt go out to them. ItвҖҷs all a trick,вҖқ said DunyГЎsha, вҖңand when YГЎkov AlpГЎtych returns let us get awayвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and please donвҖҷtвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhat is a trick?вҖқ asked Princess MГЎrya in surprise. вҖңI know it is, only listen to me for GodвҖҷs sake! Ask nurse too. They say they donвҖҷt agree to leave BoguchГЎrovo as you ordered.вҖқ вҖңYouвҖҷre making some mistake. I never ordered them to go away,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңCall DrГіnushka.вҖқ Dron came and confirmed DunyГЎshaвҖҷs words; the peasants had come by the princessвҖҷ order. вҖңBut I never sent for them,вҖқ declared the princess. вҖңYou must have given my message wrong. I only said that you were to give them the grain.вҖқ Dron only sighed in reply. вҖңIf you order it they will go away,вҖқ said he. вҖңNo, no. IвҖҷll go out to them,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya, and in spite of the nurseвҖҷs and DunyГЎshaвҖҷs protests she went out into the porch; Dron, DunyГЎsha, the nurse, and MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich following her. вҖңThey probably think I am offering them the grain to bribe them to remain here, while I myself go away leaving them to the mercy of the French,вҖқ thought Princess MГЎrya. вҖңI will offer them monthly rations and housing at our Moscow estate. I am sure AndrГ© would do even more in my place,вҖқ she thought as she went out in the twilight toward the crowd standing on the pasture by the barn. The men crowded closer together, stirred, and rapidly took off their hats. Princess MГЎrya lowered her eyes and, tripping over her skirt, came close up to them. So many different eyes, old and young, were fixed on her, and there were so many different faces, that she could not distinguish any of them and, feeling that she must speak to them all at once, did not know how to do it. But again the sense that she represented her father and her brother gave her courage, and she boldly began her speech. вҖңI am very glad you have come,вҖқ she said without raising her eyes, and feeling her heart beating quickly and violently. вҖңDrГіnushka tells me that the war has ruined you. That is our common misfortune, and I shall grudge nothing to help you. I am myself going away because it is dangerous hereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the enemy is nearвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I am giving you everything, my friends, and I beg you to take everything, all our grain, so that you may not suffer want! And if you have been told that I am giving you the grain to keep you hereвҒ вҖ”that is not true. On the contrary, I ask you to go with all your belongings to our estate near Moscow, and I promise you I will see to it that there you shall want for nothing. You shall be given food and lodging.вҖқ The princess stopped. Sighs were the only sound heard in the crowd. вҖңI am not doing this on my own account,вҖқ she continued, вҖңI do it in the name of my dead father, who was a good master to you, and of my brother and his son.вҖқ Again she paused. No one broke the silence. вҖңOurs is a common misfortune and we will share it together. All that is mine is yours,вҖқ she concluded, scanning the faces before her. All eyes were gazing at her with one and the same expression. She could not fathom whether it was curiosity, devotion, gratitude, or apprehension and distrustвҒ вҖ”but the expression on all the faces was identical. вҖңWe are all very thankful for your bounty, but it wonвҖҷt do for us to take the landlordвҖҷs grain,вҖқ said a voice at the back of the crowd. вҖңBut why not?вҖқ asked the princess. No one replied and Princess MГЎrya, looking round at the crowd, found that every eye she met now was immediately dropped. вҖңBut why donвҖҷt you want to take it?вҖқ she asked again. No one answered. The silence began to oppress the princess and she tried to catch someoneвҖҷs eye. вҖңWhy donвҖҷt you speak?вҖқ she inquired of a very old man who stood just in front of her leaning on his stick. вҖңIf you think something more is wanted, tell me! I will do anything,вҖқ said she, catching his eye. But as if this angered him, he bent his head quite low and muttered: вҖңWhy should we agree? We donвҖҷt want the grain.вҖқ вҖңWhy should we give up everything? We donвҖҷt agree. DonвҖҷt agree.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ We are sorry for you, but weвҖҷre not willing. Go away yourself, aloneвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ came from various sides of the crowd. And again all the faces in that crowd bore an identical expression, though now it was certainly not an expression of curiosity or gratitude, but of angry resolve. вҖңBut you canвҖҷt have understood me,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya with a sad smile. вҖңWhy donвҖҷt you want to go? I promise to house and feed you, while here the enemy would ruin you.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ But her voice was drowned by the voices of the crowd. вҖңWeвҖҷre not willing. Let them ruin us! We wonвҖҷt take your grain. We donвҖҷt agree.вҖқ Again Princess MГЎrya tried to catch someoneвҖҷs eye, but not a single eye in the crowd was turned to her; evidently they were all trying to avoid her look. She felt strange and awkward. вҖңOh yes, an artful tale! Follow her into slavery! Pull down your houses and go into bondage! I dare say! вҖҳIвҖҷll give you grain, indeed!вҖҷ she says,вҖқ voices in the crowd were heard saying. With drooping head Princess MГЎrya left the crowd and went back to the house. Having repeated her order to Dron to have horses ready for her departure next morning, she went to her room and remained alone with her own thoughts. XII XII For a long time that night Princess MГЎrya sat by the open window of her room hearing the sound of the peasantsвҖҷ voices that reached her from the village, but it was not of them she was thinking. She felt that she could not understand them however much she might think about them. She thought only of one thing, her sorrow, which, after the break caused by cares for the present, seemed already to belong to the past. Now she could remember it and weep or pray. After sunset the wind had dropped. The night was calm and fresh. Toward midnight the voices began to subside, a cock crowed, the full moon began to show from behind the lime trees, a fresh white dewy mist began to rise, and stillness reigned over the village and the house. Pictures of the near pastвҒ вҖ”her fatherвҖҷs illness and last momentsвҒ вҖ”rose one after another to her memory. With mournful pleasure she now lingered over these images, repelling with horror only the last one, the picture of his death, which she felt she could not contemplate even in imagination at this still and mystic hour of night. And these pictures presented themselves to her so clearly and in such detail that they seemed now present, now past, and now future. She vividly recalled the moment when he had his first stroke and was being dragged along by his armpits through the garden at Bald Hills, muttering something with his helpless tongue, twitching his gray eyebrows and looking uneasily and timidly at her. вҖңEven then he wanted to tell me what he told me the day he died,вҖқ she thought. вҖңHe had always thought what he said then.вҖқ And she recalled in all its detail the night at Bald Hills before he had the last stroke, when with a foreboding of disaster she had remained at home against his will. She had not slept and had stolen downstairs on tiptoe, and going to the door of the conservatory where he slept that night had listened at the door. In a suffering and weary voice he was saying something to TГӯkhon, speaking of the Crimea and its warm nights and of the Empress. Evidently he had wanted to talk. вҖңAnd why didnвҖҷt he call me? Why didnвҖҷt he let me be there instead of TГӯkhon?вҖқ Princess MГЎrya had thought and thought again now. вҖңNow he will never tell anyone what he had in his soul. Never will that moment return for him or for me when he might have said all he longed to say, and not TГӯkhon but I might have heard and understood him. Why didnвҖҷt I enter the room?вҖқ she thought. вҖңPerhaps he would then have said to me what he said the day he died. While talking to TГӯkhon he asked about me twice. He wanted to see me, and I was standing close by, outside the door. It was sad and painful for him to talk to TГӯkhon who did not understand him. I remember how he began speaking to him about Liza as if she were aliveвҒ вҖ”he had forgotten she was deadвҒ вҖ”and TГӯkhon reminded him that she was no more, and he shouted, вҖҳFool!вҖҷ He was greatly depressed. From behind the door I heard how he lay down on his bed groaning and loudly exclaimed, вҖҳMy God!вҖҷ Why didnвҖҷt I go in then? What could he have done to me? What could I have lost? And perhaps he would then have been comforted and would have said that word to me.вҖқ And Princess MГЎrya uttered aloud the caressing word he had said to her on the day of his death. вҖңDearest!вҖқ she repeated, and began sobbing, with tears that relieved her soul. She now saw his face before her. And not the face she had known ever since she could remember and had always seen at a distance, but the timid, feeble face she had seen for the first time quite closely, with all its wrinkles and details, when she stooped near to his mouth to catch what he said. вҖңDearest!вҖқ she repeated again. вҖңWhat was he thinking when he uttered that word? What is he thinking now?вҖқ This question suddenly presented itself to her, and in answer she saw him before her with the expression that was on his face as he lay in his coffin with his chin bound up with a white handkerchief. And the horror that had seized her when she touched him and convinced herself that that was not he, but something mysterious and horrible, seized her again. She tried to think of something else and to pray, but could do neither. With wide-open eyes she gazed at the moonlight and the shadows, expecting every moment to see his dead face, and she felt that the silence brooding over the house and within it held her fast. вҖңDunyГЎsha,вҖқ she whispered. вҖңDunyГЎsha!вҖқ she screamed wildly, and tearing herself out of this silence she ran to the servantsвҖҷ quarters to meet her old nurse and the maidservants who came running toward her. XIII XIII On the seventeenth of August RostГіv and IlyГӯn, accompanied by LavrГәshka who had just returned from captivity and by an hussar orderly, left their quarters at YankГіvo, ten miles from BoguchГЎrovo, and went for a rideвҒ вҖ”to try a new horse IlyГӯn had bought and to find out whether there was any hay to be had in the villages. For the last three days BoguchГЎrovo had lain between the two hostile armies, so that it was as easy for the Russian rearguard to get to it as for the French vanguard; RostГіv, as a careful squadron commander, wished to take such provisions as remained at BoguchГЎrovo before the French could get them. RostГіv and IlyГӯn were in the merriest of moods. On the way to BoguchГЎrovo, a princely estate with a dwelling house and farm where they hoped to find many domestic serfs and pretty girls, they questioned LavrГәshka about Napoleon and laughed at his stories, and raced one another to try IlyГӯnвҖҷs horse. RostГіv had no idea that the village he was entering was the property of that very BolkГіnski who had been engaged to his sister. RostГіv and IlyГӯn gave rein to their horses for a last race along the incline before reaching BoguchГЎrovo, and RostГіv, outstripping IlyГӯn, was the first to gallop into the village street. вҖңYouвҖҷre first!вҖқ cried IlyГӯn, flushed. вҖңYes, always first both on the grassland and here,вҖқ answered RostГіv, stroking his heated DonГ©ts horse. вҖңAnd IвҖҷd have won on my Frenchy, your excellency,вҖқ said LavrГәshka from behind, alluding to his shabby cart horse, вҖңonly I didnвҖҷt wish to mortify you.вҖқ They rode at a footpace to the barn, where a large crowd of peasants was standing. Some of the men bared their heads, others stared at the new arrivals without doffing their caps. Two tall old peasants with wrinkled faces and scanty beards emerged from the tavern, smiling, staggering, and singing some incoherent song, and approached the officers. вҖңFine fellows!вҖқ said RostГіv laughing. вҖңIs there any hay here?вҖқ вҖңAnd how like one another,вҖқ said IlyГӯn. вҖңA mo-o-st me-r-r-y co-o-m-paвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ!вҖқ sang one of the peasants with a blissful smile. One of the men came out of the crowd and went up to RostГіv. вҖңWho do you belong to?вҖқ he asked. вҖңThe French,вҖқ replied IlyГӯn jestingly, вҖңand here is Napoleon himselfвҖқвҒ вҖ”and he pointed to LavrГәshka. вҖңThen you are Russians?вҖқ the peasant asked again. вҖңAnd is there a large force of you here?вҖқ said another, a short man, coming up. вҖңVery large,вҖқ answered RostГіv. вҖңBut why have you collected here?вҖқ he added. вҖңIs it a holiday?вҖқ вҖңThe old men have met to talk over the business of the commune,вҖқ replied the peasant, moving away. At that moment, on the road leading from the big house, two women and a man in a white hat were seen coming toward the officers. вҖңThe one in pink is mine, so keep off!вҖқ said IlyГӯn on seeing DunyГЎsha running resolutely toward him. вҖңSheвҖҷll be ours!вҖқ said LavrГәshka to IlyГӯn, winking. вҖңWhat do you want, my pretty?вҖқ said IlyГӯn with a smile. вҖңThe princess ordered me to ask your regiment and your name.вҖқ вҖңThis is Count RostГіv, squadron commander, and I am your humble servant.вҖқ вҖңCo-o-om-pa-ny!вҖқ roared the tipsy peasant with a beatific smile as he looked at IlyГӯn talking to the girl. Following DunyГЎsha, AlpГЎtych advanced to RostГіv, having bared his head while still at a distance. вҖңMay I make bold to trouble your honor?вҖқ said he respectfully, but with a shade of contempt for the youthfulness of this officer and with a hand thrust into his bosom. вҖңMy mistress, daughter of General in Chief Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich BolkГіnski who died on the fifteenth of this month, finding herself in difficulties owing to the boorishness of these peopleвҖқвҒ вҖ”he pointed to the peasantsвҒ вҖ”вҖңasks you to come up to the house.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ WonвҖҷt you, please, ride on a little farther,вҖқ said AlpГЎtych with a melancholy smile, вҖңas it is not convenient in the presence ofвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ?вҖқ He pointed to the two peasants who kept as close to him as horseflies to a horse. вҖңAh!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ AlpГЎtychвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Ah, YГЎkov AlpГЎtychвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Grand! Forgive us for ChristвҖҷs sake, eh?вҖқ said the peasants, smiling joyfully at him. RostГіv looked at the tipsy peasants and smiled. вҖңOr perhaps they amuse your honor?вҖқ remarked AlpГЎtych with a staid air, as he pointed at the old men with his free hand. вҖңNo, thereвҖҷs not much to be amused at here,вҖқ said RostГіv, and rode on a little way. вҖңWhatвҖҷs the matter?вҖқ he asked. вҖңI make bold to inform your honor that the rude peasants here donвҖҷt wish to let the mistress leave the estate, and threaten to unharness her horses, so that though everything has been packed up since morning, her excellency cannot get away.вҖқ вҖңImpossible!вҖқ exclaimed RostГіv. вҖңI have the honor to report to you the actual truth,вҖқ said AlpГЎtych. RostГіv dismounted, gave his horse to the orderly, and followed AlpГЎtych to the house, questioning him as to the state of affairs. It appeared that the princessвҖҷ offer of corn to the peasants the previous day, and her talk with Dron and at the meeting, had actually had so bad an effect that Dron had finally given up the keys and joined the peasants and had not appeared when AlpГЎtych sent for him; and that in the morning when the princess gave orders to harness for her journey, the peasants had come in a large crowd to the barn and sent word that they would not let her leave the village: that there was an order not to move, and that they would unharness the horses. AlpГЎtych had gone out to admonish them, but was told (it was chiefly Karp who did the talking, Dron not showing himself in the crowd) that they could not let the princess go, that there was an order to the contrary, but that if she stayed they would serve her as before and obey her in everything. At the moment when RostГіv and IlyГӯn were galloping along the road, Princess MГЎrya, despite the dissuasions of AlpГЎtych, her nurse, and the maids, had given orders to harness and intended to start, but when the cavalrymen were espied they were taken for Frenchmen, the coachman ran away, and the women in the house began to wail. вҖңFather! Benefactor! God has sent you!вҖқ exclaimed deeply moved voices as RostГіv passed through the anteroom. Princess MГЎrya was sitting helpless and bewildered in the large sitting room, when RostГіv was shown in. She could not grasp who he was and why he had come, or what was happening to her. When she saw his Russian face, and by his walk and the first words he uttered recognized him as a man of her own class, she glanced at him with her deep radiant look and began speaking in a voice that faltered and trembled with emotion. This meeting immediately struck RostГіv as a romantic event. вҖңA helpless girl overwhelmed with grief, left to the mercy of coarse, rioting peasants! And what a strange fate sent me here! What gentleness and nobility there are in her features and expression!вҖқ thought he as he looked at her and listened to her timid story. When she began to tell him that all this had happened the day after her fatherвҖҷs funeral, her voice trembled. She turned away, and then, as if fearing he might take her words as meant to move him to pity, looked at him with an apprehensive glance of inquiry. There were tears in RostГіvвҖҷs eyes. Princess MГЎrya noticed this and glanced gratefully at him with that radiant look which caused the plainness of her face to be forgotten. вҖңI cannot express, Princess, how glad I am that I happened to ride here and am able to show my readiness to serve you,вҖқ said RostГіv, rising. вҖңGo when you please, and I give you my word of honor that no one shall dare to cause you annoyance if only you will allow me to act as your escort.вҖқ And bowing respectfully, as if to a lady of royal blood, he moved toward the door. RostГіvвҖҷs deferential tone seemed to indicate that though he would consider himself happy to be acquainted with her, he did not wish to take advantage of her misfortunes to intrude upon her. Princess MГЎrya understood this and appreciated his delicacy. вҖңI am very, very grateful to you,вҖқ she said in French, вҖңbut I hope it was all a misunderstanding and that no one is to blame for it.вҖқ She suddenly began to cry. вҖңExcuse me!вҖқ she said. RostГіv, knitting his brows, left the room with another low bow. XIV XIV вҖңWell, is she pretty? Ah, friendвҒ вҖ”my pink one is delicious; her name is DunyГЎsha.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ But on glancing at RostГіvвҖҷs face IlyГӯn stopped short. He saw that his hero and commander was following quite a different train of thought. RostГіv glanced angrily at IlyГӯn and without replying strode off with rapid steps to the village. вҖңIвҖҷll show them; IвҖҷll give it to them, the brigands!вҖқ said he to himself. AlpГЎtych at a gliding trot, only just managing not to run, kept up with him with difficulty. вҖңWhat decision have you been pleased to come to?вҖқ said he. RostГіv stopped and, clenching his fists, suddenly and sternly turned on AlpГЎtych. вҖңDecision? What decision? Old dotard!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ cried he. вҖңWhat have you been about? Eh? The peasants are rioting, and you canвҖҷt manage them? YouвҖҷre a traitor yourself! I know you. IвҖҷll flay you all alive!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ And as if afraid of wasting his store of anger, he left AlpГЎtych and went rapidly forward. AlpГЎtych, mastering his offended feelings, kept pace with RostГіv at a gliding gait and continued to impart his views. He said the peasants were obdurate and that at the present moment it would be imprudent to вҖңoverresistвҖқ them without an armed force, and would it not be better first to send for the military? вҖңIвҖҷll give them armed forceвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ IвҖҷll вҖҳoverresistвҖҷ them!вҖқ uttered RostГіv meaninglessly, breathless with irrational animal fury and the need to vent it. Without considering what he would do he moved unconciously with quick, resolute steps toward the crowd. And the nearer he drew to it the more AlpГЎtych felt that this unreasonable action might produce good results. The peasants in the crowd were similarly impressed when they saw RostГіvвҖҷs rapid, firm steps and resolute, frowning face. After the hussars had come to the village and RostГіv had gone to see the princess, a certain confusion and dissension had arisen among the crowd. Some of the peasants said that these new arrivals were Russians and might take it amiss that the mistress was being detained. Dron was of this opinion, but as soon as he expressed it Karp and others attacked their ex-Elder. вҖңHow many years have you been fattening on the commune?вҖқ Karp shouted at him. вҖңItвҖҷs all one to you! YouвҖҷll dig up your pot of money and take it away with you.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What does it matter to you whether our homes are ruined or not?вҖқ вҖңWeвҖҷve been told to keep order, and that no one is to leave their homes or take away a single grain, and thatвҖҷs all about it!вҖқ cried another. вҖңIt was your sonвҖҷs turn to be conscripted, but no fear! You begrudged your lump of a son,вҖқ a little old man suddenly began attacking DronвҒ вҖ”вҖңand so they took my VГЎnka to be shaved for a soldier! But we all have to die.вҖқ вҖңTo be sure, we all have to die. IвҖҷm not against the commune,вҖқ said Dron. вҖңThatвҖҷs itвҒ вҖ”not against it! YouвҖҷve filled your belly.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The two tall peasants had their say. As soon as RostГіv, followed by IlyГӯn, LavrГәshka, and AlpГЎtych, came up to the crowd, Karp, thrusting his fingers into his belt and smiling a little, walked to the front. Dron on the contrary retired to the rear and the crowd drew closer together. вҖңWho is your Elder here? Hey?вҖқ shouted RostГіv, coming up to the crowd with quick steps. вҖңThe Elder? What do you want with him?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ asked Karp. But before the words were well out of his mouth, his cap flew off and a fierce blow jerked his head to one side. вҖңCaps off, traitors!вҖқ shouted RostГіv in a wrathful voice. вҖңWhereвҖҷs the Elder?вҖқ he cried furiously. вҖңThe Elder.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ He wants the Elder!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Dron ZakhГЎrych, you!вҖқ meek and flustered voices here and there were heard calling and caps began to come off their heads. вҖңWe donвҖҷt riot, weвҖҷre following the orders,вҖқ declared Karp, and at that moment several voices began speaking together. вҖңItвҖҷs as the old men have decidedвҒ вҖ”thereвҖҷs too many of you giving orders.вҖқ вҖңArguing? Mutiny!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Brigands! Traitors!вҖқ cried RostГіv unmeaningly in a voice not his own, gripping Karp by the collar. вҖңBind him, bind him!вҖқ he shouted, though there was no one to bind him but LavrГәshka and AlpГЎtych. LavrГәshka, however, ran up to Karp and seized him by the arms from behind. вҖңShall I call up our men from beyond the hill?вҖқ he called out. AlpГЎtych turned to the peasants and ordered two of them by name to come and bind Karp. The men obediently came out of the crowd and began taking off their belts. вҖңWhereвҖҷs the Elder?вҖқ demanded RostГіv in a loud voice. With a pale and frowning face Dron stepped out of the crowd. вҖңAre you the Elder? Bind him, LavrГәshka!вҖқ shouted RostГіv, as if that order, too, could not possibly meet with any opposition. And in fact two more peasants began binding Dron, who took off his own belt and handed it to them, as if to aid them. вҖңAnd you all listen to me!вҖқ said RostГіv to the peasants. вҖңBe off to your houses at once, and donвҖҷt let one of your voices be heard!вҖқ вҖңWhy, weвҖҷve not done any harm! We did it just out of foolishness. ItвҖҷs all nonsense.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I said then that it was not in order,вҖқ voices were heard bickering with one another. вҖңThere! What did I say?вҖқ said AlpГЎtych, coming into his own again. вҖңItвҖҷs wrong, lads!вҖқ вҖңAll our stupidity, YГЎkov AlpГЎtych,вҖқ came the answers, and the crowd began at once to disperse through the village. The two bound men were led off to the masterвҖҷs house. The two drunken peasants followed them. вҖңAye, when I look at you!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said one of them to Karp. вҖңHow can one talk to the masters like that? What were you thinking of, you fool?вҖқ added the otherвҒ вҖ”вҖңA real fool!вҖқ Two hours later the carts were standing in the courtyard of the BoguchГЎrovo house. The peasants were briskly carrying out the proprietorвҖҷs goods and packing them on the carts, and Dron, liberated at Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs wish from the cupboard where he had been confined, was standing in the yard directing the men. вҖңDonвҖҷt put it in so carelessly,вҖқ said one of the peasants, a man with a round smiling face, taking a casket from a housemaid. вҖңYou know it has cost money! How can you chuck it in like that or shove it under the cord where itвҖҷll get rubbed? I donвҖҷt like that way of doing things. Let it all be done properly, according to rule. Look here, put it under the bast matting and cover it with hayвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs the way!вҖқ вҖңEh, books, books!вҖқ said another peasant, bringing out Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs library cupboards. вҖңDonвҖҷt catch up against it! ItвҖҷs heavy, ladsвҒ вҖ”solid books.вҖқ вҖңYes, they worked all day and didnвҖҷt play!вҖқ remarked the tall, round-faced peasant gravely, pointing with a significant wink at the dictionaries that were on the top. Unwilling to obtrude himself on the princess, RostГіv did not go back to the house but remained in the village awaiting her departure. When her carriage drove out of the house, he mounted and accompanied her eight miles from BoguchГЎrovo to where the road was occupied by our troops. At the inn at YankГіvo he respectfully took leave of her, for the first time permitting himself to kiss her hand. вҖңHow can you speak so!вҖқ he blushingly replied to Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs expressions of gratitude for her deliverance, as she termed what had occurred. вҖңAny police officer would have done as much! If we had had only peasants to fight, we should not have let the enemy come so far,вҖқ said he with a sense of shame and wishing to change the subject. вҖңI am only happy to have had the opportunity of making your acquaintance. Goodbye, Princess. I wish you happiness and consolation and hope to meet you again in happier circumstances. If you donвҖҷt want to make me blush, please donвҖҷt thank me!вҖқ But the princess, if she did not again thank him in words, thanked him with the whole expression of her face, radiant with gratitude and tenderness. She could not believe that there was nothing to thank him for. On the contrary, it seemed to her certain that had he not been there she would have perished at the hands of the mutineers and of the French, and that he had exposed himself to terrible and obvious danger to save her, and even more certain was it that he was a man of lofty and noble soul, able to understand her position and her sorrow. His kind, honest eyes, with the tears rising in them when she herself had begun to cry as she spoke of her loss, did not leave her memory. When she had taken leave of him and remained alone she suddenly felt her eyes filling with tears, and then not for the first time the strange question presented itself to her: did she love him? On the rest of the way to Moscow, though the princessвҖҷ position was not a cheerful one, DunyГЎsha, who went with her in the carriage, more than once noticed that her mistress leaned out of the window and smiled at something with an expression of mingled joy and sorrow. вҖңWell, supposing I do love him?вҖқ thought Princess MГЎrya. Ashamed as she was of acknowledging to herself that she had fallen in love with a man who would perhaps never love her, she comforted herself with the thought that no one would ever know it and that she would not be to blame if, without ever speaking of it to anyone, she continued to the end of her life to love the man with whom she had fallen in love for the first and last time in her life. Sometimes when she recalled his looks, his sympathy, and his words, happiness did not appear impossible to her. It was at those moments that DunyГЎsha noticed her smiling as she looked out of the carriage window. вҖңWas it not fate that brought him to BoguchГЎrovo, and at that very moment?вҖқ thought Princess MГЎrya. вҖңAnd that caused his sister to refuse my brother?вҖқ And in all this Princess MГЎrya saw the hand of Providence. The impression the princess made on RostГіv was a very agreeable one. To remember her gave him pleasure, and when his comrades, hearing of his adventure at BoguchГЎrovo, rallied him on having gone to look for hay and having picked up one of the wealthiest heiresses in Russia, he grew angry. It made him angry just because the idea of marrying the gentle Princess MГЎrya, who was attractive to him and had an enormous fortune, had against his will more than once entered his head. For himself personally NikolГЎy could not wish for a better wife: by marrying her he would make the countess his mother happy, would be able to put his fatherвҖҷs affairs in order, and would evenвҒ вҖ”he felt itвҒ вҖ”ensure Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs happiness. But SГіnya? And his plighted word? That was why RostГіv grew angry when he was rallied about Princess BolkГіnskaya. XV XV On receiving command of the armies KutГәzov remembered Prince AndrГ©y and sent an order for him to report at headquarters. Prince AndrГ©y arrived at TsГЎrevo-ZaymГӯshche on the very day and at the very hour that KutГәzov was reviewing the troops for the first time. He stopped in the village at the priestвҖҷs house in front of which stood the commander in chiefвҖҷs carriage, and he sat down on the bench at the gate awaiting his Serene Highness, as everyone now called KutГәzov. From the field beyond the village came now sounds of regimental music and now the roar of many voices shouting вҖңHurrah!вҖқ to the new commander in chief. Two orderlies, a courier and a majordomo, stood nearby, some ten paces from Prince AndrГ©y, availing themselves of KutГәzovвҖҷs absence and of the fine weather. A short, swarthy lieutenant colonel of hussars with thick mustaches and whiskers rode up to the gate and, glancing at Prince AndrГ©y, inquired whether his Serene Highness was putting up there and whether he would soon be back. Prince AndrГ©y replied that he was not on his Serene HighnessвҖҷ staff but was himself a new arrival. The lieutenant colonel turned to a smart orderly, who, with the peculiar contempt with which a commander in chiefвҖҷs orderly speaks to officers, replied: вҖңWhat? His Serene Highness? I expect heвҖҷll be here soon. What do you want?вҖқ The lieutenant colonel of hussars smiled beneath his mustache at the orderlyвҖҷs tone, dismounted, gave his horse to a dispatch runner, and approached BolkГіnski with a slight bow. BolkГіnski made room for him on the bench and the lieutenant colonel sat down beside him. вҖңYouвҖҷre also waiting for the commander in chief?вҖқ said he. вҖңThey say he weceives evewyone, thank God!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ItвҖҷs awful with those sausage eaters! ErmГіlov had weason to ask to be pwomoted to be a German! Now pвҖҷwaps Wussians will get a look in. As it was, devil only knows what was happening. We kept wetweating and wetweating. Did you take part in the campaign?вҖқ he asked. вҖңI had the pleasure,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңnot only of taking part in the retreat but of losing in that retreat all I held dearвҒ вҖ”not to mention the estate and home of my birthвҒ вҖ”my father, who died of grief. I belong to the province of SmolГ©nsk.вҖқ вҖңAh? YouвҖҷre Pwince BolkГіnski? Vewy glad to make your acquaintance! IвҖҷm Lieutenant Colonel DenГӯsov, better known as вҖҳVГЎska,вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ said DenГӯsov, pressing Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs hand and looking into his face with a particularly kindly attention. вҖңYes, I heard,вҖқ said he sympathetically, and after a short pause added: вҖңYes, itвҖҷs Scythian warfare. ItвҖҷs all vewy wellвҒ вҖ”only not for those who get it in the neck. So you are Pwince Andwew BolkГіnski?вҖқ He swayed his head. вҖңVewy pleased, Pwince, to make your acquaintance!вҖқ he repeated again, smiling sadly, and he again pressed Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs hand. Prince AndrГ©y knew DenГӯsov from what NatГЎsha had told him of her first suitor. This memory carried him sadly and sweetly back to those painful feelings of which he had not thought lately, but which still found place in his soul. Of late he had received so many new and very serious impressionsвҒ вҖ”such as the retreat from SmolГ©nsk, his visit to Bald Hills, and the recent news of his fatherвҖҷs deathвҒ вҖ”and had experienced so many emotions, that for a long time past those memories had not entered his mind, and now that they did, they did not act on him with nearly their former strength. For DenГӯsov, too, the memories awakened by the name of BolkГіnski belonged to a distant, romantic past, when after supper and after NatГЎshaвҖҷs singing he had proposed to a little girl of fifteen without realizing what he was doing. He smiled at the recollection of that time and of his love for NatГЎsha, and passed at once to what now interested him passionately and exclusively. This was a plan of campaign he had devised while serving at the outposts during the retreat. He had proposed that plan to Barclay de Tolly and now wished to propose it to KutГәzov. The plan was based on the fact that the French line of operation was too extended, and it proposed that instead of, or concurrently with, action on the front to bar the advance of the French, we should attack their line of communication. He began explaining his plan to Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңThey canвҖҷt hold all that line. ItвҖҷs impossible. I will undertake to bweak thwough. Give me five hundwed men and I will bweak the line, thatвҖҷs certain! ThereвҖҷs only one wayвҒ вҖ”guewilla warfare!вҖқ DenГӯsov rose and began gesticulating as he explained his plan to BolkГіnski. In the midst of his explanation shouts were heard from the army, growing more incoherent and more diffused, mingling with music and songs and coming from the field where the review was held. Sounds of hoofs and shouts were nearing the village. вҖңHeвҖҷs coming! HeвҖҷs coming!вҖқ shouted a Cossack standing at the gate. BolkГіnski and DenГӯsov moved to the gate, at which a knot of soldiers (a guard of honor) was standing, and they saw KutГәzov coming down the street mounted on a rather small sorrel horse. A huge suite of generals rode behind him. Barclay was riding almost beside him, and a crowd of officers ran after and around them shouting, вҖңHurrah!вҖқ His adjutants galloped into the yard before him. KutГәzov was impatiently urging on his horse, which ambled smoothly under his weight, and he raised his hand to his white Horse GuardвҖҷs cap with a red band and no peak, nodding his head continually. When he came up to the guard of honor, a fine set of Grenadiers mostly wearing decorations, who were giving him the salute, he looked at them silently and attentively for nearly a minute with the steady gaze of a commander and then turned to the crowd of generals and officers surrounding him. Suddenly his face assumed a subtle expression, he shrugged his shoulders with an air of perplexity. вҖңAnd with such fine fellows to retreat and retreat! Well, goodbye, General,вҖқ he added, and rode into the yard past Prince AndrГ©y and DenГӯsov. вҖңHurrah! hurrah! hurrah!вҖқ shouted those behind him. Since Prince AndrГ©y had last seen him KutГәzov had grown still more corpulent, flaccid, and fat. But the bleached eyeball, the scar, and the familiar weariness of his expression were still the same. He was wearing the white Horse GuardвҖҷs cap and a military overcoat with a whip hanging over his shoulder by a thin strap. He sat heavily and swayed limply on his brisk little horse. вҖңWhewвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ whewвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ whew!вҖқ he whistled just audibly as he rode into the yard. His face expressed the relief of relaxed strain felt by a man who means to rest after a ceremony. He drew his left foot out of the stirrup and, lurching with his whole body and puckering his face with the effort, raised it with difficulty onto the saddle, leaned on his knee, groaned, and slipped down into the arms of the Cossacks and adjutants who stood ready to assist him. He pulled himself together, looked round, screwing up his eyes, glanced at Prince AndrГ©y, and, evidently not recognizing him, moved with his waddling gait to the porch. вҖңWhewвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ whewвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ whew!вҖқ he whistled, and again glanced at Prince AndrГ©y. As often occurs with old men, it was only after some seconds that the impression produced by Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs face linked itself up with KutГәzovвҖҷs remembrance of his personality. вҖңAh, how do you do, my dear prince? How do you do, my dear boy? Come alongвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said he, glancing wearily round, and he stepped onto the porch which creaked under his weight. He unbuttoned his coat and sat down on a bench in the porch. вҖңAnd howвҖҷs your father?вҖқ вҖңI received news of his death, yesterday,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y abruptly. KutГәzov looked at him with eyes wide open with dismay and then took off his cap and crossed himself: вҖңMay the kingdom of Heaven be his! GodвҖҷs will be done to us all!вҖқ He sighed deeply, his whole chest heaving, and was silent for a while. вҖңI loved him and respected him, and sympathize with you with all my heart.вҖқ He embraced Prince AndrГ©y, pressing him to his fat breast, and for some time did not let him go. When he released him Prince AndrГ©y saw that KutГәzovвҖҷs flabby lips were trembling and that tears were in his eyes. He sighed and pressed on the bench with both hands to raise himself. вҖңCome! Come with me, weвҖҷll have a talk,вҖқ said he. But at that moment DenГӯsov, no more intimidated by his superiors than by the enemy, came with jingling spurs up the steps of the porch, despite the angry whispers of the adjutants who tried to stop him. KutГәzov, his hands still pressed on the seat, glanced at him glumly. DenГӯsov, having given his name, announced that he had to communicate to his Serene Highness a matter of great importance for their countryвҖҷs welfare. KutГәzov looked wearily at him and, lifting his hands with a gesture of annoyance, folded them across his stomach, repeating the words: вҖңFor our countryвҖҷs welfare? Well, what is it? Speak!вҖқ DenГӯsov blushed like a girl (it was strange to see the color rise in that shaggy, bibulous, timeworn face) and boldly began to expound his plan of cutting the enemyвҖҷs lines of communication between SmolГ©nsk and VyГЎzma. DenГӯsov came from those parts and knew the country well. His plan seemed decidedly a good one, especially from the strength of conviction with which he spoke. KutГәzov looked down at his own legs, occasionally glancing at the door of the adjoining hut as if expecting something unpleasant to emerge from it. And from that hut, while DenГӯsov was speaking, a general with a portfolio under his arm really did appear. вҖңWhat?вҖқ said KutГәzov, in the midst of DenГӯsovвҖҷs explanations, вҖңare you ready so soon?вҖқ вҖңReady, your Serene Highness,вҖқ replied the general. KutГәzov swayed his head, as much as to say: вҖңHow is one man to deal with it all?вҖқ and again listened to DenГӯsov. вҖңI give my word of honor as a Wussian officer,вҖқ said DenГӯsov, вҖңthat I can bweak NapoleonвҖҷs line of communication!вҖқ вҖңWhat relation are you to Intendant General KirГӯl AndrГ©evich DenГӯsov?вҖқ asked KutГәzov, interrupting him. вҖңHe is my uncle, your Sewene Highness.вҖқ вҖңAh, we were friends,вҖқ said KutГәzov cheerfully. вҖңAll right, all right, friend, stay here at the staff and tomorrow weвҖҷll have a talk.вҖқ With a nod to DenГӯsov he turned away and put out his hand for the papers KonovnГӯtsyn had brought him. вҖңWould not your Serene Highness like to come inside?вҖқ said the general on duty in a discontented voice, вҖңthe plans must be examined and several papers have to be signed.вҖқ An adjutant came out and announced that everything was in readiness within. But KutГәzov evidently did not wish to enter that room till he was disengaged. He made a grimace.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңNo, tell them to bring a small table out here, my dear boy. IвҖҷll look at them here,вҖқ said he. вҖңDonвҖҷt go away,вҖқ he added, turning to Prince AndrГ©y, who remained in the porch and listened to the generalвҖҷs report. While this was being given, Prince AndrГ©y heard the whisper of a womanвҖҷs voice and the rustle of a silk dress behind the door. Several times on glancing that way he noticed behind that door a plump, rosy, handsome woman in a pink dress with a lilac silk kerchief on her head, holding a dish and evidently awaiting the entrance of the commander in chief. KutГәzovвҖҷs adjutant whispered to Prince AndrГ©y that this was the wife of the priest whose home it was, and that she intended to offer his Serene Highness bread and salt. вҖңHer husband has welcomed his Serene Highness with the cross at the church, and she intends to welcome him in the house.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ SheвҖҷs very pretty,вҖқ added the adjutant with a smile. At those words KutГәzov looked round. He was listening to the generalвҖҷs reportвҒ вҖ”which consisted chiefly of a criticism of the position at TsГЎrevo-ZaymГӯshcheвҒ вҖ”as he had listened to DenГӯsov, and seven years previously had listened to the discussion at the Austerlitz council of war. He evidently listened only because he had ears which, though there was a piece of tow in one of them, could not help hearing; but it was evident that nothing the general could say would surprise or even interest him, that he knew all that would be said beforehand, and heard it all only because he had to, as one has to listen to the chanting of a service of prayer. All that DenГӯsov had said was clever and to the point. What the general was saying was even more clever and to the point, but it was evident that KutГәzov despised knowledge and cleverness, and knew of something else that would decide the matterвҒ вҖ”something independent of cleverness and knowledge. Prince AndrГ©y watched the commander in chiefвҖҷs face attentively, and the only expression he could see there was one of boredom, curiosity as to the meaning of the feminine whispering behind the door, and a desire to observe propriety. It was evident that KutГәzov despised cleverness and learning and even the patriotic feeling shown by DenГӯsov, but despised them not because of his own intellect, feelings, or knowledgeвҒ вҖ”he did not try to display any of theseвҒ вҖ”but because of something else. He despised them because of his old age and experience of life. The only instruction KutГәzov gave of his own accord during that report referred to looting by the Russian troops. At the end of the report the general put before him for signature a paper relating to the recovery of payment from army commanders for green oats mown down by the soldiers, when landowners lodged petitions for compensation. After hearing the matter, KutГәzov smacked his lips together and shook his head. вҖңInto the stoveвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ into the fire with it! I tell you once for all, my dear fellow,вҖқ said he, вҖңinto the fire with all such things! Let them cut the crops and burn wood to their heartsвҖҷ content. I donвҖҷt order it or allow it, but I donвҖҷt exact compensation either. One canвҖҷt get on without it. вҖҳWhen wood is chopped the chips will fly.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ He looked at the paper again. вҖңOh, this German precision!вҖқ he muttered, shaking his head. XVI XVI вҖңWell, thatвҖҷs all!вҖқ said KutГәzov as he signed the last of the documents, and rising heavily and smoothing out the folds in his fat white neck he moved toward the door with a more cheerful expression. The priestвҖҷs wife, flushing rosy red, caught up the dish she had after all not managed to present at the right moment, though she had so long been preparing for it, and with a low bow offered it to KutГәzov. He screwed up his eyes, smiled, lifted her chin with his hand, and said: вҖңAh, what a beauty! Thank you, sweetheart!вҖқ He took some gold pieces from his trouser pocket and put them on the dish for her. вҖңWell, my dear, and how are we getting on?вҖқ he asked, moving to the door of the room assigned to him. The priestвҖҷs wife smiled, and with dimples in her rosy cheeks followed him into the room. The adjutant came out to the porch and asked Prince AndrГ©y to lunch with him. Half an hour later Prince AndrГ©y was again called to KutГәzov. He found him reclining in an armchair, still in the same unbuttoned overcoat. He had in his hand a French book which he closed as Prince AndrГ©y entered, marking the place with a knife. Prince AndrГ©y saw by the cover that it was Les Chevaliers du Cygne by Madame de Genlis. вҖңWell, sit down, sit down here. LetвҖҷs have a talk,вҖқ said KutГәzov. вҖңItвҖҷs sad, very sad. But remember, my dear fellow, that I am a father to you, a second father.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Prince AndrГ©y told KutГәzov all he knew of his fatherвҖҷs death, and what he had seen at Bald Hills when he passed through it. вҖңWhatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ what they have brought us to!вҖқ KutГәzov suddenly cried in an agitated voice, evidently picturing vividly to himself from Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs story the condition Russia was in. вҖңBut give me time, give me time!вҖқ he said with a grim look, evidently not wishing to continue this agitating conversation, and added: вҖңI sent for you to keep you with me.вҖқ вҖңI thank your Serene Highness, but I fear I am no longer fit for the staff,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y with a smile which KutГәzov noticed. KutГәzov glanced inquiringly at him. вҖңBut above all,вҖқ added Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңI have grown used to my regiment, am fond of the officers, and I fancy the men also like me. I should be sorry to leave the regiment. If I decline the honor of being with you, believe meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ A shrewd, kindly, yet subtly derisive expression lit up KutГәzovвҖҷs podgy face. He cut BolkГіnski short. вҖңI am sorry, for I need you. But youвҖҷre right, youвҖҷre right! ItвҖҷs not here that men are needed. Advisers are always plentiful, but men are not. The regiments would not be what they are if the would-be advisers served there as you do. I remember you at Austerlitz.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I remember, yes, I remember you with the standard!вҖқ said KutГәzov, and a flush of pleasure suffused Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs face at this recollection. Taking his hand and drawing him downwards, KutГәzov offered his cheek to be kissed, and again Prince AndrГ©y noticed tears in the old manвҖҷs eyes. Though Prince AndrГ©y knew that KutГәzovвҖҷs tears came easily, and that he was particularly tender to and considerate of him from a wish to show sympathy with his loss, yet this reminder of Austerlitz was both pleasant and flattering to him. вҖңGo your way and God be with you. I know your path is the path of honor!вҖқ He paused. вҖңI missed you at Bucharest, but I needed someone to send.вҖқ And changing the subject, KutГәzov began to speak of the Turkish war and the peace that had been concluded. вҖңYes, I have been much blamed,вҖқ he said, вҖңboth for that war and the peaceвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but everything came at the right time. Tout vient Г  point Г  celui qui sait attendre.84 And there were as many advisers there as hereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he went on, returning to the subject of вҖңadvisersвҖқ which evidently occupied him. вҖңAh, those advisers!вҖқ said he. вҖңIf we had listened to them all we should not have made peace with Turkey and should not have been through with that war. Everything in haste, but more haste, less speed. KГЎmenski would have been lost if he had not died. He stormed fortresses with thirty thousand men. It is not difficult to capture a fortress but it is difficult to win a campaign. For that, not storming and attacking but patience and time are wanted. KГЎmenski sent soldiers to Rustchuk, but I only employed these two things and took more fortresses than KГЎmenski and made them Turks eat horseflesh!вҖқ He swayed his head. вҖңAnd the French shall too, believe me,вҖқ he went on, growing warmer and beating his chest, вҖңIвҖҷll make them eat horseflesh!вҖқ And tears again dimmed his eyes. вҖңBut shanвҖҷt we have to accept battle?вҖқ remarked Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңWe shall if everybody wants it; it canвҖҷt be helped.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But believe me, my dear boy, there is nothing stronger than those two: patience and time, they will do it all. But the advisers nвҖҷentendent pas de cette oreille, voilГ  le mal.85 Some want a thingвҒ вҖ”others donвҖҷt. WhatвҖҷs one to do?вҖқ he asked, evidently expecting an answer. вҖңWell, what do you want us to do?вҖқ he repeated and his eye shone with a deep, shrewd look. вҖңIвҖҷll tell you what to do,вҖқ he continued, as Prince AndrГ©y still did not reply: вҖңI will tell you what to do, and what I do. Dans le doute, mon cher,вҖқ he paused, вҖңabstiens-toiвҖқ86вҒ вҖ”he articulated the French proverb deliberately. вҖңWell, goodbye, my dear fellow; remember that with all my heart I share your sorrow, and that for you I am not a Serene Highness, nor a prince, nor a commander in chief, but a father! If you want anything come straight to me. Goodbye, my dear boy.вҖқ Again he embraced and kissed Prince AndrГ©y, but before the latter had left the room KutГәzov gave a sigh of relief and went on with his unfinished novel, Les Chevaliers du Cygne by Madame de Genlis. Prince AndrГ©y could not have explained how or why it was, but after that interview with KutГәzov he went back to his regiment reassured as to the general course of affairs and as to the man to whom it had been entrusted. The more he realized the absence of all personal motive in that old manвҒ вҖ”in whom there seemed to remain only the habit of passions, and in place of an intellect (grouping events and drawing conclusions) only the capacity calmly to contemplate the course of eventsвҒ вҖ”the more reassured he was that everything would be as it should. вҖңHe will not bring in any plan of his own. He will not devise or undertake anything,вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңbut he will hear everything, remember everything, and put everything in its place. He will not hinder anything useful nor allow anything harmful. He understands that there is something stronger and more important than his own willвҒ вҖ”the inevitable course of events, and he can see them and grasp their significance, and seeing that significance can refrain from meddling and renounce his personal wish directed to something else. And above all,вҖқ thought Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңone believes in him because heвҖҷs Russian, despite the novel by Genlis and the French proverbs, and because his voice shook when he said: вҖҳWhat they have brought us to!вҖҷ and had a sob in it when he said he would вҖҳmake them eat horseflesh!вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ On such feelings, more or less dimly shared by all, the unanimity and general approval were founded with which, despite court influences, the popular choice of KutГәzov as commander in chief was received. XVII XVII After the Emperor had left Moscow, life flowed on there in its usual course, and its course was so very usual that it was difficult to remember the recent days of patriotic elation and ardor, hard to believe that Russia was really in danger and that the members of the English Club were also sons of the Fatherland ready to sacrifice everything for it. The one thing that recalled the patriotic fervor everyone had displayed during the EmperorвҖҷs stay was the call for contributions of men and money, a necessity that as soon as the promises had been made assumed a legal, official form and became unavoidable. With the enemyвҖҷs approach to Moscow, the MoscovitesвҖҷ view of their situation did not grow more serious but on the contrary became even more frivolous, as always happens with people who see a great danger approaching. At the approach of danger there are always two voices that speak with equal power in the human soul: one very reasonably tells a man to consider the nature of the danger and the means of escaping it; the other, still more reasonably, says that it is too depressing and painful to think of the danger, since it is not in manвҖҷs power to foresee everything and avert the general course of events, and it is therefore better to disregard what is painful till it comes, and to think about what is pleasant. In solitude a man generally listens to the first voice, but in society to the second. So it was now with the inhabitants of Moscow. It was long since people had been as gay in Moscow as that year. RostopchГӯnвҖҷs broadsheets, headed by woodcuts of a drink shop, a potman, and a Moscow burgher called KarpГәshka ChigГӯrin, вҖңwhoвҒ вҖ”having been a militiaman and having had rather too much at the pubвҒ вҖ”heard that Napoleon wished to come to Moscow, grew angry, abused the French in very bad language, came out of the drink shop, and, under the sign of the eagle, began to address the assembled people,вҖқ were read and discussed, together with the latest of VasГӯli LvГіvich PГәshkinвҖҷs bouts rimГ©s. In the corner room at the Club, members gathered to read these broadsheets, and some liked the way KarpГәshka jeered at the French, saying: вҖңThey will swell up with Russian cabbage, burst with our buckwheat porridge, and choke themselves with cabbage soup. They are all dwarfs and one peasant woman will toss three of them with a hayfork.вҖқ Others did not like that tone and said it was stupid and vulgar. It was said that RostopchГӯn had expelled all Frenchmen and even all foreigners from Moscow, and that there had been some spies and agents of Napoleon among them; but this was told chiefly to introduce RostopchГӯnвҖҷs witty remark on that occasion. The foreigners were deported to NГӯzhni by boat, and RostopchГӯn had said to them in French: вҖңRentrez en vous-mГӘmes; entrez dans la barque, et nвҖҷen faites pas une barque de Charon.вҖқ87 There was talk of all the government offices having been already removed from Moscow, and to this ShinshГӯnвҖҷs witticism was addedвҒ вҖ”that for that alone Moscow ought to be grateful to Napoleon. It was said that MamГіnovвҖҷs regiment would cost him eight hundred thousand rubles, and that BezГәkhov had spent even more on his, but that the best thing about BezГәkhovвҖҷs action was that he himself was going to don a uniform and ride at the head of his regiment without charging anything for the show. вҖңYou donвҖҷt spare anyone,вҖқ said Julie DrubetskГЎya as she collected and pressed together a bunch of raveled lint with her thin, beringed fingers. Julie was preparing to leave Moscow next day and was giving a farewell soiree. вҖңBezГәkhov est ridicule, but he is so kind and good-natured. What pleasure is there to be so caustique?вҖқ вҖңA forfeit!вҖқ cried a young man in militia uniform whom Julie called вҖңmon chevalier,вҖқ and who was going with her to NГӯzhni. In JulieвҖҷs set, as in many other circles in Moscow, it had been agreed that they would speak nothing but Russian and that those who made a slip and spoke French should pay fines to the Committee of Voluntary Contributions. вҖңAnother forfeit for a Gallicism,вҖқ said a Russian writer who was present. вҖңвҖҠвҖҳWhat pleasure is there to beвҖҷ is not Russian!вҖқ вҖңYou spare no one,вҖқ continued Julie to the young man without heeding the authorвҖҷs remark. вҖңFor caustiqueвҒ вҖ”I am guilty and will pay, and I am prepared to pay again for the pleasure of telling you the truth. For Gallicisms I wonвҖҷt be responsible,вҖқ she remarked, turning to the author: вҖңI have neither the money nor the time, like Prince GalГӯtsyn, to engage a master to teach me Russian!вҖқ вҖңAh, here he is!вҖқ she added. вҖңQuand onвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No, no,вҖқ she said to the militia officer, вҖңyou wonвҖҷt catch me. Speak of the sun and you see its rays!вҖқ and she smiled amiably at Pierre. вҖңWe were just talking of you,вҖқ she said with the facility in lying natural to a society woman. вҖңWe were saying that your regiment would be sure to be better than MamГіnovвҖҷs.вҖқ вҖңOh, donвҖҷt talk to me of my regiment,вҖқ replied Pierre, kissing his hostessвҖҷ hand and taking a seat beside her. вҖңI am so sick of it.вҖқ вҖңYou will, of course, command it yourself?вҖқ said Julie, directing a sly, sarcastic glance toward the militia officer. The latter in PierreвҖҷs presence had ceased to be caustic, and his face expressed perplexity as to what JulieвҖҷs smile might mean. In spite of his absentmindedness and good nature, PierreвҖҷs personality immediately checked any attempt to ridicule him to his face. вҖңNo,вҖқ said Pierre, with a laughing glance at his big, stout body. вҖңI should make too good a target for the French, besides I am afraid I should hardly be able to climb onto a horse.вҖқ Among those whom JulieвҖҷs guests happened to choose to gossip about were the RostГіvs. вҖңI hear that their affairs are in a very bad way,вҖқ said Julie. вҖңAnd he is so unreasonable, the count himself I mean. The RazumГіvskis wanted to buy his house and his estate near Moscow, but it drags on and on. He asks too much.вҖқ вҖңNo, I think the sale will come off in a few days,вҖқ said someone. вҖңThough it is madness to buy anything in Moscow now.вҖқ вҖңWhy?вҖқ asked Julie. вҖңYou donвҖҷt think Moscow is in danger?вҖқ вҖңThen why are you leaving?вҖқ вҖңI? What a question! I am going becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ well, because everyone is going: and besidesвҒ вҖ”I am not Joan of Arc or an Amazon.вҖқ вҖңWell, of course, of course! Let me have some more strips of linen.вҖқ вҖңIf he manages the business properly he will be able to pay off all his debts,вҖқ said the militia officer, speaking of RostГіv. вҖңA kindly old man but not up to much. And why do they stay on so long in Moscow? They meant to leave for the country long ago. NatГЎli is quite well again now, isnвҖҷt she?вҖқ Julie asked Pierre with a knowing smile. вҖңThey are waiting for their younger son,вҖқ Pierre replied. вҖңHe joined ObolГ©nskiвҖҷs Cossacks and went to BГ©laya TsГ©rkov where the regiment is being formed. But now they have had him transferred to my regiment and are expecting him every day. The count wanted to leave long ago, but the countess wonвҖҷt on any account leave Moscow till her son returns.вҖқ вҖңI met them the day before yesterday at the ArkhГЎrovsвҖҷ. NatГЎli has recovered her looks and is brighter. She sang a song. How easily some people get over everything!вҖқ вҖңGet over what?вҖқ inquired Pierre, looking displeased. Julie smiled. вҖңYou know, Count, such knights as you are only found in Madame de SouzaвҖҷs novels.вҖқ вҖңWhat knights? What do you mean?вҖқ demanded Pierre, blushing. вҖңOh, come, my dear count! CвҖҷest la fable de tout Moscou. Je vous admire, ma parole dвҖҷhonneur!вҖқ88 вҖңForfeit, forfeit!вҖқ cried the militia officer. вҖңAll right, one canвҖҷt talkвҒ вҖ”how tiresome!вҖқ вҖңWhat is вҖҳthe talk of all MoscowвҖҷ?вҖқ Pierre asked angrily, rising to his feet. вҖңCome now, Count, you know!вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt know anything about it,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңI know you were friendly with NatГЎli, and soвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ but I was always more friendly with VГ©raвҒ вҖ”that dear VГ©ra.вҖқ вҖңNo, madame!вҖқ Pierre continued in a tone of displeasure, вҖңI have not taken on myself the role of NatГЎlya RostГіvaвҖҷs knight at all, and have not been to their house for nearly a month. But I cannot understand the crueltyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңQui sвҖҷexcuse sвҖҷaccuse,вҖқ89 said Julie, smiling and waving the lint triumphantly, and to have the last word she promptly changed the subject. вҖңDo you know what I heard today? Poor MГЎrie BolkГіnskaya arrived in Moscow yesterday. Do you know that she has lost her father?вҖқ вҖңReally? Where is she? I should like very much to see her,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңI spent the evening with her yesterday. She is going to their estate near Moscow either today or tomorrow morning, with her nephew.вҖқ вҖңWell, and how is she?вҖқ asked Pierre. вҖңShe is well, but sad. But do you know who rescued her? It is quite a romance. Nicolas RostГіv! She was surrounded, and they wanted to kill her and had wounded some of her people. He rushed in and saved her.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAnother romance,вҖқ said the militia officer. вҖңReally, this general flight has been arranged to get all the old maids married off. Catiche is one and Princess BolkГіnskaya another.вҖқ вҖңDo you know, I really believe she is un petit peu amoureuse du jeune homme.вҖқ90 вҖңForfeit, forfeit, forfeit!вҖқ вҖңBut how could one say that in Russian?вҖқ XVIII XVIII When Pierre returned home he was handed two of RostopchГӯnвҖҷs broadsheets that had been brought that day.
