The first declared that the report that Count RostopchГӯn had forbidden people to leave Moscow was false; on the contrary he was glad that ladies and tradesmenвҖҷs wives were leaving the city. вҖңThere will be less panic and less gossip,вҖқ ran the broadsheet вҖңbut I will stake my life on it that that scoundrel will not enter Moscow.вҖқ These words showed Pierre clearly for the first time that the French would enter Moscow. The second broadsheet stated that our headquarters were at VyГЎzma, that Count Wittgenstein had defeated the French, but that as many of the inhabitants of Moscow wished to be armed, weapons were ready for them at the arsenal: sabers, pistols, and muskets which could be had at a low price. The tone of the proclamation was not as jocose as in the former ChigГӯrin talks. Pierre pondered over these broadsheets. Evidently the terrible storm cloud he had desired with the whole strength of his soul but which yet aroused involuntary horror in him was drawing near. вҖңShall I join the army and enter the service, or wait?вҖқ he asked himself for the hundredth time. He took a pack of cards that lay on the table and began to lay them out for a game of patience. вҖңIf this patience comes out,вҖқ he said to himself after shuffling the cards, holding them in his hand, and lifting his head, вҖңif it comes out, it meansвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ what does it mean?вҖқ He had not decided what it should mean when he heard the voice of the eldest princess at the door asking whether she might come in. вҖңThen it will mean that I must go to the army,вҖқ said Pierre to himself. вҖңCome in, come in!вҖқ he added to the princess. Only the eldest princess, the one with the stony face and long waist, was still living in PierreвҖҷs house. The two younger ones had both married. вҖңExcuse my coming to you, cousin,вҖқ she said in a reproachful and agitated voice. вҖңYou know some decision must be come to. What is going to happen? Everyone has left Moscow and the people are rioting. How is it that we are staying on?вҖқ вҖңOn the contrary, things seem satisfactory, ma cousine,вҖқ said Pierre in the bantering tone he habitually adopted toward her, always feeling uncomfortable in the role of her benefactor. вҖңSatisfactory, indeed! Very satisfactory! VГЎrvara IvГЎnovna told me today how our troops are distinguishing themselves. It certainly does them credit! And the people too are quite mutinousвҒ вҖ”they no longer obey, even my maid has taken to being rude. At this rate they will soon begin beating us. One canвҖҷt walk in the streets. But, above all, the French will be here any day now, so what are we waiting for? I ask just one thing of you, cousin,вҖқ she went on, вҖңarrange for me to be taken to Petersburg. Whatever I may be, I canвҖҷt live under BonaparteвҖҷs rule.вҖқ вҖңOh, come, ma cousine! Where do you get your information from? On the contraryвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI wonвҖҷt submit to your Napoleon! Others may if they please.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ If you donвҖҷt want to do thisвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңBut I will, IвҖҷll give the order at once.вҖқ The princess was apparently vexed at not having anyone to be angry with. Muttering to herself, she sat down on a chair. вҖңBut you have been misinformed,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңEverything is quiet in the city and there is not the slightest danger. See! IвҖҷve just been readingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He showed her the broadsheet. вҖңCount RostopchГӯn writes that he will stake his life on it that the enemy will not enter Moscow.вҖқ вҖңOh, that count of yours!вҖқ said the princess malevolently. вҖңHe is a hypocrite, a rascal who has himself roused the people to riot. DidnвҖҷt he write in those idiotic broadsheets that anyone, вҖҳwhoever it might be, should be dragged to the lockup by his hairвҖҷ? (How silly!) вҖҳAnd honor and glory to whoever captures him,вҖҷ he says. This is what his cajolery has brought us to! VГЎrvara IvГЎnovna told me the mob near killed her because she said something in French.вҖқ вҖңOh, but itвҖҷs soвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You take everything so to heart,вҖқ said Pierre, and began laying out his cards for patience. Although that patience did come out, Pierre did not join the army, but remained in deserted Moscow ever in the same state of agitation, irresolution, and alarm, yet at the same time joyfully expecting something terrible. Next day toward evening the princess set off, and PierreвҖҷs head steward came to inform him that the money needed for the equipment of his regiment could not be found without selling one of the estates. In general the head steward made out to Pierre that his project of raising a regiment would ruin him. Pierre listened to him, scarcely able to repress a smile. вҖңWell then, sell it,вҖқ said he. вҖңWhatвҖҷs to be done? I canвҖҷt draw back now!вҖқ The worse everything became, especially his own affairs, the better was Pierre pleased and the more evident was it that the catastrophe he expected was approaching. Hardly anyone he knew was left in town. Julie had gone, and so had Princess MГЎrya. Of his intimate friends only the RostГіvs remained, but he did not go to see them. To distract his thoughts he drove that day to the village of VorontsГіvo to see the great balloon Leppich was constructing to destroy the foe, and a trial balloon that was to go up next day. The balloon was not yet ready, but Pierre learned that it was being constructed by the EmperorвҖҷs desire. The Emperor had written to Count RostopchГӯn as follows: As soon as Leppich is ready, get together a crew of reliable and intelligent men for his car and send a courier to General Koutousoff to let him know. I have informed him of the matter. Please impress upon Leppich to be very careful where he descends for the first time, that he may not make a mistake and fall into the enemyвҖҷs hands. It is essential for him to combine his movements with those of the commander in chief. On his way home from VorontsГіvo, as he was passing the BolГіtnoe Place Pierre, seeing a large crowd round the LГіbnoe Place, stopped and got out of his trap. A French cook accused of being a spy was being flogged. The flogging was only just over, and the executioner was releasing from the flogging bench a stout man with red whiskers, in blue stockings and a green jacket, who was moaning piteously. Another criminal, thin and pale, stood near. Judging by their faces they were both Frenchmen. With a frightened and suffering look resembling that on the thin FrenchmanвҖҷs face, Pierre pushed his way in through the crowd. вҖңWhat is it? Who is it? What is it for?вҖқ he kept asking. But the attention of the crowdвҒ вҖ”officials, burghers, shopkeepers, peasants, and women in cloaks and in pelissesвҒ вҖ”was so eagerly centered on what was passing in LГіbnoe Place that no one answered him. The stout man rose, frowned, shrugged his shoulders, and evidently trying to appear firm began to pull on his jacket without looking about him, but suddenly his lips trembled and he began to cry, in the way full-blooded grown-up men cry, though angry with himself for doing so. In the crowd people began talking loudly, to stifle their feelings of pity as it seemed to Pierre. вҖңHeвҖҷs cook to some prince.вҖқ вҖңEh, mounseer, Russian sauce seems to be sour to a FrenchmanвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ sets his teeth on edge!вҖқ said a wrinkled clerk who was standing behind Pierre, when the Frenchman began to cry. The clerk glanced round, evidently hoping that his joke would be appreciated. Some people began to laugh, others continued to watch in dismay the executioner who was undressing the other man. Pierre choked, his face puckered, and he turned hastily away, went back to his trap muttering something to himself as he went, and took his seat. As they drove along he shuddered and exclaimed several times so audibly that the coachman asked him: вҖңWhat is your pleasure?вҖқ вҖңWhere are you going?вҖқ shouted Pierre to the man, who was driving to LubyГЎnka Street. вҖңTo the GovernorвҖҷs, as you ordered,вҖқ answered the coachman. вҖңFool! Idiot!вҖқ shouted Pierre, abusing his coachmanвҒ вҖ”a thing he rarely did. вҖңHome, I told you! And drive faster, blockhead!вҖқ вҖңI must get away this very day,вҖқ he murmured to himself. At the sight of the tortured Frenchman and the crowd surrounding the LГіbnoe Place, Pierre had so definitely made up his mind that he could no longer remain in Moscow and would leave for the army that very day that it seemed to him that either he had told the coachman this or that the man ought to have known it for himself. On reaching home Pierre gave orders to EvstГЎfeyвҒ вҖ”his head coachman who knew everything, could do anything, and was known to all MoscowвҒ вҖ”that he would leave that night for the army at MozhГЎysk, and that his saddle horses should be sent there. This could not all be arranged that day, so on EvstГЎfeyвҖҷs representation Pierre had to put off his departure till next day to allow time for the relay horses to be sent on in advance. On the twenty-fourth the weather cleared up after a spell of rain, and after dinner Pierre left Moscow. When changing horses that night in PerkhГәshkovo, he learned that there had been a great battle that evening. (This was the battle of ShevГЎrdino.) He was told that there in PerkhГәshkovo the earth trembled from the firing, but nobody could answer his questions as to who had won. At dawn next day Pierre was approaching MozhГЎysk. Every house in MozhГЎysk had soldiers quartered in it, and at the hostel where Pierre was met by his groom and coachman there was no room to be had. It was full of officers. Everywhere in MozhГЎysk and beyond it, troops were stationed or on the march. Cossacks, foot and horse soldiers, wagons, caissons, and cannon were everywhere. Pierre pushed forward as fast as he could, and the farther he left Moscow behind and the deeper he plunged into that sea of troops the more was he overcome by restless agitation and a new and joyful feeling he had not experienced before. It was a feeling akin to what he had felt at the SlobГіda Palace during the EmperorвҖҷs visitвҒ вҖ”a sense of the necessity of undertaking something and sacrificing something. He now experienced a glad consciousness that everything that constitutes menвҖҷs happinessвҒ вҖ”the comforts of life, wealth, even life itselfвҒ вҖ”is rubbish it is pleasant to throw away, compared with somethingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ With what? Pierre could not say, and he did not try to determine for whom and for what he felt such particular delight in sacrificing everything. He was not occupied with the question of what to sacrifice for; the fact of sacrificing in itself afforded him a new and joyous sensation. XIX XIX On the twenty-fourth of August the battle of the ShevГЎrdino Redoubt was fought, on the twenty-fifth not a shot was fired by either side, and on the twenty-sixth the battle of BorodinГі itself took place. Why and how were the battles of ShevГЎrdino and BorodinГі given and accepted? Why was the battle of BorodinГі fought? There was not the least sense in it for either the French or the Russians. Its immediate result for the Russians was, and was bound to be, that we were brought nearer to the destruction of MoscowвҒ вҖ”which we feared more than anything in the world; and for the French its immediate result was that they were brought nearer to the destruction of their whole armyвҒ вҖ”which they feared more than anything in the world. What the result must be was quite obvious, and yet Napoleon offered and KutГәzov accepted that battle. If the commanders had been guided by reason, it would seem that it must have been obvious to Napoleon that by advancing thirteen hundred miles and giving battle with a probability of losing a quarter of his army, he was advancing to certain destruction, and it must have been equally clear to KutГәzov that by accepting battle and risking the loss of a quarter of his army he would certainly lose Moscow. For KutГәzov this was mathematically clear, as it is that if when playing draughts I have one man less and go on exchanging, I shall certainly lose, and therefore should not exchange. When my opponent has sixteen men and I have fourteen, I am only one eighth weaker than he, but when I have exchanged thirteen more men he will be three times as strong as I am. Before the battle of BorodinГі our strength in proportion to the French was about as five to six, but after that battle it was little more than one to two: previously we had a hundred thousand against a hundred and twenty thousand; afterwards little more than fifty thousand against a hundred thousand. Yet the shrewd and experienced KutГәzov accepted the battle, while Napoleon, who was said to be a commander of genius, gave it, losing a quarter of his army and lengthening his lines of communication still more. If it is said that he expected to end the campaign by occupying Moscow as he had ended a previous campaign by occupying Vienna, there is much evidence to the contrary. NapoleonвҖҷs historians themselves tell us that from SmolГ©nsk onwards he wished to stop, knew the danger of his extended position, and knew that the occupation of Moscow would not be the end of the campaign, for he had seen at SmolГ©nsk the state in which Russian towns were left to him, and had not received a single reply to his repeated announcements of his wish to negotiate. In giving and accepting battle at BorodinГі, KutГәzov acted involuntarily and irrationally. But later on, to fit what had occurred, the historians provided cunningly devised evidence of the foresight and genius of the generals who, of all the blind tools of history were the most enslaved and involuntary. The ancients have left us model heroic poems in which the heroes furnish the whole interest of the story, and we are still unable to accustom ourselves to the fact that for our epoch histories of that kind are meaningless. On the other question, how the battle of BorodinГі and the preceding battle of ShevГЎrdino were fought, there also exists a definite and well-known, but quite false, conception. All the historians describe the affair as follows: The Russian army, they say, in its retreat from SmolГ©nsk sought out for itself the best position for a general engagement and found such a position at BorodinГі. The Russians, they say, fortified this position in advance on the left of the high road (from Moscow to SmolГ©nsk) and almost at a right angle to it, from BorodinГі to UtГӯtsa, at the very place where the battle was fought. In front of this position, they say, a fortified outpost was set up on the ShevГЎrdino mound to observe the enemy. On the twenty-fourth, we are told, Napoleon attacked this advanced post and took it, and, on the twenty-sixth, attacked the whole Russian army, which was in position on the field of BorodinГі. So the histories say, and it is all quite wrong, as anyone who cares to look into the matter can easily convince himself. The Russians did not seek out the best position but, on the contrary, during the retreat passed many positions better than BorodinГі. They did not stop at any one of these positions because KutГәzov did not wish to occupy a position he had not himself chosen, because the popular demand for a battle had not yet expressed itself strongly enough, and because MilorГЎdovich had not yet arrived with the militia, and for many other reasons. The fact is that other positions they had passed were stronger, and that the position at BorodinГі (the one where the battle was fought), far from being strong, was no more a position than any other spot one might find in the Russian Empire by sticking a pin into the map at hazard. Not only did the Russians not fortify the position on the field of BorodinГі to the left of, and at a right angle to, the high road (that is, the position on which the battle took place), but never till the twenty-fifth of August, 1812, did they think that a battle might be fought there. This was shown first by the fact that there were no entrenchments there by the twenty fifth and that those begun on the twenty-fifth and twenty-sixth were not completed, and secondly, by the position of the ShevГЎrdino Redoubt. That redoubt was quite senseless in front of the position where the battle was accepted. Why was it more strongly fortified than any other post? And why were all efforts exhausted and six thousand men sacrificed to defend it till late at night on the twenty-fourth? A Cossack patrol would have sufficed to observe the enemy. Thirdly, as proof that the position on which the battle was fought had not been foreseen and that the ShevГЎrdino Redoubt was not an advanced post of that position, we have the fact that up to the twenty-fifth, Barclay de Tolly and BagratiГіn were convinced that the ShevГЎrdino Redoubt was the left flank of the position, and that KutГәzov himself in his report, written in hot haste after the battle, speaks of the ShevГЎrdino Redoubt as the left flank of the position. It was much later, when reports on the battle of BorodinГі were written at leisure, that the incorrect and extraordinary statement was invented (probably to justify the mistakes of a commander in chief who had to be represented as infallible) that the ShevГЎrdino Redoubt was an advanced postвҒ вҖ”whereas in reality it was simply a fortified point on the left flankвҒ вҖ”and that the battle of BorodinГі was fought by us on an entrenched position previously selected, whereas it was fought on a quite unexpected spot which was almost unentrenched. The case was evidently this: a position was selected along the river KolochГЎвҒ вҖ”which crosses the high road not at a right angle but at an acute angleвҒ вҖ”so that the left flank was at ShevГЎrdino, the right flank near the village of NГіvoe, and the center at BorodinГі at the confluence of the rivers KolochГЎ and VГіyna. To anyone who looks at the field of BorodinГі without thinking of how the battle was actually fought, this position, protected by the river KolochГЎ, presents itself as obvious for an army whose object was to prevent an enemy from advancing along the SmolГ©nsk road to Moscow. Napoleon, riding to ValГәevo on the twenty-fourth, did not see (as the history books say he did) the position of the Russians from UtГӯtsa to BorodinГі (he could not have seen that position because it did not exist), nor did he see an advanced post of the Russian army, but while pursuing the Russian rearguard he came upon the left flank of the Russian positionвҒ вҖ”at the ShevГЎrdino RedoubtвҒ вҖ”and unexpectedly for the Russians moved his army across the KolochГЎ. And the Russians, not having time to begin a general engagement, withdrew their left wing from the position they had intended to occupy and took up a new position which had not been foreseen and was not fortified. By crossing to the other side of the KolochГЎ to the left of the high road, Napoleon shifted the whole forthcoming battle from right to left (looking from the Russian side) and transferred it to the plain between UtГӯtsa, SemГ«novsk, and BorodinГівҒ вҖ”a plain no more advantageous as a position than any other plain in RussiaвҒ вҖ”and there the whole battle of the twenty-sixth of August took place. Had Napoleon not ridden out on the evening of the twenty-fourth to the KolochГЎ, and had he not then ordered an immediate attack on the redoubt but had begun the attack next morning, no one would have doubted that the ShevГЎrdino Redoubt was the left flank of our position, and the battle would have taken place where we expected it. In that case we should probably have defended the ShevГЎrdino RedoubtвҒ вҖ”our left flankвҒ вҖ”still more obstinately. We should have attacked Napoleon in the center or on the right, and the engagement would have taken place on the twenty-fifth, in the position we intended and had fortified. But as the attack on our left flank took place in the evening after the retreat of our rear guard (that is, immediately after the fight at GridnГ«va), and as the Russian commanders did not wish, or were not in time, to begin a general engagement then on the evening of the twenty-fourth, the first and chief action of the battle of BorodinГі was already lost on the twenty-fourth, and obviously led to the loss of the one fought on the twenty-sixth. After the loss of the ShevГЎrdino Redoubt, we found ourselves on the morning of the twenty-fifth without a position for our left flank, and were forced to bend it back and hastily entrench it where it chanced to be. Not only was the Russian army on the twenty-sixth defended by weak, unfinished entrenchments, but the disadvantage of that position was increased by the fact that the Russian commandersвҒ вҖ”not having fully realized what had happened, namely the loss of our position on the left flank and the shifting of the whole field of the forthcoming battle from right to leftвҒ вҖ”maintained their extended position from the village of NГіvoe to UtГӯtsa, and consequently had to move their forces from right to left during the battle. So it happened that throughout the whole battle the Russians opposed the entire French army launched against our left flank with but half as many men. (PoniatowskiвҖҷs action against UtГӯtsa, and UvГЎrovвҖҷs on the right flank against the French, were actions distinct from the main course of the battle.) So the battle of BorodinГі did not take place at all as (in an effort to conceal our commandersвҖҷ mistakes even at the cost of diminishing the glory due to the Russian army and people) it has been described. The battle of BorodinГі was not fought on a chosen and entrenched position with forces only slightly weaker than those of the enemy, but, as a result of the loss of the ShevГЎrdino Redoubt, the Russians fought the battle of BorodinГі on an open and almost unentrenched position, with forces only half as numerous as the French; that is to say, under conditions in which it was not merely unthinkable to fight for ten hours and secure an indecisive result, but unthinkable to keep an army even from complete disintegration and flight. II II The day after his son had left, Prince NikolГЎy AndrГ©evich sent for Princess MГЎrya to come to his study. вҖңWell? Are you satisfied now?вҖқ said he. вҖңYouвҖҷve made me quarrel with my son! Satisfied, are you? ThatвҖҷs all you wanted! Satisfied?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ It hurts me, it hurts. IвҖҷm old and weak and this is what you wanted. Well then, gloat over it! Gloat over it!вҖқ After that Princess MГЎrya did not see her father for a whole week. He was ill and did not leave his study. Princess MГЎrya noticed to her surprise that during this illness the old prince not only excluded her from his room, but did not admit Mademoiselle Bourienne either. TГӯkhon alone attended him. At the end of the week the prince reappeared and resumed his former way of life, devoting himself with special activity to building operations and the arrangement of the gardens and completely breaking off his relations with Mademoiselle Bourienne. His looks and cold tone to his daughter seemed to say: вҖңThere, you see? You plotted against me, you lied to Prince AndrГ©y about my relations with that Frenchwoman and made me quarrel with him, but you see I need neither her nor you!вҖқ Princess MГЎrya spent half of every day with NikolГәshka, watching his lessons, teaching him Russian and music herself, and talking to Dessalles; the rest of the day she spent over her books, with her old nurse, or with вҖңGodвҖҷs folkвҖқ who sometimes came by the back door to see her. Of the war Princess MГЎrya thought as women do think about wars. She feared for her brother who was in it, was horrified by and amazed at the strange cruelty that impels men to kill one another, but she did not understand the significance of this war, which seemed to her like all previous wars. She did not realize the significance of this war, though Dessalles with whom she constantly conversed was passionately interested in its progress and tried to explain his own conception of it to her, and though the вҖңGodвҖҷs folkвҖқ who came to see her reported, in their own way, the rumors current among the people of an invasion by Antichrist, and though Julie (now Princess DrubetskГЎya), who had resumed correspondence with her, wrote patriotic letters from Moscow. вҖңI write you in Russian, my good friend,вҖқ wrote Julie in her Frenchified Russian, вҖңbecause I have a detestation for all the French, and the same for their language which I cannot support to hear spoken.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ We in Moscow are elated by enthusiasm for our adored Emperor. вҖңMy poor husband is enduring pains and hunger in Jewish taverns, but the news which I have inspires me yet more. вҖңYou heard probably of the heroic exploit of RaГ©vski, embracing his two sons and saying: вҖҳI will perish with them but we will not be shaken!вҖҷ And truly though the enemy was twice stronger than we, we were unshakable. We pass the time as we can, but in war as in war! The princesses Aline and Sophie sit whole days with me, and we, unhappy widows of live men, make beautiful conversations over our charpie, only you, my friend, are missingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and so on. The chief reason Princess MГЎrya did not realize the full significance of this war was that the old prince never spoke of it, did not recognize it, and laughed at Dessalles when he mentioned it at dinner. The princeвҖҷs tone was so calm and confident that Princess MГЎrya unhesitatingly believed him. All that July the old prince was exceedingly active and even animated. He planned another garden and began a new building for the domestic serfs. The only thing that made Princess MГЎrya anxious about him was that he slept very little and, instead of sleeping in his study as usual, changed his sleeping place every day. One day he would order his camp bed to be set up in the glass gallery, another day he remained on the couch or on the lounge chair in the drawing room and dozed there without undressing, whileвҒ вҖ”instead of Mademoiselle BourienneвҒ вҖ”a serf boy read to him. Then again he would spend a night in the dining room. On August 1, a second letter was received from Prince AndrГ©y. In his first letter which came soon after he had left home, Prince AndrГ©y had dutifully asked his fatherвҖҷs forgiveness for what he had allowed himself to say and begged to be restored to his favor. To this letter the old prince had replied affectionately, and from that time had kept the Frenchwoman at a distance. Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs second letter, written near VГӯtebsk after the French had occupied that town, gave a brief account of the whole campaign, enclosed for them a plan he had drawn and forecasts as to the further progress of the war. In this letter Prince AndrГ©y pointed out to his father the danger of staying at Bald Hills, so near the theater of war and on the armyвҖҷs direct line of march, and advised him to move to Moscow. At dinner that day, on DessallesвҖҷ mentioning that the French were said to have already entered VГӯtebsk, the old prince remembered his sonвҖҷs letter. вҖңThere was a letter from Prince AndrГ©y today,вҖқ he said to Princess MГЎryaвҒ вҖ”вҖңHavenвҖҷt you read it?вҖқ вҖңNo, Father,вҖқ she replied in a frightened voice. She could not have read the letter as she did not even know it had arrived. вҖңHe writes about this war,вҖқ said the prince, with the ironic smile that had become habitual to him in speaking of the present war. вҖңThat must be very interesting,вҖқ said Dessalles. вҖңPrince AndrГ©y is in a position to knowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңOh, very interesting!вҖқ said Mademoiselle Bourienne. вҖңGo and get it for me,вҖқ said the old prince to Mademoiselle Bourienne. вҖңYou knowвҒ вҖ”under the paperweight on the little table.вҖқ Mademoiselle Bourienne jumped up eagerly. вҖңNo, donвҖҷt!вҖқ he exclaimed with a frown. вҖңYou go, MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich.вҖқ MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich rose and went to the study. But as soon as he had left the room the old prince, looking uneasily round, threw down his napkin and went himself. вҖңThey canвҖҷt do anythingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ always make some muddle,вҖқ he muttered. While he was away Princess MГЎrya, Dessalles, Mademoiselle Bourienne, and even little NikolГәshka exchanged looks in silence. The old prince returned with quick steps, accompanied by MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich, bringing the letter and a plan. These he put down beside himвҒ вҖ”not letting anyone read them at dinner. On moving to the drawing room he handed the letter to Princess MГЎrya and, spreading out before him the plan of the new building and fixing his eyes upon it, told her to read the letter aloud. When she had done so Princess MГЎrya looked inquiringly at her father. He was examining the plan, evidently engrossed in his own ideas. вҖңWhat do you think of it, Prince?вҖқ Dessalles ventured to ask. вҖңI? I?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said the prince as if unpleasantly awakened, and not taking his eyes from the plan of the building. вҖңVery possibly the theater of war will move so near to us thatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңHa ha ha! The theater of war!вҖқ said the prince. вҖңI have said and still say that the theater of war is Poland and the enemy will never get beyond the Niemen.вҖқ Dessalles looked in amazement at the prince, who was talking of the Niemen when the enemy was already at the Dnieper, but Princess MГЎrya, forgetting the geographical position of the Niemen, thought that what her father was saying was correct. вҖңWhen the snow melts theyвҖҷll sink in the Polish swamps. Only they could fail to see it,вҖқ the prince continued, evidently thinking of the campaign of 1807 which seemed to him so recent. вҖңBennigsen should have advanced into Prussia sooner, then things would have taken a different turnвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңBut, Prince,вҖқ Dessalles began timidly, вҖңthe letter mentions VГӯtebsk.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAh, the letter? YesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ replied the prince peevishly. вҖңYesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ yesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ His face suddenly took on a morose expression. He paused. вҖңYes, he writes that the French were beaten atвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ atвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ what river is it?вҖқ Dessalles dropped his eyes. вҖңThe prince says nothing about that,вҖқ he remarked gently. вҖңDoesnвҖҷt he? But I didnвҖҷt invent it myself.вҖқ No one spoke for a long time. вҖңYesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ yesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich,вҖқ he suddenly went on, raising his head and pointing to the plan of the building, вҖңtell me how you mean to alter it.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich went up to the plan, and the prince after speaking to him about the building looked angrily at Princess MГЎrya and Dessalles and went to his own room. Princess MГЎrya saw DessallesвҖҷ embarrassed and astonished look fixed on her father, noticed his silence, and was struck by the fact that her father had forgotten his sonвҖҷs letter on the drawing room table; but she was not only afraid to speak of it and ask Dessalles the reason of his confusion and silence, but was afraid even to think about it. In the evening MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich, sent by the prince, came to Princess MГЎrya for Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs letter which had been forgotten in the drawing room. She gave it to him and, unpleasant as it was to her to do so, ventured to ask him what her father was doing. вҖңAlways busy,вҖқ replied MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich with a respectfully ironic smile which caused Princess MГЎrya to turn pale. вҖңHeвҖҷs worrying very much about the new building. He has been reading a little, but nowвҖқвҒ вҖ”MikhГЎil IvГЎnovich went on, lowering his voiceвҒ вҖ”вҖңnow heвҖҷs at his desk, busy with his will, I expect.вҖқ (One of the princeвҖҷs favorite occupations of late had been the preparation of some papers he meant to leave at his death and which he called his вҖңwill.вҖқ) вҖңAnd AlpГЎtych is being sent to SmolГ©nsk?вҖқ asked Princess MГЎrya. вҖңOh, yes, he has been waiting to start for some time.вҖқ XX XX On the morning of the twenty-fifth Pierre was leaving MozhГЎysk. At the descent of the high steep hill, down which a winding road led out of the town past the cathedral on the right, where a service was being held and the bells were ringing, Pierre got out of his vehicle and proceeded on foot. Behind him a cavalry regiment was coming down the hill preceded by its singers. Coming up toward him was a train of carts carrying men who had been wounded in the engagement the day before. The peasant drivers, shouting and lashing their horses, kept crossing from side to side. The carts, in each of which three or four wounded soldiers were lying or sitting, jolted over the stones that had been thrown on the steep incline to make it something like a road. The wounded, bandaged with rags, with pale cheeks, compressed lips, and knitted brows, held on to the sides of the carts as they were jolted against one another. Almost all of them stared with naive, childlike curiosity at PierreвҖҷs white hat and green swallowtail coat. PierreвҖҷs coachman shouted angrily at the convoy of wounded to keep to one side of the road. The cavalry regiment, as it descended the hill with its singers, surrounded PierreвҖҷs carriage and blocked the road. Pierre stopped, being pressed against the side of the cutting in which the road ran. The sunshine from behind the hill did not penetrate into the cutting and there it was cold and damp, but above PierreвҖҷs head was the bright August sunshine and the bells sounded merrily. One of the carts with wounded stopped by the side of the road close to Pierre. The driver in his bast shoes ran panting up to it, placed a stone under one of its tireless hind wheels, and began arranging the breech-band on his little horse. One of the wounded, an old soldier with a bandaged arm who was following the cart on foot, caught hold of it with his sound hand and turned to look at Pierre. вҖңI say, fellow countryman! Will they set us down here or take us on to Moscow?вҖқ he asked. Pierre was so deep in thought that he did not hear the question. He was looking now at the cavalry regiment that had met the convoy of wounded, now at the cart by which he was standing, in which two wounded men were sitting and one was lying. One of those sitting up in the cart had probably been wounded in the cheek. His whole head was wrapped in rags and one cheek was swollen to the size of a babyвҖҷs head. His nose and mouth were twisted to one side. This soldier was looking at the cathedral and crossing himself. Another, a young lad, a fair-haired recruit as white as though there was no blood in his thin face, looked at Pierre kindly, with a fixed smile. The third lay prone so that his face was not visible. The cavalry singers were passing close by: Ah lost, quite lostвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ is my head so keen, Living in a foreign landвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ they sang their soldiersвҖҷ dance song. As if responding to them but with a different sort of merriment, the metallic sound of the bells reverberated high above and the hot rays of the sun bathed the top of the opposite slope with yet another sort of merriment. But beneath the slope, by the cart with the wounded near the panting little nag where Pierre stood, it was damp, somber, and sad. The soldier with the swollen cheek looked angrily at the cavalry singers. вҖңOh, the coxcombs!вҖқ he muttered reproachfully. вҖңItвҖҷs not the soldiers only, but IвҖҷve seen peasants today, too.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The peasantsвҒ вҖ”even they have to go,вҖқ said the soldier behind the cart, addressing Pierre with a sad smile. вҖңNo distinctions made nowadays.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ They want the whole nation to fall on themвҒ вҖ”in a word, itвҖҷs Moscow! They want to make an end of it.вҖқ In spite of the obscurity of the soldierвҖҷs words Pierre understood what he wanted to say and nodded approval. The road was clear again; Pierre descended the hill and drove on. He kept looking to either side of the road for familiar faces, but only saw everywhere the unfamiliar faces of various military men of different branches of the service, who all looked with astonishment at his white hat and green tail coat. Having gone nearly three miles he at last met an acquaintance and eagerly addressed him. This was one of the head army doctors. He was driving toward Pierre in a covered gig, sitting beside a young surgeon, and on recognizing Pierre he told the Cossack who occupied the driverвҖҷs seat to pull up. вҖңCount! Your excellency, how come you to be here?вҖқ asked the doctor. вҖңWell, you know, I wanted to seeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYes, yes, there will be something to see.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre got out and talked to the doctor, explaining his intention of taking part in a battle. The doctor advised him to apply direct to KutГәzov. вҖңWhy should you be God knows where out of sight, during the battle?вҖқ he said, exchanging glances with his young companion. вҖңAnyhow his Serene Highness knows you and will receive you graciously. ThatвҖҷs what you must do.вҖқ The doctor seemed tired and in a hurry. вҖңYou think so?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Ah, I also wanted to ask you where our position is exactly?вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңThe position?вҖқ repeated the doctor. вҖңWell, thatвҖҷs not my line. Drive past TatГЎrinova, a lot of digging is going on there. Go up the hillock and youвҖҷll see.вҖқ вҖңCan one see from there?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ If you wouldвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ But the doctor interrupted him and moved toward his gig. вҖңI would go with you but on my honor IвҖҷm up to hereвҖқвҒ вҖ”and he pointed to his throat. вҖңIвҖҷm galloping to the commander of the corps. How do matters stand?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You know, Count, thereвҖҷll be a battle tomorrow. Out of an army of a hundred thousand we must expect at least twenty thousand wounded, and we havenвҖҷt stretchers, or bunks, or dressers, or doctors enough for six thousand. We have ten thousand carts, but we need other things as wellвҒ вҖ”we must manage as best we can!вҖқ The strange thought that of the thousands of men, young and old, who had stared with merry surprise at his hat (perhaps the very men he had noticed), twenty thousand were inevitably doomed to wounds and death amazed Pierre. вҖңThey may die tomorrow; why are they thinking of anything but death?вҖқ And by some latent sequence of thought the descent of the MozhГЎysk hill, the carts with the wounded, the ringing bells, the slanting rays of the sun, and the songs of the cavalrymen vividly recurred to his mind. вҖңThe cavalry ride to battle and meet the wounded and do not for a moment think of what awaits them, but pass by, winking at the wounded. Yet from among these men twenty thousand are doomed to die, and they wonder at my hat! Strange!вҖқ thought Pierre, continuing his way to TatГЎrinova. In front of a landownerвҖҷs house to the left of the road stood carriages, wagons, and crowds of orderlies and sentinels. The commander in chief was putting up there, but just when Pierre arrived he was not in and hardly any of the staff were thereвҒ вҖ”they had gone to the church service. Pierre drove on toward GГіrki. When he had ascended the hill and reached the little village street, he saw for the first time peasant militiamen in their white shirts and with crosses on their caps, who, talking and laughing loudly, animated and perspiring, were at work on a huge knoll overgrown with grass to the right of the road. Some of them were digging, others were wheeling barrowloads of earth along planks, while others stood about doing nothing. Two officers were standing on the knoll, directing the men. On seeing these peasants, who were evidently still amused by the novelty of their position as soldiers, Pierre once more thought of the wounded men at MozhГЎysk and understood what the soldier had meant when he said: вҖңThey want the whole nation to fall on them.вҖқ The sight of these bearded peasants at work on the battlefield, with their queer, clumsy boots and perspiring necks, and their shirts opening from the left toward the middle, unfastened, exposing their sunburned collarbones, impressed Pierre more strongly with the solemnity and importance of the moment than anything he had yet seen or heard. XXI XXI Pierre stepped out of his carriage and, passing the toiling militiamen, ascended the knoll from which, according to the doctor, the battlefield could be seen. It was about eleven oвҖҷclock. The sun shone somewhat to the left and behind him and brightly lit up the enormous panorama which, rising like an amphitheater, extended before him in the clear rarefied atmosphere. From above on the left, bisecting that amphitheater, wound the SmolГ©nsk high road, passing through a village with a white church some five hundred paces in front of the knoll and below it. This was BorodinГі. Below the village the road crossed the river by a bridge and, winding down and up, rose higher and higher to the village of ValГәevo visible about four miles away, where Napoleon was then stationed. Beyond ValГәevo the road disappeared into a yellowing forest on the horizon. Far in the distance in that birch and fir forest to the right of the road, the cross and belfry of the KolochГЎ Monastery gleamed in the sun. Here and there over the whole of that blue expanse, to right and left of the forest and the road, smoking campfires could be seen and indefinite masses of troopsвҒ вҖ”ours and the enemyвҖҷs. The ground to the rightвҒ вҖ”along the course of the KolochГЎ and MoskvГЎ riversвҒ вҖ”was broken and hilly. Between the hollows the villages of BezГәbova and ZakhГЎrino showed in the distance. On the left the ground was more level; there were fields of grain, and the smoking ruins of SemГ«novsk, which had been burned down, could be seen. All that Pierre saw was so indefinite that neither the left nor the right side of the field fully satisfied his expectations. Nowhere could he see the battlefield he had expected to find, but only fields, meadows, troops, woods, the smoke of campfires, villages, mounds, and streams; and try as he would he could descry no military вҖңpositionвҖқ in this place which teemed with life, nor could he even distinguish our troops from the enemyвҖҷs. вҖңI must ask someone who knows,вҖқ he thought, and addressed an officer who was looking with curiosity at his huge unmilitary figure. вҖңMay I ask you,вҖқ said Pierre, вҖңwhat village that is in front?вҖқ вҖңBГәrdino, isnвҖҷt it?вҖқ said the officer, turning to his companion. вҖңBorodinГі,вҖқ the other corrected him. The officer, evidently glad of an opportunity for a talk, moved up to Pierre. вҖңAre those our men there?вҖқ Pierre inquired. вҖңYes, and there, further on, are the French,вҖқ said the officer. вҖңThere they are, thereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ you can see them.вҖқ вҖңWhere? Where?вҖқ asked Pierre. вҖңOne can see them with the naked eyeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Why, there!вҖқ The officer pointed with his hand to the smoke visible on the left beyond the river, and the same stern and serious expression that Pierre had noticed on many of the faces he had met came into his face. вҖңAh, those are the French! And over there?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre pointed to a knoll on the left, near which some troops could be seen. вҖңThose are ours.вҖқ вҖңAh, ours! And there?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre pointed to another knoll in the distance with a big tree on it, near a village that lay in a hollow where also some campfires were smoking and something black was visible. вҖңThatвҖҷs his again,вҖқ said the officer. (It was the ShevГЎrdino Redoubt.) вҖңIt was ours yesterday, but now it is his.вҖқ вҖңThen how about our position?вҖқ вҖңOur position?вҖқ replied the officer with a smile of satisfaction. вҖңI can tell you quite clearly, because I constructed nearly all our entrenchments. There, you see? ThereвҖҷs our center, at BorodinГі, just there,вҖқ and he pointed to the village in front of them with the white church. вҖңThatвҖҷs where one crosses the KolochГЎ. You see down there where the rows of hay are lying in the hollow, thereвҖҷs the bridge. ThatвҖҷs our center. Our right flank is over thereвҖқвҒ вҖ”he pointed sharply to the right, far away in the broken groundвҒ вҖ”вҖңThatвҖҷs where the MoskvГЎ River is, and we have thrown up three redoubts there, very strong ones. The left flankвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ here the officer paused. вҖңWell, you see, thatвҖҷs difficult to explain.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Yesterday our left flank was there at ShevГЎrdino, you see, where the oak is, but now we have withdrawn our left wingвҒ вҖ”now it is over there, do you see that village and the smoke? ThatвҖҷs SemГ«novsk, yes, there,вҖқ he pointed to RaГ©vskiвҖҷs knoll. вҖңBut the battle will hardly be there. His having moved his troops there is only a ruse; he will probably pass round to the right of the MoskvГЎ. But wherever it may be, many a man will be missing tomorrow!вҖқ he remarked. An elderly sergeant who had approached the officer while he was giving these explanations had waited in silence for him to finish speaking, but at this point, evidently not liking the officerвҖҷs remark, interrupted him. вҖңGabions must be sent for,вҖқ said he sternly. The officer appeared abashed, as though he understood that one might think of how many men would be missing tomorrow but ought not to speak of it. вҖңWell, send number three company again,вҖқ the officer replied hurriedly. вҖңAnd you, are you one of the doctors?вҖқ вҖңNo, IвҖҷve come on my own,вҖқ answered Pierre, and he went down the hill again, passing the militiamen. вҖңOh, those damned fellows!вҖқ muttered the officer who followed him, holding his nose as he ran past the men at work. вҖңThere they areвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ bringing her, comingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ There they areвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ TheyвҖҷll be here in a minuteвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ voices were suddenly heard saying; and officers, soldiers, and militiamen began running forward along the road. A church procession was coming up the hill from BorodinГі. First along the dusty road came the infantry in ranks, bareheaded and with arms reversed. From behind them came the sound of church singing. Soldiers and militiamen ran bareheaded past Pierre toward the procession. вҖңThey are bringing her, our Protectress!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The Iberian Mother of God!вҖқ someone cried. вҖңThe SmolГ©nsk Mother of God,вҖқ another corrected him. The militiamen, both those who had been in the village and those who had been at work on the battery, threw down their spades and ran to meet the church procession. Following the battalion that marched along the dusty road came priests in their vestmentsвҒ вҖ”one little old man in a hood with attendants and singers. Behind them soldiers and officers bore a large, dark-faced icon with an embossed metal cover. This was the icon that had been brought from SmolГ©nsk and had since accompanied the army. Behind, before, and on both sides, crowds of militiamen with bared heads walked, ran, and bowed to the ground. At the summit of the hill they stopped with the icon; the men who had been holding it up by the linen bands attached to it were relieved by others, the chanters relit their censers, and service began. The hot rays of the sun beat down vertically and a fresh soft wind played with the hair of the bared heads and with the ribbons decorating the icon. The singing did not sound loud under the open sky. An immense crowd of bareheaded officers, soldiers, and militiamen surrounded the icon. Behind the priest and a chanter stood the notabilities on a spot reserved for them. A bald general with a St.В GeorgeвҖҷs Cross on his neck stood just behind the priestвҖҷs back, and without crossing himself (he was evidently a German) patiently awaited the end of the service, which he considered it necessary to hear to the end, probably to arouse the patriotism of the Russian people. Another general stood in a martial pose, crossing himself by shaking his hand in front of his chest while looking about him. Standing among the crowd of peasants, Pierre recognized several acquaintances among these notables, but did not look at themвҒ вҖ”his whole attention was absorbed in watching the serious expression on the faces of the crowd of soldiers and militiamen who were all gazing eagerly at the icon. As soon as the tired chanters, who were singing the service for the twentieth time that day, began lazily and mechanically to sing: вҖңSave from calamity Thy servants, O Mother of God,вҖқ and the priest and deacon chimed in: вҖңFor to Thee under God we all flee as to an inviolable bulwark and protection,вҖқ there again kindled in all those faces the same expression of consciousness of the solemnity of the impending moment that Pierre had seen on the faces at the foot of the hill at MozhГЎysk and momentarily on many and many faces he had met that morning; and heads were bowed more frequently and hair tossed back, and sighs and the sound men made as they crossed themselves were heard. The crowd round the icon suddenly parted and pressed against Pierre. Someone, a very important personage judging by the haste with which way was made for him, was approaching the icon. It was KutГәzov, who had been riding round the position and on his way back to TatГЎrinova had stopped where the service was being held. Pierre recognized him at once by his peculiar figure, which distinguished him from everybody else. With a long overcoat on his exceedingly stout, round-shouldered body, with uncovered white head and puffy face showing the white ball of the eye he had lost, KutГәzov walked with plunging, swaying gait into the crowd and stopped behind the priest. He crossed himself with an accustomed movement, bent till he touched the ground with his hand, and bowed his white head with a deep sigh. Behind KutГәzov was Bennigsen and the suite. Despite the presence of the commander in chief, who attracted the attention of all the superior officers, the militiamen and soldiers continued their prayers without looking at him. When the service was over, KutГәzov stepped up to the icon, sank heavily to his knees, bowed to the ground, and for a long time tried vainly to rise, but could not do so on account of his weakness and weight. His white head twitched with the effort. At last he rose, kissed the icon as a child does with naively pouting lips, and again bowed till he touched the ground with his hand. The other generals followed his example, then the officers, and after them with excited faces, pressing on one another, crowding, panting, and pushing, scrambled the soldiers and militiamen. XXII XXII Staggering amid the crush, Pierre looked about him. вҖңCount Pyotr KirГӯlych! How did you get here?вҖқ said a voice. Pierre looked round. BorГӯs DrubetskГіy, brushing his knees with his hand (he had probably soiled them when he, too, had knelt before the icon), came up to him smiling. BorГӯs was elegantly dressed, with a slightly martial touch appropriate to a campaign. He wore a long coat and like KutГәzov had a whip slung across his shoulder. Meanwhile KutГәzov had reached the village and seated himself in the shade of the nearest house, on a bench which one Cossack had run to fetch and another had hastily covered with a rug. An immense and brilliant suite surrounded him. The icon was carried further, accompanied by the throng. Pierre stopped some thirty paces from KutГәzov, talking to BorГӯs. He explained his wish to be present at the battle and to see the position. вҖңThis is what you must do,вҖқ said BorГӯs. вҖңI will do the honors of the camp to you. You will see everything best from where Count Bennigsen will be. I am in attendance on him, you know; IвҖҷll mention it to him. But if you want to ride round the position, come along with us. We are just going to the left flank. Then when we get back, do spend the night with me and weвҖҷll arrange a game of cards. Of course you know DmГӯtri SergГ©evich? Those are his quarters,вҖқ and he pointed to the third house in the village of GГіrki. вҖңBut I should like to see the right flank. They say itвҖҷs very strong,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңI should like to start from the MoskvГЎ River and ride round the whole position.вҖқ вҖңWell, you can do that later, but the chief thing is the left flank.вҖқ вҖңYes, yes. But where is Prince BolkГіnskiвҖҷs regiment? Can you point it out to me?вҖқ вҖңAndrГ©y NikolГЎevichвҖҷs? We shall pass it and IвҖҷll take you to him.вҖқ вҖңWhat about the left flank?вҖқ asked Pierre. вҖңTo tell you the truth, between ourselves, God only knows what state our left flank is in,вҖқ said BorГӯs confidentially lowering his voice. вҖңIt is not at all what Count Bennigsen intended. He meant to fortify that knoll quite differently, butвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ BorГӯs shrugged his shoulders, вҖңhis Serene Highness would not have it, or someone persuaded him. You seeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ but BorГӯs did not finish, for at that moment KaysГЎrov, KutГәzovвҖҷs adjutant, came up to Pierre. вҖңAh, PaГӯsy SergГ©itch!вҖқ said BorГӯs, addressing him with an unembarrassed smile, вҖңI was just trying to explain our position to the count. It is amazing how his Serene Highness could so foresee the intentions of the French!вҖқ вҖңYou mean the left flank?вҖқ asked KaysГЎrov. вҖңYes, exactly; the left flank is now extremely strong.вҖқ Though KutГәzov had dismissed all unnecessary men from the staff, BorГӯs had contrived to remain at headquarters after the changes. He had established himself with Count Bennigsen, who, like all on whom BorГӯs had been in attendance, considered young Prince DrubetskГіy an invaluable man. In the higher command there were two sharply defined parties: KutГәzovвҖҷs party and that of Bennigsen, the chief of staff. BorГӯs belonged to the latter and no one else, while showing servile respect to KutГәzov, could so create an impression that the old fellow was not much good and that Bennigsen managed everything. Now the decisive moment of battle had come when KutГәzov would be destroyed and the power pass to Bennigsen, or even if KutГәzov won the battle it would be felt that everything was done by Bennigsen. In any case many great rewards would have to be given for tomorrowвҖҷs action, and new men would come to the front. So BorГӯs was full of nervous vivacity all day. After KaysГЎrov, others whom Pierre knew came up to him, and he had not time to reply to all the questions about Moscow that were showered upon him, or to listen to all that was told him. The faces all expressed animation and apprehension, but it seemed to Pierre that the cause of the excitement shown in some of these faces lay chiefly in questions of personal success; his mind, however, was occupied by the different expression he saw on other facesвҒ вҖ”an expression that spoke not of personal matters but of the universal questions of life and death. KutГәzov noticed PierreвҖҷs figure and the group gathered round him. вҖңCall him to me,вҖқ said KutГәzov. An adjutant told Pierre of his Serene HighnessвҖҷ wish, and Pierre went toward KutГәzovвҖҷs bench. But a militiaman got there before him. It was DГіlokhov. вҖңHow did that fellow get here?вҖқ asked Pierre. вҖңHeвҖҷs a creature that wriggles in anywhere!вҖқ was the answer. вҖңHe has been degraded, you know. Now he wants to bob up again. HeвҖҷs been proposing some scheme or other and has crawled into the enemyвҖҷs picket line at night.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ HeвҖҷs a brave fellow.вҖқ Pierre took off his hat and bowed respectfully to KutГәzov. вҖңI concluded that if I reported to your Serene Highness you might send me away or say that you knew what I was reporting, but then I shouldnвҖҷt lose anythingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ DГіlokhov was saying. вҖңYes, yes.вҖқ вҖңBut if I were right, I should be rendering a service to my Fatherland for which I am ready to die.вҖқ вҖңYes, yes.вҖқ вҖңAnd should your Serene Highness require a man who will not spare his skin, please think of me.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Perhaps I may prove useful to your Serene Highness.вҖқ вҖңYesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ YesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ KutГәzov repeated, his laughing eye narrowing more and more as he looked at Pierre. Just then BorГӯs, with his courtierlike adroitness, stepped up to PierreвҖҷs side near KutГәzov and in a most natural manner, without raising his voice, said to Pierre, as though continuing an interrupted conversation: вҖңThe militia have put on clean white shirts to be ready to die. What heroism, Count!вҖқ BorГӯs evidently said this to Pierre in order to be overheard by his Serene Highness. He knew KutГәzovвҖҷs attention would be caught by those words, and so it was. вҖңWhat are you saying about the militia?вҖқ he asked BorГӯs. вҖңPreparing for tomorrow, your Serene HighnessвҒ вҖ”for deathвҒ вҖ”they have put on clean shirts.вҖқ вҖңAhвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ a wonderful, a matchless people!вҖқ said KutГәzov; and he closed his eyes and swayed his head. вҖңA matchless people!вҖқ he repeated with a sigh. вҖңSo you want to smell gunpowder?вҖқ he said to Pierre. вҖңYes, itвҖҷs a pleasant smell. I have the honor to be one of your wifeвҖҷs adorers. Is she well? My quarters are at your service.вҖқ And as often happens with old people, KutГәzov began looking about absentmindedly as if forgetting all he wanted to say or do. Then, evidently remembering what he wanted, he beckoned to AndrГ©y SergГ©itch KaysГЎrov, his adjutantвҖҷs brother. вҖңThose versesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ those verses of MГЎrinвҖҷsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ how do they go, eh? Those he wrote about GerГЎkov: вҖҳLectures for the corps inditingвҖҷвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Recite them, recite them!вҖқ said he, evidently preparing to laugh. KaysГЎrov recited.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ KutГәzov smilingly nodded his head to the rhythm of the verses. When Pierre had left KutГәzov, DГіlokhov came up to him and took his hand. вҖңI am very glad to meet you here, Count,вҖқ he said aloud, regardless of the presence of strangers and in a particularly resolute and solemn tone. вҖңOn the eve of a day when God alone knows who of us is fated to survive, I am glad of this opportunity to tell you that I regret the misunderstandings that occurred between us and should wish you not to have any ill feeling for me. I beg you to forgive me.вҖқ Pierre looked at DГіlokhov with a smile, not knowing what to say to him. With tears in his eyes DГіlokhov embraced Pierre and kissed him. BorГӯs said a few words to his general, and Count Bennigsen turned to Pierre and proposed that he should ride with him along the line. вҖңIt will interest you,вҖқ said he. вҖңYes, very much,вҖқ replied Pierre. Half an hour later KutГәzov left for TatГЎrinova, and Bennigsen and his suite, with Pierre among them, set out on their ride along the line. XXIII XXIII From GГіrki, Bennigsen descended the high road to the bridge which, when they had looked at it from the hill, the officer had pointed out as being the center of our position and where rows of fragrant new-mown hay lay by the riverside. They rode across that bridge into the village of BorodinГі and thence turned to the left, passing an enormous number of troops and guns, and came to a high knoll where militiamen were digging. This was the redoubt, as yet unnamed, which afterwards became known as the RaГ©vski Redoubt, or the Knoll Battery, but Pierre paid no special attention to it. He did not know that it would become more memorable to him than any other spot on the plain of BorodinГі. They then crossed the hollow to SemГ«novsk, where the soldiers were dragging away the last logs from the huts and barns. Then they rode downhill and uphill, across a ryefield trodden and beaten down as if by hail, following a track freshly made by the artillery over the furrows of the plowed land, and reached some flГЁches91 which were still being dug. At the flГЁches Bennigsen stopped and began looking at the ShevГЎrdino Redoubt opposite, which had been ours the day before and where several horsemen could be descried. The officers said that either Napoleon or Murat was there, and they all gazed eagerly at this little group of horsemen. Pierre also looked at them, trying to guess which of the scarcely discernible figures was Napoleon. At last those mounted men rode away from the mound and disappeared. Bennigsen spoke to a general who approached him, and began explaining the whole position of our troops. Pierre listened to him, straining each faculty to understand the essential points of the impending battle, but was mortified to feel that his mental capacity was inadequate for the task. He could make nothing of it. Bennigsen stopped speaking and, noticing that Pierre was listening, suddenly said to him: вҖңI donвҖҷt think this interests you?вҖқ вҖңOn the contrary itвҖҷs very interesting!вҖқ replied Pierre not quite truthfully. From the flГЁches they rode still farther to the left, along a road winding through a thick, low-growing birch wood. In the middle of the wood a brown hare with white feet sprang out and, scared by the tramp of the many horses, grew so confused that it leaped along the road in front of them for some time, arousing general attention and laughter, and only when several voices shouted at it did it dart to one side and disappear in the thicket. After going through the wood for about a mile and a half they came out on a glade where troops of TГәchkovвҖҷs corps were stationed to defend the left flank. Here, at the extreme left flank, Bennigsen talked a great deal and with much heat, and, as it seemed to Pierre, gave orders of great military importance. In front of TГәchkovвҖҷs troops was some high ground not occupied by troops. Bennigsen loudly criticized this mistake, saying that it was madness to leave a height which commanded the country around unoccupied and to place troops below it. Some of the generals expressed the same opinion. One in particular declared with martial heat that they were put there to be slaughtered. Bennigsen on his own authority ordered the troops to occupy the high ground. This disposition on the left flank increased PierreвҖҷs doubt of his own capacity to understand military matters. Listening to Bennigsen and the generals criticizing the position of the troops behind the hill, he quite understood them and shared their opinion, but for that very reason he could not understand how the man who put them there behind the hill could have made so gross and palpable a blunder. Pierre did not know that these troops were not, as Bennigsen supposed, put there to defend the position, but were in a concealed position as an ambush, that they should not be seen and might be able to strike an approaching enemy unexpectedly. Bennigsen did not know this and moved the troops forward according to his own ideas without mentioning the matter to the commander in chief. XXIV XXIV On that bright evening of August 25, Prince AndrГ©y lay leaning on his elbow in a broken-down shed in the village of KnyazkГіvo at the further end of his regimentвҖҷs encampment. Through a gap in the broken wall he could see, beside the wooden fence, a row of thirty-year-old birches with their lower branches lopped off, a field on which shocks of oats were standing, and some bushes near which rose the smoke of campfiresвҒ вҖ”the soldiersвҖҷ kitchens. Narrow and burdensome and useless to anyone as his life now seemed to him, Prince AndrГ©y on the eve of battle felt agitated and irritable as he had done seven years before at Austerlitz. He had received and given the orders for next dayвҖҷs battle and had nothing more to do. But his thoughtsвҒ вҖ”the simplest, clearest, and therefore most terrible thoughtsвҒ вҖ”would give him no peace. He knew that tomorrowвҖҷs battle would be the most terrible of all he had taken part in, and for the first time in his life the possibility of death presented itself to himвҒ вҖ”not in relation to any worldly matter or with reference to its effect on others, but simply in relation to himself, to his own soulвҒ вҖ”vividly, plainly, terribly, and almost as a certainty. And from the height of this perception all that had previously tormented and preoccupied him suddenly became illumined by a cold white light without shadows, without perspective, without distinction of outline. All life appeared to him like magic-lantern pictures at which he had long been gazing by artificial light through a glass. Now he suddenly saw those badly daubed pictures in clear daylight and without a glass. вҖңYes, yes! There they are, those false images that agitated, enraptured, and tormented me,вҖқ said he to himself, passing in review the principal pictures of the magic lantern of life and regarding them now in the cold white daylight of his clear perception of death. вҖңThere they are, those rudely painted figures that once seemed splendid and mysterious. Glory, the good of society, love of a woman, the Fatherland itselfвҒ вҖ”how important these pictures appeared to me, with what profound meaning they seemed to be filled! And it is all so simple, pale, and crude in the cold white light of this morning which I feel is dawning for me.вҖқ The three great sorrows of his life held his attention in particular: his love for a woman, his fatherвҖҷs death, and the French invasion which had overrun half Russia. вҖңLoveвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ that little girl who seemed to me brimming over with mystic forces! Yes, indeed, I loved her. I made romantic plans of love and happiness with her! Oh, what a boy I was!вҖқ he said aloud bitterly. вҖңAh me! I believed in some ideal love which was to keep her faithful to me for the whole year of my absence! Like the gentle dove in the fable she was to pine apart from me.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But it was much simpler really.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ It was all very simple and horrible.вҖқ вҖңWhen my father built Bald Hills he thought the place was his: his land, his air, his peasants. But Napoleon came and swept him aside, unconscious of his existence, as he might brush a chip from his path, and his Bald Hills and his whole life fell to pieces. Princess MГЎrya says it is a trial sent from above. What is the trial for, when he is not here and will never return? He is not here! For whom then is the trial intended? The Fatherland, the destruction of Moscow! And tomorrow I shall be killed, perhaps not even by a Frenchman but by one of our own men, by a soldier discharging a musket close to my ear as one of them did yesterday, and the French will come and take me by head and heels and fling me into a hole that I may not stink under their noses, and new conditions of life will arise, which will seem quite ordinary to others and about which I shall know nothing. I shall not exist.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He looked at the row of birches shining in the sunshine, with their motionless green and yellow foliage and white bark. вҖңTo dieвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ to be killed tomorrowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ That I should not existвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ That all this should still be, but no me.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ And the birches with their light and shade, the curly clouds, the smoke of the campfires, and all that was around him changed and seemed terrible and menacing. A cold shiver ran down his spine. He rose quickly, went out of the shed, and began to walk about. After he had returned, voices were heard outside the shed. вҖңWhoвҖҷs that?вҖқ he cried. The red-nosed Captain TimГіkhin, formerly DГіlokhovвҖҷs squadron commander, but now from lack of officers a battalion commander, shyly entered the shed followed by an adjutant and the regimental paymaster. Prince AndrГ©y rose hastily, listened to the business they had come about, gave them some further instructions, and was about to dismiss them when he heard a familiar, lisping, voice behind the shed. вҖңDevil take it!вҖқ said the voice of a man stumbling over something. Prince AndrГ©y looked out of the shed and saw Pierre, who had tripped over a pole on the ground and had nearly fallen, coming his way. It was unpleasant to Prince AndrГ©y to meet people of his own set in general, and Pierre especially, for he reminded him of all the painful moments of his last visit to Moscow. вҖңYou? What a surprise!вҖқ said he. вҖңWhat brings you here? This is unexpected!вҖқ As he said this his eyes and face expressed more than coldnessвҒ вҖ”they expressed hostility, which Pierre noticed at once. He had approached the shed full of animation, but on seeing Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs face he felt constrained and ill at ease. вҖңI have comeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ simplyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ you knowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ comeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ it interests me,вҖқ said Pierre, who had so often that day senselessly repeated that word вҖңinteresting.вҖқ вҖңI wish to see the battle.вҖқ вҖңOh yes, and what do the Masonic brothers say about war? How would they stop it?вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y sarcastically. вҖңWell, and howвҖҷs Moscow? And my people? Have they reached Moscow at last?вҖқ he asked seriously. вҖңYes, they have. Julie DrubetskГЎya told me so. I went to see them, but missed them. They have gone to your estate near Moscow.вҖқ XXV XXV The officers were about to take leave, but Prince AndrГ©y, apparently reluctant to be left alone with his friend, asked them to stay and have tea. Seats were brought in and so was the tea. The officers gazed with surprise at PierreвҖҷs huge stout figure and listened to his talk of Moscow and the position of our army, round which he had ridden. Prince AndrГ©y remained silent, and his expression was so forbidding that Pierre addressed his remarks chiefly to the good-natured battalion commander. вҖңSo you understand the whole position of our troops?вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y interrupted him. вҖңYesвҒ вҖ”that is, how do you mean?вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңNot being a military man I canвҖҷt say I have understood it fully, but I understand the general position.вҖқ вҖңWell, then, you know more than anyone else, be it who it may,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңOh!вҖқ said Pierre, looking over his spectacles in perplexity at Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңWell, and what do you think of KutГәzovвҖҷs appointment?вҖқ he asked. вҖңI was very glad of his appointment, thatвҖҷs all I know,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңAnd tell me your opinion of Barclay de Tolly. In Moscow they are saying heaven knows what about him.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What do you think of him?вҖқ вҖңAsk them,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y, indicating the officers. Pierre looked at TimГіkhin with the condescendingly interrogative smile with which everybody involuntarily addressed that officer. вҖңWe see light again, since his Serenity has been appointed, your excellency,вҖқ said TimГіkhin timidly, and continually turning to glance at his colonel. вҖңWhy so?вҖқ asked Pierre. вҖңWell, to mention only firewood and fodder, let me inform you. Why, when we were retreating from SventsyГЎni we dare not touch a stick or a wisp of hay or anything. You see, we were going away, so he would get it all; wasnвҖҷt it so, your excellency?вҖқ and again TimГіkhin turned to the prince. вҖңBut we darenвҖҷt. In our regiment two officers were court-martialed for that kind of thing. But when his Serenity took command everything became straightforward. Now we see light.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңThen why was it forbidden?вҖқ TimГіkhin looked about in confusion, not knowing what or how to answer such a question. Pierre put the same question to Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңWhy, so as not to lay waste the country we were abandoning to the enemy,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y with venomous irony. вҖңIt is very sound: one canвҖҷt permit the land to be pillaged and accustom the troops to marauding. At SmolГ©nsk too he judged correctly that the French might outflank us, as they had larger forces. But he could not understand this,вҖқ cried Prince AndrГ©y in a shrill voice that seemed to escape him involuntarily: вҖңhe could not understand that there, for the first time, we were fighting for Russian soil, and that there was a spirit in the men such as I had never seen before, that we had held the French for two days, and that that success had increased our strength tenfold. He ordered us to retreat, and all our efforts and losses went for nothing. He had no thought of betraying us, he tried to do the best he could, he thought out everything, and that is why he is unsuitable. He is unsuitable now, just because he plans out everything very thoroughly and accurately as every German has to. How can I explain?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, say your father has a German valet, and he is a splendid valet and satisfies your fatherвҖҷs requirements better than you could, then itвҖҷs all right to let him serve. But if your father is mortally sick youвҖҷll send the valet away and attend to your father with your own unpracticed, awkward hands, and will soothe him better than a skilled man who is a stranger could. So it has been with Barclay. While Russia was well, a foreigner could serve her and be a splendid minister; but as soon as she is in danger she needs one of her own kin. But in your Club they have been making him out a traitor! They slander him as a traitor, and the only result will be that afterwards, ashamed of their false accusations, they will make him out a hero or a genius instead of a traitor, and that will be still more unjust. He is an honest and very punctilious German.вҖқ вҖңAnd they say heвҖҷs a skillful commander,вҖқ rejoined Pierre. вҖңI donвҖҷt understand what is meant by вҖҳa skillful commander,вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y ironically. вҖңA skillful commander?вҖқ replied Pierre. вҖңWhy, one who foresees all contingenciesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and foresees the adversaryвҖҷs intentions.вҖқ вҖңBut thatвҖҷs impossible,вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y as if it were a matter settled long ago. Pierre looked at him in surprise. вҖңAnd yet they say that war is like a game of chess?вҖқ he remarked. вҖңYes,вҖқ replied Prince AndrГ©y, вҖңbut with this little difference, that in chess you may think over each move as long as you please and are not limited for time, and with this difference too, that a knight is always stronger than a pawn, and two pawns are always stronger than one, while in war a battalion is sometimes stronger than a division and sometimes weaker than a company. The relative strength of bodies of troops can never be known to anyone. Believe me,вҖқ he went on, вҖңif things depended on arrangements made by the staff, I should be there making arrangements, but instead of that I have the honor to serve here in the regiment with these gentlemen, and I consider that on us tomorrowвҖҷs battle will depend and not on those others.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Success never depends, and never will depend, on position, or equipment, or even on numbers, and least of all on position.вҖқ вҖңBut on what then?вҖқ вҖңOn the feeling that is in me and in him,вҖқ he pointed to TimГіkhin, вҖңand in each soldier.вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y glanced at TimГіkhin, who looked at his commander in alarm and bewilderment. In contrast to his former reticent taciturnity Prince AndrГ©y now seemed excited. He could apparently not refrain from expressing the thoughts that had suddenly occurred to him. вҖңA battle is won by those who firmly resolve to win it! Why did we lose the battle at Austerlitz? The French losses were almost equal to ours, but very early we said to ourselves that we were losing the battle, and we did lose it. And we said so because we had nothing to fight for there, we wanted to get away from the battlefield as soon as we could. вҖҳWeвҖҷve lost, so let us run,вҖҷ and we ran. If we had not said that till the evening, heaven knows what might not have happened. But tomorrow we shanвҖҷt say it! You talk about our position, the left flank weak and the right flank too extended,вҖқ he went on. вҖңThatвҖҷs all nonsense, thereвҖҷs nothing of the kind. But what awaits us tomorrow? A hundred million most diverse chances which will be decided on the instant by the fact that our men or theirs run or do not run, and that this man or that man is killed, but all that is being done at present is only play. The fact is that those men with whom you have ridden round the position not only do not help matters, but hinder. They are only concerned with their own petty interests.вҖқ вҖңAt such a moment?вҖқ said Pierre reproachfully. вҖңAt such a moment!вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y repeated. вҖңTo them it is only a moment affording opportunities to undermine a rival and obtain an extra cross or ribbon. For me tomorrow means this: a Russian army of a hundred thousand and a French army of a hundred thousand have met to fight, and the thing is that these two hundred thousand men will fight and the side that fights more fiercely and spares itself least will win. And if you like I will tell you that whatever happens and whatever muddles those at the top may make, we shall win tomorrowвҖҷs battle. Tomorrow, happen what may, we shall win!вҖқ вҖңThere now, your excellency! ThatвҖҷs the truth, the real truth,вҖқ said TimГіkhin. вҖңWho would spare himself now? The soldiers in my battalion, believe me, wouldnвҖҷt drink their vodka! вҖҳItвҖҷs not the day for that!вҖҷ they say.вҖқ All were silent. The officers rose. Prince AndrГ©y went out of the shed with them, giving final orders to the adjutant. After they had gone Pierre approached Prince AndrГ©y and was about to start a conversation when they heard the clatter of three horsesвҖҷ hoofs on the road not far from the shed, and looking in that direction Prince AndrГ©y recognized Wolzogen and Clausewitz accompanied by a Cossack. They rode close by continuing to converse, and Prince AndrГ©y involuntarily heard these words: вҖңDer Krieg muss in Raum verlegt werden. Der Ansicht kann ich nicht genug Preis geben,вҖқ92 said one of them. вҖңOh, ja,вҖқ said the other, вҖңder Zweck ist nur den Feind zu schwГӨchen, so kann man gewiss nicht den Verlust der Privat-Personen in Achtung nehmen.вҖқ93 вҖңOh, no,вҖқ agreed the other. вҖңExtend widely!вҖқ said Prince AndrГ©y with an angry snort, when they had ridden past. вҖңIn that вҖҳextendвҖҷ were my father, son, and sister, at Bald Hills. ThatвҖҷs all the same to him! ThatвҖҷs what I was saying to youвҒ вҖ”those German gentlemen wonвҖҷt win the battle tomorrow but will only make all the mess they can, because they have nothing in their German heads but theories not worth an empty eggshell and havenвҖҷt in their hearts the one thing needed tomorrowвҒ вҖ”that which TimГіkhin has. They have yielded up all Europe to him, and have now come to teach us. Fine teachers!вҖқ and again his voice grew shrill. вҖңSo you think we shall win tomorrowвҖҷs battle?вҖқ asked Pierre. вҖңYes, yes,вҖқ answered Prince AndrГ©y absently. вҖңOne thing I would do if I had the power,вҖқ he began again, вҖңI would not take prisoners. Why take prisoners? ItвҖҷs chivalry! The French have destroyed my home and are on their way to destroy Moscow, they have outraged and are outraging me every moment. They are my enemies. In my opinion they are all criminals. And so thinks TimГіkhin and the whole army. They should be executed! Since they are my foes they cannot be my friends, whatever may have been said at Tilsit.вҖқ вҖңYes, yes,вҖқ muttered Pierre, looking with shining eyes at Prince AndrГ©y. вҖңI quite agree with you!вҖқ The question that had perturbed Pierre on the MozhГЎysk hill and all that day now seemed to him quite clear and completely solved. He now understood the whole meaning and importance of this war and of the impending battle. All he had seen that day, all the significant and stern expressions on the faces he had seen in passing, were lit up for him by a new light. He understood that latent heat (as they say in physics) of patriotism which was present in all these men he had seen, and this explained to him why they all prepared for death calmly, and as it were lightheartedly. вҖңNot take prisoners,вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y continued: вҖңThat by itself would quite change the whole war and make it less cruel. As it is we have played at warвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs whatвҖҷs vile! We play at magnanimity and all that stuff. Such magnanimity and sensibility are like the magnanimity and sensibility of a lady who faints when she sees a calf being killed: she is so kindhearted that she canвҖҷt look at blood, but enjoys eating the calf served up with sauce. They talk to us of the rules of war, of chivalry, of flags of truce, of mercy to the unfortunate and so on. ItвҖҷs all rubbish! I saw chivalry and flags of truce in 1805; they humbugged us and we humbugged them.
