Almost all day long the house resounded with their running feet, their cries, and their spontaneous laughter. They laughed and were gay not because there was any reason to laugh, but because gaiety and mirth were in their hearts and so everything that happened was a cause for gaiety and laughter to them. PГ©tya was in high spirits because having left home a boy he had returned (as everybody told him) a fine young man, because he was at home, because he had left BГ©laya TsГ©rkov where there was no hope of soon taking part in a battle and had come to Moscow where there was to be fighting in a few days, and chiefly because NatГЎsha, whose lead he always followed, was in high spirits. NatГЎsha was gay because she had been sad too long and now nothing reminded her of the cause of her sadness, and because she was feeling well. She was also happy because she had someone to adore her: the adoration of others was a lubricant the wheels of her machine needed to make them run freelyвҒ вҖ”and PГ©tya adored her. Above all, they were gay because there was a war near Moscow, there would be fighting at the town gates, arms were being given out, everybody was escapingвҒ вҖ”going away somewhere, and in general something extraordinary was happening, and that is always exciting, especially to the young. XIII XIII On Saturday, the thirty-first of August, everything in the RostГіvsвҖҷ house seemed topsy-turvy. All the doors were open, all the furniture was being carried out or moved about, and the mirrors and pictures had been taken down. There were trunks in the rooms, and hay, wrapping paper, and ropes were scattered about. The peasants and house serfs carrying out the things were treading heavily on the parquet floors. The yard was crowded with peasant carts, some loaded high and already corded up, others still empty. The voices and footsteps of the many servants and of the peasants who had come with the carts resounded as they shouted to one another in the yard and in the house. The count had been out since morning. The countess had a headache brought on by all the noise and turmoil and was lying down in the new sitting room with a vinegar compress on her head. PГ©tya was not at home, he had gone to visit a friend with whom he meant to obtain a transfer from the militia to the active army. SГіnya was in the ballroom looking after the packing of the glass and china. NatГЎsha was sitting on the floor of her dismantled room with dresses, ribbons, and scarves strewn all about her, gazing fixedly at the floor and holding in her hands the old ball dress (already out of fashion) which she had worn at her first Petersburg ball. NatГЎsha was ashamed of doing nothing when everyone else was so busy, and several times that morning had tried to set to work, but her heart was not in it, and she could not and did not know how to do anything except with all her heart and all her might. For a while she had stood beside SГіnya while the china was being packed and tried to help, but soon gave it up and went to her room to pack her own things. At first she found it amusing to give away dresses and ribbons to the maids, but when that was done and what was left had still to be packed, she found it dull. вҖңDunyГЎsha, you pack! You will, wonвҖҷt you, dear?вҖқ And when DunyГЎsha willingly promised to do it all for her, NatГЎsha sat down on the floor, took her old ball dress, and fell into a reverie quite unrelated to what ought to have occupied her thoughts now. She was roused from her reverie by the talk of the maids in the next room (which was theirs) and by the sound of their hurried footsteps going to the back porch. NatГЎsha got up and looked out of the window. An enormously long row of carts full of wounded men had stopped in the street. The housekeeper, the old nurse, the cooks, coachmen, maids, footmen, postilions, and scullions stood at the gate, staring at the wounded. NatГЎsha, throwing a clean pocket handkerchief over her hair and holding an end of it in each hand, went out into the street. The former housekeeper, old MГЎvra KuzmГӯnichna, had stepped out of the crowd by the gate, gone up to a cart with a hood constructed of bast mats, and was speaking to a pale young officer who lay inside. NatГЎsha moved a few steps forward and stopped shyly, still holding her handkerchief, and listened to what the housekeeper was saying. вҖңThen you have nobody in Moscow?вҖқ she was saying. вҖңYou would be more comfortable somewhere in a houseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ in ours, for instanceвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the family are leaving.вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt know if it would be allowed,вҖқ replied the officer in a weak voice. вҖңHere is our commanding officerвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ask him,вҖқ and he pointed to a stout major who was walking back along the street past the row of carts. NatГЎsha glanced with frightened eyes at the face of the wounded officer and at once went to meet the major. вҖңMay the wounded men stay in our house?вҖқ she asked. The major raised his hand to his cap with a smile. вҖңWhich one do you want, MaвҖҷamвҖҷselle?вҖқ said he, screwing up his eyes and smiling. NatГЎsha quietly repeated her question, and her face and whole manner were so serious, though she was still holding the ends of her handkerchief, that the major ceased smiling and after some reflectionвҒ вҖ”as if considering in how far the thing was possibleвҒ вҖ”replied in the affirmative. вҖңOh yes, why not? They may,вҖқ he said. With a slight inclination of her head, NatГЎsha stepped back quickly to MГЎvra KuzmГӯnichna, who stood talking compassionately to the officer. вҖңThey may. He says they may!вҖқ whispered NatГЎsha. The cart in which the officer lay was turned into the RostГіvsвҖҷ yard, and dozens of carts with wounded men began at the invitation of the townsfolk to turn into the yards and to draw up at the entrances of the houses in PovarskГЎya Street. NatГЎsha was evidently pleased to be dealing with new people outside the ordinary routine of her life. She and MГЎvra KuzmГӯnichna tried to get as many of the wounded as possible into their yard. вҖңYour Papa must be told, though,вҖқ said MГЎvra KuzmГӯnichna. вҖңNever mind, never mind, what does it matter? For one day we can move into the drawing room. They can have all our half of the house.вҖқ вҖңThere now, young lady, you do take things into your head! Even if we put them into the wing, the menвҖҷs room, or the nurseвҖҷs room, we must ask permission.вҖқ вҖңWell, IвҖҷll ask.вҖқ NatГЎsha ran into the house and went on tiptoe through the half-open door into the sitting room, where there was a smell of vinegar and HoffmanвҖҷs drops. вҖңAre you asleep, Mamma?вҖқ вҖңOh, what sleepвҒ вҖ”?вҖқ said the countess, waking up just as she was dropping into a doze. вҖңMamma darling!вҖқ said NatГЎsha, kneeling by her mother and bringing her face close to her motherвҖҷs, вҖңI am sorry, forgive me, IвҖҷll never do it again; I woke you up! MГЎvra KuzmГӯnichna has sent me: they have brought some wounded hereвҒ вҖ”officers. Will you let them come? They have nowhere to go. I knew youвҖҷd let them come!вҖқ she said quickly all in one breath. вҖңWhat officers? Whom have they brought? I donвҖҷt understand anything about it,вҖқ said the countess. NatГЎsha laughed, and the countess too smiled slightly. вҖңI knew youвҖҷd give permissionвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ so IвҖҷll tell them,вҖқ and, having kissed her mother, NatГЎsha got up and went to the door. In the hall she met her father, who had returned with bad news. вҖңWeвҖҷve stayed too long!вҖқ said the count with involuntary vexation. вҖңThe Club is closed and the police are leaving.вҖқ вҖңPapa, is it all rightвҒ вҖ”IвҖҷve invited some of the wounded into the house?вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңOf course it is,вҖқ he answered absently. вҖңThatвҖҷs not the point. I beg you not to indulge in trifles now, but to help to pack, and tomorrow we must go, go, go!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ And the count gave a similar order to the majordomo and the servants. At dinner PГ©tya having returned home told them the news he had heard. He said the people had been getting arms in the KrГ©mlin, and that though RostopchГӯnвҖҷs broadsheet had said that he would sound a call two or three days in advance, the order had certainly already been given for everyone to go armed to the Three Hills tomorrow, and that there would be a big battle there. The countess looked with timid horror at her sonвҖҷs eager, excited face as he said this. She realized that if she said a word about his not going to the battle (she knew he enjoyed the thought of the impending engagement) he would say something about men, honor, and the fatherlandвҒ вҖ”something senseless, masculine, and obstinate which there would be no contradicting, and her plans would be spoiled; and so, hoping to arrange to leave before then and take PГ©tya with her as their protector and defender, she did not answer him, but after dinner called the count aside and implored him with tears to take her away quickly, that very night if possible. With a womanвҖҷs involuntary loving cunning she, who till then had not shown any alarm, said that she would die of fright if they did not leave that very night. Without any pretense she was now afraid of everything. XIV XIV Madame Schoss, who had been out to visit her daughter, increased the countessвҖҷ fears still more by telling what she had seen at a spirit dealerвҖҷs in MyasnГӯtski Street. When returning by that street she had been unable to pass because of a drunken crowd rioting in front of the shop. She had taken a cab and driven home by a side street and the cabman had told her that the people were breaking open the barrels at the drink store, having received orders to do so. After dinner the whole RostГіv household set to work with enthusiastic haste packing their belongings and preparing for their departure. The old count, suddenly setting to work, kept passing from the yard to the house and back again, shouting confused instructions to the hurrying people, and flurrying them still more. PГ©tya directed things in the yard. SГіnya, owing to the countвҖҷs contradictory orders, lost her head and did not know what to do. The servants ran noisily about the house and yard, shouting and disputing. NatГЎsha, with the ardor characteristic of all she did suddenly set to work too. At first her intervention in the business of packing was received skeptically. Everybody expected some prank from her and did not wish to obey her; but she resolutely and passionately demanded obedience, grew angry and nearly cried because they did not heed her, and at last succeeded in making them believe her. Her first exploit, which cost her immense effort and established her authority, was the packing of the carpets. The count had valuable Gobelin tapestries and Persian carpets in the house. When NatГЎsha set to work two cases were standing open in the ballroom, one almost full up with crockery, the other with carpets. There was also much china standing on the tables, and still more was being brought in from the storeroom. A third case was needed and servants had gone to fetch it. вҖңSГіnya, wait a bitвҒ вҖ”weвҖҷll pack everything into these,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңYou canвҖҷt, Miss, we have tried to,вҖқ said the butlerвҖҷs assistant. вҖңNo, wait a minute, please.вҖқ And NatГЎsha began rapidly taking out of the case dishes and plates wrapped in paper. вҖңThe dishes must go in here among the carpets,вҖқ said she. вҖңWhy, itвҖҷs a mercy if we can get the carpets alone into three cases,вҖқ said the butlerвҖҷs assistant. вҖңOh, wait, please!вҖқ And NatГЎsha began rapidly and deftly sorting out the things. вҖңThese arenвҖҷt needed,вҖқ said she, putting aside some plates of Kiev ware. вҖңTheseвҒ вҖ”yes, these must go among the carpets,вҖқ she said, referring to the Saxony china dishes. вҖңDonвҖҷt, NatГЎsha! Leave it alone! WeвҖҷll get it all packed,вҖқ urged SГіnya reproachfully. вҖңWhat a young lady she is!вҖқ remarked the majordomo. But NatГЎsha would not give in. She turned everything out and began quickly repacking, deciding that the inferior Russian carpets and unnecessary crockery should not be taken at all. When everything had been taken out of the cases, they recommenced packing, and it turned out that when the cheaper things not worth taking had nearly all been rejected, the valuable ones really did all go into the two cases. Only the lid of the case containing the carpets would not shut down. A few more things might have been taken out, but NatГЎsha insisted on having her own way. She packed, repacked, pressed, made the butlerвҖҷs assistant and PГ©tyaвҒ вҖ”whom she had drawn into the business of packingвҒ вҖ”press on the lid, and made desperate efforts herself. вҖңThatвҖҷs enough, NatГЎsha,вҖқ said SГіnya. вҖңI see you were right, but just take out the top one.вҖқ вҖңI wonвҖҷt!вҖқ cried NatГЎsha, with one hand holding back the hair that hung over her perspiring face, while with the other she pressed down the carpets. вҖңNow press, PГ©tya! Press, VasГӯlich, press hard!вҖқ she cried. The carpets yielded and the lid closed; NatГЎsha, clapping her hands, screamed with delight and tears fell from her eyes. But this only lasted a moment. She at once set to work afresh and they now trusted her completely. The count was not angry even when they told him that NatГЎlya IlynГӯchna had countermanded an order of his, and the servants now came to her to ask whether a cart was sufficiently loaded, and whether it might be corded up. Thanks to NatГЎshaвҖҷs directions the work now went on expeditiously, unnecessary things were left, and the most valuable packed as compactly as possible. But hard as they all worked till quite late that night, they could not get everything packed. The countess had fallen asleep and the count, having put off their departure till next morning, went to bed. SГіnya and NatГЎsha slept in the sitting room without undressing. That night another wounded man was driven down the PovarskГЎya, and MГЎvra KuzmГӯnichna, who was standing at the gate, had him brought into the RostГіvsвҖҷ yard. MГЎvra KuzmГӯnichna concluded that he was a very important man. He was being conveyed in a calГЁche with a raised hood, and was quite covered by an apron. On the box beside the driver sat a venerable old attendant. A doctor and two soldiers followed the carriage in a cart. вҖңPlease come in here. The masters are going away and the whole house will be empty,вҖқ said the old woman to the old attendant. вҖңWell, perhaps,вҖқ said he with a sigh. вҖңWe donвҖҷt expect to get him home alive! We have a house of our own in Moscow, but itвҖҷs a long way from here, and thereвҖҷs nobody living in it.вҖқ вҖңDo us the honor to come in, thereвҖҷs plenty of everything in the masterвҖҷs house. Come in,вҖқ said MГЎvra KuzmГӯnichna. вҖңIs he very ill?вҖқ she asked. The attendant made a hopeless gesture. вҖңWe donвҖҷt expect to get him home! We must ask the doctor.вҖқ And the old servant got down from the box and went up to the cart. вҖңAll right!вҖқ said the doctor. The old servant returned to the calГЁche, looked into it, shook his head disconsolately, told the driver to turn into the yard, and stopped beside MГЎvra KuzmГӯnichna. вҖңO, Lord Jesus Christ!вҖқ she murmured. She invited them to take the wounded man into the house. вҖңThe masters wonвҖҷt objectвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she said. But they had to avoid carrying the man upstairs, and so they took him into the wing and put him in the room that had been Madame SchossвҖҷ. This wounded man was Prince AndrГ©y BolkГіnski. XV XV MoscowвҖҷs last day had come. It was a clear bright autumn day, a Sunday. The church bells everywhere were ringing for service, just as usual on Sundays. Nobody seemed yet to realize what awaited the city. Only two things indicated the social condition of MoscowвҒ вҖ”the rabble, that is the poor people, and the price of commodities. An enormous crowd of factory hands, house serfs, and peasants, with whom some officials, seminarists, and gentry were mingled, had gone early that morning to the Three Hills. Having waited there for RostopchГӯn who did not turn up, they became convinced that Moscow would be surrendered, and then dispersed all about the town to the public houses and cookshops. Prices too that day indicated the state of affairs. The price of weapons, of gold, of carts and horses, kept rising, but the value of paper money and city articles kept falling, so that by midday there were instances of carters removing valuable goods, such as cloth, and receiving in payment a half of what they carted, while peasant horses were fetching five hundred rubles each, and furniture, mirrors, and bronzes were being given away for nothing. In the RostГіvsвҖҷ staid old-fashioned house the dissolution of former conditions of life was but little noticeable. As to the serfs the only indication was that three out of their huge retinue disappeared during the night, but nothing was stolen; and as to the value of their possessions, the thirty peasant carts that had come in from their estates and which many people envied proved to be extremely valuable and they were offered enormous sums of money for them. Not only were huge sums offered for the horses and carts, but on the previous evening and early in the morning of the first of September, orderlies and servants sent by wounded officers came to the RostГіvsвҖҷ and wounded men dragged themselves there from the RostГіvsвҖҷ and from neighboring houses where they were accommodated, entreating the servants to try to get them a lift out of Moscow. The majordomo to whom these entreaties were addressed, though he was sorry for the wounded, resolutely refused, saying that he dare not even mention the matter to the count. Pity these wounded men as one might, it was evident that if they were given one cart there would be no reason to refuse another, or all the carts and oneвҖҷs own carriages as well. Thirty carts could not save all the wounded and in the general catastrophe one could not disregard oneself and oneвҖҷs own family. So thought the majordomo on his masterвҖҷs behalf. On waking up that morning Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evich left his bedroom softly, so as not to wake the countess who had fallen asleep only toward morning, and came out to the porch in his lilac silk dressing gown. In the yard stood the carts ready corded. The carriages were at the front porch. The majordomo stood at the porch talking to an elderly orderly and to a pale young officer with a bandaged arm. On seeing the count the majordomo made a significant and stern gesture to them both to go away. вҖңWell, VasГӯlich, is everything ready?вҖқ asked the count, and stroking his bald head he looked good-naturedly at the officer and the orderly and nodded to them. (He liked to see new faces.) вҖңWe can harness at once, your excellency.вҖқ вҖңWell, thatвҖҷs right. As soon as the countess wakes weвҖҷll be off, God willing! What is it, gentlemen?вҖқ he added, turning to the officer. вҖңAre you staying in my house?вҖқ The officer came nearer and suddenly his face flushed crimson. вҖңCount, be so good as to allow meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ for GodвҖҷs sake, to get into some corner of one of your carts! I have nothing here with me.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I shall be all right on a loaded cart.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Before the officer had finished speaking the orderly made the same request on behalf of his master. вҖңOh, yes, yes, yes!вҖқ said the count hastily. вҖңI shall be very pleased, very pleased. VasГӯlich, youвҖҷll see to it. Just unload one or two carts. Well, what of itвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ do whatвҖҷs necessaryвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said the count, muttering some indefinite order. But at the same moment an expression of warm gratitude on the officerвҖҷs face had already sealed the order. The count looked around him. In the yard, at the gates, at the window of the wings, wounded officers and their orderlies were to be seen. They were all looking at the count and moving toward the porch. вҖңPlease step into the gallery, your excellency,вҖқ said the majordomo. вҖңWhat are your orders about the pictures?вҖқ The count went into the house with him, repeating his order not to refuse the wounded who asked for a lift. вҖңWell, never mind, some of the things can be unloaded,вҖқ he added in a soft, confidential voice, as though afraid of being overheard. At nine oвҖҷclock the countess woke up, and MatrГ«na TimofГ©evna, who had been her ladyвҖҷs maid before her marriage and now performed a sort of chief gendarmeвҖҷs duty for her, came to say that Madame Schoss was much offended and the young ladiesвҖҷ summer dresses could not be left behind. On inquiry, the countess learned that Madame Schoss was offended because her trunk had been taken down from its cart, and all the loads were being uncorded and the luggage taken out of the carts to make room for wounded men whom the count in the simplicity of his heart had ordered that they should take with them. The countess sent for her husband. вҖңWhat is this, my dear? I hear that the luggage is being unloaded.вҖқ вҖңYou know, love, I wanted to tell youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Countess dearвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ an officer came to me to ask for a few carts for the wounded. After all, ours are things that can be bought but think what being left behind means to them!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Really now, in our own yardвҒ вҖ”we asked them in ourselves and there are officers among them.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You know, I think, my dearвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ let them be takenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ whereвҖҷs the hurry?вҖқ The count spoke timidly, as he always did when talking of money matters. The countess was accustomed to this tone as a precursor of news of something detrimental to the childrenвҖҷs interests, such as the building of a new gallery or conservatory, the inauguration of a private theater or an orchestra. She was accustomed always to oppose anything announced in that timid tone and considered it her duty to do so. She assumed her dolefully submissive manner and said to her husband: вҖңListen to me, Count, you have managed matters so that we are getting nothing for the house, and now you wish to throw away all ourвҒ вҖ”all the childrenвҖҷs property! You said yourself that we have a hundred thousand rublesвҖҷ worth of things in the house. I donвҖҷt consent, my dear, I donвҖҷt! Do as you please! ItвҖҷs the governmentвҖҷs business to look after the wounded; they know that. Look at the LopukhГӯns opposite, they cleared out everything two days ago. ThatвҖҷs what other people do. ItвҖҷs only we who are such fools. If you have no pity on me, have some for the children.вҖқ Flourishing his arms in despair the count left the room without replying. вҖңPapa, what are you doing that for?вҖқ asked NatГЎsha, who had followed him into her motherвҖҷs room. вҖңNothing! What business is it of yours?вҖқ muttered the count angrily. вҖңBut I heard,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңWhy does Mamma object?вҖқ вҖңWhat business is it of yours?вҖқ cried the count. NatГЎsha stepped up to the window and pondered. вҖңPapa! HereвҖҷs Berg coming to see us,вҖқ said she, looking out of the window. XVI XVI Berg, the RostГіvsвҖҷ son-in-law, was already a colonel wearing the orders of VladГӯmir and Anna, and he still filled the quiet and agreeable post of assistant to the head of the staff of the assistant commander of the first division of the Second Army. On the first of September he had come to Moscow from the army. He had nothing to do in Moscow, but he had noticed that everyone in the army was asking for leave to visit Moscow and had something to do there. So he considered it necessary to ask for leave of absence for family and domestic reasons. Berg drove up to his father-in-lawвҖҷs house in his spruce little trap with a pair of sleek roans, exactly like those of a certain prince. He looked attentively at the carts in the yard and while going up to the porch took out a clean pocket handkerchief and tied a knot in it. From the anteroom Berg ran with smooth though impatient steps into the drawing room, where he embraced the count, kissed the hands of NatГЎsha and SГіnya, and hastened to inquire after вҖңMammaвҖҷsвҖқ health. вҖңHealth, at a time like this?вҖқ said the count. вҖңCome, tell us the news! Is the army retreating or will there be another battle?вҖқ вҖңGod Almighty alone can decide the fate of our fatherland, Papa,вҖқ said Berg. вҖңThe army is burning with a spirit of heroism and the leaders, so to say, have now assembled in council. No one knows what is coming. But in general I can tell you, Papa, that such a heroic spirit, the truly antique valor of the Russian army, which theyвҒ вҖ”which itвҖқ (he corrected himself) вҖңhas shown or displayed in the battle of the twenty-sixthвҒ вҖ”there are no words worthy to do it justice! I tell you, PapaвҖқ (he smote himself on the breast as a general he had heard speaking had done, but Berg did it a trifle late for he should have struck his breast at the words вҖңRussian armyвҖқ), вҖңI tell you frankly that we, the commanders, far from having to urge the men on or anything of that kind, could hardly restrain thoseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ thoseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ yes, those exploits of antique valor,вҖқ he went on rapidly. вҖңGeneral Barclay de Tolly risked his life everywhere at the head of the troops, I can assure you. Our corps was stationed on a hillside. You can imagine!вҖқ And Berg related all that he remembered of the various tales he had heard those days. NatГЎsha watched him with an intent gaze that confused him, as if she were trying to find in his face the answer to some question. вҖңAltogether such heroism as was displayed by the Russian warriors cannot be imagined or adequately praised!вҖқ said Berg, glancing round at NatГЎsha, and as if anxious to conciliate her, replying to her intent look with a smile. вҖңвҖҠвҖҳRussia is not in Moscow, she lives in the hearts of her sons!вҖҷ IsnвҖҷt it so, Papa?вҖқ said he. Just then the countess came in from the sitting room with a weary and dissatisfied expression. Berg hurriedly jumped up, kissed her hand, asked about her health, and, swaying his head from side to side to express sympathy, remained standing beside her. вҖңYes, Mamma, I tell you sincerely that these are hard and sad times for every Russian. But why are you so anxious? You have still time to get away.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI canвҖҷt think what the servants are about,вҖқ said the countess, turning to her husband. вҖңI have just been told that nothing is ready yet. Somebody after all must see to things. One misses MГӯtenka at such times. There wonвҖҷt be any end to it.вҖқ The count was about to say something, but evidently restrained himself. He got up from his chair and went to the door. At that moment Berg drew out his handkerchief as if to blow his nose and, seeing the knot in it, pondered, shaking his head sadly and significantly. вҖңAnd I have a great favor to ask of you, Papa,вҖқ said he. вҖңHmвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said the count, and stopped. вҖңI was driving past YusГәpovвҖҷs house just now,вҖқ said Berg with a laugh, вҖңwhen the steward, a man I know, ran out and asked me whether I wouldnвҖҷt buy something. I went in out of curiosity, you know, and there is a small chiffonier and a dressing table. You know how dear VГ©rushka wanted a chiffonier like that and how we had a dispute about it.вҖқ (At the mention of the chiffonier and dressing table Berg involuntarily changed his tone to one of pleasure at his admirable domestic arrangements.) вҖңAnd itвҖҷs such a beauty! It pulls out and has a secret English drawer, you know! And dear VГ©rushka has long wanted one. I wish to give her a surprise, you see. I saw so many of those peasant carts in your yard. Please let me have one, I will pay the man well, andвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The count frowned and coughed. вҖңAsk the countess, I donвҖҷt give orders.вҖқ вҖңIf itвҖҷs inconvenient, please donвҖҷt,вҖқ said Berg. вҖңOnly I so wanted it, for dear VГ©raвҖҷs sake.вҖқ вҖңOh, go to the devil, all of you! To the devil, the devil, the devilвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ cried the old count. вҖңMy headвҖҷs in a whirl!вҖқ And he left the room. The countess began to cry. вҖңYes, Mamma! Yes, these are very hard times!вҖқ said Berg. NatГЎsha left the room with her father and, as if finding it difficult to reach some decision, first followed him and then ran downstairs. PГ©tya was in the porch, engaged in giving out weapons to the servants who were to leave Moscow. The loaded carts were still standing in the yard. Two of them had been uncorded and a wounded officer was climbing into one of them helped by an orderly. вҖңDo you know what itвҖҷs about?вҖқ PГ©tya asked NatГЎsha. She understood that he meant what were their parents quarreling about. She did not answer. вҖңItвҖҷs because Papa wanted to give up all the carts to the wounded,вҖқ said PГ©tya. вҖңVasГӯlich told me. I considerвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI consider,вҖқ NatГЎsha suddenly almost shouted, turning her angry face to PГ©tya, вҖңI consider it so horrid, so abominable, soвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I donвҖҷt know what. Are we despicable Germans?вҖқ Her throat quivered with convulsive sobs and, afraid of weakening and letting the force of her anger run to waste, she turned and rushed headlong up the stairs. Berg was sitting beside the countess consoling her with the respectful attention of a relative. The count, pipe in hand, was pacing up and down the room, when NatГЎsha, her face distorted by anger, burst in like a tempest and approached her mother with rapid steps. вҖңItвҖҷs horrid! ItвҖҷs abominable!вҖқ she screamed. вҖңYou canвҖҷt possibly have ordered it!вҖқ Berg and the countess looked at her, perplexed and frightened. The count stood still at the window and listened. вҖңMamma, itвҖҷs impossible: see what is going on in the yard!вҖқ she cried. вҖңThey will be left!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhatвҖҷs the matter with you? Who are вҖҳtheyвҖҷ? What do you want?вҖқ вҖңWhy, the wounded! ItвҖҷs impossible, Mamma. ItвҖҷs monstrous!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No, Mamma darling, itвҖҷs not the thing. Please forgive me, darling.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Mamma, what does it matter what we take away? Only look what is going on in the yardвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Mamma!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ItвҖҷs impossible!вҖқ The count stood by the window and listened without turning round. Suddenly he sniffed and put his face closer to the window. The countess glanced at her daughter, saw her face full of shame for her mother, saw her agitation, and understood why her husband did not turn to look at her now, and she glanced round quite disconcerted. вҖңOh, do as you like! Am I hindering anyone?вҖқ she said, not surrendering at once. вҖңMamma, darling, forgive me!вҖқ But the countess pushed her daughter away and went up to her husband. вҖңMy dear, you order what is right.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You know I donвҖҷt understand about it,вҖқ said she, dropping her eyes shamefacedly. вҖңThe eggsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ the eggs are teaching the hen,вҖқ muttered the count through tears of joy, and he embraced his wife who was glad to hide her look of shame on his breast. вҖңPapa! Mamma! May I see to it? May I?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ asked NatГЎsha. вҖңWe will still take all the most necessary things.вҖқ The count nodded affirmatively, and NatГЎsha, at the rapid pace at which she used to run when playing at tag, ran through the ballroom to the anteroom and downstairs into the yard. The servants gathered round NatГЎsha, but could not believe the strange order she brought them until the count himself, in his wifeвҖҷs name, confirmed the order to give up all the carts to the wounded and take the trunks to the storerooms. When they understood that order the servants set to work at this new task with pleasure and zeal. It no longer seemed strange to them but on the contrary it seemed the only thing that could be done, just as a quarter of an hour before it had not seemed strange to anyone that the wounded should be left behind and the goods carted away but that had seemed the only thing to do. The whole household, as if to atone for not having done it sooner, set eagerly to work at the new task of placing the wounded in the carts. The wounded dragged themselves out of their rooms and stood with pale but happy faces round the carts. The news that carts were to be had spread to the neighboring houses, from which wounded men began to come into the RostГіvsвҖҷ yard. Many of the wounded asked them not to unload the carts but only to let them sit on the top of the things. But the work of unloading, once started, could not be arrested. It seemed not to matter whether all or only half the things were left behind. Cases full of china, bronzes, pictures, and mirrors that had been so carefully packed the night before now lay about the yard, and still they went on searching for and finding possibilities of unloading this or that and letting the wounded have another and yet another cart. вҖңWe can take four more men,вҖқ said the steward. вҖңThey can have my trap, or else what is to become of them?вҖқ вҖңLet them have my wardrobe cart,вҖқ said the countess. вҖңDunyГЎsha can go with me in the carriage.вҖқ They unloaded the wardrobe cart and sent it to take wounded men from a house two doors off. The whole household, servants included, was bright and animated. NatГЎsha was in a state of rapturous excitement such as she had not known for a long time. вҖңWhat could we fasten this onto?вҖқ asked the servants, trying to fix a trunk on the narrow footboard behind a carriage. вҖңWe must keep at least one cart.вҖқ вҖңWhatвҖҷs in it?вҖқ asked NatГЎsha. вҖңThe countвҖҷs books.вҖқ вҖңLeave it, VasГӯlich will put it away. ItвҖҷs not wanted.вҖқ The phaeton was full of people and there was a doubt as to where Pyotr IlГҪnich could sit. вҖңOn the box. YouвҖҷll sit on the box, wonвҖҷt you, PГ©tya?вҖқ cried NatГЎsha. SГіnya too was busy all this time, but the aim of her efforts was quite different from NatГЎshaвҖҷs. She was putting away the things that had to be left behind and making a list of them as the countess wished, and she tried to get as much taken away with them as possible. XVII XVII Before two oвҖҷclock in the afternoon the RostГіvsвҖҷ four carriages, packed full and with the horses harnessed, stood at the front door. One by one the carts with the wounded had moved out of the yard. The calГЁche in which Prince AndrГ©y was being taken attracted SГіnyaвҖҷs attention as it passed the front porch. With the help of a maid she was arranging a seat for the countess in the huge high coach that stood at the entrance. вҖңWhose calГЁche is that?вҖқ she inquired, leaning out of the carriage window. вҖңWhy, didnвҖҷt you know, Miss?вҖқ replied the maid. вҖңThe wounded prince: he spent the night in our house and is going with us.вҖқ вҖңBut who is it? WhatвҖҷs his name?вҖқ вҖңItвҖҷs our intended that wasвҒ вҖ”Prince BolkГіnski himself! They say he is dying,вҖқ replied the maid with a sigh. SГіnya jumped out of the coach and ran to the countess. The countess, tired out and already dressed in shawl and bonnet for her journey, was pacing up and down the drawing room, waiting for the household to assemble for the usual silent prayer with closed doors before starting. NatГЎsha was not in the room. вҖңMamma,вҖқ said SГіnya, вҖңPrince AndrГ©y is here, mortally wounded. He is going with us.вҖқ The countess opened her eyes in dismay and, seizing SГіnyaвҖҷs arm, glanced around. вҖңNatГЎsha?вҖқ she murmured. At that moment this news had only one significance for both of them. They knew their NatГЎsha, and alarm as to what would happen if she heard this news stifled all sympathy for the man they both liked. вҖңNatГЎsha does not know yet, but he is going with us,вҖқ said SГіnya. вҖңYou say he is dying?вҖқ SГіnya nodded. The countess put her arms around SГіnya and began to cry. вҖңThe ways of God are past finding out!вҖқ she thought, feeling that the Almighty Hand, hitherto unseen, was becoming manifest in all that was now taking place. вҖңWell, Mamma? Everything is ready. WhatвҖҷs the matter?вҖқ asked NatГЎsha, as with animated face she ran into the room. вҖңNothing,вҖқ answered the countess. вҖңIf everything is ready let us start.вҖқ And the countess bent over her reticule to hide her agitated face. SГіnya embraced NatГЎsha and kissed her. NatГЎsha looked at her inquiringly. вҖңWhat is it? What has happened?вҖқ вҖңNothingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ NoвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңIs it something very bad for me? What is it?вҖқ persisted NatГЎsha with her quick intuition. SГіnya sighed and made no reply. The count, PГ©tya, Madame Schoss, MГЎvra KuzmГӯnichna, and VasГӯlich came into the drawing room and, having closed the doors, they all sat down and remained for some moments silently seated without looking at one another. The count was the first to rise, and with a loud sigh crossed himself before the icon. All the others did the same. Then the count embraced MГЎvra KuzmГӯnichna and VasГӯlich, who were to remain in Moscow, and while they caught at his hand and kissed his shoulder he patted their backs lightly with some vaguely affectionate and comforting words. The countess went into the oratory and there SГіnya found her on her knees before the icons that had been left here and there hanging on the wall. (The most precious ones, with which some family tradition was connected, were being taken with them.) In the porch and in the yard the men whom PГ©tya had armed with swords and daggers, with trousers tucked inside their high boots and with belts and girdles tightened, were taking leave of those remaining behind. As is always the case at a departure, much had been forgotten or put in the wrong place, and for a long time two menservants stood one on each side of the open door and the carriage steps waiting to help the countess in, while maids rushed with cushions and bundles from the house to the carriages, the calГЁche, the phaeton, and back again. вҖңThey always will forget everything!вҖқ said the countess. вҖңDonвҖҷt you know I canвҖҷt sit like that?вҖқ And DunyГЎsha, with clenched teeth, without replying but with an aggrieved look on her face, hastily got into the coach to rearrange the seat. вҖңOh, those servants!вҖқ said the count, swaying his head. EfГӯm, the old coachman, who was the only one the countess trusted to drive her, sat perched up high on the box and did not so much as glance round at what was going on behind him. From thirty yearsвҖҷ experience he knew it would be some time yet before the order, вҖңBe off, in GodвҖҷs name!вҖқ would be given him: and he knew that even when it was said he would be stopped once or twice more while they sent back to fetch something that had been forgotten, and even after that he would again be stopped and the countess herself would lean out of the window and beg him for the love of heaven to drive carefully down the hill. He knew all this and therefore waited calmly for what would happen, with more patience than the horses, especially the near one, the chestnut Falcon, who was pawing the ground and champing his bit. At last all were seated, the carriage steps were folded and pulled up, the door was shut, somebody was sent for a traveling case, and the countess leaned out and said what she had to say. Then EfГӯm deliberately doffed his hat and began crossing himself. The postilion and all the other servants did the same. вҖңOff, in GodвҖҷs name!вҖқ said EfГӯm, putting on his hat. вҖңStart!вҖқ The postilion started the horses, the off pole horse tugged at his collar, the high springs creaked, and the body of the coach swayed. The footman sprang onto the box of the moving coach which jolted as it passed out of the yard onto the uneven roadway; the other vehicles jolted in their turn, and the procession of carriages moved up the street. In the carriages, the calГЁche, and the phaeton, all crossed themselves as they passed the church opposite the house. Those who were to remain in Moscow walked on either side of the vehicles seeing the travelers off. Rarely had NatГЎsha experienced so joyful a feeling as now, sitting in the carriage beside the countess and gazing at the slowly receding walls of forsaken, agitated Moscow. Occasionally she leaned out of the carriage window and looked back and then forward at the long train of wounded in front of them. Almost at the head of the line she could see the raised hood of Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs calГЁche. She did not know who was in it, but each time she looked at the procession her eyes sought that calГЁche. She knew it was right in front. In KГәdrino, from the NikГӯtski, PrГ©snya, and PodnovГӯnsk Streets came several other trains of vehicles similar to the RostГіvsвҖҷ, and as they passed along the SadГіvaya Street the carriages and carts formed two rows abreast. As they were going round the SГәkharev water tower NatГЎsha, who was inquisitively and alertly scrutinizing the people driving or walking past, suddenly cried out in joyful surprise: вҖңDear me! Mamma, SГіnya, look, itвҖҷs he!вҖқ вҖңWho? Who?вҖқ вҖңLook! Yes, on my word, itвҖҷs BezГәkhov!вҖқ said NatГЎsha, putting her head out of the carriage and staring at a tall, stout man in a coachmanвҖҷs long coat, who from his manner of walking and moving was evidently a gentleman in disguise, and who was passing under the arch of the SГәkharev tower accompanied by a small, sallow-faced, beardless old man in a frieze coat. вҖңYes, it really is BezГәkhov in a coachmanвҖҷs coat, with a queer-looking old boy. Really,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, вҖңlook, look!вҖқ вҖңNo, itвҖҷs not he. How can you talk such nonsense?вҖқ вҖңMamma,вҖқ screamed NatГЎsha, вҖңIвҖҷll stake my head itвҖҷs he! I assure you! Stop, stop!вҖқ she cried to the coachman. But the coachman could not stop, for from the MeshchГЎnski Street came more carts and carriages, and the RostГіvs were being shouted at to move on and not block the way. In fact, however, though now much farther off than before, the RostГіvs all saw PierreвҒ вҖ”or someone extraordinarily like himвҒ вҖ”in a coachmanвҖҷs coat, going down the street with head bent and a serious face beside a small, beardless old man who looked like a footman. That old man noticed a face thrust out of the carriage window gazing at them, and respectfully touching PierreвҖҷs elbow said something to him and pointed to the carriage. Pierre, evidently engrossed in thought, could not at first understand him. At length when he had understood and looked in the direction the old man indicated, he recognized NatГЎsha, and following his first impulse stepped instantly and rapidly toward the coach. But having taken a dozen steps he seemed to remember something and stopped. NatГЎshaвҖҷs face, leaning out of the window, beamed with quizzical kindliness. вҖңPyotr KirГӯlych, come here! We have recognized you! This is wonderful!вҖқ she cried, holding out her hand to him. вҖңWhat are you doing? Why are you like this?вҖқ Pierre took her outstretched hand and kissed it awkwardly as he walked along beside her while the coach still moved on. вҖңWhat is the matter, Count?вҖқ asked the countess in a surprised and commiserating tone. вҖңWhat? What? Why? DonвҖҷt ask me,вҖқ said Pierre, and looked round at NatГЎsha whose radiant, happy expressionвҒ вҖ”of which he was conscious without looking at herвҒ вҖ”filled him with enchantment. вҖңAre you remaining in Moscow, then?вҖқ Pierre hesitated. вҖңIn Moscow?вҖқ he said in a questioning tone. вҖңYes, in Moscow. Goodbye!вҖқ вҖңAh, if only I were a man! IвҖҷd certainly stay with you. How splendid!вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңMamma, if youвҖҷll let me, IвҖҷll stay!вҖқ Pierre glanced absently at NatГЎsha and was about to say something, but the countess interrupted him. вҖңYou were at the battle, we heard.вҖқ вҖңYes, I was,вҖқ Pierre answered. вҖңThere will be another battle tomorrowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he began, but NatГЎsha interrupted him. вҖңBut what is the matter with you, Count? You are not like yourself.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңOh, donвҖҷt ask me, donвҖҷt ask me! I donвҖҷt know myself. TomorrowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But no! Goodbye, goodbye!вҖқ he muttered. вҖңItвҖҷs an awful time!вҖқ and dropping behind the carriage he stepped onto the pavement. NatГЎsha continued to lean out of the window for a long time, beaming at him with her kindly, slightly quizzical, happy smile. XVIII XVIII For the last two days, ever since leaving home, Pierre had been living in the empty house of his deceased benefactor, Osip BazdГ©ev. This is how it happened. When he woke up on the morning after his return to Moscow and his interview with Count RostopchГӯn, he could not for some time make out where he was and what was expected of him. When he was informed that among others awaiting him in his reception room there was a Frenchman who had brought a letter from his wife, the Countess ElГЁna VasГӯlievna, he felt suddenly overcome by that sense of confusion and hopelessness to which he was apt to succumb. He felt that everything was now at an end, all was in confusion and crumbling to pieces, that nobody was right or wrong, the future held nothing, and there was no escape from this position. Smiling unnaturally and muttering to himself, he first sat down on the sofa in an attitude of despair, then rose, went to the door of the reception room and peeped through the crack, returned flourishing his arms, and took up a book. His majordomo came in a second time to say that the Frenchman who had brought the letter from the countess was very anxious to see him if only for a minute, and that someone from Osip AlexГ©evich BazdГ©evвҖҷs widow had called to ask Pierre to take charge of her husbandвҖҷs books, as she herself was leaving for the country. вҖңOh, yes, in a minute; waitвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ or no! No, of courseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ go and say I will come directly,вҖқ Pierre replied to the majordomo. But as soon as the man had left the room Pierre took up his hat which was lying on the table and went out of his study by the other door. There was no one in the passage. He went along the whole length of this passage to the stairs and, frowning and rubbing his forehead with both hands, went down as far as the first landing. The hall porter was standing at the front door. From the landing where Pierre stood there was a second staircase leading to the back entrance. He went down that staircase and out into the yard. No one had seen him. But there were some carriages waiting, and as soon as Pierre stepped out of the gate the coachmen and the yard porter noticed him and raised their caps to him. When he felt he was being looked at he behaved like an ostrich which hides its head in a bush in order not to be seen: he hung his head and quickening his pace went down the street. Of all the affairs awaiting Pierre that day the sorting of Osip AlexГ©evichвҖҷs books and papers appeared to him the most necessary. He hired the first cab he met and told the driver to go to the PatriarchвҖҷs Ponds, where the widow BazdГ©evвҖҷs house was. Continually turning round to look at the rows of loaded carts that were making their way from all sides out of Moscow, and balancing his bulky body so as not to slip out of the ramshackle old vehicle, Pierre, experiencing the joyful feeling of a boy escaping from school, began to talk to his driver. The man told him that arms were being distributed today at the KrГ©mlin and that tomorrow everyone would be sent out beyond the Three Hills gates and a great battle would be fought there. Having reached the PatriarchвҖҷs Ponds Pierre found the BazdГ©evsвҖҷ house, where he had not been for a long time past. He went up to the gate. GerГЎsim, that sallow beardless old man Pierre had seen at TorzhГіk five years before with Osip AlexГ©evich, came out in answer to his knock. вҖңAt home?вҖқ asked Pierre. вҖңOwing to the present state of things SГіfya DanГӯlovna has gone to the TorzhГіk estate with the children, your excellency.вҖқ вҖңI will come in all the same, I have to look through the books,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңBe so good as to step in. MakГЎr AlexГ©evich, the brother of my late masterвҒ вҖ”may the kingdom of heaven be hisвҒ вҖ”has remained here, but he is in a weak state as you know,вҖқ said the old servant. Pierre knew that MakГЎr AlexГ©evich was Osip AlexГ©evichвҖҷs half-insane brother and a hard drinker. вҖңYes, yes, I know. Let us go inвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said Pierre and entered the house. A tall, bald-headed old man with a red nose, wearing a dressing gown and with galoshes on his bare feet, stood in the anteroom. On seeing Pierre he muttered something angrily and went away along the passage. вҖңHe was a very clever man but has now grown quite feeble, as your honor sees,вҖқ said GerГЎsim. вҖңWill you step into the study?вҖқ Pierre nodded. вҖңAs it was sealed up so it has remained, but SГіfya DanГӯlovna gave orders that if anyone should come from you they were to have the books.вҖқ Pierre went into that gloomy study which he had entered with such trepidation in his benefactorвҖҷs lifetime. The room, dusty and untouched since the death of Osip AlexГ©evich was now even gloomier. GerГЎsim opened one of the shutters and left the room on tiptoe. Pierre went round the study, approached the cupboard in which the manuscripts were kept, and took out what had once been one of the most important, the holy of holies of the order. This was the authentic Scotch Acts with BazdГ©evвҖҷs notes and explanations. He sat down at the dusty writing table, and, having laid the manuscripts before him, opened them out, closed them, finally pushed them away, and resting his head on his hand sank into meditation. GerГЎsim looked cautiously into the study several times and saw Pierre always sitting in the same attitude. More than two hours passed and GerГЎsim took the liberty of making a slight noise at the door to attract his attention, but Pierre did not hear him. вҖңIs the cabman to be discharged, your honor?вҖқ вҖңOh yes!вҖқ said Pierre, rousing himself and rising hurriedly. вҖңLook here,вҖқ he added, taking GerГЎsim by a button of his coat and looking down at the old man with moist, shining, and ecstatic eyes, вҖңI say, do you know that there is going to be a battle tomorrow?вҖқ вҖңWe heard so,вҖқ replied the man. вҖңI beg you not to tell anyone who I am, and to do what I ask you.вҖқ вҖңYes, your excellency,вҖқ replied GerГЎsim. вҖңWill you have something to eat?вҖқ вҖңNo, but I want something else. I want peasant clothes and a pistol,вҖқ said Pierre, unexpectedly blushing. вҖңYes, your excellency,вҖқ said GerГЎsim after thinking for a moment. All the rest of that day Pierre spent alone in his benefactorвҖҷs study, and GerГЎsim heard him pacing restlessly from one corner to another and talking to himself. And he spent the night on a bed made up for him there. GerГЎsim, being a servant who in his time had seen many strange things, accepted PierreвҖҷs taking up his residence in the house without surprise, and seemed pleased to have someone to wait on. That same eveningвҒ вҖ”without even asking himself what they were wanted forвҒ вҖ”he procured a coachmanвҖҷs coat and cap for Pierre, and promised to get him the pistol next day. MakГЎr AlexГ©evich came twice that evening shuffling along in his galoshes as far as the door and stopped and looked ingratiatingly at Pierre. But as soon as Pierre turned toward him he wrapped his dressing gown around him with a shamefaced and angry look and hurried away. It was when Pierre (wearing the coachmanвҖҷs coat which GerГЎsim had procured for him and had disinfected by steam) was on his way with the old man to buy the pistol at the SГәkharev market that he met the RostГіvs. XIX XIX KutГәzovвҖҷs order to retreat through Moscow to the RyazГЎn road was issued at night on the first of September. The first troops started at once, and during the night they marched slowly and steadily without hurry. At daybreak, however, those nearing the town at the DorogomГӯlov bridge saw ahead of them masses of soldiers crowding and hurrying across the bridge, ascending on the opposite side and blocking the streets and alleys, while endless masses of troops were bearing down on them from behind, and an unreasoning hurry and alarm overcame them. They all rushed forward to the bridge, onto it, and to the fords and the boats. KutГәzov himself had driven round by side streets to the other side of Moscow. By ten oвҖҷclock in the morning of the second of September, only the rear guard remained in the DorogomГӯlov suburb, where they had ample room. The main army was on the other side of Moscow or beyond it. At that very time, at ten in the morning of the second of September, Napoleon was standing among his troops on the PoklГіnny Hill looking at the panorama spread out before him. From the twenty-sixth of August to the second of September, that is from the battle of BorodinГі to the entry of the French into Moscow, during the whole of that agitating, memorable week, there had been the extraordinary autumn weather that always comes as a surprise, when the sun hangs low and gives more heat than in spring, when everything shines so brightly in the rare clear atmosphere that the eyes smart, when the lungs are strengthened and refreshed by inhaling the aromatic autumn air, when even the nights are warm, and when in those dark warm nights, golden stars startle and delight us continually by falling from the sky. At ten in the morning of the second of September this weather still held. The brightness of the morning was magical. Moscow seen from the PoklГіnny Hill lay spaciously spread out with her river, her gardens, and her churches, and she seemed to be living her usual life, her cupolas glittering like stars in the sunlight. The view of the strange city with its peculiar architecture, such as he had never seen before, filled Napoleon with the rather envious and uneasy curiosity men feel when they see an alien form of life that has no knowledge of them. This city was evidently living with the full force of its own life. By the indefinite signs which, even at a distance, distinguish a living body from a dead one, Napoleon from the PoklГіnny Hill perceived the throb of life in the town and felt, as it were, the breathing of that great and beautiful body. Every Russian looking at Moscow feels her to be a mother; every foreigner who sees her, even if ignorant of her significance as the mother city, must feel her feminine character, and Napoleon felt it. вҖңCette ville asiatique aux innombrables Г©glises, Moscou la sainte. La voilГ  donc enfin, cette fameuse ville! Il Г©tait temps,вҖқ99 said he, and dismounting he ordered a plan of Moscow to be spread out before him, and summoned Lelorgne dвҖҷIdeville, the interpreter. вҖңA town captured by the enemy is like a maid who has lost her honor,вҖқ thought he (he had said so to TГәchkov at SmolГ©nsk). From that point of view he gazed at the Oriental beauty he had not seen before. It seemed strange to him that his long-felt wish, which had seemed unattainable, had at last been realized. In the clear morning light he gazed now at the city and now at the plan, considering its details, and the assurance of possessing it agitated and awed him. вҖңBut could it be otherwise?вҖқ he thought. вҖңHere is this capital at my feet. Where is Alexander now, and of what is he thinking? A strange, beautiful, and majestic city; and a strange and majestic moment! In what light must I appear to them!вҖқ thought he, thinking of his troops. вҖңHere she is, the reward for all those fainthearted men,вҖқ he reflected, glancing at those near him and at the troops who were approaching and forming up. вҖңOne word from me, one movement of my hand, and that ancient capital of the Tsars would perish. But my clemency is always ready to descend upon the vanquished. I must be magnanimous and truly great. But no, it canвҖҷt be true that I am in Moscow,вҖқ he suddenly thought. вҖңYet here she is lying at my feet, with her golden domes and crosses scintillating and twinkling in the sunshine. But I shall spare her. On the ancient monuments of barbarism and despotism I will inscribe great words of justice and mercy.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ It is just this which Alexander will feel most painfully, I know him.вҖқ (It seemed to Napoleon that the chief import of what was taking place lay in the personal struggle between himself and Alexander.) вҖңFrom the height of the KrГ©mlinвҒ вҖ”yes, there is the KrГ©mlin, yesвҒ вҖ”I will give them just laws; I will teach them the meaning of true civilization, I will make generations of boyars remember their conqueror with love. I will tell the deputation that I did not, and do not, desire war, that I have waged war only against the false policy of their court; that I love and respect Alexander and that in Moscow I will accept terms of peace worthy of myself and of my people. I do not wish to utilize the fortunes of war to humiliate an honored monarch. вҖҳBoyars,вҖҷ I will say to them, вҖҳI do not desire war, I desire the peace and welfare of all my subjects.вҖҷ However, I know their presence will inspire me, and I shall speak to them as I always do: clearly, impressively, and majestically. But can it be true that I am in Moscow? Yes, there she lies.вҖқ вҖңQuвҖҷon mвҖҷamГЁne les boyars,вҖқ100 said he to his suite. A general with a brilliant suite galloped off at once to fetch the boyars. Two hours passed. Napoleon had lunched and was again standing in the same place on the PoklГіnny Hill awaiting the deputation. His speech to the boyars had already taken definite shape in his imagination. That speech was full of dignity and greatness as Napoleon understood it. He was himself carried away by the tone of magnanimity he intended to adopt toward Moscow. In his imagination he appointed days for assemblies at the palace of the Tsars, at which Russian notables and his own would mingle. He mentally appointed a governor, one who would win the hearts of the people. Having learned that there were many charitable institutions in Moscow he mentally decided that he would shower favors on them all. He thought that, as in Africa he had to put on a burnoose and sit in a mosque, so in Moscow he must be beneficent like the Tsars. And in order finally to touch the hearts of the RussiansвҒ вҖ”and being like all Frenchmen unable to imagine anything sentimental without a reference to ma chГЁre, ma tendre, ma pauvre mГЁre101вҒ вҖ”he decided that he would place an inscription on all these establishments in large letters: вҖңThis establishment is dedicated to my dear mother.вҖқ Or no, it should be simply: Maison de ma MГЁre,102 he concluded. вҖңBut am I really in Moscow? Yes, here it lies before me, but why is the deputation from the city so long in appearing?вҖқ he wondered. Meanwhile an agitated consultation was being carried on in whispers among his generals and marshals at the rear of his suite. Those sent to fetch the deputation had returned with the news that Moscow was empty, that everyone had left it. The faces of those who were not conferring together were pale and perturbed. They were not alarmed by the fact that Moscow had been abandoned by its inhabitants (grave as that fact seemed), but by the question how to tell the EmperorвҒ вҖ”without putting him in the terrible position of appearing ridiculousвҒ вҖ”that he had been awaiting the boyars so long in vain: that there were drunken mobs left in Moscow but no one else. Some said that a deputation of some sort must be scraped together, others disputed that opinion and maintained that the Emperor should first be carefully and skillfully prepared, and then told the truth. вҖңHe will have to be told, all the same,вҖқ said some gentlemen of the suite. вҖңBut, gentlemenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The position was the more awkward because the Emperor, meditating upon his magnanimous plans, was pacing patiently up and down before the outspread map, occasionally glancing along the road to Moscow from under his lifted hand with a bright and proud smile. вҖңBut itвҖҷs impossibleвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ declared the gentlemen of the suite, shrugging their shoulders but not venturing to utter the implied wordвҒ вҖ”le ridicule.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ At last the Emperor, tired of futile expectation, his actorвҖҷs instinct suggesting to him that the sublime moment having been too long drawn out was beginning to lose its sublimity, gave a sign with his hand. A single report of a signaling gun followed, and the troops, who were already spread out on different sides of Moscow, moved into the city through the Tver, KalГәga, and DorogomГӯlov gates. Faster and faster, vying with one another, they moved at the double or at a trot, vanishing amid the clouds of dust they raised and making the air ring with a deafening roar of mingling shouts. Drawn on by the movement of his troops Napoleon rode with them as far as the DorogomГӯlov gate, but there again stopped and, dismounting from his horse, paced for a long time by the KГЎmmer-KollГ©zski rampart, awaiting the deputation. II II The forces of a dozen European nations burst into Russia. The Russian army and people avoided a collision till SmolГ©nsk was reached, and again from SmolГ©nsk to BorodinГі. The French army pushed on to Moscow, its goal, its impetus ever increasing as it neared its aim, just as the velocity of a falling body increases as it approaches the earth. Behind it were seven hundred miles of hunger-stricken, hostile country; ahead were a few dozen miles separating it from its goal. Every soldier in NapoleonвҖҷs army felt this and the invasion moved on by its own momentum. The more the Russian army retreated the more fiercely a spirit of hatred of the enemy flared up, and while it retreated the army increased and consolidated. At BorodinГі a collision took place. Neither army was broken up, but the Russian army retreated immediately after the collision as inevitably as a ball recoils after colliding with another having a greater momentum, and with equal inevitability the ball of invasion that had advanced with such momentum rolled on for some distance, though the collision had deprived it of all its force. The Russians retreated eighty milesвҒ вҖ”to beyond MoscowвҒ вҖ”and the French reached Moscow and there came to a standstill. For five weeks after that there was not a single battle. The French did not move. As a bleeding, mortally wounded animal licks its wounds, they remained inert in Moscow for five weeks, and then suddenly, with no fresh reason, fled back: they made a dash for the KalГәga road, and (after a victoryвҒ вҖ”for at MГЎlo-YaroslГЎvets the field of conflict again remained theirs) without undertaking a single serious battle, they fled still more rapidly back to SmolГ©nsk, beyond SmolГ©nsk, beyond the BerГ«zina, beyond VГӯlna, and farther still. On the evening of the twenty-sixth of August, KutГәzov and the whole Russian army were convinced that the battle of BorodinГі was a victory. KutГәzov reported so to the Emperor. He gave orders to prepare for a fresh conflict to finish the enemy and did this not to deceive anyone, but because he knew that the enemy was beaten, as everyone who had taken part in the battle knew it. But all that evening and next day reports came in one after another of unheard-of losses, of the loss of half the army, and a fresh battle proved physically impossible. It was impossible to give battle before information had been collected, the wounded gathered in, the supplies of ammunition replenished, the slain reckoned up, new officers appointed to replace those who had been killed, and before the men had had food and sleep. And meanwhile, the very next morning after the battle, the French army advanced of itself upon the Russians, carried forward by the force of its own momentum now seemingly increased in inverse proportion to the square of the distance from its aim. KutГәzovвҖҷs wish was to attack next day, and the whole army desired to do so. But to make an attack the wish to do so is not sufficient, there must also be a possibility of doing it, and that possibility did not exist. It was impossible not to retreat a dayвҖҷs march, and then in the same way it was impossible not to retreat another and a third dayвҖҷs march, and at last, on the first of September when the army drew near MoscowвҒ вҖ”despite the strength of the feeling that had arisen in all ranksвҒ вҖ”the force of circumstances compelled it to retire beyond Moscow. And the troops retired one more, last, dayвҖҷs march, and abandoned Moscow to the enemy. For people accustomed to think that plans of campaign and battles are made by generalsвҒ вҖ”as any one of us sitting over a map in his study may imagine how he would have arranged things in this or that battleвҒ вҖ”the questions present themselves: Why did KutГәzov during the retreat not do this or that? Why did he not take up a position before reaching FilГӯ? Why did he not retire at once by the KalГәga road, abandoning Moscow? and so on. People accustomed to think in that way forget, or do not know, the inevitable conditions which always limit the activities of any commander in chief. The activity of a commander in chief does not at all resemble the activity we imagine to ourselves when we sit at ease in our studies examining some campaign on the map, with a certain number of troops on this and that side in a certain known locality, and begin our plans from some given moment. A commander in chief is never dealing with the beginning of any eventвҒ вҖ”the position from which we always contemplate it. The commander in chief is always in the midst of a series of shifting events and so he never can at any moment consider the whole import of an event that is occurring. Moment by moment the event is imperceptibly shaping itself, and at every moment of this continuous, uninterrupted shaping of events the commander in chief is in the midst of a most complex play of intrigues, worries, contingencies, authorities, projects, counsels, threats, and deceptions and is continually obliged to reply to innumerable questions addressed to him, which constantly conflict with one another. Learned military authorities quite seriously tell us that KutГәzov should have moved his army to the KalГәga road long before reaching FilГӯ, and that somebody actually submitted such a proposal to him. But a commander in chief, especially at a difficult moment, has always before him not one proposal but dozens simultaneously. And all these proposals, based on strategics and tactics, contradict each other. A commander in chiefвҖҷs business, it would seem, is simply to choose one of these projects. But even that he cannot do. Events and time do not wait. For instance, on the twenty-eighth it is suggested to him to cross to the KalГәga road, but just then an adjutant gallops up from MilorГЎdovich asking whether he is to engage the French or retire. An order must be given him at once, that instant. And the order to retreat carries us past the turn to the KalГәga road. And after the adjutant comes the commissary general asking where the stores are to be taken, and the chief of the hospitals asks where the wounded are to go, and a courier from Petersburg brings a letter from the sovereign which does not admit of the possibility of abandoning Moscow, and the commander in chiefвҖҷs rival, the man who is undermining him (and there are always not merely one but several such), presents a new project diametrically opposed to that of turning to the KalГәga road, and the commander in chief himself needs sleep and refreshment to maintain his energy and a respectable general who has been overlooked in the distribution of rewards comes to complain, and the inhabitants of the district pray to be defended, and an officer sent to inspect the locality comes in and gives a report quite contrary to what was said by the officer previously sent; and a spy, a prisoner, and a general who has been on reconnaissance, all describe the position of the enemyвҖҷs army differently. People accustomed to misunderstand or to forget these inevitable conditions of a commander in chiefвҖҷs actions describe to us, for instance, the position of the army at FilГӯ and assume that the commander in chief could, on the first of September, quite freely decide whether to abandon Moscow or defend it; whereas, with the Russian army less than four miles from Moscow, no such question existed. When had that question been settled? At Drissa and at SmolГ©nsk and most palpably of all on the twenty-fourth of August at ShevГЎrdino and on the twenty-sixth at BorodinГі, and each day and hour and minute of the retreat from BorodinГі to FilГӯ. XX XX Meanwhile Moscow was empty. There were still people in it, perhaps a fiftieth part of its former inhabitants had remained, but it was empty. It was empty in the sense that a dying queenless hive is empty. In a queenless hive no life is left though to a superficial glance it seems as much alive as other hives. The bees circle round a queenless hive in the hot beams of the midday sun as gaily as around the living hives; from a distance it smells of honey like the others, and bees fly in and out in the same way. But one has only to observe that hive to realize that there is no longer any life in it. The bees do not fly in the same way, the smell and the sound that meet the beekeeper are not the same. To the beekeeperвҖҷs tap on the wall of the sick hive, instead of the former instant unanimous humming of tens of thousands of bees with their abdomens threateningly compressed, and producing by the rapid vibration of their wings an aerial living sound, the only reply is a disconnected buzzing from different parts of the deserted hive. From the alighting board, instead of the former spirituous fragrant smell of honey and venom, and the warm whiffs of crowded life, comes an odor of emptiness and decay mingling with the smell of honey. There are no longer sentinels sounding the alarm with their abdomens raised, and ready to die in defense of the hive. There is no longer the measured quiet sound of throbbing activity, like the sound of boiling water, but diverse discordant sounds of disorder. In and out of the hive long black robber bees smeared with honey fly timidly and shiftily. They do not sting, but crawl away from danger. Formerly only bees laden with honey flew into the hive, and they flew out empty; now they fly out laden. The beekeeper opens the lower part of the hive and peers in. Instead of black, glossy beesвҒ вҖ”tamed by toil, clinging to one anotherвҖҷs legs and drawing out the wax, with a ceaseless hum of laborвҒ вҖ”that used to hang in long clusters down to the floor of the hive, drowsy shriveled bees crawl about separately in various directions on the floor and walls of the hive. Instead of a neatly glued floor, swept by the bees with the fanning of their wings, there is a floor littered with bits of wax, excrement, dying bees scarcely moving their legs, and dead ones that have not been cleared away. The beekeeper opens the upper part of the hive and examines the super. Instead of serried rows of bees sealing up every gap in the combs and keeping the brood warm, he sees the skillful complex structures of the combs, but no longer in their former state of purity. All is neglected and foul. Black robber bees are swiftly and stealthily prowling about the combs, and the short home bees, shriveled and listless as if they were old, creep slowly about without trying to hinder the robbers, having lost all motive and all sense of life. Drones, bumblebees, wasps, and butterflies knock awkwardly against the walls of the hive in their flight. Here and there among the cells containing dead brood and honey an angry buzzing can sometimes be heard. Here and there a couple of bees, by force of habit and custom cleaning out the brood cells, with efforts beyond their strength laboriously drag away a dead bee or bumblebee without knowing why they do it. In another corner two old bees are languidly fighting, or cleaning themselves, or feeding one another, without themselves knowing whether they do it with friendly or hostile intent. In a third place a crowd of bees, crushing one another, attack some victim and fight and smother it, and the victim, enfeebled or killed, drops from above slowly and lightly as a feather, among the heap of corpses. The keeper opens the two center partitions to examine the brood cells. In place of the former close dark circles formed by thousands of bees sitting back to back and guarding the high mystery of generation, he sees hundreds of dull, listless, and sleepy shells of bees. They have almost all died unawares, sitting in the sanctuary they had guarded and which is now no more. They reek of decay and death. Only a few of them still move, rise, and feebly fly to settle on the enemyвҖҷs hand, lacking the spirit to die stinging him; the rest are dead and fall as lightly as fish scales. The beekeeper closes the hive, chalks a mark on it, and when he has time tears out its contents and burns it clean. So in the same way Moscow was empty when Napoleon, weary, uneasy, and morose, paced up and down in front of the KГЎmmer-KollГ©zski rampart, awaiting what to his mind was a necessary, if but formal, observance of the proprietiesвҒ вҖ”a deputation. In various corners of Moscow there still remained a few people aimlessly moving about, following their old habits and hardly aware of what they were doing. When with due circumspection Napoleon was informed that Moscow was empty, he looked angrily at his informant, turned away, and silently continued to walk to and fro. вҖңMy carriage!вҖқ he said. He took his seat beside the aide-de-camp on duty and drove into the suburb. вҖңMoscow deserted!вҖқ he said to himself. вҖңWhat an incredible event!вҖқ He did not drive into the town, but put up at an inn in the DorogomГӯlov suburb. The coup de thГ©Гўtre had not come off. XXI XXI The Russian troops were passing through Moscow from two oвҖҷclock at night till two in the afternoon and bore away with them the wounded and the last of the inhabitants who were leaving. The greatest crush during the movement of the troops took place at the Stone, MoskvГЎ, and YaГәza bridges. While the troops, dividing into two parts when passing around the KrГ©mlin, were thronging the MoskvГЎ and the Stone bridges, a great many soldiers, taking advantage of the stoppage and congestion, turned back from the bridges and slipped stealthily and silently past the church of VasГӯli the Beatified and under the BorovГӯtski gate, back up the hill to the Red Square where some instinct told them they could easily take things not belonging to them. Crowds of the kind seen at cheap sales filled all the passages and alleys of the Bazaar. But there were no dealers with voices of ingratiating affability inviting customers to enter; there were no hawkers, nor the usual motley crowd of female purchasersвҒ вҖ”but only soldiers, in uniforms and overcoats though without muskets, entering the Bazaar empty-handed and silently making their way out through its passages with bundles. Tradesmen and their assistants (of whom there were but few) moved about among the soldiers quite bewildered. They unlocked their shops and locked them up again, and themselves carried goods away with the help of their assistants. On the square in front of the Bazaar were drummers beating the muster call. But the roll of the drums did not make the looting soldiers run in the direction of the drum as formerly, but made them, on the contrary, run farther away. Among the soldiers in the shops and passages some men were to be seen in gray coats, with closely shaven heads. Two officers, one with a scarf over his uniform and mounted on a lean, dark-gray horse, the other in an overcoat and on foot, stood at the corner of IlyГӯnka Street, talking. A third officer galloped up to them. вҖңThe general orders them all to be driven out at once, without fail. This is outrageous! Half the men have dispersed.вҖқ вҖңWhere are you off to?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Where?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he shouted to three infantrymen without muskets who, holding up the skirts of their overcoats, were slipping past him into the Bazaar passage. вҖңStop, you rascals!вҖқ вҖңBut how are you going to stop them?вҖқ replied another officer. вҖңThere is no getting them together. The army should push on before the rest bolt, thatвҖҷs all!вҖқ вҖңHow can one push on? They are stuck there, wedged on the bridge, and donвҖҷt move. ShouldnвҖҷt we put a cordon round to prevent the rest from running away?вҖқ вҖңCome, go in there and drive them out!вҖқ shouted the senior officer. The officer in the scarf dismounted, called up a drummer, and went with him into the arcade. Some soldiers started running away in a group. A shopkeeper with red pimples on his cheeks near the nose, and a calm, persistent, calculating expression on his plump face, hurriedly and ostentatiously approached the officer, swinging his arms. вҖңYour honor!вҖқ said he. вҖңBe so good as to protect us! We wonвҖҷt grudge trifles, you are welcome to anythingвҒ вҖ”we shall be delighted! Pray!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ IвҖҷll fetch a piece of cloth at once for such an honorable gentleman, or even two pieces with pleasure. For we feel how it is; but whatвҖҷs all thisвҒ вҖ”sheer robbery! If you please, could not guards be placed if only to let us close the shop.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Several shopkeepers crowded round the officer. вҖңEh, what twaddle!вҖқ said one of them, a thin, stern-looking man. вҖңWhen oneвҖҷs head is gone one doesnвҖҷt weep for oneвҖҷs hair! Take what any of you like!вҖқ And flourishing his arm energetically he turned sideways to the officer. вҖңItвҖҷs all very well for you, IvГЎn SidГіrych, to talk,вҖқ said the first tradesman angrily. вҖңPlease step inside, your honor!вҖқ вҖңTalk indeed!вҖқ cried the thin one. вҖңIn my three shops here I have a hundred thousand rublesвҖҷ worth of goods. Can they be saved when the army has gone? Eh, what people! вҖҳAgainst GodвҖҷs might our hands canвҖҷt fight.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ вҖңCome inside, your honor!вҖқ repeated the tradesman, bowing. The officer stood perplexed and his face showed indecision. вҖңItвҖҷs not my business!вҖқ he exclaimed, and strode on quickly down one of the passages. From one open shop came the sound of blows and vituperation, and just as the officer came up to it a man in a gray coat with a shaven head was flung out violently. This man, bent double, rushed past the tradesman and the officer. The officer pounced on the soldiers who were in the shops, but at that moment fearful screams reached them from the huge crowd on the MoskvГЎ bridge and the officer ran out into the square. вҖңWhat is it? What is it?вҖқ he asked, but his comrade was already galloping off past VasГӯli the Beatified in the direction from which the screams came. The officer mounted his horse and rode after him. When he reached the bridge he saw two unlimbered guns, the infantry crossing the bridge, several overturned carts, and frightened and laughing faces among the troops. Beside the cannon a cart was standing to which two horses were harnessed. Four borzois with collars were pressing close to the wheels. The cart was loaded high, and at the very top, beside a childвҖҷs chair with its legs in the air, sat a peasant woman uttering piercing and desperate shrieks. He was told by his fellow officers that the screams of the crowd and the shrieks of the woman were due to the fact that General ErmГіlov, coming up to the crowd and learning that soldiers were dispersing among the shops while crowds of civilians blocked the bridge, had ordered two guns to be unlimbered and made a show of firing at the bridge. The crowd, crushing one another, upsetting carts, and shouting and squeezing desperately, had cleared off the bridge and the troops were now moving forward. XXII XXII Meanwhile, the city itself was deserted. There was hardly anyone in the streets. The gates and shops were all closed, only here and there round the taverns solitary shouts or drunken songs could be heard. Nobody drove through the streets and footsteps were rarely heard.
