But this happiness on one side of her spiritual nature did not prevent her feeling grief for her brother with full force; on the contrary, that spiritual tranquility on the one side made it the more possible for her to give full play to her feeling for her brother. That feeling was so strong at the moment of leaving VorГіnezh that those who saw her off, as they looked at her careworn, despairing face, felt sure she would fall ill on the journey. But the very difficulties and preoccupations of the journey, which she took so actively in hand, saved her for a while from her grief and gave her strength. As always happens when traveling, Princess MГЎrya thought only of the journey itself, forgetting its object. But as she approached YaroslГЎvl the thought of what might await her thereвҒ вҖ”not after many days, but that very eveningвҒ вҖ”again presented itself to her and her agitation increased to its utmost limit. The courier who had been sent on in advance to find out where the RostГіvs were staying in YaroslГЎvl, and in what condition Prince AndrГ©y was, when he met the big coach just entering the town gates was appalled by the terrible pallor of the princessвҖҷ face that looked out at him from the window. вҖңI have found out everything, your excellency: the RostГіvs are staying at the merchant BrГіnnikovвҖҷs house, in the Square not far from here, right above the VГіlga,вҖқ said the courier. Princess MГЎrya looked at him with frightened inquiry, not understanding why he did not reply to what she chiefly wanted to know: how was her brother? Mademoiselle Bourienne put that question for her. вҖңHow is the prince?вҖқ she asked. вҖңHis excellency is staying in the same house with them.вҖқ вҖңThen he is alive,вҖқ thought Princess MГЎrya, and asked in a low voice: вҖңHow is he?вҖқ вҖңThe servants say he is still the same.вҖқ What вҖңstill the sameвҖқ might mean Princess MГЎrya did not ask, but with an unnoticed glance at little seven-year-old NikolГәshka, who was sitting in front of her looking with pleasure at the town, she bowed her head and did not raise it again till the heavy coach, rumbling, shaking and swaying, came to a stop. The carriage steps clattered as they were let down. The carriage door was opened. On the left there was waterвҒ вҖ”a great riverвҒ вҖ”and on the right a porch. There were people at the entrance: servants, and a rosy girl with a large plait of black hair, smiling as it seemed to Princess MГЎrya in an unpleasantly affected way. (This was SГіnya.) Princess MГЎrya ran up the steps. вҖңThis way, this way!вҖқ said the girl, with the same artificial smile, and the princess found herself in the hall facing an elderly woman of Oriental type, who came rapidly to meet her with a look of emotion. This was the countess. She embraced Princess MГЎrya and kissed her. вҖңMon enfant!вҖқ she muttered, вҖңje vous aime et vous connais depuis longtemps.вҖқ112 Despite her excitement, Princess MГЎrya realized that this was the countess and that it was necessary to say something to her. Hardly knowing how she did it, she contrived to utter a few polite phrases in French in the same tone as those that had been addressed to her, and asked: вҖңHow is he?вҖқ вҖңThe doctor says that he is not in danger,вҖқ said the countess, but as she spoke she raised her eyes with a sigh, and her gesture conveyed a contradiction of her words. вҖңWhere is he? Can I see himвҒ вҖ”can I?вҖқ asked the princess. вҖңOne moment, Princess, one moment, my dear! Is this his son?вҖқ said the countess, turning to NikolГәshka who was coming in with Dessalles. вҖңThere will be room for everybody, this is a big house. Oh, what a lovely boy!вҖқ The countess took Princess MГЎrya into the drawing room, where SГіnya was talking to Mademoiselle Bourienne. The countess caressed the boy, and the old count came in and welcomed the princess. He had changed very much since Princess MГЎrya had last seen him. Then he had been a brisk, cheerful, self-assured old man; now he seemed a pitiful, bewildered person. While talking to Princess MГЎrya he continually looked round as if asking everyone whether he was doing the right thing. After the destruction of Moscow and of his property, thrown out of his accustomed groove he seemed to have lost the sense of his own significance and to feel that there was no longer a place for him in life. In spite of her one desire to see her brother as soon as possible, and her vexation that at the moment when all she wanted was to see him they should be trying to entertain her and pretending to admire her nephew, the princess noticed all that was going on around her and felt the necessity of submitting, for a time, to this new order of things which she had entered. She knew it to be necessary, and though it was hard for her she was not vexed with these people. вҖңThis is my niece,вҖқ said the count, introducing SГіnyaвҒ вҖ”вҖңYou donвҖҷt know her, Princess?вҖқ Princess MГЎrya turned to SГіnya and, trying to stifle the hostile feeling that arose in her toward the girl, she kissed her. But she felt oppressed by the fact that the mood of everyone around her was so far from what was in her own heart. вҖңWhere is he?вҖқ she asked again, addressing them all. вҖңHe is downstairs. NatГЎsha is with him,вҖқ answered SГіnya, flushing. вҖңWe have sent to ask. I think you must be tired, Princess.вҖқ Tears of vexation showed themselves in Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs eyes. She turned away and was about to ask the countess again how to go to him, when light, impetuous, and seemingly buoyant steps were heard at the door. The princess looked round and saw NatГЎsha coming in, almost runningвҒ вҖ”that NatГЎsha whom she had liked so little at their meeting in Moscow long since. But hardly had the princess looked at NatГЎshaвҖҷs face before she realized that here was a real comrade in her grief, and consequently a friend. She ran to meet her, embraced her, and began to cry on her shoulder. As soon as NatГЎsha, sitting at the head of Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs bed, heard of Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs arrival, she softly left his room and hastened to her with those swift steps that had sounded buoyant to Princess MГЎrya. There was only one expression on her agitated face when she ran into the drawing roomвҒ вҖ”that of loveвҒ вҖ”boundless love for him, for her, and for all that was near to the man she loved; and of pity, suffering for others, and passionate desire to give herself entirely to helping them. It was plain that at that moment there was in NatГЎshaвҖҷs heart no thought of herself or of her own relations with Prince AndrГ©y. Princess MГЎrya, with her acute sensibility, understood all this at the first glance at NatГЎshaвҖҷs face, and wept on her shoulder with sorrowful pleasure. вҖңCome, come to him, MГЎrya,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, leading her into the other room. Princess MГЎrya raised her head, dried her eyes, and turned to NatГЎsha. She felt that from her she would be able to understand and learn everything. вҖңHowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she began her question but stopped short. She felt that it was impossible to ask, or to answer, in words. NatГЎshaвҖҷs face and eyes would have to tell her all more clearly and profoundly. NatГЎsha was gazing at her, but seemed afraid and in doubt whether to say all she knew or not; she seemed to feel that before those luminous eyes which penetrated into the very depths of her heart, it was impossible not to tell the whole truth which she saw. And suddenly, NatГЎshaвҖҷs lips twitched, ugly wrinkles gathered round her mouth, and covering her face with her hands she burst into sobs. Princess MГЎrya understood. But she still hoped, and asked, in words she herself did not trust: вҖңBut how is his wound? What is his general condition?вҖқ вҖңYou, youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ will see,вҖқ was all NatГЎsha could say. They sat a little while downstairs near his room till they had left off crying and were able to go to him with calm faces. вҖңHow has his whole illness gone? Is it long since he grew worse? When did this happen?вҖқ Princess MГЎrya inquired. NatГЎsha told her that at first there had been danger from his feverish condition and the pain he suffered, but at TrГіitsa that had passed and the doctor had only been afraid of gangrene. That danger had also passed. When they reached YaroslГЎvl the wound had begun to fester (NatГЎsha knew all about such things as festering) and the doctor had said that the festering might take a normal course. Then fever set in, but the doctor had said the fever was not very serious. вҖңBut two days ago this suddenly happened,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, struggling with her sobs. вҖңI donвҖҷt know why, but you will see what he is like.вҖқ вҖңIs he weaker? Thinner?вҖқ asked the princess. вҖңNo, itвҖҷs not that, but worse. You will see. O, MГЎrya, he is too good, he cannot, cannot live, becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ XV XV When NatГЎsha opened Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs door with a familiar movement and let Princess MГЎrya pass into the room before her, the princess felt the sobs in her throat. Hard as she had tried to prepare herself, and now tried to remain tranquil, she knew that she would be unable to look at him without tears. The princess understood what NatГЎsha had meant by the words: вҖңtwo days ago this suddenly happened.вҖқ She understood those words to mean that he had suddenly softened and that this softening and gentleness were signs of approaching death. As she stepped to the door she already saw in imagination AndrГәshaвҖҷs face as she remembered it in childhood, a gentle, mild, sympathetic face which he had rarely shown, and which therefore affected her very strongly. She was sure he would speak soft, tender words to her such as her father had uttered before his death, and that she would not be able to bear it and would burst into sobs in his presence. Yet sooner or later it had to be, and she went in. The sobs rose higher and higher in her throat as she more and more clearly distinguished his form and her shortsighted eyes tried to make out his features, and then she saw his face and met his gaze. He was lying in a squirrel-fur dressing gown on a divan, surrounded by pillows. He was thin and pale. In one thin, translucently white hand he held a handkerchief, while with the other he stroked the delicate mustache he had grown, moving his fingers slowly. His eyes gazed at them as they entered. On seeing his face and meeting his eyes Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs pace suddenly slackened, she felt her tears dry up and her sobs ceased. She suddenly felt guilty and grew timid on catching the expression of his face and eyes. вҖңBut in what am I to blame?вҖқ she asked herself. And his cold, stern look replied: вҖңBecause you are alive and thinking of the living, while IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ In the deep gaze that seemed to look not outwards but inwards there was an almost hostile expression as he slowly regarded his sister and NatГЎsha. He kissed his sister, holding her hand in his as was their wont. вҖңHow are you, MГЎrya? How did you manage to get here?вҖқ said he in a voice as calm and aloof as his look. Had he screamed in agony, that scream would not have struck such horror into Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs heart as the tone of his voice. вҖңAnd have you brought NikolГәshka?вҖқ he asked in the same slow, quiet manner and with an obvious effort to remember. вҖңHow are you now?вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya, herself surprised at what she was saying. вҖңThat, my dear, you must ask the doctor,вҖқ he replied, and again making an evident effort to be affectionate, he said with his lips only (his words clearly did not correspond to his thoughts): вҖңMerci, chГЁre amie, dвҖҷГӘtre venue.вҖқ113 Princess MГЎrya pressed his hand. The pressure made him wince just perceptibly. He was silent, and she did not know what to say. She now understood what had happened to him two days before. In his words, his tone, and especially in that calm, almost antagonistic look could be felt an estrangement from everything belonging to this world, terrible in one who is alive. Evidently only with an effort did he understand anything living; but it was obvious that he failed to understand, not because he lacked the power to do so but because he understood something elseвҒ вҖ”something the living did not and could not understandвҒ вҖ”and which wholly occupied his mind. вҖңThere, you see how strangely fate has brought us together,вҖқ said he, breaking the silence and pointing to NatГЎsha. вҖңShe looks after me all the time.вҖқ Princess MГЎrya heard him and did not understand how he could say such a thing. He, the sensitive, tender Prince AndrГ©y, how could he say that, before her whom he loved and who loved him? Had he expected to live he could not have said those words in that offensively cold tone. If he had not known that he was dying, how could he have failed to pity her and how could he speak like that in her presence? The only explanation was that he was indifferent, because something else, much more important, had been revealed to him. The conversation was cold and disconnected and continually broke off. вҖңMГЎrya came by way of RyazГЎn,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. Prince AndrГ©y did not notice that she called his sister MГЎrya, and only after calling her so in his presence did NatГЎsha notice it herself. вҖңReally?вҖқ he asked. вҖңThey told her that all Moscow has been burned down, and thatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ NatГЎsha stopped. It was impossible to talk. It was plain that he was making an effort to listen, but could not do so. вҖңYes, they say itвҖҷs burned,вҖқ he said. вҖңItвҖҷs a great pity,вҖқ and he gazed straight before him, absently stroking his mustache with his fingers. вҖңAnd so you have met Count NikolГЎy, MГЎrya?вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y suddenly said, evidently wishing to speak pleasantly to them. вҖңHe wrote here that he took a great liking to you,вҖқ he went on simply and calmly, evidently unable to understand all the complex significance his words had for living people. вҖңIf you liked him too, it would be a good thing for you to get married,вҖқ he added rather more quickly, as if pleased at having found words he had long been seeking. Princess MГЎrya heard his words but they had no meaning for her, except as a proof of how far away he now was from everything living. вҖңWhy talk of me?вҖқ she said quietly and glanced at NatГЎsha. NatГЎsha, who felt her glance, did not look at her. All three were again silent. вҖңAndrГ©, would you likeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Princess MГЎrya suddenly said in a trembling voice, вҖңwould you like to see NikolГәshka? He is always talking about you!вҖқ Prince AndrГ©y smiled just perceptibly and for the first time, but Princess MГЎrya, who knew his face so well, saw with horror that he did not smile with pleasure or affection for his son, but with quiet, gentle irony because he thought she was trying what she believed to be the last means of arousing him. вҖңYes, I shall be very glad to see him. Is he quite well?вҖқ When little NikolГәshka was brought into Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs room he looked at his father with frightened eyes, but did not cry, because no one else was crying. Prince AndrГ©y kissed him and evidently did not know what to say to him. When NikolГәshka had been led away, Princess MГЎrya again went up to her brother, kissed him, and unable to restrain her tears any longer began to cry. He looked at her attentively. вҖңIs it about NikolГәshka?вҖқ he asked. Princess MГЎrya nodded her head, weeping. вҖңMГЎrya, you know the GospвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ but he broke off. вҖңWhat did you say?вҖқ вҖңNothing. You mustnвҖҷt cry here,вҖқ he said, looking at her with the same cold expression. When Princess MГЎrya began to cry, he understood that she was crying at the thought that NikolГәshka would be left without a father. With a great effort he tried to return to life and to see things from their point of view. вҖңYes, to them it must seem sad!вҖқ he thought. вҖңBut how simple it is.вҖқ вҖңThe fowls of the air sow not, neither do they reap, yet your Father feedeth them,вҖқ he said to himself and wished to say to Princess MГЎrya; вҖңbut no, they will take it their own way, they wonвҖҷt understand! They canвҖҷt understand that all those feelings they prize soвҒ вҖ”all our feelings, all those ideas that seem so important to us, are unnecessary. We cannot understand one another,вҖқ and he remained silent. Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs little son was seven. He could scarcely read, and knew nothing. After that day he lived through many things, gaining knowledge, observation, and experience, but had he possessed all the faculties he afterwards acquired, he could not have had a better or more profound understanding of the meaning of the scene he had witnessed between his father, MГЎrya, and NatГЎsha, than he had then. He understood it completely, and, leaving the room without crying, went silently up to NatГЎsha who had come out with him and looked shyly at her with his beautiful, thoughtful eyes, then his uplifted, rosy upper lip trembled and leaning his head against her he began to cry. After that he avoided Dessalles and the countess who caressed him and either sat alone or came timidly to Princess MГЎrya, or to NatГЎsha of whom he seemed even fonder than of his aunt, and clung to them quietly and shyly. When Princess MГЎrya had left Prince AndrГ©y she fully understood what NatГЎshaвҖҷs face had told her. She did not speak any more to NatГЎsha of hopes of saving his life. She took turns with her beside his sofa, and did not cry any more, but prayed continually, turning in soul to that Eternal and Unfathomable, whose presence above the dying man was now so evident. XVI XVI Not only did Prince AndrГ©y know he would die, but he felt that he was dying and was already half dead. He was conscious of an aloofness from everything earthly and a strange and joyous lightness of existence. Without haste or agitation he awaited what was coming. That inexorable, eternal, distant, and unknown the presence of which he had felt continually all his lifeвҒ вҖ”was now near to him and, by the strange lightness he experienced, almost comprehensible and palpable.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Formerly he had feared the end. He had twice experienced that terribly tormenting fear of deathвҒ вҖ”the endвҒ вҖ”but now he no longer understood that fear. He had felt it for the first time when the shell spun like a top before him, and he looked at the fallow field, the bushes, and the sky, and knew that he was face to face with death. When he came to himself after being wounded and the flower of eternal, unfettered love had instantly unfolded itself in his soul as if freed from the bondage of life that had restrained it, he no longer feared death and ceased to think about it. During the hours of solitude, suffering, and partial delirium he spent after he was wounded, the more deeply he penetrated into the new principle of eternal love revealed to him, the more he unconsciously detached himself from earthly life. To love everything and everybody and always to sacrifice oneself for love meant not to love anyone, not to live this earthly life. And the more imbued he became with that principle of love, the more he renounced life and the more completely he destroyed that dreadful barrier whichвҒ вҖ”in the absence of such loveвҒ вҖ”stands between life and death. When during those first days he remembered that he would have to die, he said to himself: вҖңWell, what of it? So much the better!вҖқ But after the night in MytГӯshchi when, half delirious, he had seen her for whom he longed appear before him and, having pressed her hand to his lips, had shed gentle, happy tears, love for a particular woman again crept unobserved into his heart and once more bound him to life. And joyful and agitating thoughts began to occupy his mind. Recalling the moment at the ambulance station when he had seen KurГЎgin, he could not now regain the feeling he then had, but was tormented by the question whether KurГЎgin was alive. And he dared not inquire. His illness pursued its normal physical course, but what NatГЎsha referred to when she said: вҖңThis suddenly happened,вҖқ had occurred two days before Princess MГЎrya arrived. It was the last spiritual struggle between life and death, in which death gained the victory. It was the unexpected realization of the fact that he still valued life as presented to him in the form of his love for NatГЎsha, and a last, though ultimately vanquished, attack of terror before the unknown. It was evening. As usual after dinner he was slightly feverish, and his thoughts were preternaturally clear. SГіnya was sitting by the table. He began to doze. Suddenly a feeling of happiness seized him. вҖңAh, she has come!вҖқ thought he. And so it was: in SГіnyaвҖҷs place sat NatГЎsha who had just come in noiselessly. Since she had begun looking after him, he had always experienced this physical consciousness of her nearness. She was sitting in an armchair placed sideways, screening the light of the candle from him, and was knitting a stocking. She had learned to knit stockings since Prince AndrГ©y had casually mentioned that no one nursed the sick so well as old nurses who knit stockings, and that there is something soothing in the knitting of stockings. The needles clicked lightly in her slender, rapidly moving hands, and he could clearly see the thoughtful profile of her drooping face. She moved, and the ball rolled off her knees. She started, glanced round at him, and screening the candle with her hand stooped carefully with a supple and exact movement, picked up the ball, and regained her former position. He looked at her without moving and saw that she wanted to draw a deep breath after stooping, but refrained from doing so and breathed cautiously. At the TrГіitsa monastery they had spoken of the past, and he had told her that if he lived he would always thank God for his wound which had brought them together again, but after that they never spoke of the future. вҖңCan it or can it not be?вҖқ he now thought as he looked at her and listened to the light click of the steel needles. вҖңCan fate have brought me to her so strangely only for me to die?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Is it possible that the truth of life has been revealed to me only to show me that I have spent my life in falsity? I love her more than anything in the world! But what am I to do if I love her?вҖқ he thought, and he involuntarily groaned, from a habit acquired during his sufferings. On hearing that sound NatГЎsha put down the stocking, leaned nearer to him, and suddenly, noticing his shining eyes, stepped lightly up to him and bent over him. вҖңYou are not asleep?вҖқ вҖңNo, I have been looking at you a long time. I felt you come in. No one else gives me that sense of soft tranquillity that you doвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ that light. I want to weep for joy.вҖқ NatГЎsha drew closer to him. Her face shone with rapturous joy. вҖңNatГЎsha, I love you too much! More than anything in the world.вҖқ вҖңAnd I!вҖқвҒ вҖ”She turned away for an instant. вҖңWhy too much?вҖқ she asked. вҖңWhy too much?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well, what do you, what do you feel in your soul, your whole soulвҒ вҖ”shall I live? What do you think?вҖқ вҖңI am sure of it, sure!вҖқ NatГЎsha almost shouted, taking hold of both his hands with a passionate movement. He remained silent awhile. вҖңHow good it would be!вҖқ and taking her hand he kissed it. NatГЎsha felt happy and agitated, but at once remembered that this would not do and that he had to be quiet. вҖңBut you have not slept,вҖқ she said, repressing her joy. вҖңTry to sleepвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ please!вҖқ He pressed her hand and released it, and she went back to the candle and sat down again in her former position. Twice she turned and looked at him, and her eyes met his beaming at her. She set herself a task on her stocking and resolved not to turn round till it was finished. Soon he really shut his eyes and fell asleep. He did not sleep long and suddenly awoke with a start and in a cold perspiration. As he fell asleep he had still been thinking of the subject that now always occupied his mindвҒ вҖ”about life and death, and chiefly about death. He felt himself nearer to it. вҖңLove? What is love?вҖқ he thought. вҖңLove hinders death. Love is life. All, everything that I understand, I understand only because I love. Everything is, everything exists, only because I love. Everything is united by it alone. Love is God, and to die means that I, a particle of love, shall return to the general and eternal source.вҖқ These thoughts seemed to him comforting. But they were only thoughts. Something was lacking in them, they were not clear, they were too one-sidedly personal and brain-spun. And there was the former agitation and obscurity. He fell asleep. He dreamed that he was lying in the room he really was in, but that he was quite well and unwounded. Many various, indifferent, and insignificant people appeared before him. He talked to them and discussed something trivial. They were preparing to go away somewhere. Prince AndrГ©y dimly realized that all this was trivial and that he had more important cares, but he continued to speak, surprising them by empty witticisms. Gradually, unnoticed, all these persons began to disappear and a single question, that of the closed door, superseded all else. He rose and went to the door to bolt and lock it. Everything depended on whether he was, or was not, in time to lock it. He went, and tried to hurry, but his legs refused to move and he knew he would not be in time to lock the door though he painfully strained all his powers. He was seized by an agonizing fear. And that fear was the fear of death. It stood behind the door. But just when he was clumsily creeping toward the door, that dreadful something on the other side was already pressing against it and forcing its way in. Something not humanвҒ вҖ”deathвҒ вҖ”was breaking in through that door, and had to be kept out. He seized the door, making a final effort to hold it backвҒ вҖ”to lock it was no longer possibleвҒ вҖ”but his efforts were weak and clumsy and the door, pushed from behind by that terror, opened and closed again. Once again it pushed from outside. His last superhuman efforts were vain and both halves of the door noiselessly opened. It entered, and it was death, and Prince AndrГ©y died. But at the instant he died, Prince AndrГ©y remembered that he was asleep, and at the very instant he died, having made an effort, he awoke. вҖңYes, it was death! I diedвҒ вҖ”and woke up. Yes, death is an awakening!вҖқ And all at once it grew light in his soul and the veil that had till then concealed the unknown was lifted from his spiritual vision. He felt as if powers till then confined within him had been liberated, and that strange lightness did not again leave him. When, waking in a cold perspiration, he moved on the divan, NatГЎsha went up and asked him what was the matter. He did not answer and looked at her strangely, not understanding. That was what had happened to him two days before Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs arrival. From that day, as the doctor expressed it, the wasting fever assumed a malignant character, but what the doctor said did not interest NatГЎsha, she saw the terrible moral symptoms which to her were more convincing. From that day an awakening from life came to Prince AndrГ©y together with his awakening from sleep. And compared to the duration of life it did not seem to him slower than an awakening from sleep compared to the duration of a dream. There was nothing terrible or violent in this comparatively slow awakening. His last days and hours passed in an ordinary and simple way. Both Princess MГЎrya and NatГЎsha, who did not leave him, felt this. They did not weep or shudder and during these last days they themselves felt that they were not attending on him (he was no longer there, he had left them) but on what reminded them most closely of himвҒ вҖ”his body. Both felt this so strongly that the outward and terrible side of death did not affect them and they did not feel it necessary to foment their grief. Neither in his presence nor out of it did they weep, nor did they ever talk to one another about him. They felt that they could not express in words what they understood. They both saw that he was sinking slowly and quietly, deeper and deeper, away from them, and they both knew that this had to be so and that it was right. He confessed, and received communion: everyone came to take leave of him. When they brought his son to him, he pressed his lips to the boyвҖҷs and turned away, not because he felt it hard and sad (Princess MГЎrya and NatГЎsha understood that) but simply because he thought it was all that was required of him, but when they told him to bless the boy, he did what was demanded and looked round as if asking whether there was anything else he should do. When the last convulsions of the body, which the spirit was leaving, occurred, Princess MГЎrya and NatГЎsha were present. вҖңIs it over?вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya when his body had for a few minutes lain motionless, growing cold before them. NatГЎsha went up, looked at the dead eyes, and hastened to close them. She closed them but did not kiss them, but clung to that which reminded her most nearly of himвҒ вҖ”his body. вҖңWhere has he gone? Where is he now?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ When the body, washed and dressed, lay in the coffin on a table, everyone came to take leave of him and they all wept. NikolГәshka cried because his heart was rent by painful perplexity. The countess and SГіnya cried from pity for NatГЎsha and because he was no more. The old count cried because he felt that before long, he, too, must take the same terrible step. NatГЎsha and Princess MГЎrya also wept now, but not because of their own personal grief; they wept with a reverent and softening emotion which had taken possession of their souls at the consciousness of the simple and solemn mystery of death that had been accomplished in their presence. II II Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs presentiment was in fact fulfilled. Next day during the service at the palace church in honor of the EmperorвҖҷs birthday, Prince VolkГіnski was called out of the church and received a dispatch from Prince KutГәzov. It was KutГәzovвҖҷs report, written from TatГЎrinova on the day of the battle. KutГәzov wrote that the Russians had not retreated a step, that the French losses were much heavier than ours, and that he was writing in haste from the field of battle before collecting full information. It followed that there must have been a victory. And at once, without leaving the church, thanks were rendered to the Creator for His help and for the victory. Anna PГЎvlovnaвҖҷs presentiment was justified, and all that morning a joyously festive mood reigned in the city. Everyone believed the victory to have been complete, and some even spoke of NapoleonвҖҷs having been captured, of his deposition, and of the choice of a new ruler for France. It is very difficult for events to be reflected in their real strength and completeness amid the conditions of court life and far from the scene of action. General events involuntarily group themselves around some particular incident. So now the courtiersвҖҷ pleasure was based as much on the fact that the news had arrived on the EmperorвҖҷs birthday as on the fact of the victory itself. It was like a successfully arranged surprise. Mention was made in KutГәzovвҖҷs report of the Russian losses, among which figured the names of TГәchkov, BagratiГіn, and KutГЎysov. In the Petersburg world this sad side of the affair again involuntarily centered round a single incident: KutГЎysovвҖҷs death. Everybody knew him, the Emperor liked him, and he was young and interesting. That day everyone met with the words: вҖңWhat a wonderful coincidence! Just during the service. But what a loss KutГЎysov is! How sorry I am!вҖқ вҖңWhat did I tell about KutГәzov?вҖқ Prince VasГӯli now said with a prophetвҖҷs pride. вҖңI always said he was the only man capable of defeating Napoleon.вҖқ But next day no news arrived from the army and the public mood grew anxious. The courtiers suffered because of the suffering the suspense occasioned the Emperor. вҖңFancy the EmperorвҖҷs position!вҖқ said they, and instead of extolling KutГәzov as they had done the day before, they condemned him as the cause of the EmperorвҖҷs anxiety. That day Prince VasГӯli no longer boasted of his protГ©gГ© KutГәzov, but remained silent when the commander in chief was mentioned. Moreover, toward evening, as if everything conspired to make Petersburg society anxious and uneasy, a terrible piece of news was added. Countess ElГЁna BezГәkhova had suddenly died of that terrible malady it had been so agreeable to mention. Officially, at large gatherings, everyone said that Countess BezГәkhova had died of a terrible attack of angina pectoris, but in intimate circles details were mentioned of how the private physician of the Queen of Spain had prescribed small doses of a certain drug to produce a certain effect; but ElГЁn, tortured by the fact that the old count suspected her and that her husband to whom she had written (that wretched, profligate Pierre) had not replied, had suddenly taken a very large dose of the drug, and had died in agony before assistance could be rendered her. It was said that Prince VasГӯli and the old count had turned upon the Italian, but the latter had produced such letters from the unfortunate deceased that they had immediately let the matter drop. Talk in general centered round three melancholy facts: the EmperorвҖҷs lack of news, the loss of KutГЎysov, and the death of ElГЁn. On the third day after KutГәzovвҖҷs report a country gentleman arrived from Moscow, and news of the surrender of Moscow to the French spread through the whole town. This was terrible! What a position for the Emperor to be in! KutГәzov was a traitor, and Prince VasГӯli during the visits of condolence paid to him on the occasion of his daughterвҖҷs death said of KutГәzov, whom he had formerly praised (it was excusable for him in his grief to forget what he had said), that it was impossible to expect anything else from a blind and depraved old man. вҖңI only wonder that the fate of Russia could have been entrusted to such a man.вҖқ As long as this news remained unofficial it was possible to doubt it, but the next day the following communication was received from Count RostopchГӯn: Prince KutГәzovвҖҷs adjutant has brought me a letter in which he demands police officers to guide the army to the RyazГЎn road. He writes that he is regretfully abandoning Moscow. Sire! KutГәzovвҖҷs action decides the fate of the capital and of your empire! Russia will shudder to learn of the abandonment of the city in which her greatness is centered and in which lie the ashes of your ancestors! I shall follow the army. I have had everything removed, and it only remains for me to weep over the fate of my fatherland. On receiving this dispatch the Emperor sent Prince VolkГіnski to KutГәzov with the following rescript: Prince MikhГЎil IlariГіnovich! Since the twenty-ninth of August I have received no communication from you, yet on the first of September I received from the commander in chief of Moscow, via YaroslГЎvl, the sad news that you, with the army, have decided to abandon Moscow. You can yourself imagine the effect this news has had on me, and your silence increases my astonishment. I am sending this by Adjutant-General Prince VolkГіnski, to hear from you the situation of the army and the reasons that have induced you to take this melancholy decision. III III Nine days after the abandonment of Moscow, a messenger from KutГәzov reached Petersburg with the official announcement of that event. This messenger was Michaud, a Frenchman who did not know Russian, but who was quoique Г©tranger, russe de coeur et dвҖҷГўme,109 as he said of himself. The Emperor at once received this messenger in his study at the palace on Stone Island. Michaud, who had never seen Moscow before the campaign and who did not know Russian, yet felt deeply moved (as he wrote) when he appeared before notre trГЁs gracieux souverain110 with the news of the burning of Moscow, dont les flammes Г©clairaient sa route.111 Though the source of M. MichaudвҖҷs chagrin must have been different from that which caused Russians to grieve, he had such a sad face when shown into the EmperorвҖҷs study that the latter at once asked: вҖңHave you brought me sad news, Colonel?вҖқ вҖңVery sad, sire,вҖқ replied Michaud, lowering his eyes with a sigh. вҖңThe abandonment of Moscow.вҖқ вҖңHave they surrendered my ancient capital without a battle?вҖқ asked the Emperor quickly, his face suddenly flushing. Michaud respectfully delivered the message KutГәzov had entrusted to him, which was that it had been impossible to fight before Moscow, and that as the only remaining choice was between losing the army as well as Moscow, or losing Moscow alone, the field marshal had to choose the latter. The Emperor listened in silence, not looking at Michaud. вҖңHas the enemy entered the city?вҖқ he asked. вҖңYes, sire, and Moscow is now in ashes. I left it all in flames,вҖқ replied Michaud in a decided tone, but glancing at the Emperor he was frightened by what he had done. The Emperor began to breathe heavily and rapidly, his lower lip trembled, and tears instantly appeared in his fine blue eyes. But this lasted only a moment. He suddenly frowned, as if blaming himself for his weakness, and raising his head addressed Michaud in a firm voice: вҖңI see, Colonel, from all that is happening, that Providence requires great sacrifices of usвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I am ready to submit myself in all things to His will; but tell me, Michaud, how did you leave the army when it saw my ancient capital abandoned without a battle? Did you not notice discouragement?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Seeing that his most gracious ruler was calm once more, Michaud also grew calm, but was not immediately ready to reply to the EmperorвҖҷs direct and relevant question which required a direct answer. вҖңSire, will you allow me to speak frankly as befits a loyal soldier?вҖқ he asked to gain time. вҖңColonel, I always require it,вҖқ replied the Emperor. вҖңConceal nothing from me, I wish to know absolutely how things are.вҖқ вҖңSire!вҖқ said Michaud with a subtle, scarcely perceptible smile on his lips, having now prepared a well-phrased reply, вҖңsire, I left the whole army, from its chiefs to the lowest soldier, without exception in desperate and agonized terrorвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңHow is that?вҖқ the Emperor interrupted him, frowning sternly. вҖңWould misfortune make my Russians lose heart?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Never!вҖқ Michaud had only waited for this to bring out the phrase he had prepared. вҖңSire,вҖқ he said, with respectful playfulness, вҖңthey are only afraid lest Your Majesty, in the goodness of your heart, should allow yourself to be persuaded to make peace. They are burning for the combat,вҖқ declared this representative of the Russian nation, вҖңand to prove to Your Majesty by the sacrifice of their lives how devoted they are.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңAh!вҖқ said the Emperor reassured, and with a kindly gleam in his eyes, he patted Michaud on the shoulder. вҖңYou set me at ease, Colonel.вҖқ He bent his head and was silent for some time. вҖңWell, then, go back to the army,вҖқ he said, drawing himself up to his full height and addressing Michaud with a gracious and majestic gesture, вҖңand tell our brave men and all my good subjects wherever you go that when I have not a soldier left I shall put myself at the head of my beloved nobility and my good peasants and so use the last resources of my empire. It still offers me more than my enemies suppose,вҖқ said the Emperor growing more and more animated; вҖңbut should it ever be ordained by Divine Providence,вҖқ he continued, raising to heaven his fine eyes shining with emotion, вҖңthat my dynasty should cease to reign on the throne of my ancestors, then after exhausting all the means at my command, I shall let my beard grow to hereвҖқ (he pointed halfway down his chest) вҖңand go and eat potatoes with the meanest of my peasants, rather than sign the disgrace of my country and of my beloved people whose sacrifices I know how to appreciate.вҖқ Having uttered these words in an agitated voice the Emperor suddenly turned away as if to hide from Michaud the tears that rose to his eyes, and went to the further end of his study. Having stood there a few moments, he strode back to Michaud and pressed his arm below the elbow with a vigorous movement. The EmperorвҖҷs mild and handsome face was flushed and his eyes gleamed with resolution and anger. вҖңColonel Michaud, do not forget what I say to you here, perhaps we may recall it with pleasure somedayвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Napoleon or I,вҖқ said the Emperor, touching his breast. вҖңWe can no longer both reign together. I have learned to know him, and he will not deceive me any more.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ And the Emperor paused, with a frown. When he heard these words and saw the expression of firm resolution in the EmperorвҖҷs eyes, MichaudвҒ вҖ”quoique Г©tranger, russe de coeur et dвҖҷГўme,вҒ вҖ”at that solemn moment felt himself enraptured by all that he had heard (as he used afterwards to say), and gave expression to his own feelings and those of the Russian people whose representative he considered himself to be, in the following words: вҖңSire!вҖқ said he, вҖңYour Majesty is at this moment signing the glory of the nation and the salvation of Europe!вҖқ With an inclination of the head the Emperor dismissed him. IV IV It is natural for us who were not living in those days to imagine that when half Russia had been conquered and the inhabitants were fleeing to distant provinces, and one levy after another was being raised for the defense of the fatherland, all Russians from the greatest to the least were solely engaged in sacrificing themselves, saving their fatherland, or weeping over its downfall. The tales and descriptions of that time without exception speak only of the self-sacrifice, patriotic devotion, despair, grief, and the heroism of the Russians. But it was not really so. It appears so to us because we see only the general historic interest of that time and do not see all the personal human interests that people had. Yet in reality those personal interests of the moment so much transcend the general interests that they always prevent the public interest from being felt or even noticed. Most of the people at that time paid no attention to the general progress of events but were guided only by their private interests, and they were the very people whose activities at that period were most useful. Those who tried to understand the general course of events and to take part in it by self-sacrifice and heroism were the most useless members of society, they saw everything upside down, and all they did for the common good turned out to be useless and foolishвҒ вҖ”like PierreвҖҷs and MamГіnovвҖҷs regiments which looted Russian villages, and the lint the young ladies prepared and that never reached the wounded, and so on. Even those, fond of intellectual talk and of expressing their feelings, who discussed RussiaвҖҷs position at the time involuntarily introduced into their conversation either a shade of pretense and falsehood or useless condemnation and anger directed against people accused of actions no one could possibly be guilty of. In historic events the rule forbidding us to eat of the fruit of the Tree of Knowledge is specially applicable. Only unconscious action bears fruit, and he who plays a part in an historic event never understands its significance. If he tries to realize it his efforts are fruitless. The more closely a man was engaged in the events then taking place in Russia the less did he realize their significance. In Petersburg and in the provinces at a distance from Moscow, ladies, and gentlemen in militia uniforms, wept for Russia and its ancient capital and talked of self-sacrifice and so on; but in the army which retired beyond Moscow there was little talk or thought of Moscow, and when they caught sight of its burned ruins no one swore to be avenged on the French, but they thought about their next pay, their next quarters, of MatrГ«shka the vivandiГЁre, and like matters. As the war had caught him in the service, NikolГЎy RostГіv took a close and prolonged part in the defense of his country, but did so casually, without any aim at self-sacrifice, and he therefore looked at what was going on in Russia without despair and without dismally racking his brains over it. Had he been asked what he thought of the state of Russia, he would have said that it was not his business to think about it, that KutГәzov and others were there for that purpose, but that he had heard that the regiments were to be made up to their full strength, that fighting would probably go on for a long time yet, and that things being so it was quite likely he might be in command of a regiment in a couple of yearsвҖҷ time. As he looked at the matter in this way, he learned that he was being sent to VorГіnezh to buy remounts for his division, not only without regret at being prevented from taking part in the coming battle, but with the greatest pleasureвҒ вҖ”which he did not conceal and which his comrades fully understood. A few days before the battle of BorodinГі, NikolГЎy received the necessary money and warrants, and having sent some hussars on in advance, he set out with post horses for VorГіnezh. Only a man who has experienced itвҒ вҖ”that is, has passed some months continuously in an atmosphere of campaigning and warвҒ вҖ”can understand the delight NikolГЎy felt when he escaped from the region covered by the armyвҖҷs foraging operations, provision trains, and hospitals. WhenвҒ вҖ”free from soldiers, wagons, and the filthy traces of a campвҒ вҖ”he saw villages with peasants and peasant women, gentlemenвҖҷs country houses, fields where cattle were grazing, posthouses with stationmasters asleep in them, he rejoiced as though seeing all this for the first time. What for a long while specially surprised and delighted him were the women, young and healthy, without a dozen officers making up to each of them; women, too, who were pleased and flattered that a passing officer should joke with them. In the highest spirits NikolГЎy arrived at night at a hotel in VorГіnezh, ordered things he had long been deprived of in camp, and next day, very clean-shaven and in a full-dress uniform he had not worn for a long time, went to present himself to the authorities. The commander of the militia was a civilian general, an old man who was evidently pleased with his military designation and rank. He received NikolГЎy brusquely (imagining this to be characteristically military) and questioned him with an important air, as if considering the general progress of affairs and approving and disapproving with full right to do so. NikolГЎy was in such good spirits that this merely amused him. From the commander of the militia he drove to the governor. The governor was a brisk little man, very simple and affable. He indicated the stud farms at which NikolГЎy might procure horses, recommended to him a horse dealer in the town and a landowner fourteen miles out of town who had the best horses, and promised to assist him in every way. вҖңYou are Count IlyГЎ AndrГ©evichвҖҷs son? My wife was a great friend of your motherвҖҷs. We are at home on ThursdaysвҒ вҖ”today is Thursday, so please come and see us quite informally,вҖқ said the governor, taking leave of him. Immediately on leaving the governorвҖҷs, NikolГЎy hired post horses and, taking his squadron quartermaster with him, drove at a gallop to the landowner, fourteen miles away, who had the stud. Everything seemed to him pleasant and easy during that first part of his stay in VorГіnezh and, as usually happens when a man is in a pleasant state of mind, everything went well and easily. The landowner to whom NikolГЎy went was a bachelor, an old cavalryman, a horse fancier, a sportsman, the possessor of some century-old brandy and some old Hungarian wine, who had a snuggery where he smoked, and who owned some splendid horses. In very few words NikolГЎy bought seventeen picked stallions for six thousand rublesвҒ вҖ”to serve, as he said, as samples of his remounts. After dining and taking rather too much of the Hungarian wine, NikolГЎyвҒ вҖ”having exchanged kisses with the landowner, with whom he was already on the friendliest termsвҒ вҖ”galloped back over abominable roads, in the brightest frame of mind, continually urging on the driver so as to be in time for the governorвҖҷs party. When he had changed, poured water over his head, and scented himself, NikolГЎy arrived at the governorвҖҷs rather late, but with the phrase вҖңbetter late than neverвҖқ on his lips. It was not a ball, nor had dancing been announced, but everyone knew that KaterГӯna PetrГіvna would play valses and the Г©cossaise on the clavichord and that there would be dancing, and so everyone had come as to a ball. Provincial life in 1812 went on very much as usual, but with this difference, that it was livelier in the towns in consequence of the arrival of many wealthy families from Moscow, and as in everything that went on in Russia at that time a special recklessness was noticeable, an вҖңin for a penny, in for a poundвҒ вҖ”who cares?вҖқ spirit, and the inevitable small talk, instead of turning on the weather and mutual acquaintances, now turned on Moscow, the army, and Napoleon. The society gathered together at the governorвҖҷs was the best in VorГіnezh. There were a great many ladies and some of NikolГЎyвҖҷs Moscow acquaintances, but there were no men who could at all vie with the cavalier of St.В George, the hussar remount officer, the good-natured and well-bred Count RostГіv. Among the men was an Italian prisoner, an officer of the French army; and NikolГЎy felt that the presence of that prisoner enhanced his own importance as a Russian hero. The Italian was, as it were, a war trophy. NikolГЎy felt this, it seemed to him that everyone regarded the Italian in the same light, and he treated him cordially though with dignity and restraint. As soon as NikolГЎy entered in his hussar uniform, diffusing around him a fragrance of perfume and wine, and had uttered the words вҖңbetter late than neverвҖқ and heard them repeated several times by others, people clustered around him; all eyes turned on him, and he felt at once that he had entered into his proper position in the provinceвҒ вҖ”that of a universal favorite: a very pleasant position, and intoxicatingly so after his long privations. At posting stations, at inns, and in the landownerвҖҷs snuggery, maidservants had been flattered by his notice, and here too at the governorвҖҷs party there were (as it seemed to NikolГЎy) an inexhaustible number of pretty young women, married and unmarried, impatiently awaiting his notice. The women and girls flirted with him and, from the first day, the people concerned themselves to get this fine young daredevil of an hussar married and settled down. Among these was the governorвҖҷs wife herself, who welcomed RostГіv as a near relative and called him вҖңNicolas.вҖқ KaterГӯna PetrГіvna did actually play valses and the Г©cossaise, and dancing began in which NikolГЎy still further captivated the provincial society by his agility. His particularly free manner of dancing even surprised them all. NikolГЎy was himself rather surprised at the way he danced that evening. He had never danced like that in Moscow and would even have considered such a very free and easy manner improper and in bad form, but here he felt it incumbent on him to astonish them all by something unusual, something they would have to accept as the regular thing in the capital though new to them in the provinces. All the evening NikolГЎy paid attention to a blue-eyed, plump and pleasing little blonde, the wife of one of the provincial officials. With the naive conviction of young men in a merry mood that other menвҖҷs wives were created for them, RostГіv did not leave the ladyвҖҷs side and treated her husband in a friendly and conspiratorial style, as if, without speaking of it, they knew how capitally NikolГЎy and the lady would get on together. The husband, however, did not seem to share that conviction and tried to behave morosely with RostГіv. But the latterвҖҷs good-natured naivete was so boundless that sometimes even he involuntarily yielded to NikolГЎyвҖҷs good humor. Toward the end of the evening, however, as the wifeвҖҷs face grew more flushed and animated, the husbandвҖҷs became more and more melancholy and solemn, as though there were but a given amount of animation between them and as the wifeвҖҷs share increased the husbandвҖҷs diminished. V V NikolГЎy sat leaning slightly forward in an armchair, bending closely over the blonde lady and paying her mythological compliments with a smile that never left his face. Jauntily shifting the position of his legs in their tight riding breeches, diffusing an odor of perfume, and admiring his partner, himself, and the fine outlines of his legs in their well-fitting Hessian boots, NikolГЎy told the blonde lady that he wished to run away with a certain lady here in VorГіnezh. вҖңWhich lady?вҖқ вҖңA charming lady, a divine one. Her eyesвҖқ (NikolГЎy looked at his partner) вҖңare blue, her mouth coral and ivory; her figureвҖқ (he glanced at her shoulders) вҖңlike DianaвҖҷs.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ The husband came up and sullenly asked his wife what she was talking about. вҖңAh, NikГӯta IvГЎnych!вҖқ cried NikolГЎy, rising politely, and as if wishing NikГӯta IvГЎnych to share his joke, he began to tell him of his intention to elope with a blonde lady. The husband smiled gloomily, the wife gaily. The governorвҖҷs good-natured wife came up with a look of disapproval. вҖңAnna IgnГЎtyevna wants to see you, Nicolas,вҖқ said she, pronouncing the name so that NikolГЎy at once understood that Anna IgnГЎtyevna was a very important person. вҖңCome, Nicolas! You know you let me call you so?вҖқ вҖңOh, yes, Aunt. Who is she?вҖқ вҖңAnna IgnГЎtyevna MalvГӯntseva. She has heard from her niece how you rescued her.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Can you guess?вҖқ вҖңI rescued such a lot of them!вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңHer niece, Princess BolkГіnskaya. She is here in VorГіnezh with her aunt. Oho! How you blush. Why, areвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ?вҖқ вҖңNot a bit! Please donвҖҷt, Aunt!вҖқ вҖңVery well, very well!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Oh, what a fellow you are!вҖқ The governorвҖҷs wife led him up to a tall and very stout old lady with a blue headdress, who had just finished her game of cards with the most important personages of the town. This was MalvГӯntseva, Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs aunt on her motherвҖҷs side, a rich, childless widow who always lived in VorГіnezh. When RostГіv approached her she was standing settling up for the game. She looked at him and, screwing up her eyes sternly, continued to upbraid the general who had won from her. вҖңVery pleased, mon cher,вҖқ she then said, holding out her hand to NikolГЎy. вҖңPray come and see me.вҖқ After a few words about Princess MГЎrya and her late father, whom MalvГӯntseva had evidently not liked, and having asked what NikolГЎy knew of Prince AndrГ©y, who also was evidently no favorite of hers, the important old lady dismissed NikolГЎy after repeating her invitation to come to see her. NikolГЎy promised to come and blushed again as he bowed. At the mention of Princess MГЎrya he experienced a feeling of shyness and even of fear, which he himself did not understand. When he had parted from MalvГӯntseva NikolГЎy wished to return to the dancing, but the governorвҖҷs little wife placed her plump hand on his sleeve and, saying that she wanted to have a talk with him, led him to her sitting room, from which those who were there immediately withdrew so as not to be in her way. вҖңDo you know, dear boy,вҖқ began the governorвҖҷs wife with a serious expression on her kind little face, вҖңthat really would be the match for you: would you like me to arrange it?вҖқ вҖңWhom do you mean, Aunt?вҖқ asked NikolГЎy. вҖңI will make a match for you with the princess. KaterГӯna PetrГіvna speaks of Lily, but I say, noвҒ вҖ”the princess! Do you want me to do it? I am sure your mother will be grateful to me. What a charming girl she is, really! And she is not at all so plain, either.вҖқ вҖңNot at all,вҖқ replied NikolГЎy as if offended at the idea. вҖңAs befits a soldier, Aunt, I donвҖҷt force myself on anyone or refuse anything,вҖқ he said before he had time to consider what he was saying. вҖңWell then, remember, this is not a joke!вҖқ вҖңOf course not!вҖқ вҖңYes, yes,вҖқ the governorвҖҷs wife said as if talking to herself. вҖңBut, my dear boy, among other things you are too attentive to the other, the blonde. One is sorry for the husband, really.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңOh no, we are good friends with him,вҖқ said NikolГЎy in the simplicity of his heart; it did not enter his head that a pastime so pleasant to himself might not be pleasant to someone else. вҖңBut what nonsense I have been saying to the governorвҖҷs wife!вҖқ thought NikolГЎy suddenly at supper. вҖңShe will really begin to arrange a matchвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and SГіnyaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ?вҖқ And on taking leave of the governorвҖҷs wife, when she again smilingly said to him, вҖңWell then, remember!вҖқ he drew her aside. вҖңBut see here, to tell the truth, AuntвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңWhat is it, my dear? Come, letвҖҷs sit down here,вҖқ said she. NikolГЎy suddenly felt a desire and need to tell his most intimate thoughts (which he would not have told to his mother, his sister, or his friend) to this woman who was almost a stranger. When he afterwards recalled that impulse to unsolicited and inexplicable frankness which had very important results for him, it seemed to himвҒ вҖ”as it seems to everyone in such casesвҒ вҖ”that it was merely some silly whim that seized him: yet that burst of frankness, together with other trifling events, had immense consequences for him and for all his family. вҖңYou see, Aunt, Mamma has long wanted me to marry an heiress, but the very idea of marrying for money is repugnant to me.вҖқ вҖңOh yes, I understand,вҖқ said the governorвҖҷs wife. вҖңBut Princess BolkГіnskayaвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs another matter. I will tell you the truth. In the first place I like her very much, I feel drawn to her; and then, after I met her under such circumstancesвҒ вҖ”so strangely, the idea often occurred to me: вҖҳThis is fate.вҖҷ Especially if you remember that Mamma had long been thinking of it; but I had never happened to meet her before, somehow it had always happened that we did not meet. And as long as my sister NatГЎsha was engaged to her brother it was of course out of the question for me to think of marrying her. And it must needs happen that I should meet her just when NatГЎshaвҖҷs engagement had been broken offвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and then everythingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ So you seeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I never told this to anyone and never will, only to you.вҖқ The governorвҖҷs wife pressed his elbow gratefully. вҖңYou know SГіnya, my cousin? I love her, and promised to marry her, and will do so.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ So you see there can be no question aboutвҒ вҖ”вҖқ said NikolГЎy incoherently and blushing. вҖңMy dear boy, what a way to look at it! You know SГіnya has nothing and you yourself say your PapaвҖҷs affairs are in a very bad way. And what about your mother? It would kill her, thatвҖҷs one thing. And what sort of life would it be for SГіnyaвҒ вҖ”if sheвҖҷs a girl with a heart? Your mother in despair, and you all ruined.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No, my dear, you and SГіnya ought to understand that.вҖқ NikolГЎy remained silent. It comforted him to hear these arguments. вҖңAll the same, Aunt, it is impossible,вҖқ he rejoined with a sigh, after a short pause. вҖңBesides, would the princess have me? And besides, she is now in mourning. How can one think of it!вҖқ вҖңBut you donвҖҷt suppose IвҖҷm going to get you married at once? There is always a right way of doing things,вҖқ replied the governorвҖҷs wife. вҖңWhat a matchmaker you are, AuntвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said Nicolas, kissing her plump little hand. VI VI On reaching Moscow after her meeting with RostГіv, Princess MГЎrya had found her nephew there with his tutor, and a letter from Prince AndrГ©y giving her instructions how to get to her Aunt MalvГӯntseva at VorГіnezh. That feeling akin to temptation which had tormented her during her fatherвҖҷs illness, since his death, and especially since her meeting with RostГіv was smothered by arrangements for the journey, anxiety about her brother, settling in a new house, meeting new people, and attending to her nephewвҖҷs education. She was sad. Now, after a month passed in quiet surroundings, she felt more and more deeply the loss of her father which was associated in her mind with the ruin of Russia. She was agitated and incessantly tortured by the thought of the dangers to which her brother, the only intimate person now remaining to her, was exposed. She was worried too about her nephewвҖҷs education for which she had always felt herself incompetent, but in the depths of her soul she felt at peaceвҒ вҖ”a peace arising from consciousness of having stifled those personal dreams and hopes that had been on the point of awakening within her and were related to her meeting with RostГіv. The day after her party the governorвҖҷs wife came to see MalvГӯntseva and, after discussing her plan with the aunt, remarked that though under present circumstances a formal betrothal was, of course, not to be thought of, all the same the young people might be brought together and could get to know one another. MalvГӯntseva expressed approval, and the governorвҖҷs wife began to speak of RostГіv in MГЎryaвҖҷs presence, praising him and telling how he had blushed when Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs name was mentioned. But Princess MГЎrya experienced a painful rather than a joyful feelingвҒ вҖ”her mental tranquillity was destroyed, and desires, doubts, self-reproach, and hopes reawoke. During the two days that elapsed before RostГіv called, Princess MГЎrya continually thought of how she ought to behave to him. First she decided not to come to the drawing room when he called to see her auntвҒ вҖ”that it would not be proper for her, in her deep mourning, to receive visitors; then she thought this would be rude after what he had done for her; then it occurred to her that her aunt and the governorвҖҷs wife had intentions concerning herself and RostГіvвҒ вҖ”their looks and words at times seemed to confirm this suppositionвҒ вҖ”then she told herself that only she, with her sinful nature, could think this of them: they could not forget that situated as she was, while still wearing deep mourning, such matchmaking would be an insult to her and to her fatherвҖҷs memory. Assuming that she did go down to see him, Princess MГЎrya imagined the words he would say to her and what she would say to him, and these words sometimes seemed undeservedly cold and then to mean too much. More than anything she feared lest the confusion she felt might overwhelm her and betray her as soon as she saw him. But when on Sunday after church the footman announced in the drawing room that Count RostГіv had called, the princess showed no confusion, only a slight blush suffused her cheeks and her eyes lit up with a new and radiant light. вҖңYou have met him, Aunt?вҖқ said she in a calm voice, unable herself to understand that she could be outwardly so calm and natural. When RostГіv entered the room, the princess dropped her eyes for an instant, as if to give the visitor time to greet her aunt, and then just as NikolГЎy turned to her she raised her head and met his look with shining eyes. With a movement full of dignity and grace she half rose with a smile of pleasure, held out her slender, delicate hand to him, and began to speak in a voice in which for the first time new deep womanly notes vibrated. Mademoiselle Bourienne, who was in the drawing room, looked at Princess MГЎrya in bewildered surprise. Herself a consummate coquette, she could not have maneuvered better on meeting a man she wished to attract. вҖңEither black is particularly becoming to her or she really has greatly improved without my having noticed it. And above all, what tact and grace!вҖқ thought Mademoiselle Bourienne. Had Princess MГЎrya been capable of reflection at that moment, she would have been more surprised than Mademoiselle Bourienne at the change that had taken place in herself. From the moment she recognized that dear, loved face, a new life force took possession of her and compelled her to speak and act apart from her own will. From the time RostГіv entered, her face became suddenly transformed. It was as if a light had been kindled in a carved and painted lantern and the intricate, skillful, artistic work on its sides, that previously seemed dark, coarse, and meaningless, was suddenly shown up in unexpected and striking beauty. For the first time all that pure, spiritual, inward travail through which she had lived appeared on the surface. All her inward labor, her dissatisfaction with herself, her sufferings, her strivings after goodness, her meekness, love, and self-sacrificeвҒ вҖ”all this now shone in those radiant eyes, in her delicate smile, and in every trait of her gentle face. RostГіv saw all this as clearly as if he had known her whole life. He felt that the being before him was quite different from, and better than, anyone he had met before, and above all better than himself. Their conversation was very simple and unimportant. They spoke of the war, and like everyone else unconsciously exaggerated their sorrow about it; they spoke of their last meetingвҒ вҖ”NikolГЎy trying to change the subjectвҒ вҖ”they talked of the governorвҖҷs kind wife, of NikolГЎyвҖҷs relations, and of Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs. She did not talk about her brother, diverting the conversation as soon as her aunt mentioned AndrГ©y. Evidently she could speak of RussiaвҖҷs misfortunes with a certain artificiality, but her brother was too near her heart and she neither could nor would speak lightly of him. NikolГЎy noticed this, as he noticed every shade of Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs character with an observation unusual to him, and everything confirmed his conviction that she was a quite unusual and extraordinary being. NikolГЎy blushed and was confused when people spoke to him about the princess (as she did when he was mentioned) and even when he thought of her, but in her presence he felt quite at ease, and said not at all what he had prepared, but what, quite appropriately, occurred to him at the moment. When a pause occurred during his short visit, NikolГЎy, as is usual when there are children, turned to Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs little son, caressing him and asking whether he would like to be an hussar. He took the boy on his knee, played with him, and looked round at Princess MГЎrya. With a softened, happy, timid look she watched the boy she loved in the arms of the man she loved. NikolГЎy also noticed that look and, as if understanding it, flushed with pleasure and began to kiss the boy with good natured playfulness. As she was in mourning Princess MГЎrya did not go out into society, and NikolГЎy did not think it the proper thing to visit her again; but all the same the governorвҖҷs wife went on with her matchmaking, passing on to NikolГЎy the flattering things Princess MГЎrya said of him and vice versa, and insisting on his declaring himself to Princess MГЎrya. For this purpose she arranged a meeting between the young people at the bishopвҖҷs house before Mass. Though RostГіv told the governorвҖҷs wife that he would not make any declaration to Princess MГЎrya, he promised to go. As at Tilsit RostГіv had not allowed himself to doubt that what everybody considered right was right, so now, after a short but sincere struggle between his effort to arrange his life by his own sense of justice, and in obedient submission to circumstances, he chose the latter and yielded to the power he felt irresistibly carrying him he knew not where. He knew that after his promise to SГіnya it would be what he deemed base to declare his feelings to Princess MГЎrya. And he knew that he would never act basely. But he also knew (or rather felt at the bottom of his heart) that by resigning himself now to the force of circumstances and to those who were guiding him, he was not only doing nothing wrong, but was doing something very importantвҒ вҖ”more important than anything he had ever done in his life. After meeting Princess MГЎrya, though the course of his life went on externally as before, all his former amusements lost their charm for him and he often thought about her. But he never thought about her as he had thought of all the young ladies without exception whom he had met in society, nor as he had for a long time, and at one time rapturously, thought about SГіnya. He had pictured each of those young ladies as almost all honest-hearted young men do, that is, as a possible wife, adapting her in his imagination to all the conditions of married life: a white dressing gown, his wife at the tea table, his wifeвҖҷs carriage, little ones, Mamma and Papa, their relations to her, and so onвҒ вҖ”and these pictures of the future had given him pleasure. But with Princess MГЎrya, to whom they were trying to get him engaged, he could never picture anything of future married life. If he tried, his pictures seemed incongruous and false. It made him afraid. VII VII The dreadful news of the battle of BorodinГі, of our losses in killed and wounded, and the still more terrible news of the loss of Moscow reached VorГіnezh in the middle of September. Princess MГЎrya, having learned of her brotherвҖҷs wound only from the Gazette and having no definite news of him, prepared (so NikolГЎy heard, he had not seen her again himself) to set off in search of Prince AndrГ©y. When he received the news of the battle of BorodinГі and the abandonment of Moscow, RostГіv was not seized with despair, anger, the desire for vengeance, or any feeling of that kind, but everything in VorГіnezh suddenly seemed to him dull and tiresome, and he experienced an indefinite feeling of shame and awkwardness. The conversations he heard seemed to him insincere; he did not know how to judge all these affairs and felt that only in the regiment would everything again become clear to him. He made haste to finish buying the horses, and often became unreasonably angry with his servant and squadron quartermaster. A few days before his departure a special thanksgiving, at which NikolГЎy was present, was held in the cathedral for the Russian victory. He stood a little behind the governor and held himself with military decorum through the service, meditating on a great variety of subjects. When the service was over the governorвҖҷs wife beckoned him to her. вҖңHave you seen the princess?вҖқ she asked, indicating with a movement of her head a lady standing on the opposite side, beyond the choir. NikolГЎy immediately recognized Princess MГЎrya not so much by the profile he saw under her bonnet as by the feeling of solicitude, timidity, and pity that immediately overcame him. Princess MГЎrya, evidently engrossed by her thoughts, was crossing herself for the last time before leaving the church. NikolГЎy looked at her face with surprise. It was the same face he had seen before, there was the same general expression of refined, inner, spiritual labor, but now it was quite differently lit up. There was a pathetic expression of sorrow, prayer, and hope in it. As had occurred before when she was present, NikolГЎy went up to her without waiting to be prompted by the governorвҖҷs wife and not asking himself whether or not it was right and proper to address her here in church, and told her he had heard of her trouble and sympathized with his whole soul. As soon as she heard his voice a vivid glow kindled in her face, lighting up both her sorrow and her joy. вҖңThere is one thing I wanted to tell you, Princess,вҖқ said RostГіv. вҖңIt is that if your brother, Prince AndrГ©y NikolГЎevich, were not living, it would have been at once announced in the Gazette, as he is a colonel.вҖқ The princess looked at him, not grasping what he was saying, but cheered by the expression of regretful sympathy on his face. вҖңAnd I have known so many cases of a splinter woundвҖқ (the Gazette said it was a shell) вҖңeither proving fatal at once or being very slight,вҖқ continued NikolГЎy. вҖңWe must hope for the best, and I am sureвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Princess MГЎrya interrupted him. вҖңOh, that would be so dreadвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she began and, prevented by agitation from finishing, she bent her head with a movement as graceful as everything she did in his presence and, looking up at him gratefully, went out, following her aunt. That evening NikolГЎy did not go out, but stayed at home to settle some accounts with the horse dealers. When he had finished that business it was already too late to go anywhere but still too early to go to bed, and for a long time he paced up and down the room, reflecting on his life, a thing he rarely did. Princess MГЎrya had made an agreeable impression on him when he had met her in SmolГ©nsk province. His having encountered her in such exceptional circumstances, and his mother having at one time mentioned her to him as a good match, had drawn his particular attention to her. When he met her again in VorГіnezh the impression she made on him was not merely pleasing but powerful. NikolГЎy had been struck by the peculiar moral beauty he observed in her at this time. He was, however, preparing to go away and it had not entered his head to regret that he was thus depriving himself of chances of meeting her. But that dayвҖҷs encounter in church had, he felt, sunk deeper than was desirable for his peace of mind. That pale, sad, refined face, that radiant look, those gentle graceful gestures, and especially the deep and tender sorrow expressed in all her features agitated him and evoked his sympathy. In men RostГіv could not bear to see the expression of a higher spiritual life (that was why he did not like Prince AndrГ©y) and he referred to it contemptuously as philosophy and dreaminess, but in Princess MГЎrya that very sorrow which revealed the depth of a whole spiritual world foreign to him was an irresistible attraction. вҖңShe must be a wonderful woman. A real angel!вҖқ he said to himself. вҖңWhy am I not free? Why was I in such a hurry with SГіnya?вҖқ And he involuntarily compared the two: the lack of spirituality in the one and the abundance of it in the otherвҒ вҖ”a spirituality he himself lacked and therefore valued most highly. He tried to picture what would happen were he free. How he would propose to her and how she would become his wife. But no, he could not imagine that. He felt awed, and no clear picture presented itself to his mind. He had long ago pictured to himself a future with SГіnya, and that was all clear and simple just because it had all been thought out and he knew all there was in SГіnya, but it was impossible to picture a future with Princess MГЎrya, because he did not understand her but simply loved her. Reveries about SГіnya had had something merry and playful in them, but to dream of Princess MГЎrya was always difficult and a little frightening. вҖңHow she prayed!вҖқ he thought. вҖңIt was plain that her whole soul was in her prayer. Yes, that was the prayer that moves mountains, and I am sure her prayer will be answered. Why donвҖҷt I pray for what I want?вҖқ he suddenly thought. вҖңWhat do I want? To be free, released from SГіnyaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She was right,вҖқ he thought, remembering what the governorвҖҷs wife had said: вҖңNothing but misfortune can come of marrying SГіnya. Muddles, grief for MammaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ business difficultiesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ muddles, terrible muddles! Besides, I donвҖҷt love herвҒ вҖ”not as I should. O, God! release me from this dreadful, inextricable position!вҖқ he suddenly began to pray. вҖңYes, prayer can move mountains, but one must have faith and not pray as NatГЎsha and I used to as children, that the snow might turn into sugarвҒ вҖ”and then run out into the yard to see whether it had done so. No, but I am not praying for trifles now,вҖқ he thought as he put his pipe down in a corner, and folding his hands placed himself before the icon. Softened by memories of Princess MГЎrya he began to pray as he had not done for a long time. Tears were in his eyes and in his throat when the door opened and LavrГәshka came in with some papers. вҖңBlockhead! Why do you come in without being called?вҖқ cried NikolГЎy, quickly changing his attitude. вҖңFrom the governor,вҖқ said LavrГәshka in a sleepy voice. вҖңA courier has arrived and thereвҖҷs a letter for you.вҖқ вҖңWell, all right, thanks. You can go!вҖқ NikolГЎy took the two letters, one of which was from his mother and the other from SГіnya. He recognized them by the handwriting and opened SГіnyaвҖҷs first. He had read only a few lines when he turned pale and his eyes opened wide with fear and joy. вҖңNo, itвҖҷs not possible!вҖқ he cried aloud. Unable to sit still he paced up and down the room holding the letter and reading it. He glanced through it, then read it again, and then again, and standing still in the middle of the room he raised his shoulders, stretching out his hands, with his mouth wide open and his eyes fixed. What he had just been praying for with confidence that God would hear him had come to pass; but NikolГЎy was as much astonished as if it were something extraordinary and unexpected, and as if the very fact that it had happened so quickly proved that it had not come from God to whom he had prayed, but by some ordinary coincidence. This unexpected and, as it seemed to NikolГЎy, quite voluntary letter from SГіnya freed him from the knot that fettered him and from which there had seemed no escape. She wrote that the last unfortunate eventsвҒ вҖ”the loss of almost the whole of the RostГіvsвҖҷ Moscow propertyвҒ вҖ”and the countessвҖҷ repeatedly expressed wish that NikolГЎy should marry Princess BolkГіnskaya, together with his silence and coldness of late, had all combined to make her decide to release him from his promise and set him completely free. It would be too painful to me to think that I might be a cause of sorrow or discord in the family that has been so good to me (she wrote), and my love has no aim but the happiness of those I love; so, Nicolas, I beg you to consider yourself free, and to be assured that, in spite of everything, no one can love you more than does Your SГіnya Both letters were written from TrГіitsa. The other, from the countess, described their last days in Moscow, their departure, the fire, and the destruction of all their property. In this letter the countess also mentioned that Prince AndrГ©y was among the wounded traveling with them; his state was very critical, but the doctor said there was now more hope. SГіnya and NatГЎsha were nursing him. Next day NikolГЎy took his motherвҖҷs letter and went to see Princess MГЎrya. Neither he nor she said a word about what вҖңNatГЎsha nursing himвҖқ might mean, but thanks to this letter NikolГЎy suddenly became almost as intimate with the princess as if they were relations. The following day he saw Princess MГЎrya off on her journey to YaroslГЎvl, and a few days later left to rejoin his regiment. VIII VIII SГіnyaвҖҷs letter written from TrГіitsa, which had come as an answer to NikolГЎyвҖҷs prayer, was prompted by this: the thought of getting NikolГЎy married to an heiress occupied the old countessвҖҷ mind more and more.
