But DГіlokhov, who in Moscow had worn a Persian costume, had now the appearance of a most correct officer of the Guards. He was clean-shaven and wore a GuardsmanвҖҷs padded coat with an Order of St.В George at his buttonhole and a plain forage cap set straight on his head. He took off his wet felt cloak in a corner of the room, and without greeting anyone went up to DenГӯsov and began questioning him about the matter in hand. DenГӯsov told him of the designs the large detachments had on the transport, of the message PГ©tya had brought, and his own replies to both generals. Then he told him all he knew of the French detachment. вҖңThatвҖҷs so. But we must know what troops they are and their numbers,вҖқ said DГіlokhov. вҖңIt will be necessary to go there. We canвҖҷt start the affair without knowing for certain how many there are. I like to work accurately. Here nowвҒ вҖ”wouldnвҖҷt one of these gentlemen like to ride over to the French camp with me? I have brought a spare uniform.вҖқ вҖңI, IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ IвҖҷll go with you!вҖқ cried PГ©tya. вҖңThereвҖҷs no need for you to go at all,вҖқ said DenГӯsov, addressing DГіlokhov, вҖңand as for him, I wonвҖҷt let him go on any account.вҖқ вҖңI like that!вҖқ exclaimed PГ©tya. вҖңWhy shouldnвҖҷt I go?вҖқ вҖңBecause itвҖҷs useless.вҖқ вҖңWell, you must excuse me, becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I shall go, and thatвҖҷs all. YouвҖҷll take me, wonвҖҷt you?вҖқ he said, turning to DГіlokhov. вҖңWhy not?вҖқ DГіlokhov answered absently, scrutinizing the face of the French drummer boy. вҖңHave you had that youngster with you long?вҖқ he asked DenГӯsov. вҖңHe was taken today but he knows nothing. IвҖҷm keeping him with me.вҖқ вҖңYes, and where do you put the others?вҖқ inquired DГіlokhov. вҖңWhere? I send them away and take a weceipt for them,вҖқ shouted DenГӯsov, suddenly flushing. вҖңAnd I say boldly that I have not a single manвҖҷs life on my conscience. Would it be difficult for you to send thirty or thwee hundwed men to town under escort, instead of stainingвҒ вҖ”I speak bluntlyвҒ вҖ”staining the honor of a soldier?вҖқ вҖңThat kind of amiable talk would be suitable from this young count of sixteen,вҖқ said DГіlokhov with cold irony, вҖңbut itвҖҷs time for you to drop it.вҖқ вҖңWhy, IвҖҷve not said anything! I only say that IвҖҷll certainly go with you,вҖқ said PГ©tya shyly. вҖңBut for you and me, old fellow, itвҖҷs time to drop these amenities,вҖқ continued DГіlokhov, as if he found particular pleasure in speaking of this subject which irritated DenГӯsov. вҖңNow, why have you kept this lad?вҖқ he went on, swaying his head. вҖңBecause you are sorry for him! DonвҖҷt we know those вҖҳreceiptsвҖҷ of yours? You send a hundred men away, and thirty get there. The rest either starve or get killed. So isnвҖҷt it all the same not to send them?вҖқ The esaul, screwing up his light-colored eyes, nodded approvingly. вҖңThatвҖҷs not the point. IвҖҷm not going to discuss the matter. I do not wish to take it on my conscience. You say theyвҖҷll die. All wight. Only not by my fault!вҖқ DГіlokhov began laughing. вҖңWho has told them not to capture me these twenty times over? But if they did catch me theyвҖҷd string me up to an aspen tree, and with all your chivalry just the same.вҖқ He paused. вҖңHowever, we must get to work. Tell the Cossack to fetch my kit. I have two French uniforms in it. Well, are you coming with me?вҖқ he asked PГ©tya. вҖңI? Yes, yes, certainly!вҖқ cried PГ©tya, blushing almost to tears and glancing at DenГӯsov. While DГіlokhov had been disputing with DenГӯsov what should be done with prisoners, PГ©tya had once more felt awkward and restless; but again he had no time to grasp fully what they were talking about. вҖңIf grown-up, distinguished men think so, it must be necessary and right,вҖқ thought he. вҖңBut above all DenГӯsov must not dare to imagine that IвҖҷll obey him and that he can order me about. I will certainly go to the French camp with DГіlokhov. If he can, so can I!вҖқ And to all DenГӯsovвҖҷs persuasions, PГ©tya replied that he too was accustomed to do everything accurately and not just anyhow, and that he never considered personal danger. вҖңFor youвҖҷll admit that if we donвҖҷt know for sure how many of them there areвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ hundreds of lives may depend on it, while there are only two of us. Besides, I want to go very much and certainly will go, so donвҖҷt hinder me,вҖқ said he. вҖңIt will only make things worse.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ IX IX Having put on French greatcoats and shakos, PГ©tya and DГіlokhov rode to the clearing from which DenГӯsov had reconnoitered the French camp, and emerging from the forest in pitch darkness they descended into the hollow. On reaching the bottom, DГіlokhov told the Cossacks accompanying him to await him there and rode on at a quick trot along the road to the bridge. PГ©tya, his heart in his mouth with excitement, rode by his side. вҖңIf weвҖҷre caught, I wonвҖҷt be taken alive! I have a pistol,вҖқ whispered he. вҖңDonвҖҷt talk Russian,вҖқ said DГіlokhov in a hurried whisper, and at that very moment they heard through the darkness the challenge: вҖңQui vive?вҖқ120 and the click of a musket. The blood rushed to PГ©tyaвҖҷs face and he grasped his pistol. вҖңLanciers du 6-me,вҖқ121 replied DГіlokhov, neither hastening nor slackening his horseвҖҷs pace. The black figure of a sentinel stood on the bridge. вҖңMot dвҖҷordre.вҖқ122 DГіlokhov reined in his horse and advanced at a walk. вҖңDites donc, le colonel GГ©rard est ici?вҖқ123 he asked. вҖңMot dвҖҷordre,вҖқ repeated the sentinel, barring the way and not replying. вҖңQuand un officier fait sa ronde, les sentinelles ne demandent pas le mot dвҖҷordreвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ cried DГіlokhov suddenly flaring up and riding straight at the sentinel. вҖңJe vous demande si le colonel est ici.вҖқ124 And without waiting for an answer from the sentinel, who had stepped aside, DГіlokhov rode up the incline at a walk. Noticing the black outline of a man crossing the road, DГіlokhov stopped him and inquired where the commander and officers were. The man, a soldier with a sack over his shoulder, stopped, came close up to DГіlokhovвҖҷs horse, touched it with his hand, and explained simply and in a friendly way that the commander and the officers were higher up the hill to the right in the courtyard of the farm, as he called the landownerвҖҷs house. Having ridden up the road, on both sides of which French talk could be heard around the campfires, DГіlokhov turned into the courtyard of the landownerвҖҷs house. Having ridden in, he dismounted and approached a big blazing campfire, around which sat several men talking noisily. Something was boiling in a small cauldron at the edge of the fire and a soldier in a peaked cap and blue overcoat, lit up by the fire, was kneeling beside it stirring its contents with a ramrod. вҖңOh, heвҖҷs a hard nut to crack,вҖқ said one of the officers who was sitting in the shadow at the other side of the fire. вҖңHeвҖҷll make them get a move on, those fellows!вҖқ said another, laughing. Both fell silent, peering out through the darkness at the sound of DГіlokhovвҖҷs and PГ©tyaвҖҷs steps as they advanced to the fire leading their horses. вҖңBonjour, messieurs!вҖқ125 said DГіlokhov loudly and clearly. There was a stir among the officers in the shadow beyond the fire, and one tall, long-necked officer, walking round the fire, came up to DГіlokhov. вҖңIs that you, ClГ©ment?вҖқ he asked. вҖңWhere the devilвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ?вҖқ But, noticing his mistake, he broke off short and, with a frown, greeted DГіlokhov as a stranger, asking what he could do for him. DГіlokhov said that he and his companion were trying to overtake their regiment, and addressing the company in general asked whether they knew anything of the 6th Regiment. None of them knew anything, and PГ©tya thought the officers were beginning to look at him and DГіlokhov with hostility and suspicion. For some seconds all were silent. вҖңIf you were counting on the evening soup, you have come too late,вҖқ said a voice from behind the fire with a repressed laugh. DГіlokhov replied that they were not hungry and must push on farther that night. He handed the horses over to the soldier who was stirring the pot and squatted down on his heels by the fire beside the officer with the long neck. That officer did not take his eyes from DГіlokhov and again asked to what regiment he belonged. DГіlokhov, as if he had not heard the question, did not reply, but lighting a short French pipe which he took from his pocket began asking the officer in how far the road before them was safe from Cossacks. вҖңThose brigands are everywhere,вҖқ replied an officer from behind the fire. DГіlokhov remarked that the Cossacks were a danger only to stragglers such as his companion and himself, вҖңbut probably they would not dare to attack large detachments?вҖқ he added inquiringly. No one replied. вҖңWell, now heвҖҷll come away,вҖқ PГ©tya thought every moment as he stood by the campfire listening to the talk. But DГіlokhov restarted the conversation which had dropped and began putting direct questions as to how many men there were in the battalion, how many battalions, and how many prisoners. Asking about the Russian prisoners with that detachment, DГіlokhov said: вҖңA horrid business dragging these corpses about with one! It would be better to shoot such rabble,вҖқ and burst into loud laughter, so strange that PГ©tya thought the French would immediately detect their disguise, and involuntarily took a step back from the campfire. No one replied a word to DГіlokhovвҖҷs laughter, and a French officer whom they could not see (he lay wrapped in a greatcoat) rose and whispered something to a companion. DГіlokhov got up and called to the soldier who was holding their horses. вҖңWill they bring our horses or not?вҖқ thought PГ©tya, instinctively drawing nearer to DГіlokhov. The horses were brought. вҖңGood evening, gentlemen,вҖқ said DГіlokhov. PГ©tya wished to say вҖңGood nightвҖқ but could not utter a word. The officers were whispering together. DГіlokhov was a long time mounting his horse which would not stand still, then he rode out of the yard at a footpace. PГ©tya rode beside him, longing to look round to see whether or not the French were running after them, but not daring to. Coming out onto the road DГіlokhov did not ride back across the open country, but through the village. At one spot he stopped and listened. вҖңDo you hear?вҖқ he asked. PГ©tya recognized the sound of Russian voices and saw the dark figures of Russian prisoners round their campfires. When they had descended to the bridge PГ©tya and DГіlokhov rode past the sentinel, who without saying a word paced morosely up and down it, then they descended into the hollow where the Cossacks awaited them. вҖңWell now, goodbye. Tell DenГӯsov, вҖҳat the first shot at daybreak,вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ said DГіlokhov and was about to ride away, but PГ©tya seized hold of him. вҖңReally!вҖқ he cried, вҖңyou are such a hero! Oh, how fine, how splendid! How I love you!вҖқ вҖңAll right, all right!вҖқ said DГіlokhov. But PГ©tya did not let go of him and DГіlokhov saw through the gloom that PГ©tya was bending toward him and wanted to kiss him. DГіlokhov kissed him, laughed, turned his horse, and vanished into the darkness. I I When seeing a dying animal a man feels a sense of horror: substance similar to his own is perishing before his eyes. But when it is a beloved and intimate human being that is dying, besides this horror at the extinction of life there is a severance, a spiritual wound, which like a physical wound is sometimes fatal and sometimes heals, but always aches and shrinks at any external irritating touch. After Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs death NatГЎsha and Princess MГЎrya alike felt this. Drooping in spirit and closing their eyes before the menacing cloud of death that overhung them, they dared not look life in the face. They carefully guarded their open wounds from any rough and painful contact. Everything: a carriage passing rapidly in the street, a summons to dinner, the maidвҖҷs inquiry what dress to prepare, or worse still any word of insincere or feeble sympathy, seemed an insult, painfully irritated the wound, interrupting that necessary quiet in which they both tried to listen to the stern and dreadful choir that still resounded in their imagination, and hindered their gazing into those mysterious limitless vistas that for an instant had opened out before them. Only when alone together were they free from such outrage and pain. They spoke little even to one another, and when they did it was of very unimportant matters. Both avoided any allusion to the future. To admit the possibility of a future seemed to them to insult his memory. Still more carefully did they avoid anything relating to him who was dead. It seemed to them that what they had lived through and experienced could not be expressed in words, and that any reference to the details of his life infringed the majesty and sacredness of the mystery that had been accomplished before their eyes. Continued abstention from speech, and constant avoidance of everything that might lead up to the subjectвҒ вҖ”this halting on all sides at the boundary of what they might not mentionвҒ вҖ”brought before their minds with still greater purity and clearness what they were both feeling. But pure and complete sorrow is as impossible as pure and complete joy. Princess MГЎrya, in her position as absolute and independent arbiter of her own fate and guardian and instructor of her nephew, was the first to be called back to life from that realm of sorrow in which she had dwelt for the first fortnight. She received letters from her relations to which she had to reply; the room in which NikolГәshka had been put was damp and he began to cough; AlpГЎtych came to YaroslГЎvl with reports on the state of their affairs and with advice and suggestions that they should return to Moscow to the house on the VozdvГӯzhenka Street, which had remained uninjured and needed only slight repairs. Life did not stand still and it was necessary to live. Hard as it was for Princess MГЎrya to emerge from the realm of secluded contemplation in which she had lived till then, and sorry and almost ashamed as she felt to leave NatГЎsha alone, yet the cares of life demanded her attention and she involuntarily yielded to them. She went through the accounts with AlpГЎtych, conferred with Dessalles about her nephew, and gave orders and made preparations for the journey to Moscow. NatГЎsha remained alone and, from the time Princess MГЎrya began making preparations for departure, held aloof from her too. Princess MГЎrya asked the countess to let NatГЎsha go with her to Moscow, and both parents gladly accepted this offer, for they saw their daughter losing strength every day and thought that a change of scene and the advice of Moscow doctors would be good for her. вҖңI am not going anywhere,вҖқ NatГЎsha replied when this was proposed to her. вҖңDo please just leave me alone!вҖқ And she ran out of the room, with difficulty refraining from tears of vexation and irritation rather than of sorrow. After she felt herself deserted by Princes MГЎrya and alone in her grief, NatГЎsha spent most of the time in her room by herself, sitting huddled up feet and all in the corner of the sofa, tearing and twisting something with her slender nervous fingers and gazing intently and fixedly at whatever her eyes chanced to fall on. This solitude exhausted and tormented her but she was in absolute need of it. As soon as anyone entered she got up quickly, changed her position and expression, and picked up a book or some sewing, evidently waiting impatiently for the intruder to go. She felt all the time as if she might at any moment penetrate that on whichвҒ вҖ”with a terrible questioning too great for her strengthвҒ вҖ”her spiritual gaze was fixed. One day toward the end of December NatГЎsha, pale and thin, dressed in a black woolen gown, her plaited hair negligently twisted into a knot, was crouched feet and all in the corner of her sofa, nervously crumpling and smoothing out the end of her sash while she looked at a corner of the door. She was gazing in the direction in which he had goneвҒ вҖ”to the other side of life. And that other side of life, of which she had never before thought and which had formerly seemed to her so far away and improbable, was now nearer and more akin and more comprehensible than this side of life, where everything was either emptiness and desolation or suffering and indignity. She was gazing where she knew him to be; but she could not imagine him otherwise than as he had been here. She now saw him again as he had been at MytГӯshchi, at TrГіitsa, and at YaroslГЎvl. She saw his face, heard his voice, repeated his words and her own, and sometimes devised other words they might have spoken. There he is lying back in an armchair in his velvet cloak, leaning his head on his thin pale hand. His chest is dreadfully hollow and his shoulders raised. His lips are firmly closed, his eyes glitter, and a wrinkle comes and goes on his pale forehead. One of his legs twitches just perceptibly, but rapidly. NatГЎsha knows that he is struggling with terrible pain. вҖңWhat is that pain like? Why does he have that pain? What does he feel? How does it hurt him?вҖқ thought NatГЎsha. He noticed her watching him, raised his eyes, and began to speak seriously: вҖңOne thing would be terrible,вҖқ said he: вҖңto bind oneself forever to a suffering man. It would be continual torture.вҖқ And he looked searchingly at her. NatГЎsha as usual answered before she had time to think what she would say. She said: вҖңThis canвҖҷt go onвҒ вҖ”it wonвҖҷt. You will get wellвҒ вҖ”quite well.вҖқ She now saw him from the commencement of that scene and relived what she had then felt. She recalled his long sad and severe look at those words and understood the meaning of the rebuke and despair in that protracted gaze. вҖңI agreed,вҖқ NatГЎsha now said to herself, вҖңthat it would be dreadful if he always continued to suffer. I said it then only because it would have been dreadful for him, but he understood it differently. He thought it would be dreadful for me. He then still wished to live and feared death. And I said it so awkwardly and stupidly! I did not say what I meant. I thought quite differently. Had I said what I thought, I should have said: even if he had to go on dying, to die continually before my eyes, I should have been happy compared with what I am now. Now there is nothingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ nobody. Did he know that? No, he did not and never will know it. And now it will never, never be possible to put it right.вҖқ And now he again seemed to be saying the same words to her, only in her imagination NatГЎsha this time gave him a different answer. She stopped him and said: вҖңTerrible for you, but not for me! You know that for me there is nothing in life but you, and to suffer with you is the greatest happiness for me,вҖқ and he took her hand and pressed it as he had pressed it that terrible evening four days before his death. And in her imagination she said other tender and loving words which she might have said then but only spoke now: вҖңI love thee!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ thee! I love, loveвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she said, convulsively pressing her hands and setting her teeth with a desperate effort.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She was overcome by sweet sorrow and tears were already rising in her eyes; then she suddenly asked herself to whom she was saying this. Again everything was shrouded in hard, dry perplexity, and again with a strained frown she peered toward the world where he was. And now, now it seemed to her she was penetrating the mystery.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But at the instant when it seemed that the incomprehensible was revealing itself to her a loud rattle of the door handle struck painfully on her ears. DunyГЎsha, her maid, entered the room quickly and abruptly with a frightened look on her face and showing no concern for her mistress. вҖңCome to your Papa at once, please!вҖқ said she with a strange, excited look. вҖңA misfortuneвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ about Pyotr IlГҪnichвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ a letter,вҖқ she finished with a sob. X X The French army melted away at the uniform rate of a mathematical progression; and that crossing of the BerГ«zina about which so much has been written was only one intermediate stage in its destruction, and not at all the decisive episode of the campaign. If so much has been and still is written about the BerГ«zina, on the French side this is only because at the broken bridge across that river the calamities their army had been previously enduring were suddenly concentrated at one moment into a tragic spectacle that remained in every memory, and on the Russian side merely because in PetersburgвҒ вҖ”far from the seat of warвҒ вҖ”a plan (again one of PfuelвҖҷs) had been devised to catch Napoleon in a strategic trap at the BerГ«zina River. Everyone assured himself that all would happen according to plan, and therefore insisted that it was just the crossing of the BerГ«zina that destroyed the French army. In reality the results of the crossing were much less disastrous to the FrenchвҒ вҖ”in guns and men lostвҒ вҖ”than KrГЎsnoe had been, as the figures show. The sole importance of the crossing of the BerГ«zina lies in the fact that it plainly and indubitably proved the fallacy of all the plans for cutting off the enemyвҖҷs retreat and the soundness of the only possible line of actionвҒ вҖ”the one KutГәzov and the general mass of the army demandedвҒ вҖ”namely, simply to follow the enemy up. The French crowd fled at a continually increasing speed and all its energy was directed to reaching its goal. It fled like a wounded animal and it was impossible to block its path. This was shown not so much by the arrangements it made for crossing as by what took place at the bridges. When the bridges broke down, unarmed soldiers, people from Moscow and women with children who were with the French transport, allвҒ вҖ”carried on by vis inertiaeвҒ вҖ”pressed forward into boats and into the ice-covered water and did not surrender. That impulse was reasonable. The condition of fugitives and of pursuers was equally bad. As long as they remained with their own people each might hope for help from his fellows and the definite place he held among them. But those who surrendered, while remaining in the same pitiful plight, would be on a lower level to claim a share in the necessities of life. The French did not need to be informed of the fact that half the prisonersвҒ вҖ”with whom the Russians did not know what to doвҒ вҖ”perished of cold and hunger despite their captorsвҖҷ desire to save them; they felt that it could not be otherwise. The most compassionate Russian commanders, those favorable to the FrenchвҒ вҖ”and even the Frenchmen in the Russian serviceвҒ вҖ”could do nothing for the prisoners. The French perished from the conditions to which the Russian army was itself exposed. It was impossible to take bread and clothes from our hungry and indispensable soldiers to give to the French who, though not harmful, or hated, or guilty, were simply unnecessary. Some Russians even did that, but they were exceptions. Certain destruction lay behind the French but in front there was hope. Their ships had been burned, there was no salvation save in collective flight, and on that the whole strength of the French was concentrated. The farther they fled the more wretched became the plight of the remnant, especially after the BerГ«zina, on which (in consequence of the Petersburg plan) special hopes had been placed by the Russians, and the keener grew the passions of the Russian commanders, who blamed one another and KutГәzov most of all. Anticipation that the failure of the Petersburg BerГ«zina plan would be attributed to KutГәzov led to dissatisfaction, contempt, and ridicule, more and more strongly expressed. The ridicule and contempt were of course expressed in a respectful form, making it impossible for him to ask wherein he was to blame. They did not talk seriously to him; when reporting to him or asking for his sanction they appeared to be fulfilling a regrettable formality, but they winked behind his back and tried to mislead him at every turn. Because they could not understand him all these people assumed that it was useless to talk to the old man; that he would never grasp the profundity of their plans, that he would answer with his phrases (which they thought were mere phrases) about a вҖңgolden bridge,вҖқ about the impossibility of crossing the frontier with a crowd of tatterdemalions, and so forth. They had heard all that before. And all he saidвҒ вҖ”that it was necessary to await provisions, or that the men had no bootsвҒ вҖ”was so simple, while what they proposed was so complicated and clever, that it was evident that he was old and stupid and that they, though not in power, were commanders of genius. After the junction with the army of the brilliant admiral and Petersburg hero Wittgenstein, this mood and the gossip of the staff reached their maximum. KutГәzov saw this and merely sighed and shrugged his shoulders. Only once, after the affair of the BerГ«zina, did he get angry and write to Bennigsen (who reported separately to the Emperor) the following letter: вҖңOn account of your spells of ill health, will your excellency please be so good as to set off for KalГәga on receipt of this, and there await further commands and appointments from His Imperial Majesty.вҖқ But after BennigsenвҖҷs departure, the Grand Duke TsarГ©vich Konstantin PГЎvlovich joined the army. He had taken part in the beginning of the campaign but had subsequently been removed from the army by KutГәzov. Now having come to the army, he informed KutГәzov of the EmperorвҖҷs displeasure at the poor success of our forces and the slowness of their advance. The Emperor intended to join the army personally in a few daysвҖҷ time. The old man, experienced in court as well as in military affairsвҒ вҖ”this same KutГәzov who in August had been chosen commander in chief against the sovereignвҖҷs wishes and who had removed the Grand Duke and heir-apparent from the armyвҒ вҖ”who on his own authority and contrary to the EmperorвҖҷs will had decided on the abandonment of Moscow, now realized at once that his day was over, that his part was played, and that the power he was supposed to hold was no longer his. And he understood this not merely from the attitude of the court. He saw on the one hand that the military business in which he had played his part was ended and felt that his mission was accomplished; and at the same time he began to be conscious of the physical weariness of his aged body and of the necessity of physical rest. On the twenty-ninth of November KutГәzov entered VГӯlnaвҒ вҖ”his вҖңdear VГӯlnaвҖқ as he called it. Twice during his career KutГәzov had been governor of VГӯlna. In that wealthy town, which had not been injured, he found old friends and associations, besides the comforts of life of which he had so long been deprived. And he suddenly turned from the cares of army and state and, as far as the passions that seethed around him allowed, immersed himself in the quiet life to which he had formerly been accustomed, as if all that was taking place and all that had still to be done in the realm of history did not concern him at all. ChichagГіv, one of the most zealous вҖңcutters-offвҖқ and вҖңbreakers-up,вҖқ who had first wanted to effect a diversion in Greece and then in Warsaw but never wished to go where he was sent: ChichagГіv, noted for the boldness with which he spoke to the Emperor, and who considered KutГәzov to be under an obligation to him because when he was sent to make peace with Turkey in 1811 independently of KutГәzov, and found that peace had already been concluded, he admitted to the Emperor that the merit of securing that peace was really KutГәzovвҖҷs; this ChichagГіv was the first to meet KutГәzov at the castle where the latter was to stay. In undress naval uniform, with a dirk, and holding his cap under his arm, he handed KutГәzov a garrison report and the keys of the town. The contemptuously respectful attitude of the younger men to the old man in his dotage was expressed in the highest degree by the behavior of ChichagГіv, who knew of the accusations that were being directed against KutГәzov. When speaking to ChichagГіv, KutГәzov incidentally mentioned that the vehicles packed with china that had been captured from him at BorГӯsov had been recovered and would be restored to him. вҖңYou mean to imply that I have nothing to eat out of.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ On the contrary, I can supply you with everything even if you want to give dinner parties,вҖқ warmly replied ChichagГіv, who tried by every word he spoke to prove his own rectitude and therefore imagined KutГәzov to be animated by the same desire. KutГәzov, shrugging his shoulders, replied with his subtle penetrating smile: вҖңI meant merely to say what I said.вҖқ Contrary to the EmperorвҖҷs wish KutГәzov detained the greater part of the army at VГӯlna. Those about him said that he became extraordinarily slack and physically feeble during his stay in that town. He attended to army affairs reluctantly, left everything to his generals, and while awaiting the EmperorвҖҷs arrival led a dissipated life. Having left Petersburg on the seventh of December with his suiteвҒ вҖ”Count TolstГіy, Prince VolkГіnski, ArakchГ©ev, and othersвҒ вҖ”the Emperor reached VГӯlna on the eleventh, and in his traveling sleigh drove straight to the castle. In spite of the severe frost some hundred generals and staff officers in full parade uniform stood in front of the castle, as well as a guard of honor of the SemГ«nov regiment. A courier who galloped to the castle in advance, in a troyka with three foam-flecked horses, shouted вҖңComing!вҖқ and KonovnГӯtsyn rushed into the vestibule to inform KutГәzov, who was waiting in the hall porterвҖҷs little lodge. A minute later the old manвҖҷs large stout figure in full-dress uniform, his chest covered with orders and a scarf drawn round his stomach, waddled out into the porch. He put on his hat with its peaks to the sides and, holding his gloves in his hand and walking with an effort sideways down the steps to the level of the street, took in his hand the report he had prepared for the Emperor. There was running to and fro and whispering; another troyka flew furiously up, and then all eyes were turned on an approaching sleigh in which the figures of the Emperor and VolkГіnski could already be descried. From the habit of fifty years all this had a physically agitating effect on the old general. He carefully and hastily felt himself all over, readjusted his hat, and pulling himself together drew himself up and, at the very moment when the Emperor, having alighted from the sleigh, lifted his eyes to him, handed him the report and began speaking in his smooth, ingratiating voice. The Emperor with a rapid glance scanned KutГәzov from head to foot, frowned for an instant, but immediately mastering himself went up to the old man, extended his arms and embraced him. And this embrace too, owing to a long-standing impression related to his innermost feelings, had its usual effect on KutГәzov and he gave a sob. The Emperor greeted the officers and the SemГ«nov guard, and again pressing the old manвҖҷs hand went with him into the castle. When alone with the field marshal the Emperor expressed his dissatisfaction at the slowness of the pursuit and at the mistakes made at KrГЎsnoe and the BerГ«zina, and informed him of his intentions for a future campaign abroad. KutГәzov made no rejoinder or remark. The same submissive, expressionless look with which he had listened to the EmperorвҖҷs commands on the field of Austerlitz seven years before settled on his face now. When KutГәzov came out of the study and with lowered head was crossing the ballroom with his heavy waddling gait, he was arrested by someoneвҖҷs voice saying: вҖңYour Serene Highness!вҖқ KutГәzov raised his head and looked for a long while into the eyes of Count TolstГіy, who stood before him holding a silver salver on which lay a small object. KutГәzov seemed not to understand what was expected of him. Suddenly he seemed to remember; a scarcely perceptible smile flashed across his puffy face, and bowing low and respectfully he took the object that lay on the salver. It was the Order of St.В George of the First Class. XI XI Next day the field marshal gave a dinner and ball which the Emperor honored by his presence. KutГәzov had received the Order of St.В George of the First Class and the Emperor showed him the highest honors, but everyone knew of the imperial dissatisfaction with him. The proprieties were observed and the Emperor was the first to set that example, but everybody understood that the old man was blameworthy and good-for-nothing. When KutГәzov, conforming to a custom of CatherineвҖҷs day, ordered the standards that had been captured to be lowered at the EmperorвҖҷs feet on his entering the ballroom, the Emperor made a wry face and muttered something in which some people caught the words, вҖңthe old comedian.вҖқ The EmperorвҖҷs displeasure with KutГәzov was specially increased at VГӯlna by the fact that KutГәzov evidently could not or would not understand the importance of the coming campaign. When on the following morning the Emperor said to the officers assembled about him: вҖңYou have not only saved Russia, you have saved Europe!вҖқ they all understood that the war was not ended. KutГәzov alone would not see this and openly expressed his opinion that no fresh war could improve the position or add to the glory of Russia, but could only spoil and lower the glorious position that Russia had gained. He tried to prove to the Emperor the impossibility of levying fresh troops, spoke of the hardships already endured by the people, of the possibility of failure and so forth. This being the field marshalвҖҷs frame of mind he was naturally regarded as merely a hindrance and obstacle to the impending war. To avoid unpleasant encounters with the old man, the natural method was to do what had been done with him at Austerlitz and with Barclay at the beginning of the Russian campaignвҒ вҖ”to transfer the authority to the Emperor himself, thus cutting the ground from under the commander in chiefвҖҷs feet without upsetting the old man by informing him of the change. With this object his staff was gradually reconstructed and its real strength removed and transferred to the Emperor. Toll, KonovnГӯtsyn, and ErmГіlov received fresh appointments. Everyone spoke loudly of the field marshalвҖҷs great weakness and failing health. His health had to be bad for his place to be taken away and given to another. And in fact his health was poor. So naturally, simply, and graduallyвҒ вҖ”just as he had come from Turkey to the Treasury in Petersburg to recruit the militia, and then to the army when he was needed thereвҒ вҖ”now when his part was played out, KutГәzovвҖҷs place was taken by a new and necessary performer. The war of 1812, besides its national significance dear to every Russian heart, was now to assume another, a European, significance. The movement of peoples from west to east was to be succeeded by a movement of peoples from east to west, and for this fresh war another leader was necessary, having qualities and views differing from KutГәzovвҖҷs and animated by different motives. Alexander I was as necessary for the movement of the peoples from east to west and for the refixing of national frontiers as KutГәzov had been for the salvation and glory of Russia. KutГәzov did not understand what Europe, the balance of power, or Napoleon meant. He could not understand it. For the representative of the Russian people, after the enemy had been destroyed and Russia had been liberated and raised to the summit of her glory, there was nothing left to do as a Russian. Nothing remained for the representative of the national war but to die, and KutГәzov died. XII XII As generally happens, Pierre did not feel the full effects of the physical privation and strain he had suffered as prisoner until after they were over. After his liberation he reached OrГ«l, and on the third day there, when preparing to go to Kiev, he fell ill and was laid up for three months. He had what the doctors termed вҖңbilious fever.вҖқ But despite the fact that the doctors treated him, bled him, and gave him medicines to drink, he recovered. Scarcely any impression was left on PierreвҖҷs mind by all that happened to him from the time of his rescue till his illness. He remembered only the dull gray weather now rainy and now snowy, internal physical distress, and pains in his feet and side. He remembered a general impression of the misfortunes and sufferings of people and of being worried by the curiosity of officers and generals who questioned him, he also remembered his difficulty in procuring a conveyance and horses, and above all he remembered his incapacity to think and feel all that time. On the day of his rescue he had seen the body of PГ©tya RostГіv. That same day he had learned that Prince AndrГ©y, after surviving the battle of BorodinГі for more than a month had recently died in the RostГіvsвҖҷ house at YaroslГЎvl, and DenГӯsov who told him this news also mentioned ElГЁnвҖҷs death, supposing that Pierre had heard of it long before. All this at the time seemed merely strange to Pierre: he felt he could not grasp its significance. Just then he was only anxious to get away as quickly as possible from places where people were killing one another, to some peaceful refuge where he could recover himself, rest, and think over all the strange new facts he had learned; but on reaching OrГ«l he immediately fell ill. When he came to himself after his illness he saw in attendance on him two of his servants, TerГ©nty and VГЎska, who had come from Moscow; and also his cousin the eldest princess, who had been living on his estate at ElГ©ts and hearing of his rescue and illness had come to look after him. It was only gradually during his convalescence that Pierre lost the impressions he had become accustomed to during the last few months and got used to the idea that no one would oblige him to go anywhere tomorrow, that no one would deprive him of his warm bed, and that he would be sure to get his dinner, tea, and supper. But for a long time in his dreams he still saw himself in the conditions of captivity. In the same way little by little he came to understand the news he had been told after his rescue, about the death of Prince AndrГ©y, the death of his wife, and the destruction of the French. A joyous feeling of freedomвҒ вҖ”that complete inalienable freedom natural to man which he had first experienced at the first halt outside MoscowвҒ вҖ”filled PierreвҖҷs soul during his convalescence. He was surprised to find that this inner freedom, which was independent of external conditions, now had as it were an additional setting of external liberty. He was alone in a strange town, without acquaintances. No one demanded anything of him or sent him anywhere. He had all he wanted: the thought of his wife which had been a continual torment to him was no longer there, since she was no more. вҖңOh, how good! How splendid!вҖқ said he to himself when a cleanly laid table was moved up to him with savory beef tea, or when he lay down for the night on a soft clean bed, or when he remembered that the French had gone and that his wife was no more. вҖңOh, how good, how splendid!вҖқ And by old habit he asked himself the question: вҖңWell, and what then? What am I going to do?вҖқ And he immediately gave himself the answer: вҖңWell, I shall live. Ah, how splendid!вҖқ The very question that had formerly tormented him, the thing he had continually sought to findвҒ вҖ”the aim of lifeвҒ вҖ”no longer existed for him now. That search for the aim of life had not merely disappeared temporarilyвҒ вҖ”he felt that it no longer existed for him and could not present itself again. And this very absence of an aim gave him the complete, joyous sense of freedom which constituted his happiness at this time. He could not see an aim, for he now had faithвҒ вҖ”not faith in any kind of rule, or words, or ideas, but faith in an ever-living, ever-manifest God. Formerly he had sought Him in aims he set himself. That search for an aim had been simply a search for God, and suddenly in his captivity he had learned not by words or reasoning but by direct feeling what his nurse had told him long ago: that God is here and everywhere. In his captivity he had learned that in KaratГЎev God was greater, more infinite and unfathomable than in the Architect of the Universe recognized by the Freemasons. He felt like a man who after straining his eyes to see into the far distance finds what he sought at his very feet. All his life he had looked over the heads of the men around him, when he should have merely looked in front of him without straining his eyes. In the past he had never been able to find that great inscrutable infinite something. He had only felt that it must exist somewhere and had looked for it. In everything near and comprehensible he had seen only what was limited, petty, commonplace, and senseless. He had equipped himself with a mental telescope and looked into remote space, where petty worldliness hiding itself in misty distance had seemed to him great and infinite merely because it was not clearly seen. And such had European life, politics, Freemasonry, philosophy, and philanthropy seemed to him. But even then, at moments of weakness as he had accounted them, his mind had penetrated to those distances and he had there seen the same pettiness, worldliness, and senselessness. Now, however, he had learned to see the great, eternal, and infinite in everything, and thereforeвҒ вҖ”to see it and enjoy its contemplationвҒ вҖ”he naturally threw away the telescope through which he had till now gazed over menвҖҷs heads, and gladly regarded the ever-changing, eternally great, unfathomable, and infinite life around him. And the closer he looked the more tranquil and happy he became. That dreadful question, вҖңWhat for?вҖқ which had formerly destroyed all his mental edifices, no longer existed for him. To that question, вҖңWhat for?вҖқ a simple answer was now always ready in his soul: вҖңBecause there is a God, that God without whose will not one hair falls from a manвҖҷs head.вҖқ XIII XIII In external ways Pierre had hardly changed at all. In appearance he was just what he used to be. As before he was absentminded and seemed occupied not with what was before his eyes but with something special of his own. The difference between his former and present self was that formerly when he did not grasp what lay before him or was said to him, he had puckered his forehead painfully as if vainly seeking to distinguish something at a distance. At present he still forgot what was said to him and still did not see what was before his eyes, but he now looked with a scarcely perceptible and seemingly ironic smile at what was before him and listened to what was said, though evidently seeing and hearing something quite different. Formerly he had appeared to be a kindhearted but unhappy man, and so people had been inclined to avoid him. Now a smile at the joy of life always played round his lips, and sympathy for others shone in his eyes with a questioning look as to whether they were as contented as he was, and people felt pleased by his presence. Previously he had talked a great deal, grew excited when he talked, and seldom listened; now he was seldom carried away in conversation and knew how to listen so that people readily told him their most intimate secrets. The princess, who had never liked Pierre and had been particularly hostile to him since she had felt herself under obligations to him after the old countвҖҷs death, now after staying a short time in OrГ«lвҒ вҖ”where she had come intending to show Pierre that in spite of his ingratitude she considered it her duty to nurse himвҒ вҖ”felt to her surprise and vexation that she had become fond of him. Pierre did not in any way seek her approval, he merely studied her with interest. Formerly she had felt that he regarded her with indifference and irony, and so had shrunk into herself as she did with others and had shown him only the combative side of her nature; but now he seemed to be trying to understand the most intimate places of her heart, and, mistrustfully at first but afterwards gratefully, she let him see the hidden, kindly sides of her character. The most cunning man could not have crept into her confidence more successfully, evoking memories of the best times of her youth and showing sympathy with them. Yet PierreвҖҷs cunning consisted simply in finding pleasure in drawing out the human qualities of the embittered, hard, and (in her own way) proud princess. вҖңYes, he is a very, very kind man when he is not under the influence of bad people but of people such as myself,вҖқ thought she. His servants tooвҒ вҖ”TerГ©nty and VГЎskaвҒ вҖ”in their own way noticed the change that had taken place in Pierre. They considered that he had become much вҖңsimpler.вҖқ TerГ©nty, when he had helped him undress and wished him good night, often lingered with his masterвҖҷs boots in his hands and clothes over his arm, to see whether he would not start a talk. And Pierre, noticing that TerГ©nty wanted a chat, generally kept him there. вҖңWell, tell meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ now, how did you get food?вҖқ he would ask. And TerГ©nty would begin talking of the destruction of Moscow, and of the old count, and would stand for a long time holding the clothes and talking, or sometimes listening to PierreвҖҷs stories, and then would go out into the hall with a pleasant sense of intimacy with his master and affection for him. The doctor who attended Pierre and visited him every day, though he considered it his duty as a doctor to pose as a man whose every moment was of value to suffering humanity, would sit for hours with Pierre telling him his favorite anecdotes and his observations on the characters of his patients in general, and especially of the ladies. вҖңItвҖҷs a pleasure to talk to a man like that; he is not like our provincials,вҖқ he would say. There were several prisoners from the French army in OrГ«l, and the doctor brought one of them, a young Italian, to see Pierre. This officer began visiting Pierre, and the princess used to make fun of the tenderness the Italian expressed for him. The Italian seemed happy only when he could come to see Pierre, talk with him, tell him about his past, his life at home, and his love, and pour out to him his indignation against the French and especially against Napoleon. вҖңIf all Russians are in the least like you, it is sacrilege to fight such a nation,вҖқ he said to Pierre. вҖңYou, who have suffered so from the French, do not even feel animosity toward them.вҖқ Pierre had evoked the passionate affection of the Italian merely by evoking the best side of his nature and taking a pleasure in so doing. During the last days of PierreвҖҷs stay in OrГ«l his old Masonic acquaintance Count Willarski, who had introduced him to the lodge in 1807, came to see him. Willarski was married to a Russian heiress who had a large estate in OrГ«l province, and he occupied a temporary post in the commissariat department in that town. Hearing that BezГәkhov was in OrГ«l, Willarski, though they had never been intimate, came to him with the professions of friendship and intimacy that people who meet in a desert generally express for one another. Willarski felt dull in OrГ«l and was pleased to meet a man of his own circle and, as he supposed, of similar interests. But to his surprise Willarski soon noticed that Pierre had lagged much behind the times, and had sunk, as he expressed it to himself, into apathy and egotism. вҖңYou are letting yourself go, my dear fellow,вҖқ he said. But for all that Willarski found it pleasanter now than it had been formerly to be with Pierre, and came to see him every day. To Pierre as he looked at and listened to Willarski, it seemed strange to think that he had been like that himself but a short time before. Willarski was a married man with a family, busy with his family affairs, his wifeвҖҷs affairs, and his official duties. He regarded all these occupations as hindrances to life, and considered that they were all contemptible because their aim was the welfare of himself and his family. Military, administrative, political, and Masonic interests continually absorbed his attention. And Pierre, without trying to change the otherвҖҷs views and without condemning him, but with the quiet, joyful, and amused smile now habitual to him, was interested in this strange though very familiar phenomenon. There was a new feature in PierreвҖҷs relations with Willarski, with the princess, with the doctor, and with all the people he now met, which gained for him the general good will. This was his acknowledgment of the impossibility of changing a manвҖҷs convictions by words, and his recognition of the possibility of everyone thinking, feeling, and seeing things each from his own point of view. This legitimate peculiarity of each individual which used to excite and irritate Pierre now became a basis of the sympathy he felt for, and the interest he took in, other people. The difference, and sometimes complete contradiction, between menвҖҷs opinions and their lives, and between one man and another, pleased him and drew from him an amused and gentle smile. In practical matters Pierre unexpectedly felt within himself a center of gravity he had previously lacked. Formerly all pecuniary questions, especially requests for money to which, as an extremely wealthy man, he was very exposed, produced in him a state of hopeless agitation and perplexity. вҖңTo give or not to give?вҖқ he had asked himself. вҖңI have it and he needs it. But someone else needs it still more. Who needs it most? And perhaps they are both impostors?вҖқ In the old days he had been unable to find a way out of all these surmises and had given to all who asked as long as he had anything to give. Formerly he had been in a similar state of perplexity with regard to every question concerning his property, when one person advised one thing and another something else. Now to his surprise he found that he no longer felt either doubt or perplexity about these questions. There was now within him a judge who by some rule unknown to him decided what should or should not be done. He was as indifferent as heretofore to money matters, but now he felt certain of what ought and what ought not to be done. The first time he had recourse to his new judge was when a French prisoner, a colonel, came to him and, after talking a great deal about his exploits, concluded by making what amounted to a demand that Pierre should give him four thousand francs to send to his wife and children. Pierre refused without the least difficulty or effort, and was afterwards surprised how simple and easy had been what used to appear so insurmountably difficult. At the same time that he refused the colonelвҖҷs demand he made up his mind that he must have recourse to artifice when leaving OrГ«l, to induce the Italian officer to accept some money of which he was evidently in need. A further proof to Pierre of his own more settled outlook on practical matters was furnished by his decision with regard to his wifeвҖҷs debts and to the rebuilding of his houses in and near Moscow. His head steward came to him at OrГ«l and Pierre reckoned up with him his diminished income. The burning of Moscow had cost him, according to the head stewardвҖҷs calculation, about two million rubles. To console Pierre for these losses the head steward gave him an estimate showing that despite these losses his income would not be diminished but would even be increased if he refused to pay his wifeвҖҷs debts which he was under no obligation to meet, and did not rebuild his Moscow house and the country house on his Moscow estate, which had cost him eighty thousand rubles a year and brought in nothing. вҖңYes, of course thatвҖҷs true,вҖқ said Pierre with a cheerful smile. вҖңI donвҖҷt need all that at all. By being ruined I have become much richer.вҖқ But in January SavГ©lich came from Moscow and gave him an account of the state of things there, and spoke of the estimate an architect had made of the cost of rebuilding the town and country houses, speaking of this as of a settled matter. About the same time he received letters from Prince VasГӯli and other Petersburg acquaintances speaking of his wifeвҖҷs debts. And Pierre decided that the stewardвҖҷs proposals which had so pleased him were wrong and that he must go to Petersburg and settle his wifeвҖҷs affairs and must rebuild in Moscow. Why this was necessary he did not know, but he knew for certain that it was necessary. His income would be reduced by three fourths, but he felt it must be done. Willarski was going to Moscow and they agreed to travel together. During the whole time of his convalescence in OrГ«l Pierre had experienced a feeling of joy, freedom, and life; but when during his journey he found himself in the open world and saw hundreds of new faces, that feeling was intensified. Throughout his journey he felt like a schoolboy on holiday. EveryoneвҒ вҖ”the stagecoach driver, the post-house overseers, the peasants on the roads and in the villagesвҒ вҖ”had a new significance for him. The presence and remarks of Willarski who continually deplored the ignorance and poverty of Russia and its backwardness compared with Europe only heightened PierreвҖҷs pleasure. Where Willarski saw deadness Pierre saw an extraordinary strength and vitalityвҒ вҖ”the strength which in that vast space amid the snows maintained the life of this original, peculiar, and unique people. He did not contradict Willarski and even seemed to agree with himвҒ вҖ”an apparent agreement being the simplest way to avoid discussions that could lead to nothingвҒ вҖ”and he smiled joyfully as he listened to him. XIV XIV It would be difficult to explain why and whither ants whose heap has been destroyed are hurrying: some from the heap dragging bits of rubbish, larvae, and corpses, others back to the heap, or why they jostle, overtake one another, and fight, and it would be equally difficult to explain what caused the Russians after the departure of the French to throng to the place that had formerly been Moscow. But when we watch the ants round their ruined heap, the tenacity, energy, and immense number of the delving insects prove that despite the destruction of the heap, something indestructible, which though intangible is the real strength of the colony, still exists; and similarly, though in Moscow in the month of October there was no government and no churches, shrines, riches, or housesвҒ вҖ”it was still the Moscow it had been in August. All was destroyed, except something intangible yet powerful and indestructible. The motives of those who thronged from all sides to Moscow after it had been cleared of the enemy were most diverse and personal, and at first for the most part savage and brutal. One motive only they all had in common: a desire to get to the place that had been called Moscow, to apply their activities there. Within a week Moscow already had fifteen thousand inhabitants, in a fortnight twenty-five thousand, and so on. By the autumn of 1813 the number, ever increasing and increasing, exceeded what it had been in 1812. The first Russians to enter Moscow were the Cossacks of WintzingerodeвҖҷs detachment, peasants from the adjacent villages, and residents who had fled from Moscow and had been hiding in its vicinity. The Russians who entered Moscow, finding it plundered, plundered it in their turn. They continued what the French had begun. Trains of peasant carts came to Moscow to carry off to the villages what had been abandoned in the ruined houses and the streets. The Cossacks carried off what they could to their camps, and the householders seized all they could find in other houses and moved it to their own, pretending that it was their property. But the first plunderers were followed by a second and a third contingent, and with increasing numbers plundering became more and more difficult and assumed more definite forms. The French found Moscow abandoned but with all the organizations of regular life, with diverse branches of commerce and craftsmanship, with luxury, and governmental and religious institutions. These forms were lifeless but still existed. There were bazaars, shops, warehouses, market stalls, granariesвҒ вҖ”for the most part still stocked with goodsвҒ вҖ”and there were factories and workshops, palaces and wealthy houses filled with luxuries, hospitals, prisons, government offices, churches, and cathedrals. The longer the French remained the more these forms of town life perished, until finally all was merged into one confused, lifeless scene of plunder. The more the plundering by the French continued, the more both the wealth of Moscow and the strength of its plunderers was destroyed. But plundering by the Russians, with which the reoccupation of the city began, had an opposite effect: the longer it continued and the greater the number of people taking part in it the more rapidly was the wealth of the city and its regular life restored. Besides the plunderers, very various people, some drawn by curiosity, some by official duties, some by self-interestвҒ вҖ”house owners, clergy, officials of all kinds, tradesmen, artisans, and peasantsвҒ вҖ”streamed into Moscow as blood flows to the heart. Within a week the peasants who came with empty carts to carry off plunder were stopped by the authorities and made to cart the corpses out of the town. Other peasants, having heard of their comradesвҖҷ discomfiture, came to town bringing rye, oats, and hay, and beat down one anotherвҖҷs prices to below what they had been in former days. Gangs of carpenters hoping for high pay arrived in Moscow every day, and on all sides logs were being hewn, new houses built, and old, charred ones repaired. Tradesmen began trading in booths. Cookshops and taverns were opened in partially burned houses. The clergy resumed the services in many churches that had not been burned. Donors contributed Church property that had been stolen. Government clerks set up their baize-covered tables and their pigeonholes of documents in small rooms. The higher authorities and the police organized the distribution of goods left behind by the French. The owners of houses in which much property had been left, brought there from other houses, complained of the injustice of taking everything to the Faceted Palace in the KrГ©mlin; others insisted that as the French had gathered things from different houses into this or that house, it would be unfair to allow its owner to keep all that was found there. They abused the police and bribed them, made out estimates at ten times their value for government stores that had perished in the fire, and demanded relief. And Count RostopchГӯn wrote proclamations. XV XV At the end of January Pierre went to Moscow and stayed in an annex of his house which had not been burned. He called on Count RostopchГӯn and on some acquaintances who were back in Moscow, and he intended to leave for Petersburg two days later. Everybody was celebrating the victory, everything was bubbling with life in the ruined but reviving city. Everyone was pleased to see Pierre, everyone wished to meet him, and everyone questioned him about what he had seen. Pierre felt particularly well disposed toward them all, but was now instinctively on his guard for fear of binding himself in any way. To all questions put to himвҒ вҖ”whether important or quite triflingвҒ вҖ”such as: Where would he live? Was he going to rebuild? When was he going to Petersburg and would he mind taking a parcel for someone?вҒ вҖ”he replied: вҖңYes, perhaps,вҖқ or, вҖңI think so,вҖқ and so on. He had heard that the RostГіvs were at KostromГЎ but the thought of NatГЎsha seldom occurred to him. If it did it was only as a pleasant memory of the distant past. He felt himself not only free from social obligations but also from that feeling which, it seemed to him, he had aroused in himself. On the third day after his arrival he heard from the DrubetskГіys that Princess MГЎrya was in Moscow. The death, sufferings, and last days of Prince AndrГ©y had often occupied PierreвҖҷs thoughts and now recurred to him with fresh vividness. Having heard at dinner that Princess MГЎrya was in Moscow and living in her houseвҒ вҖ”which had not been burnedвҒ вҖ”in VozdvГӯzhenka Street, he drove that same evening to see her. On his way to the house Pierre kept thinking of Prince AndrГ©y, of their friendship, of his various meetings with him, and especially of the last one at BorodinГі. вҖңIs it possible that he died in the bitter frame of mind he was then in? Is it possible that the meaning of life was not disclosed to him before he died?вҖқ thought Pierre. He recalled KaratГЎev and his death and involuntarily began to compare these two men, so different, and yet so similar in that they had both lived and both died and in the love he felt for both of them. Pierre drove up to the house of the old prince in a most serious mood. The house had escaped the fire; it showed signs of damage but its general aspect was unchanged. The old footman, who met Pierre with a stern face as if wishing to make the visitor feel that the absence of the old prince had not disturbed the order of things in the house, informed him that the princess had gone to her own apartments, and that she received on Sundays. вҖңAnnounce me. Perhaps she will see me,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңYes, sir,вҖқ said the man. вҖңPlease step into the portrait gallery.вҖқ A few minutes later the footman returned with Dessalles, who brought word from the princess that she would be very glad to see Pierre if he would excuse her want of ceremony and come upstairs to her apartment. In a rather low room lit by one candle sat the princess and with her another person dressed in black. Pierre remembered that the princess always had lady companions, but who they were and what they were like he never knew or remembered. вҖңThis must be one of her companions,вҖқ he thought, glancing at the lady in the black dress. The princess rose quickly to meet him and held out her hand. вҖңYes,вҖқ she said, looking at his altered face after he had kissed her hand, вҖңso this is how we meet again. He spoke of you even at the very last,вҖқ she went on, turning her eyes from Pierre to her companion with a shyness that surprised him for an instant. вҖңI was so glad to hear of your safety. It was the first piece of good news we had received for a long time.вҖқ Again the princess glanced round at her companion with even more uneasiness in her manner and was about to add something, but Pierre interrupted her. вҖңJust imagineвҒ вҖ”I knew nothing about him!вҖқ said he. вҖңI thought he had been killed. All I know I heard at second hand from others. I only know that he fell in with the RostГіvs.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What a strange coincidence!вҖқ Pierre spoke rapidly and with animation. He glanced once at the companionвҖҷs face, saw her attentive and kindly gaze fixed on him, and, as often happens when one is talking, felt somehow that this companion in the black dress was a good, kind, excellent creature who would not hinder his conversing freely with Princess MГЎrya. But when he mentioned the RostГіvs, Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs face expressed still greater embarrassment. She again glanced rapidly from PierreвҖҷs face to that of the lady in the black dress and said: вҖңDo you really not recognize her?вҖқ Pierre looked again at the companionвҖҷs pale, delicate face with its black eyes and peculiar mouth, and something near to him, long forgotten and more than sweet, looked at him from those attentive eyes. вҖңBut no, it canвҖҷt be!вҖқ he thought. вҖңThis stern, thin, pale face that looks so much older! It cannot be she. It merely reminds me of her.вҖқ But at that moment Princess MГЎrya said, вҖңNatГЎsha!вҖқ And with difficulty, effort, and stress, like the opening of a door grown rusty on its hinges, a smile appeared on the face with the attentive eyes, and from that opening door came a breath of fragrance which suffused Pierre with a happiness he had long forgotten and of which he had not even been thinkingвҒ вҖ”especially at that moment. It suffused him, seized him, and enveloped him completely. When she smiled doubt was no longer possible, it was NatГЎsha and he loved her. At that moment Pierre involuntarily betrayed to her, to Princess MГЎrya, and above all to himself, a secret of which he himself had been unaware. He flushed joyfully yet with painful distress. He tried to hide his agitation. But the more he tried to hide it the more clearlyвҒ вҖ”clearer than any words could have doneвҒ вҖ”did he betray to himself, to her, and to Princess MГЎrya that he loved her. вҖңNo, itвҖҷs only the unexpectedness of it,вҖқ thought Pierre. But as soon as he tried to continue the conversation he had begun with Princess MГЎrya he again glanced at NatГЎsha, and a still-deeper flush suffused his face and a still-stronger agitation of mingled joy and fear seized his soul. He became confused in his speech and stopped in the middle of what he was saying. Pierre had failed to notice NatГЎsha because he did not at all expect to see her there, but he had failed to recognize her because the change in her since he last saw her was immense. She had grown thin and pale, but that was not what made her unrecognizable; she was unrecognizable at the moment he entered because on that face whose eyes had always shone with a suppressed smile of the joy of life, now when he first entered and glanced at her there was not the least shadow of a smile: only her eyes were kindly attentive and sadly interrogative. PierreвҖҷs confusion was not reflected by any confusion on NatГЎshaвҖҷs part, but only by the pleasure that just perceptibly lit up her whole face. XVI XVI вҖңShe has come to stay with me,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңThe count and countess will be here in a few days. The countess is in a dreadful state; but it was necessary for NatГЎsha herself to see a doctor. They insisted on her coming with me.вҖқ вҖңYes, is there a family free from sorrow now?вҖқ said Pierre, addressing NatГЎsha. вҖңYou know it happened the very day we were rescued. I saw him. What a delightful boy he was!вҖқ NatГЎsha looked at him, and by way of answer to his words her eyes widened and lit up. вҖңWhat can one say or think of as a consolation?вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңNothing! Why had such a splendid boy, so full of life, to die?вҖқ вҖңYes, in these days it would be hard to live without faithвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ remarked Princess MГЎrya. вҖңYes, yes, that is really true,вҖқ Pierre hastily interrupted her. вҖңWhy is it true?вҖқ NatГЎsha asked, looking attentively into PierreвҖҷs eyes. вҖңHow can you ask why?вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңThe thought alone of what awaitsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ NatГЎsha without waiting for Princess MГЎrya to finish again looked inquiringly at Pierre. вҖңAnd because,вҖқ Pierre continued, вҖңonly one who believes that there is a God ruling us can bear a loss such as hers andвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ yours.вҖқ NatГЎsha had already opened her mouth to speak but suddenly stopped. Pierre hurriedly turned away from her and again addressed Princess MГЎrya, asking about his friendвҖҷs last days. PierreвҖҷs confusion had now almost vanished, but at the same time he felt that his freedom had also completely gone. He felt that there was now a judge of his every word and action whose judgment mattered more to him than that of all the rest of the world. As he spoke now he was considering what impression his words would make on NatГЎsha. He did not purposely say things to please her, but whatever he was saying he regarded from her standpoint. Princess MГЎryaвҒ вҖ”reluctantly as is usual in such casesвҒ вҖ”began telling of the condition in which she had found Prince AndrГ©y. But PierreвҖҷs face quivering with emotion, his questions and his eager restless expression, gradually compelled her to go into details which she feared to recall for her own sake. вҖңYes, yes, and soвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ?вҖқ Pierre kept saying as he leaned toward her with his whole body and eagerly listened to her story. вҖңYes, yesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ so he grew tranquil and softened? With all his soul he had always sought one thingвҒ вҖ”to be perfectly goodвҒ вҖ”so he could not be afraid of death. The faults he hadвҒ вҖ”if he had anyвҒ вҖ”were not of his making. So he did soften?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ What a happy thing that he saw you again,вҖқ he added, suddenly turning to NatГЎsha and looking at her with eyes full of tears. NatГЎshaвҖҷs face twitched. She frowned and lowered her eyes for a moment. She hesitated for an instant whether to speak or not. вҖңYes, that was happiness,вҖқ she then said in her quiet voice with its deep chest notes. вҖңFor me it certainly was happiness.вҖқ She paused. вҖңAnd heвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ heвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ he said he was wishing for it at the very moment I entered the room.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ NatГЎshaвҖҷs voice broke. She blushed, pressed her clasped hands on her knees, and then controlling herself with an evident effort lifted her head and began to speak rapidly. вҖңWe knew nothing of it when we started from Moscow. I did not dare to ask about him. Then suddenly SГіnya told me he was traveling with us. I had no idea and could not imagine what state he was in, all I wanted was to see him and be with him,вҖқ she said, trembling, and breathing quickly. And not letting them interrupt her she went on to tell what she had never yet mentioned to anyoneвҒ вҖ”all she had lived through during those three weeks of their journey and life at YaroslГЎvl. Pierre listened to her with lips parted and eyes fixed upon her full of tears. As he listened he did not think of Prince AndrГ©y, nor of death, nor of what she was telling. He listened to her and felt only pity for her, for what she was suffering now while she was speaking. Princess MГЎrya, frowning in her effort to hold back her tears, sat beside NatГЎsha, and heard for the first time the story of those last days of her brotherвҖҷs and NatГЎshaвҖҷs love. Evidently NatГЎsha needed to tell that painful yet joyful tale. She spoke, mingling most trifling details with the intimate secrets of her soul, and it seemed as if she could never finish. Several times she repeated the same thing twice. DessallesвҖҷ voice was heard outside the door asking whether NikolГәshka might come in to say good night. вҖңWell, thatвҖҷs allвҒ вҖ”everything,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. She got up quickly just as NikolГәshka entered, almost ran to the door which was hidden by curtains, struck her head against it, and rushed from the room with a moan either of pain or sorrow. Pierre gazed at the door through which she had disappeared and did not understand why he suddenly felt all alone in the world. Princess MГЎrya roused him from his abstraction by drawing his attention to her nephew who had entered the room. At that moment of emotional tenderness NikolГәshkaвҖҷs face, which resembled his fatherвҖҷs, affected Pierre so much that when he had kissed the boy he got up quickly, took out his handkerchief, and went to the window. He wished to take leave of Princess MГЎrya, but she would not let him go. вҖңNo, NatГЎsha and I sometimes donвҖҷt go to sleep till after two, so please donвҖҷt go. I will order supper. Go downstairs, we will come immediately.вҖқ Before Pierre left the room Princess MГЎrya told him: вҖңThis is the first time she has talked of him like that.вҖқ XVII XVII Pierre was shown into the large, brightly lit dining room; a few minutes later he heard footsteps and Princess MГЎrya entered with NatГЎsha. NatГЎsha was calm, though a severe and grave expression had again settled on her face. They all three of them now experienced that feeling of awkwardness which usually follows after a serious and heartfelt talk. It is impossible to go back to the same conversation, to talk of trifles is awkward, and yet the desire to speak is there and silence seems like affectation. They went silently to table. The footmen drew back the chairs and pushed them up again. Pierre unfolded his cold table napkin and, resolving to break the silence, looked at NatГЎsha and at Princess MГЎrya. They had evidently both formed the same resolution; the eyes of both shone with satisfaction and a confession that besides sorrow life also has joy. вҖңDo you take vodka, Count?вҖқ asked Princess MГЎrya, and those words suddenly banished the shadows of the past. вҖңNow tell us about yourself,вҖқ said she. вҖңOne hears such improbable wonders about you.вҖқ вҖңYes,вҖқ replied Pierre with the smile of mild irony now habitual to him. вҖңThey even tell me wonders I myself never dreamed of! MГЎrya AbrГЎmovna invited me to her house and kept telling me what had happened, or ought to have happened, to me. StepГЎn StepГЎnych also instructed me how I ought to tell of my experiences. In general I have noticed that it is very easy to be an interesting man (I am an interesting man now); people invite me out and tell me all about myself.вҖқ NatГЎsha smiled and was on the point of speaking. вҖңWe have been told,вҖқ Princess MГЎrya interrupted her, вҖңthat you lost two millions in Moscow. Is that true?вҖқ вҖңBut I am three times as rich as before,вҖқ returned Pierre. Though the position was now altered by his decision to pay his wifeвҖҷs debts and to rebuild his houses, Pierre still maintained that he had become three times as rich as before. вҖңWhat I have certainly gained is freedom,вҖқ he began seriously, but did not continue, noticing that this theme was too egotistic. вҖңAnd are you building?вҖқ вҖңYes. SavГ©lich says I must!вҖқ вҖңTell me, you did not know of the countessвҖҷ death when you decided to remain in Moscow?вҖқ asked Princess MГЎrya and immediately blushed, noticing that her question, following his mention of freedom, ascribed to his words a meaning he had perhaps not intended. вҖңNo,вҖқ answered Pierre, evidently not considering awkward the meaning Princess MГЎrya had given to his words. вҖңI heard of it in OrГ«l and you cannot imagine how it shocked me. We were not an exemplary couple,вҖқ he added quickly, glancing at NatГЎsha and noticing on her face curiosity as to how he would speak of his wife, вҖңbut her death shocked me terribly. When two people quarrel they are always both in fault, and oneвҖҷs own guilt suddenly becomes terribly serious when the other is no longer alive. And then such a deathвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ without friends and without consolation! I am very, very sorry for her,вҖқ he concluded, and was pleased to notice a look of glad approval on NatГЎshaвҖҷs face. вҖңYes, and so you are once more an eligible bachelor,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. Pierre suddenly flushed crimson and for a long time tried not to look at NatГЎsha. When he ventured to glance her way again her face was cold, stern, and he fancied even contemptuous. вҖңAnd did you really see and speak to Napoleon, as we have been told?вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. Pierre laughed. вҖңNo, not once! Everybody seems to imagine that being taken prisoner means being NapoleonвҖҷs guest. Not only did I never see him but I heard nothing about himвҒ вҖ”I was in much lower company!вҖқ Supper was over, and Pierre who at first declined to speak about his captivity was gradually led on to do so. вҖңBut itвҖҷs true that you remained in Moscow to kill Napoleon?вҖқ NatГЎsha asked with a slight smile. вҖңI guessed it then when we met at the SГәkharev tower, do you remember?вҖқ Pierre admitted that it was true, and from that was gradually led by Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs questions and especially by NatГЎshaвҖҷs into giving a detailed account of his adventures. At first he spoke with the amused and mild irony now customary with him toward everybody and especially toward himself, but when he came to describe the horrors and sufferings he had witnessed he was unconsciously carried away and began speaking with the suppressed emotion of a man re-experiencing in recollection strong impressions he has lived through. Princess MГЎrya with a gentle smile looked now at Pierre and now at NatГЎsha. In the whole narrative she saw only Pierre and his goodness. NatГЎsha, leaning on her elbow, the expression of her face constantly changing with the narrative, watched Pierre with an attention that never wanderedвҒ вҖ”evidently herself experiencing all that he described. Not only her look, but her exclamations and the brief questions she put, showed Pierre that she understood just what he wished to convey. It was clear that she understood not only what he said but also what he wished to, but could not, express in words. The account Pierre gave of the incident with the child and the woman for protecting whom he was arrested was this: вҖңIt was an awful sightвҒ вҖ”children abandoned, some in the flamesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ One was snatched out before my eyesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and there were women who had their things snatched off and their earrings torn outвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he flushed and grew confused. вҖңThen a patrol arrived and all the menвҒ вҖ”all those who were not looting, that isвҒ вҖ”were arrested, and I among them.вҖқ вҖңI am sure youвҖҷre not telling us everything; I am sure you did somethingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said NatГЎsha and pausing added, вҖңsomething fine?вҖқ Pierre continued. When he spoke of the execution he wanted to pass over the horrible details, but NatГЎsha insisted that he should not omit anything. Pierre began to tell about KaratГЎev, but paused. By this time he had risen from the table and was pacing the room, NatГЎsha following him with her eyes. Then he added: вҖңNo, you canвҖҷt understand what I learned from that illiterate manвҒ вҖ”that simple fellow.вҖқ вҖңYes, yes, go on!вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңWhere is he?вҖқ вҖңThey killed him almost before my eyes.вҖқ And Pierre, his voice trembling continually, went on to tell of the last days of their retreat, of KaratГЎevвҖҷs illness and his death. He told of his adventures as he had never yet recalled them. He now, as it were, saw a new meaning in all he had gone through. Now that he was telling it all to NatГЎsha he experienced that pleasure which a man has when women listen to himвҒ вҖ”not clever women who when listening either try to remember what they hear to enrich their minds and when opportunity offers to retell it, or who wish to adopt it to some thought of their own and promptly contribute their own clever comments prepared in their little mental workshopвҒ вҖ”but the pleasure given by real women gifted with a capacity to select and absorb the very best a man shows of himself. NatГЎsha without knowing it was all attention: she did not lose a word, no single quiver in PierreвҖҷs voice, no look, no twitch of a muscle in his face, nor a single gesture. She caught the unfinished word in its flight and took it straight into her open heart, divining the secret meaning of all PierreвҖҷs mental travail. Princess MГЎrya understood his story and sympathized with him, but she now saw something else that absorbed all her attention. She saw the possibility of love and happiness between NatГЎsha and Pierre, and the first thought of this filled her heart with gladness. It was three oвҖҷclock in the morning. The footmen came in with sad and stern faces to change the candles, but no one noticed them. Pierre finished his story.
