NatГЎsha continued to look at him intently with bright, attentive, and animated eyes, as if trying to understand something more which he had perhaps left untold. Pierre in shamefaced and happy confusion glanced occasionally at her, and tried to think what to say next to introduce a fresh subject. Princess MГЎrya was silent. It occurred to none of them that it was three oвҖҷclock and time to go to bed. вҖңPeople speak of misfortunes and sufferings,вҖқ remarked Pierre, вҖңbut if at this moment I were asked: вҖҳWould you rather be what you were before you were taken prisoner, or go through all this again?вҖҷ then for heavenвҖҷs sake let me again have captivity and horseflesh! We imagine that when we are thrown out of our usual ruts all is lost, but it is only then that what is new and good begins. While there is life there is happiness. There is much, much before us. I say this to you,вҖқ he added, turning to NatГЎsha. вҖңYes, yes,вҖқ she said, answering something quite different. вҖңI too should wish nothing but to relive it all from the beginning.вҖқ Pierre looked intently at her. вҖңYes, and nothing more,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңItвҖҷs not true, not true!вҖқ cried Pierre. вҖңI am not to blame for being alive and wishing to liveвҒ вҖ”nor you either.вҖқ Suddenly NatГЎsha bent her head, covered her face with her hands, and began to cry. вҖңWhat is it, NatГЎsha?вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңNothing, nothing.вҖқ She smiled at Pierre through her tears. вҖңGood night! It is time for bed.вҖқ Pierre rose and took his leave. Princess MГЎrya and NatГЎsha met as usual in the bedroom. They talked of what Pierre had told them. Princess MГЎrya did not express her opinion of Pierre nor did NatГЎsha speak of him. вҖңWell, good night, MГЎrya!вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңDo you know, I am often afraid that by not speaking of himвҖқ (she meant Prince AndrГ©y) вҖңfor fear of not doing justice to our feelings, we forget him.вҖқ Princess MГЎrya sighed deeply and thereby acknowledged the justice of NatГЎshaвҖҷs remark, but she did not express agreement in words. вҖңIs it possible to forget?вҖқ said she. вҖңIt did me so much good to tell all about it today. It was hard and painful, but good, very good!вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңI am sure he really loved him. That is why I told himвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Was it all right?вҖқ she added, suddenly blushing. вҖңTo tell Pierre? Oh, yes. What a splendid man he is!вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңDo you know, MГЎryaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ NatГЎsha suddenly said with a mischievous smile such as Princess MГЎrya had not seen on her face for a long time, вҖңhe has somehow grown so clean, smooth, and freshвҒ вҖ”as if he had just come out of a Russian bath; do you understand? Out of a moral bath. IsnвҖҷt it true?вҖқ вҖңYes,вҖқ replied Princess MГЎrya. вҖңHe has greatly improved.вҖқ вҖңWith a short coat and his hair cropped; just as if, well, just as if he had come straight from the bathвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Papa used toвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңI understand why heвҖқ (Prince AndrГ©y) вҖңliked no one so much as him,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңYes, and yet he is quite different. They say men are friends when they are quite different. That must be true. Really he is quite unlike himвҒ вҖ”in everything.вҖқ вҖңYes, but heвҖҷs wonderful.вҖқ вҖңWell, good night,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. And the same mischievous smile lingered for a long time on her face as if it had been forgotten there. XVIII XVIII It was a long time before Pierre could fall asleep that night. He paced up and down his room, now turning his thoughts on a difficult problem and frowning, now suddenly shrugging his shoulders and wincing, and now smiling happily. He was thinking of Prince AndrГ©y, of NatГЎsha, and of their love, at one moment jealous of her past, then reproaching himself for that feeling. It was already six in the morning and he still paced up and down the room. вҖңWell, whatвҖҷs to be done if it cannot be avoided? WhatвҖҷs to be done? Evidently it has to be so,вҖқ said he to himself, and hastily undressing he got into bed, happy and agitated but free from hesitation or indecision. вҖңStrange and impossible as such happiness seems, I must do everything that she and I may be man and wife,вҖқ he told himself. A few days previously Pierre had decided to go to Petersburg on the Friday. When he awoke on the Thursday, SavГ©lich came to ask him about packing for the journey. вҖңWhat, to Petersburg? What is Petersburg? Who is there in Petersburg?вҖқ he asked involuntarily, though only to himself. вҖңOh, yes, long ago before this happened I did for some reason mean to go to Petersburg,вҖқ he reflected. вҖңWhy? But perhaps I shall go. What a good fellow he is and how attentive, and how he remembers everything,вҖқ he thought, looking at SavГ©lichвҖҷs old face, вҖңand what a pleasant smile he has!вҖқ вҖңWell, SavГ©lich, do you still not wish to accept your freedom?вҖқ Pierre asked him. вҖңWhatвҖҷs the good of freedom to me, your excellency? We lived under the late countвҒ вҖ”the kingdom of heaven be his!вҒ вҖ”and we have lived under you too, without ever being wronged.вҖқ вҖңAnd your children?вҖқ вҖңThe children will live just the same. With such masters one can live.вҖқ вҖңBut what about my heirs?вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңSupposing I suddenly marryвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ it might happen,вҖқ he added with an involuntary smile. вҖңIf I may take the liberty, your excellency, it would be a good thing.вҖқ вҖңHow easy he thinks it,вҖқ thought Pierre. вҖңHe doesnвҖҷt know how terrible it is and how dangerous. Too soon or too lateвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ it is terrible!вҖқ вҖңSo what are your orders? Are you starting tomorrow?вҖқ asked SavГ©lich. вҖңNo, IвҖҷll put it off for a bit. IвҖҷll tell you later. You must forgive the trouble I have put you to,вҖқ said Pierre, and seeing SavГ©lich smile, he thought: вҖңBut how strange it is that he should not know that now there is no Petersburg for me, and that that must be settled first of all! But probably he knows it well enough and is only pretending. Shall I have a talk with him and see what he thinks?вҖқ Pierre reflected. вҖңNo, another time.вҖқ At breakfast Pierre told the princess, his cousin, that he had been to see Princess MГЎrya the day before and had there metвҒ вҖ”вҖңWhom do you think? NatГЎsha RostГіva!вҖқ The princess seemed to see nothing more extraordinary in that than if he had seen Anna SemГ«novna. вҖңDo you know her?вҖқ asked Pierre. вҖңI have seen the princess,вҖқ she replied. вҖңI heard that they were arranging a match for her with young RostГіv. It would be a very good thing for the RostГіvs, they are said to be utterly ruined.вҖқ вҖңNo; I mean do you know NatГЎsha RostГіva?вҖқ вҖңI heard about that affair of hers at the time. It was a great pity.вҖқ вҖңNo, she either doesnвҖҷt understand or is pretending,вҖқ thought Pierre. вҖңBetter not say anything to her either.вҖқ The princess too had prepared provisions for PierreвҖҷs journey. вҖңHow kind they all are,вҖқ thought Pierre. вҖңWhat is surprising is that they should trouble about these things now when it can no longer be of interest to them. And all for me!вҖқ On the same day the Chief of Police came to Pierre, inviting him to send a representative to the Faceted Palace to recover things that were to be returned to their owners that day. вҖңAnd this man too,вҖқ thought Pierre, looking into the face of the Chief of Police. вҖңWhat a fine, good-looking officer and how kind. Fancy bothering about such trifles now! And they actually say he is not honest and takes bribes. What nonsense! Besides, why shouldnвҖҷt he take bribes? ThatвҖҷs the way he was brought up, and everybody does it. But what a kind, pleasant face and how he smiles as he looks at me.вҖқ Pierre went to Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs to dinner. As he drove through the streets past the houses that had been burned down, he was surprised by the beauty of those ruins. The picturesqueness of the chimney stacks and tumbledown walls of the burned-out quarters of the town, stretching out and concealing one another, reminded him of the Rhine and the Colosseum. The cabmen he met and their passengers, the carpenters cutting the timber for new houses with axes, the women hawkers, and the shopkeepers, all looked at him with cheerful beaming eyes that seemed to say: вҖңAh, there he is! LetвҖҷs see what will come of it!вҖқ At the entrance to Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs house Pierre felt doubtful whether he had really been there the night before and really seen NatГЎsha and talked to her. вҖңPerhaps I imagined it; perhaps I shall go in and find no one there.вҖқ But he had hardly entered the room before he felt her presence with his whole being by the loss of his sense of freedom. She was in the same black dress with soft folds and her hair was done the same way as the day before, yet she was quite different. Had she been like this when he entered the day before he could not for a moment have failed to recognize her. She was as he had known her almost as a child and later on as Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs fiancГ©e. A bright questioning light shone in her eyes, and on her face was a friendly and strangely roguish expression. Pierre dined with them and would have spent the whole evening there, but Princess MГЎrya was going to vespers and Pierre left the house with her. Next day he came early, dined, and stayed the whole evening. Though Princess MГЎrya and NatГЎsha were evidently glad to see their visitor and though all PierreвҖҷs interest was now centered in that house, by the evening they had talked over everything and the conversation passed from one trivial topic to another and repeatedly broke off. He stayed so long that Princess MГЎrya and NatГЎsha exchanged glances, evidently wondering when he would go. Pierre noticed this but could not go. He felt uneasy and embarrassed, but sat on because he simply could not get up and take his leave. Princess MГЎrya, foreseeing no end to this, rose first, and complaining of a headache began to say good night. вҖңSo you are going to Petersburg tomorrow?вҖқ she asked. вҖңNo, I am not going,вҖқ Pierre replied hastily, in a surprised tone and as though offended. вҖңYesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ noвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ to Petersburg? TomorrowвҒ вҖ”but I wonвҖҷt say goodbye yet. I will call round in case you have any commissions for me,вҖқ said he, standing before Princess MГЎrya and turning red, but not taking his departure. NatГЎsha gave him her hand and went out. Princess MГЎrya on the other hand instead of going away sank into an armchair, and looked sternly and intently at him with her deep, radiant eyes. The weariness she had plainly shown before had now quite passed off. With a deep and long-drawn sigh she seemed to be prepared for a lengthy talk. When NatГЎsha left the room PierreвҖҷs confusion and awkwardness immediately vanished and were replaced by eager excitement. He quickly moved an armchair toward Princess MГЎrya. вҖңYes, I wanted to tell you,вҖқ said he, answering her look as if she had spoken. вҖңPrincess, help me! What am I to do? Can I hope? Princess, my dear friend, listen! I know it all. I know I am not worthy of her, I know itвҖҷs impossible to speak of it now. But I want to be a brother to her. No, not that, I donвҖҷt, I canвҖҷtвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ He paused and rubbed his face and eyes with his hands. вҖңWell,вҖқ he went on with an evident effort at self-control and coherence. вҖңI donвҖҷt know when I began to love her, but I have loved her and her alone all my life, and I love her so that I cannot imagine life without her. I cannot propose to her at present, but the thought that perhaps she might someday be my wife and that I may be missing that possibilityвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ that possibilityвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ is terrible. Tell me, can I hope? Tell me what I am to do, dear princess!вҖқ he added after a pause, and touched her hand as she did not reply. вҖңI am thinking of what you have told me,вҖқ answered Princess MГЎrya. вҖңThis is what I will say. You are right that to speak to her of love at presentвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Princess MГЎrya stopped. She was going to say that to speak of love was impossible, but she stopped because she had seen by the sudden change in NatГЎsha two days before that she would not only not be hurt if Pierre spoke of his love, but that it was the very thing she wished for. вҖңTo speak to her now wouldnвҖҷt do,вҖқ said the princess all the same. вҖңBut what am I to do?вҖқ вҖңLeave it to me,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. вҖңI knowвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre was looking into Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs eyes. вҖңWell?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Well?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he said. вҖңI know that she lovesвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ will love you,вҖқ Princess MГЎrya corrected herself. Before her words were out, Pierre had sprung up and with a frightened expression seized Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs hand. вҖңWhat makes you think so? You think I may hope? You thinkвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ?вҖқ вҖңYes, I think so,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya with a smile. вҖңWrite to her parents, and leave it to me. I will tell her when I can. I wish it to happen and my heart tells me it will.вҖқ вҖңNo, it cannot be! How happy I am! But it canвҖҷt be.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ How happy I am! No, it canвҖҷt be!вҖқ Pierre kept saying as he kissed Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs hands. вҖңGo to Petersburg, that will be best. And I will write to you,вҖқ she said. вҖңTo Petersburg? Go there? Very well, IвҖҷll go. But I may come again tomorrow?вҖқ Next day Pierre came to say goodbye. NatГЎsha was less animated than she had been the day before; but that day as he looked at her Pierre sometimes felt as if he was vanishing and that neither he nor she existed any longer, that nothing existed but happiness. вҖңIs it possible? No, it canвҖҷt be,вҖқ he told himself at every look, gesture, and word that filled his soul with joy. When on saying goodbye he took her thin, slender hand, he could not help holding it a little longer in his own. вҖңIs it possible that this hand, that face, those eyes, all this treasure of feminine charm so strange to me now, is it possible that it will one day be mine forever, as familiar to me as I am to myself?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No, thatвҖҷs impossible!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңGoodbye, Count,вҖқ she said aloud. вҖңI shall look forward very much to your return,вҖқ she added in a whisper. And these simple words, her look, and the expression on her face which accompanied them, formed for two months the subject of inexhaustible memories, interpretations, and happy meditations for Pierre. вҖңвҖҠвҖҳI shall look forward very much to your return.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҷ Yes, yes, how did she say it? Yes, вҖҳI shall look forward very much to your return.вҖҷ Oh, how happy I am! What is happening to me? How happy I am!вҖқ said Pierre to himself. XIX XIX There was nothing in PierreвҖҷs soul now at all like what had troubled it during his courtship of ElГЁn. He did not repeat to himself with a sickening feeling of shame the words he had spoken, or say: вҖңOh, why did I not say that?вҖқ and, вҖңWhatever made me say вҖҳJe vous aimeвҖҷ?вҖқ On the contrary, he now repeated in imagination every word that he or NatГЎsha had spoken and pictured every detail of her face and smile, and did not wish to diminish or add anything, but only to repeat it again and again. There was now not a shadow of doubt in his mind as to whether what he had undertaken was right or wrong. Only one terrible doubt sometimes crossed his mind: вҖңWasnвҖҷt it all a dream? IsnвҖҷt Princess MГЎrya mistaken? Am I not too conceited and self-confident? I believe all thisвҒ вҖ”and suddenly Princess MГЎrya will tell her, and she will be sure to smile and say: вҖҳHow strange! He must be deluding himself. DoesnвҖҷt he know that he is a man, just a man, while IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖҠ? I am something altogether different and higher.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ That was the only doubt often troubling Pierre. He did not now make any plans. The happiness before him appeared so inconceivable that if only he could attain it, it would be the end of all things. Everything ended with that. A joyful, unexpected frenzy, of which he had thought himself incapable, possessed him. The whole meaning of lifeвҒ вҖ”not for him alone but for the whole worldвҒ вҖ”seemed to him centered in his love and the possibility of being loved by her. At times everybody seemed to him to be occupied with one thing onlyвҒ вҖ”his future happiness. Sometimes it seemed to him that other people were all as pleased as he was himself and merely tried to hide that pleasure by pretending to be busy with other interests. In every word and gesture he saw allusions to his happiness. He often surprised those he met by his significantly happy looks and smiles which seemed to express a secret understanding between him and them. And when he realized that people might not be aware of his happiness, he pitied them with his whole heart and felt a desire somehow to explain to them that all that occupied them was a mere frivolous trifle unworthy of attention. When it was suggested to him that he should enter the civil service, or when the war or any general political affairs were discussed on the assumption that everybodyвҖҷs welfare depended on this or that issue of events, he would listen with a mild and pitying smile and surprise people by his strange comments. But at this time he saw everybodyвҒ вҖ”both those who, as he imagined, understood the real meaning of life (that is, what he was feeling) and those unfortunates who evidently did not understand itвҒ вҖ”in the bright light of the emotion that shone within himself, and at once without any effort saw in everyone he met everything that was good and worthy of being loved. When dealing with the affairs and papers of his dead wife, her memory aroused in him no feeling but pity that she had not known the bliss he now knew. Prince VasГӯli, who having obtained a new post and some fresh decorations was particularly proud at this time, seemed to him a pathetic, kindly old man much to be pitied. Often in afterlife Pierre recalled this period of blissful insanity. All the views he formed of men and circumstances at this time remained true for him always. He not only did not renounce them subsequently, but when he was in doubt or inwardly at variance, he referred to the views he had held at this time of his madness and they always proved correct. вҖңI may have appeared strange and queer then,вҖқ he thought, вҖңbut I was not so mad as I seemed. On the contrary I was then wiser and had more insight than at any other time, and understood all that is worth understanding in life, becauseвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ because I was happy.вҖқ PierreвҖҷs insanity consisted in not waiting, as he used to do, to discover personal attributes which he termed вҖңgood qualitiesвҖқ in people before loving them; his heart was now overflowing with love, and by loving people without cause he discovered indubitable causes for loving them. II II Besides a feeling of aloofness from everybody NatГЎsha was feeling a special estrangement from the members of her own family. All of themвҒ вҖ”her father, mother, and SГіnyaвҒ вҖ”were so near to her, so familiar, so commonplace, that all their words and feelings seemed an insult to the world in which she had been living of late, and she felt not merely indifferent to them but regarded them with hostility. She heard DunyГЎshaвҖҷs words about Pyotr IlГҪnich and a misfortune, but did not grasp them. вҖңWhat misfortune? What misfortune can happen to them? They just live their own old, quiet, and commonplace life,вҖқ thought NatГЎsha. As she entered the ballroom her father was hurriedly coming out of her motherвҖҷs room. His face was puckered up and wet with tears. He had evidently run out of that room to give vent to the sobs that were choking him. When he saw NatГЎsha he waved his arms despairingly and burst into convulsively painful sobs that distorted his soft round face. вҖңPeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ PГ©tyaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Go, go, sheвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ is callingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and weeping like a child and quickly shuffling on his feeble legs to a chair, he almost fell into it, covering his face with his hands. Suddenly an electric shock seemed to run through NatГЎshaвҖҷs whole being. Terrible anguish struck her heart, she felt a dreadful ache as if something was being torn inside her and she were dying. But the pain was immediately followed by a feeling of release from the oppressive constraint that had prevented her taking part in life. The sight of her father, the terribly wild cries of her mother that she heard through the door, made her immediately forget herself and her own grief. She ran to her father, but he feebly waved his arm, pointing to her motherвҖҷs door. Princess MГЎrya, pale and with quivering chin, came out from that room and taking NatГЎsha by the arm said something to her. NatГЎsha neither saw nor heard her. She went in with rapid steps, pausing at the door for an instant as if struggling with herself, and then ran to her mother. The countess was lying in an armchair in a strange and awkward position, stretching out and beating her head against the wall. SГіnya and the maids were holding her arms. вҖңNatГЎsha! NatГЎsha!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ cried the countess. вҖңItвҖҷs not trueвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ itвҖҷs not trueвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ HeвҖҷs lyingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ NatГЎsha!вҖқ she shrieked, pushing those around her away. вҖңGo away, all of you; itвҖҷs not true! Killed!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ha, ha, ha!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ItвҖҷs not true!вҖқ NatГЎsha put one knee on the armchair, stooped over her mother, embraced her, and with unexpected strength raised her, turned her face toward herself, and clung to her. вҖңMummy!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ darling!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I am here, my dearest Mummy,вҖқ she kept on whispering, not pausing an instant. She did not let go of her mother but struggled tenderly with her, demanded a pillow and hot water, and unfastened and tore open her motherвҖҷs dress. вҖңMy dearest darlingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Mummy, my precious!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ she whispered incessantly, kissing her head, her hands, her face, and feeling her own irrepressible and streaming tears tickling her nose and cheeks. The countess pressed her daughterвҖҷs hand, closed her eyes, and became quiet for a moment. Suddenly she sat up with unaccustomed swiftness, glanced vacantly around her, and seeing NatГЎsha began to press her daughterвҖҷs head with all her strength. Then she turned toward her daughterвҖҷs face which was wincing with pain and gazed long at it. вҖңNatГЎsha, you love me?вҖқ she said in a soft trustful whisper. вҖңNatГЎsha, you would not deceive me? YouвҖҷll tell me the whole truth?вҖқ NatГЎsha looked at her with eyes full of tears and in her look there was nothing but love and an entreaty for forgiveness. вҖңMy darling Mummy!вҖқ she repeated, straining all the power of her love to find some way of taking on herself the excess of grief that crushed her mother. And again in a futile struggle with reality her mother, refusing to believe that she could live when her beloved boy was killed in the bloom of life, escaped from reality into a world of delirium. NatГЎsha did not remember how that day passed nor that night, nor the next day and night. She did not sleep and did not leave her mother. Her persevering and patient love seemed completely to surround the countess every moment, not explaining or consoling, but recalling her to life. During the third night the countess kept very quiet for a few minutes, and NatГЎsha rested her head on the arm of her chair and closed her eyes, but opened them again on hearing the bedstead creak. The countess was sitting up in bed and speaking softly. вҖңHow glad I am you have come. You are tired. WonвҖҷt you have some tea?вҖқ NatГЎsha went up to her. вҖңYou have improved in looks and grown more manly,вҖқ continued the countess, taking her daughterвҖҷs hand. вҖңMamma! What are you sayingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңNatГЎsha, he is no more, no more!вҖқ And embracing her daughter, the countess began to weep for the first time. XX XX After PierreвҖҷs departure that first evening, when NatГЎsha had said to Princess MГЎrya with a gaily mocking smile: вҖңHe looks just, yes, just as if he had come out of a Russian bathвҒ вҖ”in a short coat and with his hair cropped,вҖқ something hidden and unknown to herself, but irrepressible, awoke in NatГЎshaвҖҷs soul. Everything: her face, walk, look, and voice, was suddenly altered. To her own surprise a power of life and hope of happiness rose to the surface and demanded satisfaction. From that evening she seemed to have forgotten all that had happened to her. She no longer complained of her position, did not say a word about the past, and no longer feared to make happy plans for the future. She spoke little of Pierre, but when Princess MГЎrya mentioned him a long-extinguished light once more kindled in her eyes and her lips curved with a strange smile. The change that took place in NatГЎsha at first surprised Princess MГЎrya; but when she understood its meaning it grieved her. вҖңCan she have loved my brother so little as to be able to forget him so soon?вҖқ she thought when she reflected on the change. But when she was with NatГЎsha she was not vexed with her and did not reproach her. The reawakened power of life that had seized NatГЎsha was so evidently irrepressible and unexpected by her that in her presence Princess MГЎrya felt that she had no right to reproach her even in her heart. NatГЎsha gave herself up so fully and frankly to this new feeling that she did not try to hide the fact that she was no longer sad, but bright and cheerful. When Princess MГЎrya returned to her room after her nocturnal talk with Pierre, NatГЎsha met her on the threshold. вҖңHe has spoken? Yes? He has spoken?вҖқ she repeated. And a joyful yet pathetic expression which seemed to beg forgiveness for her joy settled on NatГЎshaвҖҷs face. вҖңI wanted to listen at the door, but I knew you would tell me.вҖқ Understandable and touching as the look with which NatГЎsha gazed at her seemed to Princess MГЎrya, and sorry as she was to see her agitation, these words pained her for a moment. She remembered her brother and his love. вҖңBut whatвҖҷs to be done? She canвҖҷt help it,вҖқ thought the princess. And with a sad and rather stern look she told NatГЎsha all that Pierre had said. On hearing that he was going to Petersburg NatГЎsha was astounded. вҖңTo Petersburg!вҖқ she repeated as if unable to understand. But noticing the grieved expression on Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs face she guessed the reason of that sadness and suddenly began to cry. вҖңMГЎrya,вҖқ said she, вҖңtell me what I should do! I am afraid of being bad. Whatever you tell me, I will do. Tell me.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYou love him?вҖқ вҖңYes,вҖқ whispered NatГЎsha. вҖңThen why are you crying? I am happy for your sake,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya, who because of those tears quite forgave NatГЎshaвҖҷs joy. вҖңIt wonвҖҷt be just yetвҒ вҖ”someday. Think what fun it will be when I am his wife and you marry Nicolas!вҖқ вҖңNatГЎsha, I have asked you not to speak of that. Let us talk about you.вҖқ They were silent awhile. вҖңBut why go to Petersburg?вҖқ NatГЎsha suddenly asked, and hastily replied to her own question. вҖңBut no, no, he mustвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Yes, MГЎrya, He must.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ III III Princess MГЎrya postponed her departure. SГіnya and the count tried to replace NatГЎsha but could not. They saw that she alone was able to restrain her mother from unreasoning despair. For three weeks NatГЎsha remained constantly at her motherвҖҷs side, sleeping on a lounge chair in her room, making her eat and drink, and talking to her incessantly because the mere sound of her tender, caressing tones soothed her mother. The motherвҖҷs wounded spirit could not heal. PГ©tyaвҖҷs death had torn from her half her life. When the news of PГ©tyaвҖҷs death had come she had been a fresh and vigorous woman of fifty, but a month later she left her room a listless old woman taking no interest in life. But the same blow that almost killed the countess, this second blow, restored NatГЎsha to life. A spiritual wound produced by a rending of the spiritual body is like a physical wound and, strange as it may seem, just as a deep wound may heal and its edges join, physical and spiritual wounds alike can yet heal completely only as the result of a vital force from within. NatГЎshaвҖҷs wound healed in that way. She thought her life was ended, but her love for her mother unexpectedly showed her that the essence of lifeвҒ вҖ”loveвҒ вҖ”was still active within her. Love awoke and so did life. Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs last days had bound Princess MГЎrya and NatГЎsha together; this new sorrow brought them still closer to one another. Princess MГЎrya put off her departure, and for three weeks looked after NatГЎsha as if she had been a sick child. The last weeks passed in her motherвҖҷs bedroom had strained NatГЎshaвҖҷs physical strength. One afternoon noticing NatГЎsha shivering with fever, Princess MГЎrya took her to her own room and made her lie down on the bed. NatГЎsha lay down, but when Princess MГЎrya had drawn the blinds and was going away she called her back. вҖңI donвҖҷt want to sleep, MГЎrya, sit by me a little.вҖқ вҖңYou are tiredвҒ вҖ”try to sleep.вҖқ вҖңNo, no. Why did you bring me away? She will be asking for me.вҖқ вҖңShe is much better. She spoke so well today,вҖқ said Princess MГЎrya. NatГЎsha lay on the bed and in the semidarkness of the room scanned Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs face. вҖңIs she like him?вҖқ thought NatГЎsha. вҖңYes, like and yet not like. But she is quite original, strange, new, and unknown. And she loves me. What is in her heart? All that is good. But how? What is her mind like? What does she think about me? Yes, she is splendid!вҖқ вҖңMГЎsha,вҖқ she said timidly, drawing Princess MГЎryaвҖҷs hand to herself, вҖңMГЎsha, you mustnвҖҷt think me wicked. No? MГЎsha darling, how I love you! Let us be quite, quite friends.вҖқ And NatГЎsha, embracing her, began kissing her face and hands, making Princess MГЎrya feel shy but happy by this demonstration of her feelings. From that day a tender and passionate friendship such as exists only between women was established between Princess MГЎrya and NatГЎsha. They were continually kissing and saying tender things to one another and spent most of their time together. When one went out the other became restless and hastened to rejoin her. Together they felt more in harmony with one another than either of them felt with herself when alone. A feeling stronger than friendship sprang up between them; an exclusive feeling of life being possible only in each otherвҖҷs presence. Sometimes they were silent for hours; sometimes after they were already in bed they would begin talking and go on till morning. They spoke most of what was long past. Princess MГЎrya spoke of her childhood, of her mother, her father, and her daydreams; and NatГЎsha, who with a passive lack of understanding had formerly turned away from that life of devotion, submission, and the poetry of Christian self-sacrifice, now feeling herself bound to Princess MГЎrya by affection, learned to love her past too and to understand a side of life previously incomprehensible to her. She did not think of applying submission and self-abnegation to her own life, for she was accustomed to seek other joys, but she understood and loved in another those previously incomprehensible virtues. For Princess MГЎrya, listening to NatГЎshaвҖҷs tales of childhood and early youth, there also opened out a new and hitherto uncomprehended side of life: belief in life and its enjoyment. Just as before, they never mentioned him so as not to lower (as they thought) their exalted feelings by words; but this silence about him had the effect of making them gradually begin to forget him without being conscious of it. NatГЎsha had grown thin and pale and physically so weak that they all talked about her health, and this pleased her. But sometimes she was suddenly overcome by fear not only of death but of sickness, weakness, and loss of good looks, and involuntarily she examined her bare arm carefully, surprised at its thinness, and in the morning noticed her drawn and, as it seemed to her, piteous face in her glass. It seemed to her that things must be so, and yet it was dreadfully sad. One day she went quickly upstairs and found herself out of breath. Unconsciously she immediately invented a reason for going down, and then, testing her strength, ran upstairs again, observing the result. Another time when she called DunyГЎsha her voice trembled, so she called againвҒ вҖ”though she could hear DunyГЎsha comingвҒ вҖ”called her in the deep chest tones in which she had been wont to sing, and listened attentively to herself. She did not know and would not have believed it, but beneath the layer of slime that covered her soul and seemed to her impenetrable, delicate young shoots of grass were already sprouting, which taking root would so cover with their living verdure the grief that weighed her down that it would soon no longer be seen or noticed. The wound had begun to heal from within. At the end of January Princess MГЎrya left for Moscow, and the count insisted on NatГЎshaвҖҷs going with her to consult the doctors. IV IV After the encounter at VyГЎzma, where KutГәzov had been unable to hold back his troops in their anxiety to overwhelm and cut off the enemy and so on, the farther movement of the fleeing French, and of the Russians who pursued them, continued as far as KrГЎsnoe without a battle. The flight was so rapid that the Russian army pursuing the French could not keep up with them; cavalry and artillery horses broke down, and the information received of the movements of the French was never reliable. The men in the Russian army were so worn out by this continuous marching at the rate of twenty-seven miles a day that they could not go any faster. To realize the degree of exhaustion of the Russian army it is only necessary to grasp clearly the meaning of the fact that, while not losing more than five thousand killed and wounded after TarГәtino and less than a hundred prisoners, the Russian army which left that place a hundred thousand strong reached KrГЎsnoe with only fifty thousand. The rapidity of the Russian pursuit was just as destructive to our army as the flight of the French was to theirs. The only difference was that the Russian army moved voluntarily, with no such threat of destruction as hung over the French, and that the sick Frenchmen were left behind in enemy hands while the sick Russians left behind were among their own people. The chief cause of the wastage of NapoleonвҖҷs army was the rapidity of its movement, and a convincing proof of this is the corresponding decrease of the Russian army. KutГәzov as far as was in his power, instead of trying to check the movement of the French as was desired in Petersburg and by the Russian army generals, directed his whole activity here, as he had done at TarГәtino and VyГЎzma, to hastening it on while easing the movement of our army. But besides this, since the exhaustion and enormous diminution of the army caused by the rapidity of the advance had become evident, another reason for slackening the pace and delaying presented itself to KutГәzov. The aim of the Russian army was to pursue the French. The road the French would take was unknown, and so the closer our troops trod on their heels the greater distance they had to cover. Only by following at some distance could one cut across the zigzag path of the French. All the artful maneuvers suggested by our generals meant fresh movements of the army and a lengthening of its marches, whereas the only reasonable aim was to shorten those marches. To that end KutГәzovвҖҷs activity was directed during the whole campaign from Moscow to VГӯlnaвҒ вҖ”not casually or intermittently but so consistently that he never once deviated from it. KutГәzov felt and knewвҒ вҖ”not by reasoning or science but with the whole of his Russian beingвҒ вҖ”what every Russian soldier felt: that the French were beaten, that the enemy was flying and must be driven out; but at the same time he like the soldiers realized all the hardship of this march, the rapidity of which was unparalleled for such a time of the year. But to the generals, especially the foreign ones in the Russian army, who wished to distinguish themselves, to astonish somebody, and for some reason to capture a king or a dukeвҒ вҖ”it seemed that nowвҒ вҖ”when any battle must be horrible and senselessвҒ вҖ”was the very time to fight and conquer somebody. KutГәzov merely shrugged his shoulders when one after another they presented projects of maneuvers to be made with those soldiersвҒ вҖ”ill-shod, insufficiently clad, and half starvedвҒ вҖ”who within a month and without fighting a battle had dwindled to half their number, and who at the best if the flight continued would have to go a greater distance than they had already traversed, before they reached the frontier. This longing to distinguish themselves, to maneuver, to overthrow, and to cut off showed itself particularly whenever the Russians stumbled on the French army. So it was at KrГЎsnoe, where they expected to find one of the three French columns and stumbled instead on Napoleon himself with sixteen thousand men. Despite all KutГәzovвҖҷs efforts to avoid that ruinous encounter and to preserve his troops, the massacre of the broken mob of French soldiers by worn-out Russians continued at KrГЎsnoe for three days. Toll wrote a disposition: вҖңThe first column will march to so-and-so,вҖқ etc. And as usual nothing happened in accord with the disposition. Prince EugГЁne of WГјrttemberg fired from a hill over the French crowds that were running past, and demanded reinforcements which did not arrive. The French, avoiding the Russians, dispersed and hid themselves in the forest by night, making their way round as best they could, and continued their flight. MilorГЎdovich, who said he did not want to know anything about the commissariat affairs of his detachment, and could never be found when he was wantedвҒ вҖ”that chevalier sans peur et sans reproche132 as he styled himselfвҒ вҖ”who was fond of parleys with the French, sent envoys demanding their surrender, wasted time, and did not do what he was ordered to do. вҖңI give you that column, lads,вҖқ he said, riding up to the troops and pointing out the French to the cavalry. And the cavalry, with spurs and sabers urging on horses that could scarcely move, trotted with much effort to the column presented to themвҒ вҖ”that is to say, to a crowd of Frenchmen stark with cold, frostbitten, and starvingвҒ вҖ”and the column that had been presented to them threw down its arms and surrendered as it had long been anxious to do. At KrГЎsnoe they took twenty-six thousand prisoners, several hundred cannon, and a stick called a вҖңmarshalвҖҷs staff,вҖқ and disputed as to who had distinguished himself and were pleased with their achievementвҒ вҖ”though they much regretted not having taken Napoleon, or at least a marshal or a hero of some sort, and reproached one another and especially KutГәzov for having failed to do so. These men, carried away by their passions, were but blind tools of the most melancholy law of necessity, but considered themselves heroes and imagined that they were accomplishing a most noble and honorable deed. They blamed KutГәzov and said that from the very beginning of the campaign he had prevented their vanquishing Napoleon, that he thought of nothing but satisfying his passions and would not advance from the Linen Factories because he was comfortable there, that at KrГЎsnoe he checked the advance because on learning that Napoleon was there he had quite lost his head, and that it was probable that he had an understanding with Napoleon and had been bribed by him, and so on, and so on. Not only did his contemporaries, carried away by their passions, talk in this way, but posterity and history have acclaimed Napoleon as grand, while KutГәzov is described by foreigners as a crafty, dissolute, weak old courtier, and by Russians as something indefiniteвҒ вҖ”a sort of puppet useful only because he had a Russian name. V V In 1812 and 1813 KutГәzov was openly accused of blundering. The Emperor was dissatisfied with him. And in a history recently written by order of the Highest Authorities it is said that KutГәzov was a cunning court liar, frightened of the name of Napoleon, and that by his blunders at KrГЎsnoe and the BerГ«zina he deprived the Russian army of the glory of complete victory over the French.133 Such is the fate not of great men (grands hommes) whom the Russian mind does not acknowledge, but of those rare and always solitary individuals who, discerning the will of Providence, submit their personal will to it. The hatred and contempt of the crowd punish such men for discerning the higher laws. For Russian historians, strange and terrible to say, NapoleonвҒ вҖ”that most insignificant tool of history who never anywhere, even in exile, showed human dignityвҒ вҖ”Napoleon is the object of adulation and enthusiasm; he is grand. But KutГәzovвҒ вҖ”the man who from the beginning to the end of his activity in 1812, never once swerving by word or deed from BorodinГі to VГӯlna, presented an example exceptional in history of self-sacrifice and a present consciousness of the future importance of what was happeningвҒ вҖ”KutГәzov seems to them something indefinite and pitiful, and when speaking of him and of the year 1812 they always seem a little ashamed. And yet it is difficult to imagine an historical character whose activity was so unswervingly directed to a single aim; and it would be difficult to imagine any aim more worthy or more consonant with the will of the whole people. Still more difficult would it be to find an instance in history of the aim of an historical personage being so completely accomplished as that to which all KutГәzovвҖҷs efforts were directed in 1812. KutГәzov never talked of вҖңforty centuries looking down from the Pyramids,вҖқ of the sacrifices he offered for the fatherland, or of what he intended to accomplish or had accomplished; in general he said nothing about himself, adopted no pose, always appeared to be the simplest and most ordinary of men, and said the simplest and most ordinary things. He wrote letters to his daughters and to Madame de StaГ«l, read novels, liked the society of pretty women, jested with generals, officers, and soldiers, and never contradicted those who tried to prove anything to him. When Count RostopchГӯn at the YaГәza bridge galloped up to KutГәzov with personal reproaches for having caused the destruction of Moscow, and said: вҖңHow was it you promised not to abandon Moscow without a battle?вҖқ KutГәzov replied: вҖңAnd I shall not abandon Moscow without a battle,вҖқ though Moscow was then already abandoned. When ArakchГ©ev, coming to him from the Emperor, said that ErmГіlov ought to be appointed chief of the artillery, KutГәzov replied: вҖңYes, I was just saying so myself,вҖқ though a moment before he had said quite the contrary. What did it matter to himвҒ вҖ”who then alone amid a senseless crowd understood the whole tremendous significance of what was happeningвҒ вҖ”what did it matter to him whether RostopchГӯn attributed the calamities of Moscow to him or to himself? Still less could it matter to him who was appointed chief of the artillery. Not merely in these cases but continually did that old manвҒ вҖ”who by experience of life had reached the conviction that thoughts and the words serving as their expression are not what move peopleвҒ вҖ”use quite meaningless words that happened to enter his head. But that man, so heedless of his words, did not once during the whole time of his activity utter one word inconsistent with the single aim toward which he moved throughout the whole war. Obviously in spite of himself, in very diverse circumstances, he repeatedly expressed his real thoughts with the bitter conviction that he would not be understood. Beginning with the battle of BorodinГі, from which time his disagreement with those about him began, he alone said that the battle of BorodinГі was a victory, and repeated this both verbally and in his dispatches and reports up to the time of his death. He alone said that the loss of Moscow is not the loss of Russia. In reply to LauristonвҖҷs proposal of peace, he said: There can be no peace, for such is the peopleвҖҷs will. He alone during the retreat of the French said that all our maneuvers are useless, everything is being accomplished of itself better than we could desire; that the enemy must be offered вҖңa golden bridgeвҖқ; that neither the TarГәtino, the VyГЎzma, nor the KrГЎsnoe battles were necessary; that we must keep some force to reach the frontier with, and that he would not sacrifice a single Russian for ten Frenchmen. And this courtier, as he is described to us, who lies to ArakchГ©ev to please the Emperor, he aloneвҒ вҖ”incurring thereby the EmperorвҖҷs displeasureвҒ вҖ”said in VГӯlna that to carry the war beyond the frontier is useless and harmful. Nor do words alone prove that only he understood the meaning of the events. His actionsвҒ вҖ”without the smallest deviationвҒ вҖ”were all directed to one and the same threefold end: (1) to brace all his strength for conflict with the French, (2) to defeat them, and (3) to drive them out of Russia, minimizing as far as possible the sufferings of our people and of our army. This procrastinator KutГәzov, whose motto was вҖңPatience and Time,вҖқ this enemy of decisive action, gave battle at BorodinГі, investing the preparations for it with unparalleled solemnity. This KutГәzov who before the battle of Austerlitz began said that it would be lost, he alone, in contradiction to everyone else, declared till his death that BorodinГі was a victory, despite the assurance of generals that the battle was lost and despite the fact that for an army to have to retire after winning a battle was unprecedented. He alone during the whole retreat insisted that battles, which were useless then, should not be fought, and that a new war should not be begun nor the frontiers of Russia crossed. It is easy now to understand the significance of these eventsвҒ вҖ”if only we abstain from attributing to the activity of the mass aims that existed only in the heads of a dozen individualsвҒ вҖ”for the events and results now lie before us. But how did that old man, alone, in opposition to the general opinion, so truly discern the importance of the peopleвҖҷs view of the events that in all his activity he was never once untrue to it? The source of that extraordinary power of penetrating the meaning of the events then occuring lay in the national feeling which he possessed in full purity and strength. Only the recognition of the fact that he possessed this feeling caused the people in so strange a manner, contrary to the TsarвҖҷs wish, to select himвҒ вҖ”an old man in disfavorвҒ вҖ”to be their representative in the national war. And only that feeling placed him on that highest human pedestal from which he, the commander in chief, devoted all his powers not to slaying and destroying men but to saving and showing pity on them. That simple, modest, and therefore truly great, figure could not be cast in the false mold of a European heroвҒ вҖ”the supposed ruler of menвҒ вҖ”that history has invented. To a lackey no man can be great, for a lackey has his own conception of greatness. VI VI The fifth of November was the first day of what is called the battle of KrГЎsnoe. Toward eveningвҒ вҖ”after much disputing and many mistakes made by generals who did not go to their proper places, and after adjutants had been sent about with counterordersвҒ вҖ”when it had become plain that the enemy was everywhere in flight and that there could and would be no battle, KutГәzov left KrГЎsnoe and went to DГіbroe whither his headquarters had that day been transferred. The day was clear and frosty. KutГәzov rode to DГіbroe on his plump little white horse, followed by an enormous suite of discontented generals who whispered among themselves behind his back. All along the road groups of French prisoners captured that day (there were seven thousand of them) were crowding to warm themselves at campfires. Near DГіbroe an immense crowd of tattered prisoners, buzzing with talk and wrapped and bandaged in anything they had been able to get hold of, were standing in the road beside a long row of unharnessed French guns. At the approach of the commander in chief the buzz of talk ceased and all eyes were fixed on KutГәzov who, wearing a white cap with a red band and a padded overcoat that bulged on his round shoulders, moved slowly along the road on his white horse. One of the generals was reporting to him where the guns and prisoners had been captured. KutГәzov seemed preoccupied and did not listen to what the general was saying. He screwed up his eyes with a dissatisfied look as he gazed attentively and fixedly at these prisoners, who presented a specially wretched appearance. Most of them were disfigured by frostbitten noses and cheeks, and nearly all had red, swollen and festering eyes. One group of the French stood close to the road, and two of them, one of whom had his face covered with sores, were tearing a piece of raw flesh with their hands. There was something horrible and bestial in the fleeting glance they threw at the riders and in the malevolent expression with which, after a glance at KutГәzov, the soldier with the sores immediately turned away and went on with what he was doing. KutГәzov looked long and intently at these two soldiers. He puckered his face, screwed up his eyes, and pensively swayed his head. At another spot he noticed a Russian soldier laughingly patting a Frenchman on the shoulder, saying something to him in a friendly manner, and KutГәzov with the same expression on his face again swayed his head. вҖңWhat were you saying?вҖқ he asked the general, who continuing his report directed the commander in chiefвҖҷs attention to some standards captured from the French and standing in front of the PreobrazhГ©nsk regiment. вҖңAh, the standards!вҖқ said KutГәzov, evidently detaching himself with difficulty from the thoughts that preoccupied him. He looked about him absently. Thousands of eyes were looking at him from all sides awaiting a word from him. He stopped in front of the PreobrazhГ©nsk regiment, sighed deeply, and closed his eyes. One of his suite beckoned to the soldiers carrying the standards to advance and surround the commander in chief with them. KutГәzov was silent for a few seconds and then, submitting with evident reluctance to the duty imposed by his position, raised his head and began to speak. A throng of officers surrounded him. He looked attentively around at the circle of officers, recognizing several of them. вҖңI thank you all!вҖқ he said, addressing the soldiers and then again the officers. In the stillness around him his slowly uttered words were distinctly heard. вҖңI thank you all for your hard and faithful service. The victory is complete and Russia will not forget you! Honor to you forever.вҖқ He paused and looked around. вҖңLower its head, lower it!вҖқ he said to a soldier who had accidentally lowered the French eagle he was holding before the PreobrazhГ©nsk standards. вҖңLower, lower, thatвҖҷs it. Hurrah lads!вҖқ he added, addressing the men with a rapid movement of his chin. вҖңHur-r-rah!вҖқ roared thousands of voices. While the soldiers were shouting KutГәzov leaned forward in his saddle and bowed his head, and his eye lit up with a mild and apparently ironic gleam. вҖңYou see, brothersвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said he when the shouts had ceasedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and all at once his voice and the expression of his face changed. It was no longer the commander in chief speaking but an ordinary old man who wanted to tell his comrades something very important. There was a stir among the throng of officers and in the ranks of the soldiers, who moved that they might hear better what he was going to say. вҖңYou see, brothers, I know itвҖҷs hard for you, but it canвҖҷt be helped! Bear up; it wonвҖҷt be for long now! WeвҖҷll see our visitors off and then weвҖҷll rest. The Tsar wonвҖҷt forget your service. It is hard for you, but still you are at home while theyвҒ вҖ”you see what they have come to,вҖқ said he, pointing to the prisoners. вҖңWorse off than our poorest beggars. While they were strong we didnвҖҷt spare ourselves, but now we may even pity them. They are human beings too. IsnвҖҷt it so, lads?вҖқ He looked around, and in the direct, respectful, wondering gaze fixed upon him he read sympathy with what he had said. His face grew brighter and brighter with an old manвҖҷs mild smile, which drew the corners of his lips and eyes into a cluster of wrinkles. He ceased speaking and bowed his head as if in perplexity. вҖңBut after all who asked them here? Serves them right, the bloody bastards!вҖқ he cried, suddenly lifting his head. And flourishing his whip he rode off at a gallop for the first time during the whole campaign, and left the broken ranks of the soldiers laughing joyfully and shouting вҖңHurrah!вҖқ KutГәzovвҖҷs words were hardly understood by the troops. No one could have repeated the field marshalвҖҷs address, begun solemnly and then changing into an old manвҖҷs simplehearted talk; but the hearty sincerity of that speech, the feeling of majestic triumph combined with pity for the foe and consciousness of the justice of our cause, exactly expressed by that old manвҖҷs good-natured expletives, was not merely understood but lay in the soul of every soldier and found expression in their joyous and long-sustained shouts. Afterwards when one of the generals addressed KutГәzov asking whether he wished his calГЁche to be sent for, KutГәzov in answering unexpectedly gave a sob, being evidently greatly moved. VII VII When the troops reached their nightвҖҷs halting place on the eighth of November, the last day of the KrГЎsnoe battles, it was already growing dusk. All day it had been calm and frosty with occasional lightly falling snow and toward evening it began to clear. Through the falling snow a purple-black and starry sky showed itself and the frost grew keener. An infantry regiment which had left TarГәtino three thousand strong but now numbered only nine hundred was one of the first to arrive that night at its halting placeвҒ вҖ”a village on the high road. The quartermasters who met the regiment announced that all the huts were full of sick and dead Frenchmen, cavalrymen, and members of the staff. There was only one hut available for the regimental commander. The commander rode up to his hut. The regiment passed through the village and stacked its arms in front of the last huts. Like some huge many-limbed animal, the regiment began to prepare its lair and its food. One part of it dispersed and waded knee-deep through the snow into a birch forest to the right of the village, and immediately the sound of axes and swords, the crashing of branches, and merry voices could be heard from there. Another section amid the regimental wagons and horses which were standing in a group was busy getting out cauldrons and rye biscuit, and feeding the horses. A third section scattered through the village arranging quarters for the staff officers, carrying out the French corpses that were in the huts, and dragging away boards, dry wood, and thatch from the roofs, for the campfires, or wattle fences to serve for shelter. Some fifteen men with merry shouts were shaking down the high wattle wall of a shed, the roof of which had already been removed. вҖңNow then, all togetherвҒ вҖ”shove!вҖқ cried the voices, and the huge surface of the wall, sprinkled with snow and creaking with frost, was seen swaying in the gloom of the night. The lower stakes cracked more and more and at last the wall fell, and with it the men who had been pushing it. Loud, coarse laughter and joyous shouts ensued. вҖңNow then, catch hold in twos! Hand up the lever! ThatвҖҷs it.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Where are you shoving to?вҖқ вҖңNow, all together! But wait a moment, boysвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ With a song!вҖқ All stood silent, and a soft, pleasant velvety voice began to sing. At the end of the third verse as the last note died away, twenty voices roared out at once: вҖңOo-oo-oo-oo! ThatвҖҷs it. All together! Heave away, boys!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ but despite their united efforts the wattle hardly moved, and in the silence that followed the heavy breathing of the men was audible. вҖңHere, you of the Sixth Company! Devils that you are! Lend a handвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ will you? You may want us one of these days.вҖқ Some twenty men of the Sixth Company who were on their way into the village joined the haulers, and the wattle wall, which was about thirty-five feet long and seven feet high, moved forward along the village street, swaying, pressing upon and cutting the shoulders of the gasping men. вҖңGet alongвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Falling? What are you stopping for? There now.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Merry senseless words of abuse flowed freely. вҖңWhat are you up to?вҖқ suddenly came the authoritative voice of a sergeant major who came upon the men who were hauling their burden. вҖңThere are gentry here; the general himself is in that hut, and you foul-mouthed devils, you brutes, IвҖҷll give it to you!вҖқ shouted he, hitting the first man who came in his way a swinging blow on the back. вҖңCanвҖҷt you make less noise?вҖқ The men became silent. The soldier who had been struck groaned and wiped his face, which had been scratched till it bled by his falling against the wattle. вҖңThere, how that devil hits out! HeвҖҷs made my face all bloody,вҖқ said he in a frightened whisper when the sergeant major had passed on. вҖңDonвҖҷt you like it?вҖқ said a laughing voice, and moderating their tones the men moved forward. When they were out of the village they began talking again as loud as before, interlarding their talk with the same aimless expletives. In the hut which the men had passed, the chief officers had gathered and were in animated talk over their tea about the events of the day and the maneuvers suggested for tomorrow. It was proposed to make a flank march to the left, cut off the Vice-King (Murat) and capture him. By the time the soldiers had dragged the wattle fence to its place the campfires were blazing on all sides ready for cooking, the wood crackled, the snow was melting, and black shadows of soldiers flitted to and fro all over the occupied space where the snow had been trodden down. Axes and choppers were plied all around. Everything was done without any orders being given. Stores of wood were brought for the night, shelters were rigged up for the officers, cauldrons were being boiled, and muskets and accouterments put in order. The wattle wall the men had brought was set up in a semicircle by the Eighth Company as a shelter from the north, propped up by musket rests, and a campfire was built before it. They beat the tattoo, called the roll, had supper, and settled down round the fires for the nightвҒ вҖ”some repairing their footgear, some smoking pipes, and some stripping themselves naked to steam the lice out of their shirts. VIII VIII One would have thought that under the almost incredibly wretched conditions the Russian soldiers were in at that timeвҒ вҖ”lacking warm boots and sheepskin coats, without a roof over their heads, in the snow with eighteen degrees of frost, and without even full rations (the commissariat did not always keep up with the troops)вҒ вҖ”they would have presented a very sad and depressing spectacle. On the contrary, the army had never under the best material conditions presented a more cheerful and animated aspect. This was because all who began to grow depressed or who lost strength were sifted out of the army day by day. All the physically or morally weak had long since been left behind and only the flower of the armyвҒ вҖ”physically and mentallyвҒ вҖ”remained. More men collected behind the wattle fence of the Eighth Company than anywhere else. Two sergeants major were sitting with them and their campfire blazed brighter than others. For leave to sit by their wattle they demanded contributions of fuel. вҖңEh, MakГ©ev! What has become of you, you son of a bitch? Are you lost or have the wolves eaten you? Fetch some more wood!вҖқ shouted a red-haired and red-faced man, screwing up his eyes and blinking because of the smoke but not moving back from the fire. вҖңAnd you, Jackdaw, go and fetch some wood!вҖқ said he to another soldier. This red-haired man was neither a sergeant nor a corporal, but being robust he ordered about those weaker than himself. The soldier they called вҖңJackdaw,вҖқ a thin little fellow with a sharp nose, rose obediently and was about to go but at that instant there came into the light of the fire the slender, handsome figure of a young soldier carrying a load of wood. вҖңBring it hereвҒ вҖ”thatвҖҷs fine!вҖқ They split up the wood, pressed it down on the fire, blew at it with their mouths, and fanned it with the skirts of their greatcoats, making the flames hiss and crackle. The men drew nearer and lit their pipes. The handsome young soldier who had brought the wood, setting his arms akimbo, began stamping his cold feet rapidly and deftly on the spot where he stood. вҖңMother! The dew is cold but clear.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ ItвҖҷs well that IвҖҷm a musketeerвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he sang, pretending to hiccup after each syllable. вҖңLook out, your soles will fly off!вҖқ shouted the red-haired man, noticing that the sole of the dancerвҖҷs boot was hanging loose. вҖңWhat a fellow you are for dancing!вҖқ The dancer stopped, pulled off the loose piece of leather, and threw it on the fire. вҖңRight enough, friend,вҖқ said he, and, having sat down, took out of his knapsack a scrap of blue French cloth, and wrapped it round his foot. вҖңItвҖҷs the steam that spoils them,вҖқ he added, stretching out his feet toward the fire. вҖңTheyвҖҷll soon be issuing us new ones. They say that when weвҖҷve finished hammering them, weвҖҷre to receive double kits!вҖқ вҖңAnd that son of a bitch PetrГіv has lagged behind after all, it seems,вҖқ said one sergeant major. вҖңIвҖҷve had an eye on him this long while,вҖқ said the other. вҖңWell, heвҖҷs a poor sort of soldier.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңBut in the Third Company they say nine men were missing yesterday.вҖқ вҖңYes, itвҖҷs all very well, but when a manвҖҷs feet are frozen how can he walk?вҖқ вҖңEh? DonвҖҷt talk nonsense!вҖқ said a sergeant major. вҖңDo you want to be doing the same?вҖқ said an old soldier, turning reproachfully to the man who had spoken of frozen feet. вҖңWell, you know,вҖқ said the sharp-nosed man they called Jackdaw in a squeaky and unsteady voice, raising himself at the other side of the fire, вҖңa plump man gets thin, but for a thin one itвҖҷs death. Take me, now! IвҖҷve got no strength left,вҖқ he added, with sudden resolution turning to the sergeant major. вҖңTell them to send me to hospital; IвҖҷm aching all over; anyway I shanвҖҷt be able to keep up.вҖқ вҖңThatвҖҷll do, thatвҖҷll do!вҖқ replied the sergeant major quietly. The soldier said no more and the talk went on. вҖңWhat a lot of those Frenchies were taken today, and the fact is that not one of them had what you might call real boots on,вҖқ said a soldier, starting a new theme. вҖңThey were no more than make-believes.вҖқ вҖңThe Cossacks have taken their boots. They were clearing the hut for the colonel and carried them out. It was pitiful to see them, boys,вҖқ put in the dancer. вҖңAs they turned them over one seemed still alive and, would you believe it, he jabbered something in their lingo.вҖқ вҖңBut theyвҖҷre a clean folk, lads,вҖқ the first man went on; вҖңhe was whiteвҒ вҖ”as white as birchbarkвҒ вҖ”and some of them are such fine fellows, you might think they were nobles.вҖқ вҖңWell, what do you think? They make soldiers of all classes there.вҖқ вҖңBut they donвҖҷt understand our talk at all,вҖқ said the dancer with a puzzled smile. вҖңI asked him whose subject he was, and he jabbered in his own way. A queer lot!вҖқ вҖңBut itвҖҷs strange, friends,вҖқ continued the man who had wondered at their whiteness, вҖңthe peasants at MozhГЎysk were saying that when they began burying the deadвҒ вҖ”where the battle was you knowвҒ вҖ”well, those dead had been lying there for nearly a month, and says the peasant, вҖҳthey lie as white as paper, clean, and not as much smell as a puff of powder smoke.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ вҖңWas it from the cold?вҖқ asked someone. вҖңYouвҖҷre a clever fellow! From the cold indeed! Why, it was hot. If it had been from the cold, ours would not have rotted either. вҖҳBut,вҖҷ he says, вҖҳgo up to ours and they are all rotten and maggoty. So,вҖҷ he says, вҖҳwe tie our faces up with kerchiefs and turn our heads away as we drag them off: we can hardly do it. But theirs,вҖҷ he says, вҖҳare white as paper and not so much smell as a whiff of gunpowder.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ All were silent. вҖңIt must be from their food,вҖқ said the sergeant major. вҖңThey used to gobble the same food as the gentry.вҖқ No one contradicted him. вҖңThat peasant near MozhГЎysk where the battle was said the men were all called up from ten villages around and they carted for twenty days and still didnвҖҷt finish carting the dead away. And as for the wolves, he saysвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңThat was a real battle,вҖқ said an old soldier. вҖңItвҖҷs the only one worth remembering; but since thatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ itвҖҷs only been tormenting folk.вҖқ вҖңAnd do you know, Daddy, the day before yesterday we ran at them and, my word, they didnвҖҷt let us get near before they just threw down their muskets and went on their knees. вҖҳPardon!вҖҷ they say. ThatвҖҷs only one case. They say PlГЎtov took вҖҳPoleon himself twice. But he didnвҖҷt know the right charm. He catches him and catches himвҒ вҖ”no good! He turns into a bird in his hands and flies away. And thereвҖҷs no way of killing him either.вҖқ вҖңYouвҖҷre a first-class liar, KiselГ«v, when I come to look at you!вҖқ вҖңLiar, indeed! ItвҖҷs the real truth.вҖқ вҖңIf he fell into my hands, when IвҖҷd caught him IвҖҷd bury him in the ground with an aspen stake to fix him down. What a lot of men heвҖҷs ruined!вҖқ вҖңWell, anyhow weвҖҷre going to end it. He wonвҖҷt come here again,вҖқ remarked the old soldier, yawning. The conversation flagged, and the soldiers began settling down to sleep. вҖңLook at the stars. ItвҖҷs wonderful how they shine! You would think the women had spread out their linen,вҖқ said one of the men, gazing with admiration at the Milky Way. вҖңThatвҖҷs a sign of a good harvest next year.вҖқ вҖңWe shall want some more wood.вҖқ вҖңYou warm your back and your belly gets frozen. ThatвҖҷs queer.вҖқ вҖңO Lord!вҖқ вҖңWhat are you pushing for? Is the fire only for you? Look how heвҖҷs sprawling!вҖқ In the silence that ensued, the snoring of those who had fallen asleep could be heard. Others turned over and warmed themselves, now and again exchanging a few words. From a campfire a hundred paces off came a sound of general, merry laughter. вҖңHark at them roaring there in the Fifth Company!вҖқ said one of the soldiers, вҖңand what a lot of them there are!вҖқ One of the men got up and went over to the Fifth Company. вҖңTheyвҖҷre having such fun,вҖқ said he, coming back. вҖңTwo Frenchies have turned up. OneвҖҷs quite frozen and the otherвҖҷs an awful swaggerer. HeвҖҷs singing songs.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңOh, IвҖҷll go across and have a look.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ And several of the men went over to the Fifth Company. IX IX The Fifth Company was bivouacking at the very edge of the forest. A huge campfire was blazing brightly in the midst of the snow, lighting up the branches of trees heavy with hoarfrost. About midnight they heard the sound of steps in the snow of the forest, and the crackling of dry branches. вҖңA bear, lads,вҖқ said one of the men. They all raised their heads to listen, and out of the forest into the bright firelight stepped two strangely clad human figures clinging to one another. These were two Frenchmen who had been hiding in the forest. They came up to the fire, hoarsely uttering something in a language our soldiers did not understand. One was taller than the other; he wore an officerвҖҷs hat and seemed quite exhausted. On approaching the fire he had been going to sit down, but fell. The other, a short sturdy soldier with a shawl tied round his head, was stronger. He raised his companion and said something, pointing to his mouth. The soldiers surrounded the Frenchmen, spread a greatcoat on the ground for the sick man, and brought some buckwheat porridge and vodka for both of them. The exhausted French officer was Ramballe and the man with his head wrapped in the shawl was Morel, his orderly. When Morel had drunk some vodka and finished his bowl of porridge he suddenly became unnaturally merry and chattered incessantly to the soldiers, who could not understand him. Ramballe refused food and resting his head on his elbow lay silent beside the campfire, looking at the Russian soldiers with red and vacant eyes. Occasionally he emitted a long-drawn groan and then again became silent. Morel, pointing to his shoulders, tried to impress on the soldiers the fact that Ramballe was an officer and ought to be warmed. A Russian officer who had come up to the fire sent to ask his colonel whether he would not take a French officer into his hut to warm him, and when the messenger returned and said that the colonel wished the officer to be brought to him, Ramballe was told to go. He rose and tried to walk, but staggered and would have fallen had not a soldier standing by held him up. вҖңYou wonвҖҷt do it again, eh?вҖқ said one of the soldiers, winking and turning mockingly to Ramballe. вҖңOh, you fool! Why talk rubbish, lout that you areвҒ вҖ”a real peasant!вҖқ came rebukes from all sides addressed to the jesting soldier. They surrounded Ramballe, lifted him on the crossed arms of two soldiers, and carried him to the hut. Ramballe put his arms around their necks while they carried him and began wailing plaintively: вҖңOh, you fine fellows, my kind, kind friends! These are men! Oh, my brave, kind friends,вҖқ and he leaned his head against the shoulder of one of the men like a child. Meanwhile Morel was sitting in the best place by the fire, surrounded by the soldiers. Morel, a short sturdy Frenchman with inflamed and streaming eyes, was wearing a womanвҖҷs cloak and had a shawl tied woman fashion round his head over his cap. He was evidently tipsy, and was singing a French song in a hoarse broken voice, with an arm thrown round the nearest soldier. The soldiers simply held their sides as they watched him. вҖңNow then, now then, teach us how it goes! IвҖҷll soon pick it up. How is it?вҖқ said the manвҒ вҖ”a singer and a wagвҒ вҖ”whom Morel was embracing. вҖңVive Henri Quatre! Vive ce roi valiant!вҖқ sang Morel, winking. вҖңCe diable Г  quatreвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ134 вҖңVivarika! Vif-seruvaru! Sedyablyaka!вҖқ repeated the soldier, flourishing his arm and really catching the tune. вҖңBravo! Ha, ha, ha!вҖқ rose their rough, joyous laughter from all sides. Morel, wrinkling up his face, laughed too. вҖңWell, go on, go on!вҖқ вҖңQui eut le triple talent, De boire, de battre, Et dвҖҷГӘtre un vert galant.вҖқ135 вҖңIt goes smoothly, too. Well, now, ZaletГЎev!вҖқ вҖңKeвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ ZaletГЎev, brought out with effort: вҖңke-e-e-e,вҖқ he drawled, laboriously pursing his lips, вҖңle-trip-tala-de-bu-de-ba, e de-tra-va-ga-laвҖқ he sang. вҖңFine! Just like the Frenchie! Oh, ho ho! Do you want some more to eat?вҖқ вҖңGive him some porridge: it takes a long time to get filled up after starving.вҖқ They gave him some more porridge and Morel with a laugh set to work on his third bowl. All the young soldiers smiled gaily as they watched him. The older men, who thought it undignified to amuse themselves with such nonsense, continued to lie at the opposite side of the fire, but one would occasionally raise himself on an elbow and glance at Morel with a smile. вҖңThey are men too,вҖқ said one of them as he wrapped himself up in his coat. вҖңEven wormwood grows on its own root.вҖқ вҖңO Lord, O Lord! How starry it is! Tremendous! That means a hard frost.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ They all grew silent. The stars, as if knowing that no one was looking at them, began to disport themselves in the dark sky: now flaring up, now vanishing, now trembling, they were busy whispering something gladsome and mysterious to one another. The Character of KutГәzov and Reflections on the Unsatisfactory Results of the Battles at KrГЎsnoe, by BogdГЎnovich. вҶ© вҖңLong live Henry the Fourth, that valiant king! That rowdy devil.вҖқ вҶ© Who had a triple talent For drinking, for fighting, And for being a gallant old boyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҶ© Without faith or law. вҶ© вҖңIвҖҷm your man.вҖқ вҶ© I I Seven years had passed. The storm-tossed sea of European history had subsided within its shores and seemed to have become calm. But the mysterious forces that move humanity (mysterious because the laws of their motion are unknown to us) continued to operate. Though the surface of the sea of history seemed motionless, the movement of humanity went on as unceasingly as the flow of time. Various groups of people formed and dissolved, the coming formation and dissolution of kingdoms and displacement of peoples was in course of preparation. The sea of history was not driven spasmodically from shore to shore as previously. It was seething in its depths. Historic figures were not borne by the waves from one shore to another as before. They now seemed to rotate on one spot. The historical figures at the head of armies, who formerly reflected the movement of the masses by ordering wars, campaigns, and battles, now reflected the restless movement by political and diplomatic combinations, laws, and treaties. The historians call this activity of the historical figures вҖңthe reaction.вҖқ In dealing with this period they sternly condemn the historical personages who, in their opinion, caused what they describe as the reaction. All the well-known people of that period, from Alexander and Napoleon to Madame de StaГ«l, Photius, Schelling, Fichte, Chateaubriand, and the rest, pass before their stern judgment seat and are acquitted or condemned according to whether they conduced to progress or to reaction. According to their accounts a reaction took place at that time in Russia also, and the chief culprit was Alexander I, the same man who according to them was the chief cause of the liberal movement at the commencement of his reign, being the savior of Russia. There is no one in Russian literature now, from schoolboy essayist to learned historian, who does not throw his little stone at Alexander for things he did wrong at this period of his reign. вҖңHe ought to have acted in this way and in that way. In this case he did well and in that case badly. He behaved admirably at the beginning of his reign and during 1812, but acted badly by giving a constitution to Poland, forming the Holy Alliance, entrusting power to ArakchГ©ev, favoring GolГӯtsyn and mysticism, and afterwards ShishkГіv and Photius. He also acted badly by concerning himself with the active army and disbanding the SemГ«nov regiment.вҖқ It would take a dozen pages to enumerate all the reproaches the historians address to him, based on their knowledge of what is good for humanity. What do these reproaches mean? Do not the very actions for which the historians praise Alexander I (the liberal attempts at the beginning of his reign, his struggle with Napoleon, the firmness he displayed in 1812 and the campaign of 1813) flow from the same sourcesвҒ вҖ”the circumstances of his birth, education, and lifeвҒ вҖ”that made his personality what it was and from which the actions for which they blame him (the Holy Alliance, the restoration of Poland, and the reaction of 1820 and later) also flowed? In what does the substance of those reproaches lie? It lies in the fact that an historic character like Alexander I, standing on the highest possible pinnacle of human power with the blinding light of history focused upon him; a character exposed to those strongest of all influences: the intrigues, flattery, and self-deception inseparable from power; a character who at every moment of his life felt a responsibility for all that was happening in Europe; and not a fictitious but a live character who like every man had his personal habits, passions, and impulses toward goodness, beauty, and truthвҒ вҖ”that this characterвҒ вҖ”though not lacking in virtue (the historians do not accuse him of that)вҒ вҖ”had not the same conception of the welfare of humanity fifty years ago as a present-day professor who from his youth upwards has been occupied with learning: that is, with books and lectures and with taking notes from them. But even if we assume that fifty years ago Alexander I was mistaken in his view of what was good for the people, we must inevitably assume that the historian who judges Alexander will also after the lapse of some time turn out to be mistaken in his view of what is good for humanity. This assumption is all the more natural and inevitable because, watching the movement of history, we see that every year and with each new writer, opinion as to what is good for mankind changes; so that what once seemed good, ten years later seems bad, and vice versa. And what is more, we find at one and the same time quite contradictory views as to what is bad and what is good in history: some people regard giving a constitution to Poland and forming the Holy Alliance as praiseworthy in Alexander, while others regard it as blameworthy. The activity of Alexander or of Napoleon cannot be called useful or harmful, for it is impossible to say for what it was useful or harmful. If that activity displeases somebody, this is only because it does not agree with his limited understanding of what is good. Whether the preservation of my fatherвҖҷs house in Moscow, or the glory of the Russian arms, or the prosperity of the Petersburg and other universities, or the freedom of Poland or the greatness of Russia, or the balance of power in Europe, or a certain kind of European culture called вҖңprogressвҖқ appear to me to be good or bad, I must admit that besides these things the action of every historic character has other more general purposes inaccessible to me. But let us assume that what is called science can harmonize all contradictions and possesses an unchanging standard of good and bad by which to try historic characters and events; let us say that Alexander could have done everything differently; let us say that with guidance from those who blame him and who profess to know the ultimate aim of the movement of humanity, he might have arranged matters according to the program his present accusers would have given himвҒ вҖ”of nationality, freedom, equality, and progress (these, I think, cover the ground). Let us assume that this program was possible and had then been formulated, and that Alexander had acted on it. What would then have become of the activity of all those who opposed the tendency that then prevailed in the governmentвҒ вҖ”an activity that in the opinion of the historians was good and beneficent? Their activity would not have existed: there would have been no life, there would have been nothing. If we admit that human life can be ruled by reason, the possibility of life is destroyed. X X NatГЎsha had married in the early spring of 1813, and in 1820 already had three daughters besides a son for whom she had longed and whom she was now nursing. She had grown stouter and broader, so that it was difficult to recognize in this robust, motherly woman the slim, lively NatГЎsha of former days.
