Her features were more defined and had a calm, soft, and serene expression. In her face there was none of the ever-glowing animation that had formerly burned there and constituted its charm. Now her face and body were often all that one saw, and her soul was not visible at all. All that struck the eye was a strong, handsome, and fertile woman. The old fire very rarely kindled in her face now. That happened only when, as was the case that day, her husband returned home, or a sick child was convalescent, or when she and Countess MГЎrya spoke of Prince AndrГ©y (she never mentioned him to her husband, who she imagined was jealous of Prince AndrГ©yвҖҷs memory), or on the rare occasions when something happened to induce her to sing, a practice she had quite abandoned since her marriage. At the rare moments when the old fire did kindle in her handsome, fully developed body she was even more attractive than in former days. Since their marriage NatГЎsha and her husband had lived in Moscow, in Petersburg, on their estate near Moscow, or with her mother, that is to say, in NikolГЎyвҖҷs house. The young Countess BezГәkhova was not often seen in society, and those who met her there were not pleased with her and found her neither attractive nor amiable. Not that NatГЎsha liked solitudeвҒ вҖ”she did not know whether she liked it or not, she even thought that she did notвҒ вҖ”but with her pregnancies, her confinements, the nursing of her children, and sharing every moment of her husbandвҖҷs life, she had demands on her time which could be satisfied only by renouncing society. All who had known NatГЎsha before her marriage wondered at the change in her as at something extraordinary. Only the old countess with her maternal instinct had realized that all NatГЎshaвҖҷs outbursts had been due to her need of children and a husbandвҒ вҖ”as she herself had once exclaimed at OtrГЎdnoe not so much in fun as in earnestвҒ вҖ”and her mother was now surprised at the surprise expressed by those who had never understood NatГЎsha, and she kept saying that she had always known that NatГЎsha would make an exemplary wife and mother. вҖңOnly she lets her love of her husband and children overflow all bounds,вҖқ said the countess, вҖңso that it even becomes absurd.вҖқ NatГЎsha did not follow the golden rule advocated by clever folk, especially by the French, which says that a girl should not let herself go when she marries, should not neglect her accomplishments, should be even more careful of her appearance than when she was unmarried, and should fascinate her husband as much as she did before he became her husband. NatГЎsha on the contrary had at once abandoned all her witchery, of which her singing had been an unusually powerful part. She gave it up just because it was so powerfully seductive. She took no pains with her manners or with delicacy of speech, or with her toilet, or to show herself to her husband in her most becoming attitudes, or to avoid inconveniencing him by being too exacting. She acted in contradiction to all those rules. She felt that the allurements instinct had formerly taught her to use would now be merely ridiculous in the eyes of her husband, to whom she had from the first moment given herself up entirelyвҒ вҖ”that is, with her whole soul, leaving no corner of it hidden from him. She felt that her unity with her husband was not maintained by the poetic feelings that had attracted him to her, but by something elseвҒ вҖ”indefinite but firm as the bond between her own body and soul. To fluff out her curls, put on fashionable dresses, and sing romantic songs to fascinate her husband would have seemed as strange as to adorn herself to attract herself. To adorn herself for others might perhaps have been agreeableвҒ вҖ”she did not knowвҒ вҖ”but she had no time at all for it. The chief reason for devoting no time either to singing, to dress, or to choosing her words was that she really had no time to spare for these things. We know that man has the faculty of becoming completely absorbed in a subject however trivial it may be, and that there is no subject so trivial that it will not grow to infinite proportions if oneвҖҷs entire attention is devoted to it. The subject which wholly engrossed NatГЎshaвҖҷs attention was her family: that is, her husband whom she had to keep so that he should belong entirely to her and to the home, and the children whom she had to bear, bring into the world, nurse, and bring up. And the deeper she penetrated, not with her mind only but with her whole soul, her whole being, into the subject that absorbed her, the larger did that subject grow and the weaker and more inadequate did her powers appear, so that she concentrated them wholly on that one thing and yet was unable to accomplish all that she considered necessary. There were then as now conversations and discussions about womenвҖҷs rights, the relations of husband and wife and their freedom and rights, though these themes were not yet termed questions as they are now; but these topics were not merely uninteresting to NatГЎsha, she positively did not understand them. These questions, then as now, existed only for those who see nothing in marriage but the pleasure married people get from one another, that is, only the beginnings of marriage and not its whole significance, which lies in the family. Discussions and questions of that kind, which are like the question of how to get the greatest gratification from oneвҖҷs dinner, did not then and do not now exist for those for whom the purpose of a dinner is the nourishment it affords; and the purpose of marriage is the family. If the purpose of dinner is to nourish the body, a man who eats two dinners at once may perhaps get more enjoyment but will not attain his purpose, for his stomach will not digest the two dinners. If the purpose of marriage is the family, the person who wishes to have many wives or husbands may perhaps obtain much pleasure, but in that case will not have a family. If the purpose of food is nourishment and the purpose of marriage is the family, the whole question resolves itself into not eating more than one can digest, and not having more wives or husbands than are needed for the familyвҒ вҖ”that is, one wife or one husband. NatГЎsha needed a husband. A husband was given her and he gave her a family. And she not only saw no need of any other or better husband, but as all the powers of her soul were intent on serving that husband and family, she could not imagine and saw no interest in imagining how it would be if things were different. NatГЎsha did not care for society in general, but prized the more the society of her relativesвҒ вҖ”Countess MГЎrya, and her brother, her mother, and SГіnya. She valued the company of those to whom she could come striding disheveled from the nursery in her dressing gown, and with joyful face show a yellow instead of a green stain on babyвҖҷs napkin, and from whom she could hear reassuring words to the effect that baby was much better. To such an extent had NatГЎsha let herself go that the way she dressed and did her hair, her ill-chosen words, and her jealousyвҒ вҖ”she was jealous of SГіnya, of the governess, and of every woman, pretty or plainвҒ вҖ”were habitual subjects of jest to those about her. The general opinion was that Pierre was under his wifeвҖҷs thumb, which was really true. From the very first days of their married life NatГЎsha had announced her demands. Pierre was greatly surprised by his wifeвҖҷs view, to him a perfectly novel one, that every moment of his life belonged to her and to the family. His wifeвҖҷs demands astonished him, but they also flattered him, and he submitted to them. PierreвҖҷs subjection consisted in the fact that he not only dared not flirt with, but dared not even speak smilingly to, any other woman; did not dare dine at the Club as a pastime, did not dare spend money on a whim, and did not dare absent himself for any length of time, except on businessвҒ вҖ”in which his wife included his intellectual pursuits, which she did not in the least understand but to which she attributed great importance. To make up for this, at home Pierre had the right to regulate his life and that of the whole family exactly as he chose. At home NatГЎsha placed herself in the position of a slave to her husband, and the whole household went on tiptoe when he was occupiedвҒ вҖ”that is, was reading or writing in his study. Pierre had but to show a partiality for anything to get just what he liked done always. He had only to express a wish and NatГЎsha would jump up and run to fulfill it. The entire household was governed according to PierreвҖҷs supposed orders, that is, by his wishes which NatГЎsha tried to guess. Their way of life and place of residence, their acquaintances and ties, NatГЎshaвҖҷs occupations, the childrenвҖҷs upbringing, were all selected not merely with regard to PierreвҖҷs expressed wishes, but to what NatГЎsha from the thoughts he expressed in conversation supposed his wishes to be. And she deduced the essentials of his wishes quite correctly, and having once arrived at them clung to them tenaciously. When Pierre himself wanted to change his mind she would fight him with his own weapons. Thus in a time of trouble ever memorable to him after the birth of their first child who was delicate, when they had to change the wet nurse three times and NatГЎsha fell ill from despair, Pierre one day told her of RousseauвҖҷs view, with which he quite agreed, that to have a wet nurse is unnatural and harmful. When her next baby was born, despite the opposition of her mother, the doctors, and even of her husband himselfвҒ вҖ”who were all vigorously opposed to her nursing her baby herself, a thing then unheard of and considered injuriousвҒ вҖ”she insisted on having her own way, and after that nursed all her babies herself. It very often happened that in a moment of irritation husband and wife would have a dispute, but long afterwards Pierre to his surprise and delight would find in his wifeвҖҷs ideas and actions the very thought against which she had argued, but divested of everything superfluous that in the excitement of the dispute he had added when expressing his opinion. After seven years of marriage Pierre had the joyous and firm consciousness that he was not a bad man, and he felt this because he saw himself reflected in his wife. He felt the good and bad within himself inextricably mingled and overlapping. But only what was really good in him was reflected in his wife, all that was not quite good was rejected. And this was not the result of logical reasoning but was a direct and mysterious reflection. XI XI Two months previously when Pierre was already staying with the RostГіvs he had received a letter from Prince FГ«dor, asking him to come to Petersburg to confer on some important questions that were being discussed there by a society of which Pierre was one of the principal founders. On reading that letter (she always read her husbandвҖҷs letters) NatГЎsha herself suggested that he should go to Petersburg, though she would feel his absence very acutely. She attributed immense importance to all her husbandвҖҷs intellectual and abstract interests though she did not understand them, and she always dreaded being a hindrance to him in such matters. To PierreвҖҷs timid look of inquiry after reading the letter she replied by asking him to go, but to fix a definite date for his return. He was given four weeksвҖҷ leave of absence. Ever since that leave of absence had expired, more than a fortnight before, NatГЎsha had been in a constant state of alarm, depression, and irritability. DenГӯsov, now a general on the retired list and much dissatisfied with the present state of affairs, had arrived during that fortnight. He looked at NatГЎsha with sorrow and surprise as at a bad likeness of a person once dear. A dull, dejected look, random replies, and talk about the nursery was all he saw and heard from his former enchantress. NatГЎsha was sad and irritable all that time, especially when her mother, her brother, SГіnya, or Countess MГЎrya in their efforts to console her tried to excuse Pierre and suggested reasons for his delay in returning. вҖңItвҖҷs all nonsense, all rubbishвҒ вҖ”those discussions which lead to nothing and all those idiotic societies!вҖқ NatГЎsha declared of the very affairs in the immense importance of which she firmly believed. And she would go to the nursery to nurse PГ©tya, her only boy. No one else could tell her anything so comforting or so reasonable as this little three-month-old creature when he lay at her breast and she was conscious of the movement of his lips and the snuffling of his little nose. That creature said: вҖңYou are angry, you are jealous, you would like to pay him out, you are afraidвҒ вҖ”but here am I! And I am heвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ and that was unanswerable. It was more than true. During that fortnight of anxiety NatГЎsha resorted to the baby for comfort so often, and fussed over him so much, that she overfed him and he fell ill. She was terrified by his illness, and yet that was just what she needed. While attending to him she bore the anxiety about her husband more easily. She was nursing her boy when the sound of PierreвҖҷs sleigh was heard at the front door, and the old nurseвҒ вҖ”knowing how to please her mistressвҒ вҖ”entered the room inaudibly but hurriedly and with a beaming face. вҖңHas he come?вҖқ NatГЎsha asked quickly in a whisper, afraid to move lest she should rouse the dozing baby. вҖңHeвҖҷs come, maвҖҷam,вҖқ whispered the nurse. The blood rushed to NatГЎshaвҖҷs face and her feet involuntarily moved, but she could not jump up and run out. The baby again opened his eyes and looked at her. вҖңYouвҖҷre here?вҖқ he seemed to be saying, and again lazily smacked his lips. Cautiously withdrawing her breast, NatГЎsha rocked him a little, handed him to the nurse, and went with rapid steps toward the door. But at the door she stopped as if her conscience reproached her for having in her joy left the child too soon, and she glanced round. The nurse with raised elbows was lifting the infant over the rail of his cot. вҖңGo, maвҖҷam! DonвҖҷt worry, go!вҖқ she whispered, smiling, with the kind of familiarity that grows up between a nurse and her mistress. NatГЎsha ran with light footsteps to the anteroom. DenГӯsov, who had come out of the study into the dancing room with his pipe, now for the first time recognized the old NatГЎsha. A flood of brilliant, joyful light poured from her transfigured face. вҖңHeвҖҷs come!вҖқ she exclaimed as she ran past, and DenГӯsov felt that he too was delighted that Pierre, whom he did not much care for, had returned. On reaching the vestibule NatГЎsha saw a tall figure in a fur coat unwinding his scarf. вҖңItвҖҷs he! ItвҖҷs really he! He has come!вҖқ she said to herself, and rushing at him embraced him, pressed his head to her breast, and then pushed him back and gazed at his ruddy, happy face, covered with hoarfrost. вҖңYes, it is he, happy and contented.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Then all at once she remembered the tortures of suspense she had experienced for the last fortnight, and the joy that had lit up her face vanished; she frowned and overwhelmed Pierre with a torrent of reproaches and angry words. вҖңYes, itвҖҷs all very well for you. You are pleased, youвҖҷve had a good time.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But what about me? You might at least have shown consideration for the children. I am nursing and my milk was spoiled.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ PГ©tya was at deathвҖҷs door. But you were enjoying yourself. Yes, enjoyingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre knew he was not to blame, for he could not have come sooner; he knew this outburst was unseemly and would blow over in a minute or two; above all he knew that he himself was bright and happy. He wanted to smile but dared not even think of doing so. He made a piteous, frightened face and bent down. вҖңI could not, on my honor. But how is PГ©tya?вҖқ вҖңAll right now. Come along! I wonder youвҖҷre not ashamed! If only you could see what I was like without you, how I suffered!вҖқ вҖңYou are well?вҖқ вҖңCome, come!вҖқ she said, not letting go of his arm. And they went to their rooms. When NikolГЎy and his wife came to look for Pierre he was in the nursery holding his baby son, who was again awake, on his huge right palm and dandling him. A blissful bright smile was fixed on the babyвҖҷs broad face with its toothless open mouth. The storm was long since over and there was bright, joyous sunshine on NatГЎshaвҖҷs face as she gazed tenderly at her husband and child. вҖңAnd have you talked everything well over with Prince FГ«dor?вҖқ she asked. вҖңYes, capitally.вҖқ вҖңYou see, he holds it up.вҖқ (She meant the babyвҖҷs head.) вҖңBut how he did frighten meвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ YouвҖҷve seen the princess? Is it true sheвҖҷs in love with thatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYes, just fancyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ At that moment NikolГЎy and Countess MГЎrya came in. Pierre with the baby on his hand stooped, kissed them, and replied to their inquiries. But in spite of much that was interesting and had to be discussed, the baby with the little cap on its unsteady head evidently absorbed all his attention. вҖңHow sweet!вҖқ said Countess MГЎrya, looking at and playing with the baby. вҖңNow, Nicolas,вҖқ she added, turning to her husband, вҖңI canвҖҷt understand how it is you donвҖҷt see the charm of these delicious marvels.вҖқ вҖңI donвҖҷt and canвҖҷt,вҖқ replied NikolГЎy, looking coldly at the baby. вҖңA lump of flesh. Come along, Pierre!вҖқ вҖңAnd yet heвҖҷs such an affectionate father,вҖқ said Countess MГЎrya, vindicating her husband, вҖңbut only after they are a year old or soвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңNow, Pierre nurses them splendidly,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңHe says his hand is just made for a babyвҖҷs seat. Just look!вҖқ вҖңOnly not for thisвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Pierre suddenly exclaimed with a laugh, and shifting the baby he gave him to the nurse. XII XII As in every large household, there were at Bald Hills several perfectly distinct worlds which merged into one harmonious whole, though each retained its own peculiarities and made concessions to the others. Every event, joyful or sad, that took place in that house was important to all these worlds, but each had its own special reasons to rejoice or grieve over that occurrence independently of the others. For instance, PierreвҖҷs return was a joyful and important event and they all felt it to be so. The servantsвҒ вҖ”the most reliable judges of their masters because they judge not by their conversation or expressions of feeling but by their acts and way of lifeвҒ вҖ”were glad of PierreвҖҷs return because they knew that when he was there Count NikolГЎy would cease going every day to attend to the estate, and would be in better spirits and temper, and also because they would all receive handsome presents for the holidays. The children and their governesses were glad of PierreвҖҷs return because no one else drew them into the social life of the household as he did. He alone could play on the clavichord that Г©cossaise (his only piece) to which, as he said, all possible dances could be danced, and they felt sure he had brought presents for them all. NikГіlenka BolkГіnski, now a slim lad of fifteen, delicate and intelligent, with curly light-brown hair and beautiful eyes, was delighted because Uncle Pierre as he called him was the object of his rapturous and passionate affection. No one had instilled into him this love for Pierre whom he saw only occasionally. Countess MГЎrya who had brought him up had done her utmost to make him love her husband as she loved him, and little NikГіlenka did love his uncle, but loved him with just a shade of contempt. Pierre, however, he adored. He did not want to be an hussar or a Knight of St.В George like his uncle NikolГЎy; he wanted to be learned, wise, and kind like Pierre. In PierreвҖҷs presence his face always shone with pleasure and he flushed and was breathless when Pierre spoke to him. He did not miss a single word he uttered, and would afterwards, with Dessalles or by himself, recall and reconsider the meaning of everything Pierre had said. PierreвҖҷs past life and his unhappiness prior to 1812 (of which young NikГіlenka had formed a vague poetic picture from some words he had overheard), his adventures in Moscow, his captivity, PlatГіn KaratГЎev (of whom he had heard from Pierre), his love for NatГЎsha (of whom the lad was also particularly fond), and especially PierreвҖҷs friendship with the father whom NikГіlenka could not rememberвҒ вҖ”all this made Pierre in his eyes a hero and a saint. From broken remarks about NatГЎsha and his father, from the emotion with which Pierre spoke of that dead father, and from the careful, reverent tenderness with which NatГЎsha spoke of him, the boy, who was only just beginning to guess what love is, derived the notion that his father had loved NatГЎsha and when dying had left her to his friend. But the father whom the boy did not remember appeared to him a divinity who could not be pictured, and of whom he never thought without a swelling heart and tears of sadness and rapture. So the boy also was happy that Pierre had arrived. The guests welcomed Pierre because he always helped to enliven and unite any company he was in. The grownup members of the family, not to mention his wife, were pleased to have back a friend whose presence made life run more smoothly and peacefully. The old ladies were pleased with the presents he brought them, and especially that NatГЎsha would now be herself again. Pierre felt the different outlooks of these various worlds and made haste to satisfy all their expectations. Though the most absentminded and forgetful of men, Pierre, with the aid of a list his wife drew up, had now bought everything, not forgetting his mother- and brother-in-lawвҖҷs commissions, nor the dress material for a present to BelГіva, nor toys for his wifeвҖҷs nephews. In the early days of his marriage it had seemed strange to him that his wife should expect him not to forget to procure all the things he undertook to buy, and he had been taken aback by her serious annoyance when on his first trip he forgot everything. But in time he grew used to this demand. Knowing that NatГЎsha asked nothing for herself, and gave him commissions for others only when he himself had offered to undertake them, he now found an unexpected and childlike pleasure in this purchase of presents for everyone in the house, and never forgot anything. If he now incurred NatГЎshaвҖҷs censure it was only for buying too many and too expensive things. To her other defects (as most people thought them, but which to Pierre were qualities) of untidiness and neglect of herself, she now added stinginess. From the time that Pierre began life as a family man on a footing entailing heavy expenditure, he had noticed to his surprise that he spent only half as much as before, and that his affairsвҒ вҖ”which had been in disorder of late, chiefly because of his first wifeвҖҷs debtsвҒ вҖ”had begun to improve. Life was cheaper because it was circumscribed: that most expensive luxury, the kind of life that can be changed at any moment, was no longer his nor did he wish for it. He felt that his way of life had now been settled once for all till death and that to change it was not in his power, and so that way of life proved economical. With a merry, smiling face Pierre was sorting his purchases. вҖңWhat do you think of this?вҖқ said he, unrolling a piece of stuff like a shopman. NatГЎsha, who was sitting opposite to him with her eldest daughter on her lap, turned her sparkling eyes swiftly from her husband to the things he showed her. вҖңThatвҖҷs for BelГіva? Excellent!вҖқ She felt the quality of the material. вҖңIt was a ruble an arshin, I suppose?вҖқ Pierre told her the price. вҖңToo dear!вҖқ NatГЎsha remarked. вҖңHow pleased the children will be and Mamma too! Only you need not have bought me this,вҖқ she added, unable to suppress a smile as she gazed admiringly at a gold comb set with pearls, of a kind then just coming into fashion. вҖңAdГЁle tempted me: she kept on telling me to buy it,вҖқ returned Pierre. вҖңWhen am I to wear it?вҖқ and NatГЎsha stuck it in her coil of hair. вҖңWhen I take little MГЎshenka into society? Perhaps they will be fashionable again by then. Well, letвҖҷs go now.вҖқ And collecting the presents they went first to the nursery and then to the old countessвҖҷ rooms. The countess was sitting with her companion BelГіva, playing grand-patience as usual, when Pierre and NatГЎsha came into the drawing room with parcels under their arms. The countess was now over sixty, was quite gray, and wore a cap with a frill that surrounded her face. Her face had shriveled, her upper lip had sunk in, and her eyes were dim. After the deaths of her son and husband in such rapid succession, she felt herself a being accidentally forgotten in this world and left without aim or object for her existence. She ate, drank, slept, or kept awake, but did not live. Life gave her no new impressions. She wanted nothing from life but tranquillity, and that tranquillity only death could give her. But until death came she had to go on living, that is, to use her vital forces. A peculiarity one sees in very young children and very old people was particularly evident in her. Her life had no external aimsвҒ вҖ”only a need to exercise her various functions and inclinations was apparent. She had to eat, sleep, think, speak, weep, work, give vent to her anger, and so on, merely because she had a stomach, a brain, muscles, nerves, and a liver. She did these things not under any external impulse as people in the full vigor of life do, when behind the purpose for which they strive that of exercising their functions remains unnoticed. She talked only because she physically needed to exercise her tongue and lungs. She cried as a child does, because her nose had to be cleared, and so on. What for people in their full vigor is an aim was for her evidently merely a pretext. Thus in the morningвҒ вҖ”especially if she had eaten anything rich the day beforeвҒ вҖ”she felt a need of being angry and would choose as the handiest pretext BelГіvaвҖҷs deafness. She would begin to say something to her in a low tone from the other end of the room. вҖңIt seems a little warmer today, my dear,вҖқ she would murmur. And when BelГіva replied: вҖңOh yes, theyвҖҷve come,вҖқ she would mutter angrily: вҖңO Lord! How stupid and deaf she is!вҖқ Another pretext would be her snuff, which would seem too dry or too damp or not rubbed fine enough. After these fits of irritability her face would grow yellow, and her maids knew by infallible symptoms when BelГіva would again be deaf, the snuff damp, and the countessвҖҷ face yellow. Just as she needed to work off her spleen so she had sometimes to exercise her still-existing faculty of thinkingвҒ вҖ”and the pretext for that was a game of patience. When she needed to cry, the deceased count would be the pretext. When she wanted to be agitated, NikolГЎy and his health would be the pretext, and when she felt a need to speak spitefully, the pretext would be Countess MГЎrya. When her vocal organs needed exercise, which was usually toward seven oвҖҷclock when she had had an after-dinner rest in a darkened room, the pretext would be the retelling of the same stories over and over again to the same audience. The old ladyвҖҷs condition was understood by the whole household though no one ever spoke of it, and they all made every possible effort to satisfy her needs. Only by a rare glance exchanged with a sad smile between NikolГЎy, Pierre, NatГЎsha, and Countess MГЎrya was the common understanding of her condition expressed. But those glances expressed something more: they said that she had played her part in life, that what they now saw was not her whole self, that we must all become like her, and that they were glad to yield to her, to restrain themselves for this once precious being formerly as full of life as themselves, but now so much to be pitied. вҖңMemento mori,вҖқ said these glances. Only the really heartless, the stupid ones of that household, and the little children failed to understand this and avoided her. XIII XIII When Pierre and his wife entered the drawing room the countess was in one of her customary states in which she needed the mental exertion of playing patience, and soвҒ вҖ”though by force of habit she greeted him with the words she always used when Pierre or her son returned after an absence: вҖңHigh time, my dear, high time! We were all weary of waiting for you. Well, thank God!вҖқ and received her presents with another customary remark: вҖңItвҖҷs not the gift thatвҖҷs precious, my dear, but that you give it to me, an old womanвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқвҒ вҖ”yet it was evident that she was not pleased by PierreвҖҷs arrival at that moment when it diverted her attention from the unfinished game. She finished her game of patience and only then examined the presents. They consisted of a box for cards, of splendid workmanship, a bright-blue SГЁvres tea cup with shepherdesses depicted on it and with a lid, and a gold snuffbox with the countвҖҷs portrait on the lid which Pierre had had done by a miniaturist in Petersburg. The countess had long wished for such a box, but as she did not want to cry just then she glanced indifferently at the portrait and gave her attention chiefly to the box for cards. вҖңThank you, my dear, you have cheered me up,вҖқ said she as she always did. вҖңBut best of all you have brought yourself backвҒ вҖ”for I never saw anything like it, you ought to give your wife a scolding! What are we to do with her? She is like a mad woman when you are away. DoesnвҖҷt see anything, doesnвҖҷt remember anything,вҖқ she went on, repeating her usual phrases. вҖңLook, Anna TimofГ©evna,вҖқ she added to her companion, вҖңsee what a box for cards my son has brought us!вҖқ BelГіva admired the presents and was delighted with her dress material. Though Pierre, NatГЎsha, NikolГЎy, Countess MГЎrya, and DenГӯsov had much to talk about that they could not discuss before the old countessвҒ вҖ”not that anything was hidden from her, but because she had dropped so far behindhand in many things that had they begun to converse in her presence they would have had to answer inopportune questions and to repeat what they had already told her many times: that so-and-so was dead and so-and-so was married, which she would again be unable to rememberвҒ вҖ”yet they sat at tea round the samovar in the drawing room from habit, and Pierre answered the countessвҖҷ questions as to whether Prince VasГӯli had aged and whether Countess MГЎrya AlexГ©evna had sent greetings and still thought of them, and other matters that interested no one and to which she herself was indifferent. Conversation of this kind, interesting to no one yet unavoidable, continued all through teatime. All the grownup members of the family were assembled near the round tea table at which SГіnya presided beside the samovar. The children with their tutors and governesses had had tea and their voices were audible from the next room. At tea all sat in their accustomed places: NikolГЎy beside the stove at a small table where his tea was handed to him; MГӯlka, the old gray borzoi bitch (daughter of the first MГӯlka), with a quite gray face and large black eyes that seemed more prominent than ever, lay on the armchair beside him; DenГӯsov, whose curly hair, mustache, and whiskers had turned half gray, sat beside countess MГЎrya with his generalвҖҷs tunic unbuttoned; Pierre sat between his wife and the old countess. He spoke of what he knew might interest the old lady and that she could understand. He told her of external social events and of the people who had formed the circle of her contemporaries and had once been a real, living, and distinct group, but who were now for the most part scattered about the world and like herself were garnering the last ears of the harvests they had sown in earlier years. But to the old countess those contemporaries of hers seemed to be the only serious and real society. NatГЎsha saw by PierreвҖҷs animation that his visit had been interesting and that he had much to tell them but dare not say it before the old countess. DenГӯsov, not being a member of the family, did not understand PierreвҖҷs caution and being, as a malcontent, much interested in what was occurring in Petersburg, kept urging Pierre to tell them about what had happened in the SemГ«novsk regiment, then about ArakchГ©ev, and then about the Bible Society. Once or twice Pierre was carried away and began to speak of these things, but NikolГЎy and NatГЎsha always brought him back to the health of Prince IvГЎn and Countess MГЎrya AntГіnovna. вҖңWell, and all this idiocyвҒ вҖ”Gossner and TatГЎwinova?вҖқ DenГӯsov asked. вҖңIs that weally still going on?вҖқ вҖңGoing on?вҖқ Pierre exclaimed. вҖңWhy more than ever! The Bible Society is the whole government now!вҖқ вҖңWhat is that, mon cher ami?вҖқ asked the countess, who had finished her tea and evidently needed a pretext for being angry after her meal. вҖңWhat are you saying about the government? I donвҖҷt understand.вҖқ вҖңWell, you know, Maman,вҖқ NikolГЎy interposed, knowing how to translate things into his motherвҖҷs language, вҖңPrince Alexander NikolГЎevich GolГӯtsyn has founded a society and in consequence has great influence, they say.вҖқ вҖңArakchГ©ev and GolГӯtsyn,вҖқ incautiously remarked Pierre, вҖңare now the whole government! And what a government! They see treason everywhere and are afraid of everything.вҖқ вҖңWell, and how is Prince Alexander NikolГЎevich to blame? He is a most estimable man. I used to meet him at MГЎrya AntГіnovnaвҖҷs,вҖқ said the countess in an offended tone; and still more offended that they all remained silent, she went on: вҖңNowadays everyone finds fault. A Gospel Society! Well, and what harm is there in that?вҖқ and she rose (everybody else got up too) and with a severe expression sailed back to her table in the sitting room. The melancholy silence that followed was broken by the sounds of the childrenвҖҷs voices and laughter from the next room. Evidently some jolly excitement was going on there. вҖңFinished, finished!вҖқ little NatГЎshaвҖҷs gleeful yell rose above them all. Pierre exchanged glances with Countess MГЎrya and NikolГЎy (NatГЎsha he never lost sight of) and smiled happily. вҖңThatвҖҷs delightful music!вҖқ said he. вҖңIt means that Anna MakГЎrovna has finished her stocking,вҖқ said Countess MГЎrya. вҖңOh, IвҖҷll go and see,вҖқ said Pierre, jumping up. вҖңYou know,вҖқ he added, stopping at the door, вҖңwhy IвҖҷm especially fond of that music? It is always the first thing that tells me all is well. When I was driving here today, the nearer I got to the house the more anxious I grew. As I entered the anteroom I heard AndrГәshaвҖҷs peals of laughter and that meant that all was well.вҖқ вҖңI know! I know that feeling,вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңBut I mustnвҖҷt go thereвҒ вҖ”those stockings are to be a surprise for me.вҖқ Pierre went to the children, and the shouting and laughter grew still louder. вҖңCome, Anna MakГЎrovna,вҖқ PierreвҖҷs voice was heard saying, вҖңcome here into the middle of the room and at the word of command, вҖҳOne, two,вҖҷ and when I say вҖҳthreeвҖҷвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You stand here, and you in my armsвҒ вҖ”well now! One, two!вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ said Pierre, and a silence followed: вҖңthree!вҖқ and a rapturously breathless cry of childrenвҖҷs voices filled the room. вҖңTwo, two!вҖқ they shouted. This meant two stockings, which by a secret process known only to herself Anna MakГЎrovna used to knit at the same time on the same needles, and which, when they were ready, she always triumphantly drew, one out of the other, in the childrenвҖҷs presence. XIV XIV Soon after this the children came in to say good night. They kissed everyone, the tutors and governesses made their bows, and they went out. Only young NikГіlenka and his tutor remained. Dessalles whispered to the boy to come downstairs. вҖңNo, Monsieur Dessalles, I will ask my aunt to let me stay,вҖқ replied NikГіlenka BolkГіnski also in a whisper. вҖңMa tante, please let me stay,вҖқ said he, going up to his aunt. His face expressed entreaty, agitation, and ecstasy. Countess MГЎrya glanced at him and turned to Pierre. вҖңWhen you are here he canвҖҷt tear himself away,вҖқ she said. вҖңI will bring him to you directly, Monsieur Dessalles. Good night!вҖқ said Pierre, giving his hand to the Swiss tutor, and he turned to NikГіlenka with a smile. вҖңYou and I havenвҖҷt seen anything of one another yet.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ How like he is growing, MГЎrya!вҖқ he added, addressing Countess MГЎrya. вҖңLike my father?вҖқ asked the boy, flushing crimson and looking up at Pierre with bright, ecstatic eyes. Pierre nodded, and went on with what he had been saying when the children had interrupted. Countess MГЎrya sat down doing woolwork; NatГЎsha did not take her eyes off her husband. NikolГЎy and DenГӯsov rose, asked for their pipes, smoked, went to fetch more tea from SГіnyaвҒ вҖ”who sat weary but resolute at the samovarвҒ вҖ”and questioned Pierre. The curly-headed, delicate boy sat with shining eyes unnoticed in a corner, starting every now and then and muttering something to himself, and evidently experiencing a new and powerful emotion as he turned his curly head, with his thin neck exposed by his turndown collar, toward the place where Pierre sat. The conversation turned on the contemporary gossip about those in power, in which most people see the chief interest of home politics. DenГӯsov, dissatisfied with the government on account of his own disappointments in the service, heard with pleasure of the things done in Petersburg which seemed to him stupid, and made forcible and sharp comments on what Pierre told them. вҖңOne used to have to be a GermanвҒ вҖ”now one must dance with TatГЎwinova and Madame KwГјdener, and wead EckaвҖҷtshausen and the bwethwen. Oh, they should let that fine fellow Bonaparte looseвҒ вҖ”heвҖҷd knock all this nonsense out of them! Fancy giving the command of the SemГ«nov wegiment to a fellow like that SchwaвҖҷtz!вҖқ he cried. NikolГЎy, though free from DenГӯsovвҖҷs readiness to find fault with everything, also thought that discussion of the government was a very serious and weighty matter, and the fact that A had been appointed Minister of This and B Governor General of That, and that the Emperor had said so-and-so and this minister so-and-so, seemed to him very important. And so he thought it necessary to take an interest in these things and to question Pierre. The questions put by these two kept the conversation from changing its ordinary character of gossip about the higher government circles. But NatГЎsha, knowing all her husbandвҖҷs ways and ideas, saw that he had long been wishing but had been unable to divert the conversation to another channel and express his own deeply felt idea for the sake of which he had gone to Petersburg to consult with his new friend Prince FГ«dor, and she helped him by asking how his affairs with Prince FГ«dor had gone. вҖңWhat was it about?вҖқ asked NikolГЎy. вҖңAlways the same thing,вҖқ said Pierre, looking round at his listeners. вҖңEverybody sees that things are going so badly that they cannot be allowed to go on so and that it is the duty of all decent men to counteract it as far as they can.вҖқ вҖңWhat can decent men do?вҖқ NikolГЎy inquired, frowning slightly. вҖңWhat can be done?вҖқ вҖңWhy, thisвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңCome into my study,вҖқ said NikolГЎy. NatГЎsha, who had long expected to be fetched to nurse her baby, now heard the nurse calling her and went to the nursery. Countess MГЎrya followed her. The men went into the study and NikГіlenka BolkГіnski followed them unnoticed by his uncle and sat down at the writing table in a shady corner by the window. вҖңWell, what would you do?вҖқ asked DenГӯsov. вҖңAlways some fantastic schemes,вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңWhy this,вҖқ began Pierre, not sitting down but pacing the room, sometimes stopping short, gesticulating, and lisping: вҖңthe position in Petersburg is this: the Emperor does not look into anything. He has abandoned himself altogether to this mysticismвҖқ (Pierre could not tolerate mysticism in anyone now). вҖңHe seeks only for peace, and only these people sans foi ni loi136 can give it himвҒ вҖ”people who recklessly hack at and strangle everythingвҒ вҖ”MagnГӯtski, ArakchГ©ev, and tutti quanti.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You will agree that if you did not look after your estates yourself but only wanted a quiet life, the harsher your steward was the more readily your object might be attained,вҖқ he said to NikolГЎy. вҖңWell, what does that lead up to?вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңWell, everything is going to ruin! Robbery in the law courts, in the army nothing but flogging, drilling, and Military Settlements; the people are tortured, enlightenment is suppressed. All that is young and honest is crushed! Everyone sees that this cannot go on. Everything is strained to such a degree that it will certainly break,вҖқ said Pierre (as those who examine the actions of any government have always said since governments began). вҖңI told them just one thing in Petersburg.вҖқ вҖңTold whom?вҖқ вҖңWell, you know whom,вҖқ said Pierre, with a meaning glance from under his brows. вҖңPrince FГ«dor and all those. To encourage culture and philanthropy is all very well of course. The aim is excellent but in the present circumstances something else is needed.вҖқ At that moment NikolГЎy noticed the presence of his nephew. His face darkened and he went up to the boy. вҖңWhy are you here?вҖқ вҖңWhy? Let him be,вҖқ said Pierre, taking NikolГЎy by the arm and continuing. вҖңThat is not enough, I told them. Something else is needed. When you stand expecting the overstrained string to snap at any moment, when everyone is expecting the inevitable catastrophe, as many as possible must join hands as closely as they can to withstand the general calamity. Everything that is young and strong is being enticed away and depraved. One is lured by women, another by honors, a third by ambition or money, and they go over to that camp. No independent men, such as you or I, are left. What I say is widen the scope of our society, let the mot dвҖҷordre be not virtue alone but independence and action as well!вҖқ NikolГЎy, who had left his nephew, irritably pushed up an armchair, sat down in it, and listened to Pierre, coughing discontentedly and frowning more and more. вҖңBut action with what aim?вҖқ he cried. вҖңAnd what position will you adopt toward the government?вҖқ вҖңWhy, the position of assistants. The society need not be secret if the government allows it. Not merely is it not hostile to government, but it is a society of true conservativesвҒ вҖ”a society of gentlemen in the full meaning of that word. It is only to prevent some PugachГ«v or other from killing my children and yours, and ArakchГ©ev from sending me off to some Military Settlement. We join hands only for the public welfare and the general safety.вҖқ вҖңYes, but itвҖҷs a secret society and therefore a hostile and harmful one which can only cause harm.вҖқ вҖңWhy? Did the Tugendbund which saved EuropeвҖқ (they did not then venture to suggest that Russia had saved Europe) вҖңdo any harm? The Tugendbund is an alliance of virtue: it is love, mutual helpвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ it is what Christ preached on the Cross.вҖқ NatГЎsha, who had come in during the conversation, looked joyfully at her husband. It was not what he was saying that pleased herвҒ вҖ”that did not even interest her, for it seemed to her that was all extremely simple and that she had known it a long time (it seemed so to her because she knew that it sprang from PierreвҖҷs whole soul), but it was his animated and enthusiastic appearance that made her glad. The boy with the thin neck stretching out from the turndown collarвҒ вҖ”whom everyone had forgottenвҒ вҖ”gazed at Pierre with even greater and more rapturous joy. Every word of PierreвҖҷs burned into his heart, and with a nervous movement of his fingers he unconsciously broke the sealing wax and quill pens his hands came upon on his uncleвҖҷs table. вҖңIt is not at all what you suppose; but that is what the German Tugendbund was, and what I am proposing.вҖқ вҖңNo, my fwiend! The Tugendbund is all vewy well for the sausage eaters, but I donвҖҷt understand it and canвҖҷt even pwonounce it,вҖқ interposed DenГӯsov in a loud and resolute voice. вҖңI agwee that evewything here is wotten and howwible, but the Tugendbund I donвҖҷt understand. If weвҖҷre not satisfied, let us have a bunt of our own. ThatвҖҷs all wight. Je suis votвҖҷe homme!вҖқ137 Pierre smiled, NatГЎsha began to laugh, but NikolГЎy knitted his brows still more and began proving to Pierre that there was no prospect of any great change and that all the danger he spoke of existed only in his imagination. Pierre maintained the contrary, and as his mental faculties were greater and more resourceful, NikolГЎy felt himself cornered. This made him still angrier, for he was fully convinced, not by reasoning but by something within him stronger than reason, of the justice of his opinion. вҖңI will tell you this,вҖқ he said, rising and trying with nervously twitching fingers to prop up his pipe in a corner, but finally abandoning the attempt. вҖңI canвҖҷt prove it to you. You say that everything here is rotten and that an overthrow is coming: I donвҖҷt see it. But you also say that our oath of allegiance is a conditional matter, and to that I reply: вҖҳYou are my best friend, as you know, but if you formed a secret society and began working against the governmentвҒ вҖ”be it what it mayвҒ вҖ”I know it is my duty to obey the government. And if ArakchГ©ev ordered me to lead a squadron against you and cut you down, I should not hesitate an instant, but should do it.вҖҷ And you may argue about that as you like!вҖқ An awkward silence followed these words. NatГЎsha was the first to speak, defending her husband and attacking her brother. Her defense was weak and inapt but she attained her object. The conversation was resumed, and no longer in the unpleasantly hostile tone of NikolГЎyвҖҷs last remark. When they all got up to go in to supper, NikГіlenka BolkГіnski went up to Pierre, pale and with shining, radiant eyes. вҖңUncle Pierre, youвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ noвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ If Papa were aliveвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ would he agree with you?вҖқ he asked. And Pierre suddenly realized what a special, independent, complex, and powerful process of thought and feeling must have been going on in this boy during that conversation, and remembering all he had said he regretted that the lad should have heard him. He had, however, to give him an answer. вҖңYes, I think so,вҖқ he said reluctantly, and left the study. The lad looked down and seemed now for the first time to notice what he had done to the things on the table. He flushed and went up to NikolГЎy. вҖңUncle, forgive me, I did thatвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ unintentionally,вҖқ he said, pointing to the broken sealing wax and pens. NikolГЎy started angrily. вҖңAll right, all right,вҖқ he said, throwing the bits under the table. And evidently suppressing his vexation with difficulty, he turned away from the boy. вҖңYou ought not to have been here at all,вҖқ he said. XV XV The conversation at supper was not about politics or societies, but turned on the subject NikolГЎy liked bestвҒ вҖ”recollections of 1812. DenГӯsov started these and Pierre was particularly agreeable and amusing about them. The family separated on the most friendly terms. After supper NikolГЎy, having undressed in his study and given instructions to the steward who had been waiting for him, went to the bedroom in his dressing gown, where he found his wife still at her table, writing. вҖңWhat are you writing, MГЎrya?вҖқ NikolГЎy asked. Countess MГЎrya blushed. She was afraid that what she was writing would not be understood or approved by her husband. She had wanted to conceal what she was writing from him, but at the same time was glad he had surprised her at it and that she would now have to tell him. вҖңA diary, Nicolas,вҖқ she replied, handing him a blue exercise book filled with her firm, bold writing. вҖңA diary?вҖқ NikolГЎy repeated with a shade of irony, and he took up the book. It was in French. December 4. Today when AndrГәsha (her eldest boy) woke up he did not wish to dress and Mademoiselle Louise sent for me. He was naughty and obstinate. I tried threats, but he only grew angrier. Then I took the matter in hand: I left him alone and began with nurseвҖҷs help to get the other children up, telling him that I did not love him. For a long time he was silent, as if astonished, then he jumped out of bed, ran to me in his shirt, and sobbed so that I could not calm him for a long time. It was plain that what troubled him most was that he had grieved me. Afterwards in the evening when I gave him his ticket, he again began crying piteously and kissing me. One can do anything with him by tenderness. вҖңWhat is a вҖҳticketвҖҷ?вҖқ NikolГЎy inquired. вҖңI have begun giving the elder ones marks every evening, showing how they have behaved.вҖқ NikolГЎy looked into the radiant eyes that were gazing at him, and continued to turn over the pages and read. In the diary was set down everything in the childrenвҖҷs lives that seemed noteworthy to their mother as showing their characters or suggesting general reflections on educational methods. They were for the most part quite insignificant trifles, but did not seem so to the mother or to the father either, now that he read this diary about his children for the first time. Under the date вҖң5вҖқ was entered: MГӯtya was naughty at table. Papa said he was to have no pudding. He had none, but looked so unhappily and greedily at the others while they were eating! I think that punishment by depriving children of sweets only develops their greediness. Must tell Nicolas this. NikolГЎy put down the book and looked at his wife. The radiant eyes gazed at him questioningly: would he approve or disapprove of her diary? There could be no doubt not only of his approval but also of his admiration for his wife. Perhaps it need not be done so pedantically, thought NikolГЎy, or even done at all, but this untiring, continual spiritual effort of which the sole aim was the childrenвҖҷs moral welfare delighted him. Had NikolГЎy been able to analyze his feelings he would have found that his steady, tender, and proud love of his wife rested on his feeling of wonder at her spirituality and at the lofty moral world, almost beyond his reach, in which she had her being. He was proud of her intelligence and goodness, recognized his own insignificance beside her in the spiritual world, and rejoiced all the more that she with such a soul not only belonged to him but was part of himself. вҖңI quite, quite approve, my dearest!вҖқ said he with a significant look, and after a short pause he added: вҖңAnd I behaved badly today. You werenвҖҷt in the study. We began disputingвҒ вҖ”Pierre and IвҒ вҖ”and I lost my temper. But he is impossible: such a child! I donвҖҷt know what would become of him if NatГЎsha didnвҖҷt keep him in hand.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Have you any idea why he went to Petersburg? They have formedвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYes, I know,вҖқ said Countess MГЎrya. вҖңNatГЎsha told me.вҖқ вҖңWell, then, you know,вҖқ NikolГЎy went on, growing hot at the mere recollection of their discussion, вҖңhe wanted to convince me that it is every honest manвҖҷs duty to go against the government, and that the oath of allegiance and dutyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I am sorry you werenвҖҷt there. They all fell on meвҒ вҖ”DenГӯsov and NatГЎshaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ NatГЎsha is absurd. How she rules over him! And yet there need only be a discussion and she has no words of her own but only repeats his sayingsвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ added NikolГЎy, yielding to that irresistible inclination which tempts us to judge those nearest and dearest to us. He forgot that what he was saying about NatГЎsha could have been applied word for word to himself in relation to his wife. вҖңYes, I have noticed that,вҖқ said Countess MГЎrya. вҖңWhen I told him that duty and the oath were above everything, he started proving goodness knows what! A pity you were not thereвҒ вҖ”what would you have said?вҖқ вҖңAs I see it you were quite right, and I told NatГЎsha so. Pierre says everybody is suffering, tortured, and being corrupted, and that it is our duty to help our neighbor. Of course he is right there,вҖқ said Countess MГЎrya, вҖңbut he forgets that we have other duties nearer to us, duties indicated to us by God Himself, and that though we might expose ourselves to risks we must not risk our children.вҖқ вҖңYes, thatвҖҷs it! ThatвҖҷs just what I said to him,вҖқ put in NikolГЎy, who fancied he really had said it. вҖңBut they insisted on their own view: love of oneвҖҷs neighbor and ChristianityвҒ вҖ”and all this in the presence of NikГіlenka, who had gone into my study and broke all my things.вҖқ вҖңAh, Nicolas, do you know I am often troubled about NikГіlenka,вҖқ said Countess MГЎrya. вҖңHe is such an exceptional boy. I am afraid I neglect him in favor of my own: we all have children and relations while he has no one. He is constantly alone with his thoughts.вҖқ вҖңWell, I donвҖҷt think you need reproach yourself on his account. All that the fondest mother could do for her son you have done and are doing for him, and of course I am glad of it. He is a fine lad, a fine lad! This evening he listened to Pierre in a sort of trance, and fancyвҒ вҖ”as we were going in to supper I looked and he had broken everything on my table to bits, and he told me of it himself at once! I never knew him to tell an untruth. A fine lad, a fine lad!вҖқ repeated NikolГЎy, who at heart was not fond of NikГіlenka but was always anxious to recognize that he was a fine lad. вҖңStill, I am not the same as his own mother,вҖқ said Countess MГЎrya. вҖңI feel I am not the same and it troubles me. A wonderful boy, but I am dreadfully afraid for him. It would be good for him to have companions.вҖқ вҖңWell it wonвҖҷt be for long. Next summer IвҖҷll take him to Petersburg,вҖқ said NikolГЎy. вҖңYes, Pierre always was a dreamer and always will be,вҖқ he continued, returning to the talk in the study which had evidently disturbed him. вҖңWell, what business is it of mine what goes on thereвҒ вҖ”whether ArakchГ©ev is bad, and all that? What business was it of mine when I married and was so deep in debt that I was threatened with prison, and had a mother who could not see or understand it? And then there are you and the children and our affairs. Is it for my own pleasure that I am at the farm or in the office from morning to night? No, but I know I must work to comfort my mother, to repay you, and not to leave the children such beggars as I was.вҖқ Countess MГЎrya wanted to tell him that man does not live by bread alone and that he attached too much importance to these matters. But she knew she must not say this and that it would be useless to do so. She only took his hand and kissed it. He took this as a sign of approval and a confirmation of his thoughts, and after a few minutesвҖҷ reflection continued to think aloud. вҖңYou know, MГЎrya, today IlyГЎ MitrofГЎnychвҖқ (this was his overseer) вҖңcame back from the TambГіv estate and told me they are already offering eighty thousand rubles for the forest.вҖқ And with an eager face NikolГЎy began to speak of the possibility of repurchasing OtrГЎdnoe before long, and added: вҖңAnother ten years of life and I shall leave the childrenвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ in an excellent position.вҖқ Countess MГЎrya listened to her husband and understood all that he told her. She knew that when he thought aloud in this way he would sometimes ask her what he had been saying, and be vexed if he noticed that she had been thinking about something else. But she had to force herself to attend, for what he was saying did not interest her at all. She looked at him and did not think, but felt, about something different. She felt a submissive tender love for this man who would never understand all that she understood, and this seemed to make her love for him still stronger and added a touch of passionate tenderness. Besides this feeling which absorbed her altogether and hindered her from following the details of her husbandвҖҷs plans, thoughts that had no connection with what he was saying flitted through her mind. She thought of her nephew. Her husbandвҖҷs account of the boyвҖҷs agitation while Pierre was speaking struck her forcibly, and various traits of his gentle, sensitive character recurred to her mind; and while thinking of her nephew she thought also of her own children. She did not compare them with him, but compared her feeling for them with her feeling for him, and felt with regret that there was something lacking in her feeling for NikГіlenka. Sometimes it seemed to her that this difference arose from the difference in their ages, but she felt herself to blame toward him and promised in her heart to do better and to accomplish the impossibleвҒ вҖ”in this life to love her husband, her children, NikГіlenka, and all her neighbors, as Christ loved mankind. Countess MГЎryaвҖҷs soul always strove toward the infinite, the eternal, and the absolute, and could therefore never be at peace. A stern expression of the lofty, secret suffering of a soul burdened by the body appeared on her face. NikolГЎy gazed at her. вҖңO God! What will become of us if she dies, as I always fear when her face is like that?вҖқ thought he, and placing himself before the icon he began to say his evening prayers. XVI XVI NatГЎsha and Pierre, left alone, also began to talk as only a husband and wife can talk, that is, with extraordinary clearness and rapidity, understanding and expressing each otherвҖҷs thoughts in ways contrary to all rules of logic, without premises, deductions, or conclusions, and in a quite peculiar way. NatГЎsha was so used to this kind of talk with her husband that for her it was the surest sign of something being wrong between them if Pierre followed a line of logical reasoning. When he began proving anything, or talking argumentatively and calmly and she, led on by his example, began to do the same, she knew that they were on the verge of a quarrel. From the moment they were alone and NatГЎsha came up to him with wide-open happy eyes, and quickly seizing his head pressed it to her bosom, saying: вҖңNow you are all mine, mine! You wonвҖҷt escape!вҖқвҒ вҖ”from that moment this conversation began, contrary to all the laws of logic and contrary to them because quite different subjects were talked about at one and the same time. This simultaneous discussion of many topics did not prevent a clear understanding but on the contrary was the surest sign that they fully understood one another. Just as in a dream when all is uncertain, unreasoning, and contradictory, except the feeling that guides the dream, so in this intercourse contrary to all laws of reason, the words themselves were not consecutive and clear but only the feeling that prompted them. NatГЎsha spoke to Pierre about her brotherвҖҷs life and doings, of how she had suffered and lacked life during his own absence, and of how she was fonder than ever of MГЎrya, and how MГЎrya was in every way better than herself. In saying this NatГЎsha was sincere in acknowledging MГЎryaвҖҷs superiority, but at the same time by saying it she made a demand on Pierre that he should, all the same, prefer her to MГЎrya and to all other women, and that now, especially after having seen many women in Petersburg, he should tell her so afresh. Pierre, answering NatГЎshaвҖҷs words, told her how intolerable it had been for him to meet ladies at dinners and balls in Petersburg. вҖңI have quite lost the knack of talking to ladies,вҖқ he said. вҖңIt was simply dull. Besides, I was very busy.вҖқ NatГЎsha looked intently at him and went on: вҖңMГЎrya is so splendid,вҖқ she said. вҖңHow she understands children! It is as if she saw straight into their souls. Yesterday, for instance, MГӯtenka was naughtyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңHow like his father he is,вҖқ Pierre interjected. NatГЎsha knew why he mentioned MГӯtenkaвҖҷs likeness to NikolГЎy: the recollection of his dispute with his brother-in-law was unpleasant and he wanted to know what NatГЎsha thought of it. вҖңNikГіlenka has the weakness of never agreeing with anything not generally accepted. But I understand that you value what opens up a fresh line,вҖқ said she, repeating words Pierre had once uttered. вҖңNo, the chief point is that to NikolГЎy ideas and discussions are an amusementвҒ вҖ”almost a pastime,вҖқ said Pierre. вҖңFor instance, he is collecting a library and has made it a rule not to buy a new book till he has read what he had already boughtвҒ вҖ”Sismondi and Rousseau and Montesquieu,вҖқ he added with a smile. вҖңYou know how much IвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ he began to soften down what he had said; but NatГЎsha interrupted him to show that this was unnecessary. вҖңSo you say ideas are an amusement to him.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYes, and for me nothing else is serious. All the time in Petersburg I saw everyone as in a dream. When I am taken up by a thought, all else is mere amusement.вҖқ вҖңAh, IвҖҷm so sorry I wasnвҖҷt there when you met the children,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. вҖңWhich was most delighted? Lisa, IвҖҷm sure.вҖқ вҖңYes,вҖқ Pierre replied, and went on with what was in his mind. вҖңNikolГЎy says we ought not to think. But I canвҖҷt help it. Besides, when I was in Petersburg I felt (I can say this to you) that the whole affair would go to pieces without meвҒ вҖ”everyone was pulling his own way. But I succeeded in uniting them all; and then my idea is so clear and simple. You see, I donвҖҷt say that we ought to oppose this and that. We may be mistaken. What I say is: вҖҳJoin hands, you who love the right, and let there be but one bannerвҒ вҖ”that of active virtue.вҖҷ Prince SergГ©y is a fine fellow and clever.вҖқ NatГЎsha would have had no doubt as to the greatness of PierreвҖҷs idea, but one thing disconcerted her. вҖңCan a man so important and necessary to society be also my husband? How did this happen?вҖқ She wished to express this doubt to him. вҖңNow who could decide whether he is really cleverer than all the others?вҖқ she asked herself, and passed in review all those whom Pierre most respected. Judging by what he had said there was no one he had respected so highly as PlatГіn KaratГЎev. вҖңDo you know what I am thinking about?вҖқ she asked. вҖңAbout PlatГіn KaratГЎev. Would he have approved of you now, do you think?вҖқ Pierre was not at all surprised at this question. He understood his wifeвҖҷs line of thought. вҖңPlatГіn KaratГЎev?вҖқ he repeated, and pondered, evidently sincerely trying to imagine KaratГЎevвҖҷs opinion on the subject. вҖңHe would not have understoodвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ yet perhaps he would.вҖқ вҖңI love you awfully!вҖқ NatГЎsha suddenly said. вҖңAwfully, awfully!вҖқ вҖңNo, he would not have approved,вҖқ said Pierre, after reflection. вҖңWhat he would have approved of is our family life. He was always so anxious to find seemliness, happiness, and peace in everything, and I should have been proud to let him see us. There nowвҒ вҖ”you talk of my absence, but you wouldnвҖҷt believe what a special feeling I have for you after a separation.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңYes, I should thinkвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ NatГЎsha began. вҖңNo, itвҖҷs not that. I never leave off loving you. And one couldnвҖҷt love more, but this is something special.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Yes, of courseвҒ вҖ”вҖқ he did not finish because their eyes meeting said the rest. вҖңWhat nonsense it is,вҖқ NatГЎsha suddenly exclaimed, вҖңabout honeymoons, and that the greatest happiness is at first! On the contrary, now is the best of all. If only you did not go away! Do you remember how we quarreled? And it was always my fault. Always mine. And what we quarreled aboutвҒ вҖ”I donвҖҷt even remember!вҖқ вҖңAlways about the same thing,вҖқ said Pierre with a smile. вҖңJealoвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ вҖңDonвҖҷt say it! I canвҖҷt bear it!вҖқ NatГЎsha cried, and her eyes glittered coldly and vindictively. вҖңDid you see her?вҖқ she added, after a pause. вҖңNo, and if I had I shouldnвҖҷt have recognized her.вҖқ They were silent for a while. вҖңOh, do you know? While you were talking in the study I was looking at you,вҖқ NatГЎsha began, evidently anxious to disperse the cloud that had come over them. вҖңYou are as like him as two peasвҒ вҖ”like the boy.вҖқ (She meant her little son.) вҖңOh, itвҖҷs time to go to him.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The milkвҖҷs come.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But IвҖҷm sorry to leave you.вҖқ They were silent for a few seconds. Then suddenly turning to one another at the same time they both began to speak. Pierre began with self-satisfaction and enthusiasm, NatГЎsha with a quiet, happy smile. Having interrupted one another they both stopped to let the other continue. вҖңNo. What did you say? Go on, go on.вҖқ вҖңNo, you go on, I was talking nonsense,вҖқ said NatГЎsha. Pierre finished what he had begun. It was the sequel to his complacent reflections on his success in Petersburg. At that moment it seemed to him that he was chosen to give a new direction to the whole of Russian society and to the whole world. вҖңI only wished to say that ideas that have great results are always simple ones. My whole idea is that if vicious people are united and constitute a power, then honest folk must do the same. Now thatвҖҷs simple enough.вҖқ вҖңYes.вҖқ вҖңAnd what were you going to say?вҖқ вҖңI? Only nonsense.вҖқ вҖңBut all the same?вҖқ вҖңOh nothing, only a trifle,вҖқ said NatГЎsha, smiling still more brightly. вҖңI only wanted to tell you about PГ©tya: today nurse was coming to take him from me, and he laughed, shut his eyes, and clung to me. IвҖҷm sure he thought he was hiding. Awfully sweet! There, now heвҖҷs crying. Well, goodbye!вҖқ and she left the room. Meanwhile downstairs in NikГіlenka BolkГіnskiвҖҷs bedroom a little lamp was burning as usual. (The boy was afraid of the dark and they could not cure him of it.) Dessalles slept propped up on four pillows and his Roman nose emitted sounds of rhythmic snoring. NikГіlenka, who had just waked up in a cold perspiration, sat up in bed and gazed before him with wide-open eyes. He had awaked from a terrible dream. He had dreamed that he and Uncle Pierre, wearing helmets such as were depicted in his Plutarch, were leading a huge army. The army was made up of white slanting lines that filled the air like the cobwebs that float about in autumn and which Dessalles called les fils de la ViГЁrge. In front was Glory, which was similar to those threads but rather thicker. He and Pierre were borne along lightly and joyously, nearer and nearer to their goal. Suddenly the threads that moved them began to slacken and become entangled and it grew difficult to move. And Uncle NikolГЎy stood before them in a stern and threatening attitude. вҖңHave you done this?вҖқ he said, pointing to some broken sealing wax and pens. вҖңI loved you, but I have orders from ArakchГ©ev and will kill the first of you who moves forward.вҖқ NikГіlenka turned to look at Pierre but Pierre was no longer there. In his place was his fatherвҒ вҖ”Prince AndrГ©yвҒ вҖ”and his father had neither shape nor form, but he existed, and when NikГіlenka perceived him he grew faint with love: he felt himself powerless, limp, and formless. His father caressed and pitied him. But Uncle NikolГЎy came nearer and nearer to them. Terror seized NikГіlenka and he awoke. вҖңMy father!вҖқ he thought. (Though there were two good portraits of Prince AndrГ©y in the house, NikГіlenka never imagined him in human form.) вҖңMy father has been with me and caressed me. He approved of me and of Uncle Pierre. Whatever he may tell me, I will do it. Mucius Scaevola burned his hand. Why should not the same sort of thing happen to me? I know they want me to learn. And I will learn. But someday I shall have finished learning, and then I will do something. I only pray God that something may happen to me such as happened to PlutarchвҖҷs men, and I will act as they did. I will do better. Everyone shall know me, love me, and be delighted with me!вҖқ And suddenly his bosom heaved with sobs and he began to cry. вҖңAre you ill?вҖқ he heard DessallesвҖҷ voice asking. вҖңNo,вҖқ answered NikГіlenka, and lay back on his pillow. вҖңHe is good and kind and I am fond of him!вҖқ he thought of Dessalles. вҖңBut Uncle Pierre! Oh, what a wonderful man he is! And my father? Oh, Father, Father! Yes, I will do something with which even he would be satisfied.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ II II If we assume as the historians do that great men lead humanity to the attainment of certain endsвҒ вҖ”the greatness of Russia or of France, the balance of power in Europe, the diffusion of the ideas of the Revolution, general progress, or anything elseвҒ вҖ”then it is impossible to explain the facts of history without introducing the conceptions of chance and genius. If the aim of the European wars at the beginning of the nineteenth century had been the aggrandizement of Russia, that aim might have been accomplished without all the preceding wars and without the invasion. If the aim was the aggrandizement of France, that might have been attained without the Revolution and without the Empire. If the aim was the dissemination of ideas, the printing press could have accomplished that much better than warfare. If the aim was the progress of civilization, it is easy to see that there are other ways of diffusing civilization more expedient than by the destruction of wealth and of human lives. Why did it happen in this and not in some other way? Because it happened so! вҖңChance created the situation; genius utilized it,вҖқ says history. But what is chance? What is genius? The words chance and genius do not denote any really existing thing and therefore cannot be defined. Those words only denote a certain stage of understanding of phenomena. I do not know why a certain event occurs; I think that I cannot know it; so I do not try to know it and I talk about chance. I see a force producing effects beyond the scope of ordinary human agencies; I do not understand why this occurs and I talk of genius. To a herd of rams, the ram the herdsman drives each evening into a special enclosure to feed and that becomes twice as fat as the others must seem to be a genius. And it must appear an astonishing conjunction of genius with a whole series of extraordinary chances that this ram, who instead of getting into the general fold every evening goes into a special enclosure where there are oatsвҒ вҖ”that this very ram, swelling with fat, is killed for meat. But the rams need only cease to suppose that all that happens to them happens solely for the attainment of their sheepish aims; they need only admit that what happens to them may also have purposes beyond their ken, and they will at once perceive a unity and coherence in what happened to the ram that was fattened. Even if they do not know for what purpose they are fattened, they will at least know that all that happened to the ram did not happen accidentally, and will no longer need the conceptions of chance or genius. Only by renouncing our claim to discern a purpose immediately intelligible to us, and admitting the ultimate purpose to be beyond our ken, may we discern the sequence of experiences in the lives of historic characters and perceive the cause of the effect they produce (incommensurable with ordinary human capabilities), and then the words chance and genius become superfluous. We need only confess that we do not know the purpose of the European convulsions and that we know only the factsвҒ вҖ”that is, the murders, first in France, then in Italy, in Africa, in Prussia, in Austria, in Spain, and in RussiaвҒ вҖ”and that the movements from the west to the east and from the east to the west form the essence and purpose of these events, and not only shall we have no need to see exceptional ability and genius in Napoleon and Alexander, but we shall be unable to consider them to be anything but like other men, and we shall not be obliged to have recourse to chance for an explanation of those small events which made these people what they were, but it will be clear that all those small events were inevitable. By discarding a claim to knowledge of the ultimate purpose, we shall clearly perceive that just as one cannot imagine a blossom or seed for any single plant better suited to it than those it produces, so it is impossible to imagine any two people more completely adapted down to the smallest detail for the purpose they had to fulfill, than Napoleon and Alexander with all their antecedents. III III The fundamental and essential significance of the European events of the beginning of the nineteenth century lies in the movement of the mass of the European peoples from west to east and afterwards from east to west. The commencement of that movement was the movement from west to east. For the peoples of the west to be able to make their warlike movement to Moscow it was necessary: (1) that they should form themselves into a military group of a size able to endure a collision with the warlike military group of the east, (2) that they should abandon all established traditions and customs, and (3) that during their military movement they should have at their head a man who could justify to himself and to them the deceptions, robberies, and murders which would have to be committed during that movement. And beginning with the French Revolution the old inadequately large group was destroyed, as well as the old habits and traditions, and step by step a group was formed of larger dimensions with new customs and traditions, and a man was produced who would stand at the head of the coming movement and bear the responsibility for all that had to be done. A man without convictions, without habits, without traditions, without a name, and not even a Frenchman, emergesвҒ вҖ”by what seem the strangest chancesвҒ вҖ”from among all the seething French parties, and without joining any one of them is borne forward to a prominent position. The ignorance of his colleagues, the weakness and insignificance of his opponents, the frankness of his falsehoods, and the dazzling and self-confident limitations of this man raise him to the head of the army. The brilliant qualities of the soldiers of the army sent to Italy, his opponentsвҖҷ reluctance to fight, and his own childish audacity and self-confidence secure him military fame. Innumerable so-called chances accompany him everywhere. The disfavor into which he falls with the rulers of France turns to his advantage. His attempts to avoid his predestined path are unsuccessful: he is not received into the Russian service, and the appointment he seeks in Turkey comes to nothing. During the war in Italy he is several times on the verge of destruction and each time is saved in an unexpected manner. Owing to various diplomatic considerations the Russian armiesвҒ вҖ”just those which might have destroyed his prestigeвҒ вҖ”do not appear upon the scene till he is no longer there. On his return from Italy he finds the government in Paris in a process of dissolution in which all those who are in it are inevitably wiped out and destroyed. And by chance an escape from this dangerous position presents itself in the form of an aimless and senseless expedition to Africa. Again so-called chance accompanies him. Impregnable Malta surrenders without a shot; his most reckless schemes are crowned with success. The enemyвҖҷs fleet, which subsequently did not let a single boat pass, allows his entire army to elude it. In Africa a whole series of outrages are committed against the almost unarmed inhabitants. And the men who commit these crimes, especially their leader, assure themselves that this is admirable, this is gloryвҒ вҖ”it resembles Caesar and Alexander the Great and is therefore good. This ideal of glory and grandeurвҒ вҖ”which consists not merely in considering nothing wrong that one does but in priding oneself on every crime one commits, ascribing to it an incomprehensible supernatural significanceвҒ вҖ”that ideal, destined to guide this man and his associates, had scope for its development in Africa. Whatever he does succeeds. The plague does not touch him. The cruelty of murdering prisoners is not imputed to him as a fault. His childishly rash, uncalled-for, and ignoble departure from Africa, leaving his comrades in distress, is set down to his credit, and again the enemyвҖҷs fleet twice lets him slip past. When, intoxicated by the crimes he has committed so successfully, he reaches Paris, the dissolution of the republican government, which a year earlier might have ruined him, has reached its extreme limit, and his presence there now as a newcomer free from party entanglements can only serve to exalt himвҒ вҖ”and though he himself has no plan, he is quite ready for his new role. He had no plan, he was afraid of everything, but the parties snatched at him and demanded his participation. He aloneвҒ вҖ”with his ideal of glory and grandeur developed in Italy and Egypt, his insane self-adulation, his boldness in crime and frankness in lyingвҒ вҖ”he alone could justify what had to be done. He is needed for the place that awaits him, and so almost apart from his will and despite his indecision, his lack of a plan, and all his mistakes, he is drawn into a conspiracy that aims at seizing power and the conspiracy is crowned with success. He is pushed into a meeting of the legislature. In alarm he wishes to flee, considering himself lost. He pretends to fall into a swoon and says senseless things that should have ruined him. But the once proud and shrewd rulers of France, feeling that their part is played out, are even more bewildered than he, and do not say the words they should have said to destroy him and retain their power. Chance, millions of chances, give him power, and all men as if by agreement cooperate to confirm that power. Chance forms the characters of the rulers of France, who submit to him; chance forms the character of Paul I of Russia who recognizes his government; chance contrives a plot against him which not only fails to harm him but confirms his power. Chance puts the Duc dвҖҷEnghien in his hands and unexpectedly causes him to kill himвҒ вҖ”thereby convincing the mob more forcibly than in any other way that he had the right, since he had the might. Chance contrives that though he directs all his efforts to prepare an expedition against England (which would inevitably have ruined him) he never carries out that intention, but unexpectedly falls upon Mack and the Austrians, who surrender without a battle. Chance and genius give him the victory at Austerlitz; and by chance all men, not only the French but all EuropeвҒ вҖ”except England which does not take part in the events about to happenвҒ вҖ”despite their former horror and detestation of his crimes, now recognize his authority, the title he has given himself, and his ideal of grandeur and glory, which seems excellent and reasonable to them all. As if measuring themselves and preparing for the coming movement, the western forces push toward the east several times in 1805, 1806, 1807, and 1809, gaining strength and growing. In 1811 the group of people that had formed in France unites into one group with the peoples of Central Europe. The strength of the justification of the man who stands at the head of the movement grows with the increased size of the group. During the ten-year preparatory period this man had formed relations with all the crowned heads of Europe.
